### [1] [Learning Under Extreme Data Scarcity: Subject-Level Evaluation of Lightweight CNNs for fMRI-Based Prodromal Parkinsons Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00060)
*Naimur Rahman*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Image-level data splits in small fMRI cohorts cause severe information leakage and inflated accuracy; enforcing subject-level splits reveals modest performance (60–81%), with lightweight MobileNet generalizing better than deeper CNNs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep-learning studies in neuroimaging often face extreme data scarcity and correlated samples (many slices per subject), yet common evaluation practices (image-level splits) can leak subject-specific information, overstating performance—especially critical for prodromal Parkinson’s detection where subjects are few and data are hard to obtain.

Method: Using resting-state fMRI from 40 subjects (20 prodromal PD, 20 controls), the authors fine-tune ImageNet-pretrained CNNs (VGG19, Inception V3, Inception-ResNet V2, MobileNet V1). They compare two partitioning strategies: (1) image-level splits that let slices from the same subject appear in train and test; (2) strict subject-level splits that isolate subjects between sets. Models are evaluated by test accuracy under each strategy.

Result: Image-level splitting yields near-perfect test accuracy due to information leakage across slices from the same subject. Under strict subject-level evaluation, test accuracy drops to 60–81%. Among architectures, MobileNet V1 (lower capacity) shows the most reliable generalization, outperforming deeper models (VGG19, Inception V3, Inception-ResNet V2) in this low-data regime.

Conclusion: In extreme low-data neuroimaging settings, evaluation strategy and model capacity dominate performance: enforce subject-level splits to avoid leakage and favor smaller-capacity models like MobileNet for better generalization. Reported high accuracies from image-level splits are likely artifacts. The study is limited to one 40-subject cohort without external or cross-validation but offers concrete recommendations for robust evaluation under severe data scarcity.

Abstract: Deep learning is often applied in settings where data are limited, correlated, and difficult to obtain, yet evaluation practices do not always reflect these constraints. Neuroimaging for prodromal Parkinsons disease is one such case, where subject numbers are small and individual scans produce many highly related samples. This work examines prodromal Parkinsons detection from resting-state fMRI as a machine learning problem centered on learning under extreme data scarcity.
  Using fMRI data from 40 subjects, including 20 prodromal Parkinsons cases and 20 healthy controls, ImageNet-pretrained convolutional neural networks are fine-tuned and evaluated under two different data partitioning strategies. Results show that commonly used image-level splits allow slices from the same subject to appear in both training and test sets, leading to severe information leakage and near-perfect accuracy. When a strict subject-level split is enforced, performance drops substantially, yielding test accuracies between 60 and 81 percent.
  Models with different capacity profiles are compared, including VGG19, Inception V3, Inception ResNet V2, and the lightweight MobileNet V1. Under subject-level evaluation, MobileNet demonstrates the most reliable generalization, outperforming deeper architectures despite having significantly fewer parameters. These results indicate that in extreme low-data regimes, evaluation strategy and model capacity have a greater impact on performance than architectural depth. Although the analysis is limited to a single cohort of 40 subjects and does not include external validation or cross-validation, it provides a concrete case study and practical recommendations for evaluating deep learning models under severe data scarcity.

</details>


### [2] [Automated Quality Check of Sensor Data Annotations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00114)
*Niklas Freund,Zekiye Ilknur-Öz,Tobias Klockau,Patrick Naumann,Philipp Neumaier,Martin Köppel*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Open-source automatic QA tool for railway multi‑sensor training data that detects nine common dataset errors with near‑perfect precision, reducing manual effort and supporting safer AI for GoA2–GoA4 train automation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety‑critical AI for route and environment monitoring in automated trains requires large volumes of high‑quality training data; manual quality assurance is costly, slow, and error‑prone.

Method: Proposes an open‑source framework that automatically checks multi‑sensor datasets from railway vehicles for nine frequent error types. The authors validate the framework by manually reviewing all automatically flagged issues to measure precision.

Result: Six error‑detection methods achieved 100% precision; three additional methods achieved 96–97% precision when validated against manual review, indicating highly reliable error identification and reduced need for manual QA.

Conclusion: Automated dataset quality assurance is feasible and effective for safety‑relevant railway perception systems, substantially lowering manual workload and accelerating development toward higher automation levels (GoA2–GoA4).

Abstract: The monitoring of the route and track environment plays an important role in automated driving. For example, it can be used as an assistance system for route monitoring in automation level Grade of Automation (GoA) 2, where the train driver is still on board. In fully automated, driverless driving at automation level GoA4, these systems finally take over environment monitoring completely independently. With the help of artificial intelligence (AI), they react automatically to risks and dangerous events on the route. To train such AI algorithms, large amounts of training data are required, which must meet high-quality standards due to their safety relevance. In this publication we present an automatic method for assuring the quality of training data, significantly reducing the manual workload and accelerating the development of these systems. We propose an open-source tool designed to detect nine common errors found in multi-sensor datasets for railway vehicles. To evaluate the performance of the framework, all detected errors were manually validated. Six issue detection methods achieved 100% precision, while three additional methods reached precision rates 96% and 97%.

</details>


### [3] [VoxelDiffusionCut: Non-destructive Internal-part Extraction via Iterative Cutting and Structure Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00116)
*Takumi Hachimine,Yuhwan Kwon,Cheng-Yu Kuo,Tomoya Yamanokuchi,Takamitsu Matsubara*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VoxelDiffusionCut infers hidden 3D internal structures from observed cut surfaces using a diffusion model over voxels, quantifies uncertainty, and plans safe cuts to non‑destructively extract target parts; simulations indicate it works effectively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recycling/disposal sites need to remove internal components (e.g., batteries, motors) without damage, but product diversity and missing disassembly info make choosing cut locations hard. The system must estimate where the target part likely is from partial observations, yet high‑dimensional 3D modeling and mode collapse in conventional conditional generative models lead to overconfident, unsafe predictions.

Method: Represent objects as labeled voxel grids and use a conditional diffusion model to complete the 3D voxel representation given observed cutting surfaces. Iteratively: observe cut surfaces, infer probabilistic internal layouts (including target presence probabilities and uncertainty), and plan next cuts that avoid damaging targets while exposing them, leveraging the uncertainty estimates.

Result: In simulation, the approach recovered internal structure from partial observations, produced uncertainty‑aware predictions in unobserved regions, and enabled non‑destructive extraction of target parts by exploiting estimated uncertainty, outperforming conventional approaches prone to overconfidence (e.g., CVAEs).

Conclusion: Diffusion‑based voxel completion offers a tractable, uncertainty‑aware framework for planning non‑destructive extraction under sparse observations. Results suggest practical potential for safer cutting strategies; next steps would include real‑world validation and integration with richer sensing or priors.

Abstract: Non-destructive extraction of the target internal part, such as batteries and motors, by cutting surrounding structures is crucial at recycling and disposal sites. However, the diversity of products and the lack of information on disassembly procedures make it challenging to decide where to cut. This study explores a method for non-destructive extraction of a target internal part that iteratively estimates the internal structure from observed cutting surfaces and formulates cutting plans based on the estimation results. A key requirement is to estimate the probability of the target part's presence from partial observations. However, learning conditional generative models for this task is challenging: The high dimensionality of 3D shape representations makes learning difficult, and conventional models (e.g., conditional variational autoencoders) often fail to capture multi-modal predictive uncertainty due to mode collapse, resulting in overconfident predictions. To address these issues, we propose VoxelDiffusionCut, which iteratively estimates the internal structure represented as voxels using a diffusion model and plans cuts for non-destructive extraction of the target internal part based on the estimation results. Voxel representation allows the model to predict only attributes at fixed grid positions, i.e., types of constituent parts, making learning more tractable. The diffusion model completes the voxel representation conditioned on observed cutting surfaces, capturing uncertainty in unobserved regions to avoid erroneous cuts. Experimental results in simulation suggest that the proposed method can estimate internal structures from observed cutting surfaces and enable non-destructive extraction of the target internal part by leveraging the estimated uncertainty.

</details>


### [4] [Efficient Image Super-Resolution with Multi-Scale Spatial Adaptive Attention Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00118)
*Sushi Rao,Jingwei Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MSAAN is a lightweight super-resolution network that couples multi-scale spatial adaptive attention with efficient local/global feature modeling to deliver SOTA-level PSNR/SSIM at markedly lower parameters and compute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SR models struggle to balance high-fidelity reconstruction with low complexity. There is a need to jointly capture fine local details and long-range context while keeping models efficient and reducing channel redundancy.

Method: Propose MSAAN with a core MSAA module composed of: (1) Global Feature Modulation (GFM) for coherent texture learning via differential feature extraction; (2) Multi-scale Feature Aggregation (MFA) that fuses local-to-global features through pyramidal processing. Two auxiliary components: Local Enhancement Block (LEB) to boost local geometric perception; Feature Interactive Gated Feed-Forward (FIGFF) to enhance nonlinearity and curb channel redundancy. Benchmarked on Set5/14, B100, Urban100, Manga109 at ×2/×3/×4 with ablations.

Result: Both MSAAN and MSAAN-light achieve superior or competitive PSNR/SSIM versus SOTA while using significantly fewer parameters and FLOPs. Visuals show sharper edges and more realistic textures. Ablations confirm each component’s contribution.

Conclusion: Efficient multi-scale attention with targeted local enhancement and gated feed-forward design strikes a strong accuracy–efficiency trade-off, making MSAAN practical for resource-constrained SR without sacrificing detail fidelity.

Abstract: This paper introduces a lightweight image super-resolution (SR) network, termed the Multi-scale Spatial Adaptive Attention Network (MSAAN), to address the common dilemma between high reconstruction fidelity and low model complexity in existing SR methods. The core of our approach is a novel Multi-scale Spatial Adaptive Attention Module (MSAA), designed to jointly model fine-grained local details and long-range contextual dependencies. The MSAA comprises two synergistic components: a Global Feature Modulation Module (GFM) that learns coherent texture structures through differential feature extraction, and a Multi-scale Feature Aggregation Module (MFA) that adaptively fuses features from local to global scales using pyramidal processing. To further enhance the network's capability, we propose a Local Enhancement Block (LEB) to strengthen local geometric perception and a Feature Interactive Gated Feed-Forward Module (FIGFF) to improve nonlinear representation while reducing channel redundancy. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks (Set5, Set14, B100, Urban100, Manga109) across $\times2$, $\times3$, and $\times4$ scaling factors demonstrate that both our lightweight (MSAAN-light) and standard (MSAAN) versions achieve superior or competitive performance in terms of PSNR and SSIM, while maintaining significantly lower parameters and computational costs than state-of-the-art methods. Ablation studies validate the contribution of each component, and visual results show that MSAAN reconstructs sharper edges and more realistic textures.

</details>


### [5] [BiSe-Unet: A Lightweight Dual-path U-Net with Attention-refined Context for Real-time Medical Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00119)
*M Iffat Hossain,Laura Brattain*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BiSe-UNet is a lightweight dual-path U-Net that preserves fine spatial detail while capturing context, using a depthwise-separable decoder to achieve competitive polyp segmentation on Kvasir-Seg at >30 FPS on a Raspberry Pi 5.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable accurate, real-time segmentation during image-guided procedures (e.g., colonoscopy) on resource-constrained edge devices. Existing high-accuracy models are too heavy for embedded inference, while lighter models often lose spatial precision and context, degrading boundary quality and reliability.

Method: Propose BiSe-UNet, a dual-path architecture: (1) an attention-refined context path for semantic understanding; (2) a shallow spatial path to retain fine details; followed by a depthwise separable decoder for efficient feature fusion and reconstruction, tailored for edge deployment.

Result: On the Kvasir-Seg dataset (1,000 annotated polyp images), the model attains competitive Dice and IoU relative to heavier baselines while maintaining real-time throughput (>30 FPS) on Raspberry Pi 5.

Conclusion: BiSe-UNet effectively balances accuracy and efficiency, making real-time, deployable medical image segmentation feasible on constrained hardware.

Abstract: During image-guided procedures, real-time image segmentation is often required. This demands lightweight AI models that can operate on resource-constrained devices. One important use case is endoscopy-guided colonoscopy, where polyps must be detected in real time. The Kvasir-Seg dataset, a publicly available benchmark for this task, contains 1,000 high-resolution endoscopic images of polyps with corresponding pixel-level segmentation masks. Achieving real-time inference speed for clinical deployment in constrained environments requires highly efficient and lightweight network architectures. However, many existing models remain too computationally intensive for embedded deployment. Lightweight architectures, although faster, often suffer from reduced spatial precision and weaker contextual understanding, leading to degraded boundary quality and reduced diagnostic reliability. To address these challenges, we introduce BiSe-UNet, a lightweight dual-path U-Net that integrates an attention-refined context path with a shallow spatial path for detailed feature preservation, followed by a depthwise separable decoder for efficient reconstruction. Evaluated on the Kvasir-Seg dataset, BiSe-UNet achieves competitive Dice and IoU scores while sustaining real-time throughput exceeding 30 FPS on Raspberry Pi 5, demonstrating its effectiveness for accurate, lightweight, and deployable medical image segmentation on edge hardware.

</details>


### [6] [NovaLAD: A Fast, CPU-Optimized Document Extraction Pipeline for Generative AI and Data Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00122)
*Aman Ulla*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: NovaLAD is a CPU-first, parallel document parsing system that fuses dual YOLO detectors (elements + layout), rule-based grouping, and selectively applied vision-language modeling (gated by a ViT image relevance classifier) to produce layout-aware structured outputs (JSON, Markdown, RAG text, knowledge graphs), achieving 96.49% TEDS and 98.51% NID on DP-Bench, outperforming commercial and open-source parsers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-quality document extraction is a bottleneck for RAG, knowledge bases, and downstream GenAI, which require accurate text and layout structure. Existing parsers can be slow, costly (especially with VLMs), GPU-dependent, or noisy (processing non-informative images). There is a need for a fast, accurate, layout-aware, and cost-efficient CPU solution that outputs multiple downstream-ready formats.

Method: NovaLAD processes page images through two concurrent YOLO models: an element detector (e.g., title, header, text, table, image) and a layout detector (e.g., layout_box, column_group, multi_column, row_group). Detected regions are combined via rule-based grouping to form coherent structures. Images/figures are first filtered by a ViT relevance classifier; only relevant ones are sent to a Vision LLM to extract titles, summaries, and structured info, reducing noise and cost. The pipeline runs on CPU with parallel execution across detection, classification, OCR, and conversion, and emits multiple representations including structured JSON, Markdown, RAG-ready text, and knowledge graphs.

Result: On the DP-Bench benchmark (upstage/dp-bench), NovaLAD reports 96.49% TEDS and 98.51% NID, surpassing both commercial and open-source document parsers. The design also claims reduced noise and inference cost by filtering images prior to Vision LLM calls, and practical throughput via CPU-based parallelization.

Conclusion: NovaLAD offers a pragmatic, GPU-free document parsing pipeline that balances speed, cost, and accuracy through dual detection, rule-based grouping, and selective VLM use. Empirical results on DP-Bench indicate state-of-the-art quality, and the multi-format outputs make it directly useful for RAG and knowledge-base construction.

Abstract: Document extraction is an important step before retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), knowledge bases, and downstream generative AI can work. It turns unstructured documents like PDFs and scans into structured text and layout-aware representations. We introduce NovaLAD, a comprehensive document parsing system that integrates two concurrent YOLO object detection models - element detection and layout detection - with rule-based grouping and optional vision-language enhancement. When a page image is sent in, the first thing that happens is that it goes through both models at the same time. The element model finds semantic content like the title, header, text, table, image, and so on, and the layout model finds structural regions like layout_box, column_group, multi_column, row_group, and so on. A key design decision is to first send an image or figure through an image classifier (ViT) that decides whether it is relevant or not. Only useful images are then submitted to the Vision LLM for title, summary, and structured information, which cuts down on noise and costs. NovaLAD is built for speed: it works on CPU, employs parallel execution for detection, classification, OCR, and conversion, and generates several forms, including structured JSON, Markdown, RAG-ready texts, and knowledge graphs. We test on the DP-Bench benchmark (upstage/dp-bench) and get 96.49% TEDS and 98.51% NID, which is better than both commercial and open-source parsers. This paper explains how to extract data, how the architecture works, how data flows, and how to make NovaLAD both accurate and usable without needing a GPU.

</details>


### [7] [CT-Flow: Orchestrating CT Interpretation Workflow with Model Context Protocol Servers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00123)
*Yannian Gu,Xizhuo Zhang,Linjie Mu,Yongrui Yu,Zhongzhen Huang,Shaoting Zhang,Xiaofan Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CT-Flow is an MCP-enabled, tool-aware agent for 3D CT that decomposes clinical queries into multi-step tool sequences; paired with the new CT-FlowBench benchmark, it achieves SOTA, +41% diagnostic accuracy over baselines and 95% autonomous tool-use success, advancing interoperable radiology workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between static, single-pass LVLM inference and the dynamic, tool-mediated workflow of real radiology reading (slice review, measurement, radiomics, segmentation) for 3D CT.

Method: Propose CT-Flow, an agentic framework that leverages the Model Context Protocol (MCP) to interoperate with clinical tools and orchestrate multi-step reasoning. Curate CT-FlowBench, a large-scale instruction-tuning dataset focused on 3D CT tool use and sequential reasoning. The agent decomposes natural-language queries into automated tool-invocation sequences for volumetric interpretation.

Result: On CT-FlowBench and standard 3D VQA datasets, CT-Flow attains state-of-the-art performance, with a 41% diagnostic-accuracy gain over baselines and a 95% success rate in autonomous tool invocation.

Conclusion: Tool-aware, agentic LVLMs guided by standardized interfaces (MCP) better align with clinical practice for volumetric CT, offering a scalable path to real-world integration of autonomous assistance in radiology.

Abstract: Recent advances in Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have shown strong potential for multi-modal radiological reasoning, particularly in tasks like diagnostic visual question answering (VQA) and radiology report generation. However, most existing approaches for 3D CT analysis largely rely on static, single-pass inference. In practice, clinical interpretation is a dynamic, tool-mediated workflow where radiologists iteratively review slices and use measurement, radiomics, and segmentation tools to refine findings. To bridge this gap, we propose CT-Flow, an agentic framework designed for interoperable volumetric interpretation. By leveraging the Model Context Protocol (MCP), CT-Flow shifts from closed-box inference to an open, tool-aware paradigm. We curate CT-FlowBench, the first large-scale instruction-tuning benchmark tailored for 3D CT tool-use and multi-step reasoning. Built upon this, CT-Flow functions as a clinical orchestrator capable of decomposing complex natural language queries into automated tool-use sequences. Experimental evaluations on CT-FlowBench and standard 3D VQA datasets demonstrate that CT-Flow achieves state-of-the-art performance, surpassing baseline models by 41% in diagnostic accuracy and achieving a 95% success rate in autonomous tool invocation. This work provides a scalable foundation for integrating autonomous, agentic intelligence into real-world clinical radiology.

</details>


### [8] [OrthoAI: A Lightweight Deep Learning Framework for Automated Biomechanical Analysis in Clear Aligner Orthodontics -- A Methodological Proof-of-Concept](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00124)
*Edouard Lansiaux,Margaux Leman,Mehdi Ammi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OrthoAI is an open-source, proof-of-concept system that combines lightweight 3D tooth segmentation with a rule-based biomechanical analyzer to help clinicians evaluate clear-aligner treatment plans; it runs in under 4 seconds but is trained on landmark-derived point clouds and not yet validated on real intraoral meshes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clear aligner therapy is widespread, yet manual review of digital treatment plans (e.g., ClinCheck) is slow and error-prone. There is a need for automated, evidence-grounded decision support to flag unrealistic tooth movements and summarize plan predictability.

Method: A Dynamic Graph CNN (≈60.7k parameters) segments teeth from landmark-reconstructed point clouds (3DTeethLand). A rule-based biomechanical engine, informed by orthodontic literature (Kravitz 2009; Simon 2014), decomposes per-tooth motion into six degrees of freedom, estimates movement-specific predictability, issues alerts when limits are exceeded, and computes a composite index. Emphasis is on tooth identity and centroid/axis estimation rather than precise meshes.

Result: On surrogate point clouds, the model achieves an 81.4% Tooth Identification Rate and 8.25% mIoU, reflecting the coarse supervision from sparse landmarks. Despite coarse spatial boundaries, the downstream biomechanical analysis remains usable for identity- and axis-based metrics. The full pipeline executes in under 4 seconds on consumer hardware; code, weights, and tools are released.

Conclusion: The system demonstrates feasibility and provides a baseline for future work with full-mesh data, but current performance is limited by sparse landmark supervision and lack of validation on real intraoral meshes. It should not be assumed to generalize beyond landmark-derived representations.

Abstract: Clear aligner therapy now dominates orthodontics, yet clinician review of digitally planned tooth movements-typically via ClinCheck (Align Technology)-remains slow and error-prone. We present OrthoAI, an open-source proof-of-concept decision-support system combining lightweight 3D dental segmentation with automated biomechanical analysis to assist treatment-plan evaluation. The framework uses a Dynamic Graph CNN trained on landmark-reconstructed point clouds from 3DTeethLand (MICCAI) and integrates a rule-based biomechanical engine grounded in orthodontic evidence (Kravitz et al 2009; Simon et al 2014). The system decomposes per-tooth motion across six degrees of freedom, computes movement-specific predictability, issues alerts when biomechanical limits are exceeded, and derives an exploratory composite index. With 60,705 trainable parameters, segmentation reaches a Tooth Identification Rate of $81.4\%$ and mIoU of $8.25\%$ on surrogate point clouds-reflecting sparse landmark supervision rather than dense meshes. Although spatial boundaries are coarse, downstream analysis depends mainly on tooth identity and approximate centroid/axis estimation. Results establish a baseline for future full-mesh training and highlight current perceptual limits. The end-to-end pipeline runs in $<4s$ on consumer hardware. Code, weights, and analysis tools are released to support reproducible research in geometric deep learning and digital orthodontics. The system has not been validated on real intraoral meshes and should not be assumed to generalize beyond landmark-derived representations.

</details>


### [9] [QuickGrasp: Responsive Video-Language Querying Service via Accelerated Tokenization and Edge-Augmented Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00126)
*Miao Zhang,Ruixiao Zhang,Jianxin Shi,Hengzhi Wang,Hao Fang,Jiangchuan Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: QuickGrasp is a QoS‑aware, local‑first serving system for video‑language queries that shares visual representations across VLM variants and selectively augments with edge compute to match large‑model accuracy while cutting latency by up to 12.8×.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large VLMs are accurate but resource‑hungry and slow, especially when served remotely; small on‑device VLMs respond fast but lose accuracy. Real‑world video querying needs both responsiveness and reliability, motivating a system that reconciles the latency–accuracy trade‑off.

Method: Adopts a local‑first architecture with on‑demand edge augmentation. Exploits VLM modularity to share vision representations across model variants, avoiding redundant computation. Adds three designs: (1) accelerated video tokenization, (2) query‑adaptive edge augmentation to invoke stronger/edge models only when beneficial, and (3) delay‑aware, accuracy‑preserving configuration of vision token density.

Result: A prototype evaluated on multiple video understanding benchmarks matches the accuracy of large VLMs while reducing response delay by up to 12.8×.

Conclusion: QoS‑aware orchestration plus representation sharing enables responsive, accurate video querying on constrained hardware. QuickGrasp advances practical, open‑world VLM services by combining local execution with targeted edge assistance and compute‑efficient token handling.

Abstract: Video-language models (VLMs) are reshaping video querying services, bringing unified solutions to complex perception and reasoning tasks. However, deploying large VLMs in real-world systems remains challenging due to their high resource demands, and remote-based deployment often results in unacceptable response delays. Although small, locally deployable VLMs offer faster responses, they unavoidably fall short in accuracy. To reconcile this trade-off, we propose QuickGrasp, a responsive, quality of service (QoS)-aware system that bridges this gap through a local-first architecture with on-demand edge augmentation. Built upon the highly modular architecture of VLMs, QuickGrasp shares the vision representation across model variants to avoid redundant computation. To maximize system-wide efficiency, QuickGrasp introduces three key designs: accelerated video tokenization, query-adaptive edge augmentation, and delay-aware, accuracy-preserving vision token density configuration. We implement a prototype of QuickGrasp and evaluate it across multiple video understanding benchmarks. The results show that QuickGrasp matches the accuracy of large VLMs while achieving up to a 12.8x reduction in response delay. QuickGrasp represents a key advancement toward building responsive video querying services for open-world understanding that fully leverage the capabilities of VLMs.

</details>


### [10] [Segmenting Low-Contrast XCTs of Concretes: An Unsupervised Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00127)
*Kaustav Das,Gaston Rauchs,Jan Sykora,Anna Kucerova*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They propose an unsupervised, self-annotation method that trains a CNN to semantically segment low-contrast XCT scans of concrete by combining superpixel-based local grouping with CNN receptive-field context, showing it can identify semantically similar structures without manual labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Concrete XCT images have low contrast because aggregates and mortar have similar X-ray attenuation, making segmentation difficult. CNNs work well but need labeled data, which is costly or unavailable for new datasets. An unsupervised alternative is needed.

Method: Use superpixel algorithms to cluster perceptually similar local regions (superpixels) and exploit the CNN’s receptive field to tie these local regions to global image context. The network is trained via self-annotation/pseudo-labels derived from superpixels to learn global–local relationships and perform semantic segmentation without human-provided labels.

Result: On their concrete XCT datasets, the approach learns to identify semantically similar structures despite low contrast; they report the method’s performance (details not specified in the abstract) and show qualitative capability to segment relevant phases.

Conclusion: Self-annotation with superpixels enables unsupervised CNN training for challenging, low-contrast XCT concrete segmentation, reducing dependence on manual labels. Results are promising, and further improvements are possible and discussed by the authors.

Abstract: This work tests a self-annotation-based unsupervised methodology for training a convolutional neural network (CNN) model for semantic segmentation of X-ray computed tomography (XCT) scans of concretes. Concrete poses a unique challenge for XCT imaging due to similar X-ray attenuation coefficients of aggregates and mortar, resulting in low-contrast between the two phases in the ensuing images. While CNN-based models are a proven technique for semantic segmentation in such challenging cases, they typically require labeled training data, which is often unavailable for new datasets or are costly to obtain. To counter that limitation, a self-annotation technique is used here which leverages superpixel algorithms to identify perceptually similar local regions in an image and relates them to the global context in the image by utilizing the receptive field of a CNN-based model. This enables the model to learn a global-local relationship in the images and enables identification of semantically similar structures. We therefore present the performance of the unsupervised training methodology on our XCT datasets and discuss potential avenues for further improvements.

</details>


### [11] [Predicting Local Climate Zones using Urban Morphometrics and Satellite Imagery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00132)
*Hugo Majer,Martin Fleischmann*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They test whether 2D urban morphometrics can predict Local Climate Zones (LCZs) and whether fusing morphometrics with satellite imagery improves accuracy. The mapping from morphometrics to LCZs is selective, inconsistent, and site-dependent; fusion gives modest, uneven gains. Overall, LCZs have a tenuous link to measurable urban form, so they should be used cautiously in morphological studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LCZs are widely used to represent urban form but are typically mapped from satellite imagery. Urban morphometrics offers a systematic, numerical description of form. The study asks if morphometrics alone can recover LCZ classes and whether combining them with imagery yields better, more reliable LCZ predictions across places.

Method: Compute 321 2D morphometric features from building footprints and street networks at multiple spatial scales. Build four classifiers: (1) morphometric-only LCZ prediction, (2) baseline image-only LCZ prediction, and (3–4) two fusion schemes that combine morphometrics with imagery. Evaluate performance across five urban sites.

Result: Morphometric-only classification shows selective and inconsistent correspondence between 2D morphometrics and LCZ types; performance varies by site. It also reveals that more urban-form properties matter for LCZ discrimination than the usual limited set. Fusion versus image-only yields clear accuracy gains at two sites but negligible or slightly negative changes at others—net benefits are modest and inconsistent.

Conclusion: Measurable, visible aspects of 2D urban form do not robustly determine LCZs across sites. Fusion with imagery provides limited, context-dependent benefits. Therefore, LCZs should be applied with caution in morphological analyses and not assumed to generalize from morphometrics or imagery alone.

Abstract: The Local Climate Zone (LCZ) framework is commonly employed to represent urban form in morphological analyses despite its mapping predominantly relies on satellite imagery. Urban morphometrics, describing urban form via numerical measures of physical aspects and spatial relationships of its elements, offers another avenue. This study evaluates the ability of morphometric assessment to predict LCZs using a) a morphometric-based LCZ prediction, and b) a fusion-based LCZ prediction combining morphometrics with satellite imagery. We calculate 321 2D morphometric attributes from building footprints and street networks, covering their various properties at multiple spatial scales. Subsequently, we develop four classification schemes: morphometric-based prediction, baseline image-based prediction, and two techniques fusing morphometrics with imagery. We evaluate them across five sites. Results from the morphometric-based prediction indicate that the correspondence between 2D urban morphometrics and urban LCZ types is selective and inconsistent, rendering the efficacy of this method site-dependent. Nevertheless, it demonstrated that a much broader range of urban form properties is relevant for distinguishing LCZ types compared to standard parameters. Relative to the image-based baseline, the fusion yielded relatively distinct accuracy improvements for urban LCZ types at two sites; however, gains at the remaining sites were negligible or even slightly negative, suggesting that the benefits of fusion are modest and inconsistent. Collectively, these results indicate that the relationship between the LCZs and the measurable, visible aspects of urban form is tenuous, thus the LCZ framework should be used with caution in morphological studies.

</details>


### [12] [You Don't Need All That Attention: Surgical Memorization Mitigation in Text-to-Image Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00133)
*Kairan Zhao,Eleni Triantafillou,Peter Triantafillou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GUARD is an inference-time guidance framework for text-to-image diffusion models that reduces training data memorization by dynamically attenuating cross-attention at statistically identified prompt positions, steering generations away from memorized images while preserving prompt alignment and image quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models can memorize and reproduce training images, posing privacy and copyright risks. The goal is to mitigate memorization without degrading image fidelity or prompt adherence.

Method: Introduce Guidance Using Attractive-Repulsive Dynamics (GUARD), which modifies the denoising trajectory to repel from training-image basins and attract toward distinct yet prompt-aligned outputs. A concrete instantiation uses a novel statistical test to automatically find prompt token positions that are prone to memorization and then attenuates cross-attention at those positions during inference, yielding surgical, per-prompt control without retraining.

Result: Across two diffusion architectures, GUARD consistently achieves state-of-the-art mitigation of both verbatim and template memorization, while maintaining or improving image quality metrics.

Conclusion: A practical, dynamic, per-prompt, inference-time solution to curb memorization in text-to-image diffusion models that preserves output quality and alignment, establishing a robust SOTA baseline for responsible image generation.

Abstract: Generative models have been shown to "memorize" certain training data, leading to verbatim or near-verbatim generating images, which may cause privacy concerns or copyright infringement. We introduce Guidance Using Attractive-Repulsive Dynamics (GUARD), a novel framework for memorization mitigation in text-to-image diffusion models. GUARD adjusts the image denoising process to guide the generation away from an original training image and towards one that is distinct from training data while remaining aligned with the prompt, guarding against reproducing training data, without hurting image generation quality. We propose a concrete instantiation of this framework, where the positive target that we steer towards is given by a novel method for (cross) attention attenuation based on (i) a novel statistical mechanism that automatically identifies the prompt positions where cross attention must be attenuated and (ii) attenuating cross-attention in these per-prompt locations. The resulting GUARD offers a surgical, dynamic per-prompt inference-time approach that, we find, is by far the most robust method in terms of consistently producing state-of-the-art results for memorization mitigation across two architectures and for both verbatim and template memorization, while also improving upon or yielding comparable results in terms of image quality.

</details>


### [13] [TinyVLM: Zero-Shot Object Detection on Microcontrollers via Vision-Language Distillation with Matryoshka Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00136)
*Bibin Wilson*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TinyVLM enables zero-shot object detection on microcontrollers under 1MB by decoupling text and vision, using nested (Matryoshka) distilled embeddings and quantized prototype storage, achieving competitive accuracy with very low memory and real-time FPS on commodity MCUs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Zero-shot object detection typically depends on large VLMs (e.g., CLIP) that require hundreds of MB of memory, making them infeasible for MCUs. There is a need to bring open-vocabulary visual recognition to severely resource-constrained edge devices without sacrificing too much accuracy or latency.

Method: 1) Decoupled architecture: run only a tiny vision encoder on-device while precomputing/storing class text embeddings in flash, avoiding a runtime text encoder. 2) Matryoshka distillation: train nested embeddings at multiple dimensions (16–256) to allow dynamic accuracy–memory trade-offs. 3) Quantized embedding storage: compress class prototypes (4× smaller) with minimal accuracy loss. Trained on CC3M; deploy compact vision encoder; compare zero-shot performance across benchmarks.

Result: Competitive zero-shot performance on COCO, Flowers102, and Food101 while using just ~285KB RAM and ~892KB flash for the on-device vision encoder. Achieves 26 FPS on STM32H7 and >1,000 FPS on MAX78000 with a CNN accelerator.

Conclusion: A practical path to open-vocabulary object detection on MCUs is feasible: precompute and compress language embeddings, distill scalable representations, and keep only a tiny vision model on-device. TinyVLM delivers strong accuracy within sub-megabyte memory and real-time throughput, enabling true edge deployment for zero-shot detection.

Abstract: Zero-shot object detection enables recognising novel objects without task-specific training, but current approaches rely on large vision language models (VLMs) like CLIP that require hundreds of megabytes of memory - far exceeding the constraints of micro controller units (MCUs). We present TinyVLM, the first framework enabling zero-shot object detection on resource-constrained MCUs with less than 1MB of memory. Our approach introduces three key innovations: (1) a decoupled architecture that separates visual inference from text encoding, allowing precomputed class embeddings to be stored in flash memory; (2) Matryoshka distillation that trains nested embeddings at multiple dimensions (16-256), enabling flexible accuracy-memory trade-offs; and (3) quantized embedding storage that reduces class prototype memory by 4x with minimal accuracy loss. Trained on Conceptual Captions 3M (CC3M), TinyVLM achieves competitive zero-shot accuracy on COCO, Flowers102, and Food101 while requiring only 285KB of RAM and 892KB of flash memory for the deployed vision encoder. We demonstrate real-time inference at 26 FPS on STM32H7 and over 1,000 FPS on MAX78000 with its CNN accelerator, enabling practical zero-shot detection on edge devices for the first time.

</details>


### [14] [Latent Replay Detection: Memory-Efficient Continual Object Detection on Microcontrollers via Task-Adaptive Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00138)
*Bibin Wilson*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LRD introduces a memory-lean latent replay pipeline for continual object detection on MCUs, using FiLM-conditioned task-adaptive compression and IoU-space diverse exemplar selection to preserve detection accuracy within a ~64KB budget and real MCU latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Edge MCUs can run object detection but cannot learn new classes post-deployment because continual learning typically requires storing raw images that exceed tiny MCU memories (tens of KB). A method is needed to support on-device continual detection within strict memory and compute limits.

Method: LRD stores replay exemplars in a compact latent space (~150 bytes/sample) instead of raw images. It uses: (1) Task-Adaptive Compression via learnable encoders modulated by FiLM using task embeddings to preserve task-discriminative features, outperforming fixed PCA; (2) Spatial-Diverse Exemplar Selection that maximizes bounding-box diversity using farthest-point sampling in IoU space to avoid localization bias; (3) An MCU-deployable pipeline that fits a 64KB buffer holding 400+ exemplars and runs on common MCUs.

Result: On CORe50 (50 classes across 5 tasks), LRD maintains strong mAP@50 after the initial task and clearly outperforms naive fine-tuning under strict memory constraints. It achieves 4.9–97.5 ms per inference on STM32H753ZI, ESP32-S3, and MAX78000, all within a 64KB memory budget.

Conclusion: LRD makes practical, memory-feasible continual object detection on MCUs possible. Its FiLM-based adaptive compression and IoU-diverse sampling synergistically preserve detection capabilities across tasks while meeting real device constraints.

Abstract: Deploying object detection on microcontrollers (MCUs) enables intelligent edge devices but current models cannot learn new object categories after deployment. Existing continual learning methods require storing raw images far exceeding MCU memory budgets of tens of kilobytes. We present Latent Replay Detection (LRD), the first framework for continual object detection under MCU memory constraints. Our key contributions are: 1. Task-Adaptive Compression: Unlike fixed PCA, we propose learnable compression with FiLM (Feature-wise Linear Modulation) conditioning, where task specific embeddings modulate the compression to preserve discriminative features for each task's distribution; 2. Spatial-Diverse Exemplar Selection: Traditional sampling ignores spatial information critical for detection - we select exemplars maximizing bounding box diversity via farthest-point sampling in IoU space, preventing localization bias in replay; 3. MCU-Deployable System: Our latent replay stores 150 bytes per sample versus >10KB for images, enabling a 64KB buffer to hold 400+ exemplars. Experiments on CORe50 (50 classes, 5 tasks) demonstrate that LRD achieves mAP@50 on the initial task and maintains strong performance across subsequent tasks - a significant improvement over naive fine-tuning while operating within strict MCU constraints. Our task-adaptive FiLM compression and spatial diverse exemplar selection work synergistically to preserve detection capabilities. Deployed on STM32H753ZI, ESP32-S3, and MAX78000 MCUs, LRD achieves 4.9-97.5ms latency per inference within a 64KB memory budget-enabling practical continual detection on edge devices for the first time.

</details>


### [15] [Towards Data-driven Nitrogen Estimation in Wheat Fields using Multispectral Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00139)
*Andreas Tritsarolis,Tomaž Bokan,Matej Brumen,Domen Mongus,Yannis Theodoridis*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes TerrAI, a neural network that leverages spatio-temporal variability from remote sensing to enable more precise targeted spraying and fertilization (TSF); real-world experiments indicate it works effectively for data-driven agriculture.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agriculture needs to optimize inputs and reduce environmental impact. TSF can help but is hard to do accurately due to variability in crop type, growth phase, soil, and weather. A robust, data-driven method that accounts for these factors is needed.

Method: Introduce TerrAI, a neural-network-based approach that models spatio-temporal variability across parcels using remote sensing data to guide TSF decisions. It integrates multi-parcel, time-aware signals to recommend precise input application.

Result: On a real-world remote sensing dataset, TerrAI’s performance is empirically validated; results suggest the approach is sound for practical TSF, though the abstract provides no quantitative metrics or baseline comparisons.

Conclusion: TerrAI is a promising data-driven solution for precise TSF that can improve resource efficiency and environmental sustainability; initial real-world validation supports its viability, warranting further quantitative evaluation and field trials.

Abstract: The modernization of agriculture has motivated the development of advanced analytics and decision-support systems to improve resource utilization and reduce environmental impacts. Targeted Spraying and Fertilization (TSF) is a critical operation that enables farmers to apply inputs more precisely, optimizing resource use and promoting environmental sustainability. However, accurate TSF is a challenging problem, due to external factors such as crop type, fertilization phase, soil conditions, and weather dynamics. In this paper, we present TerrAI, a Neural Network-based solution for TSF, which considers the spatio-temporal variability across different parcels. Our experimental study over a real-world remote sensing dataset validates the soundness of TerrAI on data-driven agricultural practices.

</details>


### [16] [Steering Away from Memorization: Reachability-Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Text-to-Image Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00140)
*Sathwik Karnik,Juyeop Kim,Sanmi Koyejo,Jong-Seok Lee,Somil Bansal*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RADS is an inference-time controller that steers text-to-image diffusion away from memorized training images using reachability analysis and constrained RL on text embeddings, preserving image quality and prompt alignment while improving diversity—without modifying the diffusion backbone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Text-to-image diffusion models can reproduce training images (memorization), undermining generalization and safety. Existing mitigations reduce memorization but at the cost of image quality or prompt adherence. A method is needed that curbs memorization while maintaining fidelity and requires no model retraining.

Method: Model the denoising process as a dynamical system and approximate the backward reachable tube—the set of intermediate states that will evolve into memorized samples. Cast mitigation as a constrained RL problem that learns a policy to apply minimal perturbations to the caption embedding to steer trajectories away from these regions, preserving fidelity and alignment. Implemented entirely at inference, plug-and-play over existing diffusion backbones.

Result: Across benchmarks, RADS attains a better Pareto frontier than state-of-the-art baselines on diversity (SSCD), quality (FID), and alignment (CLIP). It robustly reduces memorization without changing the diffusion backbone.

Conclusion: Reachability-aware steering with constrained RL provides an effective, plug-and-play way to mitigate diffusion memorization while retaining quality and alignment, pointing to safe generation controls that operate purely at inference.

Abstract: Text-to-image diffusion models often memorize training data, revealing a fundamental failure to generalize beyond the training set. Current mitigation strategies typically sacrifice image quality or prompt alignment to reduce memorization. To address this, we propose Reachability-Aware Diffusion Steering (RADS), an inference-time framework that prevents memorization while preserving generation fidelity. RADS models the diffusion denoising process as a dynamical system and applies concepts from reachability analysis to approximate the "backward reachable tube"--the set of intermediate states that inevitably evolve into memorized samples. We then formulate mitigation as a constrained reinforcement learning (RL) problem, where a policy learns to steer the trajectory away from memorization via minimal perturbations in the caption embedding space. Empirical evaluations show that RADS achieves a superior Pareto frontier between generation diversity (SSCD), quality (FID), and alignment (CLIP) compared to state-of-the-art baselines. Crucially, RADS provides robust mitigation without modifying the diffusion backbone, offering a plug-and-play solution for safe generation. Our website is available at: https://s-karnik.github.io/rads-memorization-project-page/.

</details>


### [17] [From Scale to Speed: Adaptive Test-Time Scaling for Image Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00141)
*Xiangyan Qu,Zhenlong Yuan,Jing Tang,Rui Chen,Datao Tang,Meng Yu,Lei Sun,Yancheng Bai,Xiangxiang Chu,Gaopeng Gou,Gang Xiong,Yujun Cai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ADE-CoT is an adaptive, on-demand test-time scaling framework for image editing that allocates compute by estimated edit difficulty, performs edit-aware early verification, and stops opportunistically, yielding better edits with >2× speedup versus Best-of-N across multiple editors and benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Image Chain-of-Thought (Image-CoT) improves text-to-image generation by spending more inference time, but image editing is goal-directed and constrained by the source image and instruction. Applying generic Image-CoT causes (1) inefficient fixed sampling budgets, (2) unreliable early verification with general-purpose MLLM scores, and (3) redundant, near-duplicate edited samples—calling for an editing-specific, efficient test-time scaling approach.

Method: ADE-CoT (Adaptive Edit-CoT) introduces: (1) difficulty-aware resource allocation that dynamically assigns sampling budgets based on predicted edit difficulty; (2) edit-specific early pruning using region localization and caption-consistency checks to keep promising candidates; and (3) depth-first opportunistic stopping guided by an instance-specific verifier that halts once intent-aligned results are found.

Result: On three SOTA editing models (Step1X-Edit, BAGEL, FLUX.1 Kontext) and three benchmarks, ADE-CoT achieves superior performance–efficiency trade-offs; with comparable sampling budgets it delivers higher editing quality and more than 2× speedup over Best-of-N.

Conclusion: Tailoring test-time scaling to the constraints and goals of image editing—via adaptive budget allocation, edit-aware verification, and early stopping—improves both quality and efficiency and generalizes across different editing models. This suggests future extensions with better difficulty estimators and verifiers and broader evaluation settings.

Abstract: Image Chain-of-Thought (Image-CoT) is a test-time scaling paradigm that improves image generation by extending inference time. Most Image-CoT methods focus on text-to-image (T2I) generation. Unlike T2I generation, image editing is goal-directed: the solution space is constrained by the source image and instruction. This mismatch causes three challenges when applying Image-CoT to editing: inefficient resource allocation with fixed sampling budgets, unreliable early-stage verification using general MLLM scores, and redundant edited results from large-scale sampling. To address this, we propose ADaptive Edit-CoT (ADE-CoT), an on-demand test-time scaling framework to enhance editing efficiency and performance. It incorporates three key strategies: (1) a difficulty-aware resource allocation that assigns dynamic budgets based on estimated edit difficulty; (2) edit-specific verification in early pruning that uses region localization and caption consistency to select promising candidates; and (3) depth-first opportunistic stopping, guided by an instance-specific verifier, that terminates when intent-aligned results are found. Extensive experiments on three SOTA editing models (Step1X-Edit, BAGEL, FLUX.1 Kontext) across three benchmarks show that ADE-CoT achieves superior performance-efficiency trade-offs. With comparable sampling budgets, ADE-CoT obtains better performance with more than 2x speedup over Best-of-N.

</details>


### [18] [GrapHist: Graph Self-Supervised Learning for Histopathology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00143)
*Sevda Öğüt,Cédric Vincent-Cuaz,Natalia Dubljevic,Carlos Hurtado,Vaishnavi Subramanian,Pascal Frossard,Dorina Thanou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GrapHist is a self-supervised, graph-based framework that models histology images as cell graphs and trains with masked autoencoding plus heterophilic GNNs, delivering competitive (often superior) performance to vision SSL while using ~4× fewer parameters, with strong transfer across tasks; datasets and code are released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mainstream self-supervised vision models (e.g., transformers) are domain-agnostic and do not explicitly model the cellular entities and interactions that define histopathology. The authors hypothesize that a biologically informed representation—cells as nodes and their relationships as edges—will yield more efficient and generalizable embeddings for pathology tasks.

Method: Build large-scale cell graphs from histopathology slides and pretrain a graph-based SSL model, GrapHist, that integrates masked autoencoders with heterophilic graph neural networks tailored to capture heterogeneous tumor microenvironments. Pretraining is done on 11 million breast-tissue cell graphs to learn transferable, structure-aware embeddings for slide-, region-, and cell-level tasks.

Result: GrapHist matches or surpasses vision-based SSL baselines across multiple granularities (slide/region/cell) while using ~4× fewer parameters, and it markedly outperforms fully supervised graph models on cancer subtyping. It demonstrates transferability on in-domain and out-of-domain benchmarks.

Conclusion: Biologically grounded cell-graph self-supervision is an efficient and effective alternative to image-based SSL for digital pathology. The work also contributes the first large-scale graph benchmark in this field and releases five datasets and code, facilitating reproducibility and future research.

Abstract: Self-supervised vision models have achieved notable success in digital pathology. However, their domain-agnostic transformer architectures are not originally designed to account for fundamental biological elements of histopathology images, namely cells and their complex interactions. In this work, we hypothesize that a biologically-informed modeling of tissues as cell graphs offers a more efficient representation learning. Thus, we introduce GrapHist, a novel graph-based self-supervised learning framework for histopathology, which learns generalizable and structurally-informed embeddings that enable diverse downstream tasks. GrapHist integrates masked autoencoders and heterophilic graph neural networks that are explicitly designed to capture the heterogeneity of tumor microenvironments. We pre-train GrapHist on a large collection of 11 million cell graphs derived from breast tissues and evaluate its transferability across in- and out-of-domain benchmarks. Our results show that GrapHist achieves competitive performance compared to its vision-based counterparts in slide-, region-, and cell-level tasks, while requiring four times fewer parameters. It also drastically outperforms fully-supervised graph models on cancer subtyping tasks. Finally, we also release five graph-based digital pathology datasets used in our study at https://huggingface.co/ogutsevda/datasets , establishing the first large-scale graph benchmark in this field. Our code is available at https://github.com/ogutsevda/graphist .

</details>


### [19] [Disentangled Hierarchical VAE for 3D Human-Human Interaction Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00144)
*Zichen Geng,Zeeshan Hayder,Bo Miao,Jian Liu,Wei Liu,Ajmal Mian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DHVAE: a disentangled hierarchical VAE combined with latent diffusion generates physically plausible, text-aligned 3D human–human interactions with higher fidelity and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current HHI generators compress all motion into a single latent, which blurs fine-grained per-agent actions and shared interaction context, causing semantic drift and physical artifacts (e.g., penetrations, missed contacts).

Method: Introduce Disentangled Hierarchical VAE (DHVAE) that separates global interaction context from individual motion latents via a CoTransformer. Enforce a discriminative, physically plausible interaction space with contrastive learning focused on contact consistency. Perform high-fidelity synthesis using DDIM-based diffusion in the hierarchical latent space, with a skip-connected AdaLN-Transformer denoiser.

Result: Across extensive evaluations, DHVAE outperforms prior work in motion fidelity, text-to-motion alignment, and physical plausibility, while also being more computationally efficient.

Conclusion: Disentangling interaction context and per-agent motion, coupled with contrastive contact constraints and efficient latent-space diffusion, yields more realistic, controllable, and efficient 3D HHI generation.

Abstract: Generating realistic 3D Human-Human Interaction (HHI) requires coherent modeling of the physical plausibility of the agents and their interaction semantics. Existing methods compress all motion information into a single latent representation, limiting their ability to capture fine-grained actions and inter-agent interactions. This often leads to semantic misalignment and physically implausible artifacts, such as penetration or missed contact. We propose Disentangled Hierarchical Variational Autoencoder (DHVAE) based latent diffusion for structured and controllable HHI generation. DHVAE explicitly disentangles the global interaction context and individual motion patterns into a decoupled latent structure by employing a CoTransformer module. To mitigate implausible and physically inconsistent contacts in HHI, we incorporate contrastive learning constraints with our DHVAE to promote a more discriminative and physically plausible latent interaction space. For high-fidelity interaction synthesis, DHVAE employs a DDIM-based diffusion denoising process in the hierarchical latent space, enhanced by a skip-connected AdaLN-Transformer denoiser. Extensive evaluations show that DHVAE achieves superior motion fidelity, text alignment, and physical plausibility with greater computational efficiency.

</details>


### [20] [M-Gaussian: An Magnetic Gaussian Framework for Efficient Multi-Stack MRI Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00145)
*Kangyuan Zheng,Xuan Cai,Jiangqi Wang,Guixing Fu,Zhuoshuo Li,Yazhou Chen,Xinting Ge,Liangqiong Qu,Mengting Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: M-Gaussian adapts 3D Gaussian Splatting for fast, high-quality reconstruction of isotropic 3D MRI volumes from multi-stack thick-slice data, delivering 40.31 dB PSNR and 14× speedup on FeTA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-stack thick-slice MRI shortens scans and reduces motion sensitivity (e.g., in fetal imaging) but induces severe through-plane anisotropy that impairs volumetric analysis. Existing implicit neural representations can recover isotropic volumes with high quality but are computationally inefficient. A faster, robust method is needed for practical clinical use.

Method: Introduce M-Gaussian, a 3D Gaussian Splatting approach tailored to MRI: (1) magnetic Gaussian primitives with physics-consistent volumetric rendering that respects MRI slice formation; (2) a neural residual field to refine high-frequency details; and (3) a multi-resolution progressive training schedule to stabilize and accelerate optimization.

Result: On the FeTA fetal brain dataset, M-Gaussian attains 40.31 dB PSNR and is 14× faster than prior approaches, marking the first successful application of 3D Gaussian Splatting to multi-stack MRI reconstruction.

Conclusion: M-Gaussian offers an improved quality–speed trade-off for reconstructing isotropic HR volumes from anisotropic multi-stack MRI, paving the way for more practical fetal and other motion-prone MRI workflows. Its physics-aware rendering and residual refinement are key to its performance, though broader validation will be needed.

Abstract: Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) is a crucial non-invasive imaging modality. In routine clinical practice, multi-stack thick-slice acquisitions are widely used to reduce scan time and motion sensitivity, particularly in challenging scenarios such as fetal brain imaging. However, the resulting severe through-plane anisotropy compromises volumetric analysis and downstream quantitative assessment, necessitating robust reconstruction of isotropic high-resolution volumes. Implicit neural representation methods, while achieving high quality, suffer from computational inefficiency due to complex network structures. We present M-Gaussian, adapting 3D Gaussian Splatting to MRI reconstruction. Our contributions include: (1) Magnetic Gaussian primitives with physics-consistent volumetric rendering, (2) neural residual field for high-frequency detail refinement, and (3) multi-resolution progressive training. Our method achieves an optimal balance between quality and speed. On the FeTA dataset, M-Gaussian achieves 40.31 dB PSNR while being 14 times faster, representing the first successful adaptation of 3D Gaussian Splatting to multi-stack MRI reconstruction.

</details>


### [21] [Leveraging GenAI for Segmenting and Labeling Centuries-old Technical Documents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00147)
*Carlos Monroy,Benjamin Navarro*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They prototype a domain-aware pipeline that segments and labels images from 16th–17th century shipbuilding treatises by combining SAM2 for segmentation, Florence2/ChatGPT for labeling, and a nautical ontology/glossary, showing promising early results for improved curation and retrieval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern image models excel on contemporary images with abundant training data, but historical document imagery lacks data and is highly domain-specific. Automating segmentation/recognition would aid curation, cataloging, and public/scholarly access to rare collections.

Method: Apply SAM2 to segment images; use Florence2 and ChatGPT to generate labels; constrain and enrich labels with the ontoShip ontology and glosShip glossary tailored to nautical architecture; target digitized images from Age of Exploration shipbuilding treatises.

Result: Preliminary experiments indicate that combining foundation models with domain knowledge can produce useful segmentations and labels that improve discoverability and management of the collection; the paper also reports encountered challenges and limitations.

Conclusion: A hybrid approach—foundation-model vision/LLM components guided by a specialized ontology—appears viable for historical document processing. Future work aims to mitigate data scarcity and domain-adaptation issues and to strengthen the pipeline’s robustness and evaluation.

Abstract: Image segmentation and image recognition are well established computational techniques in the broader discipline of image processing. Segmentation allows to locate areas in an image, while recognition identifies specific objects within an image. These techniques have shown remarkable accuracy with modern images, mainly because the amount of training data is vast. Achieving similar accuracy in digitized images of centuries-old documents is more challenging. This difficulty is due to two main reasons: first, the lack of sufficient training data, and second, because the degree of specialization in a given domain. Despite these limitations, the ability to segment and recognize objects in these collections is important for automating the curation, cataloging, and dissemination of knowledge, making the contents of priceless collections accessible to scholars and the general public. In this paper, we report on our ongoing work in segmenting and labeling images pertaining to shipbuilding treatises from the XVI and XVII centuries, a historical period known as the Age of Exploration. To this end, we leverage SAM2 for image segmentation; Florence2 and ChatGPT for labeling; and a specialized ontology ontoShip and glossary glosShip of nautical architecture for enhancing the labeling process. Preliminary results demonstrate the potential of marrying these technologies for improving curation and retrieval of priceless historical documents. We also discuss the challenges and limitations encountered in this approach and ideas on how to overcome them in the future.

</details>


### [22] [Mechanistically Guided LoRA Improves Paraphrase Consistency in Medical Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00148)
*Binesh Sadanandan,Vahid Behzadan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They reduce paraphrase-induced answer flips in a medical vision-language model (MedGemma-4B) by LoRA fine-tuning with a joint consistency+accuracy loss, cutting flip rates ~3× on MIMIC-CXR (14.6%→4.4%) and ~40% on PadChest (13.6%→7.8%) while keeping accuracy roughly stable; margins shrink markedly, and Gemma Scope 2 SAEs transfer near-perfectly (R²≈0.997). Early-layer adaptations help most.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical VQA systems can give contradictory yes/no answers to rephrasings of the same question, undermining trust and clinical usability. The authors aim to quantify this inconsistency, improve consistency without sacrificing accuracy, and test whether interpretable features from Gemma Scope 2 SAEs transfer to MedGemma to guide interventions.

Method: 1) Measure paraphrase consistency on PSF-Med using flip rate and mean margin difference for MedGemma-4B. 2) Validate transfer of Gemma Scope 2 SAEs to MedGemma by assessing reconstruction/activation mapping quality (R²≈0.997) on both medical and general prompts. 3) Fine-tune LoRA adapters with a combined objective balancing paraphrase-consistency and answer-accuracy to avoid mode collapse observed with pure consistency training. 4) Conduct layer-range ablations (early vs mechanistically selected middle layers). Evaluate on MIMIC-CXR and PadChest Balanced.

Result: - Baseline (MIMIC-CXR, n=158): flip rate 14.6%, mean margin diff 1.63 logits. After joint-loss LoRA: flip rate 4.4% (p=0.002, two-proportion z-test), margin 0.33 (79.5% reduction); accuracy 84.2%→82.3% (−1.9pp, not significant). - PadChest Balanced (n=250): flip 13.6%→7.8%, margin 1.08→0.35 (67.9% reduction), accuracy 66.4%→69.4% (+3.0pp). - SAEs transfer to MedGemma with R²≈0.997 on both medical and general text (n=100 each; p<0.001 for exceeding 0.95 threshold). - Early-layer edits reduce margins more than mechanistically selected middle layers.

Conclusion: Joint consistency+accuracy LoRA fine-tuning substantially improves paraphrase consistency in medical VQA with minimal accuracy trade-off and generalizes across datasets. Gemma Scope 2 SAEs appear highly transferable to MedGemma, suggesting shared features and enabling targeted interventions. Early layers are effective levers for reducing sensitivity to paraphrasing. Future work should test broader tasks, larger samples, and clinical settings, and analyze why early layers dominate.

Abstract: Medical Vision-Language Models can give different yes or no answers to rephrasings of the same clinical question. We study this in MedGemma-4B using PSF-Med Sadanandan and Behzadan (2025), which provides paraphrase pairs for systematic consistency evaluation on medical VQA. On MIMIC-CXR binary questions (n = 158), the baseline flip rate is 14.6% and mean margin difference is 1.63 logits. We validate that Gemma Scope 2 Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) transfer to MedGemma activations, achieving R2 ~= 0.997 on both medical and general text (n = 100 prompts each, p < 0.001 for exceeding a 0.95 threshold). We then fine-tune Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) adapters with a combined loss that balances paraphrase consistency with answer accuracy. This combined approach prevents mode collapse that occurs with pure consistency training while reducing flip rate from 14.6% to 4.4% (p = 0.002, two-proportion z-test) and margin difference from 1.63 to 0.33 (79.5% reduction). Accuracy remains stable at 84.2% baseline versus 82.3% after training (-1.9pp, not significant). On PadChest Balanced (n = 250), flip rate drops from 13.6% to 7.8%, mean margin difference drops from 1.08 to 0.35 (67.9% reduction), and accuracy increases from 66.4% to 69.4%. A layer-range ablation shows that early layers reduce margin differences more than mechanistically selected middle layers.

</details>


### [23] [Physics-Consistent Diffusion for Efficient Fluid Super-Resolution via Multiscale Residual Correction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00149)
*Zhihao Li,Shengwei Dong,Chuang Yi,Junxuan Gao,Zhilu Lai,Zhiqiang Liu,Wei Wang,Guangtao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ReMD is a physics-consistent diffusion framework for fluid super-resolution that couples data consistency with lightweight physics cues and multigrid residual corrections in a multi-wavelet hierarchy, yielding higher accuracy, better spectra, lower divergence, and similar quality with far fewer sampling steps than standard diffusion baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generic image SR and diffusion models perform poorly on fluid SR: they require many sampling steps, ignore physical constraints, and produce spectral errors and spurious divergence. A method is needed that is efficient, respects flow physics, and preserves multi-scale fluid structures.

Method: During each reverse diffusion step, ReMD computes an update direction that fuses data consistency with lightweight physics guidance, then applies a multigrid residual correction across scales. A multi-wavelet basis builds the coarse-to-fine hierarchy to capture both large-scale structures and fine vortical details. The approach is equation-free yet physics-consistent, accelerating convergence and preserving details.

Result: On atmospheric and oceanic benchmarks, ReMD improves reconstruction accuracy and spectral fidelity, reduces divergence artifacts, and achieves comparable quality with significantly fewer sampling steps than diffusion baselines.

Conclusion: Embedding physics consistency inside the diffusion process—via multigrid residual correction and multi-wavelet multiscale modeling—is an effective, efficient strategy for fluid super-resolution.

Abstract: Existing image SR and generic diffusion models transfer poorly to fluid SR: they are sampling-intensive, ignore physical constraints, and often yield spectral mismatch and spurious divergence. We address fluid super-resolution (SR) with \textbf{ReMD} (\underline{Re}sidual-\underline{M}ultigrid \underline{D}iffusion), a physics-consistent diffusion framework. At each reverse step, ReMD performs a \emph{multigrid residual correction}: the update direction is obtained by coupling data consistency with lightweight physics cues and then correcting the residual across scales; the multiscale hierarchy is instantiated with a \emph{multi-wavelet} basis to capture both large structures and fine vortical details. This coarse-to-fine design accelerates convergence and preserves fine structures while remaining equation-free. Across atmospheric and oceanic benchmarks, ReMD improves accuracy and spectral fidelity, reduces divergence, and reaches comparable quality with markedly fewer sampling steps than diffusion baselines. Our results show that enforcing physics consistency \emph{inside} the diffusion process via multigrid residual correction and multi-wavelet multiscale modeling is an effective route to efficient fluid SR. Our code are available on https://github.com/lizhihao2022/ReMD.

</details>


### [24] [Attention to Neural Plagiarism: Diffusion Models Can Plagiarize Your Copyrighted Images!](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00150)
*Zihang Zou,Boqing Gong,Liqiang Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Demonstrates a gradient-based attack that steers diffusion models to reproduce or ambiguate copyrighted images by starting from an inverted latent “anchor” and adding small cross-attention “shim” perturbations over timesteps, defeating both visible and invisible watermarks without extra training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Expose weaknesses in current copyright protection (particularly watermarking) against modern generative models, and highlight the risk of neural plagiarism to motivate stronger defenses and provenance tools.

Method: Use latent inversion to obtain an anchor latent for a target copyrighted image, then perform a purely gradient-based search for small perturbations (shims) applied to the model’s cross-attention at selected timesteps to control semantic drift. This produces either close replicas or subtly altered variants that evade watermark/copyright detection. No additional model training or fine-tuning is required.

Result: On MS-COCO and real-world copyrighted images, the approach yields outputs that reproduce protected content and bypass both visible trademarks/signatures and invisible watermarks, indicating that diffusion models can be induced to plagiarize despite protections.

Conclusion: Neural plagiarism with diffusion models is practical under the described conditions; current watermarking and copyright-detection mechanisms are vulnerable. Stronger, more robust provenance and defense mechanisms are urgently needed.

Abstract: In this paper, we highlight a critical threat posed by emerging neural models: data plagiarism. We demonstrate how modern neural models (e.g., diffusion models) can replicate copyrighted images, even when protected by advanced watermarking techniques. To expose vulnerabilities in copyright protection and facilitate future research, we propose a general approach to neural plagiarism that can either forge replicas of copyrighted data or introduce copyright ambiguity. Our method, based on "anchors and shims", employs inverse latents as anchors and finds shim perturbations that gradually deviate the anchor latents, thereby evading watermark or copyright detection. By applying perturbations to the cross-attention mechanism at different timesteps, our approach induces varying degrees of semantic modification in copyrighted images, enabling it to bypass protections ranging from visible trademarks and signatures to invisible watermarks. Notably, our method is a purely gradient-based search that requires no additional training or fine-tuning. Experiments on MS-COCO and real-world copyrighted images show that diffusion models can replicate copyrighted images, underscoring the urgent need for countermeasures against neural plagiarism.

</details>


### [25] [Dr. Seg: Revisiting GRPO Training for Visual Large Language Models through Perception-Oriented Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00152)
*Haoxiang Sun,Tao Wang,Chenwei Tang,Li Yuan,Jiancheng Lv*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Naively porting GRPO from text-only LLMs to VLLMs for perception tasks underperforms; the authors identify output-space breadth and reward granularity/stability as key gaps and introduce Dr. Seg—adding a Look-to-Confirm step and a Distribution-Ranked Reward—to significantly boost reasoning-based segmentation without changing model architecture.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recent attempts to apply GRPO (successful in language reasoning) to visual perception assume seamless transfer. The authors argue this is flawed because perception involves dense, structured outputs and requires fine-grained feedback. They aim to bridge this mismatch so VLLMs can better handle complex visual segmentation with RL-style training.
