Method: Dr. Seg is a plug-and-play, GRPO-based training framework for VLLMs featuring: (1) Look-to-Confirm to expand/verify the model’s output space during training/inference, and (2) Distribution-Ranked Reward to provide fine-grained, stable reward signals by ranking outputs over a distribution instead of using sparse/binary metrics. No architectural changes are needed; it integrates with existing GRPO pipelines.

Result: Across extensive experiments on reasoning-oriented segmentation and complex visual scenarios, Dr. Seg consistently improves performance and preserves strong generalization compared to baseline GRPO adaptations. Code and models will be released.

Conclusion: Language-reasoning RL paradigms do not directly transfer to perception. Addressing output-space breadth and reward stability is crucial. Dr. Seg offers a simple, architecture-agnostic fix that materially improves VLLM-based segmentation performance while maintaining generalization.

Abstract: Following the success of Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) in foundation LLMs, an increasing number of works have sought to adapt GRPO to Visual Large Language Models (VLLMs) for visual perception tasks (e.g., detection and segmentation). However, much of this line of research rests on a long-standing yet unexamined assumption: training paradigms developed for language reasoning can be transferred seamlessly to visual perception. Our experiments show that this assumption is not valid, revealing intrinsic differences between reasoning-oriented and perception-oriented settings. Using reasoning segmentation as a representative case, we surface two overlooked factors: (i) the need for a broader output space, and (ii) the importance of fine-grained, stable rewards. Building on these observations, we propose Dr.~Seg, a simple, plug-and-play GRPO-based framework consisting of a Look-to-Confirm mechanism and a Distribution-Ranked Reward module, requiring no architectural modifications and integrating seamlessly with existing GRPO-based VLLMs. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Dr.~Seg improves performance in complex visual scenarios while maintaining strong generalization. Code and models will be available at https://github.com/xVI-group-SCU/Dr-Seg.

</details>


### [26] [EfficientPosterGen: Semantic-aware Efficient Poster Generation via Token Compression and Accurate Violation Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00155)
*Wenxin Tang,Jingyu Xiao,Yanpei Gong,Fengyuan Ran,Tongchuan Xia,Junliang Liu,Man Ho Lam,Wenxuan Wang,Michael R. Lyu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EfficientPosterGen is an end-to-end system for automated academic poster generation that combines semantic key-content retrieval, visual-based context compression, and deterministic layout checks to cut token usage and improve layout reliability while preserving poster quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLM-based poster generators struggle when ingesting full papers: the input is low-signal/high-noise, token costs are high, and layout validation via additional MLLMs is unreliable. A scalable approach is needed to keep key information, reduce tokens, and guarantee layout correctness.

Method: Three modules: (1) Semantic-aware Key Information Retrieval (SKIR) builds a semantic contribution graph over paper segments to rank and retain salient content. (2) Visual-based Context Compression (VCC) renders selected text into images, shifting information to the visual channel to lower text tokens and produce poster-ready bullet points. (3) Agentless Layout Violation Detection (ALVD) uses a deterministic color-gradient algorithm to flag overflow and sparsity without auxiliary MLLMs. Integrated into an end-to-end poster generator.

Result: Experiments show large reductions in token consumption and more reliable layout verification, with poster quality maintained at a high level compared to prior MLLM-based methods. Code is released.

Conclusion: By jointly filtering content semantically, compressing context via visuals, and using deterministic layout checks, EfficientPosterGen provides a more token-efficient and reliable pipeline for automated poster generation, offering a scalable path beyond agent-heavy MLLM approaches.

Abstract: Automated academic poster generation aims to distill lengthy research papers into concise, visually coherent presentations. Existing Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) based approaches, however, suffer from three critical limitations: low information density in full-paper inputs, excessive token consumption, and unreliable layout verification. We present EfficientPosterGen, an end-to-end framework that addresses these challenges through semantic-aware retrieval and token-efficient multimodal generation. EfficientPosterGen introduces three core innovations: (1) Semantic-aware Key Information Retrieval (SKIR), which constructs a semantic contribution graph to model inter-segment relationships and selectively preserves important content; (2) Visual-based Context Compression (VCC), which renders selected text segments into images to shift textual information into the visual modality, significantly reducing token usage while generating poster-ready bullet points; and (3) Agentless Layout Violation Detection (ALVD), a deterministic color-gradient-based algorithm that reliably detects content overflow and spatial sparsity without auxiliary MLLMs. Extensive experiments demonstrate that EfficientPosterGen achieves substantial improvements in token efficiency and layout reliability while maintaining high poster quality, offering a scalable solution for automated academic poster generation. Our code is available at https://github.com/vinsontang1/EfficientPosterGen-Code.

</details>


### [27] [BiCLIP: Bidirectional and Consistent Language-Image Processing for Robust Medical Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00156)
*Saivan Talaei,Fatemeh Daneshfar,Abdulhady Abas Abdullah,Mustaqeem Khan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BiCLIP is a robust multimodal medical image segmentation framework that bidirectionally fuses vision and language while enforcing augmentation consistency, outperforming SOTA on QaTa-COV19 and MosMedData+, remaining strong with only 1% labels and under clinical degradations (blur, low-dose noise).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clinical “in-the-wild” settings suffer from scarce annotations and hardware/operation-induced artifacts, where existing image-only and even vision-language models can lose robustness. The paper aims to improve semantic alignment and resilience of medical segmentation under limited labels and degraded inputs.

Method: Introduce BiCLIP with (1) bidirectional multimodal fusion where visual features iteratively refine textual representations for tighter vision-language alignment, and (2) an augmentation consistency objective that regularizes intermediate representations across perturbed input views to stabilize learning and robustness.

Result: On QaTa-COV19 and MosMedData+, BiCLIP consistently surpasses image-only and multimodal baselines. It maintains high performance with just 1% labeled data and shows strong resistance to motion blur and low-dose CT noise.

Conclusion: Bidirectional vision-language refinement plus consistency regularization yields label-efficient and robust medical segmentation suitable for real-world clinical conditions, improving over prior SOTA and demonstrating resilience to artifacts.

Abstract: Medical image segmentation is a cornerstone of computer-assisted diagnosis and treatment planning. While recent multimodal vision-language models have shown promise in enhancing semantic understanding through textual descriptions, their resilience in "in-the-wild" clinical settings-characterized by scarce annotations and hardware-induced image degradations-remains under-explored.
  We introduce BiCLIP (Bidirectional and Consistent Language-Image Processing), a framework engineered to bolster robustness in medical segmentation. BiCLIP features a bidirectional multimodal fusion mechanism that enables visual features to iteratively refine textual representations, ensuring superior semantic alignment. To further stabilize learning, we implement an augmentation consistency objective that regularizes intermediate representations against perturbed input views.
  Evaluation on the QaTa-COV19 and MosMedData+ benchmarks demonstrates that BiCLIP consistently surpasses state-of-the-art image-only and multimodal baselines. Notably, BiCLIP maintains high performance when trained on as little as 1% of labeled data and exhibits significant resistance to clinical artifacts, including motion blur and low-dose CT noise.

</details>


### [28] [FujiView: Multimodal Late-Fusion for Predicting Scenic Visibility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00157)
*Bryceton Bible,Shah Md Nehal Hasnaeen,Hairong Qi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FujiView introduces a multimodal, late-fusion system and dataset to forecast visibility of Mount Fuji by combining webcam imagery with meteorological data, achieving strong accuracy across near- and next-day horizons.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scenic visibility strongly influences tourism planning and visitor satisfaction, but it is hard to predict due to rapidly changing atmospheric conditions and the lack of benchmarks/datasets tailored to this task.

Method: Build a Scenic Visibility Forecasting (SVF) framework using late fusion: extract image-based class probabilities from YOLO-driven vision features and combine them with structured numerical weather variables to classify visibility into five levels. Curate a growing dataset of 100k+ time-aligned webcam images from 40+ cameras around Mount Fuji with concurrent and forecasted weather data; evaluate across horizons (nowcasting, same-day, next-day, >+1 day).

Result: Vision features dominate short-term (nowcasting and same-day) predictions; weather features dominate at horizons beyond +1 day. Late fusion consistently performs best, reaching ~0.89 accuracy for same-day and up to 84% for next-day forecasts.

Conclusion: Late-fusion multimodal learning is effective for scenic visibility prediction, and the released FujiView dataset establishes SVF as a new benchmark task for multimodal research and environmental forecasting.

Abstract: Visibility of natural landmarks such as Mount Fuji is a defining factor in both tourism planning and visitor experience, yet it remains difficult to predict due to rapidly changing atmospheric conditions. We present FujiView, a multimodal learning framework and dataset for predicting scenic visibility by fusing webcam imagery with structured meteorological data. Our late-fusion approach combines image-derived class probabilities with numerical weather features to classify visibility into five categories. The dataset currently comprises over 100,000 webcam images paired with concurrent and forecasted weather conditions from more than 40 cameras around Mount Fuji, and continues to expand; it will be released to support further research in environmental forecasting. Experiments show that YOLO-based vision features dominate short-term horizons such as "nowcasting" and "samedaycasting", while weather-driven forecasts increasingly take over as the primary predictive signal beyond $+1$d. Late fusion consistently yields the highest overall accuracy, achieving ACC of approx 0.89 for same-day prediction and up to 84% for next-day forecasts. These results position Scenic Visibility Forecasting (SVF) as a new benchmark task for multimodal learning.

</details>


### [29] [FlowPortrait: Reinforcement Learning for Audio-Driven Portrait Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00159)
*Weiting Tan,Andy T. Liu,Ming Tu,Xinghua Qu,Philipp Koehn,Lu Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FlowPortrait is an RL post-training framework for audio-driven talking-head generation that uses MLLM-based, human-aligned rewards plus consistency regularizers to improve lip-sync, expressiveness, and motion quality, yielding higher-quality videos by automatic and human evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Talking-head synthesis still suffers from lip-sync errors, unnatural motion, and weak correlation between common metrics and human perception. A human-aligned evaluation and training signal is needed to close this gap.

Method: Build on a multimodal autoregressive audio-to-video generator and post-train it with reinforcement learning (GRPO). Construct a composite reward: MLLM-based assessments of lip-sync, expressiveness, and motion quality, combined with perceptual and temporal consistency regularizers to stabilize learning.

Result: Across automatic metrics and human preference studies, FlowPortrait outperforms baselines, producing more realistic, better-synchronized, and more expressive talking-head videos.

Conclusion: Human-aligned, MLLM-driven rewards coupled with RL (via GRPO) effectively improve portrait animation, suggesting RL is a promising path for aligning generative video models with perceptual quality.

Abstract: Generating realistic talking-head videos remains challenging due to persistent issues such as imperfect lip synchronization, unnatural motion, and evaluation metrics that correlate poorly with human perception. We propose FlowPortrait, a reinforcement-learning framework for audio-driven portrait animation built on a multimodal backbone for autoregressive audio-to-video generation. FlowPortrait introduces a human-aligned evaluation system based on Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to assess lip-sync accuracy, expressiveness, and motion quality. These signals are combined with perceptual and temporal consistency regularizers to form a stable composite reward, which is used to post-train the generator via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). Extensive experiments, including both automatic evaluations and human preference studies, demonstrate that FlowPortrait consistently produces higher-quality talking-head videos, highlighting the effectiveness of reinforcement learning for portrait animation.

</details>


### [30] [DINOv3 Meets YOLO26 for Weed Detection in Vegetable Crops](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00160)
*Boyang Deng,Yuzhen Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They build a foundational crop–weed detector by self-supervising a ViT (DINOv3, small) on a large, curated multi-season dataset and plugging it into YOLO26 (single or dual backbone with feature-alignment loss). It boosts mAP50 notably in- and cross-domain while remaining real-time (~28.5 fps), and they will release data/code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Precision weeding needs robust detectors that generalize across fields and seasons, but progress is limited by the lack of large, diverse, well-annotated weed–crop datasets. The authors aim to reduce annotation dependence and improve cross-domain generalization.

Method: - Aggregate 618,642 crop–weed images; curate them sequentially to 199,388 for training.
- Fine-tune a self-supervised DINOv3 ViT-small backbone using the curated set.
- Integrate the fine-tuned backbone into YOLO26 either as the sole backbone or within a dual-backbone design.
- Introduce a lightweight feature alignment loss to better fuse dual-backbone features.
- Evaluate across multiple seasonal domains; report accuracy, parameter count, latency, and fps.

Result: - In-domain (2025) mAP50: up to +5.4% over standard YOLO26-large.
- Cross-domain mAP50 gains: +14.0% (2021–2023) and +11.9% (2024).
- Model size/latency: +45.6% parameters, 2.9× inference latency, yet still ~28.5 fps (real-time).

Conclusion: Self-supervised ViT backbones, when carefully curated and fused (feature alignment) within YOLO-style detectors, substantially improve robustness and generalization for crop–weed detection without sacrificing real-time throughput. The forthcoming public dataset and code should facilitate further advances.

Abstract: Developing robust models for precision vegetable weeding is currently constrained by the scarcity of large-scale, annotated weed-crop datasets. To address this limitation, this study proposes a foundational crop-weed detection model by integrating heterogeneous datasets and leveraging self-supervised learning. A total of 618,642 crop-weed images were initially collected and subsequently refined to 199,388 filtered images for fine-tuning a DINOv3 vision transformer (ViT-small) through a sequential curation strategy. The fine-tuned DINOv3 backbone was then integrated into YOLO26, serving either as a primary backbone or part of a dual-backbone architecture. A feature alignment loss was introduced in the dual backbone framework to enhance feature fusion with minimal computational overhead. Experimental results show that the proposed DINOv3-finetuned ViT-small-based YOLO26-large achieved up to a +5.4% mAP50 gain on in-domain images collected in the 2025 season. Moreover, it demonstrated strong cross-domain generalization with mAP50 improvements of +14.0% on the 2021-2023 season dataset and +11.9% on the 2024 season dataset, compared to the standard YOLO26-large. Although the DINOv3-YOLO26-large model has 45.6% more parameters and a 2.9x increase in inference latency, it maintains real-time performance at ~28.5 frames per second (fps). The curated dataset and software programs developed in this study will be made publicly available.

</details>


### [31] [SKINOPATHY AI: Smartphone-Based Ophthalmic Screening and Longitudinal Tracking Using Lightweight Computer Vision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00161)
*S. Kalaycioglu,C. Hong,M. Zhu,H. Xie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Smartphone-only, privacy-preserving web app (SKINOPATHY AI) delivers five explainable ophthalmic screening modules using deterministic vision algorithms—demonstrating on-device, multi-signal eye triage without cloud AI and positioning the system as a foundation for future clinical tools.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Early eye screening in low-resource/remote settings is hampered by scarce specialists and equipment. Ubiquitous smartphones could enable accessible, explainable, non-diagnostic triage that preserves privacy and reduces reliance on expert hardware and connectivity.

Method: Implemented five complementary modules on commodity phones: (1) conjunctival redness via LAB a* normalization; (2) blink-rate from MediaPipe FaceMesh Eye Aspect Ratio with adaptive thresholds; (3) pupil light reflex via Pupil-to-Iris Ratio time-series; (4) scleral color indices (icterus/anemia proxies) via LAB/HSV stats; (5) iris-landmark-calibrated lesion encroachment with millimeter estimates and longitudinal tracking. Built as React/FastAPI with OpenCV/MediaPipe, MongoDB sessions, and PDF reporting. Algorithms are fully deterministic, run on-device, and are framed for consumer triage, not diagnosis.

Result: A working, explainable, multi-signal screening prototype runs entirely on unmodified smartphones, generating quantified metrics and reports without cloud inference. The paper describes architecture, algorithms, evaluation approach, and clinical/ethical context but does not claim clinical validation or comparative accuracy gains.

Conclusion: On-device, explainable ophthalmic screening is feasible with commodity smartphones. SKINOPATHY AI offers a privacy-preserving, non-diagnostic triage platform and groundwork for future, clinically validated mobile ophthalmoscopy solutions with defined ethical boundaries.

Abstract: Early ophthalmic screening in low-resource and remote settings is constrained by access to specialized equipment and trained practitioners. We present SKINOPATHY AI, a smartphone-first web application that delivers five complementary, explainable screening modules entirely through commodity mobile hardware: (1) redness quantification via LAB a* color-space normalization; (2) blink-rate estimation using MediaPipe FaceMesh Eye Aspect Ratio (EAR) with adaptive thresholding; (3) pupil light reflex characterization through Pupil-to-Iris Ratio (PIR) time-series analysis; (4) scleral color indexing foricterus and anemia proxies via LAB/HSV statistics; and (5) iris-landmark-calibrated lesion encroachment measurement with millimeter-scale estimates and longitudinal trend tracking. The system is implemented as a React/FastAPI stack with OpenCV and MediaPipe, MongoDB-backed session persistence, and PDF report generation. All algorithms are fully deterministic, privacy-preserving, and designed for non-diagnostic consumer triage. We detail system architecture, algorithm design, evaluation methodology, clinical context, and ethical boundaries of the platform. SKINOPATHY AI demonstrates that multi-signal ophthalmic screening is feasible on unmodified smartphones without cloud-based AI inference, providing a foundation for future clinically validated mobile ophthalmoscopy tools.

</details>


### [32] [A Boundary-Metric Evaluation Protocol for Whiteboard Stroke Segmentation Under Extreme Imbalance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00163)
*Nicholas Korcynski*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Severe class imbalance in whiteboard stroke segmentation hides thin-stroke failures under standard region metrics; an evaluation protocol with boundary-aware metrics and equity analysis shows overlap-based losses (Dice/Tversky) on DeepLabV3-MNV3 markedly outperform cross-entropy, while classical binarization has higher mean F1 but worse worst-case; higher training resolution further boosts F1.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Thin strokes occupy ~1–2% of pixels, so region metrics (F1/IoU) are dominated by background and can misrepresent performance. The authors want an evaluation that surfaces thin-stroke errors, boundary quality, and robustness/worst-case behavior to guide loss-function choice and model deployment.

Method: They propose a protocol combining region metrics with boundary metrics (BF1, B-IoU), a core vs thin-subset equity analysis, and per-image robustness stats (median, IQR, worst-case). Using seeded multi-run training with non-parametric significance tests, they train DeepLabV3-MobileNetV3 with five losses (cross-entropy, focal, Dice, Dice+focal, Tversky), evaluate on 12 held-out images split into core/thin subsets, compare against adaptive thresholding and Sauvola at native resolution, and test doubling training resolution.

Result: Overlap-based losses improve mean F1 by >20 points over cross-entropy (0.663 vs 0.438, p<0.001) and also improve boundary precision. Sauvola achieves higher mean F1 (0.787) than learned models but substantially worse worst-case F1 (0.452 vs 0.565 for Tversky), revealing a consistency–accuracy trade-off. Doubling training resolution adds +12.7 F1 points.

Conclusion: Evaluations must include boundary-aware metrics and subset equity to avoid masking thin-stroke failures. Overlap-based losses are preferable to cross-entropy for this task; learned models offer better worst-case reliability despite classical binarization’s higher mean F1. Higher training resolution materially improves performance, underscoring the importance of spatial detail.

Abstract: The binary segmentation of whiteboard strokes is hindered by extreme class imbalance, caused by stroke pixels that constitute only $1.79%$ of the image on average, and in addition, the thin-stroke subset averages $1.14% \pm 0.41%$ in the foreground. Standard region metrics (F1, IoU) can mask thin-stroke failures because the vast majority of the background dominates the score. In contrast, adding boundary-aware metrics and a thin-subset equity analysis changes how loss functions rank and exposes hidden trade-offs. We contribute an evaluation protocol that jointly examines region metrics, boundary metrics (BF1, B-IoU), a core/thin-subset equity analysis, and per-image robustness statistics (median, IQR, worst-case) under seeded, multi-run training with non-parametric significance testing. Five losses -- cross-entropy, focal, Dice, Dice+focal, and Tversky -- are trained three times each on a DeepLabV3-MobileNetV3 model and evaluated on 12 held-out images split into core and thin subsets. Overlap-based losses improve F1 by more than 20 points over cross-entropy ($0.663$ vs $0.438$, $p < 0.001$). In addition, the boundary metrics confirm that the gain extends to the precision of the contour. Adaptive thresholding and Sauvola binarization at native resolution achieve a higher mean F1 ($0.787$ for Sauvola) but with substantially worse worst-case performance (F1 $= 0.452$ vs $0.565$ for Tversky), exposing a consistency-accuracy trade-off: classical baselines lead on mean F1 while the learned model delivers higher worst-case reliability. Doubling training resolution further increases F1 by 12.7 points.

</details>


### [33] [ConFoThinking: Consolidated Focused Attention Driven Thinking for Visual Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00165)
*Zhaodong Wu,Haochen Xue,Qi Cao,Wenqi Mo,Yu Pei,Wenqi Xu,Jionglong Su,Yang Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ConFoThinking consolidates fragmented multi-layer attention into a single intermediate layer and uses concise “what-to-look” cues to extract and zoom into salient regions, yielding significant gains on fine-grained VQA for multimodal LLMs across five benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tool-augmented VQA relies on grounding that current MLLMs perform unreliably, while attention-driven ROI cropping suffers from dispersed attention across layers and noisy, question-conditioned attention. Empirically, models often look at the right place but produce wrong coordinates, attention is layer-fragmented, and extraction is sensitive to query phrasing.

Method: Introduce a Consolidated-Focused-Attention-Driven framework that (1) learns to aggregate cross-layer attention into a designated intermediate layer, (2) mines high-salience regions from that layer to crop/zoom for downstream reasoning, and (3) drives attention using concise semantic cues (what-to-look tokens) rather than full questions/redundant text to reduce semantic noise.

Result: Across five VQA benchmarks, the approach improves perception-oriented performance versus prior MLLM baselines and prior attention-based cropping methods (exact numbers not given in the abstract).

Conclusion: Consolidating where-to-look signals and conditioning on minimal semantic cues improves visual grounding and fine-grained perception for MLLMs, mitigating query sensitivity and enabling better VQA. Code, checkpoints, and data will be released upon acceptance.

Abstract: Thinking with Images improves fine-grained VQA for MLLMs by emphasizing visual cues. However, tool-augmented methods depend on the capacity of grounding, which remains unreliable for MLLMs. In parallel, attention-driven methods to crop the Region of Interest (ROIs) are proposed but they are constrained by (1) fragmented attention signals scattered across layers, leading to suboptimal localization and (2) relying on question- or redundant-text-conditioned attention extraction. Our analysis reveals three patterns: MLLMs may attend to the correct region yet generate incorrect coordinates, where-to-look attention is often fragmented across layers, and attention extraction is query-sensitive. Motivated by these, We propose ConFoThinking, a Consolidated-Focused-Attention-Driven Thinking framework that learns to aggregate attention into a designated intermediate layer, from which we mine and zoom in salient regions for downstream visual understanding. Moreover, we extract attention using concise semantic cues of what to look into, which mitigates the semantic noise introduced by question- or redundant-text-based attention extraction. Experiments across five VQA benchmarks demonstrate ConFoThinking significantly improves perception performance. The code, checkpoints, and dataset will be released after being accepted.

</details>


### [34] [Exploring the AI Obedience: Why is Generating a Pure Color Image Harder than CyberPunk?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00166)
*Hongyu Li,Kuan Liu,Yuan Chen,Juntao Hu,Huimin Lu,Guanjie Chen,Xue Liu,Guangming Lu,Hong Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Defines and benchmarks “Obedience” in generative AI—models often fail simple deterministic instructions despite producing complex imagery; introduces a hierarchy of obedience levels and a new visual benchmark (VIOLIN) that exposes systematic gaps in state-of-the-art models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative models show a Paradox of Simplicity: they can generate rich content but struggle with straightforward, rule-based tasks. The field lacks a unified notion and measurement of instruction faithfulness across difficulty levels.

Method: (1) Formalize Obedience as instruction alignment; (2) Propose a hierarchical grading scheme from coarse semantic alignment to pixel-level precision; (3) Conduct case studies showing where generative priors override logical rules; (4) Create VIOLIN, a Level-4 visual benchmark centered on pure color generation with six variants; (5) Evaluate multiple SOTA models on this benchmark.

Result: SOTA models exhibit fundamental obedience limitations on VIOLIN; analyses reveal recurrent failure modes where aesthetic/prior biases trump logical constraints, providing diagnostic insights into instruction-following gaps.

Conclusion: A principled framework and benchmark are introduced to structure research on instruction faithfulness in generative models. Findings highlight the need for methods that enforce logical and constraint satisfaction, encouraging future work to close obedience gaps.

Abstract: Recent advances in generative AI have demonstrated remarkable ability to produce high-quality content. However, these models often exhibit "Paradox of Simplicity": while they can render intricate landscapes, they often fail at simple, deterministic tasks. To address this, we formalize Obedience as the ability to align with instructions and establish a hierarchical grading system ranging from basic semantic alignment to pixel-level systemic precision, which provides a unified paradigm for incorporating and categorizing existing literature. Then, we conduct case studies to identify common obedience gaps, revealing how generative priors often override logical constraints. To evaluate high-level obedience, we present VIOLIN (VIsual Obedience Level-4 EvaluatIoN), the first benchmark focused on pure color generation across six variants. Extensive experiments on SOTA models reveal fundamental obedience limitations and further exploratory insights. By establishing this framework, we aim to draw more attention on AI Obedience and encourage deeper exploration to bridge this gap.

</details>


### [35] [Image-Based Classification of Olive Species Specific to Turkiye with Deep Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00168)
*Irfan Atabas,Hatice Karatas*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Transfer-learning CNNs (MobileNetV2, EfficientNetB0) classify five Turkish olive species from stereo-camera images; EfficientNetB0 performs best with 94.5% accuracy, indicating practical viability for automated identification and quality control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual identification of olive cultivars is time-consuming, subjective, and impractical at scale; agriculture needs fast, reliable, and automated varietal classification to support management, traceability, and quality control.

Method: Images of five local olive species were captured with a stereo camera and preprocessed. Two convolutional neural networks—MobileNetV2 and EfficientNetB0—were fine-tuned using transfer learning for image classification, and performance was evaluated on training/testing splits.

Result: EfficientNetB0 achieved the best performance with 94.5% accuracy; MobileNetV2 underperformed relative to EfficientNetB0 (exact figure not provided).

Conclusion: Deep learning, particularly EfficientNetB0 with transfer learning, is effective for high-accuracy classification of olive species and shows strong potential for deployment in automated identification and quality control of agricultural products.

Abstract: In this study, image processing and deep learning methodologies were employed to automatically classify local olive species cultivated in Turkiye. A stereo camera was utilized to capture images of five distinct olive species, which were then preprocessed to ensure their suitability for analysis. Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architectures, specifically MobileNetV2 and EfficientNetB0, were employed for image classification. These models were optimized through a transfer learning approach. The training and testing results indicated that the EfficientNetB0 model exhibited the optimal performance, with an accuracy of 94.5%. The findings demonstrate that deep learning-based systems offer an effective solution for classifying olive species with high accuracy. The developed method has significant potential for application in areas such as automatic identification and quality control of agricultural products.

</details>


### [36] [A Novel Evolutionary Method for Automated Skull-Face Overlay in Computer-Aided Craniofacial Superimposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00170)
*Práxedes Martínez-Moreno,Andrea Valsecchi,Pablo Mesejo,Pilar Navarro-Ramírez,Valentino Lugli,Sergio Damas*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Lilium is an automated, constraint-driven evolutionary approach to Skull-Face Overlay that models soft‑tissue variability with 3D cones and optimizes alignment via Differential Evolution, yielding higher accuracy and robustness than prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Skull-Face Overlay in craniofacial superimposition is hampered by person-specific soft‑tissue thickness variability, which introduces large uncertainty into aligning a 3D skull with a 2D facial photo. A more accurate and robust, preferably automated, method is needed to handle this uncertainty.

Method: Represent soft‑tissue variability around craniofacial landmarks using a 3D cone-based model. Optimize cone and alignment parameters with a Differential Evolution algorithm under multiple plausibility constraints that emulate forensic practice: landmark correspondence, consistent camera intrinsics/extrinsics, head pose alignment, ensuring the skull fits within facial boundaries, and enforcing region parallelism.

Result: When evaluated against the current state of the art, Lilium achieves better accuracy and robustness in SFO; although specific metrics are not provided in the abstract, comparisons indicate consistent improvements.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling soft‑tissue uncertainty and solving SFO with multi-constraint evolutionary optimization produces more reliable overlays. Lilium advances automated craniofacial superimposition by better handling anatomical and photographic plausibility, outperforming existing methods.

Abstract: Craniofacial Superimposition is a forensic technique for identifying skeletal remains by comparing a post-mortem skull with ante-mortem facial photographs. A critical step in this process is Skull-Face Overlay (SFO). This stage involves aligning a 3D skull model with a 2D facial image, typically guided by cranial and facial landmarks' correspondence. However, its accuracy is undermined by individual variability in soft-tissue thickness, introducing significant uncertainty into the overlay. This paper introduces Lilium, an automated evolutionary method to enhance the accuracy and robustness of SFO. Lilium explicitly models soft-tissue variability using a 3D cone-based representation whose parameters are optimized via a Differential Evolution algorithm. The method enforces anatomical, morphological, and photographic plausibility through a combination of constraints: landmark matching, camera parameter consistency, head pose alignment, skull containment within facial boundaries, and region parallelism. This emulation of the usual forensic practitioners' approach leads Lilium to outperform the state-of-the-art method in terms of both accuracy and robustness.

</details>


### [37] [AdaFocus: Knowing When and Where to Look for Adaptive Visual Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00171)
*Yuxiang Shen,Hailong Huang,Zhenkun Gao,Xueheng Li,Chengjun Xie,Xuanhua He,Jie Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AdaFocus is a training-free, two-stage cropping framework for MLLMs that adaptively decides when and where to zoom into images, reducing redundant views and aligning crops with user-intended semantics, yielding higher accuracy and ~4× faster inference than ZoomEyes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale training for image reasoning in MLLMs is expensive. Existing training-free zoom-in methods over-crop indiscriminately (adding noise/overhead) and misalign crops with the user’s semantic focus due to drift between intent and spatial attention. A method is needed that adaptively focuses without retraining.

Method: A training-free, two-stage pipeline: (1) confidence-based decision to determine whether cropping is needed (when to crop); (2) semantic-guided localization to select the most relevant region (where to crop). The framework integrates with MLLM inference to enable adaptive visual reasoning without additional training.

Result: Substantial accuracy improvements and approximately 4.0× inference speedup relative to the SOTA training-free baseline ZoomEyes. No additional training required.

Conclusion: AdaFocus mitigates perceptual redundancy and semantic–spatial drift, providing a practical, efficient upgrade path for MLLM visual reasoning. It advances both accuracy and efficiency in a training-free setting and may serve as a strong default for adaptive visual focus in MLLMs.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are shifting towards "Thinking with Images" by actively exploring image details. While effective, large-scale training is computationally expensive, which has spurred growing interest in lightweight, training-free solutions. However, existing training-free methods suffer from two flaws: perceptual redundancy from indiscriminate cropping, which adds overhead and noise; and a drift between semantic intent and spatial attention, which prevents accurate localization of user-focused regions. To address these challenges, we propose AdaFocus, a novel training-free framework designed for adaptive visual reasoning. AdaFocus follows a two-stage pipeline: a confidence-based module decides when to crop, and a semantic-guided localization module determines where to crop. This enables adaptive visual reasoning without additional training. Experimentally, AdaFocus delivers substantial performance gains while achieving approximately 4.0\times speedup inference speedup than the SOTA method ZoomEyes, representing a significant advance in both accuracy and efficiency.

</details>


### [38] [Summer-22B: A Systematic Approach to Dataset Engineering and Training at Scale for Video Foundation Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00173)
*Simo Ryu,Chunghwan Han*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Experience report on building Summer-22B, a from-scratch video foundation model trained on ~50M clips; success hinged on metadata-driven, multi-stage data curation (Lavender Data), μP-based hyperparameter transfer, and hypersphere-constrained optimization; architectural variants mattered less than data engineering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide practical, engineering-focused guidance for scaling a video foundation model from raw footage to a large, functional system, clarifying which design and training decisions actually moved the needle in practice.

Method: Combine metadata-driven dataset curation with multi-stage filtering; implement the Lavender Data management system; adopt inference-aware architectural choices; use μP parameterization for transferable hyperparameters across scales; apply hypersphere-constrained optimization; train from scratch on roughly 50 million video clips.

Result: Produced a functional Summer-22B video foundation model and found: (1) dataset engineering dominated effort and impact; (2) architectural variants had smaller-than-expected performance differences; (3) μP hyperparameter transfer worked even under geometric (hypersphere) constraints.

Conclusion: In large-scale video model training, prioritize dataset quality and tooling; expect diminishing returns from architecture tweaks relative to data work; μP is effective for scaling under constraints; the documented practices can inform similar large video modeling efforts.

Abstract: We describe our experience training Summer-22B, a video foundation model developed from scratch. This report documents the engineering challenges, design decisions, and lessons learned while scaling from raw footage collection to a functional model trained on approximately 50 million clips. We outline our approach combining metadata-driven dataset curation, multi-stage filtering, $μ$P parameterization, and hypersphere-constrained optimization. We developed the Lavender Data system for dataset management and adopted inference-aware architectural choices. We share observations on what worked in our setting: dataset engineering consumed the majority of effort, architectural variants showed smaller differences than we expected, and $μ$P hyperparameter transfer appeared effective even under geometric constraints. We hope this account proves useful to others undertaking similar projects.

</details>


### [39] [Infinite Self-Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00175)
*Giorgio Roffo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Reinterprets self-attention as a graph diffusion process and derives a linear-time variant (Linear-InfSA) that approximates the dominant eigenvector of the implicit attention operator, enabling scalable, interpretable, and efficient Vision Transformers with strong accuracy and robustness on high-resolution images.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Softmax attention has quadratic cost in sequence length, making high-resolution vision processing slow, memory-heavy, and energy-inefficient. The authors seek a theoretically grounded, interpretable reformulation that scales linearly with tokens while preserving or improving accuracy and robustness under distribution shift.

Method: 1) Infinite Self-Attention (InfSA): Treat each attention layer as a diffusion step on a content-adaptive token graph and accumulate multi-hop interactions via a discounted Neumann series over attention matrices. This connects attention to classical graph centrality (Katz, PageRank, eigenvector). The Neumann kernel equals the fundamental matrix of an absorbing Markov chain, making token weights interpretable as expected random-walk visits before absorption. 2) Linear-InfSA: A linear-time approximation that estimates the principal eigenvector of the implicit attention operator without forming the full attention matrix. It maintains a fixed-size auxiliary state proportional to per-head dimension dh (independent of sequence length N), is drop-in compatible with ViTs, and supports very large resolutions.

Result: - Scalability: Trains stably at 4096×4096 and infers at 9216×9216 (≈332k tokens) without OOM; only tested model to complete such inference. - Accuracy: In a 4-layer ViT (53.5M params, 59 GFLOPs at 224×224), Linear-InfSA achieves 84.7% top-1 on ImageNet-1K, a +3.2 point gain over equal-depth softmax ViT trained identically. - Robustness: On ImageNet-V2, InfViT variants reach up to 79.8% vs 76.8% for baselines. - Efficiency: On A100 40GB, Linear-InfViT runs at 231 images/s and 0.87 J/image, about 13× better throughput and energy than equal-depth ViT. - Fidelity: Linear approximation closely matches the quadratic operator’s dominant eigenvector (cosine 0.985).

Conclusion: InfSA offers an interpretable spectral/graph view of attention, while Linear-InfSA delivers practical linear-time attention that is drop-in for ViTs and scales to extreme resolutions with strong accuracy, better robustness, and large efficiency gains. The approach suggests eigenvector-centric token weighting as a viable, scalable alternative to softmax attention in high-resolution vision.

Abstract: The quadratic cost of softmax attention limits Transformer scalability in high-resolution vision. We introduce Infinite Self-Attention (InfSA), a spectral reformulation that treats each attention layer as a diffusion step on a content-adaptive token graph, accumulating multi-hop interactions through a discounted Neumann series over attention matrices. This links self-attention to classical graph centrality (Katz, PageRank, eigenvector centrality) for interpretable token weighting. We also show the Neumann kernel equals the fundamental matrix of an absorbing Markov chain, so a token's centrality is its expected number of random-walk visits before absorption. We then propose Linear-InfSA, a linear-time variant that approximates the principal eigenvector of the implicit attention operator without forming the full attention matrix. It keeps an auxiliary state of fixed size proportional to per-head dimension dh (independent of sequence length N), is drop-in compatible with Vision Transformers, and supports stable training at 4096 by 4096 and inference at 9216 by 9216 (about 332k tokens). In a 4-layer ViT (53.5M parameters, 59 GFLOPs at 224 by 224), Linear-InfSA reaches 84.7% top-1 on ImageNet-1K, a +3.2 point architectural gain over an equal-depth softmax ViT trained with the same recipe. On ImageNet-V2, InfViT variants outperform all compared baselines (up to 79.8% vs 76.8%), indicating robustness under distribution shift. On an A100 40GB GPU, Linear-InfViT runs at 231 images/s and 0.87 J/image (13x better throughput and energy than equal-depth ViT) and is the only tested model to complete 9216 by 9216 inference without out-of-memory. The linear approximation closely matches the dominant eigenvector of the quadratic operator (cosine 0.985).

</details>


### [40] [Zero-Shot and Supervised Bird Image Segmentation Using Foundation Models: A Dual-Pipeline Approach with Grounding DINO~1.5, YOLOv11, and SAM~2.1](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00184)
*Abhinav Munagala*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Dual-pipeline bird segmentation using a frozen SAM 2.1 with detector prompts: zero-shot via Grounding DINO 1.5 using the text prompt “bird,” and supervised via a lightly fine-tuned YOLOv11. Achieves state-of-the-art binary segmentation on CUB-200-2011 (IoU 0.912 supervised; 0.831 zero-shot) without retraining the segmenter, showing prompt-based foundation model pipelines can beat task-specific networks with minimal adaptation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bird segmentation is difficult due to large pose variation, intricate plumage, and diverse lighting. Existing end-to-end segmentation models require labeled data and re-training per domain/species. The authors seek a scalable, generalizable approach that minimizes annotation and training by leveraging recent foundation models and prompt-based workflows.

Method: Use SAM 2.1 as a frozen segmentation backbone prompted by bounding boxes. Two modes: (1) Zero-shot—Grounding DINO 1.5 detects birds from the text prompt “bird,” producing boxes for SAM; (2) Supervised—fine-tune YOLOv11 on CUB-200-2011 to detect birds; its boxes prompt SAM for masks. No segmentation fine-tuning; only the detector is optionally fine-tuned for domain adaptation. Evaluate on CUB with IoU/Dice/F1.

Result: On CUB-200-2011, the supervised pipeline reaches IoU 0.912, Dice 0.954, F1 0.953, surpassing prior baselines like SegFormer-B2 (IoU 0.842) by +7 points. The zero-shot pipeline reaches IoU 0.831 using just the text prompt, reportedly the first zero-shot text-prompt result on this benchmark. Code, scripts, and weights are released.

Conclusion: Prompt-based detection-to-segmentation pipelines built on strong foundation models can outperform specialized end-to-end segmentation networks while requiring only light detector fine-tuning (~1 hour) for new domains. Keeping the segmenter frozen enables quick adaptation without retraining and generalizes across species; resources are provided for reproducibility.

Abstract: Bird image segmentation remains a challenging task in computer vision due to extreme pose diversity, complex plumage patterns, and variable lighting conditions. This paper presents a dual-pipeline framework for binary bird image segmentation leveraging 2025 foundation models. We introduce two operating modes built upon Segment Anything Model 2.1 (SAM 2.1) as a shared frozen backbone: (1) a zero-shot pipeline using Grounding DINO 1.5 to detect birds via the text prompt "bird" before prompting SAM 2.1 with bounding boxes requiring no labelled bird data; and (2) a supervised pipeline that fine-tunes YOLOv11 on the CUB-200-2011 dataset for high-precision detection, again prompting SAM 2.1 for pixel-level masks. The segmentation model is never retrained for new species or domains. On CUB-200-2011 (11,788 images, 200 species), the supervised pipeline achieves IoU 0.912, Dice 0.954, and F1 0.953 outperforming all prior baselines including SegFormer-B2 (IoU 0.842) by +7.0 percentage points. The zero-shot pipeline achieves IoU 0.831 using only a text prompt, the first such result reported on this benchmark. We demonstrate that prompt-based foundation model pipelines outperform task specific end-to-end trained segmentation networks, while requiring only lightweight detector fine-tuning (~1 hour) for domain adaptation. Complete PyTorch implementation, dataset preparation scripts, and trained weights are publicly available.

</details>


### [41] [Efficient Long-Horizon GUI Agents via Training-Free KV Cache Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00188)
*Bowen Zhou,Zhou Xu,Wanli Li,Jingyu Xiao,Haoqian Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ST-Lite is a training-free KV-cache compression framework for GUI-focused VLM agents that keeps only 10–20% of cache while preserving or improving task performance and delivering ~2.45× faster decoding, by leveraging GUI-specific attention sparsity and spatio-trajectory cues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon GUI interactions with VLM agents incur prohibitive memory/latency from growing KV caches. Existing LLM/VLM cache compression underperforms because it assumes layer-varying sparsity, whereas GUI attention shows uniformly high sparsity across layers. A GUI-tailored approach that respects spatial UI structure and temporal redundancy is needed.

Method: Introduce ST-Lite, a training-free, scoring-based KV cache compression. It uses a dual-branch policy: (1) Component-centric Spatial Saliency (CSS) scores tokens by local neighborhood saliency to preserve the structural integrity of interactive UI elements; (2) Trajectory-aware Semantic Gating (TSG) dynamically filters historically redundant, visually repetitive KV pairs along the interaction trajectory. The scheme explicitly models dynamic spatio-trajectory dependencies and selects a compact subset of KVs under a small budget.

Result: Across extensive evaluations, with only a 10–20% KV-cache budget, ST-Lite achieves about 2.45× decoding acceleration while maintaining comparable or even superior performance relative to full-cache baselines.

Conclusion: By aligning with GUI-specific attention patterns and jointly leveraging spatial saliency and trajectory-aware redundancy filtering, ST-Lite offers a practical, training-free, and scalable solution that substantially reduces KV-cache memory/latency for resource-constrained GUI agents without sacrificing accuracy.

Abstract: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have emerged as powerful engines for autonomous GUI agents, yet their deployment is severely constrained by the substantial memory footprint and latency of the Key-Value (KV) cache during long-horizon interactions. While existing cache compression methods have proven effective for LLMs, we empirically demonstrate that they suffer from suboptimal performance in GUI scenarios due to a fundamental misalignment: unlike general visual tasks where attention sparsity varies across layers, GUI attention patterns exhibit uniform high-sparsity across all transformer layers. Motivated by this insight, we propose ST-Lite, a training-free KV cache compression framework tailored for efficient GUI agents that explicitly addresses the dynamic spatio-trajectory dependencies within GUI data streams. ST-Lite introduces a novel dual-branch scoring policy incorporating Component-centric Spatial Saliency (CSS) and Trajectory-aware Semantic Gating (TSG). Specifically, CSS preserves the structural integrity of interactive UI elements by evaluating local neighborhood saliency, while TSG mitigates historical redundancy by dynamically filtering visually repetitive KV pairs within the interaction trajectory. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that with only a 10-20% cache budget, ST-Lite achieves a 2.45x decoding acceleration while maintaining comparable or even superior performance compared to full-cache baselines, offering a scalable solution for resource-constrained GUI agents.

</details>


### [42] [SKeDA: A Generative Watermarking Framework for Text-to-video Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00194)
*Yang Yang,Xinze Zou,Zehua Ma,Han Fang,Weiming Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SKeDA introduces a robust, lossless generative watermark for text-to-video diffusion by using permutation-based keying and differential attention, making watermarks resilient to frame reordering/loss and temporal distortions while preserving video quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Text-to-video generation heightens authenticity, copyright, and misuse concerns. Existing generative image watermarking—via key-controlled initial noise—doesn’t transfer well to video because it requires strict frame-to-key alignment and is brittle to video-specific distortions (e.g., inter-frame compression).

Method: SKeDA targets text-to-video diffusion with two parts: (1) Shuffle-Key-based Distribution-preserving Sampling (SKe) encrypts with a single base pseudo-random binary sequence and per-frame permutations, converting extraction from sequence decoding to permutation-tolerant set aggregation, thus tolerating frame reordering and frame loss; (2) Differential Attention (DA) leverages inter-frame differences to adapt attention weights during extraction, improving robustness to temporal distortions and compression, while keeping lossless embedding via initial noise control.

Result: Extensive experiments show that SKeDA maintains high video generation fidelity and substantially improves watermark reliability under frame reordering, frame dropping, and video-specific distortions such as inter-frame compression.

Conclusion: Combining permutation-derived keys with difference-aware attention yields a synchronization-agnostic, temporally robust generative video watermark that effectively regulates AI-generated videos without degrading perceptual quality.

Abstract: The rise of text-to-video generation models has raised growing concerns over content authenticity, copyright protection, and malicious misuse. Watermarking serves as an effective mechanism for regulating such AI-generated content, where high fidelity and strong robustness are particularly critical. Recent generative image watermarking methods provide a promising foundation by leveraging watermark information and pseudo-random keys to control the initial sampling noise, enabling lossless embedding. However, directly extending these techniques to videos introduces two key limitations: Existing designs implicitly rely on strict alignment between video frames and frame-dependent pseudo-random binary sequences used for watermark encryption. Once this alignment is disrupted, subsequent watermark extraction becomes unreliable; and Video-specific distortions, such as inter-frame compression, significantly degrade watermark reliability. To address these issues, we propose SKeDA, a generative watermarking framework tailored for text-to-video diffusion models. SKeDA consists of two components: (1) Shuffle-Key-based Distribution-preserving Sampling (SKe) employs a single base pseudo-random binary sequence for watermark encryption and derives frame-level encryption sequences through permutation. This design transforms watermark extraction from synchronization-sensitive sequence decoding into permutation-tolerant set-level aggregation, substantially improving robustness against frame reordering and loss; and (2) Differential Attention (DA), which computes inter-frame differences and dynamically adjusts attention weights during extraction, enhancing robustness against temporal distortions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SKeDA preserves high video generation quality and watermark robustness.

</details>


### [43] [A Case Study on Concept Induction for Neuron-Level Interpretability in CNN](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00197)
*Moumita Sen Sarma,Samatha Ereshi Akkamahadevi,Pascal Hitzler*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They apply a previously proposed concept-induction framework for interpreting hidden neurons to the SUN2012 scene dataset and show it generalizes, yielding interpretable neuron labels validated with images and statistics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hidden neuron semantics in DNNs remain opaque. A prior framework worked on ADE20K; the open question is whether it transfers to other large-scale scene datasets, indicating broader utility.

Method: Reuse the same concept-induction workflow on SUN2012: induce candidate concepts for neurons, assign interpretable semantic labels, and validate via web-sourced example images and statistical tests.

Result: Interpretable labels were successfully assigned to neurons on SUN2012; validation via external images and statistical testing supports the correctness, indicating transferability of the method.

Conclusion: The concept-induction approach generalizes from ADE20K to SUN2012, suggesting broader applicability for interpreting DNN neurons across datasets and domains.

Abstract: Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) have advanced applications in domains such as healthcare, autonomous systems, and scene understanding, yet the internal semantics of their hidden neurons remain poorly understood. Prior work introduced a Concept Induction-based framework for hidden neuron analysis and demonstrated its effectiveness on the ADE20K dataset. In this case study, we investigate whether the approach generalizes by applying it to the SUN2012 dataset, a large-scale scene recognition benchmark. Using the same workflow, we assign interpretable semantic labels to neurons and validate them through web-sourced images and statistical testing. Our findings confirm that the method transfers to SUN2012, showing its broader applicability.

</details>


### [44] [Stateful Token Reduction for Long-Video Hybrid VLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00198)
*Jindong Jiang,Amala Sanjay Deshmukh,Kateryna Chumachenko,Karan Sapra,Zhiding Yu,Guilin Liu,Andrew Tao,Pavlo Molchanov,Jan Kautz,Wonmin Byeon*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They analyze query-conditioned token reduction for hybrid video VLMs (attention + Mamba) and propose a progressive, language-aware pruning scheme with a unified scoring rule (incl. an implicit-attention proxy for Mamba). Prune lightly early and more later across all layers. At 25% tokens, it yields 3.8–4.2x prefilling speedups with near-baseline accuracy; slight finetuning recovers/improves long-context performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing token-reduction methods target dense Transformers and overlook hybrids that interleave attention with linear-time state-space blocks (e.g., Mamba). Moreover, token-importance is sparse per layer but unstable across depth, making aggressive early pruning unsafe. The goal is to accelerate long-video VLMs under these hybrid constraints without sacrificing accuracy.

Method: 1) Empirically study two properties: layerwise sparsity (few tokens carry query-relevant info per layer) and importance stability (rankings change across layers). 2) Design a low-to-high progressive reduction schedule that increases pruning later in the network. 3) Introduce a unified, language-aware token-importance scoring applicable to both attention and Mamba blocks by using an implicit-attention proxy for Mamba, enabling all-layer reduction in hybrids. 4) Apply light finetuning under the reduced regime.

Result: With only 25% of visual tokens retained, the method achieves 3.8–4.2x prefilling speedups while maintaining near-baseline test accuracy; modest finetuning further improves results on long-context video benchmarks.

Conclusion: Early aggressive pruning is unreliable in hybrid VLMs because important tokens shift across layers. A progressive, unified, language-aware reduction strategy enables substantial speedups with minimal accuracy loss, and finetuning under reduction boosts performance on long-context video tasks.

Abstract: Token reduction is an effective way to accelerate long-video vision-language models (VLMs), but most existing methods are designed for dense Transformers and do not directly account for hybrid architectures that interleave attention with linear-time state-space blocks (e.g., Mamba). We study query-conditioned token reduction for hybrid video VLMs and analyze reduction behavior through two properties: layerwise sparsity (how many tokens capture query-relevant information) and importance stability (whether token-importance rankings persist across depth). Although token importance is sparse within each layer, the set of important tokens changes across layers, so aggressive early pruning is unreliable. Motivated by this, we propose a low-to-high progressive reduction schedule and a unified language-aware scoring mechanism for both attention and Mamba blocks (using an implicit-attention proxy for Mamba), enabling all-layer token reduction in hybrids. Under an aggressive compression setting (retaining 25% of visual tokens), our approach delivers substantial prefilling speedups (3.8--4.2x) with near-baseline accuracy at test time, and light finetuning under reduction further improves performance on long-context video benchmarks.

</details>


### [45] [AdURA-Net: Adaptive Uncertainty and Region-Aware Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00201)
*Antik Aich Roy,Ujjwal Bhattacharya*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AdURA-Net is an uncertainty-aware thoracic disease classifier that augments a DenseNet backbone with adaptive dilated convolutions and multiscale deformable alignment, and trains with a dual-head loss (masked BCE on logits + Dirichlet evidential objective) to produce reliable predictions that can explicitly express uncertainty on multi-label chest X-ray datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clinical radiology labels often include an 'uncertain' state due to report ambiguity and extraction limits (e.g., CheXpert, MIMIC-CXR). For high-risk decisions, forcing overconfident predictions is unsafe; models should recognize and communicate when evidence is insufficient. The work aims to handle uncertain labels and produce calibrated, abstention-capable outputs.

Method: Architecture: DenseNet backbone enhanced by geometry-driven modules—adaptive dilated convolutions and multiscale deformable alignment—to better capture anatomical variability. Learning: a Dual Head Loss that combines masked binary cross-entropy on logits (to deal with partial/uncertain supervision) with a Dirichlet evidential learning objective that models class-wise evidence and uncertainty, encouraging confident predictions only when supported by data.

Result: The abstract does not report quantitative results; it claims a framework for reliable thoracic disease classification that can indicate uncertainty. The implied outcomes are improved handling of uncertain labels and more calibrated predictions, but specific metrics are not provided.

Conclusion: By integrating geometry-aware feature extraction with evidential uncertainty modeling, AdURA-Net targets safer, more reliable chest X-ray classification in multi-label settings, enabling the model to withhold confidence when evidence is lacking—an essential property for clinical decision support.

Abstract: One of the common issues in clinical decision-making is the presence of uncertainty, which often arises due to ambiguity in radiology reports, which often reflect genuine diagnostic uncertainty or limitations of automated label extraction in various complex cases. Especially the case of multilabel datasets such as CheXpert, MIMIC-CXR, etc., which contain labels such as positive, negative, and uncertain. In clinical decision-making, the uncertain label plays a tricky role as the model should not be forced to provide a confident prediction in the absence of sufficient evidence. The ability of the model to say it does not understand whenever it is not confident is crucial, especially in the cases of clinical decision-making involving high risks. Here, we propose AdURA-Net, a geometry-driven adaptive uncertainty-aware framework for reliable thoracic disease classification. The key highlights of the proposed model are: a) Adaptive dilated convolution and multiscale deformable alignment coupled with the backbone Densenet architecture capturing the anatomical complexities of the medical images, and b) Dual Head Loss, which combines masked binary cross entropy with logit and a Dirichlet evidential learning objective.

</details>


### [46] [TACIT Benchmark: A Programmatic Visual Reasoning Benchmark for Generative and Discriminative Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00206)
*Daniel Nobrega Medeiros*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TACIT is a fully programmatic, deterministic visual reasoning benchmark spanning six domains with both generative and discriminative tracks; it uses structurally controlled near-miss distractors and automatic verification, and releases 6k puzzles (108k images) with code/eval under Apache 2.0.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current visual reasoning benchmarks often depend on natural-language prompts, cover limited reasoning skills, and rely on subjective scoring (e.g., LLM-as-judge), making evaluation noisy, narrow, and gameable. A need exists for objective, structure-sensitive, and reproducible assessments of visual reasoning.

Method: Design 10 tasks across six reasoning domains (spatial navigation, abstract pattern completion, causal simulation, logical constraint satisfaction, graph theory, topology). Provide dual-track evaluation: (1) generative—models output solution images verified by deterministic computer-vision pipelines; (2) discriminative—five-way multiple choice with near-miss distractors where each option violates exactly one structural constraint. Use seeded deterministic generation and reproducible verification; release dataset, generation code, and evaluation harness.

Result: Version 0.1.0 publishes 6,000 puzzles yielding 108,000 PNGs at three resolutions, with fully deterministic generation and verification. All assets are released on HuggingFace (DOI: 10.57967/hf/7904) under Apache 2.0. No model baselines are reported in the abstract.

Conclusion: TACIT provides an objective, reproducible, and structure-sensitive benchmark for visual reasoning, aiming to reduce shortcut exploitation and subjective evaluation. It establishes a foundation for standardized comparisons and future progress in multi-domain visual reasoning.

Abstract: Existing visual reasoning benchmarks predominantly rely on natural language prompts, evaluate narrow reasoning modalities, or depend on subjective scoring procedures such as LLM-as-judge. We introduce the TACIT Benchmark, a programmatic visual reasoning benchmark comprising 10 tasks across 6 reasoning domains: spatial navigation, abstract pattern completion, causal simulation, logical constraint satisfaction, graph theory, and topology. The benchmark provides dual-track evaluation: a generative track in which models must produce solution images verified through deterministic computer-vision pipelines, and a discriminative track offering five-way multiple choice with structurally plausible near-miss distractors. Each distractor violates exactly one structural constraint, requiring models to reason about fine-grained visual differences rather than exploit superficial cues. Version 0.1.0 distributes 6,000 puzzles (108,000 PNG images across three resolutions) with fully deterministic seeded generation and reproducible verification. The dataset, generation code, and evaluation harness are released under the Apache 2.0 license on HuggingFace (DOI: 10.57967/hf/7904).

</details>


### [47] [VisRef: Visual Refocusing while Thinking Improves Test-Time Scaling in Multi-Modal Large Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00207)
*Soumya Suvra Ghosal,Youngeun Kim,Zhuowei Li,Ritwick Chaudhry,Linghan Xu,Hongjing Zhang,Jakub Zablocki,Yifan Xing,Qin Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VisRef is a test-time framework that keeps multimodal reasoning grounded by periodically re-injecting a small, diverse, and semantically relevant set of visual tokens during inference, yielding up to 6.4% gains over existing test-time scaling without extra RL fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extended textual reasoning in multimodal LLMs often drifts from the image, over-relying on language priors as attention to visual tokens fades. Prior fixes rely on RL-based routing/refocusing, which is costly due to data generation and policy optimization.

Method: At inference, VisRef selects a coreset of image tokens that are both semantically aligned with the current reasoning context and diverse/global in coverage, then re-injects them into the context to refocus the model on visual evidence. It is a test-time-only, model-agnostic procedure that avoids RL fine-tuning while preserving compute budgets.

Result: Across three visual reasoning benchmarks with state-of-the-art multimodal reasoning models, VisRef consistently outperforms existing test-time scaling approaches under equal compute, with gains up to 6.4%.

Conclusion: Actively guiding reasoning via targeted re-injection of representative visual tokens mitigates visual grounding loss during long chains of thought, providing a compute-efficient alternative to RL-based methods and improving accuracy on vision-dependent tasks.

Abstract: Advances in large reasoning models have shown strong performance on complex reasoning tasks by scaling test-time compute through extended reasoning. However, recent studies observe that in vision-dependent tasks, extended textual reasoning at inference time can degrade performance as models progressively lose attention to visual tokens and increasingly rely on textual priors alone. To address this, prior works use reinforcement learning (RL)-based fine-tuning to route visual tokens or employ refocusing mechanisms during reasoning. While effective, these methods are computationally expensive, requiring large-scale data generation and policy optimization. To leverage the benefits of test-time compute without additional RL fine-tuning, we propose VisRef, a visually grounded test-time scaling framework. Our key idea is to actively guide the reasoning process by re-injecting a coreset of visual tokens that are semantically relevant to the reasoning context while remaining diverse and globally representative of the image, enabling more grounded multi-modal reasoning. Experiments on three visual reasoning benchmarks with state-of-the-art multi-modal large reasoning models demonstrate that, under fixed test-time compute budgets, VisRef consistently outperforms existing test-time scaling approaches by up to 6.4%.

</details>


### [48] [Physical Evaluation of Naturalistic Adversarial Patches for Camera-Based Traffic-Sign Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00217)
*Brianna D'Urso,Tahmid Hasan Sakib,Syed Rafay Hasan,Terry N. Guo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They build an in-domain composite traffic-sign dataset (CompGTSRB), train YOLOv5, and show naturalistic adversarial patches crafted via GAN latent-space optimization physically reduce STOP-sign detection confidence on a Quanser QCar across distances, sizes, and placements; they also propose practical protocols for credible patch testing and call for defenses against localized patch attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate whether Naturalistic Adversarial Patches transfer to a realistic physical AV setting when the detector is trained on an in-domain dataset, and provide standardized data/protocols for credible physical evaluation—addressing gaps in out-of-domain training, unrealistic backgrounds, and ad‑hoc test procedures—while probing robustness and motivating defenses.

Method: Construct CompGTSRB by pasting GTSRB sign instances onto undistorted backgrounds captured from the target platform; train a YOLOv5 detector on this dataset; generate NAPs using a GAN with latent-space optimization per prior NAP methods; deploy patches in physical tests on a Quanser QCar using its front CSI camera; vary distance, patch size, and placement; measure effects on the detector’s STOP class confidence.

Result: Across tested configurations, NAPs reliably reduce the YOLOv5 detector’s STOP class confidence in physical experiments. The study documents step‑by‑step procedures and shows that the CompGTSRB dataset plus systematic protocols yield credible, reproducible evaluations.

Conclusion: NAPs remain effective in a realistic AV pipeline even with in-domain training, validating transfer to the physical world. The CompGTSRB dataset and detailed protocols are useful resources for evaluation. The findings motivate research on defenses targeting localized patch corruption in embedded perception systems.

Abstract: This paper studies how well Naturalistic Adversarial Patches (NAPs) transfer to a physical traffic sign setting when the detector is trained on a customized dataset for an autonomous vehicle (AV) environment. We construct a composite dataset, CompGTSRB (which is customized dataset for AV environment), by pasting traffic sign instances from the German Traffic Sign Recognition Benchmark (GTSRB) onto undistorted backgrounds captured from the target platform. CompGTSRB is used to train a YOLOv5 model and generate patches using a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) with latent space optimization, following existing NAP methods. We carried out a series of experiments on our Quanser QCar testbed utilizing the front CSI camera provided in QCar. Across configurations, NAPs reduce the detector's STOP class confidence. Different configurations include distance, patch sizes, and patch placement. These results along with a detailed step-by-step methodology indicate the utility of CompGTSRB dataset and the proposed systematic physical protocols for credible patch evaluation. The research further motivate researching the defenses that address localized patch corruption in embedded perception pipelines.

</details>


### [49] [Pretty Good Measurement for Radiomics: A Quantum-Inspired Multi-Class Classifier for Lung Cancer Subtyping and Prostate Cancer Risk Stratification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00223)
*Giuseppe Sergioli,Carlo Cuccu,Giovanni Pasini,Alessandro Stefano,Giorgio Russo,Andrés Camilo Granda Arango,Roberto Giuntini*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They propose a quantum-inspired, genuinely multi-class classifier built from the Pretty Good Measurement (PGM). Each class is encoded as a density operator and a single POVM is used for prediction. On radiomics tasks (NSCLC subtyping and PCa risk), it is competitive with, and sometimes better than, classical baselines; performance degrades gracefully as class overlap increases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical multi-class schemes often reduce to one-vs-rest/pairwise decompositions and may not exploit a unified decision rule. The authors aim to leverage quantum state discrimination—specifically the PGM—to obtain a principled, single-shot multi-class classifier and to test whether this yields practical benefits in challenging biomedical radiomics problems.

Method: Map data from each class to a mixed quantum state (density matrix) via an encoding; construct the PGM (square-root measurement) for the ensemble of class-dependent states to form a single POVM; classify by the outcome associated with each class. Evaluate on NSCLC (binary/3-class/4-class) and PCa risk stratification using standard radiomics protocols and feature-selection scenarios, enabling direct comparison with prior baselines.

Result: The PGM-based classifier is consistently competitive and often improves upon classical baselines. It performs particularly well on NSCLC binary and three-class tasks, remains competitive in the more challenging four-class task with higher class overlap, and in PCa matches or is close to the strongest ensemble baseline with favorable sensitivity–specificity trade-offs.

Conclusion: A PGM-based, quantum-inspired classifier is a viable, principled multi-class alternative to reduction-based methods. Its effectiveness depends on the encoding and class-overlap geometry, yet it can match or surpass established baselines in radiomics tasks and offers clinically relevant operating points.

Abstract: We investigate a quantum-inspired approach to supervised multi-class classification based on the \emph{Pretty Good Measurement} (PGM), viewed as an operator-valued decision rule derived from quantum state discrimination. The method associates each class with an encoded mixed state and performs classification through a single POVM construction, thus providing a genuinely multi-class strategy without reduction to pairwise or one-vs-rest schemes. In this perspective, classification is reformulated as the discrimination of a finite ensemble of class-dependent density operators, with performance governed by the geometry induced by the encoding map and by the overlap structure among classes. To assess the practical scope of this framework, we apply the PGM-based classifier to two biomedical radiomics case studies: histopathological subtyping of non-small-cell lung carcinoma (NSCLC) and prostate cancer (PCa) risk stratification. The evaluation is conducted under protocols aligned with previously reported radiomics studies, enabling direct comparison with established classical baselines. The results show that the PGM-based classifier is consistently competitive and, in several settings, improves upon standard methods. In particular, the method performs especially well in the NSCLC binary and three-class tasks, while remaining competitive in the four-class case, where increased class overlap yields a more demanding discrimination geometry. In the PCa study, the PGM classifier remains close to the strongest ensemble baseline and exhibits clinically relevant sensitivity--specificity trade-offs across feature-selection scenarios.

</details>


### [50] [Adversarial Patch Generation for Visual-Infrared Dense Prediction Tasks via Joint Position-Color Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00266)
*He Li,Wenyue He,Weihang Kong,Xingchen Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AP-PCO is a black-box adversarial patch framework that jointly optimizes patch position and color to attack visual–infrared (VI) dense prediction models, using crossmodal color adaptation to remain stealthy across spectra while achieving strong, architecture-agnostic attack performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
