Motivation: Adversarial patches designed for single-modal inputs fail in VI settings because visible and infrared modalities have heterogeneous spectral properties and intensity distributions, causing poor cross-spectral effectiveness and conspicuous artifacts. There is a need for an attack that is effective, stealthy, and black-box for VI dense prediction tasks.

Method: Introduce AP-PCO, which simultaneously optimizes patch placement and color composition via a fitness function computed from model outputs (no internal gradients needed). A crossmodal color adaptation constrains the patch’s appearance to align with infrared grayscale characteristics while maintaining strong visible-domain perturbations, enabling one patch to perturb both modalities and reduce cross-spectral saliency.

Result: Across multiple architectures and VI dense prediction tasks, AP-PCO consistently degrades performance, demonstrating strong, transferable black-box attack effectiveness and establishing a practical benchmark for evaluating robustness of VI perception systems.

Conclusion: Joint position–color optimization plus crossmodal color adaptation yields a stealthy, effective, and flexible black-box adversarial patch method for VI dense prediction, helping bridge spectral discrepancies and providing a useful benchmark for robustness assessment in VI systems.

Abstract: Multimodal adversarial attacks for dense prediction remain largely underexplored. In particular, visual-infrared (VI) perception systems introduce unique challenges due to heterogeneous spectral characteristics and modality-specific intensity distributions. Existing adversarial patch methods are primarily designed for single-modal inputs and fail to account for crossspectral inconsistencies, leading to reduced attack effectiveness and poor stealthiness when applied to VI dense prediction models. To address these challenges, we propose a joint position-color optimization framework (AP-PCO) for generating adversarial patches in visual-infrared settings. The proposed method optimizes patch placement and color composition simultaneously using a fitness function derived from model outputs, enabling a single patch to perturb both visible and infrared modalities. To further bridge spectral discrepancies, we introduce a crossmodal color adaptation strategy that constrains patch appearance according to infrared grayscale characteristics while maintaining strong perturbations in the visible domain, thereby reducing cross-spectral saliency. The optimization procedure operates without requiring internal model information, supporting flexible black-box attacks. Extensive experiments on visual-infrared dense prediction tasks demonstrate that the proposed AP-PCO achieves consistently strong attack performance across multiple architectures, providing a practical benchmark for robustness evaluation in VI perception systems.

</details>


### [51] [Ozone Cues Mitigate Reflected Downwelling Radiance in LWIR Absorption-Based Ranging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00273)
*Unay Dorken Gallastegi,Wentao Shangguan,Vaibhav Choudhary,Akshay Agarwal,Hoover Rueda-Chacón,Martin J. Stevens,Vivek K Goyal*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Two passive LWIR ranging methods exploit ozone absorption features to estimate and remove reflected downwelling radiance, enabling accurate distance estimates from thermal emission. A four-band “quadspectral” closed-form approach and a broader “hyperspectral” fit markedly reduce ranging error (to ~6.8 m and ~1.2 m, respectively).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Passive LWIR absorption-based ranging has been hampered by unmodeled reflected downwelling radiance, which violates the common assumption that reflected radiance is negligible and leads to large distance errors, especially in low thermal-contrast scenes. A robust way to quantify and correct this reflected component is needed.

Method: Leverage characteristic spectral signatures—particularly ozone absorption—to estimate the reflected downwelling contribution. 1) Quadspectral: take four narrowband measurements (two near a water vapor line, two near an ozone line) and compute a closed-form range estimate that corrects for downwelling. 2) Hyperspectral: fit radiance over a wider LWIR band to jointly estimate range, object temperature, emissivity profiles, and downwelling from multiple zenith angles.

Result: Experiments show substantial error reductions when modeling reflected downwelling via ozone features: a case with >100 m error (when ignoring reflection) improves to 6.8 m using the quadspectral method and to 1.2 m using the hyperspectral method. The hyperspectral approach also retrieves temperature, emissivity, and angular downwelling contributions.

Conclusion: Modeling reflected downwelling radiance using ozone absorption enables accurate passive LWIR ranging under conditions that previously caused large errors. The quadspectral method offers simplicity with good accuracy, while the hyperspectral method provides best-in-class accuracy and richer scene/atmospheric parameter estimates, broadening the practicality of passive LWIR ranging.

Abstract: Passive long-wave infrared (LWIR) absorption-based ranging relies on atmospheric absorption to estimate distances to objects from their emitted thermal radiation. First demonstrated decades ago for objects much hotter than the air and recently extended to scenes with low temperature variations, this ranging has depended on reflected radiance being negligible. Downwelling radiance is especially problematic, sometimes causing large inaccuracies. In two new ranging methods, we use characteristic features from ozone absorption to estimate the contribution of reflected downwelling radiance. The quadspectral method gives a simple closed-form range estimate from four narrowband measurements, two at a water vapor absorption line and two at an ozone absorption line. The hyperspectral method uses a broader spectral range to improve accuracy while also providing estimates of temperature, emissivity profiles, and contributions of downwelling from a collection of zenith angles. Experimental results demonstrate improved ranging accuracy, in one case reducing error from over 100 m when reflected light is not modeled to 6.8 m with the quadspectral method and 1.2 m with the hyperspectral method.

</details>


### [52] [Seeking Necessary and Sufficient Information from Multimodal Medical Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00289)
*Boyu Chen,Weiye Bao,Junjie Liu,Michael Shen,Bo Peng,Paul Taylor,Zhu Li,Mengyue Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They extend causal Probability of Necessity and Sufficiency (PNS) to multimodal medical learning by decomposing features into modality-invariant and modality-specific parts and optimizing tractable PNS objectives, yielding better accuracy and robustness—especially when modalities are missing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal models often fail to learn features that are both necessary and sufficient for outcomes, limiting predictive power and robustness to missing modalities. Capturing such causal features can improve performance and ensure each modality carries adequate signal on its own.

Method: Decompose multimodal representations into (1) modality-invariant and (2) modality-specific components, then derive and optimize tractable PNS-based learning objectives separately for these components to approximate necessity and sufficiency in a multimodal setting where standard PNS assumptions are violated.

Result: On synthetic and real-world medical datasets, the approach outperforms baselines and is more robust to missing modalities, indicating successful capture of necessary-and-sufficient predictive signals. Code to be released.

Conclusion: By re-framing multimodal learning through PNS with a decomposition into invariant/specific components, the method improves predictive accuracy and robustness and offers a causally grounded path for multimodal representation learning in medical AI.

Abstract: Learning multimodal representations from medical images and other data sources can provide richer information for decision-making. While various multimodal models have been developed for this, they overlook learning features that are both necessary (must be present for the outcome to occur) and sufficient (enough to determine the outcome). We argue learning such features is crucial as they can improve model performance by capturing essential predictive information, and enhance model robustness to missing modalities as each modality can provide adequate predictive signals. Such features can be learned by leveraging the Probability of Necessity and Sufficiency (PNS) as a learning objective, an approach that has proven effective in unimodal settings. However, extending PNS to multimodal scenarios remains underexplored and is non-trivial as key conditions of PNS estimation are violated. We address this by decomposing multimodal representations into modality-invariant and modality-specific components, then deriving tractable PNS objectives for each. Experiments on synthetic and real-world medical datasets demonstrate our method's effectiveness. Code will be available on GitHub.

</details>


### [53] [Proof-of-Perception: Certified Tool-Using Multimodal Reasoning with Compositional Conformal Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00324)
*Arya Fayyazi,Haleh Akrami*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PoP is a tool-using, executable-graph framework for multimodal QA that attaches conformal-certificates to each step, enabling calibrated, stepwise uncertainty and adaptive compute. It outperforms strong CoT/ReAct/PoT baselines on document, chart, and multi-image QA while using less computation and reducing hallucinations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal reasoning systems often lack explicit reliability, compound errors across steps, hallucinate, and waste computation without clear accuracy–compute trade-offs. The goal is to ground answers in verifiable evidence and deliver calibrated uncertainty with efficient compute use.

Method: Model reasoning as an executable graph where each perception/logic node outputs a conformal set that certifies calibrated uncertainty. A lightweight controller uses these per-step certificates to adaptively allocate tool calls under a compute budget—expanding when uncertainty is high and stopping early when sufficient confidence is reached—thereby grounding outputs in evidence.

Result: Across document, chart, and multi-image QA benchmarks, PoP improves accuracy and reliability versus strong chain-of-thought, ReAct-style, and program-of-thought baselines, while achieving better compute efficiency and reducing error compounding and hallucinations.

Conclusion: Attaching conformal, per-step guarantees to a tool-using reasoning graph enables principled accuracy–compute trade-offs and more reliable, grounded multimodal QA. PoP offers a practical path toward calibrated, evidence-based reasoning with adaptive compute allocation.

Abstract: We present Proof-of-Perception (PoP), a tool-using framework that casts multimodal reasoning as an executable graph with explicit reliability guarantees. Each perception or logic node outputs a conformal set, yielding calibrated, stepwise uncertainty; a lightweight controller uses these certificates to allocate compute under a budget, expanding with extra tool calls only when needed and stopping early otherwise. This grounds answers in verifiable evidence, reduces error compounding and hallucinations, and enables principled accuracy-compute trade-offs. Across document, chart, and multi-image QA benchmarks, PoP improves performance and reliability over strong chain-of-thought, ReAct-style, and program-of-thought baselines while using computation more efficiently.

</details>


### [54] [Diffusion-Based Low-Light Image Enhancement with Color and Luminance Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00337)
*Xuanshuo Fu,Lei Kang,Javier Vazquez-Corral*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A conditional diffusion model for low‑light image enhancement guided by a Structured Control Embedding Module (SCEM) that decomposes inputs into illumination and invariant cues; trained on LOLv1 yet reaches SOTA and generalizes across multiple benchmarks without fine‑tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low‑light images exhibit low contrast, noise, and color distortion that harm visual quality and downstream tasks; existing approaches often lack strong physical priors or generalization. The goal is a physically grounded, generalizable enhancer.

Method: SCEM decomposes each low‑light image into four components—illumination, illumination‑invariant features, shadow priors, and color‑invariant cues—which serve as control signals to condition a U‑Net‑based diffusion model trained with a simplified noise‑prediction loss, yielding structured, physics‑guided enhancement.

Result: Trained solely on LOLv1 and evaluated (without fine‑tuning) on LOLv2‑real, LSRW, DICM, MEF, and LIME, the approach achieves state‑of‑the‑art results on quantitative and perceptual metrics, showing strong cross‑dataset generalization.

Conclusion: Physically motivated, structured control embeddings within diffusion models can deliver robust, generalizable low‑light enhancement, underscoring the value of integrating physical priors into generative restoration frameworks.

Abstract: Low-light images often suffer from low contrast, noise, and color distortion, degrading visual quality and impairing downstream vision tasks. We propose a novel conditional diffusion framework for low-light image enhancement that incorporates a Structured Control Embedding Module (SCEM). SCEM decomposes a low-light image into four informative components including illumination, illumination-invariant features, shadow priors, and color-invariant cues. These components serve as control signals that condition a U-Net-based diffusion model trained with a simplified noise-prediction loss. Thus, the proposed SCEM equipped Diffusion method enforces structured enhancement guided by physical priors. In experiments, our model is trained only on the LOLv1 dataset and evaluated without fine-tuning on LOLv2-real, LSRW, DICM, MEF, and LIME. The method achieves state-of-the-art performance in quantitative and perceptual metrics, demonstrating strong generalization across benchmarks. https://casted.github.io/scem/.

</details>


### [55] [Percept-Aware Surgical Planning for Visual Cortical Prostheses with Vascular Avoidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00362)
*Galen Pogoncheff,Alvin Wang,Jacob Granley,Michael Beyeler*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Electrode placement for cortical visual prostheses is cast as a differentiable, safety‑constrained optimization that directly minimizes perceptual task error, yielding better simulated reconstructions than coverage heuristics while avoiding vasculature and respecting gray‑matter constraints; it also supports co‑optimizing multi‑electrode thread layouts under insertion budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High‑density, flexible interfaces in V1 make 3D electrode placement a key surgical problem. Existing strategies prioritize visual‑field coverage and anatomical rules of thumb but do not directly optimize for predicted perceptual outcomes or explicitly enforce safety, motivating a principled planning framework that links anatomy, perception, and constraints.

Method: Treat electrode coordinates as learnable parameters in anatomical space and optimize them end‑to‑end with a differentiable forward model of prosthetic vision. The objective minimizes task‑level perceptual error (e.g., reading, natural‑image reconstruction) while imposing vascular‑avoidance and gray‑matter feasibility constraints, using realistic folded cortical geometry (FreeSurfer fsaverage). The framework also enables co‑optimization of multi‑electrode thread configurations under fixed insertion budgets.

Result: Across simulated reading and natural‑image tasks, percept‑aware optimization consistently improves reconstruction fidelity relative to coverage‑based placement. Enforcing vascular constraints eliminates margin violations without sacrificing perceptual performance. The approach supports co‑optimizing multi‑thread insertion plans within budget limits.

Conclusion: Differentiable perceptual models can guide anatomically grounded, safety‑aware, computer‑assisted planning for cortical visual prostheses, providing a foundation to optimize next‑generation devices beyond simple coverage heuristics.

Abstract: Cortical visual prostheses aim to restore sight by electrically stimulating neurons in early visual cortex (V1). With the emergence of high-density and flexible neural interfaces, electrode placement within three-dimensional cortex has become a critical surgical planning problem. Existing strategies emphasize visual field coverage and anatomical heuristics but do not directly optimize predicted perceptual outcomes under safety constraints. We present a percept-aware framework for surgical planning of cortical visual prostheses that formulates electrode placement as a constrained optimization problem in anatomical space. Electrode coordinates are treated as learnable parameters and optimized end-to-end using a differentiable forward model of prosthetic vision. The objective minimizes task-level perceptual error while incorporating vascular avoidance and gray matter feasibility constraints. Evaluated on simulated reading and natural image tasks using realistic folded cortical geometry (FreeSurfer fsaverage), percept-aware optimization consistently improves reconstruction fidelity relative to coverage-based placement strategies. Importantly, vascular safety constraints eliminate margin violations while preserving perceptual performance. The framework further enables co-optimization of multi-electrode thread configurations under fixed insertion budgets. These results demonstrate how differentiable percept models can inform anatomically grounded, safety-aware computer-assisted planning for cortical neural interfaces and provide a foundation for optimizing next-generation visual prostheses.

</details>


### [56] [Unsupervised Semantic Segmentation in Synchrotron Computed Tomography with Self-Correcting Pseudo Labels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00372)
*Austin Yunker,Peter Kenesei,Hemant Sharma,Jun-Sang Park,Antonino Miceli,Rajkumar Kettimuthu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Unsupervised-to-semi-supervised pipeline for SR-CT segmentation: voxel-intensity clustering to create pseudo labels, then refine via Unbiased Teacher self-training, yielding large gains over raw pseudo labels and generalizing across samples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SR-CT yields extremely large, high-quality 3D datasets whose manual annotation is impractical, creating a bottleneck for analysis. Deep learning could automate segmentation but typically requires extensive labeled data, which is infeasible for SR-CT.

Method: 1) Generate pseudo labels by clustering voxel attenuation values to obtain an initial semantic map. 2) Train a segmentation network on these pseudo labels. 3) Apply the Unbiased Teacher semi-supervised approach to self-correct and refine the labels via teacher–student consistency, improving final segmentations. Conduct ablations on model choice, loss, pseudo-labeling strategy, and input type.

Result: On a magnesium crystal SR-CT dataset, the method improves pixel accuracy by 13.31% and mIoU by 15.94% over the baseline pseudo labels. Evaluations on two additional samples show segmentations substantially better than the initial pseudo labels, indicating robustness.

Conclusion: The framework removes the need for manual labeling in SR-CT segmentation while achieving accurate results and generalizing to multiple samples. It offers a practical, scalable solution to a key bottleneck in SR-CT workflows and demonstrates strong gains over naive pseudo labeling.

Abstract: X-ray computed tomography (CT) is a widely used imaging technique that provides detailed examinations into the internal structure of an object with synchrotron CT (SR-CT) enabling improved data quality by using higher energy, monochromatic X-rays. While SR-CT allows for improved resolution, time-resolved experimentation, and reduced imaging artifacts, it also produces significantly larger datasets than conventional CT. Accurate and efficient evaluation of these datasets is a critical component of these workflows; yet is often done manually representing a major bottleneck in the analysis phase. While deep learning has emerged as a powerful tool capable of providing a wide range of purely data-driven solutions, it requires a substantial amount of labeled data for training and manual annotation of SR-CT datasets is impractical in practice. In this paper, we introduce a novel framework that enables automatic segmentation of large, high-resolution SR-CT datasets by eliminating the need to hand label images for deep learning training. First, we generate pseudo labels by clustering on the voxel values identifying regions in the volume with similar attenuation coefficients producing an initial semantic map. Afterwards, we train a segmentation model on the pseudo labels before utilizing the Unbiased Teacher approach to self-correct them ensuring accurate final segmentations. We find our approach improves pixel-wise accuracy and mIoU by 13.31% and 15.94%, respectively, over the baseline pseudo labels when using a magnesium crystal SR-CT sample. Additionally, we extensively evaluate the different components of our workflow including segmentation model, loss function, pseudo labeling strategy, and input type. Finally, we evaluate our approach on to two additional samples highlighting our frameworks ability to produce segmentations that are considerably better than the original pseudo labels.

</details>


### [57] [DiffSOS: Acoustic Conditional Diffusion Model for Speed-of-Sound Reconstruction in Ultrasound Computed Tomography](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00382)
*Yujia Wu,Shuoqi Chen,Shiru Wang,Yucheng Tang,Petr Bruza,Geoffrey P. Luke*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DiffSOS is a ControlNet‑conditioned diffusion model that reconstructs high‑fidelity ultrasound speed‑of‑sound (SoS) maps directly from acoustic waveforms, delivering near–real‑time (10‑step DDIM) inference, state‑of‑the‑art quality on OpenPros USCT (MS‑SSIM 0.957), and pixel‑wise uncertainty estimates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current SoS reconstruction in USCT faces a trade‑off: physics‑based FWI is accurate but prohibitively slow, while learning‑based methods are fast but oversmoothed and lack uncertainty quantification. There is a need for fast, detailed, and reliable SoS maps to reveal subtle anatomy/pathology and support clinical decision‑making.

Method: Introduce DiffSOS, a conditional diffusion model that maps measured acoustic waveforms to SoS images. Use a specialized acoustic ControlNet to tightly condition the denoising process on physical measurements. Train with a hybrid loss combining noise prediction accuracy, spatial reconstruction fidelity, and control of noise frequency content. Use stochastic DDIM with only 10 sampling steps for fast inference. Leverage the model’s stochasticity to estimate per‑pixel uncertainty.

Result: On the OpenPros USCT benchmark, DiffSOS significantly outperforms prior networks, achieving an average MS‑SSIM of 0.957. It preserves fine structural details, reconstructs quickly (near real time with 10 DDIM steps), and outputs uncertainty maps indicating reconstruction reliability.

Conclusion: DiffSOS provides high‑fidelity, fast SoS reconstructions grounded in measured acoustics and augmented with principled uncertainty estimates, offering a more clinically practical alternative to slow FWI and oversmoothed deterministic deep networks.

Abstract: Accurate Speed-of-Sound (SoS) reconstruction from acoustic waveforms is a cornerstone of ultrasound computed tomography (USCT), enabling quantitative velocity mapping that reveals subtle anatomical details and pathological variations often invisible in conventional imaging. However, practical utility is hindered by the limitations of existing algorithms; traditional Full Waveform Inversion (FWI) is computationally intensive, while current deep learning approaches tend to produce oversmoothed results lacking fine details. We propose DiffSOS, a conditional diffusion model that directly maps acoustic waveforms to SoS maps. Our framework employs a specialized acoustic ControlNet to strictly ground the denoising process in physical wave measurements. To ensure structural consistency, we optimize a hybrid loss function that integrates noise prediction, spatial reconstruction, and noise frequency content. To accelerate inference, we employ stochastic Denoising Diffusion Implicit Model (DDIM) sampling, achieving near real-time reconstruction with only 10 steps. Crucially, we exploit the stochastic generative nature of our framework to estimate pixel-wise uncertainty, providing a measure of reliability that is often absent in deterministic approaches. Evaluated on the OpenPros USCT benchmark, DiffSOS significantly outperforms state-of-the-art networks, achieving an average Multi-scale Structural Similarity of 0.957. Our approach provides high-fidelity SoS maps with a principled measure of confidence, facilitating safer and faster clinical interpretation.

</details>


### [58] [SSR: Pushing the Limit of Spatial Intelligence with Structured Scene Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00409)
*Yi Zhang,Youya Xia,Yong Wang,Meng Song,Xin Wu,Wenjun Wan,Bingbing Liu,AiXue Ye,Hongbo Zhang,Feng Wen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SSR is a lightweight 2D–3D integration and structured scene reasoning framework that enables small MLLMs (~7B) to perform strong geometric/spatial reasoning without costly alignment pretraining, achieving SOTA (73.9 on VSI-Bench).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs are strong semantically but weak at spatial/geometric reasoning due to expensive modality alignment and insufficient fine-grained structural modeling. The goal is to enable precise, scalable spatial intelligence and global 3D grounding with lower training cost.

Method: Anchor 3D geometric features to pre-aligned 2D visual semantics via cross-modal addition and token interleaving to avoid large-scale alignment pretraining. Generate scene graphs as chains of independent local triplets with relative coordinates, and use an incremental generation algorithm to build LLM-friendly structural scaffolds. Extend to global-scale 3D grounding with absolute metric precision across heterogeneous data sources.

Result: At 7B parameters, SSR achieves state-of-the-art results on multiple spatial intelligence benchmarks, including 73.9 on VSI-Bench, and outperforms larger models while maintaining efficient feature alignment. It also demonstrates accurate 3D global grounding with absolute metric precision.

Conclusion: Efficient 2D–3D feature anchoring and structured, incremental scene reasoning are key to genuine spatial intelligence; with SSR, smaller models can surpass larger ones in spatial tasks without heavy alignment pretraining.

Abstract: While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) excel in semantic tasks, they frequently lack the "spatial sense" essential for sophisticated geometric reasoning. Current models typically suffer from exorbitant modality-alignment costs and deficiency in fine-grained structural modeling precision.We introduce SSR, a framework designed for Structured Scene Reasoning that seamlessly integrates 2D and 3D representations via a lightweight alignment mechanism. To minimize training overhead, our framework anchors 3D geometric features to the large language model's pre-aligned 2D visual semantics through cross-modal addition and token interleaving, effectively obviating the necessity for large-scale alignment pre-training. To underpin complex spatial reasoning, we propose a novel scene graph generation pipeline that represents global layouts as a chain of independent local triplets defined by relative coordinates. This is complemented by an incremental generation algorithm, enabling the model to construct "language-model-friendly" structural scaffolds for complex environments. Furthermore, we extend these capabilities to global-scale 3D global grounding task, achieving absolute metric precision across heterogeneous data sources. At a 7B parameter scale, SSR achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple spatial intelligence benchmarks, notably scoring 73.9 on VSI-Bench. Our approach significantly outperforms much larger models, demonstrating that efficient feature alignment and structured scene reasoning are the cornerstones of authentic spatial intelligence.

</details>


### [59] [PointAlign: Feature-Level Alignment Regularization for 3D Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00412)
*Yuanhao Su,Shaofeng Zhang,Xiaosong Jia,Qi Fan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PointAlign adds explicit feature-level alignment to 3D vision-language models by supervising intermediate point-cloud tokens to match visual input tokens via a consistency loss, trained with a lightweight projector and LoRA. This preserves fine-grained 3D geometry and yields notable gains on classification (avg +2.08 pp; +7.50 pp on open-vocab Objaverse) and captioning (+4.88 pp).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Paired 3D–text data are scarce, and current 3D VLMs use only next-token prediction on language, which underutilizes 3D signals and causes geometric information in intermediate representations to degrade during language modeling.

Method: Introduce a feature-level alignment regularizer that constrains intermediate point-cloud tokens inside the LLM to stay consistent with the original visual (point-cloud) input tokens. Implemented via a consistency loss and trained by only a lightweight alignment projector plus LoRA adapters, adding minimal compute while providing explicit supervision to preserve geometry/semantics.

Result: Across ModelNet40 and Objaverse, the method improves classification by 2.08 percentage points on average, achieves +7.50 pp on open-vocabulary Objaverse classification, and improves 3D object captioning by 4.88 pp as evaluated by Qwen2-72B-Instruct. Code: https://github.com/yharoldsu0627/PointAlign.

Conclusion: Feature-level supervision of intermediate tokens effectively prevents geometric degradation in 3D VLMs, making better use of limited 3D data with low overhead and improving both classification and captioning performance.

Abstract: The development of 3D Vision-Language Models (VLMs), crucial for applications in robotics, autonomous driving, and augmented reality, is severely constrained by the scarcity of paired 3D-text data. Existing methods rely solely on next-token prediction loss, using only language tokens for supervision. This results in inefficient utilization of limited 3D data and leads to a significant degradation and loss of valuable geometric information in intermediate representations. To address these limitations, we propose {\mname}, a novel feature-level alignment regularization method. {\mname} explicitly supervises intermediate point cloud tokens to preserve fine-grained 3D geometric-semantic information throughout the language modeling process. Specifically, we constrain the intermediate point cloud tokens within the LLM to align with visual input tokens via a consistency loss. By training only a lightweight alignment projector and LoRA adapters, {\mname} achieves explicit feature-level supervision with minimal computational overhead, effectively preventing geometric degradation. Extensive experiments on ModelNet40 and Objaverse datasets demonstrate that our method achieves \textbf{2.08} pp improvement on average for classification tasks, with a substantial \textbf{7.50} pp gain on the challenging open-vocabulary Objaverse classification task and \textbf{4.88} pp improvement on 3D object captioning evaluated by Qwen2-72B-Instruct, validating the effectiveness of {\mname}. Code is publicly available at \href{https://github.com/yharoldsu0627/PointAlign}{https://github.com/yharoldsu0627/PointAlign}.

</details>


### [60] [DiffTrans: Differentiable Geometry-Materials Decomposition for Reconstructing Transparent Objects](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00413)
*Changpu Li,Shuang Wu,Songlin Tang,Guangming Lu,Jun Yu,Wenjie Pei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DiffTrans is a CUDA-accelerated, differentiable rendering framework that jointly reconstructs geometry and material properties (index of refraction, absorption) of transparent objects and the surrounding environment from multi-view images, outperforming prior methods in complex, real-world scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing transparent-object reconstruction methods rely on restrictive assumptions (uniform topology, ideal transparency, only surface reflections), limiting applicability to real-world objects with diverse shapes, complex textures, and intricate light transport. There is a need for a general, efficient approach that can handle complex scenes and jointly recover geometry and material properties.

Method: 1) Initialize geometry using FlexiCubes with dilation and smoothness regularization from multi-view silhouettes as an iso-surface. 2) Recover the scene’s environment via an environment light radiance field. 3) Perform unified, end-to-end optimization with a recursive differentiable ray tracer to refine geometry and estimate index of refraction and absorption. 4) Implement the tracer in CUDA to reduce computational cost.

Result: Across multiple benchmarks, DiffTrans achieves superior reconstruction quality of transparent objects—particularly in scenes with diverse topology and complex textures—while being computationally efficient due to GPU-accelerated (CUDA) ray tracing. Code is publicly available.

Conclusion: By combining FlexiCubes-based initialization, environment radiance recovery, and a CUDA-implemented recursive differentiable ray tracer, DiffTrans provides accurate and efficient joint reconstruction of transparent-object geometry and materials in challenging, realistic scenarios, advancing the state of the art.

Abstract: Reconstructing transparent objects from a set of multi-view images is a challenging task due to the complicated nature and indeterminate behavior of light propagation. Typical methods are primarily tailored to specific scenarios, such as objects following a uniform topology, exhibiting ideal transparency and surface specular reflections, or with only surface materials, which substantially constrains their practical applicability in real-world settings. In this work, we propose a differentiable rendering framework for transparent objects, dubbed DiffTrans, which allows for efficient decomposition and reconstruction of the geometry and materials of transparent objects, thereby reconstructing transparent objects accurately in intricate scenes with diverse topology and complex texture. Specifically, we first utilize FlexiCubes with dilation and smoothness regularization as the iso-surface representation to reconstruct an initial geometry efficiently from the multi-view object silhouette. Meanwhile, we employ the environment light radiance field to recover the environment of the scene. Then we devise a recursive differentiable ray tracer to further optimize the geometry, index of refraction and absorption rate simultaneously in a unified and end-to-end manner, leading to high-quality reconstruction of transparent objects in intricate scenes. A prominent advantage of the designed ray tracer is that it can be implemented in CUDA, enabling a significantly reduced computational cost. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate the superior reconstruction performance of our DiffTrans compared with other methods, especially in intricate scenes involving transparent objects with diverse topology and complex texture. The code is available at https://github.com/lcp29/DiffTrans.

</details>


### [61] [Station2Radar: query conditioned gaussian splatting for precipitation field](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00418)
*Doyi Kim,Minseok Seo,Changick Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a query-conditioned Gaussian splatting framework that fuses satellite imagery with automatic weather station (AWS) observations to generate high-resolution precipitation fields efficiently, rendering only precipitation-supporting regions and achieving >50% lower RMSE than conventional gridded products across scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Radar offers accurate precipitation structure but is costly and geographically limited; AWS data are accurate but sparse points; satellites are dense and global but do not directly retrieve rainfall. There is a need to fuse these heterogeneous sources to produce accurate, high-resolution, real-time precipitation fields without full-scene rendering costs.

Method: Query-Conditioned Gaussian Splatting (QCGS) selectively renders only queried precipitation regions rather than the full 2D image plane. A point proposal network (radar-inspired) identifies rainfall-supporting locations, and an implicit neural representation (INR) predicts Gaussian parameters per point. AWS observations and satellite features are fused to generate precipitation fields at arbitrary resolutions with preserved sharp structures and reduced computation.

Result: QCGS produces precipitation fields in real time with resolution flexibility, preserves sharp precipitation structures, and achieves over 50% RMSE improvement versus conventional gridded precipitation products, maintaining strong performance over multiple spatial and temporal scales.

Conclusion: Selective, query-conditioned splatting combined with AWS–satellite fusion is an effective alternative/complement to traditional gridded precipitation products, enabling accurate, efficient, and scalable precipitation field generation; the approach generalizes across scales and is suitable for real-time applications.

Abstract: Precipitation forecasting relies on heterogeneous data. Weather radar is accurate, but coverage is geographically limited and costly to maintain. Weather stations provide accurate but sparse point measurements, while satellites offer dense, high-resolution coverage without direct rainfall retrieval. To overcome these limitations, we propose Query-Conditioned Gaussian Splatting (QCGS), the first framework to fuse automatic weather station (AWS) observations with satellite imagery for generating precipitation fields. Unlike conventional 2D Gaussian splatting, which renders the entire image plane, QCGS selectively renders only queried precipitation regions, avoiding unnecessary computation in non-precipitating areas while preserving sharp precipitation structures. The framework combines a radar point proposal network that identifies rainfall-support locations with an implicit neural representation (INR) network that predicts Gaussian parameters for each point. QCGS enables efficient, resolution-flexible precipitation field generation in real time. Through extensive evaluation with benchmark precipitation products, QCGS demonstrates over 50\% improvement in RMSE compared to conventional gridded precipitation products, and consistently maintains high performance across multiple spatiotemporal scales.

</details>


### [62] [An Interpretable Local Editing Model for Counterfactual Medical Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00423)
*Hyungi Min,Taeseung You,Hangyeul Lee,Yeongjae Cho,Sungzoon Cho*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces InstructX2X, an instruction-driven, region-specific counterfactual editor for chest X-rays that prevents collateral demographic changes and provides visual guidance maps; plus a new MIMIC-EDIT-INSTRUCTION dataset; achieves state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing counterfactual medical image generators often (1) unintentionally alter non-target attributes (e.g., demographics) when only disease features should change, and (2) lack interpretability, limiting clinical trust and deployment. There is a need for controllable, faithful edits with transparent rationale.

Method: InstructX2X performs interpretable local editing via Region-Specific Editing that constrains modifications to pathology-relevant areas and outputs a Guidance Map for visual explanation. It is instruction-driven, leveraging a new dataset (MIMIC-EDIT-INSTRUCTION) derived from expert-verified medical VQA pairs to guide counterfactual transformations.

Result: Across major evaluation metrics, InstructX2X attains state-of-the-art performance and produces high-quality counterfactual chest X-ray images accompanied by inherently interpretable guidance maps. Specific metric values are not provided in the abstract.

Conclusion: Constrained, interpretable local editing improves the faithfulness and clinical usability of counterfactual medical image generation. The accompanying dataset supports instruction-based editing research. The approach offers better control, reduced unintended attribute shifts, and clearer explanations, positioning it for practical medical AI applications.

Abstract: Counterfactual medical image generation have emerged as a critical tool for enhancing AI-driven systems in medical domain by answering "what-if" questions. However, existing approaches face two fundamental limitations: First, they fail to prevent unintended modifications, resulting collateral changes in demographic attributes when only disease features should be affected. Second, they lack interpretability in their editing process, which significantly limits their utility in real-world medical applications. To address these limitations, we present InstructX2X, a novel interpretable local editing model for counterfactual medical image generation featuring Region-Specific Editing. This approach restricts modifications to specific regions, effectively preventing unintended changes while simultaneously providing a Guidance Map that offers inherently interpretable visual explanations of the editing process. Additionally, we introduce MIMIC-EDIT-INSTRUCTION, a dataset for counterfactual medical image generation derived from expert-verified medical VQA pairs. Through extensive experiments, InstructX2X achieve state-of-the-art performance across all major evaluation metrics. Our model successfully generates high-quality counterfactual chest X-ray images along with interpretable explanations.

</details>


### [63] [Taxonomy-Aware Representation Alignment for Hierarchical Visual Recognition with Large Multimodal Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00431)
*Hulingxiao He,Zhi Tan,Yuxin Peng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TARA aligns LMM visual and token representations with taxonomy-aware embeddings from biology foundation models to inject hierarchical knowledge, boosting hierarchical consistency and leaf-node accuracy for both seen and novel categories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LMMs excel at fine-grained recognition of known classes but struggle to produce coherent coarse-to-fine label paths and to generalize to novel categories with scarce or no images—capabilities crucial for biological taxonomies.

Method: Leverage biology foundation models (trained via hierarchical contrastive learning) as taxonomic priors. Align LMM intermediate visual features to BFM representations to encode tree-structured relations, and align the first answer token’s representation to the ground-truth label to flexibly handle user-requested granularity.

Result: Experiments show consistent gains in hierarchical consistency and leaf-node accuracy, enabling reliable recognition across both known and unseen categories within complex biological taxonomies.

Conclusion: Taxonomy-Aware Representation Alignment is a simple, effective way to infuse LMMs with taxonomic structure, improving hierarchical visual recognition and generalization to novel categories in biological domains.

Abstract: A high-performing, general-purpose visual understanding model should map visual inputs to a taxonomic tree of labels, identify novel categories beyond the training set for which few or no publicly available images exist. Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) have achieved remarkable progress in fine-grained visual recognition (FGVR) for known categories. However, they remain limited in hierarchical visual recognition (HVR) that aims at predicting consistent label paths from coarse to fine categories, especially for novel categories. To tackle these challenges, we propose Taxonomy-Aware Representation Alignment (TARA), a simple yet effective strategy to inject taxonomic knowledge into LMMs. TARA leverages representations from biology foundation models (BFMs) that encode rich biological relationships through hierarchical contrastive learning. By aligning the intermediate representations of visual features with those of BFMs, LMMs are encouraged to extract discriminative visual cues well structured in the taxonomy tree. Additionally, we align the representations of the first answer token with the ground-truth label, flexibly bridging the gap between contextualized visual features and categories of varying granularity according to user intent. Experiments demonstrate that TARA consistently enhances LMMs' hierarchical consistency and leaf node accuracy, enabling reliable recognition of both known and novel categories within complex biological taxonomies. Code is available at https://github.com/PKU-ICST-MIPL/TARA_CVPR2026.

</details>


### [64] [TAP-SLF: Parameter-Efficient Adaptation of Vision Foundation Models for Multi-Task Ultrasound Image Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00433)
*Hui Wan,Libin Lan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A parameter‑efficient multi‑task ultrasound framework (TAP‑SLF) that combines task‑aware soft prompts with selective high‑layer LoRA tuning on a frozen vision foundation model, achieving competitive challenge results while updating only a small portion of parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi‑task medical image analysis (segmentation, classification, detection, regression) suffers from limited data, overfitting with full VFM fine‑tuning, high compute, and task‑agnostic PEFT that ignores task‑specific needs and layer sensitivity during adaptation.

Method: TAP‑SLF injects task‑aware soft prompts into the input token sequence to encode task priors and applies LoRA to selected top encoder layers while keeping the VFM backbone frozen. This unifies adaptation for diverse tasks within a shared backbone by updating only a small parameter subset.

Result: On the FMC_UIA 2026 Challenge test set, TAP‑SLF placed fifth; evaluations on the official training data with an 8:2 split further support effectiveness. The approach achieves competitive performance with substantially fewer trainable parameters.

Conclusion: Encoding task priors via prompts and fine‑tuning only sensitive higher layers via LoRA provides an efficient, generalizable way to adapt VFMs for multiple ultrasound tasks in a single backbone, mitigating overfitting and reducing compute.

Abstract: Executing multiple tasks simultaneously in medical image analysis, including segmentation, classification, detection, and regression, often introduces significant challenges regarding model generalizability and the optimization of shared feature representations. While Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) provide powerful general representations, full fine-tuning on limited medical data is prone to overfitting and incurs high computational costs. Moreover, existing parameter-efficient fine-tuning approaches typically adopt task-agnostic adaptation protocols, overlooking both task-specific mechanisms and the varying sensitivity of model layers during fine-tuning. In this work, we propose Task-Aware Prompting and Selective Layer Fine-Tuning (TAP-SLF), a unified framework for multi-task ultrasound image analysis. TAP-SLF incorporates task-aware soft prompts to encode task-specific priors into the input token sequence and applies LoRA to selected specific top layers of the encoder. This strategy updates only a small fraction of the VFM parameters while keeping the pre-trained backbone frozen. By combining task-aware prompts with selective high-layer fine-tuning, TAP-SLF enables efficient VFM adaptation to diverse medical tasks within a shared backbone. Results on the FMC_UIA 2026 Challenge test set, where TAP-SLF wins fifth place, combined with evaluations on the officially released training dataset using an 8:2 train-test split, demonstrate that task-aware prompting and selective layer tuning are effective strategies for efficient VFM adaptation.

</details>


### [65] [Self-Correction Inside the Model: Leveraging Layer Attention to Mitigate Hallucinations in Large Vision Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00437)
*April Fu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a lightweight internal self-correction mechanism (ICLA) that adds diagonal cross-layer attention so each layer can pull from all preceding layers during generation, reducing visual hallucinations in strong LVLMs with minimal extra parameters, improving visual grounding across benchmarks on LLaVA1.5-7B and Qwen2.5-VL-7B.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite advances, LVLMs still hallucinate, and prior mitigation strategies tied to specific failure patterns (e.g., linguistic bias, overthinking) are less reliable as models evolve. A need exists for a model-internal, training-efficient mechanism that can adaptively refine generation without external signals.

Method: Introduce Internal self-Correction via Layer Attention (ICLA): during decoding, each transformer layer applies a diagonal cross-layer attention over hidden states from all earlier layers, enabling selective retrieval and self-refinement. Trains only ~0.2M/0.1M new parameters when integrated into LLaVA1.5-7B and Qwen2.5-VL-7B, without external correction modules.

Result: Consistent improvements in visual grounding and reduced hallucinations across multiple benchmarks on two strong LVLMs, achieved with negligible parameter overhead (~0.1–0.2M).

Conclusion: A simple, lightweight cross-layer attention mechanism can serve as an effective internal self-correction method for advanced LVLMs, improving grounding without external signals and potentially generalizing across architectures.

Abstract: Although Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have made substantial progress, hallucination, where generated text is not grounded in the visual input, remains a challenge. As LVLMs become stronger, previously reported hallucination patterns, such as linguistic bias and overthinking phenomenon, become far less consistent, making the corresponding mitigation techniques substantially less effective. In this paper, we introduce an Internal self-Correction mechanism utilizing Layer Attention (ICLA) that operates directly on hidden states during generation. Each layer selectively retrieves information from all preceding layers through a diagonal cross-layer attention mechanism, enabling self-refinement without any external correction signals. With introducing and training only 0.2M and 0.1M additional parameters on LLaVA1.5-7B and Qwen2.5-VL-7B, \ours consistently improves visual grounding across multiple hallucination benchmarks, demonstrating its effectiveness for more advanced LVLMs.

</details>


### [66] [Mamba-CAD: State Space Model For 3D Computer-Aided Design Generative Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00439)
*Xueyang Li,Yunzhong Lou,Yu Song,Xiangdong Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Mamba-CAD learns a latent space for long, fine-grained parametric CAD sequences with a Mamba-based encoder–decoder trained via self-supervised reconstruction, then uses a GAN to generate latent codes that are decoded back into CAD programs. A new 77k-model dataset supports training; experiments show longer valid generations and strong overall metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial component CAD models require long, complex parametric sequences that current sequence models struggle to model and generate validly. There is a need for methods that handle long-range dependencies and produce realistic, valid CAD programs at industrial complexity.

Method: Two-stage self-supervised generative framework: (1) Pre-train a Mamba-based encoder–decoder on a CAD reconstruction task to learn robust latent representations of long parametric CAD sequences. (2) Train a generative adversarial network to synthesize plausible latent representations, which the decoder converts back into parametric CAD sequences. Training leverages a new dataset of 77,078 CAD models with longer sequences.

Result: Comprehensive experiments indicate effectiveness across multiple evaluation metrics, with notable gains in the maximum/average length of validly generated parametric CAD sequences. The authors release code and the 77k-model dataset.

Conclusion: Modeling CAD programs in a learned latent space with a Mamba encoder–decoder and adversarial latent generation enables scalable synthesis of complex, long-horizon parametric CAD designs. The new dataset and open-source release position Mamba-CAD as a step forward for industrial-grade generative CAD.

Abstract: Computer-Aided Design (CAD) generative modeling has a strong and long-term application in the industry. Recently, the parametric CAD sequence as the design logic of an object has been widely mined by sequence models. However, the industrial CAD models, especially in component objects, are fine-grained and complex, requiring a longer parametric CAD sequence to define. To address the problem, we introduce Mamba-CAD, a self-supervised generative modeling for complex CAD models in the industry, which can model on a longer parametric CAD sequence. Specifically, we first design an encoder-decoder framework based on a Mamba architecture and pair it with a CAD reconstruction task for pre-training to model the latent representation of CAD models; and then we utilize the learned representation to guide a generative adversarial network to produce the fake representation of CAD models, which would be finally recovered into parametric CAD sequences via the decoder of MambaCAD. To train Mamba-CAD, we further create a new dataset consisting of 77,078 CAD models with longer parametric CAD sequences. Comprehensive experiments are conducted to demonstrate the effectiveness of our model under various evaluation metrics, especially in the generation length of valid parametric CAD sequences. The code and dataset can be achieved from https://github.com/Sunny-Hack/Code-for-Mamba-CAD-AAAI-2025-.

</details>


### [67] [SesaHand: Enhancing 3D Hand Reconstruction via Controllable Generation with Semantic and Structural Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00443)
*Zhuoran Zhao,Xianghao Kong,Linlin Yang,Zheng Wei,Pan Hui,Anyi Rao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SesaHand proposes semantics- and structure-aligned controllable hand image generation that produces diverse, well-aligned hands and bodies, yielding superior training data and improved 3D hand reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Game-engine synthetic datasets lack texture/environment diversity and often omit arms and interacting objects; existing generative approaches suffer semantic and structural misalignment between hands and the rest of the scene/body. The field needs human-centric, controllable, and structurally consistent synthetic images to better train 3D hand reconstruction models.

Method: Dual-alignment generative pipeline: (1) Semantic alignment via Vision-Language Model captions followed by Chain-of-Thought reasoning to extract human behavior semantics, suppressing irrelevant environmental details and enforcing human-centric contexts in prompts; (2) Structural alignment via hierarchical structural fusion that injects multi-granularity structural cues to refine features and better align hands with the whole body, plus a hand-structure attention enhancement module to increase focus on hand regions. The generated images are then used to train 3D hand reconstruction models.

Result: Empirically outperforms prior image-generation baselines in diversity and alignment quality, and provides measurable accuracy gains for 3D hand reconstruction when trained with the generated images.

Conclusion: Controllable generation with explicit semantic and structural alignment yields higher-quality synthetic hand images that address misalignment and diversity gaps of prior methods, translating into improved 3D hand reconstruction performance.

Abstract: Recent studies on 3D hand reconstruction have demonstrated the effectiveness of synthetic training data to improve estimation performance. However, most methods rely on game engines to synthesize hand images, which often lack diversity in textures and environments, and fail to include crucial components like arms or interacting objects. Generative models are promising alternatives to generate diverse hand images, but still suffer from misalignment issues. In this paper, we present SesaHand, which enhances controllable hand image generation from both semantic and structural alignment perspectives for 3D hand reconstruction. Specifically, for semantic alignment, we propose a pipeline with Chain-of-Thought inference to extract human behavior semantics from image captions generated by the Vision-Language Model. This semantics suppresses human-irrelevant environmental details and ensures sufficient human-centric contexts for hand image generation. For structural alignment, we introduce hierarchical structural fusion to integrate structural information with different granularity for feature refinement to better align the hand and the overall human body in generated images. We further propose a hand structure attention enhancement method to efficiently enhance the model's attention on hand regions. Experiments demonstrate that our method not only outperforms prior work in generation performance but also improves 3D hand reconstruction with the generated hand images.

</details>


### [68] [Improved Adversarial Diffusion Compression for Real-World Video Super-Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00458)
*Bin Chen,Weiqi Li,Shijie Zhao,Xuanyu Zhang,Junlin Li,Li Zhang,Jian Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They introduce a temporally aware adversarial diffusion compression for Real-VSR that distills a 3D spatio-temporal DiT (DOVE) into a pruned 2D Stable Diffusion–based model augmented with lightweight 1D temporal convolutions and a dual-head adversarial distillation (pixel + feature) scheme, achieving 95% parameter reduction and 8× speedup while preserving competitive video quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models deliver realistic details for Real-VSR but are slow due to multi-step sampling; one-step variants are still huge and slow. Prior adversarial diffusion compression struggles on videos, lacking temporal awareness and conflating detail versus consistency, thus failing to balance spatial fidelity and temporal coherence.

Method: Distill a large DiT-based teacher (DOVE with 3D spatio-temporal attention) into a compact 2D Stable Diffusion backbone (AdcSR) pruned for efficiency and augmented with lightweight 1D temporal convolutions for temporal cues. Introduce dual-head adversarial distillation with discriminators in the pixel and feature domains to decouple optimization of spatial detail and temporal consistency.

Result: The compressed AdcVSR model cuts parameters by 95% and accelerates inference by 8× relative to the DOVE teacher, while maintaining competitive Real-VSR quality and efficiency.

Conclusion: A temporally aware, dual-head adversarial distillation framework enables aggressive compression of Real-VSR diffusion models, delivering large speed/size gains without sacrificing spatial detail or temporal coherence, offering a practical path to fast, high-quality Real-VSR.

Abstract: While many diffusion models have achieved impressive results in real-world video super-resolution (Real-VSR) by generating rich and realistic details, their reliance on multi-step sampling leads to slow inference. One-step networks like SeedVR2, DOVE, and DLoRAL alleviate this through condensing generation into one single step, yet they remain heavy, with billions of parameters and multi-second latency. Recent adversarial diffusion compression (ADC) offers a promising path via pruning and distilling these models into a compact AdcSR network, but directly applying it to Real-VSR fails to balance spatial details and temporal consistency due to its lack of temporal awareness and the limitations of standard adversarial learning. To address these challenges, we propose an improved ADC method for Real-VSR. Our approach distills a large diffusion Transformer (DiT) teacher DOVE equipped with 3D spatio-temporal attentions, into a pruned 2D Stable Diffusion (SD)-based AdcSR backbone, augmented with lightweight 1D temporal convolutions, achieving significantly higher efficiency. In addition, we introduce a dual-head adversarial distillation scheme, in which discriminators in both pixel and feature domains explicitly disentangle the discrimination of details and consistency into two heads, enabling both objectives to be effectively optimized without sacrificing one for the other. Experiments demonstrate that the resulting compressed AdcVSR model reduces complexity by 95% in parameters and achieves an 8$\times$ acceleration over its DiT teacher DOVE, while maintaining competitive video quality and efficiency.

</details>


### [69] [Explainable Continuous-Time Mask Refinement with Local Self-Similarity Priors for Medical Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00459)
*Rajdeep Chatterjee,Sudip Chakrabarty,Trishaani Acharjee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An explainable foot-ulcer segmentation model (LSS-LTCNet) that fuses local self-similarity texture priors with continuous-time ODE-based mask refinement achieves state-of-the-art boundary accuracy and efficiency on MICCAI FUSeg.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Foot-ulcer boundaries are difficult to delineate due to tissue heterogeneity and low contrast with surrounding skin, and existing intensity-based, black-box networks lack robustness and transparency needed for clinical and mHealth use.

Method: Combine Local Self-Similarity (LSS) to extract dense, illumination-invariant texture descriptors that separate necrotic tissue from background with a Liquid Time-Constant (LTC) refinement module that models boundary evolution as a continuous-time ODE, iteratively refining masks; delivers ante-hoc explainability with a compact 25.70M-parameter model.

Result: On the MICCAI FUSeg dataset, the approach attains a Dice score of 86.96% and an HD95 of 8.91 pixels, outperforming heavier U-Net and transformer baselines in boundary alignment and parameter efficiency.

Conclusion: By uniting explicit structural priors with neural ODE dynamics, LSS-LTCNet offers robust, transparent, and efficient foot-ulcer segmentation with visual auditability suitable for mobile healthcare deployment.

Abstract: Accurate semantic segmentation of foot ulcers is essential for automated wound monitoring, yet boundary delineation remains challenging due to tissue heterogeneity and poor contrast with surrounding skin. To overcome the limitations of standard intensity-based networks, we present LSS-LTCNet:an ante-hoc explainable framework synergizing deterministic structural priors with continuous-time neural dynamics. Our architecture departs from traditional black-box models by employing a Local Self-Similarity (LSS) mechanism that extracts dense, illumination-invariant texture descriptors to explicitly disentangle necrotic tissue from background artifacts. To enforce topological precision, we introduce a Liquid Time-Constant (LTC) refinement module that treats boundary evolution as an ODEgoverned dynamic system, iteratively refining masks over continuous time-steps. Comprehensive evaluation on the MICCAI FUSeg dataset demonstrates that LSS-LTCNet achieves state-of-the-art boundary alignment, securing a peak Dice score of 86.96% and an exceptional 95th percentile Hausdorff Distance (HD95) of 8.91 pixels. Requiring merely 25.70M parameters, the model significantly outperforms heavier U-Net and transformer baselines in efficiency. By providing inherent visual audit trails alongside high-fidelity predictions, LSS-LTCNet offers a robust and transparent solution for computer-aided diagnosis in mobile healthcare (mHealth) settings.

</details>


### [70] [ReMoT: Reinforcement Learning with Motion Contrast Triplets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00461)
*Cong Wan,Zeyu Guo,Jiangyang Li,SongLin Dong,Yifan Bai,Lin Peng,Zhiheng Ma,Yihong Gong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ReMoT is a unified recipe—an auto-built motion-contrast dataset (ReMoT‑16K) plus Group Relative Policy Optimization—that markedly improves VLM spatio‑temporal consistency, achieving state‑of‑the‑art results and a 25.1% gain on spatio‑temporal reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current vision‑language models struggle with spatio‑temporal consistency (e.g., tracking motion direction and timing), a weakness that can cause critical failures in navigation, robotics, and autonomous driving. The paper seeks a scalable way to teach VLMs fine‑grained motion discrimination and consistent reasoning over time.

Method: (1) Data: A rule‑based automatic pipeline converts video meta‑annotations into ReMoT‑16K, a 16.5K triplet motion‑contrast dataset, avoiding costly manual/model‑generated labeling. (2) Learning: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) is proposed and empirically shown to learn contrastive motion reasoning more effectively and data‑efficiently than standard supervised fine‑tuning. (3) Evaluation: A new benchmark of fine‑grained motion‑contrast triplets targets subtle motion attributes (e.g., opposing directions).

Result: Models trained with GRPO on ReMoT‑16K achieve state‑of‑the‑art on the new motion‑contrast benchmark and multiple standard VLM benchmarks, with a reported 25.1% performance improvement on spatio‑temporal reasoning tasks; GRPO outperforms supervised fine‑tuning in both performance and data efficiency.

Conclusion: Pairing scalable, rule‑based motion‑contrast data generation with GRPO effectively remedies VLM spatio‑temporal inconsistency and sets a strong, practical recipe and benchmark for motion‑aware VLMs, with clear implications for safety‑critical domains.

Abstract: We present ReMoT, a unified training paradigm to systematically address the fundamental shortcomings of VLMs in spatio-temporal consistency -- a critical failure point in navigation, robotics, and autonomous driving. ReMoT integrates two core components: (1) A rule-based automatic framework that generates ReMoT-16K, a large-scale (16.5K triplets) motion-contrast dataset derived from video meta-annotations, surpassing costly manual or model-based generation. (2) Group Relative Policy Optimization, which we empirically validate yields optimal performance and data efficiency for learning this contrastive reasoning, far exceeding standard Supervised Fine-Tuning. We also construct the first benchmark for fine-grained motion contrast triplets to measure a VLM's discrimination of subtle motion attributes (e.g., opposing directions). The resulting model achieves state-of-the-art performance on our new benchmark and multiple standard VLM benchmarks, culminating in a remarkable 25.1% performance leap on spatio-temporal reasoning tasks.

</details>


### [71] [OPGAgent: An Agent for Auditable Dental Panoramic X-ray Interpretation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00462)
*Zhaolin Yu,Litao Yang,Ben Babicka,Ming Hu,Jing Hao,Anthony Huang,James Huang,Yueming Jin,Jiasong Wu,Zongyuan Ge*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OPGAgent is an agentic, multi-tool system for panoramic dental radiograph (OPG) interpretation that hierarchically gathers evidence and reconciles outputs via anatomical constraints, and it introduces OPG-Bench for structured-report evaluation; it outperforms existing VLMs and agent frameworks on OPG tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OPGs require multi-task, full-arch analysis. Generic vision-language models can express multi-task outputs via natural language but lag behind task-specific models on accuracy and reliability. An orchestrated, auditable tool-using approach promises both versatility and precision, yet is largely unexplored in dental imaging. Current VQA-style metrics also miss hallucinations and lack structured coverage of findings.

Method: Design OPGAgent with three parts: (1) Hierarchical Evidence Gathering that decomposes analysis into global, quadrant, and tooth-level phases while dynamically invoking tools; (2) a Specialized Toolbox spanning spatial reasoning, detection, utility, and expert model “zoos”; and (3) a Consensus Subagent that enforces anatomical constraints to resolve conflicts. Additionally, propose OPG-Bench, a structured-report protocol built from (Location, Field, Value) triples derived from real clinical reports to comprehensively assess findings and hallucinations.

Result: On the proposed OPG-Bench and the public MMOral-OPG benchmark, OPGAgent surpasses state-of-the-art dental VLMs and general medical agent frameworks on both structured-report and VQA-style evaluations.

Conclusion: An agentic orchestration of specialized tools delivers more accurate, auditable, and comprehensive OPG interpretation than monolithic VLMs. The structured-report evaluation (OPG-Bench) provides a more faithful assessment than VQA indicators. Code to be released upon acceptance; the approach suggests a generalizable path for multi-task medical imaging analysis.

Abstract: Orthopantomograms (OPGs) are the standard panoramic radiograph in dentistry, used for full-arch screening across multiple diagnostic tasks. While Vision Language Models (VLMs) now allow multi-task OPG analysis through natural language, they underperform task-specific models on most individual tasks. Agentic systems that orchestrate specialized tools offer a path to both versatility and accuracy, this approach remains unexplored in the field of dental imaging. To address this gap, we propose OPGAgent, a multi-tool agentic system for auditable OPG interpretation. OPGAgent coordinates specialized perception modules with a consensus mechanism through three components: (1) a Hierarchical Evidence Gathering module that decomposes OPG analysis into global, quadrant, and tooth-level phases with dynamically invoking tools, (2) a Specialized Toolbox encapsulating spatial, detection, utility, and expert zoos, and (3) a Consensus Subagent that resolves conflicts through anatomical constraints. We further propose OPG-Bench, a structured-report protocol based on (Location, Field, Value) triples derived from real clinical reports, which enables a comprehensive review of findings and hallucinations, extending beyond the limitations of VQA indicators. On our OPG-Bench and the public MMOral-OPG benchmark, OPGAgent outperforms current dental VLMs and medical agent frameworks across both structured-report and VQA evaluation. Code will be released upon acceptance.

</details>


### [72] [DreamWorld: Unified World Modeling in Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00466)
*Boming Tan,Xiangdong Zhang,Ning Liao,Yuqing Zhang,Shaofeng Zhang,Xue Yang,Qi Fan,Yanyong Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DreamWorld is a video-generation framework that fuses multiple forms of world knowledge by jointly predicting pixels and foundation-model features, stabilized by training-time constraint annealing and inference-time inner guidance, yielding stronger temporal, geometric, and semantic consistency and outperforming Wan2.1 on VBench by 2.26 points.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current video generators achieve surface plausibility but lack a unified, coherent world model. Prior work typically injects a single type of knowledge or uses rigid alignment, which cannot capture heterogeneous dimensions like physical commonsense, 3D geometry, and temporal consistency; naïve multi-objective optimization causes instability and flicker.

Method: Joint World Modeling Paradigm: jointly predict video pixels and features from external foundation models to encode temporal dynamics, spatial geometry, and semantic consistency. Training uses Consistent Constraint Annealing (progressively increasing world-level constraints) to avoid instability; inference uses Multi-Source Inner-Guidance to apply learned world priors.

Result: Extensive evaluations indicate improved world consistency; the method surpasses Wan2.1 by +2.26 on VBench. Qualitatively reduces visual instability and temporal flickering. Code announced for release.

Conclusion: Integrating complementary world knowledge through joint pixel–feature prediction, with annealed constraints and inner guidance, advances coherent world modeling in video generation and yields measurable gains in consistency and benchmark performance.

Abstract: Despite impressive progress in video generation, existing models remain limited to surface-level plausibility, lacking a coherent and unified understanding of the world. Prior approaches typically incorporate only a single form of world-related knowledge or rely on rigid alignment strategies to introduce additional knowledge. However, aligning the single world knowledge is insufficient to constitute a world model that requires jointly modeling multiple heterogeneous dimensions (e.g., physical commonsense, 3D and temporal consistency). To address this limitation, we introduce \textbf{DreamWorld}, a unified framework that integrates complementary world knowledge into video generators via a \textbf{Joint World Modeling Paradigm}, jointly predicting video pixels and features from foundation models to capture temporal dynamics, spatial geometry, and semantic consistency. However, naively optimizing these heterogeneous objectives can lead to visual instability and temporal flickering. To mitigate this issue, we propose \textit{Consistent Constraint Annealing (CCA)} to progressively regulate world-level constraints during training, and \textit{Multi-Source Inner-Guidance} to enforce learned world priors at inference. Extensive evaluations show that DreamWorld improves world consistency, outperforming Wan2.1 by 2.26 points on VBench. Code will be made publicly available at \href{https://github.com/ABU121111/DreamWorld}{\textcolor{mypink}{\textbf{Github}}}.

</details>


### [73] [High Dynamic Range Imaging Based on an Asymmetric Event-SVE Camera System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00467)
*Pengju Sun,Banglei Guan,Jing Tao,Zhenbao Yu,Xuanyu Bai,Yang Shang,Qifeng Yu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Co-designs a dual-sensor HDR system that fuses a spatially varying exposure (SVE) micro‑attenuation camera with an event camera via cross‑modal alignment and a learned fusion network, yielding better highlight recovery, edge fidelity, and robustness than frame‑only or event‑only approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional cameras saturate under extreme illumination, limiting HDR capture. Event cameras offer high dynamic range and temporal resolution but lack absolute intensity; SVE sensors provide per‑pixel exposure diversity in one shot but still face artifacts. Combining the two requires solving non‑coaxial geometry and heterogeneous optics to enable reliable HDR in fast, high‑contrast scenes.

Method: Hardware–algorithm co-design: an asymmetric dual‑modality rig (SVE micro‑attenuation camera + event sensor). A two‑stage cross‑modal alignment pipeline first estimates a coarse homography via feature guidance, then refines alignment with a multi‑scale module using spatial pooling and frequency‑domain filtering. On aligned data, a cross‑modal HDR reconstruction network performs convolutional fusion with mutual‑information regularization and a learnable fusion loss that balances radiometric (intensity) cues from SVE frames with structural constraints derived from events.

Result: Across synthetic datasets and real captures, the system consistently improves highlight recovery, preserves sharper edges, and is more robust to dynamics and radiometric challenges than baselines relying solely on frames or events.

Conclusion: Jointly optimizing optical design, cross‑modal alignment, and computational fusion is an effective strategy for reliable HDR perception in highly dynamic, high‑contrast environments, demonstrating clear gains over single‑modality pipelines.

Abstract: High dynamic range (HDR) imaging under extreme illumination remains challenging for conventional cameras due to overexposure. Event cameras provide microsecond temporal resolution and high dynamic range, while spatially varying exposure (SVE) sensors offer single-shot radiometric diversity.We present a hardware--algorithm co-designed HDR imaging system that tightly integrates an SVE micro-attenuation camera with an event sensor in an asymmetric dual-modality configuration. To handle non-coaxial geometry and heterogeneous optics, we develop a two-stage cross-modal alignment framework that combines feature-guided coarse homography estimation with a multi-scale refinement module based on spatial pooling and frequency-domain filtering. On top of aligned representations, we develop a cross-modal HDR reconstruction network with convolutional fusion, mutual-information regularization, and a learnable fusion loss that adaptively balances intensity cues and event-derived structural constraints. Comprehensive experiments on both synthetic benchmarks and real captures demonstrate that the proposed system consistently improves highlight recovery, edge fidelity, and robustness compared with frame-only or event-only HDR pipelines. The results indicate that jointly optimizing optical design, cross-modal alignment, and computational fusion provides an effective foundation for reliable HDR perception in highly dynamic and radiometrically challenging environments.

</details>


### [74] [U-VLM: Hierarchical Vision Language Modeling for Report Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00479)
*Pengcheng Shi,Minghui Zhang,Kehan Song,Jiaqi Liu,Yun Gu,Xinglin Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: U-VLM introduces hierarchical vision–language modeling for 3D radiology report generation, combining progressive pretraining (segmentation→classification→reporting) with multi-layer visual injection from a U-Net encoder into corresponding language-model layers, achieving SOTA with a small 0.1B decoder and showing strong vision pretraining can outperform reliance on huge LMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated 3D radiology report generation is difficult; current VLMs (a) ignore powerful segmentation-pretrained encoders and (b) inject visual features only at the LM input, losing multi-scale information—limiting accuracy and clinical usefulness.

Method: U-VLM uses hierarchical design and training: (1) progressive pretraining across tasks—segmentation, then classification, then report generation—potentially on different datasets without unified labels; (2) multi-layer visual injection that routes multi-scale U-Net encoder features to matching layers of the language model, enabling richer cross-modal alignment.
