A compact 0.1B decoder LM is trained from scratch on top of the pretrained vision encoder.

Result: State-of-the-art on CT-RATE (F1 0.414 vs 0.258; BLEU-mean 0.349 vs 0.305) and AbdomenAtlas 3.0 (segmentation-based detection F1 0.624 vs 0.518), despite using only a 0.1B decoder. Ablations: progressive pretraining chiefly improves F1; multi-layer injection chiefly improves BLEU-mean.

Conclusion: Hierarchical training plus multi-layer cross-modal fusion yields markedly better 3D report generation. Careful vision-encoder pretraining and multi-scale feature injection can outweigh benefits from 7B+ pretrained LMs, enabling efficient, accurate systems; code is publicly available.

Abstract: Automated radiology report generation is key for reducing radiologist workload and improving diagnostic consistency, yet generating accurate reports for 3D medical imaging remains challenging. Existing vision-language models face two limitations: they do not leverage segmentation-pretrained encoders, and they inject visual features only at the input layer of language models, losing multi-scale information. We propose U-VLM, which enables hierarchical vision-language modeling in both training and architecture: (1) progressive training from segmentation to classification to report generation, and (2) multi-layer visual injection that routes U-Net encoder features to corresponding language model layers. Each training stage can leverage different datasets without unified annotations. U-VLM achieves state-of-the-art performance on CT-RATE (F1: 0.414 vs 0.258, BLEU-mean: 0.349 vs 0.305) and AbdomenAtlas 3.0 (F1: 0.624 vs 0.518 for segmentation-based detection) using only a 0.1B decoder trained from scratch, demonstrating that well-designed vision encoder pretraining outweighs the benefits of 7B+ pre-trained language models. Ablation studies show that progressive pretraining significantly improves F1, while multi-layer injection improves BLEU-mean. Code is available at https://github.com/yinghemedical/U-VLM.

</details>


### [75] [TokenCom: Vision-Language Model for Multimodal and Multitask Token Communications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00482)
*Feibo Jiang,Siwei Tu,Li Dong,Xiaolong Li,Kezhi Wang,Cunhua Pan,Zhu Han,Jiangzhou Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TaiChi is a VLM with dual visual tokenizers, bilateral attention fusion, and a KAN-based projector that yields compact, better-aligned visual tokens for efficient multimodal token communications, demonstrating superior performance and system feasibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLMs face coarse token granularity, excessively long visual token sequences that hinder efficiency (especially in communications), and weak cross‑modal alignment, motivating a framework that produces compact tokens and precise vision-to-text alignment for intelligent communication systems.

Method: TaiChi employs (1) a dual-visual tokenizer operating on high- and low-resolution inputs to capture pixel-level detail and global semantics, (2) a Bilateral Attention Network to fuse multi-scale tokens and compress them into informative, compact representations, and (3) a KAN-based modality projector with learnable activations to perform accurate nonlinear mapping from visual features into the text semantic space. TaiChi is integrated into a multimodal, multitask token communication system using a joint VLM–channel coding scheme.

Result: Experiments (details not provided in the abstract) show TaiChi outperforms baselines, produces more compact visual tokens, improves cross-modal alignment, and operates effectively within a token-based communication system, validating feasibility and performance gains.

Conclusion: TaiChi addresses key bottlenecks of VLMs—token granularity, sequence length, and cross-modal alignment—enabling efficient and accurate token-level multimodal communication; the integrated system appears effective, pending quantitative evidence.

Abstract: Visual-Language Models (VLMs), with their strong capabilities in image and text understanding, offer a solid foundation for intelligent communications. However, their effectiveness is constrained by limited token granularity, overlong visual token sequences, and inadequate cross-modal alignment. To overcome these challenges, we propose TaiChi, a novel VLM framework designed for token communications. TaiChi adopts a dual-visual tokenizer architecture that processes both high- and low-resolution images to collaboratively capture pixel-level details and global conceptual features. A Bilateral Attention Network (BAN) is introduced to intelligently fuse multi-scale visual tokens, thereby enhancing visual understanding and producing compact visual tokens. In addition, a Kolmogorov Arnold Network (KAN)-based modality projector with learnable activation functions is employed to achieve precise nonlinear alignment from visual features to the text semantic space, thus minimizing information loss. Finally, TaiChi is integrated into a multimodal and multitask token communication system equipped with a joint VLM-channel coding scheme. Experimental results validate the superior performance of TaiChi, as well as the feasibility and effectiveness of the TaiChi-driven token communication system.

</details>


### [76] [RAISE: Requirement-Adaptive Evolutionary Refinement for Training-Free Text-to-Image Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00483)
*Liyao Jiang,Ruichen Chen,Chao Gao,Di Niu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RAISE is a training-free, requirement-driven evolutionary framework that adaptively allocates inference-time compute for text-to-image diffusion, using checklist-based verification and diverse refinement actions to improve prompt-image alignment, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy with less compute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Text-to-image models still misalign with complex prompts (multiple objects, relations, attributes). Existing approaches either use fixed, non-adaptive inference budgets or require reflection datasets and heavy joint fine-tuning that can overfit and fail to transfer across models.

Method: Formulate generation as requirement-driven adaptive scaling. Maintain a population of candidate images and iteratively refine them via actions such as prompt rewriting, noise resampling, and instructional editing. After each generation, verify candidates against a structured requirement checklist to detect unmet items and focus further computation where needed. This enables multi-round, training-free, model-agnostic self-improvement at test time.

Result: On GenEval and DrawBench, RAISE reaches state-of-the-art alignment (0.94 overall on GenEval), while reducing generated samples by 30–40% and vision-language model (VLM) calls by ~80% compared to prior scaling and reflection-tuned baselines.

Conclusion: RAISE delivers efficient, generalizable, and model-agnostic adaptive test-time scaling for better prompt-image alignment without additional training or curated reflection datasets, demonstrating robust multi-round self-improvement. Code is available at the provided repository.

Abstract: Recent text-to-image (T2I) diffusion models achieve remarkable realism, yet faithful prompt-image alignment remains challenging, particularly for complex prompts with multiple objects, relations, and fine-grained attributes. Existing training-free inference-time scaling methods rely on fixed iteration budgets that cannot adapt to prompt difficulty, while reflection-tuned models require carefully curated reflection datasets and extensive joint fine-tuning of diffusion and vision-language models, often overfitting to reflection paths data and lacking transferability across models. We introduce RAISE (Requirement-Adaptive Self-Improving Evolution), a training-free, requirement-driven evolutionary framework for adaptive T2I generation. RAISE formulates image generation as a requirement-driven adaptive scaling process, evolving a population of candidates at inference time through a diverse set of refinement actions-including prompt rewriting, noise resampling, and instructional editing. Each generation is verified against a structured checklist of requirements, enabling the system to dynamically identify unsatisfied items and allocate further computation only where needed. This achieves adaptive test-time scaling that aligns computational effort with semantic query complexity. On GenEval and DrawBench, RAISE attains state-of-the-art alignment (0.94 overall GenEval) while incurring fewer generated samples (reduced by 30-40%) and VLM calls (reduced by 80%) than prior scaling and reflection-tuned baselines, demonstrating efficient, generalizable, and model-agnostic multi-round self-improvement. Code is available at https://github.com/LiyaoJiang1998/RAISE.

</details>


### [77] [Random Wins All: Rethinking Grouping Strategies for Vision Tokens](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00486)
*Qihang Fan,Yuang Ai,Huaibo Huang,Ran He*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper argues that complex token-grouping schemes for efficient Vision Transformers are unnecessary; a simple random grouping of tokens, under four key conditions, matches or outperforms sophisticated methods across vision and other modalities, with stronger gains on downstream tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-attention’s quadratic cost in Transformers motivates token grouping/pooling to reduce compute. Many crafted grouping strategies exist, but it’s unclear if their complexity is needed. The authors question necessity and seek a simple, unified alternative.

Method: Introduce random grouping: partition vision tokens into groups at random for intra-group attention or pooling. Evaluate on multiple ViT baselines and downstream tasks; analyze why it works, identifying four necessary design elements: (1) positional information, (2) head feature diversity, (3) global receptive field, (4) fixed grouping pattern. Extend experiments to point clouds and vision–language models.

Result: Across diverse baselines, random grouping nearly always outperforms other grouping strategies and shows larger gains when transferred to object detection. The approach generalizes across modalities, maintaining or improving accuracy while simplifying design.

Conclusion: Elaborate grouping heuristics are largely superfluous if four conditions are met; simple random grouping is an effective, unified strategy for efficient attention across vision and beyond. Code will be released for reproducibility.

Abstract: Since Transformers are introduced into vision architectures, their quadratic complexity has always been a significant issue that many research efforts aim to address. A representative approach involves grouping tokens, performing self-attention calculations within each group, or pooling the tokens within each group into a single token. To this end, various carefully designed grouping strategies have been proposed to enhance the performance of Vision Transformers. Here, we pose the following questions: \textbf{Are these carefully designed grouping methods truly necessary? Is there a simpler and more unified token grouping method that can replace these diverse methods?} Therefore, we propose the random grouping strategy, which involves a simple and fast random grouping strategy for vision tokens. We validate this approach on multiple baselines, and experiments show that random grouping almost outperforms all other grouping methods. When transferred to downstream tasks, such as object detection, random grouping demonstrates even more pronounced advantages. In response to this phenomenon, we conduct a detailed analysis of the advantages of random grouping from multiple perspectives and identify several crucial elements for the design of grouping strategies: positional information, head feature diversity, global receptive field, and fixed grouping pattern. We demonstrate that as long as these four conditions are met, vision tokens require only an extremely simple grouping strategy to efficiently and effectively handle various visual tasks. We also validate the effectiveness of our proposed random method across multiple modalities, including visual tasks, point cloud processing, and vision-language models. Code will be available at https://github.com/qhfan/random.

</details>


### [78] [ArtiFixer: Enhancing and Extending 3D Reconstruction with Auto-Regressive Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00492)
*Riccardo de Lutio,Tobias Fischer,Yen-Yu Chang,Yuxuan Zhang,Jay Zhangjie Wu,Xuanchi Ren,Tianchang Shen,Katarina Tothova,Zan Gojcic,Haithem Turki*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Two-stage generative pipeline for novel view synthesis that fixes poor extrapolation in under-observed regions while scaling to hundreds of views in one pass; achieves +1–3 dB PSNR over prior SOTA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Per-scene methods (e.g., 3D Gaussian Splatting) excel in well-observed areas but extrapolate poorly. Generative-prior methods could help yet are limited by (1) scalability—diffusion/bidirectional video models can produce only few views per pass, needing costly iterative distillation for consistency—and (2) quality—outputs often conflict with observed content and fail in unseen regions.

Method: Stage 1: Train a strong bidirectional generative model with a novel opacity mixing strategy that balances adherence to observed content with the capacity to hallucinate plausible content in unobserved regions. Stage 2: Distill this model into a causal autoregressive generator that produces hundreds of frames in a single pass. The distilled model can directly render novel views or act as pseudo-supervision to refine the underlying 3D representation efficiently.

Result: The approach yields plausible reconstructions where prior methods fail and surpasses all baselines on common benchmarks, improving PSNR by 1–3 dB.

Conclusion: By combining opacity-aware bidirectional generation with efficient autoregressive distillation, the method resolves both scalability and quality issues in generative-augmented view synthesis, enabling consistent, large-batch novel view generation and stronger 3D reconstructions that set a new state of the art.

Abstract: Per-scene optimization methods such as 3D Gaussian Splatting provide state-of-the-art novel view synthesis quality but extrapolate poorly to under-observed areas. Methods that leverage generative priors to correct artifacts in these areas hold promise but currently suffer from two shortcomings. The first is scalability, as existing methods use image diffusion models or bidirectional video models that are limited in the number of views they can generate in a single pass (and thus require a costly iterative distillation process for consistency). The second is quality itself, as generators used in prior work tend to produce outputs that are inconsistent with existing scene content and fail entirely in completely unobserved regions. To solve these, we propose a two-stage pipeline that leverages two key insights. First, we train a powerful bidirectional generative model with a novel opacity mixing strategy that encourages consistency with existing observations while retaining the model's ability to extrapolate novel content in unseen areas. Second, we distill it into a causal auto-regressive model that generates hundreds of frames in a single pass. This model can directly produce novel views or serve as pseudo-supervision to improve the underlying 3D representation in a simple and highly efficient manner. We evaluate our method extensively and demonstrate that it can generate plausible reconstructions in scenarios where existing approaches fail completely. When measured on commonly benchmarked datasets, we outperform existing all existing baselines by a wide margin, exceeding prior state-of-the-art methods by 1-3 dB PSNR.

</details>


### [79] [COG: Confidence-aware Optimal Geometric Correspondence for Unsupervised Single-reference Novel Object Pose Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00493)
*Yuchen Che,Jingtu Wu,Hao Zheng,Asako Kanezaki*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: COG reframes cross-view matching for 6DoF pose as a confidence-weighted optimal transport problem, producing soft, balanced correspondences regularized by foundation-model semantics; this enables stable, largely unsupervised pose estimation from a single reference view, matching or surpassing supervised baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Estimating 6DoF pose of novel objects from a single reference view is brittle due to occlusion, viewpoint changes, and outliers. Discrete one-to-one matching is non-differentiable, collapses to sparse keypoints, and mishandles non-overlapping regions—motivating a robust, differentiable, dense, and confidence-aware correspondence mechanism.

Method: Formulate correspondence estimation as optimal transport with network-predicted point-wise confidences injected as marginals to suppress non-overlapping regions and balance mass. Produce soft, many-to-many correspondences and regularize them using semantic priors from vision foundation models. Integrate confidence into both correspondence estimation and pose solving, enabling unsupervised training via geometric objectives.

Result: Unsupervised COG attains performance comparable to supervised approaches; when trained with supervision, COG surpasses existing supervised methods. The resulting correspondences and poses are more stable under occlusions and viewpoint changes.

Conclusion: Confidence-aware optimal transport, augmented with semantic priors, yields robust, differentiable, and balanced correspondences that enable effective unsupervised learning and state-of-the-art 6DoF pose estimation from a single reference view, particularly in challenging, partially overlapping scenarios.

Abstract: Estimating the 6DoF pose of a novel object with a single reference view is challenging due to occlusions, view-point changes, and outliers. A core difficulty lies in finding robust cross-view correspondences, as existing methods often rely on discrete one-to-one matching that is non-differentiable and tends to collapse onto sparse key-points. We propose Confidence-aware Optimal Geometric Correspondence (COG), an unsupervised framework that formulates correspondence estimation as a confidence-aware optimal transport problem. COG produces balanced soft correspondences by predicting point-wise confidences and injecting them as optimal transport marginals, suppressing non-overlapping regions. Semantic priors from vision foundation models further regularize the correspondences, leading to stable pose estimation. This design integrates confidence into the correspondence finding and pose estimation pipeline, enabling unsupervised learning. Experiments show unsupervised COG achieves comparable performance to supervised methods, and supervised COG outperforms them.

</details>


### [80] [M$^2$: Dual-Memory Augmentation for Long-Horizon Web Agents via Trajectory Summarization and Insight Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00503)
*Dawei Yan,Haokui Zhang,Guangda Huzhang,Yang Li,Yibo Wang,Qing-Guo Chen,Zhao Xu,Weihua Luo,Ying Li,Wei Dong,Chunhua Shen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: M^2 is a training‑free, dual‑memory augmentation for multimodal LLM web agents that compresses interaction history and retrieves actionable insights, boosting long‑horizon navigation success while cutting tokens and compute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long‑horizon web tasks overwhelm MLLM agents: context grows, token/compute costs spike, and reasoning degrades. Prevailing methods depend on heavy data collection and training yet still falter on complex, extended scenarios. A training‑free approach that improves context efficiency and decision robustness is needed.

Method: A dual‑tier memory framework: (1) Internal Memory via Dynamic Trajectory Summarization condenses verbose interaction histories into succinct state updates; (2) External Memory via Insight Retrieval Augmentation pulls actionable guidelines from an offline insight bank to guide decisions. The framework is training‑free and aims to be plug‑and‑play, optimizing context length and robustness.

Result: On WebVoyager and OnlineMind2Web, M^2 outperforms baselines: up to +19.6% success rate and −58.7% token usage for Qwen3‑VL‑32B; proprietary models (e.g., Claude) gain up to +12.5% accuracy with notably lower computational overhead.

Conclusion: Training‑free memory augmentation substantially improves long‑horizon web navigation by enhancing reasoning and context handling while reducing compute. M^2 appears model‑agnostic and practical, suggesting a cost‑effective path to more robust autonomous web agents.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) based agents have demonstrated remarkable potential in autonomous web navigation. However, handling long-horizon tasks remains a critical bottleneck. Prevailing strategies often rely heavily on extensive data collection and model training, yet still struggle with high computational costs and insufficient reasoning capabilities when facing complex, long-horizon scenarios. To address this, we propose M$^2$, a training-free, memory-augmented framework designed to optimize context efficiency and decision-making robustness. Our approach incorporates a dual-tier memory mechanism that synergizes Dynamic Trajectory Summarization (Internal Memory) to compress verbose interaction history into concise state updates, and Insight Retrieval Augmentation (External Memory) to guide the agent with actionable guidelines retrieved from an offline insight bank. Extensive evaluations across WebVoyager and OnlineMind2Web demonstrate that M$^2$ consistently surpasses baselines, yielding up to a 19.6% success rate increase and 58.7% token reduction for Qwen3-VL-32B, while proprietary models like Claude achieve accuracy gains up to 12.5% alongside significantly lower computational overhead.

</details>


### [81] [Hierarchical Classification for Improved Histopathology Image Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00504)
*Keunho Byeon,Jinsol Song,Seong Min Hong,Yosep Chong,Jin Tae Kwak*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HiClass is a MIL-based hierarchical WSI classifier that enables bidirectional information flow between coarse- and fine-grained levels and uses hierarchy-aware losses, yielding superior performance on a gastric biopsy dataset with 4 coarse and 14 fine classes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Flat classifiers ignore known label hierarchies in pathology WSIs, limiting their ability to capture both coarse and fine histopathological patterns needed for reliable diagnosis.

Method: Build on multiple instance learning and introduce bidirectional feature integration between coarse- and fine-level representations. Train with hierarchy-tailored objectives: hierarchical consistency loss (align coarse/fine predictions), intra-/inter-class distance loss (compactness/separability within and across levels), and group-wise cross-entropy (optimize class groups under parents).

Result: On a gastric biopsy dataset (4 coarse, 14 fine classes), HiClass achieves superior accuracy for both coarse-grained and fine-grained classification compared with existing methods (no specific metrics provided in the abstract).

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling label hierarchies and enforcing consistency across levels improves WSI classification by capturing multi-scale histopathological characteristics; HiClass offers a practical path to better diagnostic performance in pathology.

Abstract: Whole-slide image analysis is essential for diagnostic tasks in pathology, yet existing deep learning methods primarily rely on flat classification, ignoring hierarchical relationships among class labels. In this study, we propose HiClass, a hierarchical classification framework for improved histopathology image analysis, that enhances both coarse-grained and fine-grained WSI classification. Built based upon a multiple instance learning approach, HiClass extends it by introducing bidirectional feature integration that facilitates information exchange between coarse-grained and fine-grained feature representations, effectively learning hierarchical features. Moreover, we introduce tailored loss functions, including hierarchical consistency loss, intra- and inter-class distance loss, and group-wise cross-entropy loss, to further optimize hierarchical learning. We assess the performance of HiClass on a gastric biopsy dataset with 4 coarse-grained and 14 fine-grained classes, achieving superior classification performance for both coarse-grained classification and fine-grained classification. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of HiClass in improving WSI classification by capturing coarse-grained and fine-grained histopathological characteristics.

</details>


### [82] [What Do Visual Tokens Really Encode? Uncovering Sparsity and Redundancy in Multimodal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00510)
*Yingqi Fan,Junlong Tong,Anhao Zhao,Xiaoyu Shen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They probe multimodal LLMs with a tool (EmbedLens) and find strong semantic sparsity in visual tokens: only ~60% “alive” tokens carry image-specific meaning already before entering the LLM. For most tasks, internal visual computations are largely redundant; for highly vision-centric tasks, injecting these alive tokens at mid LLM layers is sufficient. This enables pruning, reduced computation, and simpler architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The internal structure and processing of visual tokens in MLLMs are opaque. Understanding where and how visual semantics are represented could improve interpretability and enable more efficient inference/training by avoiding unnecessary computation.

Method: Introduce a probing framework (EmbedLens) to analyze visual token embeddings and their propagation. Categorize visual tokens into sink, dead, and alive classes. Use a targeted patch-compression benchmark to test whether alive tokens already encode fine-grained cues (objects, colors, OCR) pre-LLM. Examine layer-wise alignment to see at which LLM depths visual information is effectively used and whether mid-layer injection suffices.

Result: Visual tokens partition consistently: only the ~60% alive tokens contain image-specific semantics; sink/dead tokens do not. Alive tokens already capture rich fine-grained cues before LLM processing. For most standard tasks, visual attention/FFN computations are redundant. For a small set of vision-centric tasks, the useful visual signals align with intermediate LLM layers; shallow-layer processing is unnecessary, and direct mid-layer injection of alive tokens works well.

Conclusion: Visual token processing in MLLMs is semantically sparse and largely front-loaded in the vision encoder. Efficient, interpretable designs are possible via selective token pruning, minimizing internal visual computation, and injecting visual features at mid LLM layers rather than at the input.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) project visual tokens into the embedding space of language models, yet the internal structuring and processing of visual semantics remain poorly understood. In this work, we introduce a two-fold analytical framework featuring a novel probing tool, $\textbf{EmbedLens}$, to conduct a fine-grained analysis. We uncover a pronounced semantic sparsity at the input level: visual tokens consistently partition into sink, dead, and alive categories. Remarkably, only the alive tokens, comprising $\approx60\%$ of the total input, carry image-specific meaning. Furthermore, using a targeted patch-compression benchmark, we demonstrate that these alive tokens already encode rich, fine-grained cues (e.g., objects, colors, and OCR) prior to entering the LLM. Internal visual computations (such as visual attention and feed-forward networks) are redundant for most standard tasks. For the small subset of highly vision-centric tasks that actually benefit from internal processing, we reveal that alive tokens naturally align with intermediate LLM layers rather than the initial embedding space, indicating that shallow-layer processing is unnecessary and that direct mid-layer injection is both sufficient. Ultimately, our findings provide a unified mechanistic view of visual token processing, paving the way for more efficient and interpretable MLLM architectures through selective token pruning, minimized visual computation, and mid-layer injection. The code is released at: https://github.com/EIT-NLP/EmbedLens.

</details>


### [83] [Multimodal Adaptive Retrieval Augmented Generation through Internal Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00511)
*Ruoshuang Du,Xin Sun,Qiang Liu,Bowen Song,Zhongqi Chen,Weiqiang Wang,Liang Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MMA-RAG is an adaptive multimodal RAG framework for VQA that decides, via a confidence-based classifier over internal visual–text representations, when to use retrieved evidence, reducing hallucinations and improving accuracy on three datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VQA models hallucinate by producing answers misaligned with images or facts. Standard RAG helps but static retrieval can insert irrelevant or conflicting evidence—especially in vision, where visually similar yet semantically wrong items are retrieved. There is a need to selectively trust internal knowledge vs. external retrieval.

Method: Introduce Multimodal Adaptive RAG (MMA-RAG) that dynamically gates the use of external knowledge. A decision classifier, trained with layer-wise analysis of joint visual and textual representations, estimates confidence in the model’s internal knowledge and determines whether to incorporate reverse image–retrieved content into generation.

Result: On three VQA datasets, MMA-RAG yields significant performance gains over baselines. Ablation studies show that using internal representations for the gating decision is crucial to the improvements.

Conclusion: Adaptive, confidence-aware retrieval mitigates hallucinations and balances external knowledge use with inference robustness, leading to better VQA performance across diverse multimodal scenarios.

Abstract: Visual Question Answering systems face reliability issues due to hallucinations, where models generate answers misaligned with visual input or factual knowledge. While Retrieval Augmented Generation frameworks mitigate this issue by incorporating external knowledge, static retrieval often introduces irrelevant or conflicting content, particularly in visual RAG settings where visually similar but semantically incorrect evidence may be retrieved. To address this, we propose Multimodal Adaptive RAG (MMA-RAG), which dynamically assesses the confidence in the internal knowledge of the model to decide whether to incorporate the retrieved external information into the generation process. Central to MMA-RAG is a decision classifier trained through a layer-wise analysis, which leverages joint internal visual and textual representations to guide the use of reverse image retrieval. Experiments demonstrated that the model achieves a significant improvement in response performance in three VQA datasets. Meanwhile, ablation studies highlighted the importance of internal representations in adaptive retrieval decisions. In general, the experimental results demonstrated that MMA-RAG effectively balances external knowledge utilization and inference robustness in diverse multimodal scenarios.

</details>


### [84] [Wavelet-based Frame Selection by Detecting Semantic Boundary for Long Video Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00512)
*Wang Chen,Yuhui Zeng,Yongdong Luo,Tianyu Xie,Luojun Lin,Jiayi Ji,Yan Zhang,Xiawu Zheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Training-free, wavelet-based frame selection for LVLMs that detects semantic boundaries from denoised query–frame similarity, segments videos into coherent clips, allocates frame budgets per clip, and picks diverse/relevant frames via MMR, yielding consistent SOTA gains on long-video benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing frame selectors favor per-frame query relevance and ignore narrative continuity; abrupt-change detection on similarity signals is noisy due to model uncertainty and transient visuals. There is a need to robustly capture semantic shifts under limited LVLM context windows.

Method: Compute query–frame similarity across the video; apply a wavelet transform to obtain a multi-scale decomposition; use the coarsest-scale (low-frequency) component to derive a clean semantic-change signal; detect local extrema as semantic boundaries to segment the video into coherent clips. Then: (1) adaptively allocate a frame budget to each clip via a composite importance score; (2) within each clip, select frames using Maximal Marginal Relevance to balance relevance and diversity. Entire pipeline is training-free.

Result: On VideoMME, MLVU, and LongVideoBench, WFS-SB improves LVLM accuracy by +5.5%, +9.5%, and +6.2%, respectively, outperforming prior SOTA across evaluations.

Conclusion: Modeling and exploiting semantic boundaries—rather than only high-relevance frames—yields better long-video understanding for LVLMs. Wavelet-based multi-resolution analysis provides robust boundary detection, and the two-stage allocation+MMR strategy translates these boundaries into superior frame subsets and consistent performance gains.

Abstract: Frame selection is crucial due to high frame redundancy and limited context windows when applying Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) to long videos. Current methods typically select frames with high relevance to a given query, resulting in a disjointed set of frames that disregard the narrative structure of video. In this paper, we introduce Wavelet-based Frame Selection by Detecting Semantic Boundary (WFS-SB), a training-free framework that presents a new perspective: effective video understanding hinges not only on high relevance but, more importantly, on capturing semantic shifts - pivotal moments of narrative change that are essential to comprehending the holistic storyline of video. However, direct detection of abrupt changes in the query-frame similarity signal is often unreliable due to high-frequency noise arising from model uncertainty and transient visual variations. To address this, we leverage the wavelet transform, which provides an ideal solution through its multi-resolution analysis in both time and frequency domains. By applying this transform, we decompose the noisy signal into multiple scales and extract a clean semantic change signal from the coarsest scale. We identify the local extrema of this signal as semantic boundaries, which segment the video into coherent clips. Building on this, WFS-SB comprises a two-stage strategy: first, adaptively allocating a frame budget to each clip based on a composite importance score; and second, within each clip, employing the Maximal Marginal Relevance approach to select a diverse yet relevant set of frames. Extensive experiments show that WFS-SB significantly boosts LVLM performance, e.g., improving accuracy by 5.5% on VideoMME, 9.5% on MLVU, and 6.2% on LongVideoBench, consistently outperforming state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [85] [MLLM-4D: Towards Visual-based Spatial-Temporal Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00515)
*Xingyilang Yin,Chengzhengxu Li,Jiahao Chang,Chi-Man Pun,Xiaodong Cun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MLLM-4D introduces a data+post-training framework that converts stereo video corpora into 4D spatiotemporal instruction data and trains MLLMs via SFT followed by GRPO with spatiotemporal CoT and rewards, achieving state-of-the-art 4D understanding and reasoning from 2D RGB without changing architecture, plus releasing datasets and a benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humans naturally reason about how 3D scenes evolve over time (4D), but current MLLMs struggle with such spatiotemporal understanding due to gaps in suitable training data and effective post-training strategies. The work aims to close these gaps to unlock robust 4D reasoning from visual inputs.

Method: 1) A cost-efficient pipeline repurposes stereo video datasets into high-quality 4D instructional data. They curate MLLM4D-2M (for SFT) and MLLM4D-R1-30k (for RFT) and build MLLM4D-Bench for evaluation. 2) Post-training starts with SFT to instill basic 4D understanding, then applies Group Relative Policy Optimization with Spatiotemporal Chain-of-Thought prompting and Spatiotemporal reward functions to enhance reasoning—without modifying the base MLLM architecture.

Result: Across extensive experiments, the approach reaches state-of-the-art performance on spatial-temporal understanding and reasoning tasks using only 2D RGB inputs. The paper reports strong gains attributed to the curated data and the GRPO+ST-CoT+ST-reward training recipe.

Conclusion: A data curation plus policy-optimization post-training recipe can substantially elevate MLLMs’ 4D spatiotemporal competence without architectural changes. The released datasets and benchmark provide a foundation for further progress and standardized evaluation.

Abstract: Humans are born with vision-based 4D spatial-temporal intelligence, which enables us to perceive and reason about the evolution of 3D space over time from purely visual inputs. Despite its importance, this capability remains a significant bottleneck for current multimodal large language models (MLLMs). To tackle this challenge, we introduce MLLM-4D, a comprehensive framework designed to bridge the gaps in training data curation and model post-training for spatiotemporal understanding and reasoning. On the data front, we develop a cost-efficient data curation pipeline that repurposes existing stereo video datasets into high-quality 4D spatiotemporal instructional data. This results in the MLLM4D-2M and MLLM4D-R1-30k datasets for Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (RFT), alongside MLLM4D-Bench for comprehensive evaluation. Regarding model training, our post-training strategy establishes a foundational 4D understanding via SFT and further catalyzes 4D reasoning capabilities by employing Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with specialized Spatiotemporal Chain of Thought (ST-CoT) prompting and Spatiotemporal reward functions (ST-reward) without involving the modification of architecture. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MLLM-4D achieves state-of-the-art spatial-temporal understanding and reasoning capabilities from purely 2D RGB inputs. Project page: https://github.com/GVCLab/MLLM-4D.

</details>


### [86] [Vision-TTT: Efficient and Expressive Visual Representation Learning with Test-Time Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00518)
*Quan Kong,Yanru Xiao,Yuhao Shen,Cong Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Vision-TTT is a linear-time alternative to ViT self-attention that compresses visual tokens via a self-supervised, bidirectional scan augmented with Conv2d, achieving competitive ImageNet accuracy while drastically reducing FLOPs, latency, and memory—positioning it as an efficient, scalable visual backbone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-attention in Vision Transformers scales quadratically with token count, making high-resolution inference expensive. The field needs backbones that preserve global context and accuracy while improving computational and memory efficiency.

Method: Introduce a linear-time sequence modeling approach (TTT) to vision that compresses token sequences in a self-supervised way. Use a bidirectional scan to capture long-range dependencies and add Conv2d modules to model 2D spatial correlations, yielding global receptive fields without quadratic attention. Provide model variants (Vittt-T/S/B).

Result: On ImageNet, Vittt-T/S/B achieve 77.3%/81.2%/82.5% Top-1. At 1280×1280, Vittt-T reduces FLOPs by 79.4%, runs 4.38× faster, and uses 88.9% less memory than DeiT-T; also outperforms counterparts on multiple downstream tasks.

Conclusion: Vision-TTT delivers strong accuracy–efficiency trade-offs and scales well to high resolutions, making it a promising candidate to replace quadratic self-attention as a general-purpose visual backbone.

Abstract: Learning efficient and expressive visual representation has long been the pursuit of computer vision research. While Vision Transformers (ViTs) gradually replace traditional Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) as more scalable vision learners, their applications are plagued by the quadratic complexity of the self-attention mechanism. To address the challenge, we introduce a new linear-time sequence modeling method Test-Time Training (TTT) into vision and propose Vision-TTT, which compresses the visual token sequence in a novel self-supervised learning manner. By incorporating bidirectional scan strategy and the Conv2d module, Vision-TTT effectively extends vanilla TTT to model 2D visual correlations with global receptive fields. Extensive experiments show that \texttt{Vittt-T/S/B} achieve 77.3%,81.2%,82.5% Top-1 accuracy on ImageNet classification and also greatly outperform their counterparts on downstream tasks. At 1280x1280 resolution, \texttt{Vittt-T} reduces FLOPs by 79.4% and runs 4.38x faster with 88.9% less memory than DeiT-T. These results demonstrate the expressiveness and efficiency of Vision-TTT as a strong candidate for the next-generation generic visual backbone.

</details>


### [87] [Jano: Adaptive Diffusion Generation with Early-stage Convergence Awareness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00519)
*Yuyang Chen,Linqian Zeng,Yijin ZHou,Hengjie Li,Jidong Zhai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Jano is a training-free, region-aware inference framework for Diffusion Transformers that detects which spatial tokens converge early and adaptively allocates compute, delivering ~2× speedups (up to 2.4×) without harming quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion Transformers are compute-heavy due to full attention and uniform processing across all tokens/regions, even though different regions in generated content converge at different rates. Leveraging this heterogeneity could cut unnecessary computation.

Method: Identify regional convergence needs early in the denoising process via an early-stage complexity recognition algorithm, then use an adaptive token scheduling runtime to allocate computational resources (e.g., attention/steps) preferentially to harder regions while reducing effort on easier ones. The approach is training-free and operates at inference time.

Result: Across state-of-the-art diffusion models, Jano achieves substantial acceleration with an average 2.0× speedup and up to 2.4×, while preserving generation quality.

Conclusion: Region-aware, adaptive computation during diffusion denoising effectively accelerates DiTs without retraining, challenging the convention of uniform processing and offering a practical path to large-scale, efficient generation.

Abstract: Diffusion models have achieved remarkable success in generative AI, yet their computational efficiency remains a significant challenge, particularly for Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) requiring intensive full-attention computation. While existing acceleration approaches focus on content-agnostic uniform optimization strategies, we observe that different regions in generated content exhibit heterogeneous convergence patterns during the denoising process. We present Jano, a training-free framework that leverages this insight for efficient region-aware generation. Jano introduces an early-stage complexity recognition algorithm that accurately identifies regional convergence requirements within initial denoising steps, coupled with an adaptive token scheduling runtime that optimizes computational resource allocation. Through comprehensive evaluation on state-of-the-art models, Jano achieves substantial acceleration (average 2.0 times speedup, up to 2.4 times) while preserving generation quality. Our work challenges conventional uniform processing assumptions and provides a practical solution for accelerating large-scale content generation. The source code of our implementation is available at https://github.com/chen-yy20/Jano.

</details>


### [88] [Mesh-Pro: Asynchronous Advantage-guided Ranking Preference Optimization for Artist-style Quadrilateral Mesh Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00526)
*Zhen Zhou,Jian Liu,Biwen Lei,Jing Xu,Haohan Weng,Yiling Zhu,Zhuo Chen,Junfeng Fan,Yunkai Ma,Dazhao Du,Song Guo,Fengshui Jing,Chunchao Guo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Async online RL + ARPO + Mesh‑Pro (mesh‑aware tokenization and ray‑based reward) delivers 3.75× faster training than synchronous RL and achieves SOTA on artistic and dense 3D meshes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bring RL’s gains from text/image to 3D mesh generation, overcoming the inefficiency and weak generalization of offline DPO‑based methods; need mesh‑specific representations and reward signals that better capture geometric integrity.

Method: (1) First asynchronous online RL framework tailored to 3D mesh generation. (2) Advantage‑guided Ranking Preference Optimization (ARPO) to balance efficiency and generalization versus DPO/GRPO. (3) Mesh‑Pro built on asynchronous ARPO with diagonal‑aware mixed triangular‑quadrilateral tokenization and a ray‑based geometric‑integrity reward.

Result: Asynchronous framework is 3.75× faster than synchronous RL; ARPO achieves a better efficiency‑generalization trade‑off than DPO/GRPO; Mesh‑Pro attains SOTA on artistic and dense mesh benchmarks.

Conclusion: Asynchronous online RL combined with ARPO and mesh‑aware representation/reward substantially improves both training efficiency and generation quality for 3D meshes, establishing new SOTA and underscoring RL’s promise for 3D generation.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has demonstrated remarkable success in text and image generation, yet its potential in 3D generation remains largely unexplored. Existing attempts typically rely on offline direct preference optimization (DPO) method, which suffers from low training efficiency and limited generalization. In this work, we aim to enhance both the training efficiency and generation quality of RL in 3D mesh generation. Specifically, (1) we design the first asynchronous online RL framework tailored for 3D mesh generation post-training efficiency improvement, which is 3.75$\times$ faster than synchronous RL. (2) We propose Advantage-guided Ranking Preference Optimization (ARPO), a novel RL algorithm that achieves a better trade-off between training efficiency and generalization than current RL algorithms designed for 3D mesh generation, such as DPO and group relative policy optimization (GRPO). (3) Based on asynchronous ARPO, we propose Mesh-Pro, which additionally introduces a novel diagonal-aware mixed triangular-quadrilateral tokenization for mesh representation and a ray-based reward for geometric integrity. Mesh-Pro achieves state-of-the-art performance on artistic and dense meshes.

</details>


### [89] [TP-Spikformer: Token Pruned Spiking Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00527)
*Wenjie Wei,Xiaolong Zhou,Malu Zhang,Ammar Belatreche,Qian Sun,Yimeng Shan,Dehao Zhang,Zijian Zhou,Zeyu Ma,Yang Yang,Haizhou Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TP-Spikformer is a training-free token-pruning framework for spiking transformers that scores tokens by spatiotemporal importance and applies block-level early stopping to low-importance tokens, cutting compute and storage while keeping accuracy competitive across tasks and backbones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spiking neural networks are energy-efficient, but recent spiking transformers chase accuracy with large, compute-heavy models that are hard to deploy on resource-constrained devices. There is a need to reduce computation and memory without retraining or sacrificing much accuracy.

Method: Introduce a heuristic spatiotemporal information-retaining criterion to score token importance; retain high-scoring tokens and apply a block-level early stopping strategy to low-scoring tokens (instead of hard deletion) to better preserve information. The framework plugs into various spiking transformer backbones and operates in a training-free manner.

Result: Across multiple spiking transformer architectures (Spikformer, QKFormer, Spike-driven Transformer V1/V3) and tasks (image classification, object detection, semantic segmentation, event-based object tracking), TP-Spikformer reduces storage and computation while maintaining competitive performance, demonstrating effectiveness, efficiency, and scalability—without additional training.

Conclusion: TP-Spikformer offers a practical, efficient path to deploy spiking transformers on resource-limited hardware, showing that informed, information-preserving token pruning with early stopping can yield significant resource savings with minimal accuracy loss and broad applicability.

Abstract: Spiking neural networks (SNNs) offer an energy-efficient alternative to traditional neural networks due to their event-driven computing paradigm. However, recent advancements in spiking transformers have focused on improving accuracy with large-scale architectures, which require significant computational resources and limit deployment on resource-constrained devices. In this paper, we propose a simple yet effective token pruning method for spiking transformers, termed TP-Spikformer, that reduces storage and computational overhead while maintaining competitive performance. Specifically, we first introduce a heuristic spatiotemporal information-retaining criterion that comprehensively evaluates tokens' importance, assigning higher scores to informative tokens for retention and lower scores to uninformative ones for pruning. Based on this criterion, we propose an information-retaining token pruning framework that employs a block-level early stopping strategy for uninformative tokens, instead of removing them outright. This also helps preserve more information during token pruning. We demonstrate the effectiveness, efficiency and scalability of TP-Spikformer through extensive experiments across diverse architectures, including Spikformer, QKFormer and Spike-driven Transformer V1 and V3, and a range of tasks such as image classification, object detection, semantic segmentation and event-based object tracking. Particularly, TP-Spikformer performs well in a training-free manner. These results reveal its potential as an efficient and practical solution for deploying SNNs in real-world applications with limited computational resources.

</details>


### [90] [CaptionFool: Universal Image Captioning Model Attacks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00529)
*Swapnil Parekh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CaptionFool is a universal, input-agnostic adversarial attack that edits about 1.2% of image patches to force transformer-based captioning models to output attacker-chosen captions with 94–96% success, including harmful text and slang that evades moderation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern image captioning systems are trained on large image–text corpora and are widely deployed, making their vulnerability to adversarial manipulation a pressing safety and security concern—especially for generating harmful or filter-evading content.

Method: Learn a universal perturbation that modifies only 7 of 577 image patches in transformer captioners, steering outputs toward arbitrary target captions. The attack is input-agnostic, works across images, and includes crafting “slang” terms intended to bypass content-moderation filters.

Result: With only sparse patch edits (~1.2% of the image), the attack achieves 94–96% targeted success and can induce offensive or policy-evading captions, demonstrating practical, scalable manipulation of state-of-the-art captioning models.

Conclusion: Transformer-based captioners have critical robustness gaps: tiny, universal image perturbations can reliably hijack captions and evade moderation. Stronger defenses—robust training, detection, and content-safety mechanisms—are urgently needed.

Abstract: Image captioning models are encoder-decoder architectures trained on large-scale image-text datasets, making them susceptible to adversarial attacks. We present CaptionFool, a novel universal (input-agnostic) adversarial attack against state-of-the-art transformer-based captioning models. By modifying only 7 out of 577 image patches (approximately 1.2% of the image), our attack achieves 94-96% success rate in generating arbitrary target captions, including offensive content. We further demonstrate that CaptionFool can generate "slang" terms specifically designed to evade existing content moderation filters. Our findings expose critical vulnerabilities in deployed vision-language models and underscore the urgent need for robust defenses against such attacks. Warning: This paper contains model outputs which are offensive in nature.

</details>


### [91] [RAFM: Retrieval-Augmented Flow Matching for Unpaired CBCT-to-CT Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00535)
*Xianhao Zhou,Jianghao Wu,Lanfeng Zhong,Ku Zhao,Jinlong He,Shaoting Zhang,Guotai Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RAFM introduces retrieval-augmented rectified flow for unpaired CBCT→CT translation, using DINOv3 features and a global CT memory bank to build semantically aligned pseudo-pairs, stabilizing training and achieving state-of-the-art sCT quality on SynthRAD2023 across multiple metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CBCT is common in radiotherapy but has artifacts and unreliable HUs, limiting dose calculation. Generating sCT from CBCT helps, yet paired CBCT–CT data are rare or unreliable due to timing gaps, anatomy changes, and registration errors. While rectified flow could enable unpaired learning, small datasets and batch sizes in medical imaging make naive pseudo-pairing unstable due to semantic mismatches.

Method: Adapt rectified flow to the medical unpaired setting by Retrieval-Augmented Flow Matching (RAFM): compute high-level features with a frozen DINOv3 encoder and retrieve nearest CT exemplars from a global memory bank to form retrieval-guided pseudo pairs. This improves distribution coupling and stabilizes deterministic flow-based training versus random or batch-local pairing.

Result: On SynthRAD2023 with a strict subject-level true-unpaired protocol, RAFM outperforms prior methods on FID, MAE, SSIM, PSNR, and SegScore, indicating better visual fidelity, HU accuracy, structural similarity, and segmentation consistency.

Conclusion: Retrieval-guided pseudo pairing makes rectified flow practical for unpaired CBCT→CT translation on small medical datasets, yielding more reliable sCT. The approach generalizes the RF framework to clinical constraints and is released with code for reproducibility.

Abstract: Cone-beam CT (CBCT) is routinely acquired in radiotherapy but suffers from severe artifacts and unreliable Hounsfield Unit (HU) values, limiting its direct use for dose calculation. Synthetic CT (sCT) generation from CBCT is therefore an important task, yet paired CBCT--CT data are often unavailable or unreliable due to temporal gaps, anatomical variation, and registration errors. In this work, we introduce rectified flow (RF) into unpaired CBCT-to-CT translation in medical imaging. Although RF is theoretically compatible with unpaired learning through distribution-level coupling and deterministic transport, its practical effectiveness under small medical datasets and limited batch sizes remains underexplored. Direct application with random or batch-local pseudo pairing can produce unstable supervision due to semantically mismatched endpoint samples. To address this challenge, we propose Retrieval-Augmented Flow Matching (RAFM), which adapts RF to the medical setting by constructing retrieval-guided pseudo pairs using a frozen DINOv3 encoder and a global CT memory bank. This strategy improves empirical coupling quality and stabilizes unpaired flow-based training. Experiments on SynthRAD2023 under a strict subject-level true-unpaired protocol show that RAFM outperforms existing methods across FID, MAE, SSIM, PSNR, and SegScore. The code is available at https://github.com/HiLab-git/RAFM.git.

</details>


### [92] [Adaptive Dynamic Dehazing via Instruction-Driven and Task-Feedback Closed-Loop Optimization for Diverse Downstream Task Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00542)
*Yafei Zhang,Shuaitian Song,Huafeng Li,Shujuan Wang,Yu Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Adaptive, closed‑loop dehazing that uses downstream task feedback and text instructions to adjust outputs at inference, improving multi‑task performance without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional dehazing focuses on visual clarity, which can conflict with diverse downstream task needs (e.g., detection, segmentation). Real deployments need dehazing that is task-aware, adjustable, and controllable without retraining.

Method: A framework that combines: (1) a multi-task feedback loop to evaluate downstream performance and dynamically modulate dehazing outputs, and (2) a text-instruction interface so users can specify high-level task preferences. This enables closed-loop, instruction-guided refinement during inference to tailor outputs per task post-training.

Result: Extensive experiments across various vision tasks indicate strong effectiveness, robustness, and generalizability, with improved performance and adaptability over static dehazing approaches (no specific metrics provided in the abstract).

Conclusion: Introduces an interactive, task-adaptive dehazing paradigm that collaborates with downstream applications, delivering customizable dehazing for multiple tasks without additional training.

Abstract: In real-world vision systems,haze removal is required not only to enhance image visibility but also to meet the specific needs of diverse downstream tasks.To address this challenge,we propose a novel adaptive dynamic dehazing framework that incorporates a closed-loop optimization mechanism.It enables feedback-driven refinement based on downstream task performance and user instruction-guided adjustment during inference,allowing the model to satisfy the specific requirements of multiple downstream tasks without retraining.Technically,our framework integrates two complementary and innovative mechanisms: (1)a task feedback loop that dynamically modulates dehazing outputs based on performance across multiple downstream tasks,and (2) a text instruction interface that allows users to specify high-level task preferences.This dual-guidance strategy enables the model to adapt its dehazing behavior after training,tailoring outputs in real time to the evolving needs of multiple tasks.Extensive experiments across various vision tasks demonstrate the strong effectiveness,robustness,and generalizability of our approach.These results establish a new paradigm for interactive,task-adaptive dehazing that actively collaborates with downstream applications.

</details>


### [93] [Cross-Scale Pansharpening via ScaleFormer and the PanScale Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00543)
*Ke Cao,Xuanhua He,Xueheng Li,Lingting Zhu,Yingying Wang,Ao Ma,Zhanjie Zhang,Man Zhou,Chengjun Xie,Jie Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They introduce PanScale (a large cross-scale pansharpening dataset and benchmark) and ScaleFormer, a transformer that reframes resolution generalization as sequence-length generalization via patch tokenization, achieving superior fusion quality and cross-scale generalization versus SOTA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most pansharpening methods are trained/evaluated at limited, low-resolution scales and fail to generalize to real-world high-resolution imagery. There is also a lack of datasets/benchmarks to rigorously test cross-scale performance.

Method: 1) Build PanScale and PanScale-Bench to evaluate generalization across resolutions/scales. 2) Propose ScaleFormer: tokenize images into fixed-resolution patches whose sequence length scales with image size; use a Scale-Aware Patchify module to train from fixed-size crops; decouple intra-patch spatial feature learning from inter-patch sequence modeling; apply Rotary Positional Encoding to improve extrapolation to unseen scales.

Result: Extensive experiments show ScaleFormer outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both fusion quality and cross-scale generalization on the new benchmark.

Conclusion: Treating resolution changes as sequence-length changes enables better scale generalization in pansharpening. Together with a new dataset/benchmark, the approach provides a practical path toward real-world, high-resolution fusion; data/code will be released upon acceptance.

Abstract: Pansharpening aims to generate high-resolution multi-spectral images by fusing the spatial detail of panchromatic images with the spectral richness of low-resolution MS data. However, most existing methods are evaluated under limited, low-resolution settings, limiting their generalization to real-world, high-resolution scenarios. To bridge this gap, we systematically investigate the data, algorithmic, and computational challenges of cross-scale pansharpening. We first introduce PanScale, the first large-scale, cross-scale pansharpening dataset, accompanied by PanScale-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating generalization across varying resolutions and scales. To realize scale generalization, we propose ScaleFormer, a novel architecture designed for multi-scale pansharpening. ScaleFormer reframes generalization across image resolutions as generalization across sequence lengths: it tokenizes images into patch sequences of the same resolution but variable length proportional to image scale. A Scale-Aware Patchify module enables training for such variations from fixed-size crops. ScaleFormer then decouples intra-patch spatial feature learning from inter-patch sequential dependency modeling, incorporating Rotary Positional Encoding to enhance extrapolation to unseen scales. Extensive experiments show that our approach outperforms SOTA methods in fusion quality and cross-scale generalization. The datasets and source code are available upon acceptance.

</details>


### [94] [Multiple Inputs and Mixwd data for Alzheimer's Disease Classification Based on 3D Vision Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00545)
*Juan A. Castro-Silva,Maria N. Moreno Garcia,Diego H. Peluffo-Ordoñez*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a 3D Vision Transformer that fuses multiple brain ROIs and non-imaging data (demographics, cognitive scores) to classify Alzheimer’s vs normal cognition, reporting 97.14% accuracy on pooled ADNI/AIBL/OASIS—exceeding prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior MRI-based AD classifiers often: (1) use 2D models that ignore 3D context; (2) restrict analysis to a few ROIs although AD is multi-regional; and (3) rely on a single data source despite clinical diagnosis requiring multiple tests and demographics. The work aims to recover lost 3D information, expand anatomical coverage, and integrate heterogeneous clinical data to improve diagnostic accuracy.

Method: Introduces MIMD-3DVT, a Multiple Inputs and Mixed Data 3D Vision Transformer. It (a) processes consecutive MRI slices jointly to capture 3D spatial features; (b) fuses multiple 3D ROI volumes; and (c) integrates mixed, non-imaging inputs (demographics, cognitive assessments) with imaging features. Evaluated on a combined dataset from ADNI, AIBL, and OASIS for NC vs AD classification.

Result: Using single or multiple ROIs, MIMD-3DVT achieved 97.14% accuracy in distinguishing NC from AD, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines on the pooled datasets.

Conclusion: Jointly modeling 3D MRI context, multi-ROI information, and mixed clinical data can substantially improve AD vs NC classification over traditional 2D or unimodal approaches. Results are promising, warranting broader validation and assessment of clinical utility.

Abstract: The current methods for diagnosing Alzheimer Disease using Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) have significant limitations. Many previous studies used 2D Transformers to analyze individual brain slices independently, potentially losing critical 3D contextual information. Region of interest-based models often focus on only a few brain regions despite Alzheimer's affecting multiple areas. Additionally, most classification models rely on a single test, whereas diagnosing Alzheimer's requires a multifaceted approach integrating diverse data sources for a more accurate assessment. This study introduces a novel methodology called the Multiple Inputs and Mixed Data 3D Vision Transformer (MIMD-3DVT). This method processes consecutive slices together to capture the feature dimensions and spatial information, fuses multiple 3D ROI imaging data inputs, and integrates mixed data from demographic factors, cognitive assessments, and brain imaging. The proposed methodology was experimentally evaluated using a combined dataset that included the Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI), the Australian Imaging, Biomarker, and Lifestyle Flagship Study of Ageing (AIBL), and the Open Access Series of Imaging Studies (OASIS). Our MIMD-3DVT, utilizing single or multiple ROIs, achieved an accuracy of 97.14%, outperforming the state-of-the-art methods in distinguishing between Normal Cognition and Alzheimer's Disease.

</details>


### [95] [Weakly Supervised Video Anomaly Detection with Anomaly-Connected Components and Intention Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00550)
*Yu Wang,Shengjie Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LAS-VAD is a weakly supervised video anomaly detection framework that learns anomaly semantics via (1) anomaly-connected component grouping, (2) intention-aware discrimination of similar actions, and (3) auxiliary anomaly-attribute cues, achieving state-of-the-art results on XD-Violence and UCF-Crime.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: WS-VAD only has video-level labels and lacks dense frame annotations, making it hard to learn fine-grained anomaly semantics and to differentiate visually similar normal vs. abnormal behaviors.

Method: Introduce an anomaly-connected component mechanism that clusters frames within each video into semantic groups so frames in the same group share semantics; add an intention-aware mechanism to separate look-alike normal/abnormal actions (e.g., taking vs. stealing); further inject anomaly attribute information (e.g., flames, smoke) as guidance to better model anomaly characteristics—all trained with only video-level supervision.

Result: On XD-Violence and UCF-Crime, LAS-VAD surpasses prior state-of-the-art WS-VAD methods by notable margins (exact metrics not provided in the abstract).

Conclusion: Encoding anomaly semantics through intra-video semantic grouping, intention awareness, and attribute guidance substantially improves weakly supervised anomaly localization and yields SOTA performance across benchmarks.

Abstract: Weakly supervised video anomaly detection (WS-VAD) involves identifying the temporal intervals that contain anomalous events in untrimmed videos, where only video-level annotations are provided as supervisory signals. However, a key limitation persists in WS-VAD, as dense frame-level annotations are absent, which often leaves existing methods struggling to learn anomaly semantics effectively. To address this issue, we propose a novel framework named LAS-VAD, short for Learning Anomaly Semantics for WS-VAD, which integrates anomaly-connected component mechanism and intention awareness mechanism. The former is designed to assign video frames into distinct semantic groups within a video, and frame segments within the same group are deemed to share identical semantic information. The latter leverages an intention-aware strategy to distinguish between similar normal and abnormal behaviors (e.g., taking items and stealing). To further model the semantic information of anomalies, as anomaly occurrence is accompanied by distinct characteristic attributes (i.e., explosions are characterized by flames and thick smoke), we additionally incorporate anomaly attribute information to guide accurate detection. Extensive experiments on two benchmark datasets, XD-Violence and UCF-Crime, demonstrate that our LAS-VAD outperforms current state-of-the-art methods with remarkable gains.

</details>


### [96] [Geometry OR Tracker: Universal Geometric Operating Room Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00560)
*Yihua Shao,Kang Chen,Feng Xue,Siyu Chen,Long Bai,Hongyuan Yu,Hao Tang,Jinlin Wu,Nassir Navab*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Geometry OR Tracker is a two-stage system that first rectifies imperfect multi-view camera geometry to a single, scale-consistent world frame and then tracks 3D points robustly to occlusions, yielding large gains in metric, world-frame tracking quality in operating rooms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Operating-room applications (e.g., surgeon behavior analysis) require accurate, metric 3D tracking, but real deployments suffer from unreliable camera calibration and RGB‑D registration. This causes cross-view geometric inconsistency (e.g., ghosting) and degrades fused 3D trajectories, motivating a method that restores metric and geometric consistency across views.

Method: A two-stage pipeline: (1) Multi-view Metric Geometry Rectification that converts imprecise calibrations into a geometrically and scale-consistent camera setup with a single global scale; (2) Occlusion-Robust 3D Point Tracking performed directly in the unified OR world coordinate frame.

Result: On the MM-OR benchmark, the rectification front-end reduces cross-view depth disagreement by over 30× compared to raw calibration and translates these geometric improvements into better 3D tracking. Ablations quantify how improving calibration quality directly improves world-frame tracking accuracy.

Conclusion: Rectifying multi-view geometry to enforce metric, cross-view consistency is key for reliable world-frame 3D tracking in ORs; better geometric consistency leads to stronger, more robust tracking performance.

Abstract: In operating rooms (OR), world-scale multi-view 3D tracking supports downstream applications such as surgeon behavior recognition, where physically meaningful quantities such as distances and motion statistics must be measured in meters. However, real clinical deployments rarely satisfy the geometric prerequisites for stable multi-view fusion and tracking: camera calibration and RGB-D registration are always unreliable, leading to cross-view geometric inconsistency that produces "ghosting" during fusion and degrades 3D trajectories in a shared OR coordinate frame. To address this, we introduce Geometry OR Tracker, a two-stage pipeline that first rectifies imprecise calibration into a scaleconsistent and geometrically consistent camera setup with a single global scale via a Multi-view Metric Geometry Rectification module, and then performs Occlusion-Robust 3D Point Tracking directly in the unified OR world frame. On the MM-OR benchmark, improved geometric consistency translates into tracking gains: our rectification front-end reduces cross-view depth disagreement by more than 30$\times$ compared to raw calibration. Ablation studies further demonstrate the relationship between calibration quality and tracking accuracy, showing that improved geometric consistency yields stronger world-frame tracking.

</details>


### [97] [MIDAS: Multi-Image Dispersion and Semantic Reconstruction for Jailbreaking MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00565)
*Yilian Liu,Xiaojun Jia,Guoshun Nan,Jiuyang Lyu,Zhican Chen,Tao Guan,Shuyuan Luo,Zhongyi Zhai,Yang Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MIDAS is a multimodal jailbreak technique that splits harmful intent into small semantic pieces spread across multiple images, forcing cross-image reasoning that reconstructs the malicious request while evading safety filters, achieving high attack success on strong closed-source MLLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing jailbreaks mainly use single-image masking or isolated visual cues, which only marginally lengthen reasoning and are ineffective against strongly aligned commercial MLLMs. Since added inference steps can disrupt security attention, the authors seek a method that systematically extends and structures reasoning to better bypass safeguards.

Method: Multi-Image Dispersion and Semantic Reconstruction (MIDAS): decompose harmful semantics into risk-bearing subunits; distribute them across multiple visual clues/images; require the model to perform chained, cross-image reasoning that gradually reassembles the malicious intent. This increases reliance on visual inputs, delays revealing harmful semantics, and reduces the model’s security attention during the reasoning process.

Result: Across multiple datasets and MLLMs (including four closed-source systems), MIDAS outperforms prior jailbreak attacks and attains an average attack success rate of 81.46% on the closed-source models.

Conclusion: Structured multi-image, cross-modal reasoning can be exploited to bypass current safety mechanisms in MLLMs. By dispersing and delaying exposure of harmful semantics, MIDAS substantially improves jailbreak effectiveness against advanced models. The authors release code to facilitate further study.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable performance but remain vulnerable to jailbreak attacks that can induce harmful content and undermine their secure deployment. Previous studies have shown that introducing additional inference steps, which disrupt security attention, can make MLLMs more susceptible to being misled into generating malicious content. However, these methods rely on single-image masking or isolated visual cues, which only modestly extend reasoning paths and thus achieve limited effectiveness, particularly against strongly aligned commercial closed-source models. To address this problem, in this paper, we propose Multi-Image Dispersion and Semantic Reconstruction (MIDAS), a multimodal jailbreak framework that decomposes harmful semantics into risk-bearing subunits, disperses them across multiple visual clues, and leverages cross-image reasoning to gradually reconstruct the malicious intent, thereby bypassing existing safety mechanisms. The proposed MIDAS enforces longer and more structured multi-image chained reasoning, substantially increases the model's reliance on visual cues while delaying the exposure of malicious semantics and significantly reducing the model's security attention, thereby improving the performance of jailbreak against advanced MLLMs. Extensive experiments across different datasets and MLLMs demonstrate that the proposed MIDAS outperforms state-of-the-art jailbreak attacks for MLLMs and achieves an average attack success rate of 81.46% across 4 closed-source MLLMs. Our code is available at this [link](https://github.com/Winnie-Lian/MIDAS).

</details>


### [98] [Decoupling Stability and Plasticity for Multi-Modal Test-Time Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00574)
*Yongbo He,Zirun Guo,Tao Jin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DASP diagnoses biased vs. unbiased modalities via redundancy in the shared latent space, then adapts asymmetrically with decoupled (stable/plastic) adapters: update plastic for biased modality, update stable (with KL regularization) for unbiased modality—yielding strong multi‑modal test‑time adaptation and SOTA results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-modal models face shifting test-time distributions. Existing adaptation often harms the strong (unbiased) modality via negative transfer and causes forgetting in the weak (biased) modality. A method is needed to distinguish modalities’ needs and balance stability vs. plasticity during adaptation.

Method: 1) Diagnose modality bias by measuring interdimensional redundancy (feature correlations) in the unified latent space—higher redundancy indicates a biased modality. 2) Use a decoupled, modality-specific adapter with stable and plastic components. 3) Asymmetric adaptation: for the biased modality, activate and update the plastic component while freezing the stable one; for the unbiased modality, bypass plastic and update the stable component with KL regularization to preserve generalizable knowledge and avoid negative transfer.

Result: Across multiple multi-modal benchmarks, DASP consistently outperforms state-of-the-art test-time adaptation methods, mitigating negative transfer in the unbiased modality and catastrophic forgetting in the biased modality. (Abstract does not provide numbers.)

Conclusion: Diagnosing redundancy to identify biased modalities and decoupling adaptation into stable vs. plastic paths enables targeted, asymmetric updates that preserve general knowledge while acquiring domain-specific signals, producing robust multi-modal test-time adaptation and SOTA performance.

Abstract: Adapting pretrained multi-modal models to evolving test-time distributions, known as multi-modal test-time adaptation, presents a significant challenge. Existing methods frequently encounter negative transfer in the unbiased modality and catastrophic forgetting in the biased modality. To address these challenges, we propose Decoupling Adaptation for Stability and Plasticity (DASP), a novel diagnose-then-mitigate framework. Our analysis reveals a critical discrepancy within the unified latent space: the biased modality exhibits substantially higher interdimensional redundancy (i.e., strong correlations across feature dimensions) compared to the unbiased modality. Leveraging this insight, DASP identifies the biased modality and implements an asymmetric adaptation strategy. This strategy employs a decoupled architecture where each modality-specific adapter is divided into stable and plastic components. The asymmetric mechanism works as follows: for the biased modality, which requires plasticity, the plastic component is activated and updated to capture domain-specific information, while the stable component remains fixed. Conversely, for the unbiased modality, which requires stability, the plastic component is bypassed, and the stable component is updated using KL regularization to prevent negative transfer. This asymmetric design enables the model to adapt flexibly to new domains while preserving generalizable knowledge. Comprehensive evaluations on diverse multi-modal benchmarks demonstrate that DASP significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [99] [WildActor: Unconstrained Identity-Preserving Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00586)
*Qin Guo,Tianyu Yang,Xuanhua He,Fei Shen,Yong Zhang,Zhuoliang Kang,Xiaoming Wei,Dan Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Actor-18M is a large human video dataset and WildActor is a new any-view conditioned generator that uses asymmetric identity-preserving attention and viewpoint-adaptive Monte Carlo sampling to keep full-body identity consistent across shots and motions, outperforming prior methods on a new benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Production human video generation needs strict, full-body identity consistency across varying viewpoints, shots, and motions; existing methods are often face-centric or cause rigid, copy-paste artifacts via pose locking.
