Method: (1) Build Actor-18M: 1.6M videos with 18M human images spanning arbitrary views and canonical three-view captures. (2) Propose WildActor: an any-view conditioned video generator with Asymmetric Identity-Preserving Attention to emphasize identity features and a Viewpoint-Adaptive Monte Carlo Sampling scheme that re-weights reference conditions by marginal utility to achieve balanced manifold coverage.

Result: On the proposed Actor-Bench, WildActor maintains body identity under diverse compositions, large viewpoint changes, and significant motions, consistently outperforming existing approaches.

Conclusion: A data-plus-method package (Actor-18M + WildActor) advances body-level identity preservation for human video generation and establishes a benchmark; results suggest improved production readiness, with room to explore broader generalization, ethics, and evaluation metrics in future work.

Abstract: Production-ready human video generation requires digital actors to maintain strictly consistent full-body identities across dynamic shots, viewpoints and motions, a setting that remains challenging for existing methods. Prior methods often suffer from face-centric behavior that neglects body-level consistency, or produce copy-paste artifacts where subjects appear rigid due to pose locking. We present Actor-18M, a large-scale human video dataset designed to capture identity consistency under unconstrained viewpoints and environments. Actor-18M comprises 1.6M videos with 18M corresponding human images, covering both arbitrary views and canonical three-view representations. Leveraging Actor-18M, we propose WildActor, a framework for any-view conditioned human video generation. We introduce an Asymmetric Identity-Preserving Attention mechanism coupled with a Viewpoint-Adaptive Monte Carlo Sampling strategy that iteratively re-weights reference conditions by marginal utility for balanced manifold coverage. Evaluated on the proposed Actor-Bench, WildActor consistently preserves body identity under diverse shot compositions, large viewpoint transitions, and substantial motions, surpassing existing methods in these challenging settings.

</details>


### [100] [AlignVAR: Towards Globally Consistent Visual Autoregression for Image Super-Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00589)
*Cencen Liu,Dongyang Zhang,Wen Yin,Jielei Wang,Tianyu Li,Ji Guo,Wenbo Jiang,Guoqing Wang,Guoming Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AlignVAR is a visual autoregressive framework for image super‑resolution that fixes VAR’s locality bias and error accumulation by reweighting attention toward globally coherent regions and supervising full reconstructions at every scale, yielding more consistent, perceptually pleasing images with much faster, leaner inference than diffusion baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Applying visual autoregressive (VAR) models to image super‑resolution is appealing due to stable training and non‑iterative inference, but current VARs suffer from locality‑biased attention (breaking spatial structure) and residual‑only supervision (errors snowball across scales), harming global consistency.

Method: Introduce AlignVAR with two components: (1) Spatial Consistency Autoregression (SCA) that uses an adaptive attention mask to emphasize structurally correlated regions, improving long‑range dependencies; (2) Hierarchical Consistency Constraint (HCC) that adds full reconstruction supervision at each scale alongside residual learning, surfacing accumulated deviations early and stabilizing coarse‑to‑fine refinement.

Result: Across extensive experiments, AlignVAR improves structural coherence and perceptual fidelity over generative baselines while being >10× faster at inference and using ~50% fewer parameters than leading diffusion‑based methods.

Conclusion: Reweighting attention for structural alignment and supervising full reconstructions hierarchically makes VAR a competitive, efficient paradigm for ISR, delivering globally consistent outputs with strong speed/size advantages over diffusion approaches.

Abstract: Visual autoregressive (VAR) models have recently emerged as a promising alternative for image generation, offering stable training, non-iterative inference, and high-fidelity synthesis through next-scale prediction. This encourages the exploration of VAR for image super-resolution (ISR), yet its application remains underexplored and faces two critical challenges: locality-biased attention, which fragments spatial structures, and residual-only supervision, which accumulates errors across scales, severely compromises global consistency of reconstructed images. To address these issues, we propose AlignVAR, a globally consistent visual autoregressive framework tailored for ISR, featuring two key components: (1) Spatial Consistency Autoregression (SCA), which applies an adaptive mask to reweight attention toward structurally correlated regions, thereby mitigating excessive locality and enhancing long-range dependencies; and (2) Hierarchical Consistency Constraint (HCC), which augments residual learning with full reconstruction supervision at each scale, exposing accumulated deviations early and stabilizing the coarse-to-fine refinement process. Extensive experiments demonstrate that AlignVAR consistently enhances structural coherence and perceptual fidelity over existing generative methods, while delivering over 10x faster inference with nearly 50% fewer parameters than leading diffusion-based approaches, establishing a new paradigm for efficient ISR.

</details>


### [101] [UNICBench: UNIfied Counting Benchmark for MLLM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00595)
*Chenggang Rong,Tao Han,Zhiyuan Zhao,Yaowu Fan,Jia Wan,Song Guo,Yuan Yuan,Junyu Gao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UNICBench is a unified multimodal counting benchmark and toolkit spanning images, documents, and audio, with rigorous ground truth, standardized evaluation, and stratified reporting; testing 45 MLLMs shows they do well on simple counts but struggle on reasoning-heavy and hardest cases, leaving ample room for improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Counting is a fundamental capability for MLLMs, yet evaluation has been fragmented and inconsistent across modalities. There is no single benchmark with reliable annotations and comparable protocols to measure counting from images, text/documents, and audio, particularly for harder reasoning-centric cases.

Method: Construct a multimodal corpus (5,300 images with 5,508 QA, 872 documents with 5,888 QA, and 2,069 audio clips with 2,905 QA). Annotate with a three-level capability taxonomy and difficulty tags. Provide deterministic numeric parsing, modality-specific matching rules, and stratified reporting. Define a standardized evaluation protocol (fixed splits/prompts/seeds). Evaluate 45 state-of-the-art MLLMs across modalities under these rules.

Result: MLLMs achieve strong performance on basic counting tasks but show significant performance gaps on reasoning-intensive and hardest partitions. Errors exhibit long-tail characteristics, revealing substantial headroom for improving general counting across modalities.

Conclusion: UNICBench delivers a rigorous, comparable basis for measuring multimodal counting and a public toolkit to enable reproducible evaluation. The observed shortcomings on harder and reasoning-based tasks underscore the need for methods that improve general and robust counting across modalities.

Abstract: Counting is a core capability for multimodal large language models (MLLMs), yet there is no unified counting dataset to rigorously evaluate this ability across image, text, and audio. We present UNICBench, a unified multimodal, multi level counting benchmark and evaluation toolkit with accurate ground truth, deterministic numeric parsing, and stratified reporting. The corpus comprises 5,300 images (5,508 QA), 872 documents (5,888 QA), and 2,069 audio clips (2,905 QA), annotated with a three level capability taxonomy and difficulty tags. Under a standardized protocol with fixed splits/prompts/seeds and modality specific matching rules, we evaluate 45 state-of-the-art MLLMs across modalities. Results show strong performance on some basic counting tasks but significant gaps on reasoning and the hardest partitions, highlighting long-tail errors and substantial headroom for improving general counting. UNICBench offers a rigorous and comparable basis for measurement and a public toolkit to accelerate progress.

</details>


### [102] [Data-Centric Benchmark for Label Noise Estimation and Ranking in Remote Sensing Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00604)
*Keiller Nogueira,Codrut-Andrei Diaconu,Dávid Kerekes,Jakob Gawlikowski,Cédric Léonard,Nassim Ait Ali Braham,June Moh Goo,Zichao Zeng,Zhipeng Liu,Pallavi Jain,Andrea Nascetti,Ronny Hänsch*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They release a data-centric benchmark, a public dataset, and two complementary techniques to detect, quantify, and rank label noise in remote-sensing semantic segmentation using model uncertainty, prediction consistency, and representation analysis, outperforming baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pixel-wise labels for remote-sensing images are costly and prone to errors; such noisy annotations degrade segmentation performance and robustness, so there is a need to reliably identify and measure label noise in training data.

Method: They propose two methods that leverage (i) model uncertainty, (ii) prediction consistency across perturbations/runs, and (iii) representation-space analysis to score samples by noise level. They also build a data-centric benchmark and a public dataset for evaluating label-noise identification in remote-sensing segmentation.

Result: Across varied experimental settings, the proposed techniques consistently outperform established baselines for detecting and ranking noisy training samples. All resources and code are publicly released.

Conclusion: Reliable noise scoring via uncertainty-, consistency-, and representation-based signals can better identify mislabeled pixels, supporting more robust remote-sensing segmentation workflows; the accompanying benchmark and dataset enable standardized evaluation and future research.

Abstract: High-quality pixel-level annotations are essential for the semantic segmentation of remote sensing imagery. However, such labels are expensive to obtain and often affected by noise due to the labor-intensive and time-consuming nature of pixel-wise annotation, which makes it challenging for human annotators to label every pixel accurately. Annotation errors can significantly degrade the performance and robustness of modern segmentation models, motivating the need for reliable mechanisms to identify and quantify noisy training samples. This paper introduces a novel Data-Centric benchmark, together with a novel, publicly available dataset and two techniques for identifying, quantifying, and ranking training samples according to their level of label noise in remote sensing semantic segmentation. Such proposed methods leverage complementary strategies based on model uncertainty, prediction consistency, and representation analysis, and consistently outperform established baselines across a range of experimental settings. The outcomes of this work are publicly available at https://github.com/keillernogueira/label_noise_segmentation.

</details>


### [103] [IdGlow: Dynamic Identity Modulation for Multi-Subject Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00607)
*Honghao Cai,Xiangyuan Wang,Yunhao Bai,Tianze Zhou,Sijie Xu,Yuyang Hao,Zezhou Cui,Yuyuan Yang,Wei Zhu,Yibo Chen,Xu Tang,Yao Hu,Zhen Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: IdGlow is a mask-free, two-stage Flow Matching diffusion framework that combines task-adaptive timestep scheduling, gated identity injection, VLM-guided prompt synthesis, and group-level weighted-margin DPO to harmonize multiple identities under complex deformations (e.g., age transformation), delivering a superior trade-off between identity fidelity and aesthetic quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-subject image generation must keep multiple identities consistent while composing a coherent scene and accommodating large structural changes (like age transformation). Prior methods relying on masks or localized attention face a stability–plasticity dilemma, causing identity drift, attribute leakage, and poor handling of structural deformations.

Method: Stage 1 (SFT): task-adaptive timestep scheduling aligned with diffusion dynamics—(a) a linear decay schedule that progressively relaxes constraints for natural group composition, and (b) a temporal gating mechanism to inject identity information within a critical semantic window so adult facial semantics are preserved without overwriting child anatomy; augmented with a badcase-driven VLM to synthesize precise, context-aware prompts without explicit layouts. Stage 2: Fine-Grained Group-Level DPO with a weighted-margin formulation to penalize multi-subject artifacts, improve texture harmony, and recalibrate identity fidelity toward real-world distributions. Built on Flow Matching diffusion models.

Result: On two benchmarks—direct multi-person fusion and age-transformed group generation—IdGlow substantially reduces stability–plasticity conflicts, removes multi-subject artifacts, improves texture harmony, preserves identity under structural changes, and achieves a superior Pareto balance between state-of-the-art facial fidelity and commercial-grade aesthetics.

Conclusion: A progressive, mask-free pipeline that aligns training dynamics with diffusion timesteps, leverages VLM-guided prompting, and applies group-level preference optimization can resolve identity–structure trade-offs in multi-subject generation, yielding robust, high-fidelity, aesthetically pleasing results across challenging settings like age-transformed group synthesis.

Abstract: Multi-subject image generation requires seamlessly harmonizing multiple reference identities within a coherent scene. However, existing methods relying on rigid spatial masks or localized attention often struggle with the "stability-plasticity dilemma," particularly failing in tasks that require complex structural deformations, such as identity-preserving age transformation. To address this, we present IdGlow, a mask-free, progressive two-stage framework built upon Flow Matching diffusion models. In the supervised fine-tuning (SFT) stage, we introduce task-adaptive timestep scheduling aligned with diffusion generative dynamics: a linear decay schedule that progressively relaxes constraints for natural group composition, and a temporal gating mechanism that concentrates identity injection within a critical semantic window, successfully preserving adult facial semantics without overriding child-like anatomical structures. To resolve attribute leakage and semantic ambiguity without explicit layout inputs, we further integrate a badcase-driven Vision-Language Model (VLM) for precise, context-aware prompt synthesis. In the second stage, we design a Fine-Grained Group-Level Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) with a weighted margin formulation to simultaneously eliminate multi-subject artifacts, elevate texture harmony, and recalibrate identity fidelity towards real-world distributions. Extensive experiments on two challenging benchmarks -- direct multi-person fusion and age-transformed group generation -- demonstrate that IdGlow fundamentally mitigates the stability-plasticity conflict, achieving a superior Pareto balance between state-of-the-art facial fidelity and commercial-grade aesthetic quality.

</details>


### [104] [Linking Modality Isolation in Heterogeneous Collaborative Perception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00609)
*Changxing Liu,Zichen Chao,Siheng Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CodeAlign is a co-occurrence-free alignment framework for multi-agent collaborative perception that aligns heterogeneous sensor modalities via feature–code–feature translations using modality-specific codebooks, delivering SOTA accuracy with ~8% of prior methods’ training parameters and 1024× lower communication on OPV2V and DAIR-V2X.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Collaborative perception benefits from sharing information across agents, but domain gaps from heterogeneous sensors—and especially modality isolation where modalities never co-occur in the same training frames—break existing alignment methods that rely on spatially overlapping observations. A method is needed to align modalities without co-occurrence supervision while also being efficient in training and communication.

Method: Introduce modality-specific codebooks to regularize feature spaces into compact code spaces. Learn cross-modal feature–code–feature (FCF) translations: map features from one modality into the code space of another modality, then decode back into target-modality features. This removes the need for spatial correspondence and enables smooth, co-occurrence-free alignment while also reducing communication by transmitting codes instead of full features.

Result: When integrating three modalities, CodeAlign uses only ~8% of the training parameters compared to prior alignment methods, cuts communication load by 1024×, and achieves state-of-the-art perception performance on OPV2V and DAIR-V2X datasets.

Conclusion: CodeAlign provides an efficient, scalable, and co-occurrence-free modality alignment mechanism for collaborative perception. By leveraging codebook-regularized representations and FCF translations, it bridges cross-modal domain gaps without spatial overlap supervision, improving performance and reducing bandwidth and training cost.

Abstract: Collaborative perception leverages data exchange among multiple agents to enhance overall perception capabilities. However, heterogeneity across agents introduces domain gaps that hinder collaboration, and this is further exacerbated by an underexplored issue: modality isolation. It arises when multiple agents with different modalities never co-occur in any training data frame, enlarging cross-modal domain gaps. Existing alignment methods rely on supervision from spatially overlapping observations, thus fail to handle modality isolation. To address this challenge, we propose CodeAlign, the first efficient, co-occurrence-free alignment framework that smoothly aligns modalities via cross-modal feature-code-feature(FCF) translation. The key idea is to explicitly identify the representation consistency through codebook, and directly learn mappings between modality-specific feature spaces, thereby eliminating the need for spatial correspondence. Codebooks regularize feature spaces into code spaces, providing compact yet expressive representations. With a prepared code space for each modality, CodeAlign learns FCF translations that map features to the corresponding codes of other modalities, which are then decoded back into features in the target code space, enabling effective alignment. Experiments show that, when integrating three modalities, CodeAlign requires only 8% of the training parameters of prior alignment methods, reduces communication load by 1024x, and achieves state-of-the-art perception performance on both OPV2V and DAIR-V2X dataset. Code will be released on https://github.com/cxliu0314/CodeAlign.

</details>


### [105] [Exploring Spatiotemporal Feature Propagation for Video-Level Compressive Spectral Reconstruction: Dataset, Model and Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00611)
*Lijing Cai,Zhan Shi,Chenglong Huang,Jinyao Wu,Qiping Li,Zikang Huo,Linsen Chen,Chongde Zi,Xun Cao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They move SCI reconstruction from image-level to video-level by introducing a transformer (PG-SVRT) with spatial-then-temporal attention and a bridged token for efficiency, release the first dynamic HSI dataset (DynaSpec), and validate on simulations of four SCI systems and a real DD-CASSI prototype, achieving state-of-the-art quality, spectral fidelity, and temporal consistency with low FLOPs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Image-based SCI reconstructions struggle because the encoding entangles spatial–spectral cues, making single-measurement recovery uncertain, and frame-by-frame processing breaks temporal consistency essential for video understanding. There is also a lack of high-quality dynamic HSI datasets to develop and benchmark video-based approaches.

Method: 1) Build DynaSpec: 30 dynamic hyperspectral video sequences via frame-scanning acquisition. 2) Propose PG-SVRT (Propagation-Guided Spectral Video Reconstruction Transformer): apply spatial-first, then temporal attention to exploit complementary inter-frame information; introduce a bridged token to reduce computation while maintaining context propagation. 3) Evaluate in simulation across four SCI system models and on real data from a constructed DD-CASSI prototype; benchmark against existing methods.

Result: Across extensive experiments, PG-SVRT outperforms prior approaches in reconstruction quality, spectral fidelity, and temporal consistency, while using minimal FLOPs. It succeeds both in simulated settings for multiple SCI systems and on real captures from the DD-CASSI prototype.

Conclusion: Video-level spectral reconstruction with PG-SVRT effectively mitigates uncertainty from compressed measurements and enforces temporal consistency, setting a new performance bar. The released DynaSpec dataset and DD-CASSI prototype provide practical benchmarks and promote further research, with efficiency enabling real-world deployment.

Abstract: Recently, Spectral Compressive Imaging (SCI) has achieved remarkable success, unlocking significant potential for dynamic spectral vision. However, existing reconstruction methods, primarily image-based, suffer from two limitations: (i) Encoding process masks spatial-spectral features, leading to uncertainty in reconstructing missing information from single compressed measurements, and (ii) The frame-by-frame reconstruction paradigm fails to ensure temporal consistency, which is crucial in the video perception. To address these challenges, this paper seeks to advance spectral reconstruction from the image level to the video level, leveraging the complementary features and temporal continuity across adjacent frames in dynamic scenes. Initially, we construct the first high-quality dynamic hyperspectral image dataset (DynaSpec), comprising 30 sequences obtained through frame-scanning acquisition. Subsequently, we propose the Propagation-Guided Spectral Video Reconstruction Transformer (PG-SVRT), which employs a spatial-then-temporal attention to effectively reconstruct spectral features from abundant video information, while using a bridged token to reduce computational complexity. Finally, we conduct simulation experiments to assess the performance of four SCI systems, and construct a DD-CASSI prototype for real-world data collection and benchmarking. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PG-SVRT achieves superior performance in reconstruction quality, spectral fidelity, and temporal consistency, while maintaining minimal FLOPs. Project page: https://github.com/nju-cite/DynaSpec

</details>


### [106] [Position: Evaluation of Visual Processing Should Be Human-Centered, Not Metric-Centered](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00643)
*Jinfan Hu,Fanghua Yu,Zhiyuan You,Xiang Yin,Hongyu An,Xinqi Lin,Chao Dong,Jinjin Gu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Single-metric image quality metrics are inadequate for modern, especially generative and perception-oriented, visual processing; the paper advocates shifting to human-centered, context-aware, fine-grained evaluation frameworks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Objective IQA benchmarks, while scalable and reproducible, increasingly misalign with human perception and user preferences—particularly evident in image restoration—risking misdirected research and stifled innovation across visual tasks.

Method: As a position paper, it synthesizes observations and arguments rather than experiments, proposing a rebalancing of evaluation: complement objective metrics with human-in-the-loop assessments, context/task-aware criteria, and more granular perceptual measures.

Result: No empirical results are reported; the contribution is a conceptual critique of current metric-centric evaluation and a set of recommendations/principles for more perceptually aligned assessment of visual models.

Conclusion: Evaluation should move beyond single-number IQA scores toward hybrid, human-centered, context-sensitive, and fine-grained protocols to better capture perceived quality and practical utility, guiding healthier progress in visual processing research.

Abstract: This position paper argues that the evaluation of modern visual processing systems should no longer be driven primarily by single-metric image quality assessment benchmarks, particularly in the era of generative and perception-oriented methods. Image restoration exemplifies this divergence: while objective IQA metrics enable reproducible, scalable evaluation, they have increasingly drifted apart from human perception and user preferences. We contend that this mismatch risks constraining innovation and misguiding research progress across visual processing tasks. Rather than rejecting metrics altogether, this paper calls for a rebalancing of evaluation paradigms, advocating a more human-centered, context-aware, and fine-grained approach to assessing the visual models' outcomes.

</details>


### [107] [Exploring 3D Dataset Pruning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00651)
*Xiaohan Zhao,Xinyi Shang,Jiacheng Liu,Zhiqiang Shen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A 3D dataset pruning framework that selects a weighted, representation-aware subset with per‑class quotas and uses prior‑invariant teacher supervision (calibrated soft labels + embedding distillation) to mitigate long‑tail bias, achieving controllable OA–mAcc trade‑offs and improved performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D datasets often have long‑tailed class distributions, making Overall Accuracy (OA) and Mean Accuracy (mAcc) inherently conflicting. Existing 2D pruning methods don’t address this, so naive pruning can worsen class coverage or skew priors. The goal is to prune while approximating full‑data risk and respecting downstream metric preferences.

Method: Formulate pruning as approximating full‑data expected risk with a weighted subset, identifying two errors: (1) coverage error from poor representativeness; (2) prior‑mismatch bias between subset‑induced class weights and target metrics. Propose: (a) representation‑aware subset selection with per‑class retention quotas to preserve long‑tail coverage and to control OA–mAcc trade‑off; (b) prior‑invariant teacher supervision using calibrated soft labels and embedding‑geometry distillation to reduce bias from class prior shifts.

Result: On multiple 3D datasets, the pruned subsets trained with the proposed supervision improve both OA and mAcc versus baselines and allow tuning the OA–mAcc balance. Results are robust across different settings; code is publicly available.

Conclusion: Accounting for long‑tail coverage and metric‑prior mismatch enables effective 3D dataset pruning that approximates full‑data performance, improves OA and mAcc, and offers a controllable trade‑off, suggesting a practical recipe for pruning long‑tailed 3D data.

Abstract: Dataset pruning has been widely studied for 2D images to remove redundancy and accelerate training, while particular pruning methods for 3D data remain largely unexplored. In this work, we study dataset pruning for 3D data, where its observed common long-tail class distribution nature make optimization under conventional evaluation metrics Overall Accuracy (OA) and Mean Accuracy (mAcc) inherently conflicting, and further make pruning particularly challenging. To address this, we formulate pruning as approximating the full-data expected risk with a weighted subset, which reveals two key errors: coverage error from insufficient representativeness and prior-mismatch bias from inconsistency between subset-induced class weights and target metrics. We propose representation-aware subset selection with per-class retention quotas for long-tail coverage, and prior-invariant teacher supervision using calibrated soft labels and embedding-geometry distillation. The retention quota also serves as a switch to control the OA-mAcc trade-off. Extensive experiments on 3D datasets show that our method can improve both metrics across multiple settings while adapting to different downstream preferences. Our code is available at https://github.com/XiaohanZhao123/3D-Dataset-Pruning.

</details>


### [108] [RC-GeoCP: Geometric Consensus for Radar-Camera Collaborative Perception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00654)
*Xiaokai Bai,Lianqing Zheng,Runwei Guan,Siyuan Cao,Huiliang Shen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RC-GeoCP is a collaborative perception framework that fuses 4D radar with cameras using radar-anchored geometry to align multi-agent features, communicate only informative cues, and assemble a coherent global scene—achieving SOTA on V2X-Radar/V2X-R with less bandwidth.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LiDAR delivers accurate geometry but is costly and degrades in adverse weather. Cameras and 4D radar offer complementary strengths (dense semantics + robust spatial measurements), yet their joint use for multi-agent collaborative perception is underexplored and plagued by cross-agent misalignment (depth ambiguity, spatial dispersion).

Method: Proposes a radar-anchored geometric consensus. (1) Geometric Structure Rectification (GSR): aligns image semantics to radar-derived geometry for geometry-consistent features. (2) Uncertainty-Aware Communication (UAC): selects features to transmit via conditional-entropy-based prioritization driven by inter-agent disagreement. (3) Consensus-Driven Assembler (CDA): aggregates multi-agent information using shared radar-based geometric anchors to form a globally coherent representation.

Result: Introduces the first unified radar-camera CP benchmark on V2X-Radar and V2X-R; RC-GeoCP attains state-of-the-art accuracy while significantly reducing communication overhead. (No exact metrics provided in the abstract.)

Conclusion: Radar-anchored geometric consensus plus uncertainty-aware, selective communication enables effective multi-modal, multi-agent fusion, yielding coherent global perceptions with better accuracy–bandwidth trade-offs. Code to be released.

Abstract: Collaborative perception (CP) enhances scene understanding through multi-agent information sharing. While LiDAR-centric systems offer precise geometry, high costs and performance degradation in adverse weather necessitate multi-modal alternatives. Despite dense visual semantics and robust spatial measurements, the synergy between cameras and 4D radar remains underexplored in collaborative settings. This work introduces RC-GeoCP, the first framework to explore the fusion of 4D radar and images in CP. To resolve misalignment caused by depth ambiguity and spatial dispersion across agents, RC-GeoCP establishes a radar-anchored geometric consensus. Specifically, Geometric Structure Rectification (GSR) aligns visual semantics with geometry derived from radar to generate spatially grounded, geometry-consistent representations. Uncertainty-Aware Communication (UAC) formulates selective transmission as a conditional entropy reduction process to prioritize informative features based on inter-agent disagreement. Finally, the Consensus-Driven Assembler (CDA) aggregates multi-agent information via shared geometric anchors to form a globally coherent representation. We establish the first unified radar-camera CP benchmark on V2X-Radar and V2X-R, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance with significantly reduced communication overhead. Code will be released soon.

</details>


### [109] [Stateful Cross-layer Vision Modulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00655)
*Ying Liu,Yudong Han,Kean Shi,Liyuan Pan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SCVM adds a cross-layer memory and feedback modulation inside the vision encoder to control how visual representations evolve, preserving fine details and aligning semantics so MLLMs improve on VQA and hallucination tasks without changing the LLM or increasing tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-hoc fusion of multi-layer visual features can suppress fine-grained early information, and directly feeding shallow features to the LLM mismatches the semantic distribution its cross-attention expects—often forcing LLM adaptation or fine-tuning.

Method: Introduce a recursively updated cross-layer memory within the vision encoder to capture long-range inter-layer dependencies; apply layer-wise feedback modulation that refreshes token representations using the accumulated memory to steer representation evolution; add an auxiliary semantic alignment loss supervising the final memory state to encourage progressive compression and emphasis of task-relevant content.

Result: Across multiple VQA and hallucination benchmarks, SCVM reportedly yields consistent performance gains while keeping the LLM frozen, not adding extra vision encoders, and not expanding the number of visual tokens.

Conclusion: Regulating representation evolution via internal memory and feedback produces better aligned, information-preserving visual features for MLLMs, delivering efficient, architecture-compatible improvements without incurring LLM-side costs.

Abstract: Recent multimodal large language models (MLLMs) widely adopt multi-layer visual feature fusion to enhance visual representation. However, existing approaches typically perform static concatenation or weighted aggregation after visual encoding, without intervening in the representation formation process itself. As a result, fine-grained details from early layers may be progressively suppressed during hierarchical abstraction. Moreover, directly introducing shallow-layer features into the language model often leads to semantic distribution mismatch with the visual feature space that the LLM's cross-attention layers were pretrained on, which typically requires additional adaptation or fine-tuning of the LLM. To address these limitations, we revisit visual representation learning from the perspective of representation evolution control and propose a cross-layer memory-modulated vision framework(SCVM). Specifically, we introduce a recursively updated cross-layer memory state inside the vision encoder to model long-range inter-layer dependencies. We further design a layer-wise feedback modulation mechanism that refreshes token representations at each layer based on the accumulated memory, thereby structurally regulating the representation evolution trajectory. In addition, we incorporate an auxiliary semantic alignment objective that explicitly supervises the final memory state, encouraging progressive compression and reinforcement of task-relevant information. Experimental results on multiple visual question answering and hallucination evaluation benchmarks demonstrate that SCVM achieves consistent performance improvements without expanding visual tokens, introducing additional vision encoders, or modifying or fine-tuning the language model.

</details>


### [110] [Act Like a Pathologist: Tissue-Aware Whole Slide Image Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00667)
*Wentao Huang,Weimin Lyu,Peiliang Lou,Qingqiao Hu,Xiaoling Hu,Shahira Abousamra,Wenchao Han,Ruifeng Guo,Jiawei Zhou,Chao Chen,Chen Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HistoSelect is a question-guided, tissue-aware, coarse-to-fine retrieval framework for pathology VQA that first finds relevant tissue regions and then selects the most informative patches, cutting visual tokens by ~70% while improving accuracy and interpretability across large-scale QA benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Whole-slide images (WSIs) are gigapixel and contain far more information than needed for a given clinical question. Existing VLM approaches use uniform patch sampling or broad attention, often focusing on irrelevant areas and missing key evidence. Pathologists instead scan broadly and zoom in selectively based on the question. The paper aims to align computational models with this human search behavior to boost efficiency, accuracy, and grounding.

Method: Introduce HistoSelect, a two-stage, question-guided retrieval pipeline: (1) a group sampler identifies tissue regions relevant to the question (tissue-aware coarse search); (2) a patch selector retrieves the most informative patches within those regions (fine search). Selected patches are fed to a VLM for QA, reducing token count and focusing attention on clinically pertinent evidence.

Result: On 356k question–answer pairs spanning three pathology QA tasks, HistoSelect outperforms prior methods, reduces visual token usage by about 70% on average, and grounds answers in interpretable regions that align with pathologist attention.

Conclusion: Human-like, question-guided coarse-to-fine search substantially improves WSI reasoning, yielding more efficient, accurate, and interpretable pathology VLMs. Incorporating search and attention patterns that mimic expert workflows is a promising direction for practical, reliable computational pathology systems.

Abstract: Computational pathology has advanced rapidly in recent years, driven by domain-specific image encoders and growing interest in using vision-language models to answer natural-language questions about diseases. Yet, the core problem behind pathology question-answering remains unsolved, considering that a gigapixel slide contains far more information than necessary for a given question. Pathologists naturally navigate tissue and morphology complexity by scanning broadly, and zooming in selectively according to the clinical questions. Current models, in contrast, rely on uniform patch sampling or broad attention maps, often attending equally to irrelevant regions while overlooking key visual evidence. In this work, we try to bring models closer to how humans actually examine slides. We propose a question-guided, tissue-aware, and coarse-to-fine retrieval framework, HistoSelect, that consists of two key components: a group sampler that identifies question-relevant tissue regions, followed by a patch selector that retrieves the most informative patches within those regions. By selecting only the most informative patches, our method becomes significantly more efficient: reducing visual token usage by 70% on average, while improving accuracy across three pathology QA tasks. Evaluated on 356,000 question-answer pairs, our approach outperforms existing methods and produces answers grounded in interpretable, pathologist-consistent regions. Our results suggest that bringing human-like search and attention patterns into WSI reasoning is a promising direction for building practical and reliable pathology VLMs.

</details>


### [111] [Direct low-field MRI super-resolution using undersampled k-space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00668)
*Daniel Tweneboah Anyimadu,Mohammed M. Abdelsamea,Ahmed Karam Eldaly*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A dual‑channel k‑space U‑Net reconstructs high‑field‑like MR images directly from undersampled low‑field k‑space, outperforming image‑domain SR/IQT and approaching full k‑space quality on brain MRI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low‑field MRI is cheaper but slow and lower quality; while k‑space undersampling accelerates scans, most SR/IQT methods work post‑hoc in the image domain. There is a need to recover high‑quality images directly from undersampled low‑field k‑space to both speed acquisition and boost quality.

Method: A k‑space dual‑channel U‑Net processes real and imaginary components of undersampled low‑field k‑space to infer missing frequency content, followed by image reconstruction. This contrasts with conventional spatial‑domain super‑resolution/IQT pipelines.

Result: On low‑field brain MRI, the proposed k‑space approach consistently outperforms comparable spatial‑domain methods, and reconstructions from undersampled k‑space achieve image quality comparable to full k‑space acquisitions.

Conclusion: Learning directly in k‑space enables accelerated, high‑quality low‑field MRI, producing high‑field‑like images from undersampled data; this is the first study to apply SR/IQT to low‑field MRI directly in k‑space.

Abstract: Low-field magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) provides affordable access to diagnostic imaging but suffers from prolonged acquisition and limited image quality. Accelerated imaging can be achieved with k-space undersampling, while super-resolution (SR) and image quality transfer (IQT) methods typically rely on spatial-domain post-processing. In this work, we propose a novel framework for reconstructing high-field MR like images directly from undersampled low-field k-space. Our approach employs a k-space dual channel U-Net that processes the real and imaginary components of undersampled k-space to restore missing frequency content. Experiments on low-field brain MRI demonstrate that our k-space-driven image enhancement consistently outperforms the counterpart spatial-domain method. Furthermore, reconstructions from undersampled k-space achieve image quality comparable to full k-space acquisitions. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work that investigates low-field MRI SR/IQT directly from undersampled k-space.

</details>


### [112] [Specializing Foundation Models via Mixture of Low-Rank Experts for Comprehensive Head CT Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00675)
*Youngjin Yoo,Han Liu,Bogdan Georgescu,Yanbo Zhang,Sasa Grbic,Michael Baumgartner,Thomas J. Re,Jyotipriya Das,Poikavila Ullaskrishnan,Eva Eibenberger,Andrei Chekkoury,Uttam K. Bodanapally,Savvas Nicolaou,Pina C. Sanelli,Thomas J. Schroeppel,Yvonne W. Lui,Eli Gibson*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They introduce MoLRE, a mixture-of-experts extension to LoRA that conditionally adapts features via multiple low‑rank adapters with unsupervised soft routing, improving multi‑label head CT finding detection across diverse foundation models with <0.5% extra parameters; best AUC 0.917 and largest gains on general/medical-domain models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning like LoRA applies a single, uniform adaptation that may be suboptimal for heterogeneous, multi-label clinical tasks (e.g., 75 head CT findings). Foundation models’ adaptation to such complex diagnostics is underexplored; there is a need for conditional, pathology-aware adaptation without requiring explicit supervision.

Method: MoLRE (Mixture of Low-Rank Experts): augment each adapted layer with multiple specialized low-rank adapters (experts) and an unsupervised soft routing mechanism that mixes experts per input, enabling conditional feature adaptation. It is parameter‑efficient (<0.5% overhead) and broadly applicable across 2D/3D architectures and pretraining regimes. Evaluated on >70k non-contrast head CT scans with 75 labeled findings.

Result: Across six state-of-the-art medical imaging foundation models (7M–431M params), MoLRE yields consistent improvements. Largest gains on general-purpose and medical-domain models (e.g., DINOv3‑Base +4.6%, MedGemma +4.3%); modest gains on 3D CT-specialized or very large models (+0.2–1.3%). MoLRE+MedGemma achieves the top average detection AUC of 0.917.

Conclusion: Conditional, expert-based PEFT improves multi-label head CT detection without explicit pathology supervision, with small parameter overhead. Performance depends on pretraining domain, architecture, and scale, underscoring the need for systematic, task-specific benchmarking. MoLRE is a practical, general strategy for enhancing medical imaging foundation models on complex diagnostic tasks.

Abstract: Foundation models pre-trained on large-scale datasets demonstrate strong transfer learning capabilities; however, their adaptation to complex multi-label diagnostic tasks-such as comprehensive head CT finding detection-remains understudied. Standard parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods such as LoRA apply uniform adaptations across pathology types, which may limit performance for diverse medical findings. We propose a Mixture of Low-Rank Experts (MoLRE) framework that extends LoRA with multiple specialized low-rank adapters and unsupervised soft routing. This approach enables conditional feature adaptation with less than 0.5% additional parameters and without explicit pathology supervision. We present a comprehensive benchmark of MoLRE across six state-of-the-art medical imaging foundation models spanning 2D and 3D architectures, general-domain, medical-domain, and head CT-specific pretraining, and model sizes ranging from 7M to 431M parameters. Using over 70,000 non-contrast head CT scans with 75 annotated findings-including hemorrhage, infarction, trauma, mass lesions, structural abnormalities, and chronic changes-our experiments demonstrate consistent performance improvements across all models. Gains vary substantially: general-purpose and medical-domain models show the largest improvements (DINOv3-Base: +4.6%; MedGemma: +4.3%), whereas 3D CT-specialized or very large models show more modest gains (+0.2-1.3%). The combination of MoLRE and MedGemma achieves the highest average detection AUC of 0.917. These findings highlight the importance of systematic benchmarking on target clinical tasks, as pretraining domain, architecture, and model scale interact in non-obvious ways.

</details>


### [113] [CoLC: Communication-Efficient Collaborative Perception with LiDAR Completion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00682)
*Yushan Han,Hui Zhang,Qiming Xia,Yi Jin,Yidong Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CoLC is a communication-efficient early-fusion framework for collaborative LiDAR perception that samples informative foreground points, reconstructs dense representations from sparse transmissions, and aligns features during training, yielding better accuracy–bandwidth trade-offs and robustness across heterogeneous models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Early fusion provides strong perceptual complementarity and robustness to model heterogeneity, but its prohibitive communication cost limits real-world use; the paper seeks to retain early-fusion benefits while drastically reducing bandwidth without sacrificing scene completeness.

Method: Three key components: (1) Foreground-Aware Point Sampling (FAPS) at neighbors selects semantically informative points under bandwidth limits; (2) Completion-Enhanced Early Fusion (CEEF) at the ego reconstructs dense pillars from received sparse points and adaptively fuses them with ego observations to restore spatial completeness; (3) Dense-Guided Dual Alignment (DGDA) imposes semantic and geometric consistency between enhanced and dense pillars during training for stable, robust features.

Result: On both simulated and real-world datasets, CoLC outperforms baselines on perception–communication trade-offs and maintains robustness in heterogeneous model settings; qualitative and quantitative gains are reported (exact numbers not provided in the abstract).

Conclusion: By coupling foreground-aware sampling, LiDAR completion, and dual alignment, CoLC makes early collaborative perception practical under tight bandwidth, preserving early-fusion strengths while improving robustness; released code supports reproducibility.

Abstract: Collaborative perception empowers autonomous agents to share complementary information and overcome perception limitations. While early fusion offers more perceptual complementarity and is inherently robust to model heterogeneity, its high communication cost has limited its practical deployment, prompting most existing works to favor intermediate or late fusion. To address this, we propose a communication-efficient early Collaborative perception framework that incorporates LiDAR Completion to restore scene completeness under sparse transmission, dubbed as CoLC. Specifically, the CoLC integrates three complementary designs. First, each neighbor agent applies Foreground-Aware Point Sampling (FAPS) to selectively transmit informative points that retain essential structural and contextual cues under bandwidth constraints. The ego agent then employs Completion-Enhanced Early Fusion (CEEF) to reconstruct dense pillars from the received sparse inputs and adaptively fuse them with its own observations, thereby restoring spatial completeness. Finally, the Dense-Guided Dual Alignment (DGDA) strategy enforces semantic and geometric consistency between the enhanced and dense pillars during training, ensuring consistent and robust feature learning. Experiments on both simulated and real-world datasets demonstrate that CoLC achieves superior perception-communication trade-offs and remains robust under heterogeneous model settings. The code is available at https://github.com/CatOneTwo/CoLC.

</details>


### [114] [SCOUT: Fast Spectral CT Imaging in Ultra LOw-data Regimes via PseUdo-label GeneraTion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00687)
*Guoquan Wei,Liu Shi,Shaoyu Wang,Mohan Li,Cunfeng Wei,Qiegen Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Self-supervised CT reconstruction from ultra-low raw data that needs no external data or pretraining; leverages spatial nonlocal similarity and projection-domain conjugacy to synthesize pseudo-3D supervision, enabling fast, high-fidelity reconstructions that suppress ring artifacts and recover fine details.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional CT recon methods are either slow or depend on data-hungry supervised models that overlook intrinsic structure in medical 3D data. The aim is to exploit the information already present in raw projections to achieve high-quality reconstruction without labels, external data, or long pretraining—especially under ultra-low data conditions.

Method: Use spatial nonlocal similarity and conjugate properties of the projection (sinogram) domain to construct pseudo-3D data for self-supervised training. The model is trained quickly on the case’s own raw projections, avoiding external datasets and lengthy pretraining, to produce artifact-robust reconstructions.

Result: Experiments show rapid attainment of high-fidelity images, strong suppression of detector-induced ring artifacts, and unprecedented detail recovery for ultra-low-data CT. Public code (SCOUT) supports reproducibility.

Conclusion: A fast, label-free, self-supervised CT reconstruction paradigm that capitalizes on intrinsic redundancy in projection data can deliver artifact-robust, detail-preserving images under ultra-low data, offering a practical alternative to slow iterative reconstructions and data-hungry supervised approaches.

Abstract: Noise and artifacts during computed tomography (CT) scans are a fundamental challenge affecting disease diagnosis. However, current methods either involve excessively long reconstruction times or rely on data-driven models for optimization, failing to adequately consider the valuable information inherent in the data itself, especially medical 3D data. This work proposes a reconstruction method under ultra-low raw data conditions, requiring no external data and avoiding lengthy pre-training processes. By leveraging spatial nonlocal similarity and the conjugate properties of the projection domain to generate pseudo-3D data for self-supervised training, high-fidelity results can be achieved in a very short time. Extensive experiments demonstrate that this method not only mitigates detector-induced ring artifacts but also exhibits unprecedented capabilities in detail recovery. This method provides a new paradigm for research using unlabeled raw projection data. Code is available at https://github.com/yqx7150/SCOUT.

</details>


### [115] [STMI: Segmentation-Guided Token Modulation with Cross-Modal Hypergraph Interaction for Multi-Modal Object Re-Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00695)
*Xingguo Xu,Zhanyu Liu,Weixiang Zhou,Yuansheng Gao,Junjie Cao,Yuhao Wang,Jixiang Luo,Dell Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: STMI is a multi-modal ReID framework that uses segmentation-guided feature modulation, token reallocation, and cross-modal hypergraph interaction to enhance discriminative cues, reduce background noise, and model high-order relations, showing effective and robust performance on RGBNT201/100 and MSVR310.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-modal ReID methods often rely on hard token filtering and simplistic fusion, which discard useful information and amplify background interference. The authors aim to retain discriminative cues while suppressing noise and to better capture cross-modal, high-order semantics.

Method: STMI comprises three modules: (1) Segmentation-Guided Feature Modulation (SFM) uses SAM-generated masks to boost foreground features and downweight background via learnable attention; (2) Semantic Token Reallocation (STR) replaces hard token dropping with learnable queries and adaptive reallocation to form compact, informative representations without losing tokens; (3) Cross-Modal Hypergraph Interaction (CHI) builds a unified hypergraph across modalities to model high-order semantic relationships for robust matching.

Result: Across public benchmarks (RGBNT201, RGBNT100, MSVR310), experiments indicate that STMI improves multi-modal ReID effectiveness and robustness; specific metrics are not provided in the abstract.

Conclusion: By enhancing foreground focus, preserving informative tokens, and modeling high-order cross-modal relations, STMI addresses key limitations of prior approaches and delivers robust performance for multi-modal ReID on several benchmarks.

Abstract: Multi-modal object Re-Identification (ReID) aims to exploit complementary information from different modalities to retrieve specific objects. However, existing methods often rely on hard token filtering or simple fusion strategies, which can lead to the loss of discriminative cues and increased background interference. To address these challenges, we propose STMI, a novel multi-modal learning framework consisting of three key components: (1) Segmentation-Guided Feature Modulation (SFM) module leverages SAM-generated masks to enhance foreground representations and suppress background noise through learnable attention modulation; (2) Semantic Token Reallocation (STR) module employs learnable query tokens and an adaptive reallocation mechanism to extract compact and informative representations without discarding any tokens; (3) Cross-Modal Hypergraph Interaction (CHI) module constructs a unified hypergraph across modalities to capture high-order semantic relationships. Extensive experiments on public benchmarks (i.e., RGBNT201, RGBNT100, and MSVR310) demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of our proposed STMI framework in multi-modal ReID scenarios.

</details>


### [116] [TokenSplat: Token-aligned 3D Gaussian Splatting for Feed-forward Pose-free Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00697)
*Yihui Li,Chengxin Lv,Zichen Tang,Hongyu Yang,Di Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TokenSplat is a feed‑forward method that jointly reconstructs 3D Gaussians and estimates camera poses from unposed multi‑view images by aligning cross‑view features with tokens and disentangling viewpoint from scene content.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pose‑free 3D reconstruction and novel‑view synthesis remain challenging because cross‑view correspondences are hard to establish without known poses, and iterative optimization (e.g., SfM/BA) is slow and brittle. Existing pose‑free methods often entangle camera viewpoint cues with scene semantics, leading to unstable pose estimates and redundant or inconsistent 3D representations. The paper seeks a fast, coherent, and robust feed‑forward alternative.

Method: Introduce a Token‑aligned Gaussian Prediction module that directly aligns semantically corresponding features across views, guided by coarse token locations and a fusion‑confidence mechanism. Aggregate multi‑scale contextual features to support long‑range cross‑view reasoning and suppress redundant overlapping Gaussians. Use learnable camera tokens plus an Asymmetric Dual‑Flow Decoder (ADF‑Decoder) that restricts how camera and image tokens communicate, promoting clean factorization between viewpoint cues and scene semantics. The entire pipeline is feed‑forward, avoiding iterative refinement while outputting both 3D Gaussian splats and camera poses.

Result: Across extensive experiments, TokenSplat yields higher reconstruction fidelity and novel‑view synthesis quality in pose‑free settings, and achieves notably better pose estimation accuracy compared to prior pose‑free approaches.

Conclusion: Carefully aligned token interactions and asymmetric message passing enable coherent 3D Gaussian reconstruction and stable pose estimation in a single feed‑forward pass. This design reduces redundancy, improves cross‑view reasoning, and offers a practical alternative to iterative pose‑recovery pipelines.

Abstract: We present TokenSplat, a feed-forward framework for joint 3D Gaussian reconstruction and camera pose estimation from unposed multi-view images. At its core, TokenSplat introduces a Token-aligned Gaussian Prediction module that aligns semantically corresponding information across views directly in the feature space. Guided by coarse token positions and fusion confidence, it aggregates multi-scale contextual features to enable long-range cross-view reasoning and reduce redundancy from overlapping Gaussians. To further enhance pose robustness and disentangle viewpoint cues from scene semantics, TokenSplat employs learnable camera tokens and an Asymmetric Dual-Flow Decoder (ADF-Decoder) that enforces directionally constrained communication between camera and image tokens. This maintains clean factorization within a feed-forward architecture, enabling coherent reconstruction and stable pose estimation without iterative refinement. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TokenSplat achieves higher reconstruction fidelity and novel-view synthesis quality in pose-free settings, and significantly improves pose estimation accuracy compared to prior pose-free methods. Project page: https://kidleyh.github.io/tokensplat/.

</details>


### [117] [Towards Universal Khmer Text Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00702)
*Marry Kong,Rina Buoy,Sovisal Chenda,Nguonly Taing,Masakazu Iwamura,Koichi Kise*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They introduce a single universal Khmer OCR model with a modality-aware adaptive feature selection module to handle printed, handwritten, and scene text, achieving state-of-the-art results and releasing a new benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Khmer OCR is hampered by complex script and scarce data, especially for handwritten and scene text. Training separate models per modality prevents transfer to low-data settings, adds memory/routing overhead, and naïve joint training biases toward majority modalities, hurting underrepresented ones.

Method: Universal Khmer Text Recognition (UKTR): one model trained across modalities. Core component is Modality-Aware Adaptive Feature Selection (MAFS), which conditions feature extraction/selection on the input modality to balance contributions across modalities and improve robustness, enabling cross-modality transfer without separate models.

Result: Extensive experiments show state-of-the-art performance across Khmer text modalities, with improved robustness on underrepresented modalities. The unified model reduces deployment complexity and memory footprint compared to multiple modality-specific models.

Conclusion: A modality-adaptive, single-model framework is effective for low-resource, multi-modality Khmer text recognition. Alongside SOTA results, the work provides the first comprehensive universal Khmer benchmark to catalyze future research; datasets/models will be released (gated) pending review.

Abstract: Khmer is a low-resource language characterized by a complex script, presenting significant challenges for optical character recognition (OCR). While document printed text recognition has advanced because of available datasets, performance on other modalities, such as handwritten and scene text, remains limited by data scarcity. Training modality-specific models for each modality does not allow cross-modality transfer learning, from which modalities with limited data could otherwise benefit. Moreover, deploying many modality-specific models results in significant memory overhead and requires error-prone routing each input image to the appropriate model. On the other hand, simply training on a combined dataset with a non-uniform data distribution across different modalities often leads to degraded performance on underrepresented modalities. To address these, we propose a universal Khmer text recognition (UKTR) framework capable of handling diverse text modalities. Central to our method is a novel modality-aware adaptive feature selection (MAFS) technique designed to adapt visual features according to a particular input image modality and enhance recognition robustness across modalities. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our model achieves state-of-the-art (SoTA) performance. Furthermore, we introduce the first comprehensive benchmark for universal Khmer text recognition, which we release to the community to facilitate future research. Our datasets and models can be accessible via this gated repository\footnote{in review}.

</details>


### [118] [Towards Khmer Scene Document Layout Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00707)
*Marry Kong,Rina Buoy,Sovisal Chenda,Nguonly Taing,Masakazu Iwamura,Koichi Kise*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: First comprehensive study on Khmer scene document layout detection, releasing a Khmer-specific dataset, a scene-document augmentation tool, and YOLO-based oriented-bounding-box baselines to handle geometric distortions; resources are promised public (gated, in review).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Layout analysis progress in Latin scripts has not translated to Khmer due to scarce annotations and Khmer script complexities (diacritics, stacked characters). Scene documents add perspective and background clutter, and Latin-trained models fail on dense Khmer regions (e.g., lists).

Method: (1) Construct a robust training/benchmark dataset for Khmer scene layouts; (2) build an open-source augmentation tool to synthesize realistic Khmer scene documents for scaling data; (3) develop and benchmark YOLO-family detectors using oriented bounding boxes (OBB) to better capture rotated/skewed regions and dense text layouts.

Result: They provide the dataset, code, and baseline models; establish benchmarking protocols and OBB-based YOLO baselines for Khmer scene layout detection. The abstract does not report quantitative metrics but positions OBB-based detectors as suitable for geometric distortions.

Conclusion: This work fills a key resource gap and sets a baseline for Khmer DAR. The released tools and data aim to catalyze research; OBB-centric detection appears promising for non-Latin, distortion-prone scene documents, though detailed performance evidence is not provided in the abstract.

Abstract: While document layout analysis for Latin scripts has advanced significantly, driven by the advent of large multimodal models (LMMs), progress for the Khmer language remains constrained because of the scarcity of annotated training data. This gap is particularly acute for scene documents, where perspective distortions and complex backgrounds challenge traditional methods. Given the structural complexities of Khmer script, such as diacritics and multi-layer character stacking, existing Latin-based layout analysis models fail to accurately delineate semantic layout units, particularly for dense text regions (e.g., list items). In this paper, we present the first comprehensive study on Khmer scene document layout detection. We contribute a novel framework comprising three key elements: (1) a robust training and benchmarking dataset specifically for Khmer scene layouts; (2) an open-source document augmentation tool capable of synthesizing realistic scene documents to scale training data; and (3) layout detection baselines utilizing YOLO-based architectures with oriented bounding boxes (OBB) to handle geometric distortions. To foster further research in the Khmer document analysis and recognition (DAR) community, we release our models, code, and datasets in this gated repository (in review).

</details>


### [119] [A Reconstruction System for Industrial Pipeline Inner Walls Using Panoramic Image Stitching with Endoscopic Imaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00714)
*Rui Ma,Yifeng Wang,Ziteng Yang,Xinghui Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A GUI-based system reconstructs pipeline inner walls from endoscope videos by unwrapping annular frames via polar transformation and stitching them into seamless panoramas, greatly improving inspection efficiency and preserving fine defect details.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional frame-by-frame endoscope review is time-consuming and makes it hard to form an intuitive, comprehensive view of pipeline interiors. Industrial conditions make accurate visualization and defect localization challenging, creating the need for efficient, detail-preserving reconstruction tools.

Method: From endoscope video, the system selects keyframes through a custom GUI, applies polar coordinate transformation to unwrap circular/annular frames into planar strips, and stitches these unwrapped images into a continuous panoramic view of the pipe’s inner surface.

Result: Experiments show efficient processing of industrial endoscope videos; the stitched panoramas maintain complete, detailed features of the inner wall, enabling intuitive visualization for defect detection and condition assessment. The approach significantly outperforms traditional frame-by-frame review in efficiency and shows strong engineering applicability.

Conclusion: Panoramic stitching with polar unwrapping provides an effective, practical solution for reconstructing pipeline interiors from endoscope footage, improving both inspection speed and visual clarity for reliable defect assessment in real-world industrial settings.

Abstract: Visual analysis and reconstruction of pipeline inner walls remain challenging in industrial inspection scenarios. This paper presents a dedicated reconstruction system for pipeline inner walls via industrial endoscopes, which is built on panoramic image stitching technology. Equipped with a custom graphical user interface (GUI), the system extracts key frames from endoscope video footage, and integrates polar coordinate transformation with image stitching techniques to unwrap annular video frames of pipeline inner walls into planar panoramic images. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method enables efficient processing of industrial endoscope videos, and the generated panoramic stitched images preserve all detailed features of pipeline inner walls in their entirety. This provides intuitive and accurate visual support for defect detection and condition assessment of pipeline inner walls. In comparison with the traditional frame-by-frame video review method, the proposed approach significantly elevates the efficiency of pipeline inner wall reconstruction and exhibits considerable engineering application value.

</details>


### [120] [Diversity over Uniformity: Rethinking Representation in Generated Image Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00717)
*Qinghui He,Haifeng Zhang,Qiao Qin,Bo Liu,Xiuli Bi,Bin Xiao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They propose an anti-feature-collapse learning framework for generated image detection that preserves multiple complementary forgery cues rather than overfitting to a few salient ones, yielding stronger cross-model generalization and ~5% accuracy gains over SOTA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current detectors tend to latch onto a small set of highly salient artifacts left by specific generators, which harms generalization to unseen generative mechanisms. Reliable detection should integrate multiple judgment perspectives so decisions don’t hinge on a single cue that may disappear under distribution shift.

Method: An anti-feature-collapse training framework that (1) filters task-irrelevant components and (2) suppresses excessive overlap among different forgery cues in the representation space. By discouraging discriminative information from concentrating along a few dominant feature directions, the model retains diverse, complementary evidence for real-vs-generated discrimination. Likely implemented via feature-space regularization/decoupling, diversity or orthogonality constraints, and cue-specific separation to reduce redundancy.

Result: Across multiple public benchmarks and cross-model settings, the method outperforms prior state-of-the-art detectors, with a reported +5.02% accuracy improvement and better generalization and reliability on unseen generators.

Conclusion: Maintaining diversity among discriminative features increases robustness to unseen generative mechanisms. By preventing representation collapse onto a few cues, the approach yields more reliable generated image detection; released code supports reproducibility.

Abstract: With the rapid advancement of generative models, generated image detection has become an important task in visual forensics. Although existing methods have achieved remarkable progress, they often rely, after training, on only a small subset of highly salient forgery cues, which limits their ability to generalize to unseen generative mechanisms. We argue that reliably generated image detection should not depend on a single decision path but should preserve multiple judgment perspectives, enabling the model to understand the differences between real and generated images from diverse viewpoints. Based on this idea, we propose an anti-feature-collapse learning framework that filters task-irrelevant components and suppresses excessive overlap among different forgery cues in the representation space, preventing discriminative information from collapsing into a few dominant feature directions. This design maintains diverse and complementary evidence within the model, reduces reliance on a small set of salient cues, and enhances robustness under unseen generative settings. Extensive experiments on multiple public benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed method significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art approaches in cross-model scenarios, achieving an accuracy improvement of 5.02% and exhibiting superior generalization and detection reliability. The source code is available at https://github.com/Yanmou-Hui/DoU.

</details>


### [121] [Unified Vision-Language Modeling via Concept Space Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01096)
*Yifu Qiu,Paul-Ambroise Duquenne,Holger Schwenk*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: V-SONAR aligns a vision encoder into the omnilingual SONAR embedding space, enabling a unified vision–language latent space that delivers competitive retrieval and state-of-the-art video captioning; extending this to V-LCM with instruction tuning yields near-SOTA or better performance on captioning/QA, especially across many low-resource languages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unify vision and language within a single, massively multilingual embedding space to: (1) leverage SONAR’s coverage (1500 text, 177 speech languages); (2) enable zero-shot multimodal understanding from largely English-only pretraining; and (3) improve performance and fairness across rich- to low-resource languages without bespoke per-language modeling.

Method: Post-hoc alignment maps an existing vision encoder’s representations into the SONAR text embedding space, forming V-SONAR. Using the OMNISONAR text decoder enables caption generation from these aligned embeddings. Building on this, V-LCM encodes both modalities into a single latent sequence (via V-SONAR for vision and SONAR for text) and trains with LCM’s latent diffusion next-embedding prediction objective using a large multilingual, multimodal instruction-tuning corpus.

Result: V-SONAR achieves competitive text-to-video retrieval and surpasses prior SOTA on video captioning: DREAM-1K (BLEU 23.9 vs. 19.6) and PE-VIDEO (BLEU 39.0 vs. 30.0). The SONAR-based LCM shows zero-shot single- and multi-visual concept understanding. V-LCM matches SOTA on image/video captioning and QA, while significantly outperforming alternatives in 61 of 62 evaluated languages.

Conclusion: A post-hoc alignment into SONAR effectively yields an omnilingual vision–language space. Coupled with LCM-style latent diffusion training, this unified latent approach scales well across modalities and languages, delivering strong captioning/QA performance and notable gains in low-resource settings, highlighting a promising path toward omnilingual multimodal models.

Abstract: We introduce V-SONAR, a vision-language embedding space extended from the text-only embedding space SONAR (Omnilingual Embeddings Team et al., 2026), which supports 1500 text languages and 177 speech languages. To construct V-SONAR, we propose a post-hoc alignment pipeline that maps the representations of an existing vision encoder into the SONAR space. We thoroughly evaluate V-SONAR and show that its embeddings achieve competitive performance on text-to-video retrieval. Equipped with the OMNISONAR text decoder, V-SONAR further surpasses state-of-the-art vision-language models on video captioning tasks, including DREAM-1K (BLEU 23.9 vs. 19.6) and PE-VIDEO (BLEU 39.0 vs. 30.0).
  Leveraging V-SONAR, we first demonstrate that the Large Concept Model (LCM; LCM team et al. 2024) operating in SONAR and trained with English text only, can perform both single- and multi-visual concept understanding in a zero-shot manner. Finally, we introduce V-LCM, which extends the LCM with vision-language instruction tuning. V-LCM encodes vision and language inputs into an unified sequence of latent embeddings via V-SONAR and SONAR, and it is trained with the same latent diffusion objective for next-embedding prediction as in LCM's text-only pre-training. Experiments on a large-scale multilingual and -modal instruction-tuning data mixture highlight the potential of V-LCM: V-LCM matches state-of-the-art vision-language models on tasks covering image/video captioning and question answering, while significantly outperforming them across 61 rich- to low-resource languages out of all 62 tested languages.

</details>


### [122] [BornoViT: A Novel Efficient Vision Transformer for Bengali Handwritten Basic Characters Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00755)
*Rafi Hassan Chowdhury,Naimul Haque,Kaniz Fatiha*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BornoViT is a lightweight Vision Transformer with simplified CNN components for Bengali handwritten basic characters and digits, reaching 95.77% accuracy on BanglaLekha Isolated and 91.51% on a small self-collected set, while using only 0.65M parameters, ~0.62 MB, and 0.16 GFLOPs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bengali handwritten character recognition is hard due to high visual variability, while common models are computationally heavy and data-hungry—unsuitable for low-resource languages and deployment on constrained devices. The goal is to deliver accurate recognition with minimal compute and memory.

Method: Design a compact ViT (BornoViT) that integrates streamlined DCNN elements for efficient feature/patch extraction and classification, emphasizing a very low parameter count and FLOPs. Train/evaluate on BanglaLekha Isolated for basic characters/digits and test on a small, self-collected Bornomala set to gauge real-world robustness.

Result: BornoViT attains 95.77% accuracy on BanglaLekha Isolated and 91.51% on the 222-sample Bornomala dataset, with markedly reduced compute (0.16 GFLOPs), parameters (0.65M), and reported model size (~0.62 MB), outperforming state-of-the-art methods in efficiency.

Conclusion: A highly efficient and compact architecture can maintain strong accuracy for Bengali handwriting recognition, making it suitable for resource-limited settings. The approach appears to generalize beyond the benchmark dataset, suggesting potential for practical deployment.

Abstract: Handwritten character classification in the Bengali script is a significant challenge due to the complexity and variability of the characters. The models commonly used for classification are often computationally expensive and data-hungry, making them unsuitable for resource-limited languages such as Bengali. In this experiment, we propose a novel, efficient, and lightweight Vision Transformer model that effectively classifies Bengali handwritten basic characters and digits, addressing several shortcomings of traditional methods. The proposed solution utilizes a deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) in a more simplified manner compared to traditional DCNN architectures, with the aim of reducing computational burden. With only 0.65 million parameters, a model size of 0.62 MB, and 0.16 GFLOPs, our model, BornoViT, is significantly lighter than current state-of-the-art models, making it more suitable for resource-limited environments, which is essential for Bengali handwritten character classification. BornoViT was evaluated on the BanglaLekha Isolated dataset, achieving an accuracy of 95.77%, and demonstrating superior efficiency compared to existing state-of-the-art approaches. Furthermore, the model was evaluated on our self-collected dataset, Bornomala, consisting of approximately 222 samples from different age groups, where it achieved an accuracy of 91.51%.

</details>


### [123] [Stroke outcome and evolution prediction from CT brain using a spatiotemporal diffusion autoencoder](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00756)
*Adam Marcus,Paul Bentley,Daniel Rueckert*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Self-supervised, diffusion-model-based representations from CT—augmented with longitudinal scans and time-since-onset—improve prediction of next-day stroke severity and discharge functional outcome, outperforming baselines on a two-center cohort (5,824 scans, 3,573 patients) with minimal labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Stroke outcome prediction could individualize care and improve decisions, but modeling tissue fate is hard due to complex spatiotemporal evolution, limited labels, and heterogeneity. CT is widely available yet underutilized for robust, semantically meaningful, label-efficient prognostics.

Method: Leverage diffusion probabilistic models to learn self-supervised, semantically meaningful representations from acute stroke CT images. Extend the framework to incorporate longitudinal imaging and conditioning on time-from-onset, capturing temporal disease dynamics while using minimal labels for downstream prediction.

Result: On 5,824 CT scans from 3,573 patients across two medical centers, the proposed representations achieved the best comparative performance for predicting next-day stroke severity and functional outcome at discharge, surpassing alternative methods in minimally supervised settings.
