Conclusion: Time-aware, longitudinal, self-supervised diffusion representations from CT enable stronger, label-efficient prediction of early and discharge outcomes in stroke, suggesting a practical path toward more individualized, data-driven clinical decision-making using routine imaging.

Abstract: Stroke is a major cause of death and disability worldwide. Accurate outcome and evolution prediction has the potential to revolutionize stroke care by individualizing clinical decision-making leading to better outcomes. However, despite a plethora of attempts and the rich data provided by neuroimaging, modelling the ultimate fate of brain tissue remains a challenging task. In this work, we apply recent ideas in the field of diffusion probabilistic models to generate a self-supervised semantically meaningful stroke representation from Computed Tomography (CT) images. We then improve this representation by extending the method to accommodate longitudinal images and the time from stroke onset. The effectiveness of our approach is evaluated on a dataset consisting of 5,824 CT images from 3,573 patients across two medical centers with minimal labels. Comparative experiments show that our method achieves the best performance for predicting next-day severity and functional outcome at discharge.

</details>


### [124] [Analyzing and Improving Fast Sampling of Text-to-Image Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00763)
*Zhenyu Zhou,Defang Chen,Siwei Lyu,Chun Chen,Can Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces a training-free sampling schedule, TORS, that uses geometric insights (Frenet–Serret) to allocate time steps so total rotation along the diffusion trajectory is constant, markedly improving few-step generation (≈10 steps) and outperforming prior acceleration methods across models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models are slow at low sampling budgets. Existing training-free acceleration techniques are developed in isolation, with unclear interactions and no clear understanding of what matters most. The authors aim to map this design space, identify the key driver of performance, and devise a principled schedule that yields high-quality images in very few steps.

Method: Systematically analyze training-free sampling acceleration methods and ablations to pinpoint the most critical factor—time scheduling. Using a geometric view of diffusion trajectories via Frenet–Serret formulas, propose the constant total rotation schedule (TORS) that enforces uniform geometric variation (rotation) along the trajectory. The approach is plug-and-play, modifying only the step schedule without retraining, and is evaluated on multiple text-to-image models.

Result: TORS consistently outperforms previous training-free acceleration baselines and produces high-quality images with as few as 10 steps on Flux.1-Dev and Stable Diffusion 3.5. Experiments show robustness to different models, hyperparameters, and downstream tasks, indicating strong adaptability and generalization.

Conclusion: Time scheduling is the dominant lever in training-free diffusion sampling. A geometry-aware schedule (TORS) that equalizes trajectory rotation enables efficient, high-quality few-step sampling and generalizes across models and settings, offering a simple, compatible drop-in improvement over existing methods.

Abstract: Text-to-image diffusion models have achieved unprecedented success but still struggle to produce high-quality results under limited sampling budgets. Existing training-free sampling acceleration methods are typically developed independently, leaving the overall performance and compatibility among these methods unexplored. In this paper, we bridge this gap by systematically elucidating the design space, and our comprehensive experiments identify the sampling time schedule as the most pivotal factor. Inspired by the geometric properties of diffusion models revealed through the Frenet-Serret formulas, we propose constant total rotation schedule (TORS), a scheduling strategy that ensures uniform geometric variation along the sampling trajectory. TORS outperforms previous training-free acceleration methods and produces high-quality images with 10 sampling steps on Flux.1-Dev and Stable Diffusion 3.5. Extensive experiments underscore the adaptability of our method to unseen models, hyperparameters, and downstream applications.

</details>


### [125] [DUCX: Decomposing Unfairness in Tool-Using Chest X-ray Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00777)
*Zikang Xu,Ruinan Jin,Xiaoxiao Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They propose a stage‑wise fairness audit for chest X‑ray tool‑using agents and show sizable demographic disparities not only in end‑to‑end accuracy but also inside the agent’s pipeline (tool access, routing, and reasoning), motivating process‑level debiasing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tool‑using medical agents combine vision and language modules and routing logic; this complexity can introduce new, hidden fairness failures beyond standalone models. Current evaluations largely focus on end‑to‑end outcomes, which can miss where and how disparities arise. The authors aim to localize and quantify bias sources to enable targeted mitigation and safer clinical deployment.

Method: Instantiate chest X‑ray QA agents with MedRAX and propose a stage‑wise fairness decomposition that separates overall bias into: (1) tool exposure bias—utility gaps conditioned on tool availability; (2) tool transition bias—subgroup differences in routing/which tools get called; and (3) model reasoning bias—subgroup differences in how information is synthesized. Evaluate across multiple tool‑based agentic frameworks and five backbone models using metrics like equalized odds and subgroup utility gaps while analyzing tool usage patterns and reasoning traces.

Result: Demographic gaps persist in end‑to‑end performance (equalized odds up to 20.79%) with notable fairness‑utility tradeoffs (as low as 28.65%). Internal pipeline behaviors show even larger, non‑obvious disparities; e.g., when a segmentation tool is available, subgroup utility gaps can reach ~50%. End‑to‑end metrics do not predict these internal disparities.

Conclusion: Fairness issues in clinical agentic systems arise at multiple pipeline stages. Audits must move beyond end‑to‑end metrics to process‑level evaluation and debiasing (tool availability, routing, and reasoning) to ensure equitable deployment. Code is released to support reproducibility and further research.

Abstract: Tool-using medical agents can improve chest X-ray question answering by orchestrating specialized vision and language modules, but this added pipeline complexity also creates new pathways for demographic bias beyond standalone models. We present ours (Decomposing Unfairness in Chest X-ray agents), a systematic audit of chest X-ray agents instantiated with MedRAX. To localize where disparities arise, we introduce a stage-wise fairness decomposition that separates end-to-end bias from three agent-specific sources: tool exposure bias (utility gaps conditioned on tool presence), tool transition bias (subgroup differences in tool-routing patterns), and model reasoning bias (subgroup differences in synthesis behaviors). Extensive experiments on tool-used based agentic frameworks across five driver backbones reveal that (i) demographic gaps persist in end-to-end performance, with equalized odds up to 20.79%, and the lowest fairness-utility tradeoff down to 28.65%, and (ii) intermediate behaviors, tool usage, transition patterns, and reasoning traces exhibit distinct subgroup disparities that are not predictable from end-to-end evaluation alone (e.g., conditioned on segmentation-tool availability, the subgroup utility gap reaches as high as 50%). Our findings underscore the need for process-level fairness auditing and debiasing to ensure the equitable deployment of clinical agentic systems. Code is available here: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/DUCK-E5FE/README.md

</details>


### [126] [From Verbatim to Gist: Distilling Pyramidal Multimodal Memory via Semantic Information Bottleneck for Long-Horizon Video Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01455)
*Niu Lian,Yuting Wang,Hanshu Yao,Jinpeng Wang,Bin Chen,Yaowei Wang,Min Zhang,Shu-Tao Xia*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MM-Mem is a Fuzzy-Trace–inspired hierarchical memory for multimodal LLMs that enables long-horizon video understanding via progressive compression, information-bottleneck–guided training, and uncertainty-aware, top-down retrieval, yielding strong results on offline and streaming benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs handle short-term reasoning but fail on long videos due to limited context and static memories. Existing solutions either cache dense vision features (slow, redundant) or over-caption (losing details, hallucinating). The goal is a cognitively efficient memory that balances fidelity and abstraction while scaling to long horizons.

Method: Introduce MM-Mem, a pyramidal multimodal memory with three tiers: (1) Sensory Buffer for fine-grained perceptual traces (verbatim), (2) Episodic Stream for temporally organized events, and (3) Symbolic Schema for high-level semantic gist. Train memory construction with a Semantic Information Bottleneck objective and optimize using SIB-GRPO to trade compression with task-relevant retention. At inference, use entropy-driven top-down retrieval: start with the abstract schema and drill down to episodic/sensory levels when uncertainty is high.

Result: Across four benchmarks, MM-Mem improves long-horizon video QA/understanding in both offline and streaming settings, showing better efficiency and robustness than prior vision- or text-centric baselines; it generalizes well across tasks. (No specific metrics given in the abstract.)

Conclusion: Cognition-inspired hierarchical memory plus an information-bottleneck training signal and uncertainty-aware retrieval effectively address long-context video understanding, mitigating latency, redundancy, and hallucination while preserving task-relevant details. Code is released for reproducibility.

Abstract: While multimodal large language models have demonstrated impressive short-term reasoning, they struggle with long-horizon video understanding due to limited context windows and static memory mechanisms that fail to mirror human cognitive efficiency. Existing paradigms typically fall into two extremes: vision-centric methods that incur high latency and redundancy through dense visual accumulation, or text-centric approaches that suffer from detail loss and hallucination via aggressive captioning. To bridge this gap, we propose MM-Mem, a pyramidal multimodal memory architecture grounded in Fuzzy-Trace Theory. MM-Mem structures memory hierarchically into a Sensory Buffer, Episodic Stream, and Symbolic Schema, enabling the progressive distillation of fine-grained perceptual traces (verbatim) into high-level semantic schemas (gist). Furthermore, to govern the dynamic construction of memory, we derive a Semantic Information Bottleneck objective and introduce SIB-GRPO to optimize the trade-off between memory compression and task-relevant information retention. In inference, we design an entropy-driven top-down memory retrieval strategy, which first tries with the abstract Symbolic Schema and progressively "drills down" to the Sensory Buffer and Episodic Stream under high uncertainty. Extensive experiments across 4 benchmarks confirm the effectiveness of MM-Mem on both offline and streaming tasks, demonstrating robust generalization and validating the effectiveness of cognition-inspired memory organization. Code is available at https://github.com/EliSpectre/MM-Mem.

</details>


### [127] [Neural Functional Alignment Space: Brain-Referenced Representation of Artificial Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00793)
*Ruiyu Yan,Hanqi Jiang,Yi Pan,Xiaobo Li,Tianming Liu,Xi Jiang,Lin Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They introduce NFAS, a brain-referenced space that characterizes neural networks by the dynamics of their internal representations, using DMD to extract a stable mode and projecting it onto neural data; it reveals structured, brain-aligned organization across 45 models and modalities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional model-brain alignment often compares layer-by-layer features or task activations, which can be task- or architecture-specific and miss how representations evolve. The goal is to place diverse ANNs on equal functional grounds and link them to biological organization across modalities.

Method: Treat layer-wise embeddings as a depth-wise dynamical system; apply Dynamic Mode Decomposition to obtain a stable mode summarizing representational evolution; project this mode into a biologically anchored coordinate system derived from distributed neural responses; introduce the Signal-to-Noise Consistency Index (SNCI) to quantify cross-model, modality-level consistency.

Result: Across 45 pretrained vision, audio, and language models, NFAS shows structured organization: modality-specific clusters and cross-modal convergence in integrative cortical systems. The SNCI provides a quantitative measure of consistency across models at the modality level.

Conclusion: Representation dynamics furnish a principled, brain-referenced basis for comparing and organizing ANNs across modalities. NFAS (plus SNCI) offers a unified framework that captures both modality-specific structure and cross-modal integration aligned with cortical organization.

Abstract: We propose the Neural Functional Alignment Space (NFAS), a brain-referenced representational framework for characterizing artificial neural networks on equal functional grounds. NFAS departs from conventional alignment approaches that rely on layer-wise features or task-specific activations by modeling the intrinsic dynamical evolution of stimulus representations across network depth. Specifically, we model layer-wise embeddings as a depth-wise dynamical trajectory and apply Dynamic Mode Decomposition (DMD) to extract the stable mode. This representation is then projected into a biologically anchored coordinate system defined by distributed neural responses. We also introduce the Signal-to-Noise Consistency Index (SNCI) to quantify cross-model consistency at the modality level. Across 45 pretrained models spanning vision, audio, and language, NFAS reveals structured organization within this brain-referenced space, including modality-specific clustering and cross-modal convergence in integrative cortical systems. Our findings suggest that representation dynamics provide a principled basis for

</details>


### [128] [NERFIFY: A Multi-Agent Framework for Turning NeRF Papers into Code](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00805)
*Seemandhar Jain,Keshav Gupta,Kunal Gupta,Manmohan Chandraker*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: NERFIFY is a domain-aware, multi-agent system that converts NeRF papers into runnable Nerfstudio plugins using grammar-constrained and dependency-ordered code synthesis, component retrieval from citations, and visual-feedback loops, achieving near-human reproduction quality in minutes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Re-implementing NeRF papers is slow and error-prone, and generic paper-to-code LLMs often fail to produce executable, high-quality implementations. A domain-specific approach could accelerate reproducible research by making paper-to-code translation reliable and fast.

Method: Six components: (1) Constrain LLM generation with a context-free grammar encoding Nerfstudio’s architectural invariants. (2) Graph-of-Thought, multi-file agent synthesis that generates repositories in topological dependency order with contract/error validation. (3) Automatically retrieve and compose referenced modules (samplers, encoders, proposal networks) via citation graphs. (4) Iterative visual feedback loops using PSNR-minima ROI analysis, cross-view geometric checks, and VLM-guided patches. (5) Knowledge enhancement to add novel optimizations beyond strict reproduction. (6) Benchmark framework covering 30 NeRF papers to evaluate paper-to-code executability and quality.

Result: For papers without public code, NERFIFY reaches visual quality close to expert implementations (±0.5 dB PSNR, ±0.2 SSIM) and cuts implementation time from weeks to minutes. Code/data/implementations will be released.

Conclusion: A domain-constrained, multi-agent design enables reliable paper-to-code translation for complex NeRF methods, supporting faster, democratized, and more reproducible research.

Abstract: The proliferation of neural radiance field (NeRF) research requires significant efforts to reimplement papers before building upon them. We introduce NERFIFY, a multi-agent framework that reliably converts NeRF research papers into trainable Nerfstudio plugins, in contrast to generic paper-to-code methods and frontier models like GPT-5 that usually fail to produce runnable code. NERFIFY achieves domain-specific executability through six key innovations: (1) Context-free grammar (CFG): LLM synthesis is constrained by Nerfstudio formalized as a CFG, ensuring generated code satisfies architectural invariants. (2) Graph-of-Thought code synthesis: Specialized multi-file-agents generate repositories in topological dependency order, validating contracts and errors at each node. (3) Compositional citation recovery: Agents automatically retrieve and integrate components (samplers, encoders, proposal networks) from citation graphs of references. (4) Visual feedback: Artifacts are diagnosed through PSNR-minima ROI analysis, cross-view geometric validation, and VLM-guided patching to iteratively improve quality. (5) Knowledge enhancement: Beyond reproduction, methods can be improved with novel optimizations. (6) Benchmarking: An evaluation framework is designed for NeRF paper-to-code synthesis across 30 diverse papers. On papers without public implementations, NERFIFY achieves visual quality matching expert human code (+/-0.5 dB PSNR, +/-0.2 SSIM) while reducing implementation time from weeks to minutes. NERFIFY demonstrates that a domain-aware design enables code translation for complex vision papers, potentiating accelerated and democratized reproducible research. Code, data and implementations will be publicly released.

</details>


### [129] [COMBAT: Conditional World Models for Behavioral Agent Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00825)
*Anmol Agarwal,Pranay Meshram,Sumer Singh,Saurav Suman,Andrew Lapp,Shahbuland Matiana,Louis Castricato,Spencer Frazier*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: COMBAT is a 1.2B-parameter diffusion-transformer world model trained on Tekken 3 that generates action-conditioned, real-time video with a dynamically reactive opponent whose behavior emerges implicitly from single-player data, enabled by latent conditioning, causal distillation, and diffusion forcing, alongside new benchmarks for evaluating such emergent agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most video/world models produce 3D-consistent scenes but struggle to model interactive, reactive agents. Traditional imitation learning also needs full action labels. The paper seeks a way to learn a responsive opponent policy implicitly from partially observed data while maintaining real-time generation.

Method: Train a 1.2B Diffusion Transformer conditioned on compressed video latents from a deep autoencoder. Use causal distillation and diffusion forcing to accelerate/guide sampling for real-time inference. Train only on single-player inputs, letting the opponent’s behavior emerge implicitly. Provide control over Player 1 actions and evaluate with new metrics for responsiveness/agency.

Result: The model produces a dynamic opponent that reacts coherently to Player 1 in real time, displaying sophisticated emergent behavior without explicit opponent action labels. It learns effectively from partially observed data and supports interactive control. The paper additionally offers novel evaluation protocols to benchmark this emergent agent behavior.

Conclusion: Diffusion-based world models can host interactive agents whose policies emerge from data without explicit supervision, achieving real-time, action-conditioned simulation. This establishes a practical foundation and evaluation suite for training and assessing interactive agents within diffusion world models.

Abstract: Recent advances in video generation have spurred the development of world models capable of simulating 3D-consistent environments and interactions with static objects. However, a significant limitation remains in their ability to model dynamic, reactive agents that can intelligently influence and interact with the world. To address this gap, we introduce COMBAT, a real-time, action-controlled world model trained on the complex 1v1 fighting game Tekken 3. Our work demonstrates that diffusion models can successfully simulate a dynamic opponent that reacts to player actions, learning its behavior implicitly.
  Our approach utilizes a 1.2 billion parameter Diffusion Transformer, conditioned on latent representations from a deep compression autoencoder. We employ state-of-the-art techniques, including causal distillation and diffusion forcing, to achieve real-time inference. Crucially, we observe the emergence of sophisticated agent behavior by training the model solely on single-player inputs, without any explicit supervision for the opponent's policy. Unlike traditional imitation learning methods, which require complete action labels, COMBAT learns effectively from partially observed data to generate responsive behaviors for a controllable Player 1. We present an extensive study and introduce novel evaluation methods to benchmark this emergent agent behavior, establishing a strong foundation for training interactive agents within diffusion-based world models.

</details>


### [130] [Learning to Read Where to Look: Disease-Aware Vision-Language Pretraining for 3D CT](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02026)
*Simon Ging,Philipp Arnold,Sebastian Walter,Hani Alnahas,Hannah Bast,Elmar Kotter,Jiancheng Yang,Behzad Bozorgtabar,Thomas Brox*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A large-scale 3D CT vision–language model trained with contrastive and prompt-based disease supervision achieves state-of-the-art retrieval, competitive classification, and introduces a new intra-scan text-to-slice localization task with strong gains, forming a unified model for retrieval, classification, and grounding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior 3D CT VLMs depend on small public datasets and only provide coarse, scan-level supervision, limiting fine-grained grounding and generalization. Radiology reports often reference exact series/slice indices, offering untapped supervision for precise alignment between text and axial locations.

Method: Aggregate 98k CT report–volume pairs (50k patients) from one hospital with public datasets. Pretrain using SigLIP-style image–text contrastive learning and add prompt-based disease supervision in a shared vision–text embedding space. Automatically mine 262k report snippet–slice pairs from explicit slice references and introduce an intra-scan snippet localization objective to predict axial depth. Train a unified model jointly for retrieval, disease classification, and intra-scan grounding.

Result: On CT-RATE: text-to-image retrieval R@10 improves to 31.5 from 22.2; disease classification AUC equals the best at 83.8. On Rad-ChestCT: AUC 77.0 vs. 77.3 (competitive). For intra-scan snippet localization: mean absolute error drops to 36.3 mm at 12 mm feature resolution, beating the best baseline (67.0 mm). Adding the localization objective leaves retrieval/classification within confidence bounds.

Conclusion: Leveraging large-scale paired CT–report data with both global (contrastive, disease prompts) and local (snippet–slice) supervision yields a unified 3D CT VLM that substantially improves intra-scan grounding without sacrificing retrieval or classification. The work introduces a practical localization task and demonstrates the value of mining report-embedded slice references for fine-grained alignment.

Abstract: Recent 3D CT vision-language models align volumes with reports via contrastive pretraining, but typically rely on limited public data and provide only coarse global supervision. We train a 3D CT vision-language model on 98k report-volume pairs (50k patients) collected at a single hospital, combined with public datasets, using SigLIP-style contrastive pretraining together with prompt-based disease supervision in the shared vision-text embedding space. On CT-RATE, our model achieves state-of-the-art text-to-image retrieval (R@10 31.5 vs. 22.2) and competitive disease classification (AUC 83.8 vs. 83.8), with consistent results on Rad-ChestCT (AUC 77.0 vs. 77.3). We further observe that radiologists routinely reference specific images within their reports (e.g., ``series X, image Y''), linking textual descriptions to precise axial locations. We automatically mine 262k such snippet-slice pairs and introduce the task of intra-scan snippet localization -- predicting the axial depth referred to by a text snippet -- reducing mean absolute error to 36.3 mm at 12 mm feature resolution, compared with 67.0 mm for the best baseline. Adding this localization objective leaves retrieval and classification broadly unchanged within confidence bounds, yielding a single unified model for retrieval, classification, and intra-scan grounding.

</details>


### [131] [MME: Mixture of Mesh Experts with Random Walk Transformer Gating](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00828)
*Amir Belder,Ayellet Tal*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Mixture-of-Experts for 3D meshes with an attention-based gate and dynamic loss balancing that exploits complementary expert strengths, delivering state-of-the-art results on mesh classification, retrieval, and segmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Different mesh analysis methods excel on different object classes; combining them can better leverage their complementary strengths than relying on a single model.

Method: Introduce a Mixture of Mesh Experts (MME) with a new gate: (1) use random walks over the mesh surface to capture regions each expert attends to; (2) equip the gate with an attention mechanism to focus on areas most informative for each expert’s decision; and (3) apply dynamic loss balancing to trade off diversity (promote expert specialization) and similarity (enable knowledge sharing) during training.

Result: The framework reports state-of-the-art performance on mesh classification, retrieval, and semantic segmentation tasks.

Conclusion: Attention-guided gating plus adaptive diversity/similarity objectives fosters expert specialization while sharing knowledge, effectively leveraging complementary methods for stronger mesh analysis; code is publicly available for reproducibility.

Abstract: In recent years, various methods have been proposed for mesh analysis, each offering distinct advantages and often excelling on different object classes. We present a novel Mixture of Experts (MoE) framework designed to harness the complementary strengths of these diverse approaches. We propose a new gate architecture that encourages each expert to specialise in the classes it excels in. Our design is guided by two key ideas: (1) random walks over the mesh surface effectively capture the regions that individual experts attend to, and (2) an attention mechanism that enables the gate to focus on the areas most informative for each expert's decision-making. To further enhance performance, we introduce a dynamic loss balancing scheme that adjusts a trade-off between diversity and similarity losses throughout the training, where diversity prompts expert specialization, and similarity enables knowledge sharing among the experts. Our framework achieves state-of-the-art results in mesh classification, retrieval, and semantic segmentation tasks. Our code is available at: https://github.com/amirbelder/MME-Mixture-of-Mesh-Experts.

</details>


### [132] [Neural Discrimination-Prompted Transformers for Efficient UHD Image Restoration and Enhancement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00853)
*Cong Wang,Jinshan Pan,Liyan Wang,Wei Wang,Yang Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UHDPromer is a Transformer-based UHD image restoration/enhancement model that injects Neural Discrimination Priors (differences between high- and low-resolution features) into attention and gating, plus a super-resolution–guided reconstruction step, achieving state-of-the-art quality with best-in-class computational efficiency on low-light enhancement, dehazing, and deblurring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: UHD restoration/enhancement is computationally heavy and current models struggle to represent low-resolution features effectively. The authors observe implicit neural differences between high- and low-resolution features and posit that explicitly modeling these differences can improve feature representation and efficiency for UHD tasks.

Method: 1) Define Neural Discrimination Priors (NDP) to quantify differences between high- and low-resolution features. 2) Embed NDP into attention via Neural Discrimination-Prompted Attention (NDPA) to globally emphasize discriminative information. 3) Build a Neural Discrimination-Prompted Network (NDPN) with an NDP-guided continuous gating mechanism that selectively passes beneficial content. 4) Add a super-resolution-guided reconstruction that super-resolves low-resolution features to guide final UHD restoration. The overall backbone is a Transformer tailored for UHD image restoration/enhancement.

Result: Across three UHD tasks—low-light image enhancement, image dehazing, and image deblurring—UHDPromer achieves state-of-the-art performance while offering the best computational efficiency among compared methods. Specific metrics are not provided in the abstract; code and pre-trained models will be released.

Conclusion: Explicitly leveraging neural discrimination between high- and low-resolution features to prompt attention and gating, plus SR-guided reconstruction, yields an efficient yet SOTA UHD restoration framework that generalizes across multiple tasks.

Abstract: We propose a simple yet effective UHDPromer, a neural discrimination-prompted Transformer, for Ultra-High-Definition (UHD) image restoration and enhancement. Our UHDPromer is inspired by an interesting observation that there implicitly exist neural differences between high-resolution and low-resolution features, and exploring such differences can facilitate low-resolution feature representation. To this end, we first introduce Neural Discrimination Priors (NDP) to measure the differences and then integrate NDP into the proposed Neural Discrimination-Prompted Attention (NDPA) and Neural Discrimination-Prompted Network (NDPN). The proposed NDPA re-formulates the attention by incorporating NDP to globally perceive useful discrimination information, while the NDPN explores a continuous gating mechanism guided by NDP to selectively permit the passage of beneficial content. To enhance the quality of restored images, we propose a super-resolution-guided reconstruction approach, which is guided by super-resolving low-resolution features to facilitate final UHD image restoration. Experiments show that UHDPromer achieves the best computational efficiency while still maintaining state-of-the-art performance on $3$ UHD image restoration and enhancement tasks, including low-light image enhancement, image dehazing, and image deblurring. The source codes and pre-trained models will be made available at https://github.com/supersupercong/uhdpromer.

</details>


### [133] [PPC-MT: Parallel Point Cloud Completion with Mamba-Transformer Hybrid Architecture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00870)
*Jie Li,Shengwei Tian,Long Yu,Xin Ning*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PPC-MT is a PCA-guided, parallel point cloud completion framework that combines a Mamba encoder (linear complexity) with a Transformer decoder to achieve efficient, high-fidelity reconstructions, outperforming prior work on PCN, ShapeNet-55/34, and KITTI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current completion methods struggle to simultaneously achieve high detail fidelity, uniform point distribution, and computational efficiency, partly due to the unordered nature of point sets and the cost of global relational modeling.

Method: Impose geometric structure via PCA to order the point cloud and decompose it into multiple subsets. Reconstruct these subsets in parallel with a multi-head reconstructor. Use Mamba for efficient feature extraction in the encoder and a Transformer in the decoder to capture fine-grained multi-sequence relationships, balancing speed and accuracy.

Result: Across standard benchmarks (PCN, ShapeNet-55/34, KITTI), PPC-MT achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple metrics, with improved uniformity and detail while maintaining computational efficiency.

Conclusion: A PCA-guided parallel synthesis strategy coupled with a hybrid Mamba-Transformer architecture effectively balances efficiency and reconstruction quality for point cloud completion, validated by superior results on diverse benchmarks.

Abstract: Existing point cloud completion methods struggle to balance high-quality reconstruction with computational efficiency. To address this, we propose PPC-MT, a novel parallel framework for point cloud completion leveraging a hybrid Mamba-Transformer architecture. Our approach introduces an innovative parallel completion strategy guided by Principal Component Analysis (PCA), which imposes a geometrically meaningful structure on unordered point clouds, transforming them into ordered sets and decomposing them into multiple subsets. These subsets are reconstructed in parallel using a multi-head reconstructor. This structured parallel synthesis paradigm significantly enhances the uniformity of point distribution and detail fidelity, while preserving computational efficiency. By integrating Mamba's linear complexity for efficient feature extraction during encoding with the Transformer's capability to model fine-grained multi-sequence relationships during decoding, PPC-MT effectively balances efficiency and reconstruction accuracy. Extensive quantitative and qualitative experiments on benchmark datasets, including PCN, ShapeNet-55/34, and KITTI, demonstrate that PPC-MT outperforms state-of-the-art methods across multiple metrics, validating the efficacy of our proposed framework.

</details>


### [134] [MMTA: Multi Membership Temporal Attention for Fine-Grained Stroke Rehabilitation Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00878)
*Halil Ismail Helvaci,Justin Huber,Jihye Bae,Sen-ching Samson Cheung*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Multi-Membership Temporal Attention (MMTA), a transformer that lets each frame attend to multiple locally normalized temporal windows simultaneously, improving boundary sensitivity for fine-grained action segmentation in rehabilitation videos/IMU, with consistent Edit Score gains on StrokeRehab and 50Salads.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fine-grained rehabilitation assessment needs sub-second, temporally precise segmentation of actions. Existing TAS models blur rapid phase transitions and miss micro-movements, undermining downstream motor recovery assessments, especially under resource constraints and across modalities (video and IMU).

Method: A high-resolution temporal transformer where each frame obtains multiple concurrent attention contexts (multi-membership) over locally normalized temporal windows in the same layer. These are fused via feature-space overlap resolution to preserve competing local contexts at transitions while allowing longer-range reasoning through layer-wise propagation. Single-stage architecture supports both video and IMU; increases boundary sensitivity without extra depth or multi-stage refinement.

Result: Consistent improvement over a Global Attention transformer: +1.3 (Video) and +1.6 (IMU) Edit Score on StrokeRehab; +3.3 Edit Score on 50Salads. Ablations indicate gains derive from multi-membership temporal views rather than added architectural complexity.

Conclusion: MMTA provides a practical, modality-agnostic, single-stage solution for precise micro-movement boundary detection in rehabilitation TAS, improving edit scores while remaining suitable for resource-constrained clinical and home settings.

Abstract: To empower the iterative assessments involved during a person's rehabilitation, automated assessment of a person's abilities during daily activities requires temporally precise segmentation of fine-grained actions in therapy videos. Existing temporal action segmentation (TAS) models struggle to capture sub-second micro-movements while retaining exercise context, blurring rapid phase transitions and limiting reliable downstream assessment of motor recovery. We introduce Multi-Membership Temporal Attention (MMTA), a high-resolution temporal transformer for fine-grained rehabilitation assessment. Unlike standard temporal attention, which assigns each frame a single attention context per layer, MMTA lets each frame attend to multiple locally normalized temporal attention windows within the same layer. We fuse these concurrent temporal views via feature-space overlap resolution, preserving competing local contexts near transitions while enabling longer-range reasoning through layer-wise propagation. This increases boundary sensitivity without additional depth or multi-stage refinement. MMTA supports both video and wearable IMU inputs within a unified single-stage architecture, making it applicable to both clinical and home settings. MMTA consistently improves over the Global Attention transformer, boosting Edit Score by +1.3 (Video) and +1.6 (IMU) on StrokeRehab while further improving 50Salads by +3.3. Ablations confirm that performance gains stem from multi-membership temporal views rather than architectural complexity, offering a practical solution for resource-constrained rehabilitation assessment.

</details>


### [135] [Uncertainty-Aware Concept and Motion Segmentation for Semi-Supervised Angiography Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00881)
*Yu Luo,Guangyu Wei,Yangfan Li,Jieyu He,Yueming Lyu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SMART is a semi-supervised, SAM3-based teacher–student framework that adds motion-aware consistency and progressive confidence regularization to segment coronary vessels in X-ray angiography videos, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy with far fewer annotations across three multi-institutional datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate coronary vessel segmentation in XCA is hard due to blurred boundaries, inconsistent contrast, complex motion, and limited labeled data. Existing semi-supervised methods underperform because they mishandle temporal dynamics and provide unreliable uncertainty/consistency cues.

Method: A SAM3-driven promptable teacher–student architecture: (1) leverage SAM3 for concept-prompted segmentation in both teacher and student; (2) incorporate vessel mask warping and a motion consistency loss to capture temporal vessel dynamics; (3) introduce progressive confidence-aware consistency regularization to downweight unreliable teacher pseudo-labels under blur/low contrast and stabilize training.

Result: On three XCA video datasets from different institutions, SMART delivers state-of-the-art segmentation performance while requiring substantially fewer labeled images than fully supervised baselines and prior SSL approaches.

Conclusion: Combining SAM3 prompting, motion-aware temporal consistency, and progressive confidence regularization yields label-efficient, robust coronary vessel segmentation suitable for real-world clinical settings with scarce annotations; code is publicly available.

Abstract: Segmentation of the main coronary artery from X-ray coronary angiography (XCA) sequences is crucial for the diagnosis of coronary artery diseases. However, this task is challenging due to issues such as blurred boundaries, inconsistent radiation contrast, complex motion patterns, and a lack of annotated images for training. Although Semi-Supervised Learning (SSL) can alleviate the annotation burden, conventional methods struggle with complicated temporal dynamics and unreliable uncertainty quantification. To address these challenges, we propose SAM3-based Teacher-student framework with Motion-Aware consistency and Progressive Confidence Regularization (SMART), a semi-supervised vessel segmentation approach for X-ray angiography videos. First, our method utilizes SAM3's unique promptable concept segmentation design and innovates a SAM3-based teacher-student framework to maximize the performance potential of both the teacher and the student. Second, we enhance segmentation by integrating the vessel mask warping technique and motion consistency loss to model complex vessel dynamics. To address the issue of unreliable teacher predictions caused by blurred boundaries and minimal contrast, we further propose a progressive confidence-aware consistency regularization to mitigate the risk of unreliable outputs. Extensive experiments on three datasets of XCA sequences from different institutions demonstrate that SMART achieves state-of-the-art performance while requiring significantly fewer annotations, making it particularly valuable for real-world clinical applications where labeled data is scarce. Our code is available at: https://github.com/qimingfan10/SMART.

</details>


### [136] [VEMamba: Efficient Isotropic Reconstruction of Volume Electron Microscopy with Axial-Lateral Consistent Mamba](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00887)
*Longmi Gao,Pan Gao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VEMamba is an efficient Mamba-based framework for reconstructing isotropic 3D electron microscopy volumes from anisotropic inputs by reordering 3D dependencies into optimized 1D sequences (ALCSSM), adaptively aggregating them (DWAM), and leveraging degradation-aware MoCo pretraining; it reports competitive quality with lower compute on simulated and real VEM data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Volume EM often yields anisotropic stacks with poor axial resolution, limiting visualization and analysis. Prior isotropic reconstruction methods underuse axial information and rely on simplistic downsampling to mimic anisotropy, leading to domain gaps and suboptimal reconstructions.

Method: Introduce a 3D Dependency Reordering paradigm. (1) Axial-Lateral Chunking Selective Scan Module (ALCSSM) remaps complex 3D spatial dependencies—both axial and lateral—into optimized 1D sequences for efficient Mamba sequence modeling, enforcing axial–lateral consistency. (2) Dynamic Weights Aggregation Module (DWAM) adaptively fuses multiple reordered sequence outputs to strengthen representations. (3) A realistic degradation simulator is used to create training pairs and Momentum Contrast (MoCo) integrates degradation-aware priors via contrastive learning.

Result: Across simulated and real anisotropic VEM datasets, VEMamba achieves highly competitive reconstruction metrics while requiring fewer computational resources than alternatives (exact numbers not provided in the abstract).

Conclusion: Modeling axial–lateral dependencies via dependency reordering combined with degradation-aware contrastive pretraining yields efficient, accurate isotropic reconstruction for VEM; open-source code facilitates reproducibility and adoption.

Abstract: Volume Electron Microscopy (VEM) is crucial for 3D tissue imaging but often produces anisotropic data with poor axial resolution, hindering visualization and downstream analysis. Existing methods for isotropic reconstruction often suffer from neglecting abundant axial information and employing simple downsampling to simulate anisotropic data. To address these limitations, we propose VEMamba, an efficient framework for isotropic reconstruction. The core of VEMamba is a novel 3D Dependency Reordering paradigm, implemented via two key components: an Axial-Lateral Chunking Selective Scan Module (ALCSSM), which intelligently re-maps complex 3D spatial dependencies (both axial and lateral) into optimized 1D sequences for efficient Mamba-based modeling, explicitly enforcing axial-lateral consistency; and a Dynamic Weights Aggregation Module (DWAM) to adaptively aggregate these reordered sequence outputs for enhanced representational power. Furthermore, we introduce a realistic degradation simulation and then leverage Momentum Contrast (MoCo) to integrate this degradation-aware knowledge into the network for superior reconstruction. Extensive experiments on both simulated and real-world anisotropic VEM datasets demonstrate that VEMamba achieves highly competitive performance across various metrics while maintaining a lower computational footprint. The source code is available on GitHub: https://github.com/I2-Multimedia-Lab/VEMamba

</details>


### [137] [pySpatial: Generating 3D Visual Programs for Zero-Shot Spatial Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00905)
*Zhanpeng Luo,Ce Zhang,Silong Yong,Cunxi Dai,Qianwei Wang,Haoxi Ran,Guanya Shi,Katia Sycara,Yaqi Xie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: pySpatial lets MLLMs perform explicit 3D spatial reasoning by generating Python calls to spatial tools (reconstruction, pose, rendering) from image sequences and queries, achieving strong zero-shot gains and enabling real-world robot navigation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: General-purpose MLLMs are weak at tasks needing 3D spatial understanding; there’s a need to turn 2D inputs into structured 3D representations that models can reason over without costly fine-tuning.

Method: A visual programming framework where an MLLM generates Python code that composes calls to spatial tools (3D reconstruction, camera-pose recovery, novel-view rendering). This builds an explorable 3D scene from image sequences, enabling explicit reasoning over structured spatial data in a zero-shot, no-gradient setting.

Result: On MindCube and Omni3D-Bench, pySpatial surpasses strong MLLM baselines, e.g., +12.94% over GPT-4.1-mini on MindCube. In real-world indoor navigation, robots successfully traverse complex environments using pySpatial-generated route plans.

Conclusion: Interfacing MLLMs with spatial toolchains via code effectively overcomes 3D reasoning gaps without fine-tuning, improving benchmark performance and demonstrating practical utility for embodied tasks like navigation.

Abstract: Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in general-purpose perception and reasoning, but they still struggle with tasks that require spatial understanding of the 3D world. To address this, we introduce pySpatial, a visual programming framework that equips MLLMs with the ability to interface with spatial tools via Python code generation. Given an image sequence and a natural-language query, the model composes function calls to spatial tools including 3D reconstruction, camera-pose recovery, novel-view rendering, etc. These operations convert raw 2D inputs into an explorable 3D scene, enabling MLLMs to reason explicitly over structured spatial representations. Notably, pySpatial requires no gradient-based fine-tuning and operates in a fully zero-shot setting. Experimental evaluations on the challenging MindCube and Omni3D-Bench benchmarks demonstrate that our framework pySpatial consistently surpasses strong MLLM baselines; for instance, it outperforms GPT-4.1-mini by 12.94% on MindCube. Furthermore, we conduct real-world indoor navigation experiments where the robot can successfully traverse complex environments using route plans generated by pySpatial, highlighting the practical effectiveness of our approach.

</details>


### [138] [ShiftLUT: Spatial Shift Enhanced Look-Up Tables for Efficient Image Restoration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00906)
*Xiaolong Zeng,Yitong Yu,Shiyao Xiong,Jinhua Hao,Ming Sun,Chao Zhou,Bin Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ShiftLUT is an efficient LUT-based image restoration framework that achieves the largest receptive field to date for LUT methods via learnable spatial shifts, an asymmetric dual-branch design, and error-bounded adaptive LUT compression, yielding better PSNR than TinyLUT with similar latency and storage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LUT-based image restoration improves quality mainly by enlarging receptive fields, but doing so typically inflates compute and storage, limiting deployment on edge devices. The goal is to maximize receptive field and accuracy without sacrificing the lightweight benefits of LUTs.

Method: Three components: (1) Learnable Spatial Shift (LSS) applies channel-wise, learnable spatial offsets to features, effectively expanding the receptive field without heavy convolutional cost. (2) An asymmetric dual-branch network allocates more computation to the information-dense branch while keeping a lightweight companion branch, reducing latency with minimal quality loss. (3) Error-bounded Adaptive Sampling (EAS) compresses feature-level LUTs by sampling adaptively under an error constraint, cutting storage while preserving accuracy.

Result: Relative to TinyLUT, ShiftLUT achieves a 3.8× larger receptive field and >0.21 dB average PSNR gain on multiple standard benchmarks, while maintaining small model size and fast inference comparable to prior SOTA.

Conclusion: ShiftLUT advances LUT-based restoration by jointly maximizing receptive field and efficiency, enabling better accuracy–efficiency trade-offs suitable for edge deployment; methods like LSS and EAS can generalize to other efficient IR architectures.

Abstract: Look-Up Table based methods have emerged as a promising direction for efficient image restoration tasks. Recent LUT-based methods focus on improving their performance by expanding the receptive field. However, they inevitably introduce extra computational and storage overhead, which hinders their deployment in edge devices. To address this issue, we propose ShiftLUT, a novel framework that attains the largest receptive field among all LUT-based methods while maintaining high efficiency. Our key insight lies in three complementary components. First, Learnable Spatial Shift module (LSS) is introduced to expand the receptive field by applying learnable, channel-wise spatial offsets on feature maps. Second, we propose an asymmetric dual-branch architecture that allocates more computation to the information-dense branch, substantially reducing inference latency without compromising restoration quality. Finally, we incorporate a feature-level LUT compression strategy called Error-bounded Adaptive Sampling (EAS) to minimize the storage overhead. Compared to the previous state-of-the-art method TinyLUT, ShiftLUT achieves a 3.8$\times$ larger receptive field and improves an average PSNR by over 0.21 dB across multiple standard benchmarks, while maintaining a small storage size and inference time.

</details>


### [139] [UD-SfPNet: An Underwater Descattering Shape-from-Polarization Network for 3D Normal Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00908)
*Puyun Wang,Kaimin Yu,Huayang He,Feng Huang,Xianyu Wu,Yating Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UD-SfPNet is a unified deep network that jointly performs underwater descattering and shape-from-polarization (SfP) to predict accurate 3D surface normals, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy (15.12° mean angular error) on MuS-Polar3D.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Underwater imaging suffers from severe scattering, which degrades both appearance and geometry recovery. Polarization offers complementary cues for descattering and SfP, but sequential pipelines accumulate errors and miss joint optimization across tasks.

Method: A single pipeline that co-optimizes polarization-based descattering and SfP normal estimation. It introduces (1) a color embedding module that exploits correlations between color encodings and surface orientation to enforce geometric consistency, and (2) a detail enhancement convolution module to recover high-frequency geometric details typically lost to scattering.

Result: On the MuS-Polar3D dataset, the approach significantly improves reconstruction accuracy, reaching a mean surface normal angular error of 15.12°, the best among compared methods.

Conclusion: Jointly coupling descattering with polarization-based shape inference yields more accurate underwater 3D normals and better preserves geometric detail, demonstrating practical value for optical 3D imaging in challenging underwater scenes. Code is publicly available.

Abstract: Underwater optical imaging is severely hindered by scattering, but polarization imaging offers the unique dual advantages of descattering and shape-from-polarization (SfP) 3D reconstruction. To exploit these advantages, this paper proposes UD-SfPNet, an underwater descattering shape-from-polarization network that leverages polarization cues for improved 3D surface normal prediction. The framework jointly models polarization-based image descattering and SfP normal estimation in a unified pipeline, avoiding error accumulation from sequential processing and enabling global optimization across both tasks. UD-SfPNet further incorporates a novel color embedding module to enhance geometric consistency by exploiting the relationship between color encodings and surface orientation. A detail enhancement convolution module is also included to better preserve high-frequency geometric details that are lost under scattering. Experiments on the MuS-Polar3D dataset show that the proposed method significantly improves reconstruction accuracy, achieving a mean surface normal angular error of 15.12$^\circ$ (the lowest among compared methods). These results confirm the efficacy of combining descattering with polarization-based shape inference, and highlight the practical significance and potential applications of UD-SfPNet for optical 3D imaging in challenging underwater environments. The code is available at https://github.com/WangPuyun/UD-SfPNet.

</details>


### [140] [On the Exact Algorithmic Extraction of Finite Tesselations Through Prime Extraction of Minimal Representative Forms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00911)
*Sushish Baral,Paulo Garcia,Warisa Sritriratanarak*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A deterministic, hierarchical algorithm exactly detects axis‑aligned rectangular tessellations in finite grids by combining region discovery, normalization, and prime extraction; it is fast on simple repeats (<1 ms) but shows exponential runtime on complex cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Approximate, statistical methods can recognize noisy or probabilistic patterns, but there is a gap in symbolic, deterministic extraction of exact periodic structures in discrete grids needed for reasoning, algorithm synthesis, and structural optimization.

Method: Use a hierarchical pipeline: (1) composite discovery via dual inspection and breadth‑first pruning to find rectangular regions with internal repetition; (2) normalization to a minimal canonical representative tile; (3) prime extraction using selective duplication and hierarchical memoization to handle irregular dimensions and reduce computation. Supports coexisting patterns within a hierarchy; restricted to finite, axis‑aligned, planar grids.

Result: On 2×2–32×32 grids, simple overlapping repeating tiles are detected in under 1 ms; complex patterns that necessitate exhaustive search show exponential growth in processing time.

Conclusion: The approach fills a gap in symbolic grid analysis by providing deterministic identification of exact rectangular tessellations, useful for puzzle solving and discrete symbolic structure discovery; performance is strong for simple patterns but fundamentally limited on complex, combinatorial cases.

Abstract: The identification of repeating patterns in discrete grids is rudimentary within symbolic reasoning, algorithm synthesis and structural optimization across diverse computational domains. Although statistical approaches targeting noisy data can approximately recognize patterns, symbolic analysis utilizing deterministic extraction of periodic structures is underdeveloped. This paper aims to fill this gap by employing a hierarchical algorithm that discovers exact tessellations in finite planar grids, addressing the problem where multiple independent patterns may coexist within a hierarchical structure. The proposed method utilizes composite discovery (dual inspection and breadth-first pruning) for identifying rectangular regions with internal repetition, normalization to a minimal representative form, and prime extraction (selective duplication and hierarchical memoization) to account for irregular dimensions and to achieve efficient computation time. We evaluate scalability on grid sizes from 2x2 to 32x32, showing overlap detection on simple repeating tiles exhibits processing time under 1ms, while complex patterns which require exhaustive search and systematic exploration shows exponential growth. This algorithm provides deterministic behavior for exact, axis-aligned, rectangular tessellations, addressing a critical gap in symbolic grid analysis techniques, applicable to puzzle solving reasoning tasks and identification of exact repeating structures in discrete symbolic domains.

</details>


### [141] [VGGT-Det: Mining VGGT Internal Priors for Sensor-Geometry-Free Multi-View Indoor 3D Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00912)
*Yang Cao,Feize Wu,Dave Zhenyu Chen,Yingji Zhong,Lanqing Hong,Dan Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VGGT-Det is a calibration- and depth-free multi-view indoor 3D detector that plugs a VGGT encoder into a transformer and introduces attention-guided query initialization and query-driven multi-level feature aggregation, achieving SOTA mAP@0.25 on ScanNet and ARKitScenes without sensor geometry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-view indoor 3D detectors rely on precise camera poses or depth, which are costly, brittle, and limit real-world deployment. The goal is to perform 3D object detection from multi-view images alone by exploiting strong 3D cues learned directly from images.

Method: Integrate the VGGT encoder into a transformer-based detection pipeline. Two key components: (i) Attention-Guided Query Generation uses VGGT attention maps as semantic priors to initialize object queries, focusing queries on likely object regions while preserving global spatial structure. (ii) Query-Driven Feature Aggregation introduces a learnable “See-Query” that interacts with object queries to request and dynamically aggregate multi-level geometric features across VGGT layers that progressively lift 2D features into 3D, enabling pose/depth-free multi-view fusion.

Result: VGGT-Det surpasses the strongest SG-Free baseline by +4.4 and +8.6 mAP@0.25 on ScanNet and ARKitScenes, respectively. Ablations show that both AG and QD effectively leverage VGGT’s internal semantic and geometric priors.

Conclusion: Replacing explicit sensor geometry with learned priors from VGGT is viable for multi-view indoor 3D detection. The proposed AG and QD components successfully harness these priors, delivering state-of-the-art SG-Free performance and pointing toward calibration-free, deployable 3D perception systems.

Abstract: Current multi-view indoor 3D object detectors rely on sensor geometry that is costly to obtain (i.e., precisely calibrated multi-view camera poses) to fuse multi-view information into a global scene representation, limiting deployment in real-world scenes. We target a more practical setting: Sensor-Geometry-Free (SG-Free) multi-view indoor 3D object detection, where there are no sensor-provided geometric inputs (multi-view poses or depth). Recent Visual Geometry Grounded Transformer (VGGT) shows that strong 3D cues can be inferred directly from images. Building on this insight, we present VGGT-Det, the first framework tailored for SG-Free multi-view indoor 3D object detection. Rather than merely consuming VGGT predictions, our method integrates VGGT encoder into a transformer-based pipeline. To effectively leverage both the semantic and geometric priors from inside VGGT, we introduce two novel key components: (i) Attention-Guided Query Generation (AG): exploits VGGT attention maps as semantic priors to initialize object queries, improving localization by focusing on object regions while preserving global spatial structure; (ii) Query-Driven Feature Aggregation (QD): a learnable See-Query interacts with object queries to 'see' what they need, and then dynamically aggregates multi-level geometric features across VGGT layers that progressively lift 2D features into 3D. Experiments show that VGGT-Det significantly surpasses the best-performing method in the SG-Free setting by 4.4 and 8.6 mAP@0.25 on ScanNet and ARKitScenes, respectively. Ablation study shows that VGGT's internally learned semantic and geometric priors can be effectively leveraged by our AG and QD.

</details>


### [142] [Improving Text-to-Image Generation with Intrinsic Self-Confidence Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00918)
*Seungwook Kim,Minsu Cho*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ARC is an unsupervised post-training method for text-to-image models that turns a model’s own self-denoising confidence into a scalar reward, reinforcing high-confidence generations to improve composition, text rendering, and alignment, and it complements external rewards while reducing reward hacking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: External reward supervision for post-training (human labels, reward models) is costly, limits scalability, and can be gamed (“reward hacking”). The authors seek a scalable, label-free signal that better aligns generation quality with human-relevant objectives like compositionality, factuality, and aesthetics.

Method: Inject noise and probe the model’s ability to self-denoise; measure how accurately it recovers the injected noise to derive an intrinsic “self-confidence” score per generation. Convert this score into scalar rewards and reinforce the model toward high-confidence outputs. The framework is fully unsupervised and can be combined with external reward signals.

Result: ARC yields consistent gains over baseline in compositional generation, text rendering, and text–image alignment. When combined with external rewards, ARC provides complementary improvements and reduces reward hacking behaviors.

Conclusion: Self-confidence–based rewarding is a practical, annotation-free path for post-training text-to-image models, improving key qualities and synergizing with external feedback while mitigating reward hacking. ARC suggests a scalable approach to preference alignment without additional datasets or reward models.

Abstract: Text-to-image generation powers content creation across design, media, and data augmentation. Post-training of text-to-image generative models is a promising path to better match human preferences, factuality, and improved aesthetics. We introduce ARC (Adaptive Rewarding by self-Confidence), a post-training framework that replaces external reward supervision with an internal self-confidence signal, obtained by evaluating how accurately the model recovers injected noise under self-denoising probes. ARC converts this intrinsic signal into scalar rewards, enabling fully unsupervised optimization without additional datasets, annotators, or reward models. Empirically, by reinforcing high-confidence generations, ARC delivers consistent gains in compositional generation, text rendering and text-image alignment over the baseline. We also find that integrating ARC with external rewards results in a complementary improvement, with alleviated reward hacking.

</details>


### [143] [DriveCode: Domain Specific Numerical Encoding for LLM-Based Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00919)
*Zhiye Wang,Yanbo Jiang,Rui Zhou,Bo Zhang,Fang Zhang,Zhenhua Xu,Yaqin Zhang,Jianqiang Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DriveCode replaces text tokenization of numbers with learned numeric embeddings projected into an LLM’s hidden space, yielding better precision and efficiency for autonomous driving tasks and improving trajectory and control prediction on multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs struggle with precise numbers because digit-tokenization obscures positional significance, harms numerical reasoning, and forces a trade-off between decoding efficiency and precision—critical issues for processing sensor data and issuing exact control in autonomous driving.

Method: Introduce DriveCode, which represents numbers as dedicated embeddings rather than discrete tokens. A number projector maps raw numbers directly into the LLM hidden space, allowing seamless fusion with visual and textual features in a unified multimodal sequence.

Result: On OmniDrive, DriveGPT4, and DriveGPT4-V2 datasets, DriveCode outperforms baselines in trajectory prediction and control signal generation, indicating stronger numerical handling within LLM-based driving pipelines.

Conclusion: Embedding numbers via a projector overcomes tokenization-induced numeracy limits, enabling more accurate and efficient multimodal reasoning and control in LLM-based autonomous driving systems.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have shown great promise for autonomous driving. However, discretizing numbers into tokens limits precise numerical reasoning, fails to reflect the positional significance of digits in the training objective, and makes it difficult to achieve both decoding efficiency and numerical precision. These limitations affect both the processing of sensor measurements and the generation of precise control commands, creating a fundamental barrier for deploying LLM-based autonomous driving systems. In this paper, we introduce DriveCode, a novel numerical encoding method that represents numbers as dedicated embeddings rather than discrete text tokens. DriveCode employs a number projector to map numbers into the language model's hidden space, enabling seamless integration with visual and textual features in a unified multimodal sequence. Evaluated on OmniDrive, DriveGPT4, and DriveGPT4-V2 datasets, DriveCode demonstrates superior performance in trajectory prediction and control signal generation, confirming its effectiveness for LLM-based autonomous driving systems.

</details>


### [144] [Learning to Weigh Waste: A Physics-Informed Multimodal Fusion Framework and Large-Scale Dataset for Commercial and Industrial Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00931)
*Md. Adnanul Islam,Wasimul Karim,Md Mahbub Alam,Subhey Sadi Rahman,Md. Abdur Rahman,Arefin Ittesafun Abian,Mohaimenul Azam Khan Raiaan,Kheng Cher Yeo,Deepika Mathur,Sami Azam*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They propose a multimodal model that fuses RGB images with physics-informed metadata (dimensions, camera pose, category) to estimate waste weight, release a 10K real-world dataset, and show strong accuracy across light to heavy objects with explainable outputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Image-only weight estimation is unreliable because visually similar waste can have different densities and apparent size varies with camera distance/perspective. Accurate weight prediction is operationally important for logistics and recycling.

Method: Introduce the Multimodal Weight Predictor (MWP): Vision Transformer for image features, a metadata encoder for geometric and category cues, and Stacked Mutual Attention Fusion so visual and physical signals inform each other. Train with Mean Squared Logarithmic Error to stabilize learning across a wide dynamic range. Release Waste-Weight-10K (10,421 image–metadata pairs; 11 categories; 3.5–3,450 kg). Provide SHAP + LLM explanations for predictions.

Result: On the test set: 88.06 kg MAE, 6.39% MAPE, R^2 = 0.9548. For 0–100 kg objects: 2.38 kg MAE, 3.1% MAPE. For 1,000–2,000 kg objects: 11.1% MAPE, indicating robust performance across scales.

Conclusion: Fusing vision with physics-informed metadata and mutual-attention fusion addresses perspective and material-density ambiguities, yielding accurate, explainable weight estimates over a broad range and offering a valuable dataset for future research.

Abstract: Accurate weight estimation of commercial and industrial waste is important for efficient operations, yet image-based estimation remains difficult because similar-looking objects may have different densities, and the visible size changes with camera distance. Addressing this problem, we propose Multimodal Weight Predictor (MWP) framework that estimates waste weight by combining RGB images with physics-informed metadata, including object dimensions, camera distance, and camera height. We also introduce Waste-Weight-10K, a real-world dataset containing 10,421 synchronized image-metadata collected from logistics and recycling sites. The dataset covers 11 waste categories and a wide weight range from 3.5 to 3,450 kg. Our model uses a Vision Transformer for visual features and a dedicated metadata encoder for geometric and category information, combining them with Stacked Mutual Attention Fusion that allows visual and physical cues guide each other. This helps the model manage perspective effects and link objects to material properties. To ensure stable performance across the wide weight range, we train the model using Mean Squared Logarithmic Error. On the test set, the proposed method achieves 88.06 kg Mean Absolute Error (MAE), 6.39% Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE), and an R2 coefficient of 0.9548. The model shows strong accuracy for light objects in the 0-100 kg range with 2.38 kg MAE and 3.1% MAPE, maintaining reliable performance for heavy waste in the 1000-2000 kg range with 11.1% MAPE. Finally, we incorporate a physically grounded explanation module using Shapley Additive Explanations (SHAP) and a large language model to provide clear, human-readable explanations for each prediction.

</details>


### [145] [Seeing Beyond 8bits: Subjective and Objective Quality Assessment of HDR-UGC Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00938)
*Shreshth Saini,Bowen Chen,Neil Birkbeck,Yilin Wang,Balu Adsumilli,Alan C. Bovik*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Releases a large HDR UGC video-quality dataset (Beyond8Bits) and introduces HDR-Q, an MLLM with an HDR-aware encoder and an RL finetuning scheme (HAPO), achieving state-of-the-art HDR-VQA results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HDR UGC is widespread, but most VQA methods are built for SDR and miss HDR-specific artifacts (e.g., near-black crushing, highlight clipping, banding, exposure flicker). There is a need for HDR-aware assessment tools and data.

Method: (1) Curate Beyond8Bits: 44K videos from 6.5K sources with 1.5M crowd ratings across diverse scenes, capture conditions, and compression levels. (2) Propose HDR-Q, the first MLLM for HDR-UGC VQA, featuring a novel HDR-aware vision encoder to yield HDR-sensitive embeddings. (3) Introduce HDR-Aware Policy Optimization (HAPO), an RL finetuning framework augmenting GRPO with an HDR–SDR contrastive KL to encourage reliance on HDR cues and a Gaussian-weighted regression reward for fine-grained MOS calibration.

Result: HDR-Q attains state-of-the-art performance on the new Beyond8Bits dataset and multiple public HDR-VQA benchmarks.

Conclusion: Large-scale HDR-UGC data plus HDR-aware encoding and policy optimization substantially improve perceptual quality assessment for HDR videos, offering resources and a method likely to catalyze future progress in HDR VQA.

Abstract: High Dynamic Range (HDR) user-generated (UGC) videos are rapidly proliferating across social platforms, yet most perceptual video quality assessment (VQA) systems remain tailored to Standard Dynamic Range (SDR). HDR has a higher bit depth, wide color gamut, and elevated luminance range, exposing distortions such as near-black crushing, highlight clipping, banding, and exposure flicker that amplify UGC artifacts and challenge SDR models. To catalyze progress, we curate Beyond8Bits, a large-scale subjective dataset of 44K videos from 6.5K sources with over 1.5M crowd ratings, spanning diverse scenes, capture conditions, and compression settings. We further introduce HDR-Q, the first Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) for HDR-UGC VQA. We propose (i) a novel HDR-aware vision encoder to produce HDR-sensitive embeddings, and (ii) HDR-Aware Policy Optimization (HAPO), an RL finetuning framework that anchors reasoning to HDR cues. HAPO augments GRPO via an HDR-SDR contrastive KL that encourages token reliance on HDR inputs and a Gaussian weighted regression reward for fine-grained MOS calibration. Across Beyond8Bits and public HDR-VQA benchmarks, HDR-Q delivers state-of-the-art performance.

</details>


### [146] [Mobile-VTON: High-Fidelity On-Device Virtual Try-On](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00947)
*Zhenchen Wan,Ce Chen,Runqi Lin,Jiaxin Huang,Tianxi Chen,Yanwu Xu,Tongliang Liu,Mingming Gong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Mobile-VTON delivers high-fidelity, privacy-preserving virtual try-on entirely on commodity mobile devices via a compact, distillation-driven TGT (TeacherNet–GarmentNet–TryonNet) pipeline, matching or surpassing server-based baselines at 1024×768 with low compute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most high-quality VTON systems need cloud GPUs and user photo uploads, creating privacy risks and blocking true on-device use. The goal is to make VTON both high-quality and fully offline on mobile hardware.

Method: A modular TGT architecture: (1) TeacherNet provides supervision; (2) GarmentNet performs garment-conditioned generation and is trained with a trajectory-consistency loss to preserve garment semantics across diffusion steps; (3) TryonNet aligns garment to person using latent concatenation and lightweight cross‑modal conditioning, avoiding large-scale pretraining. A Feature-Guided Adversarial (FGA) Distillation combines teacher supervision with adversarial learning to better match real-world image distributions and compress the model for mobile efficiency.

Result: On VITON-HD and DressCode at 1024×768, Mobile-VTON matches or outperforms strong server-based baselines while running fully offline on mobile, achieving high-fidelity outputs with low computational overhead.

Conclusion: High-quality, privacy-preserving VTON is feasible and practical on-device. The proposed distillation and alignment strategies enable a secure, efficient mobile deployment without sacrificing visual quality.

Abstract: Virtual try-on (VTON) has recently achieved impressive visual fidelity, but most existing systems require uploading personal photos to cloud-based GPUs, raising privacy concerns and limiting on-device deployment. To address this, we present Mobile-VTON, a high-quality, privacy-preserving framework that enables fully offline virtual try-on on commodity mobile devices using only a single user image and a garment image. Mobile-VTON introduces a modular TeacherNet-GarmentNet-TryonNet (TGT) architecture that integrates knowledge distillation, garment-conditioned generation, and garment alignment into a unified pipeline optimized for on-device efficiency. Within this framework, we propose a Feature-Guided Adversarial (FGA) Distillation strategy that combines teacher supervision with adversarial learning to better match real-world image distributions. GarmentNet is trained with a trajectory-consistency loss to preserve garment semantics across diffusion steps, while TryonNet uses latent concatenation and lightweight cross-modal conditioning to enable robust garment-to-person alignment without large-scale pretraining. By combining these components, Mobile-VTON achieves high-fidelity generation with low computational overhead. Experiments on VITON-HD and DressCode at 1024 x 768 show that it matches or outperforms strong server-based baselines while running entirely offline. These results demonstrate that high-quality VTON is not only feasible but also practical on-device, offering a secure solution for real-world applications.

</details>


### [147] [StegoNGP: 3D Cryptographic Steganography using Instant-NGP](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00949)
*Wenxiang Jiang,Yujun Lan,Shuo Zhao,Yuanshan Liu,Mingzhu Zhou,Jinxin Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: StegoNGP hides a complete 3D scene inside an Instant-NGP model by using key-controlled hash encodings to switch between a visible cover scene and a secret scene—without changing the architecture or adding decoders. A multi-key scheme across hash levels expands the key space and boosts robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fast 3D reconstruction via Instant-NGP is compelling, but securely and imperceptibly embedding large-capacity data (entire scenes) into such models is difficult. Prior methods need external decoders or architectural changes and have limited capacity, making them easier to detect.

Method: Use the Instant-NGP hash encoding as a key-controlled scene switcher. Train a single network so that a default key yields the cover scene while a secret key reconstructs the hidden scene, both interwoven in shared weights. Extend to a Multi-Key scheme that assigns independent keys per hash level to enlarge the key space and resist partial key disclosure attacks.

Result: A trained model remains architecturally identical to standard Instant-NGP with the same parameter count, yet can reconstruct either high-quality cover or hidden scenes depending on the key. Experiments show high-capacity hiding with strong imperceptibility and improved security/robustness (especially under partial key leakage).

Conclusion: StegoNGP establishes a parameter-free, undetectable, high-capacity approach to 3D steganography in neural fields. The multi-key design further enhances security, suggesting a practical path for secure information hiding without modifying Instant-NGP architecture.

Abstract: Recently, Instant Neural Graphics Primitives (Instant-NGP) has achieved significant success in rapid 3D scene reconstruction, but securely embedding high-capacity hidden data, such as an entire 3D scene, remains a challenge. Existing methods rely on external decoders, require architectural modifications, and suffer from limited capacity, which makes them easily detectable. We propose a novel parameter-free 3D Cryptographic Steganography using Instant-NGP (StegoNGP), which leverages the Instant-NGP hash encoding function as a key-controlled scene switcher. By associating a default key with a cover scene and a secret key with a hidden scene, our method trains a single model to interweave both representations within the same network weights. The resulting model is indistinguishable from a standard Instant-NGP in architecture and parameter count. We also introduce an enhanced Multi-Key scheme, which assigns multiple independent keys across hash levels, dramatically expanding the key space and providing high robustness against partial key disclosure attacks. Experimental results demonstrated that StegoNGP can hide a complete high-quality 3D scene with strong imperceptibility and security, providing a new paradigm for high-capacity, undetectable information hiding in neural fields. The code can be found at https://github.com/jiang-wenxiang/StegoNGP.

</details>
