### [148] [Decoupling Motion and Geometry in 4D Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00952)
*Yi Zhang,Yulei Kang,Jian-Fang Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VeGaS is a velocity-based 4D Gaussian Splatting method that decouples motion from geometry using a Galilean shearing matrix and a deformation network, enabling faithful reconstruction of complex, non-linear dynamics and achieving state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 4D Gaussian Splatting ties motion and geometry within a single covariance, limiting expressiveness for complex motion and causing visual artifacts. A more flexible, explicit motion model that does not distort geometric attributes is needed for high-fidelity dynamic scene reconstruction.

Method: Introduce a velocity-based 4DGS framework that (1) explicitly models time-varying velocity via a Galilean shearing matrix to capture complex non-linear motions while keeping motion separate from geometry, and (2) adds a Geometric Deformation Network to refine Gaussian shapes and orientations using spatio-temporal context and velocity cues, improving temporal geometric consistency.

Result: Across public benchmarks, VeGaS outperforms prior methods, delivering state-of-the-art reconstruction quality for dynamic scenes (specific metrics not provided in the abstract).

Conclusion: Decoupling motion from geometry with an explicit velocity-driven shearing model plus a deformation network reduces artifacts and enhances temporal geometric modeling, yielding high-fidelity dynamic scene reconstructions and SOTA performance.

Abstract: High-fidelity reconstruction of dynamic scenes is an important yet challenging problem. While recent 4D Gaussian Splatting (4DGS) has demonstrated the ability to model temporal dynamics, it couples Gaussian motion and geometric attributes within a single covariance formulation, which limits its expressiveness for complex motions and often leads to visual artifacts. To address this, we propose VeGaS, a novel velocity-based 4D Gaussian Splatting framework that decouples Gaussian motion and geometry. Specifically, we introduce a Galilean shearing matrix that explicitly incorporates time-varying velocity to flexibly model complex non-linear motions, while strictly isolating the effects of Gaussian motion from the geometry-related conditional Gaussian covariance. Furthermore, a Geometric Deformation Network is introduced to refine Gaussian shapes and orientations using spatio-temporal context and velocity cues, enhancing temporal geometric modeling. Extensive experiments on public datasets demonstrate that VeGaS achieves state-of-the-art performance.

</details>


### [149] [PreciseCache: Precise Feature Caching for Efficient and High-fidelity Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00976)
*Jiangshan Wang,Kang Zhao,Jiayi Guo,Jiayu Wang,Hang Guo,Chenyang Zhu,Xiu Li,Xiangyu Yue*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PreciseCache is a plug-and-play caching framework for video generation that precisely detects redundant computations at both step and block levels, yielding up to ~2.6× faster inference without noticeable quality loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video generation models are slow and costly at inference. Prior acceleration via feature caching often harms quality because it cannot reliably distinguish redundant from important features, causing critical computations to be skipped.

Method: Introduce PreciseCache with two modules: (1) LFCache for step-wise caching, using the Low-Frequency Difference (LFD) between current and previously cached prediction features to detect truly redundant steps and reuse cached features; (2) BlockCache for block-wise caching within non-skipped steps to skip redundant computations at the network block level. LFD is empirically validated as an effective redundancy indicator.

Result: Across multiple backbones, PreciseCache accelerates inference substantially; e.g., it achieves an average 2.6× speedup on Wan2.1-14B with no noticeable degradation in generation quality.

Conclusion: Accurate redundancy detection at both temporal (step-wise) and architectural (block-wise) levels enables substantial inference acceleration for video generation without sacrificing quality. PreciseCache is general, plug-and-play, and mitigates the core failure mode of prior caching methods.

Abstract: High computational costs and slow inference hinder the practical application of video generation models. While prior works accelerate the generation process through feature caching, they often suffer from notable quality degradation. In this work, we reveal that this issue arises from their inability to distinguish truly redundant features, which leads to the unintended skipping of computations on important features. To address this, we propose \textbf{PreciseCache}, a plug-and-play framework that precisely detects and skips truly redundant computations, thereby accelerating inference without sacrificing quality. Specifically, PreciseCache contains two components: LFCache for step-wise caching and BlockCache for block-wise caching. For LFCache, we compute the Low-Frequency Difference (LFD) between the prediction features of the current step and those from the previous cached step. Empirically, we observe that LFD serves as an effective measure of step-wise redundancy, accurately detecting highly redundant steps whose computation can be skipped through reusing cached features. To further accelerate generation within each non-skipped step, we propose BlockCache, which precisely detects and skips redundant computations at the block level within the network. Extensive experiments on various backbones demonstrate the effectiveness of our PreciseCache, such as achieving an average of $2.6\times$ speedup on Wan2.1-14B without noticeable quality loss.

</details>


### [150] [EraseAnything++: Enabling Concept Erasure in Rectified Flow Transformers Leveraging Multi-Object Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00978)
*Zhaoxin Fan,Nanxiang Jiang,Daiheng Gao,Shiji Zhou,Wenjun Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EraseAnything++ is a unified, utility-preserving concept-erasure framework for modern flow-matching, transformer-based text-to-image and text-to-video diffusion models, combining constrained multi-objective optimization, implicit gradient surgery, LoRA tuning, and attention-level regularization—with an anchor-and-propagate strategy for video—to achieve state-of-the-art erasure effectiveness, fidelity, and temporal consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing concept-erasure techniques struggle with new T2I/T2V architectures (flow-matching, transformer backbones, long-horizon video) such as SD v3, Flux, and OpenSora. There is a pressing need to remove undesired concepts without degrading overall generative quality or temporal coherence.

Method: Formulate erasure as a constrained multi-objective optimization problem balancing concept removal and generative utility. Resolve conflicting objectives via a utility-preserving unlearning approach using implicit gradient surgery. Use LoRA-based parameter tuning plus attention-level regularization to anchor erasure on key visual representations and propagate it spatially and temporally. For video, apply an anchor-and-propagate mechanism that seeds erasure on reference frames and enforces it through subsequent transformer layers to curb temporal drift.

Result: Across image and video benchmarks, the approach surpasses prior methods on erasure effectiveness, generative fidelity, and temporal consistency, setting a new state of the art.

Conclusion: EraseAnything++ offers a general, efficient, and temporally consistent solution for concept erasure in next-generation diffusion models, preserving utility by targeting attention representations and explicitly balancing competing objectives, and is applicable to both images and videos under flow-matching training.

Abstract: Removing undesired concepts from large-scale text-to-image (T2I) and text-to-video (T2V) diffusion models while preserving overall generative quality remains a major challenge, particularly as modern models such as Stable Diffusion v3, Flux, and OpenSora employ flow-matching and transformer-based architectures and extend to long-horizon video generation. Existing concept erasure methods, designed for earlier T2I/T2V models, often fail to generalize to these paradigms. To address this issue, we propose EraseAnything++, a unified framework for concept erasure in both image and video diffusion models with flow-matching objectives. Central to our approach is formulating concept erasure as a constrained multi-objective optimization problem that explicitly balances concept removal with preservation of generative utility. To solve the resulting conflicting objectives, we introduce an efficient utility-preserving unlearning strategy based on implicit gradient surgery. Furthermore, by integrating LoRA-based parameter tuning with attention-level regularization, our method anchors erasure on key visual representations and propagates it consistently across spatial and temporal dimensions. In the video setting, we further enhance consistency through an anchor-and-propagate mechanism that initializes erasure on reference frames and enforces it throughout subsequent transformer layers, thereby mitigating temporal drift. Extensive experiments on both image and video benchmarks demonstrate that EraseAnything++ substantially outperforms prior methods in erasure effectiveness, generative fidelity, and temporal consistency, establishing a new state of the art for concept erasure in next-generation diffusion models.

</details>


### [151] [Fake It Right: Injecting Anatomical Logic into Synthetic Supervised Pre-training for Medical Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00979)
*Jiaqi Tang,Mengyan Zheng,Shu Zhang,Fandong Zhang,Qingchao Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An anatomy-informed synthetic supervised pretraining scheme for 3D medical segmentation replaces generic shapes with de-identified organ masks and uses structure-aware placement to synthesize realistic training data, yielding better performance than FDSL and SSL baselines and improving with more synthetic data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ViTs need large labeled datasets; SSL reduces labels but is constrained by privacy/logistics. FDSL scales without real data but its generic primitives lack anatomical realism and inter-organ structure, limiting the ability to learn global anatomical priors.

Method: Build a lightweight shape bank from de-identified, label-only segmentation masks (5 subjects). During synthetic patch generation, replace random placement with a structure-aware sequential process: spatial anchors enforce physiologically plausible localization; a topological graph encodes inter-organ relations to avoid impossible overlaps. Pretrain models on this anatomy-informed synthetic data, then evaluate on real datasets.

Result: On BTCV and MSD, the approach surpasses state-of-the-art FDSL by 1.74% and SSL by up to 1.66%, and shows a positive scaling trend where performance increases as more synthetic data are generated.

Conclusion: Anatomy-informed synthetic supervised pretraining offers a privacy-preserving, data-efficient way to impart global anatomical priors to segmentation models, improving accuracy over existing FDSL and SSL methods and benefiting from scalable data synthesis; code to be released.

Abstract: Vision Transformers (ViTs) excel in 3D medical segmentation but require massive annotated datasets. While Self-Supervised Learning (SSL) mitigates this using unlabeled data, it still faces strict privacy and logistical barriers. Formula-Driven Supervised Learning (FDSL) offers a privacy-preserving alternative by pre-training on synthetic mathematical primitives. However, a critical semantic gap limits its efficacy: generic shapes lack the morphological fidelity, fixed spatial layouts, and inter-organ relationships of real anatomy, preventing models from learning essential global structural priors. To bridge this gap, we propose an Anatomy-Informed Synthetic Supervised Pre-training framework unifying FDSL's infinite scalability with anatomical realism. We replace basic primitives with a lightweight shape bank with de-identified, label-only segmentation masks from 5 subjects. Furthermore, we introduce a structure-aware sequential placement strategy to govern the patch synthesis process. Instead of random placement, we enforce physiological plausibility using spatial anchors for correct localization and a topological graph to manage inter-organ interactions (e.g., preventing impossible overlaps). Extensive experiments on BTCV and MSD datasets demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art FDSL baselines and SSL methods by 1.74\% and up to 1.66\%, while exhibiting a robust scaling effect where performance improves with increased synthetic data volume. This provides a data-efficient, privacy-compliant solution for medical segmentation. The code will be made publicly available upon acceptance.

</details>


### [152] [Event-Anchored Frame Selection for Effective Long-Video Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00983)
*Wang Chen,Yongdong Luo,Yuhui Zeng,Luojun Lin,Tianyu Xie,Fei Chao,Rongrong Ji,Xiawu Zheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Event-Anchored Frame Selection (EFS), a training-free, hierarchical, event-aware frame selector that segments videos via DINO embeddings, picks per-event anchor frames by query relevance, and performs a global adaptive MMR refinement to balance event coverage, relevance, and diversity—yielding notable gains when plugged into existing LVLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long videos contain heavy frame redundancy while LVLMs have limited context windows; flat/naive sampling ignores event structure and query intent, hurting understanding and accuracy. An event-aware, query-guided selection is needed to feed LVLMs the most informative frames.

Method: 1) Use self-supervised DINO features to partition the video into visually homogeneous temporal segments as proxies for events. 2) Within each event, pick the most query-relevant frame as an anchor. 3) Use these anchors to guide a global, adaptive Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR) selection that jointly optimizes event coverage, query relevance, and visual diversity. The module is training-free and plug-and-play for LVLMs.

Result: When integrated with LLaVA-Video-7B, EFS improves accuracy by +4.7% on VideoMME, +4.9% on LongVideoBench, and +8.8% on MLVU.

Conclusion: Hierarchical, event-aware, query-guided frame selection via EFS effectively mitigates context limits and redundancy, improving long-video understanding in LVLMs without extra training and offering a practical drop-in enhancement.

Abstract: Massive frame redundancy and limited context window make efficient frame selection crucial for long-video understanding with large vision-language models (LVLMs). Prevailing approaches, however, adopt a flat sampling paradigm which treats the video as an unstructured collection of frames. In this paper, we introduce Event-Anchored Frame Selection (EFS), a hierarchical, event-aware pipeline. Leveraging self-supervised DINO embeddings, EFS first partitions the video stream into visually homogeneous temporal segments, which serve as proxies for semantic events. Within each event, it then selects the most query-relevant frame as an anchor. These anchors act as structural priors that guide a global refinement stage using an adaptive Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR) scheme. This pipeline ensures the final keyframe set jointly optimizes for event coverage, query relevance, and visual diversity. As a training-free, plug-and-play module, EFS can be seamlessly integrated into off-the-shelf LVLMs, yielding substantial gains on challenging video understanding benchmarks. Specifically, when applied to LLaVA-Video-7B, EFS improves accuracy by 4.7%, 4.9%, and 8.8% on VideoMME, LongVideoBench, and MLVU, respectively.

</details>


### [153] [The Texture-Shape Dilemma: Boundary-Safe Synthetic Generation for 3D Medical Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00985)
*Jiaqi Tang,Weixuan Xu,Shu Zhang,Fandong Zhang,Qingchao Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They introduce a physics-inspired, spatially‑decoupled synthetic data generator for FDSL that shields boundaries and injects realistic spectral textures into object cores, fixing boundary aliasing and boosting ViT segmentation on BTCV/MSD without real patient data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ViTs need large labeled datasets, which are scarce in medicine due to privacy and annotation cost. Existing FDSL uses overly simple, texture‑free shapes; naïvely adding high‑frequency textures corrupts gradients near boundaries (boundary aliasing), hurting anatomical delineation. A method is needed to learn robust shapes while gaining invariance to modality‑specific noise/textures.

Method: Physics‑inspired Spatially‑Decoupled Synthesis: orthogonalize shape and texture synthesis. (1) Build a gradient‑shielded buffer zone around object boundaries based on distance transforms to preserve clear edge signals for learning shapes. (2) Inject physics‑driven, spectral (frequency‑domain) textures into the object core to mimic CT/MRI acquisition noise and tissue textures, leaving boundaries uncorrupted. Use the synthesized data to supervise ViT training.

Result: On BTCV and MSD segmentation benchmarks, the approach outperforms prior FDSL and even SSL methods trained on real medical data, improving performance by 1.43% on BTCV and up to 1.51% on MSD.

Conclusion: Spatially decoupling boundary learning from texture synthesis mitigates boundary aliasing, enabling scalable, annotation‑free pretraining for medical ViTs that can rival or surpass SSL on real data. Code will be released upon acceptance.

Abstract: Vision Transformers (ViTs) have revolutionized medical image analysis, yet their data-hungry nature clashes with the scarcity and privacy constraints of clinical archives. Formula-Driven Supervised Learning (FDSL) has emerged as a promising solution to this bottleneck, synthesizing infinite annotated samples from mathematical formulas without utilizing real patient data. However, existing FDSL paradigms rely on simple geometric shapes with homogeneous intensities, creating a substantial gap by neglecting tissue textures and noise patterns inherent in modalities like CT and MRI. In this paper, we identify a critical optimization conflict termed boundary aliasing: when high-frequency synthetic textures are naively added, they corrupt the image gradient signals necessary for learning structural boundaries, causing the model to fail in delineating real anatomical margins. To bridge this gap, we propose a novel Physics-inspired Spatially-Decoupled Synthesis framework. Our approach orthogonalizes the synthesis process: it first constructs a gradient-shielded buffer zone based on boundary distance to ensure stable shape learning, and subsequently injects physics-driven spectral textures into the object core. This design effectively reconciles robust shape representation learning with invariance to acquisition noise. Extensive experiments on the BTCV and MSD datasets demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms previous FDSL, as well as SSL methods trained on real-world medical datasets, by 1.43% on BTCV and up to 1.51% on MSD task, offering a scalable, annotation-free foundation for medical ViTs. The code will be made publicly available upon acceptance.

</details>


### [154] [Foundation Models in Remote Sensing: Evolving from Unimodality to Multimodality](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00988)
*Danfeng Hong,Chenyu Li,Xuyang Li,Gustau Camps-Valls,Jocelyn Chanussot*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A comprehensive survey of foundation models for remote sensing that frames the field’s shift from unimodal to multimodal approaches, catalogs existing models, and offers practical, tutorial-style guidance for training and applying them.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RS data volume and heterogeneity are exploding, outpacing traditional modeling and understanding methods. Foundation models promise scalable, generalizable representations that can unlock broader RS applications; newcomers need a structured entry point to this fast-moving area.

Method: Systematic literature review and taxonomy building, categorizing RS foundation models into unimodal and multimodal; framing around three questions (what, why, how). Includes a tutorial-style section detailing training practices and application workflows for RS tasks.

Result: A synthesized taxonomy of RS foundation models, a perspective on their evolution from unimodal to multimodal, clarified motivations and definitions, and practical guidance for beginners to train and deploy such models. Primarily a knowledge resource rather than reporting new empirical benchmarks.

Conclusion: Foundation models are becoming central to RS; understanding their modalities, training paradigms, and application patterns is crucial. The survey provides a structured roadmap and hands-on guidance to lower the barrier for researchers, especially juniors, to adopt and extend foundation models across RS tasks.

Abstract: Remote sensing (RS) techniques are increasingly crucial for deepening our understanding of the planet. As the volume and diversity of RS data continue to grow exponentially, there is an urgent need for advanced data modeling and understanding capabilities to manage and interpret these vast datasets effectively. Foundation models present significant new growth opportunities and immense potential to revolutionize the RS field. In this paper, we conduct a comprehensive technical survey on foundation models in RS, offering a brand-new perspective by exploring their evolution from unimodality to multimodality. We hope this work serves as a valuable entry point for researchers interested in both foundation models and RS and helps them launch new projects or explore new research topics in this rapidly evolving area. This survey addresses the following three key questions: What are foundation models in RS? Why are foundation models needed in RS? How can we effectively guide junior researchers in gaining a comprehensive and practical understanding of foundation models in RS applications? More specifically, we begin by outlining the background and motivation, emphasizing the importance of foundation models in RS. We then review existing foundation models in RS, systematically categorizing them into unimodal and multimodal approaches. Additionally, we provide a tutorial-like section to guide researchers, especially beginners, on how to train foundation models in RS and apply them to real-world tasks. The survey aims to equip researchers in RS with a deeper and more efficient understanding of foundation models, enabling them to get started easily and effectively apply these models across various RS applications.

</details>


### [155] [MLRecon: Robust Markerless Freehand 3D Ultrasound Reconstruction via Coarse-to-Fine Pose Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00990)
*Yi Zhang,Puxun Tu,Kun Wang,Yulin Yan,Tao Ying,Xiaojun Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MLRecon is a low-cost, markerless freehand 3D ultrasound reconstruction system that uses a single RGB-D camera plus vision foundation models, a divergence detector, and a dual-stage pose refinement to achieve drift-resilient 6-DoF probe tracking and sub-millimeter 3D reconstructions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Freehand 3D US needs accurate probe tracking. Existing options either require expensive external markers, intrusive onboard sensors, or suffer from drift when sensorless. There is a need for accessible, robust, and low-drift tracking to enable volumetric US in resource-limited settings.

Method: Use a commodity RGB-D camera to continuously track the 6-DoF pose of a standard 2D US probe via models built on vision foundation models for generalization. Add a vision-guided divergence detector to monitor tracking integrity and automatically trigger recovery on failures. Introduce a dual-stage pose refinement network that explicitly removes high-frequency jitter and corrects low-frequency bias, preserving operator motion kinematics while denoising the trajectory. Reconstruct 3D US volumes from the stabilized poses and 2D slices.

Result: On complex probe trajectories, MLRecon achieves average position errors down to 0.88 mm and produces 3D reconstructions with sub-millimeter mean surface accuracy, outperforming both sensorless and sensor-aided baselines while maintaining continuous scanning through automatic recovery.

Conclusion: Markerless, RGB-D–based tracking augmented with foundation-model priors and explicit two-scale pose refinement can deliver robust, low-drift freehand 3D US at low cost, setting a new performance baseline and improving accessibility for clinical use, especially in resource-constrained environments.

Abstract: Freehand 3D ultrasound (US) reconstruction promises volumetric imaging with the flexibility of standard 2D probes, yet existing tracking paradigms face a restrictive trilemma: marker-based systems demand prohibitive costs, inside-out methods require intrusive sensor attachment, and sensorless approaches suffer from severe cumulative drift. To overcome these limitations, we present MLRecon, a robust markerless 3D US reconstruction framework delivering drift-resilient 6D probe pose tracking using a single commodity RGB-D camera. Leveraging the generalization power of vision foundation models, our pipeline enables continuous markerless tracking of the probe, augmented by a vision-guided divergence detector that autonomously monitors tracking integrity and triggers failure recovery to ensure uninterrupted scanning. Crucially, we further propose a dual-stage pose refinement network that explicitly disentangles high-frequency jitter from low-frequency bias, effectively denoising the trajectory while maintaining the kinematic fidelity of operator maneuvers. Experiments demonstrate that MLRecon significantly outperforms competing sensorless and sensor-aided methods, achieving average position errors as low as 0.88 mm on complex trajectories and yielding high-quality 3D reconstructions with sub-millimeter mean surface accuracy. This establishes a new benchmark for low-cost, accessible volumetric US imaging in resource-limited clinical settings.

</details>


### [156] [Let Your Image Move with Your Motion! -- Implicit Multi-Object Multi-Motion Transfer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01000)
*Yuze Li,Dong Gong,Xiao Cao,Junchao Yuan,Dongsheng Li,Lei Zhou,Yun Sing Koh,Cheng Yan,Xinyu Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FlexiMMT is an implicit image-to-video framework that enables multi-object, multi-motion transfer by assigning motions from multiple reference videos to different objects via object-specific masked attention and efficient mask propagation, achieving state-of-the-art precision and compositionality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current motion transfer methods mainly handle single objects and fail when multiple objects require distinct motions due to cross-object motion entanglement. There is a need for precise, independent motion control per object and flexible recombination of motions from different sources.

Method: From a static multi-object image and multiple reference videos, FlexiMMT independently extracts motion representations for each reference and maps them to target objects. It introduces (1) Motion Decoupled Mask Attention that uses object-specific masks to constrain attention so motion/text tokens influence only designated regions, and (2) Differentiated Mask Propagation that derives object-specific masks from diffusion attention and progressively propagates them across frames for efficiency and temporal consistency.

Result: Experiments show precise, compositional motion transfer with accurate motion-to-object assignment and state-of-the-art performance in I2V-based multi-object multi-motion settings.

Conclusion: By decoupling cross-object interactions using masked attention and propagating object masks over time, FlexiMMT provides flexible, scalable, and accurate multi-object motion transfer from multiple references, setting a new state of the art.

Abstract: Motion transfer has emerged as a promising direction for controllable video generation, yet existing methods largely focus on single-object scenarios and struggle when multiple objects require distinct motion patterns. In this work, we present FlexiMMT, the first implicit image-to-video (I2V) motion transfer framework that explicitly enables multi-object, multi-motion transfer. Given a static multi-object image and multiple reference videos, FlexiMMT independently extracts motion representations and accurately assigns them to different objects, supporting flexible recombination and arbitrary motion-to-object mappings. To address the core challenge of cross-object motion entanglement, we introduce a Motion Decoupled Mask Attention Mechanism that uses object-specific masks to constrain attention, ensuring that motion and text tokens only influence their designated regions. We further propose a Differentiated Mask Propagation Mechanism that derives object-specific masks directly from diffusion attention and progressively propagates them across frames efficiently. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FlexiMMT achieves precise, compositional, and state-of-the-art performance in I2V-based multi-object multi-motion transfer.

</details>


### [157] [Dr.Occ: Depth- and Region-Guided 3D Occupancy from Surround-View Cameras for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01007)
*Xubo Zhu,Haoyang Zhang,Fei He,Rui Wu,Yanhu Shan,Wen Yang,Huai Yu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Dr.Occ tackles geometric misalignment and spatial semantic imbalance in 3D semantic occupancy by combining a depth-guided 2D→3D view transformer with a region-guided expert transformer, yielding notable gains over BEVDet4D on Occ3D-nuScenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods lack pixel-accurate depth for view transformation, causing poor 2D–3D geometric alignment, and suffer from strong spatial class imbalance/anisotropy that hampers semantic learning.

Method: Introduce Dr.Occ with two complementary components: (1) D^2-VFormer, a depth-guided 2D-to-3D view transformer that uses high-quality dense depth from MoGe-2 to build reliable geometric priors and achieve precise voxel feature alignment; (2) R/R^2-EFormer, a region-guided Mixture-of-Experts transformer that allocates region-specific experts to attend to different spatial regions, mitigating spatial semantic variation.

Result: On the Occ3D-nuScenes benchmark (vision-only), Dr.Occ improves the strong BEVDet4D baseline by +7.43% mIoU and +3.09% IoU.

Conclusion: Depth guidance provides accurate geometric alignment while region-specific experts strengthen semantic modeling; together they effectively address key obstacles in 3D semantic occupancy prediction and deliver substantial performance improvements.

Abstract: 3D semantic occupancy prediction is crucial for autonomous driving perception, offering comprehensive geometric scene understanding and semantic recognition. However, existing methods struggle with geometric misalignment in view transformation due to the lack of pixel-level accurate depth estimation, and severe spatial class imbalance where semantic categories exhibit strong spatial anisotropy. To address these challenges, we propose Dr.Occ, a depth- and region-guided occupancy prediction framework. Specifically, we introduce a depth-guided 2D-to-3D View Transformer (D$^2$-VFormer) that effectively leverages high-quality dense depth cues from MoGe-2 to construct reliable geometric priors, thereby enabling precise geometric alignment of voxel features. Moreover, inspired by the Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) framework, we propose a region-guided Expert Transformer (R/R$^2$-EFormer) that adaptively allocates region-specific experts to focus on different spatial regions, effectively addressing spatial semantic variations. Thus, the two components make complementary contributions: depth guidance ensures geometric alignment, while region experts enhance semantic learning. Experiments on the Occ3D-nuScenes benchmark demonstrate that \textbf{Dr.Occ} improves the strong baseline BEVDet4D by 7.43\% mIoU and 3.09\% IoU under the full vision-only setting.

</details>


### [158] [GeodesicNVS: Probability Density Geodesic Flow Matching for Novel View Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01010)
*Xuqin Wang,Tao Wu,Yanfeng Zhang,Lu Liu,Mingwei Sun,Yongliang Wang,Niclas Zeller,Daniel Cremers*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Replace stochastic diffusion with deterministic data-to-data flow matching for novel view synthesis, then regularize the flows with geodesics computed from diffusion-model density; this yields more view-consistent, geometrically coherent, and smoother view transitions, outperforming diffusion baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion-based NVS uses noise-to-data sampling that is inherently stochastic and can obscure deterministic scene structure, leading to cross-view inconsistencies and weak geometric coherence. A method that explicitly couples paired views and respects data-manifold geometry is needed to improve consistency.

Method: 1) Data-to-Data Flow Matching: learn deterministic transformations directly between paired views to enforce explicit correspondence and reduce stochastic variability. 2) Probability Density Geodesic Flow Matching (PDG-FM): constrain learned flows using geodesic interpolants induced by probability density metrics extracted from a pretrained diffusion model, encouraging trajectories to follow high-density regions of the data manifold for realistic, structure-preserving interpolants.

Result: Across NVS benchmarks, the approach surpasses diffusion-based baselines, exhibiting improved structural coherence and smoother transitions between viewpoints (i.e., more consistent geometry and appearance across views).

Conclusion: Deterministic flow matching augmented with data-dependent geometric regularization provides a principled way to achieve consistent novel view generation, highlighting advantages over standard diffusion paradigms for multi-view consistency.

Abstract: Recent advances in generative modeling have substantially enhanced novel view synthesis, yet maintaining consistency across viewpoints remains challenging. Diffusion-based models rely on stochastic noise-to-data transitions, which obscure deterministic structures and yield inconsistent view predictions. We propose a Data-to-Data Flow Matching framework that learns deterministic transformations directly between paired views, enhancing view-consistent synthesis through explicit data coupling. To further enhance geometric coherence, we introduce Probability Density Geodesic Flow Matching (PDG-FM), which constrains flow trajectories using geodesic interpolants derived from probability density metrics of pretrained diffusion models. Such alignment with high-density regions of the data manifold promotes more realistic interpolants between samples. Empirically, our method surpasses diffusion-based NVS baselines, demonstrating improved structural coherence and smoother transitions across views. These results highlight the advantages of incorporating data-dependent geometric regularization into deterministic flow matching for consistent novel view generation.

</details>


### [159] [Implementation of Licensed Plate Detection and Noise Removal in Image Processing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01016)
*Yiquan Gao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Conceptual overview arguing that rising vehicle numbers in Malaysia create strong demand for car license plate recognition (LPR/ANPR) and listing key application domains; no technical method or empirical results provided.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rapid growth of vehicles in Malaysia increases the need for automated identification to improve payments, tolling, surveillance, and law enforcement; highlights broader cross-domain utility.

Method: Descriptive/position paper: defines LPR (ANPR/OCR), surveys prospective applications, and argues for deployment; no algorithmic details, datasets, or experimental setup.

Result: Establishes use cases (parking payment, toll collection, traffic monitoring, police tools) and posits feasibility and demand; provides no quantitative performance, prototypes, or evaluations.

Conclusion: LPR is timely and valuable in Malaysia and could integrate with other fields (e.g., biology, aerospace) to address specialized problems; further work should specify technical approach, implementation, and validation.

Abstract: Car license plate recognition system is an image processing technology used to identify vehicles by capturing their Car License Plates. The car license plate recognition technology is also known as automatic number-plate recognition, automatic vehicle identification, car license plate recognition or optical character recognition for cars. In Malaysia, as the number of vehicle is increasing rapidly nowadays, a pretty great number of vehicle on the road has brought about the considerable demands of car license plate recognition system. Car license plate recognition system can be implemented in electronic parking payment system, highway toll-fee system, traffic surveillance system and as police enforcement tools. Additionally, car license plate recognition system technology also has potential to be combined with various techniques in other different fields like biology, aerospace and so on to achieve the goal of solving some specialized problems.

</details>


### [160] [RaUF: Learning the Spatial Uncertainty Field of Radar](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01026)
*Shengpeng Wang,Kuangyu Wang,Wei Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RaUF is a radar spatial-perception framework that learns an anisotropic spatial uncertainty field and couples spatial structure with Doppler cues via bidirectional attention to resolve azimuth ambiguity and suppress clutter, yielding reliable, well‑calibrated detections and stronger downstream performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: mmWave radar is robust to adverse weather but suffers from low spatial fidelity, azimuth ambiguity, and clutter/multipath. Prior cross‑modal, coarse‑to‑fine supervision often ignores ambiguous feature‑to‑label mappings, causing ill‑posed geometry and hurting downstream tasks. A physically grounded way to model measurement uncertainty and leverage Doppler is needed.

Method: Model radar measurements as an anisotropic uncertainty field (direction‑dependent spread) learned via an anisotropic probabilistic model that predicts fine‑grained per‑measurement uncertainty. Introduce Bidirectional Domain Attention to jointly reason over spatial structure and Doppler consistency, using their complementarity to suppress spurious or multipath echoes. Train/evaluate on public benchmarks and real‑world data.

Result: On public and in‑the‑wild datasets, RaUF delivers spatial detections with better reliability and well‑calibrated uncertainty, and more effectively suppresses ambiguous/spurious returns. Downstream case studies show improved robustness and scalability in challenging driving scenarios.

Conclusion: Physically grounded anisotropic uncertainty modeling plus cross‑domain (spatial↔Doppler) attention resolves feature‑label ambiguity in radar perception, leading to more trustworthy spatial detection and benefits to downstream autonomous‑driving tasks.

Abstract: Millimeter-wave radar offers unique advantages in adverse weather but suffers from low spatial fidelity, severe azimuth ambiguity, and clutter-induced spurious returns. Existing methods mainly focus on improving spatial perception effectiveness via coarse-to-fine cross-modal supervision, yet often overlook the ambiguous feature-to-label mapping, which may lead to ill-posed geometric inference and pose fundamental challenges to downstream perception tasks. In this work, we propose RaUF, a spatial uncertainty field learning framework that models radar measurements through their physically grounded anisotropic properties. To resolve conflicting feature-to-label mapping, we design an anisotropic probabilistic model that learns fine-grained uncertainty. To further enhance reliability, we propose a Bidirectional Domain Attention mechanism that exploits the mutual complementarity between spatial structure and Doppler consistency, effectively suppressing spurious or multipath-induced reflections. Extensive experiments on public benchmarks and real-world datasets demonstrate that RaUF delivers highly reliable spatial detections with well-calibrated uncertainty. Moreover, downstream case studies further validate the enhanced reliability and scalability of RaUF under challenging real-world driving scenarios.

</details>


### [161] [Content-Aware Frequency Encoding for Implicit Neural Representations with Fourier-Chebyshev Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01028)
*Junbo Ke,Yangyang Xu,You-Wei Wen,Chao Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CAFE introduces a content-aware frequency encoding for INRs by mixing multiple parallel Fourier-based linear layers via a Hadamard product to explicitly synthesize richer, task-adaptive frequency bases; CAFE+ augments this with Chebyshev features for stronger, more stable representations, achieving superior results across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Implicit Neural Representations struggle with spectral bias, tending to capture low frequencies and missing high-frequency detail. Fixed Fourier encodings make MLPs compose required high frequencies indirectly and inefficiently, constraining representational capacity.

Method: CAFE constructs Fourier features through several parallel linear layers and combines them element-wise (Hadamard product) to explicitly synthesize a wider set of frequency bases. Learned weights select task-relevant frequencies. CAFE+ further incorporates Chebyshev features to complement Fourier bases, aiming for stronger and more stable frequency representations.

Result: Across multiple benchmarks, the method is reported as both effective and efficient, consistently outperforming existing frequency-encoding approaches for INRs.

Conclusion: Content-aware frequency synthesis mitigates spectral bias and enhances high-frequency modeling in INRs. The Chebyshev-augmented variant improves stability and strength of the representation. The approach is validated empirically and released with code.

Abstract: Implicit Neural Representations (INRs) have emerged as a powerful paradigm for various signal processing tasks, but their inherent spectral bias limits the ability to capture high-frequency details. Existing methods partially mitigate this issue by using Fourier-based features, which usually rely on fixed frequency bases. This forces multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) to inefficiently compose the required frequencies, thereby constraining their representational capacity. To address this limitation, we propose Content-Aware Frequency Encoding (CAFE), which builds upon Fourier features through multiple parallel linear layers combined via a Hadamard product. CAFE can explicitly and efficiently synthesize a broader range of frequency bases, while the learned weights enable the selection of task-relevant frequencies. Furthermore, we extend this framework to CAFE+, which incorporates Chebyshev features as a complementary component to Fourier bases. This combination provides a stronger and more stable frequency representation. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks validate the effectiveness and efficiency of our approach, consistently achieving superior performance over existing methods. Our code is available at https://github.com/JunboKe0619/CAFE.

</details>


### [162] [Vision-Language Feature Alignment for Road Anomaly Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01029)
*Zhuolin He,Jiacheng Tang,Jian Pu,Xiangyang Xue*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VL-Anomaly fuses vision-language priors (CLIP) with a prompt-learned alignment of Mask2Former features and a multi-source inference scheme to cut background false positives and boost recall in road anomaly segmentation, achieving SOTA on RoadAnomaly, SMIYC, and Fishyscapes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pixel-statistics-based anomaly segmentation misfires on semantically normal backgrounds (sky, vegetation) and misses true OOD obstacles—unsafe for autonomous systems. Incorporating high-level semantics from VLMs can better distinguish true anomalies from benign background regions.

Method: 1) Prompt-learning alignment module: adapt Mask2Former visual features to CLIP text embeddings of known classes to suppress spurious anomaly activations in background. 2) Multi-source inference: combine text-guided pixel similarity, global CLIP image–text similarity, and detector confidence to form a more reliable anomaly score.

Result: Consistently outperforms prior methods (state-of-the-art) on RoadAnomaly, SMIYC, and Fishyscapes benchmarks, reducing background false positives and improving anomaly recall. Code is publicly available.

Conclusion: Injecting VLM semantic priors and fusing complementary signals at inference yields more dependable road anomaly segmentation, improving safety-relevant perception. The approach appears broadly applicable to OOD detection in complex scenes and is practical due to released code.

Abstract: Safe autonomous systems in complex environments require robust road anomaly segmentation to identify unknown obstacles. However, existing approaches often rely on pixel-level statistics to determine whether a region appears anomalous. This reliance leads to high false-positive rates on semantically normal background regions such as sky or vegetation, and poor recall of true Out-of-distribution (OOD) instances, thereby posing safety risks for robotic perception and decision-making. To address these challenges, we propose VL-Anomaly, a vision-language anomaly segmentation framework that incorporates semantic priors from pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs). Specifically, we design a prompt learning-driven alignment module that adapts Mask2Forme's visual features to CLIP text embeddings of known categories, effectively suppressing spurious anomaly responses in background regions. At inference time, we further introduce a multi-source inference strategy that integrates text-guided similarity, CLIP-based image-text similarity and detector confidence, enabling more reliable anomaly prediction by leveraging complementary information sources. Extensive experiments demonstrate that VL-Anomaly achieves state-of-the-art performance on benchmark datasets including RoadAnomaly, SMIYC and Fishyscapes.Code is released on https://github.com/NickHezhuolin/VL-aligner-Road-anomaly-segment.

</details>


### [163] [Reparameterized Tensor Ring Functional Decomposition for Multi-Dimensional Data Recovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01034)
*Yangyang Xu,Junbo Ke,You-Wei Wen,Chao Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They generalize Tensor Ring (TR) decomposition to a continuous, INR-parameterized setting, reveal a frequency bottleneck that limits high-frequency detail, and fix it via a reparameterized TR factorization using a learnable latent tensor plus a fixed basis, with theory (better training dynamics, Lipschitz continuity) and SOTA results on image and point-cloud restoration tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical TR works on discrete meshgrids and struggles with non-meshgrid data and fine-scale details when naively made continuous. There is a need for a continuous TR model that can capture high frequencies and train reliably on both meshgrid and irregular data.

Method: 1) Define TR functional decomposition with factors parameterized by implicit neural representations (INRs). 2) Perform frequency-domain analysis showing TR factors’ spectra control the reconstructible frequencies, exposing limited high-frequency capacity. 3) Reparameterize each TR factor as a structured combination of a learnable latent tensor and a fixed basis to enhance frequency coverage and training dynamics. 4) Provide a principled initialization for the basis and prove Lipschitz continuity of the model.

Result: Empirically outperforms prior methods on image inpainting, denoising, super-resolution, and point cloud recovery. Qualitative and quantitative gains suggest better high-frequency modeling; code released for reproducibility.

Conclusion: Reparameterizing TR factors over a fixed basis alleviates spectral limitations, improves optimization, and enables continuous TR models to capture fine details across grid and non-grid data, establishing a strong practical baseline supported by theory.

Abstract: Tensor Ring (TR) decomposition is a powerful tool for high-order data modeling, but is inherently restricted to discrete forms defined on fixed meshgrids. In this work, we propose a TR functional decomposition for both meshgrid and non-meshgrid data, where factors are parameterized by Implicit Neural Representations (INRs). However, optimizing this continuous framework to capture fine-scale details is intrinsically difficult. Through a frequency-domain analysis, we demonstrate that the spectral structure of TR factors determines the frequency composition of the reconstructed tensor and limits the high-frequency modeling capacity. To mitigate this, we propose a reparameterized TR functional decomposition, in which each TR factor is a structured combination of a learnable latent tensor and a fixed basis. This reparameterization is theoretically shown to improve the training dynamics of TR factor learning. We further derive a principled initialization scheme for the fixed basis and prove the Lipschitz continuity of our proposed model. Extensive experiments on image inpainting, denoising, super-resolution, and point cloud recovery demonstrate that our method achieves consistently superior performance over existing approaches. Code is available at https://github.com/YangyangXu2002/RepTRFD.

</details>


### [164] [SMR-Net:Robot Snap Detection Based on Multi-Scale Features and Self-Attention Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01036)
*Kuanxu Hou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Purpose-built sensor plus SMR-Net (an attention-enhanced multi-scale detector) delivers more robust, precise snap detection/localization for robotic assembly, beating Faster R-CNN by ~6% IoU and ~2% mAP on two datasets, especially in challenging transparent/low-contrast cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-precision robotic snap assembly hinges on accurate detection and localization, but traditional vision struggles in complex scenes (transparent, low-contrast snaps), leading to robustness issues and large localization errors that degrade assembly quality.

Method: Design a dedicated sensor and introduce SMR-Net: (1) an attention-embedded feature extractor to amplify snap cues and suppress noise; (2) parallel processing of three multi-scale feature maps using standard and dilated convolutions to unify dimensions while preserving resolution; (3) an adaptive reweighting network to fuse features by dynamically weighting scales, integrating fine details with global semantics for improved localization/detection.

Result: On Type A and Type B snap datasets vs Faster R-CNN: IoU improves by 6.52% and 5.8%; mAP increases by 2.8% and 1.5%, respectively.

Conclusion: The sensor+SMR-Net pipeline significantly enhances robustness and precision for snap detection/localization under challenging conditions, better meeting high-precision robotic assembly requirements, as evidenced by consistent gains over a strong baseline on two datasets.

Abstract: In robot automated assembly, snap assembly precision and efficiency directly determine overall production quality. As a core prerequisite, snap detection and localization critically affect subsequent assembly success. Traditional visual methods suffer from poor robustness and large localization errors when handling complex scenarios (e.g., transparent or low-contrast snaps), failing to meet high-precision assembly demands. To address this, this paper designs a dedicated sensor and proposes SMR-Net, an self-attention-based multi-scale object detection algorithm, to synergistically enhance detection and localization performance. SMR-Net adopts an attention-enhanced multi-scale feature fusion architecture: raw sensor data is encoded via an attention-embedded feature extractor to strengthen key snap features and suppress noise; three multi-scale feature maps are processed in parallel with standard and dilated convolution for dimension unification while preserving resolution; an adaptive reweighting network dynamically assigns weights to fused features, generating fine representations integrating details and global semantics. Experimental results on Type A and Type B snap datasets show SMR-Net outperforms traditional Faster R-CNN significantly: Intersection over Union (IoU) improves by 6.52% and 5.8%, and mean Average Precision (mAP) increases by 2.8% and 1.5% respectively. This fully demonstrates the method's superiority in complex snap detection and localization tasks.

</details>


### [165] [From Intuition to Investigation: A Tool-Augmented Reasoning MLLM Framework for Generalizable Face Anti-Spoofing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01038)
*Haoyuan Zhang,Keyao Wang,Guosheng Zhang,Haixiao Yue,Zhiwen Tan,Siran Peng,Tianshuo Zhang,Xiao Tan,Kunbin Chen,Wei He,Jingdong Wang,Ajian Liu,Xiangyu Zhu,Zhen Lei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TAR-FAS reframes face anti-spoofing as tool-augmented chain-of-thought reasoning in an MLLM, training it to invoke external visual tools for fine-grained clues. With a new ToolFAS-16K dataset of multi-turn tool-use traces and a DT-GRPO optimization scheme, it achieves state-of-the-art cross-domain performance under a one-to-eleven protocol while providing interpretable, stepwise analyses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Binary FAS and prior MLLM-based description generation generalize poorly because they emphasize high-level, intuitive cues and miss subtle, fine-grained spoof artifacts. The work seeks a method that captures nuanced visual patterns to improve robustness across domains.

Method: Introduce Tool-Augmented Reasoning FAS (TAR-FAS): reformulate FAS as Chain-of-Thought with Visual Tools (CoT-VT) where the MLLM starts with coarse observations and selectively calls external visual tools (e.g., texture/frequency/depth/rPPG/edge analyses). Build ToolFAS-16K with annotated multi-turn tool-use trajectories via a tool-augmented data pipeline. Train with a tool-aware procedure using Diverse-Tool Group Relative Policy Optimization (DT-GRPO) so the model learns when and which tools to use efficiently.

Result: Under a challenging one-to-eleven cross-domain protocol, TAR-FAS delivers state-of-the-art results and yields fine-grained, interpretable investigations of spoof cues in experiments.

Conclusion: Tool-augmented reasoning enables MLLMs to capture subtle spoof patterns and generalize better across domains. The provided dataset and DT-GRPO training pipeline facilitate effective tool use, offering improved performance and more trustworthy, transparent FAS decisions.

Abstract: Face recognition remains vulnerable to presentation attacks, calling for robust Face Anti-Spoofing (FAS) solutions. Recent MLLM-based FAS methods reformulate the binary classification task as the generation of brief textual descriptions to improve cross-domain generalization. However, their generalizability is still limited, as such descriptions mainly capture intuitive semantic cues (e.g., mask contours) while struggling to perceive fine-grained visual patterns. To address this limitation, we incorporate external visual tools into MLLMs to encourage deeper investigation of subtle spoof clues. Specifically, we propose the Tool-Augmented Reasoning FAS (TAR-FAS) framework, which reformulates the FAS task as a Chain-of-Thought with Visual Tools (CoT-VT) paradigm, allowing MLLMs to begin with intuitive observations and adaptively invoke external visual tools for fine-grained investigation. To this end, we design a tool-augmented data annotation pipeline and construct the ToolFAS-16K dataset, which contains multi-turn tool-use reasoning trajectories. Furthermore, we introduce a tool-aware FAS training pipeline, where Diverse-Tool Group Relative Policy Optimization (DT-GRPO) enables the model to autonomously learn efficient tool use. Extensive experiments under a challenging one-to-eleven cross-domain protocol demonstrate that TAR-FAS achieves SOTA performance while providing fine-grained visual investigation for trustworthy spoof detection.

</details>


### [166] [MM-DeepResearch: A Simple and Effective Multimodal Agentic Search Baseline](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01050)
*Huanjin Yao,Qixiang Yin,Min Yang,Ziwang Zhao,Yibo Wang,Haotian Luo,Jingyi Zhang,Jiaxing Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MM-DeepResearch is a multimodal research agent that tackles search-intensive tasks by generating cross-modal QA data with a hypergraph method (Hyper-Search), learning specialized search-tool experts recombined via tree search (DR-TTS), and training with an offline multi-tool search engine—achieving superior performance across benchmarks at lower cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable an agent that can explicitly reason, plan, use multiple tools, and synthesize visual-text information for deep research. Current obstacles are: scarce multimodal, search-heavy QA data; ineffective/absent search trajectories; and high cost of training with online search APIs.

Method: (1) Hyper-Search: builds a hypergraph over visual and textual nodes within/across modalities to generate search-intensive multimodal QA pairs requiring multi-tool use. (2) DR-TTS: decomposes tasks by search-tool type, trains specialized tool experts, and recomposes them with tree search to discover successful multi-step trajectories. (3) Offline multi-tool search engine to support agentic reinforcement learning without online API costs. These are integrated into the MM-DeepResearch agent for explicit reasoning/planning and cross-modal synthesis.

Result: Extensive experiments show MM-DeepResearch outperforms baselines on multiple benchmarks; it successfully solves complex multimodal tasks requiring various search tools. Code is released for reproducibility.

Conclusion: A unified data-generation, trajectory-discovery, and cost-efficient training pipeline yields a capable multimodal research agent. The approach mitigates data scarcity, trajectory quality, and cost barriers, advancing practical, tool-augmented multimodal reasoning and search.

Abstract: We aim to develop a multimodal research agent capable of explicit reasoning and planning, multi-tool invocation, and cross-modal information synthesis, enabling it to conduct deep research tasks. However, we observe three main challenges in developing such agents: (1) scarcity of search-intensive multimodal QA data, (2) lack of effective search trajectories, and (3) prohibitive cost of training with online search APIs. To tackle them, we first propose Hyper-Search, a hypergraph-based QA generation method that models and connects visual and textual nodes within and across modalities, enabling to generate search-intensive multimodal QA pairs that require invoking various search tools to solve. Second, we introduce DR-TTS, which first decomposes search-involved tasks into several categories according to search tool types, and respectively optimize specialized search tool experts for each tool. It then recomposes tool experts to jointly explore search trajectories via tree search, producing trajectories that successfully solve complex tasks using various search tools. Third, we build an offline search engine supporting multiple search tools, enabling agentic reinforcement learning without using costly online search APIs. With the three designs, we develop MM-DeepResearch, a powerful multimodal deep research agent, and extensive results shows its superiority across benchmarks. Code is available at https://github.com/HJYao00/MM-DeepResearch

</details>


### [167] [Unleashing VLA Potentials in Autonomous Driving via Explicit Learning from Failures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01063)
*Yuechen Luo,Qimao Chen,Fang Li,Shaoqing Xu,Jaxin Liu,Ziying Song,Zhi-xin Yang,Fuxi Wen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ELF-VLA augments RL for autonomous-driving VLA agents with structured failure diagnostics and feedback-guided refinements, converting sparse zero-reward episodes into targeted, high-reward training signals. This breaks exploration plateaus and achieves SOTA on NAVSIM (PDMS, EPDMS, and high-level planning accuracy).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA policies fine-tuned via supervision often get stuck during RL because exploration is constrained and rewards in critical, long-tail cases are sparse and uninformative (frequent zero scores). Scalar rewards signal failure but not its cause (planning, reasoning, or execution), preventing effective credit assignment and improvement.

Method: Explicit Learning from Failures (ELF-VLA): generate detailed, interpretable failure reports that identify the error mode; use these diagnostics to create a feedback-guided refinement of the policy’s outputs; inject the corrected, high-reward samples back into the RL training batch to provide targeted gradients, enabling learning in scenarios where unguided exploration fails.

Result: Compared to RL baselines, ELF-VLA unlocks latent capabilities of VLA models and delivers state-of-the-art results on the NAVSIM benchmark for overall PDMS, EPDMS, and high-level planning accuracy.

Conclusion: Structuring and leveraging explicit failure feedback during RL enables better credit assignment and targeted updates, helping VLA driving agents overcome exploration plateaus and handle long-tail failures, leading to superior performance on standard benchmarks.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for autonomous driving often hit a performance plateau during Reinforcement Learning (RL) optimization. This stagnation arises from exploration capabilities constrained by previous Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), leading to persistent failures in long-tail scenarios. In these critical situations, all explored actions yield a zero-value driving score. This information-sparse reward signals a failure, yet fails to identify its root cause -- whether it is due to incorrect planning, flawed reasoning, or poor trajectory execution. To address this limitation, we propose VLA with Explicit Learning from Failures (ELF-VLA), a framework that augments RL with structured diagnostic feedback. Instead of relying on a vague scalar reward, our method produces detailed, interpretable reports that identify the specific failure mode. The VLA policy then leverages this explicit feedback to generate a Feedback-Guided Refinement. By injecting these corrected, high-reward samples back into the RL training batch, our approach provides a targeted gradient, which enables the policy to solve critical scenarios that unguided exploration cannot. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method unlocks the latent capabilities of VLA models, achieving state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance on the public NAVSIM benchmark for overall PDMS, EPDMS score and high-level planning accuracy.

</details>


### [168] [LLaDA-o: An Effective and Length-Adaptive Omni Diffusion Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01068)
*Zebin You,Xiaolu Zhang,Jun Zhou,Chongxuan Li,Ji-Rong Wen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LLaDA-o is a unified, length-adaptive omni diffusion model that decouples text understanding (discrete masked diffusion) and image generation (continuous diffusion) while coupling them via a shared attention backbone, achieving state-of-the-art multimodal understanding/generation and 87.04 on DPG-Bench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for a single, efficient diffusion framework that can both understand text and generate images, reduce redundant computation when conditions are fixed, and enable flexible-length decoding in multimodal tasks without changing model architecture.

Method: Propose a Mixture of Diffusion (MoD) framework: use discrete masked diffusion for text understanding and continuous diffusion for visual generation; couple both via a shared, simple, and efficient attention backbone to avoid redundant computation. Introduce a data-centric length adaptation strategy to support flexible-length decoding across modalities without architectural modifications.

Result: LLaDA-o delivers state-of-the-art results among omni-diffusion models on multimodal understanding and generation benchmarks, including a score of 87.04 on DPG-Bench for text-to-image generation, and demonstrates computational efficiency due to the shared backbone.

Conclusion: A unified MoD approach with a shared attention backbone and data-driven length adaptation is effective for multimodal understanding and generation, combining accuracy, efficiency, and flexibility. Open-source code supports reproducibility and adoption.

Abstract: We present \textbf{LLaDA-o}, an effective and length-adaptive omni diffusion model for multimodal understanding and generation. LLaDA-o is built on a Mixture of Diffusion (MoD) framework that decouples discrete masked diffusion for text understanding and continuous diffusion for visual generation, while coupling them through a shared, simple, and efficient attention backbone that reduces redundant computation for fixed conditions. Building on MoD, we further introduce a data-centric length adaptation strategy that enables flexible-length decoding in multimodal settings without architectural changes. Extensive experiments show that LLaDA-o achieves state-of-the-art performance among omni-diffusion models on multimodal understanding and generation benchmarks, and reaches 87.04 on DPG-Bench for text-to-image generation, supporting the effectiveness of unified omni diffusion modeling. Code is available at https://github.com/ML-GSAI/LLaDA-o.

</details>


### [169] [Flow Matching-enabled Test-Time Refinement for Unsupervised Cardiac MR Registration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01073)
*Yunguan Fu,Wenjia Bai,Wen Yan,Matthew J Clarkson,Rhodri Huw Davies,Yipeng Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FlowReg is a fast, few-step, unsupervised cardiac MR image registration framework that uses flow-matching in displacement space with a warmup–reflow training scheme and an Initial Guess feedback loop. It achieves state-of-the-art accuracy with as few as two inference steps, improves LVEF estimation, generalizes across datasets, and adds only 0.7% parameters, without using segmentation labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion-based unsupervised registration works well but requires expensive multi-step inference, limiting practicality. There is a need for accurate registration that is fast (few steps), optionally refinable, generalizes across datasets, and avoids reliance on segmentation labels or heavyweight pretraining pipelines used by prior methods.

Method: FlowReg performs flow-matching directly in displacement field space. Training uses warmup–reflow: first train a strong single-step teacher; then train a student to refine from arbitrary intermediate states, enabling variable-step inference and eliminating dependence on external pretrained models. At inference, an Initial Guess strategy feeds the current prediction back as the next starting point, enabling effective refinement from step 2 onward, with minimal parameter overhead (~0.7%).

Result: On ACDC and MM2 across six tasks (including cross-dataset generalization), FlowReg beats state of the art on five tasks by +0.6% mean Dice on average, with the largest gain in left ventricle (+1.09%), and reduces LVEF error on all six tasks by −2.58 percentage points. It achieves strong performance in as few as two steps, with only 0.7% additional parameters and no segmentation labels.

Conclusion: Few-step flow-matching with warmup–reflow and Initial Guess feedback yields practical, accurate, and flexible unsupervised cardiac MR registration. It provides consistent accuracy gains, better clinical metric estimation, minimal computational overhead, and good generalization, making it suitable for real-world cardiac cine MR workflows; code is publicly available.

Abstract: Diffusion-based unsupervised image registration has been explored for cardiac cine MR, but expensive multi-step inference limits practical use. We propose FlowReg, a flow-matching framework in displacement field space that achieves strong registration in as few as two steps and supports further refinement with more steps. FlowReg uses warmup-reflow training: a single-step network first acts as a teacher, then a student learns to refine from arbitrary intermediate states, removing the need for a pre-trained model as in existing methods. An Initial Guess strategy feeds back the model prediction as the next starting point, improving refinement from step two onward. On ACDC and MM2 across six tasks (including cross-dataset generalization), FlowReg outperforms the state of the art on five tasks (+0.6% mean Dice score on average), with the largest gain in the left ventricle (+1.09%), and reduces LVEF estimation error on all six tasks (-2.58 percentage points), using only 0.7% extra parameters and no segmentation labels. Code is available at https://github.com/mathpluscode/FlowReg.

</details>


### [170] [Adaptive Augmentation-Aware Latent Learning for Robust LiDAR Semantic Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01074)
*Wangkai Li,Zhaoyang Li,Yuwen Pan,Rui Sun,Yujia Chen,Tianzhu Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A3Point adaptively exploits diverse data augmentations while localizing and mitigating augmentation‑induced semantic shift, yielding state‑of‑the‑art robustness for LiDAR point‑cloud semantic segmentation in adverse weather.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adverse weather causes large distribution shifts that degrade LiDAR segmentation. Existing augmentation-based defenses face a trade‑off: mild augmentations underfit the shift, while aggressive ones can change semantic meaning (semantic shift), limiting robustness gains.

Method: A3Point is an augmentation‑aware latent learning framework with two components: (1) Semantic Confusion Prior (SCP) latent learning to capture the model’s inherent class‑confusion structure; (2) Semantic Shift Region (SSR) localization to identify augmentation‑induced semantic changes, decouple confusion from true semantic shift, and apply adaptive optimization strategies matched to disturbance levels—thereby leveraging a wide spectrum of augmentations without corrupting semantics.

Result: Across multiple generalized LiDAR segmentation benchmarks under adverse weather, A3Point attains new state‑of‑the‑art performance and improved robustness versus prior augmentation methods (no specific metrics provided in the abstract).

Conclusion: By learning a confusion prior and localizing regions affected by augmentation‑induced semantic shift, A3Point can safely harness diverse augmentations, substantially improving LiDAR segmentation robustness in adverse conditions.

Abstract: Adverse weather conditions significantly degrade the performance of LiDAR point cloud semantic segmentation networks by introducing large distribution shifts. Existing augmentation-based methods attempt to enhance robustness by simulating weather interference during training. However, they struggle to fully exploit the potential of augmentations due to the trade-off between minor and aggressive augmentations. To address this, we propose A3Point, an adaptive augmentation-aware latent learning framework that effectively utilizes a diverse range of augmentations while mitigating the semantic shift, which refers to the change in the semantic meaning caused by augmentations. A3Point consists of two key components: semantic confusion prior (SCP) latent learning, which captures the model's inherent semantic confusion information, and semantic shift region (SSR) localization, which decouples semantic confusion and semantic shift, enabling adaptive optimization strategies for different disturbance levels. Extensive experiments on multiple standard generalized LiDAR segmentation benchmarks under adverse weather demonstrate the effectiveness of our method, setting new state-of-the-art results.

</details>


### [171] [Beyond Global Similarity: Towards Fine-Grained, Multi-Condition Multimodal Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01082)
*Xuan Lu,Kangle Li,Haohang Huang,Rui Meng,Wenjun Zeng,Xiaoyu Shen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They introduce MCMR, a large-scale benchmark to test fine-grained, multi-condition cross-modal retrieval with natural-language queries that combine visual and textual constraints across five product domains. Benchmarking multiple MLLM-based retrievers and rerankers, they find modality asymmetries, visual dominance in early ranks, text stabilizing long-tail order, and strong gains from MLLM pointwise reranking via explicit consistency checks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real user queries often specify multiple interdependent constraints spanning visuals and text, but existing benchmarks mostly assess coarse-grained or single-condition alignment, failing to reflect compositional, constraint-aware retrieval needs.

Method: Build MCMR: a dataset spanning upper/bottom clothing, jewelry, shoes, and furniture, with long-form metadata to support compositional matching. Construct natural-language queries that mix complementary visual and textual attributes. Evaluate a suite of MLLM-based multimodal retrievers and vision–language rerankers, including pointwise rerankers that verify query–candidate consistency.

Result: Empirical findings: (i) models exhibit modality asymmetries; (ii) visual signals drive top-rank precision, whereas textual metadata improves stability deeper in the ranking; (iii) MLLM-based pointwise rerankers substantially boost fine-grained matching by explicitly checking condition satisfaction.

Conclusion: MCMR provides a challenging, diagnostic benchmark for advancing compositional, constraint-aware, interpretable multimodal retrieval, highlighting the value of pairing strong visual encoders with text-rich metadata and using MLLM pointwise reranking for condition verification.

Abstract: Recent advances in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have substantially expanded the capabilities of multimodal retrieval, enabling systems to align and retrieve information across visual and textual modalities. Yet, existing benchmarks largely focus on coarse-grained or single-condition alignment, overlooking real-world scenarios where user queries specify multiple interdependent constraints across modalities. To bridge this gap, we introduce MCMR (Multi-Conditional Multimodal Retrieval): a large-scale benchmark designed to evaluate fine-grained, multi-condition cross-modal retrieval under natural-language queries. MCMR spans five product domains: upper and bottom clothing, jewelry, shoes, and furniture. It also preserves rich long-form metadata essential for compositional matching. Each query integrates complementary visual and textual attributes, requiring models to jointly satisfy all specified conditions for relevance. We benchmark a diverse suite of MLLM-based multimodal retrievers and vision-language rerankers to assess their condition-aware reasoning abilities. Experimental results reveal: (i) distinct modality asymmetries across models; (ii) visual cues dominate early-rank precision, while textual metadata stabilizes long-tail ordering; and (iii) MLLM-based pointwise rerankers markedly improve fine-grained matching by explicitly verifying query-candidate consistency. Overall, MCMR establishes a challenging and diagnostic benchmark for advancing multimodal retrieval toward compositional, constraint-aware, and interpretable understanding. Our code and dataset is available at https://github.com/EIT-NLP/MCMR

</details>


### [172] [Can Vision Language Models Assess Graphic Design Aesthetics? A Benchmark, Evaluation, and Dataset Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01083)
*Arctanx An,Shizhao Sun,Danqing Huang,Mingxi Cheng,Yan Gao,Ji Li,Yu Qiao,Jiang Bian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They introduce AesEval-Bench, a comprehensive, fully-quantifiable benchmark and dataset for assessing graphic design aesthetics, systematically evaluate multiple VLM families (proprietary, open, reasoning-augmented), expose sizable performance gaps, and provide a scalable, human-guided labeling and indicator-grounded reasoning pipeline to fine-tune VLMs for this domain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Aesthetic quality is core to graphic design but poorly handled by current VLMs due to narrow benchmarks, coarse evaluation protocols, lack of systematic model comparisons, and limited task-specific training data.

Method: 1) Build a benchmark covering 4 aesthetic dimensions, 12 indicators, and 3 measurable tasks (aesthetic judgment, region selection, precise localization). 2) Run a systematic evaluation across proprietary, open-source, and reasoning-augmented VLMs. 3) Construct a scalable training dataset via human-guided VLM labeling and indicator-grounded reasoning that links abstract indicators to concrete regions, enabling domain fine-tuning.

Result: The evaluation shows clear gaps between current VLM capabilities and the nuanced requirements of aesthetic assessment. They also produce a large training dataset with explicit indicator-region grounding and plan to release code and data to enable community progress.

Conclusion: This work establishes the first systematic framework for assessing graphic design aesthetics with VLMs, highlighting shortcomings of current models and providing resources and a methodology to develop and measure improved aesthetic reasoning.

Abstract: Assessing the aesthetic quality of graphic design is central to visual communication, yet remains underexplored in vision language models (VLMs). We investigate whether VLMs can evaluate design aesthetics in ways comparable to humans. Prior work faces three key limitations: benchmarks restricted to narrow principles and coarse evaluation protocols, a lack of systematic VLM comparisons, and limited training data for model improvement. In this work, we introduce AesEval-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark spanning four dimensions, twelve indicators, and three fully quantifiable tasks: aesthetic judgment, region selection, and precise localization. Then, we systematically evaluate proprietary, open-source, and reasoning-augmented VLMs, revealing clear performance gaps against the nuanced demands of aesthetic assessment. Moreover, we construct a training dataset to fine-tune VLMs for this domain, leveraging human-guided VLM labeling to produce task labels at scale and indicator-grounded reasoning to tie abstract indicators to concrete design regions.Together, our work establishes the first systematic framework for aesthetic quality assessment in graphic design.
