Our code and dataset will be released at: \href{https://github.com/arctanxarc/AesEval-Bench}{https://github.com/arctanxarc/AesEval-Bench}

</details>


### [173] [Differential privacy representation geometry for medical image analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01098)
*Soroosh Tayebi Arasteh,Marziyeh Mohammadi,Sven Nebelung,Daniel Truhn*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They propose DP-RGMI, a framework that explains how differential privacy (DP) degrades utility in medical imaging by separating effects on representation geometry from how task heads use those representations. On four chest X-ray datasets, DP mainly increases a utilization gap (linear-probe vs. end-to-end) even when linear separability remains, and it reshapes representation anisotropy in a non-monotonic, init-/dataset-dependent way. The framework offers reproducible diagnostics to guide privacy model choice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end metrics alone obscure why DP harms performance in medical imaging. Practitioners need interpretable diagnostics to distinguish whether utility loss comes from changes to learned features (encoder geometry) or from the task head’s ability to exploit them, to better select DP settings and architectures.

Method: Model DP as a structured transformation of representation space. Decompose performance degradation into (1) encoder geometry and (2) task-head utilization. Quantify geometry via representation displacement from initialization and spectral effective dimension; quantify utilization as the gap between linear-probe and end-to-end performance. Apply across multiple chest X-ray datasets and pretrained initializations; analyze correlations among metrics and performance.

Result: Across 594k images and four chest X-ray datasets, DP consistently increases a utilization gap even when features stay linearly separable. Representation displacement and spectral effective dimension change non-monotonically, depending on dataset and initialization, implying DP alters representation anisotropy rather than causing uniform feature collapse. Correlation analyses show end-to-end performance tracks utilization across datasets but depends on initialization; geometric metrics capture additional prior- and dataset-specific variation.

Conclusion: DP-RGMI provides a reproducible, interpretable way to diagnose privacy-induced failure modes. It reveals that under DP, much of the utility loss stems from how task heads utilize representations rather than loss of separability, and that DP reshapes feature anisotropy in complex, context-dependent ways—informing DP mechanism and model selection.

Abstract: Differential privacy (DP)'s effect in medical imaging is typically evaluated only through end-to-end performance, leaving the mechanism of privacy-induced utility loss unclear. We introduce Differential Privacy Representation Geometry for Medical Imaging (DP-RGMI), a framework that interprets DP as a structured transformation of representation space and decomposes performance degradation into encoder geometry and task-head utilization. Geometry is quantified by representation displacement from initialization and spectral effective dimension, while utilization is measured as the gap between linear-probe and end-to-end utility. Across over 594,000 images from four chest X-ray datasets and multiple pretrained initializations, we show that DP is consistently associated with a utilization gap even when linear separability is largely preserved. At the same time, displacement and spectral dimension exhibit non-monotonic, initialization- and dataset-dependent reshaping, indicating that DP alters representation anisotropy rather than uniformly collapsing features. Correlation analysis reveals that the association between end-to-end performance and utilization is robust across datasets but can vary by initialization, while geometric quantities capture additional prior- and dataset-conditioned variation. These findings position DP-RGMI as a reproducible framework for diagnosing privacy-induced failure modes and informing privacy model selection.

</details>


### [174] [ClinCoT: Clinical-Aware Visual Chain-of-Thought for Medical Vision Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01124)
*Xiwei Liu,Yulong Li,Xinlin Zhuang,Xuhui Li,Jianxu Chen,Haolin Yang,Imran Razzak,Yutong Xie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ClinCoT is a clinical-aware visual chain-of-thought training framework that grounds Med-VLM reasoning in image regions by generating region-centric preference pairs, supervising with multi-LLM rankings and scores, and optimizing with a margin-aware objective in an iterative loop—yielding better factual grounding and state-of-the-art results on medical VQA and report generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Med-VLMs hallucinate because their reasoning is weakly tied to localized pathological evidence. Existing preference optimization mainly fixes final answers, not the visual reasoning path, and standard CoT is text-centric, limiting integration of clinical visual cues. The goal is to align intermediate reasoning with actual image regions to reduce hallucinations and improve clinical reliability.

Method: 1) Automatic data generation: create hypotheses-driven region proposals and produce multiple responses with associated visual reasoning. 2) Multi-Med-LLM evaluators rank and score responses for clinical correctness and grounding. 3) Use rankings as supervision and a scoring-based, margin-aware optimization that leverages both rank order and score gaps to refine region-level reasoning trajectories. 4) Iterative learning regenerates preference data as the model policy evolves, maintaining alignment.

Result: Across three benchmarks (medical VQA and report generation), ClinCoT consistently improves factual grounding and surpasses prior preference-based alignment baselines in overall performance; qualitative and quantitative gains are reported (details not given in the abstract).

Conclusion: Shifting from response-level alignment to visually grounded, region-level chain-of-thought with margin-aware and iterative preference optimization is effective for reducing hallucinations and improving clinical decision-support performance in Med-VLMs.

Abstract: Medical Vision-Language Models have shown promising potential in clinical decision support, yet they remain prone to factual hallucinations due to insufficient grounding in localized pathological evidence. Existing medical alignment methods primarily operate at the response level through preference optimization, improving output correctness but leaving intermediate reasoning weakly connected to visual regions. Although chain-of-thought (CoT) enhances multimodal reasoning, it remains largely text-centric, limiting effective integration of clinical visual cues. To address this gap, we propose ClinCoT, a clinical-aware visual chain-of-thought framework that transforms preference optimization from response-level correction to visual-driven reasoning. We introduce an automatic data generation pipeline that constructs clinically grounded preference pairs through reasoning with hypotheses-driven region proposals. Multiple Med-LLMs evaluators rank and assign scores to each response, and these rankings serve as supervision to train the target model. We further introduce a scoring-based margin-aware optimization strategy that incorporates both preference ranking and score difference to refine region-level reasoning trajectories. To maintain alignment as the model's policy evolves during training, we adopt an iterative learning scheme that dynamically regenerates preference data. Extensive experiments on three medical VQA and report generation benchmarks demonstrate that ClinCoT consistently improves factual grounding and achieves superior performance compared with existing preference-based alignment methods.

</details>


### [175] [Predictive Reasoning with Augmented Anomaly Contrastive Learning for Compositional Visual Relations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01125)
*Chengtai Li,Yuting He,Jianfeng Ren,Ruibin Bai,Yitian Zhao,Heng Yu,Xudong Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces PR-A^2CL, a predict-and-verify visual reasoning framework that detects the outlier among four images by combining Augmented Anomaly Contrastive Learning with Predictive Anomaly Reasoning Blocks; achieves state-of-the-art results on SVRT, CVR, and MC^2R.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Compositional visual relations (CVR) are harder than simple analogies and remain underexplored. Existing methods struggle to model the large, diverse set of compositional rules and to generalize across them. A method is needed that both learns rule-sensitive representations and performs explicit rule-based reasoning to spot violations.

Method: Two core ideas: (1) Augmented Anomaly Contrastive Learning (A^2CL) that learns discriminative, generalizable features by pushing normal instances together and pulling apart normal vs. anomalous (outlier) samples, aided by data augmentation; (2) a predict-and-verify reasoning paradigm using Predictive Anomaly Reasoning Blocks (PARBs) that iteratively use features from any three images to predict the fourth, then verify discrepancies to localize rule violations. The task is formulated as choosing the outlier image among four that breaks the shared compositional rule.

Result: On SVRT, CVR, and MC^2R benchmarks, PR-A^2CL significantly outperforms prior reasoning models (exact numbers not given in the abstract). The PARB-based verification progressively identifies rule-driven discrepancies.

Conclusion: Framing CVR as anomaly detection plus predictive verification yields stronger generalization and interpretability for rule-based visual reasoning. PR-A^2CL advances state of the art on multiple benchmarks and offers a principled approach to complex visual analogies.

Abstract: While visual reasoning for simple analogies has received significant attention, compositional visual relations (CVR) remain relatively unexplored due to their greater complexity. To solve CVR tasks, we propose Predictive Reasoning with Augmented Anomaly Contrastive Learning (PR-A$^2$CL), \ie, to identify an outlier image given three other images that follow the same compositional rules. To address the challenge of modelling abundant compositional rules, an Augmented Anomaly Contrastive Learning is designed to distil discriminative and generalizable features by maximizing similarity among normal instances while minimizing similarity between normal and anomalous outliers. More importantly, a predict-and-verify paradigm is introduced for rule-based reasoning, in which a series of Predictive Anomaly Reasoning Blocks (PARBs) iteratively leverage features from three out of the four images to predict those of the remaining one. Throughout the subsequent verification stage, the PARBs progressively pinpoint the specific discrepancies attributable to the underlying rules. Experimental results on SVRT, CVR and MC$^2$R datasets show that PR-A$^2$CL significantly outperforms state-of-the-art reasoning models.

</details>


### [176] [HeroGS: Hierarchical Guidance for Robust 3D Gaussian Splatting under Sparse Views](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01099)
*Jiashu Li,Xumeng Han,Zhaoyang Wei,Zipeng Wang,Kuiran Wang,Guorong Li,Zhenjun Han,Jianbin Jiao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HeroGS introduces hierarchical guidance to make 3D Gaussian Splatting robust under sparse-view settings, converting sparse supervision into pseudo-dense signals and enforcing consistency across image, feature, and parameter levels for sharper, more faithful reconstructions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard 3D Gaussian Splatting needs dense camera coverage; with few views it suffers from irregular Gaussian distributions, blurred backgrounds, and distorted high-frequency content. The paper aims to improve structural fidelity and rendering quality when only sparse viewpoints are available.

Method: A three-level guidance framework: (1) Image-level: transform sparse supervision into pseudo-dense guidance to globally regularize Gaussian placement. (2) Feature-level: Feature-Adaptive Densification and Pruning (FADP) uses low-level features to refine high-frequency details and adaptively add/remove Gaussians, especially in background regions. (3) Parameter-level: Co-Pruned Geometry Consistency (CPG) enforces geometric consistency via parameter freezing and co-pruning to eliminate inconsistent splats. These stages jointly constrain and optimize Gaussian distributions.

Result: Across extensive experiments, the approach yields high-fidelity reconstructions with improved structural fidelity and rendering quality, consistently outperforming state-of-the-art baselines in sparse-view scenarios.

Conclusion: Hierarchical, cross-level guidance effectively regularizes and optimizes 3D Gaussian distributions, enabling robust, high-quality novel view synthesis from sparse inputs and surpassing contemporary methods.

Abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has recently emerged as a promising approach in novel view synthesis, combining photorealistic rendering with real-time efficiency. However, its success heavily relies on dense camera coverage; under sparse-view conditions, insufficient supervision leads to irregular Gaussian distributions, characterized by globally sparse coverage, blurred background, and distorted high-frequency areas. To address this, we propose HeroGS, Hierarchical Guidance for Robust 3D Gaussian Splatting, a unified framework that establishes hierarchical guidance across the image, feature, and parameter levels. At the image level, sparse supervision is converted into pseudo-dense guidance, globally regularizing the Gaussian distributions and forming a consistent foundation for subsequent optimization. Building upon this, Feature-Adaptive Densification and Pruning (FADP) at the feature level leverages low-level features to refine high-frequency details and adaptively densifies Gaussians in background regions. The optimized distributions then support Co-Pruned Geometry Consistency (CPG) at parameter level, which guides geometric consistency through parameter freezing and co-pruning, effectively removing inconsistent splats. The hierarchical guidance strategy effectively constrains and optimizes the overall Gaussian distributions, thereby enhancing both structural fidelity and rendering quality. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HeroGS achieves high-fidelity reconstructions and consistently surpasses state-of-the-art baselines under sparse-view conditions.

</details>


### [177] [TC-SSA: Token Compression via Semantic Slot Aggregation for Gigapixel Pathology Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01143)
*Zhuo Chen,Shawn Young,Lijian Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces TC-SSA, a learnable token-compression method that aggregates WSI patch features into a small set of semantic slots via gated Top-2 routing, cutting tokens to ~1.7% while preserving diagnostic signal and improving accuracy over sampling baselines; it also generalizes well to MIL tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Gigapixel WSIs produce >10^5 patches, yielding sequence lengths that exceed standard Transformer limits. Common spatial sampling discards potentially critical diagnostic evidence. There is a need for a method that keeps global slide coverage under strict token budgets without losing key semantics.

Method: Token Compression via Semantic Slot Aggregation (TC-SSA): a gated routing module sparsely assigns each patch to Top-2 semantic slots; patches are then weighted and aggregated into a fixed number of learnable slots, ensuring global coverage and a compact token set usable by vision-language models or MIL pipelines.

Result: Reduces visual tokens to ~1.7% of the original. On SlideBench (TCGA): 78.34% overall accuracy and 77.14% on the diagnosis subset, outperforming sampling-based baselines at comparable token budgets. For MIL classification: AUCs of 95.83% (TCGA-BRCA), 98.27% (TCGA-NSCLC), and 79.80% (PANDA).

Conclusion: Learnable semantic aggregation provides an effective efficiency–performance trade-off for gigapixel pathology reasoning, maintaining diagnostic fidelity under tight token budgets and outperforming sampling approaches; the approach is broadly applicable beyond VLMs to MIL tasks.

Abstract: The application of large vision-language models to computational pathology holds great promise for diagnostic assistants but faces a critical computational bottleneck: the gigapixel scale of Whole Slide Images (WSIs). A single WSI typically contains over 105 patches, creating sequence lengths that exceed the constraints of standard Transformer architectures. Existing solutions often resort to spatial sampling, which risks discarding diagnostically critical evidence. To address this, we propose TC-SSA (Token Compression via Semantic Slot Aggregation), a learnable token compression framework that aggregates patch features into a fixed number of semantic slots. A gated routing module assigns patches to slots using sparse Top-2 routing, followed by weighted aggregation, enabling global slide coverage under a strict token budget. The resulting representation retains diagnostically relevant information while reducing the number of visual tokens to 1.7% of the original sequence. On the SlideBench(TCGA), our model achieves 78.34% overall accuracy and 77.14% on the diagnosis subset, outperforming sampling-based baselines under comparable token budgets. The method also generalizes to MIL classification, reaching AUC of 95.83% on TCGA-BRCA, 98.27% on TCGA-NSCLC and 79.80% on PANDA. These results suggest that learnable semantic aggregation provides an effective trade-off between efficiency and diagnostic performance for gigapixel pathology reasoning.

</details>


### [178] [Data-Efficient Brushstroke Generation with Diffusion Models for Oil Painting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01103)
*Dantong Qin,Alessandro Bozzon,Xian Yang,Xun Zhang,Yike Guo,Pan Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: StrokeDiff is a diffusion-based, data-efficient method that learns human-like, controllable brushstroke primitives from only 470 samples using Smooth Regularization and Bézier-based conditioning, enabling diverse, coherent strokes and richer paintings validated by metrics and humans.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Creative systems rely on stroke/texture primitives that are scarce and unlike natural images, making it hard for modern generative models to learn expressive, controllable elements for process-aware content creation.

Method: Introduce StrokeDiff, a diffusion framework with Smooth Regularization (SmR) that injects stochastic visual priors during training to stabilize learning under sparse supervision without changing inference; add a Bézier-based conditioning module for control; integrate into a full stroke-based painting pipeline (prediction, generation, ordering, compositing).

Result: The approach generates diverse, structurally coherent brushstrokes and produces paintings with richer textures and layering; improvements are supported by automatic metrics and human studies.

Conclusion: Diffusion with SmR enables data-efficient, controllable primitive modeling, providing stable training from limited samples and supporting expressive, structured multimedia content creation.

Abstract: Many creative multimedia systems are built upon visual primitives such as strokes or textures, which are difficult to collect at scale and fundamentally different from natural image data. This data scarcity makes it challenging for modern generative models to learn expressive and controllable primitives, limiting their use in process-aware content creation. In this work, we study the problem of learning human-like brushstroke generation from a small set of hand-drawn samples (n=470) and propose StrokeDiff, a diffusion-based framework with Smooth Regularization (SmR). SmR injects stochastic visual priors during training, providing a simple mechanism to stabilize diffusion models under sparse supervision without altering the inference process. We further show how the learned primitives can be made controllable through a Bézier-based conditioning module and integrated into a complete stroke-based painting pipeline, including prediction, generation, ordering, and compositing. This demonstrates how data-efficient primitive modeling can support expressive and structured multimedia content creation. Experiments indicate that the proposed approach produces diverse and structurally coherent brushstrokes and enables paintings with richer texture and layering, validated by both automatic metrics and human evaluation.

</details>


### [179] [GRAD-Former: Gated Robust Attention-based Differential Transformer for Change Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01161)
*Durgesh Ameta,Ujjwal Mishra,Praful Hambarde,Amit Shukla*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GRAD-Former is a compact, context-aware change detection model for very high-resolution satellite imagery that couples global–local feature reasoning (via AFRAR: SEA + GLFR) to outperform prior CNN/Transformer/SSM methods across standard benchmarks while using fewer parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CNN/Transformer/SSM approaches struggle to finely delineate change regions in VHR imagery, suffer quadratic complexity and data inefficiency, and underuse rich spatial context—creating a need for a more efficient, globally and locally aware model that works well with limited training data.

Method: Introduce GRAD-Former with a lightweight encoder–fusion–decoder design. The encoder uses AFRAR (Adaptive Feature Relevance and Refinement), comprising: (1) Selective Embedding Amplification (SEA) with gating to amplify relevant features and suppress irrelevant ones; (2) Global–Local Feature Refinement (GLFR) using differential attention and multiple softmax heaps to integrate global–local context. Fusion and decoder blocks produce precise change maps with reduced model size.

Result: Across three challenging datasets (LEVIR-CD, CDD, DSIFN-CD), GRAD-Former achieves state-of-the-art performance on all reported metrics while using fewer parameters than competing methods, indicating both accuracy and efficiency gains.

Conclusion: By unifying selective amplification with global–local differential attention in a compact architecture, GRAD-Former sets a new benchmark for remote sensing change detection, offering precise delineation with lower computational and parameter costs; code is to be released on GitHub.

Abstract: Change detection (CD) in remote sensing aims to identify semantic differences between satellite images captured at different times. While deep learning has significantly advanced this field, existing approaches based on convolutional neural networks (CNNs), transformers and Selective State Space Models (SSMs) still struggle to precisely delineate change regions. In particular, traditional transformer-based methods suffer from quadratic computational complexity when applied to very high-resolution (VHR) satellite images and often perform poorly with limited training data, leading to under-utilization of the rich spatial information available in VHR imagery. We present GRAD-Former, a novel framework that enhances contextual understanding while maintaining efficiency through reduced model size. The proposed framework consists of a novel encoder with Adaptive Feature Relevance and Refinement (AFRAR) module, fusion and decoder blocks. AFRAR integrates global-local contextual awareness through two proposed components: the Selective Embedding Amplification (SEA) module and the Global-Local Feature Refinement (GLFR) module. SEA and GLFR leverage gating mechanisms and differential attention, respectively, which generates multiple softmax heaps to capture important features while minimizing the captured irreverent features. Multiple experiments across three challenging CD datasets (LEVIR-CD, CDD, DSIFN-CD) demonstrate GRAD-Former's superior performance compared to existing approaches. Notably, GRAD-Former outperforms the current state-of-the-art models across all the metrics and all the datasets while using fewer parameters. Our framework establishes a new benchmark for remote sensing change detection performance. Our code will be released at: https://github.com/Ujjwal238/GRAD-Former

</details>


### [180] [GroundedSurg: A Multi-Procedure Benchmark for Language-Conditioned Surgical Tool Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01108)
*Tajamul Ashraf,Abrar Ul Riyaz,Wasif Tak,Tavaheed Tariq,Sonia Yadav,Moloud Abdar,Janibul Bashir*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GroundedSurg introduces a language-conditioned, instance-level grounding benchmark for surgical scenes, pairing natural-language queries with precise spatial annotations to localize specific tools. Experiments reveal sizable gaps between current VLMs/segmentation models and clinically needed performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real clinical decisions hinge on identifying a specific instrument instance (by role, spatial relation, or anatomical interaction), not merely its category. Existing benchmarks focus on category-level segmentation and miss reference resolution to individual tools in cluttered, multi-instrument surgical views. A realistic evaluation is needed to advance context-aware intraoperative assistance.

Method: Construct a multi-domain dataset (ophthalmic, laparoscopic, robotic, open) where each data point couples a surgical image with a natural-language description targeting one instrument. Provide structured spatial grounding (bounding boxes plus point-level anchors). Evaluate models jointly on language reference resolution and localization, benchmarking modern segmentation and vision-language models.

Result: Across diverse scenes and instruments, state-of-the-art segmentation models and VLMs underperform on instance-level language grounding, evidencing substantial performance gaps in resolving text-to-instrument references and precise localization.

Conclusion: GroundedSurg enables systematic, clinically realistic evaluation of vision-language grounding in surgery and exposes the need for better clinically grounded vision-language reasoning. Public release of code/data should catalyze research toward safer, context-aware intraoperative AI (e.g., handoff guidance, collision avoidance, workflow-aware robotics).

Abstract: Clinically reliable perception of surgical scenes is essential for advancing intelligent, context-aware intraoperative assistance such as instrument handoff guidance, collision avoidance, and workflow-aware robotic support. Existing surgical tool benchmarks primarily evaluate category-level segmentation, requiring models to detect all instances of predefined instrument classes. However, real-world clinical decisions often require resolving references to a specific instrument instance based on its functional role, spatial relation, or anatomical interaction capabilities not captured by current evaluation paradigms. We introduce GroundedSurg, the first language-conditioned, instance-level surgical grounding benchmark. Each instance pairs a surgical image with a natural-language description targeting a single instrument, accompanied by structured spatial grounding annotations including bounding boxes and point-level anchors. The dataset spans ophthalmic, laparoscopic, robotic, and open procedures, encompassing diverse instrument types, imaging conditions, and operative complexities. By jointly evaluating linguistic reference resolution and pixel-level localization, GroundedSurg enables a systematic and realistic evaluation of vision-language models in clinically realistic multi-instrument scenes. Extensive experiments demonstrate substantial performance gaps across modern segmentation and VLMs, highlighting the urgent need for clinically grounded vision-language reasoning in surgical AI systems. Code and data are publicly available at https://github.com/gaash-lab/GroundedSurg

</details>


### [181] [TripleSumm: Adaptive Triple-Modality Fusion for Video Summarization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01169)
*Sumin Kim,Hyemin Jeong,Mingu Kang,Yejin Kim,Yoori Oh,Joonseok Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TripleSumm introduces adaptive, frame-level fusion of visual, text, and audio signals for video summarization and pairs it with MoSu, a new large-scale tri-modal benchmark, achieving state-of-the-art results across multiple datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video summarization methods rely on static or modality-agnostic fusion, ignoring that the saliency of visual, textual, and audio cues varies over time. Progress is also constrained by the absence of comprehensive tri-modal benchmarks.

Method: Propose TripleSumm, which dynamically weights and fuses visual, text, and audio modalities at each frame to capture time-varying saliency. Additionally, release MoSu (Most Replayed Multimodal Video Summarization), a large-scale dataset offering all three modalities for training and evaluation.

Result: TripleSumm outperforms prior methods by a significant margin on four benchmarks, including the new MoSu dataset. Code and data are publicly available at the provided repository.

Conclusion: Adaptive, frame-level multimodal fusion is effective for complex video summarization, and MoSu fills a key benchmarking gap, enabling and demonstrating state-of-the-art performance.

Abstract: The exponential growth of video content necessitates effective video summarization to efficiently extract key information from long videos. However, current approaches struggle to fully comprehend complex videos, primarily because they employ static or modality-agnostic fusion strategies. These methods fail to account for the dynamic, frame-dependent variations in modality saliency inherent in video data. To overcome these limitations, we propose TripleSumm, a novel architecture that adaptively weights and fuses the contributions of visual, text, and audio modalities at the frame level. Furthermore, a significant bottleneck for research into multimodal video summarization has been the lack of comprehensive benchmarks. Addressing this bottleneck, we introduce MoSu (Most Replayed Multimodal Video Summarization), the first large-scale benchmark that provides all three modalities. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TripleSumm achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming existing methods by a significant margin on four benchmarks, including MoSu. Our code and dataset are available at https://github.com/smkim37/TripleSumm.

</details>


### [182] [DeAR: Fine-Grained VLM Adaptation by Decomposing Attention Head Roles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01111)
*Yiming Ma,Hongkun Yang,Lionel Z. Wang,Bin Chen,Weizhi Xian,Jianzhi Teng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DeAR adapts vision-language models by decomposing deep-layer attention heads into functional roles (Attribute, Generalization, Mixed) via a Concept Entropy metric, then uses role-based attention masks, specialized tokens, and task-adaptive fusion to inject task knowledge while isolating generalization heads, achieving better adaptation–generalization trade-offs across 15 datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prompt tuning in VLMs often assumes a layer-level separation between general and task-specific knowledge, leading to uncontrolled interaction between learnable prompts and model tokens that can erode zero-shot generalization. The authors aim to preserve core generalization while enabling strong task adaptation by moving from a coarse layer-centric to a fine-grained head-centric view of specialization.

Method: 1) Hypothesize that functional specialization emerges at the attention-head level in deeper layers. 2) Propose Concept Entropy to quantify and classify head roles into Attribute, Generalization, and Mixed. 3) Introduce role-guided adaptation: specialized attribute tokens and a Role-Based Attention Mask to prevent task signals from contaminating generalization heads. 4) Add a Task-Adaptive Fusion strategy at inference to combine signals from different head roles.

Result: Across 15 datasets and various tasks, DeAR reportedly outperforms prior prompt-learning baselines while maintaining stronger zero-shot generalization, indicating an improved balance between adaptation and generalization. No specific metrics are provided in the abstract.

Conclusion: Fine-grained, head-level control of information flow enables targeted task adaptation without sacrificing the model’s general priors. DeAR suggests that decomposing attention-head roles and constraining interactions is an effective strategy for VLM adaptation, offering a better trade-off than layer-centric prompt learning.

Abstract: Prompt learning is a dominant paradigm for adapting pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to downstream tasks. However, existing methods often rely on a simplistic, layer-centric view, assuming shallow layers capture general features while deep layers handle task-specific knowledge. This assumption results in uncontrolled interactions between learnable tokens and original tokens. Task-specific knowledge could degrades the model's core generalization and creates a trade-off between task adaptation and the preservation of zero-shot generalization. To address this, we challenge the layer-centric view and propose \textbf{DeAR}, a framework that achieves fine-grained VLM adaptation by \textbf{De}composing \textbf{A}ttention head \textbf{R}oles. We posit that the functional specialization within VLMs occurs not between layers, but at the finer-grained level of individual attention heads in the deeper layers. Based on this insight, we introduce a novel metric, Concept Entropy, to systematically classify attention heads into distinct functional roles: \textit{Attribute}, \textit{Generalization}, and \textit{Mixed}. Guided by these roles, we introduce specialized attribute tokens and a Role-Based Attention Mask mechanism to precisely control information flow, ensuring generalization heads remain isolated from task-specific knowledge. We further incorporate a Task-Adaptive Fusion Strategy for inference. Extensive experiments on fifteen datasets show that DeAR achieves a strong balance between task adaptation and generalization, outperforming previous methods across various tasks.

</details>


### [183] [GuiDINO: Rethinking Vision Foundation Model in Medical Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01115)
*Zhuonan Liang,Wei Guo,Jie Gan,Yaxuan Song,Runnan Chen,Hang Chang,Weidong Cai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GuiDINO uses DINOv3 features to generate a spatial guide mask that gates activations in standard medical segmentation backbones, improving segmentation and boundary accuracy without full fine-tuning; it supports LoRA for parameter-efficient adaptation and consistently boosts performance across datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Foundation vision models trained on natural images often misalign with medical segmentation tasks due to domain shift. Fully fine-tuning them is costly and may discard inductive biases of specialized medical architectures. A method is needed to leverage foundation priors efficiently while retaining domain-specific model strengths.

Method: Treat the foundation model (DINOv3) as a guidance generator rather than a full encoder. Extract visual tokens, compute token–prototype similarities via a lightweight TokenBook to form a spatial guide mask, and use this mask to gate activations in various segmentation backbones. Train with a guide supervision loss aligning the mask to ground-truth regions; optionally add a boundary-focused hinge loss to sharpen structures. Allow parameter-efficient adaptation via LoRA on the DINOv3 guidance path. Use nnUNet-style inference for fair comparison.

Result: Across multiple medical datasets, the approach consistently improves overall segmentation quality and boundary robustness compared to baselines, indicating the guidance mechanism effectively injects foundation priors. Code is publicly available for reproducibility.

Conclusion: Positioning foundation models as guidance generators offers a practical, parameter-efficient alternative to full fine-tuning, preserving the inductive biases and efficiency of established medical segmentation backbones while improving accuracy and boundaries. This reframes how foundation models can serve medical vision tasks.

Abstract: Foundation vision models are increasingly adopted in medical image analysis. Due to domain shift, these pretrained models misalign with medical image segmentation needs without being fully fine-tuned or lightly adapted. We introduce GuiDINO, a framework that repositions native foundation model to acting as a visual guidance generator for downstream segmentation. GuiDINO extracts visual feature representation from DINOv3 and converts them into a spatial guide mask via a lightweight TokenBook mechanism, which aggregates token-prototype similarities. This guide mask gates feature activations in multiple segmentation backbones, thereby injecting foundation-model priors while preserving the inductive biases and efficiency of medical dedicated architectures. Training relies on a guide supervision objective loss that aligns the guide mask to ground-truth regions, optionally augmented by a boundary-focused hinge loss to sharpen fine structures. GuiDINO also supports parameter-efficient adaptation through LoRA on the DINOv3 guide backbone. Across diverse medical datasets and nnUNet-style inference, GuiDINO consistently improves segmentation quality and boundary robustness, suggesting a practical alternative to fine-tuning and offering a new perspective on how foundation models can best serve medical vision. Code is available at https://github.com/Hi-FishU/GuiDINO

</details>


### [184] [VisNec: Measuring and Leveraging Visual Necessity for Multimodal Instruction Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01195)
*Mingkang Dong,Hongyi Cai,Jie Li,Sifan Zhou,Bin Ren,Kunyu Peng,Yuqian Fu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VisNec scores how much an image actually helps in instruction tuning, then keeps only vision‑critical, diverse samples—matching or surpassing full‑data performance with a fraction of the data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many multimodal instruction datasets contain text‑solvable (visually redundant) items and misaligned supervision that wastes compute and can harm learning. The field needs a principled way to quantify when visual input is truly necessary for a training instance.

Method: Compute a Visual Necessity Score by comparing predictive loss with versus without the image for each training sample. Use this to label samples as vision‑critical, redundant, or misaligned. To avoid losing coverage, apply semantic clustering and select high‑necessity examples within each cluster to preserve task diversity.

Result: Using only 15% of LLaVA‑665K chosen by VisNec attains 100.2% of the full‑data performance across 10 benchmarks. On Vision‑Flan‑186K, VisNec’s subset is even smaller yet exceeds full‑data training by 15.8%.

Conclusion: Quantifying and leveraging visual necessity yields more efficient and robust multimodal instruction tuning: less data, equal or better performance. Code and curated subsets will be released upon acceptance.

Abstract: The effectiveness of multimodal instruction tuning depends not only on dataset scale, but critically on whether training samples genuinely require visual reasoning. However, existing instruction datasets often contain a substantial portion of visually redundant samples (solvable from text alone), as well as multimodally misaligned supervision that can degrade learning. To address this, we propose VisNec (Visual Necessity Score), a principled data selection framework that measures the marginal contribution of visual input during instruction tuning. By comparing predictive loss with and without visual context, VisNec identifies whether a training instance is vision-critical, redundant, or misaligned. To preserve task diversity, we combine VisNec with semantic clustering and select high-necessity samples within each cluster. Across 10 downstream benchmarks, training on only 15% of the LLaVA-665K dataset selected by VisNec achieves 100.2% of full-data performance. On the smaller Vision-Flan-186K dataset, our selection not only further reduces data size but also surpasses full-data training by 15.8%. These results demonstrate that measuring and leveraging visual necessity provides an effective solution for both efficient and robust multimodal instruction tuning. Codes and selected subsets will be released upon acceptance.

</details>


### [185] [Improved MambdaBDA Framework for Robust Building Damage Assessment Across Disaster Domains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01116)
*Alp Eren Gençoğlu,Hazım Kemal Ekenel*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They strengthen a state-of-the-art satellite-based building damage assessment model (MambaBDA) with focal loss, attention gates, and a feature alignment module, delivering consistent accuracy gains in-domain and large improvements on unseen disasters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-disaster damage mapping from satellites suffers from class imbalance (few damaged vs many undamaged buildings), background clutter that confuses models, and domain shift across different disasters and regions; improving robustness and generalization is critical for reliable, rapid response.

Method: Augment ChangeMamba’s MambaBDA with three modular components: (i) Focal Loss to address class imbalance in damage classification; (ii) lightweight Attention Gates to filter irrelevant context; (iii) a compact Alignment Module that spatially warps pre-event features toward post-event imagery before decoding. Evaluate across multiple datasets (xBD, Pakistan Flooding, Turkey Earthquake, Ida Hurricane) with both in-domain and cross-dataset tests.

Result: Consistent improvements over the baseline: +0.8% to +5% in-domain and up to +27% on unseen disaster datasets, indicating notable robustness gains under domain shift.

Conclusion: Simple, modular add-ons to MambaBDA effectively mitigate imbalance, suppress clutter, and align temporal features, substantially enhancing generalization to new disasters and making BDA more reliable across geographies and event types.

Abstract: Reliable post-disaster building damage assessment (BDA) from satellite imagery is hindered by severe class imbalance, background clutter, and domain shift across disaster types and geographies. In this work, we address these problems and explore ways to improve the MambaBDA, the BDA network of ChangeMamba architecture, one of the most successful BDA models. The approach enhances the MambaBDA with three modular components: (i) Focal Loss to mitigate class imbalance damage classification, (ii) lightweight Attention Gates to suppress irrelevant context, and (iii) a compact Alignment Module to spatially warp pre-event features toward post-event content before decoding. We experiment on multiple satellite imagery datasets, including xBD, Pakistan Flooding, Turkey Earthquake, and Ida Hurricane, and conduct in-domain and crossdataset tests. The proposed modular enhancements yield consistent improvements over the baseline model, with 0.8% to 5% performance gains in-domain, and up to 27% on unseen disasters. This indicates that the proposed enhancements are especially beneficial for the generalization capability of the system.

</details>


### [186] [Monocular 3D Object Position Estimation with VLMs for Human-Robot Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01224)
*Ari Wahl,Dorian Gawlinski,David Przewozny,Paul Chojecki,Felix Bießmann,Sebastian Bosse*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They finetune a general-purpose vision-language model with QLoRA and a small regression head, plus conditional routing, to predict 3D object positions from a single wrist-mounted RGB image, language prompts, and robot states, achieving 13 mm median MAE, 5× better than a no-finetune baseline, while preserving general visual query abilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between 2D VLM competencies and the 3D localization needed for intuitive human-robot interaction. Existing VLMs rarely output 3D coordinates; enabling this would allow language-driven manipulation with monocular cameras.

Method: Curate >100k heterogeneous wrist-camera images paired with language inputs and robot states. Finetune a pre-trained VLM using QLoRA, attach a custom regression head for 3D coordinates, and use conditional routing to switch between standard VLM tasks and specialized 3D estimation.

Result: On a test set, the model achieves a median absolute error of 13 mm, a five-fold improvement over a simpler non-finetuned baseline. About 25% of predictions fall within a tolerance considered sufficient for robotic interaction.

Conclusion: VLMs can be adapted to return 3D positions from monocular inputs while keeping general visual understanding via conditional routing. Although accuracy suffices for interaction in only a quarter of cases, the approach is promising and invites further improvements in data diversity, calibration, temporal cues, and uncertainty handling.

Abstract: Pre-trained general-purpose Vision-Language Models (VLM) hold the potential to enhance intuitive human-machine interactions due to their rich world knowledge and 2D object detection capabilities. However, VLMs for 3D coordinates detection tasks are rare. In this work, we investigate interactive abilities of VLMs by returning 3D object positions given a monocular RGB image from a wrist-mounted camera, natural language input, and robot states. We collected and curated a heterogeneous dataset of more than 100,000 images and finetuned a VLM using QLoRA with a custom regression head. By implementing conditional routing, our model maintains its ability to process general visual queries while adding specialized 3D position estimation capabilities. Our results demonstrate robust predictive performance with a median MAE of 13 mm on the test set and a five-fold improvement over a simpler baseline without finetuning. In about 25% of the cases, predictions are within a range considered acceptable for the robot to interact with objects.

</details>


### [187] [The MAMA-MIA Challenge: Advancing Generalizability and Fairness in Breast MRI Tumor Segmentation and Treatment Response Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01250)
*Lidia Garrucho,Smriti Joshi,Kaisar Kushibar,Richard Osuala,Maciej Bobowicz,Xavier Bargalló,Paulius Jaruševičius,Kai Geissler,Raphael Schäfer,Muhammad Alberb,Tony Xu,Anne Martel,Daniel Sleiman,Navchetan Awasthi,Hadeel Awwad,Joan C. Vilanova,Robert Martí,Daan Schouten,Jeong Hoon Lee,Mirabela Rusu,Eleonora Poeta,Luisa Vargas,Eliana Pastor,Maria A. Zuluaga,Jessica Kächele,Dimitrios Bounias,Alexandra Ertl,Katarzyna Gwoździewicz,Maria-Laura Cosaka,Pasant M. Abo-Elhoda,Sara W. Tantawy,Shorouq S. Sakrana,Norhan O. Shawky-Abdelfatah,Amr Muhammad Abdo-Salem,Androniki Kozana,Eugen Divjak,Gordana Ivanac,Katerina Nikiforaki,Michail E. Klontzas,Rosa García-Dosdá,Meltem Gulsun-Akpinar,Oğuz Lafcı,Carlos Martín-Isla,Oliver Díaz,Laura Igual,Karim Lekadir*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MAMA-MIA is a large, multi-institutional benchmark for breast DCE-MRI that jointly evaluates tumor segmentation and pre-treatment pCR prediction, revealing significant generalization gaps and accuracy–fairness trade-offs on an external, cross-continental test set, while providing standardized datasets and evaluation resources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most breast MRI AI models are trained on single-center data and judged by aggregate metrics that obscure generalization and subgroup disparities. There is a need for a standardized benchmark that evaluates both clinical utility and fairness across demographics.

Method: A challenge was designed with 1,506 patients from multiple U.S. institutions for training and 574 patients from three independent European centers for external testing. Tasks: primary tumor segmentation and pCR prediction from pre-treatment DCE-MRI. A unified scoring framework combined predictive accuracy with subgroup consistency across age, menopausal status, and breast density. Twenty-six international teams participated.

Result: External testing showed substantial performance variability across submissions, with clear trade-offs between achieving high overall accuracy and maintaining fairness across demographic subgroups. The setup quantified cross-continental and cross-institutional generalization performance.

Conclusion: Developing robust and equitable breast MRI AI requires evaluation beyond aggregate metrics. The MAMA-MIA Challenge offers standardized datasets, protocols, and public resources to catalyze methods that generalize well and maintain subgroup consistency.

Abstract: Breast cancer is the most frequently diagnosed malignancy among women worldwide and a leading cause of cancer-related mortality. Dynamic contrast-enhanced magnetic resonance imaging plays a central role in tumor characterization and treatment monitoring, particularly in patients receiving neoadjuvant chemotherapy. However, existing artificial intelligence models for breast magnetic resonance imaging are often developed using single-center data and evaluated using aggregate performance metrics, limiting their generalizability and obscuring potential performance disparities across demographic subgroups. The MAMA-MIA Challenge was designed to address these limitations by introducing a large-scale benchmark that jointly evaluates primary tumor segmentation and prediction of pathologic complete response using pre-treatment magnetic resonance imaging only. The training cohort comprised 1,506 patients from multiple institutions in the United States, while evaluation was conducted on an external test set of 574 patients from three independent European centers to assess cross-continental and cross-institutional generalization. A unified scoring framework combined predictive performance with subgroup consistency across age, menopausal status, and breast density. Twenty-six international teams participated in the final evaluation phase. Results demonstrate substantial performance variability under external testing and reveal trade-offs between overall accuracy and subgroup fairness. The challenge provides standardized datasets, evaluation protocols, and public resources to promote the development of robust and equitable artificial intelligence systems for breast cancer imaging.

</details>


### [188] [Teacher-Guided Causal Interventions for Image Denoising: Orthogonal Content-Noise Disentanglement in Vision Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01140)
*Kuai Jiang,Zhaoyan Ding,Guijuan Zhang,Dianjie Lu,Zhuoran Zheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They propose a ViT-based denoiser (TCD-Net) that uses “causal” interventions to disentangle content from noise via de-biasing, orthogonal dual branches, and a teacher-guided prior from a powerful image generator, claiming SOTA fidelity and real-time 104.2 FPS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional denoisers learn spurious correlations between environment/context and noise, and confuse high-frequency textures with stochastic noise, causing over-smoothing or leftover artifacts and poor robustness under distribution shift.

Method: Within a Vision Transformer, they apply: (1) Environmental Bias Adjustment projecting features into a stable, de-centered subspace to suppress global environmental confounding; (2) a dual-branch head for separate content/noise representations with an orthogonality constraint to prevent leakage; (3) a teacher-guided causal prior using an external generative model to pull content features toward the natural-image manifold via structured feature-space interventions.

Result: They report superior performance to mainstream methods across several benchmarks in both quality and speed, and claim real-time throughput of 104.2 FPS on a single RTX 5090 GPU.

Conclusion: Explicit causal-style disentanglement and a teacher prior improve robustness and efficiency in image denoising, better preserving textures while removing noise and generalizing across shifts.

Abstract: Conventional image denoising models often inadvertently learn spurious correlations between environmental factors and noise patterns. Moreover, due to high-frequency ambiguity, they struggle to reliably distinguish subtle textures from stochastic noise, resulting in over-removed details or residual noise artifacts. We therefore revisit denoising via causal intervention, arguing that purely correlational fitting entangles intrinsic content with extrinsic noise, which directly degrades robustness under distribution shifts. Motivated by this, we propose the Teacher-Guided Causal Disentanglement Network (TCD-Net), which explicitly decomposes the generative mechanism via structured interventions on feature spaces within a Vision Transformer framework. Specifically, our method integrates three key components: (1) An Environmental Bias Adjustment (EBA) module projects features into a stable, de-centered subspace to suppress global environmental bias (de-confounding). (2) A dual-branch disentanglement head employs an orthogonality constraint to force a strict separation between content and noise representations, preventing information leakage. (3) To resolve structural ambiguity, we leverage Nano Banana Pro, Google's reasoning-guided AI image generation model, to guide a causal prior, effectively pulling content representations back onto the natural-image manifold. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TCD-Net outperforms mainstream methods across multiple benchmarks in both fidelity and efficiency, achieving a real-time speed of 104.2 FPS on a single RTX 5090 GPU.

</details>


### [189] [ArtLLM: Generating Articulated Assets via 3D LLM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01142)
*Penghao Wang,Siyuan Xie,Hongyu Yan,Xianghui Yang,Jingwei Huang,Chunchao Guo,Jiayuan Gu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ArtLLM is a 3D multimodal LLM that, from a single object mesh/point cloud, autoregressively infers parts and joints (kinematic layout) and then synthesizes high-fidelity part geometries, outperforming prior methods and enabling digital twins for robot learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods either fit joints via slow per-object optimization and handle only simple kinematics, or assemble parts from limited libraries that harm diversity and generalization. A scalable approach is needed to directly recover an object’s articulated structure and geometry from complete 3D data.

Method: Curate a large articulation dataset from prior sources and procedurally generated objects; train a 3D multimodal large language model to autoregressively predict a variable number of parts and joints from point clouds, yielding a unified kinematic layout. Condition a downstream 3D generative model on this layout to synthesize high-quality part geometries.

Result: On PartNet-Mobility, ArtLLM significantly improves part layout accuracy and joint prediction over SOTA and generalizes robustly to real-world objects.

Conclusion: By jointly inferring kinematic structure and generating detailed parts directly from meshes, ArtLLM produces high-quality articulated assets and facilitates digital-twin creation, promising scalable benefits for robot learning and simulation.

Abstract: Creating interactive digital environments for gaming, robotics, and simulation relies on articulated 3D objects whose functionality emerges from their part geometry and kinematic structure. However, existing approaches remain fundamentally limited: optimization-based reconstruction methods require slow, per-object joint fitting and typically handle only simple, single-joint objects, while retrieval-based methods assemble parts from a fixed library, leading to repetitive geometry and poor generalization. To address these challenges, we introduce ArtLLM, a novel framework for generating high-quality articulated assets directly from complete 3D meshes. At its core is a 3D multimodal large language model trained on a large-scale articulation dataset curated from both existing articulation datasets and procedurally generated objects. Unlike prior work, ArtLLM autoregressively predicts a variable number of parts and joints, inferring their kinematic structure in a unified manner from the object's point cloud. This articulation-aware layout then conditions a 3D generative model to synthesize high-fidelity part geometries. Experiments on the PartNet-Mobility dataset show that ArtLLM significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both part layout accuracy and joint prediction, while generalizing robustly to real-world objects. Finally, we demonstrate its utility in constructing digital twins, highlighting its potential for scalable robot learning.

</details>


### [190] [ConVibNet: Needle Detection during Continuous Insertion via Frequency-Inspired Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01147)
*Jiamei Guo,Zhehao Duan,Maria Neiiendam,Dianye Huang,Nassir Navab,Zhongliang Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ConVibNet is a real-time, temporally aware model for continuous ultrasound needle detection that improves tip and angle estimation over strong baselines while preserving real-time performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Needle placement under ultrasound often fails due to poor, intermittent needle visibility caused by artifacts, occlusions, and low contrast; clinicians require accurate, continuous, real-time tracking of the tip and shaft during insertion.

Method: Extend VibNet with temporal modeling across consecutive frames to jointly estimate tip location and shaft angle; introduce an intersection-and-difference loss to exploit motion correlations between frames; curate a dedicated dataset; benchmark against VibNet and UNet-LSTM.

Result: On the curated dataset, ConVibNet outperformed baselines with a tip error of 2.80±2.42 mm and an angle error of 1.69±2.00°, achieving a 0.75 mm better tip localization than the best baseline and maintaining real-time inference.

Conclusion: Temporal correlation modeling plus the novel intersection-and-difference loss improves robustness and accuracy for real-time ultrasound-guided needle tracking, indicating promise for integration into autonomous insertion systems.

Abstract: Purpose: Ultrasound-guided needle interventions are widely used in clinical practice, but their success critically depends on accurate needle placement, which is frequently hindered by the poor and intermittent visibility of needles in ultrasound images. Existing approaches remain limited by artifacts, occlusions, and low contrast, and often fail to support real-time continuous insertion. To overcome these challenges, this study introduces a robust real-time framework for continuous needle detection.
  Methods: We present ConVibNet, an extension of VibNet for detecting needles with significantly reduced visibility, addressing real-time, continuous needle tracking during insertion. ConVibNet leverages temporal dependencies across successive ultrasound frames to enable continuous estimation of both needle tip position and shaft angle in dynamic scenarios. To strengthen temporal awareness of needle-tip motion, we introduce a novel intersection-and-difference loss that explicitly leverages motion correlations across consecutive frames. In addition, we curated a dedicated dataset for model development and evaluation.
  Results: The performance of the proposed ConVibNet model was evaluated on our dataset, demonstrating superior accuracy compared to the baseline VibNet and UNet-LSTM models. Specifically, ConVibNet achieved a tip error of 2.80+-2.42 mm and an angle error of 1.69+-2.00 deg. These results represent a 0.75 mm improvement in tip localization accuracy over the best-performing baseline, while preserving real-time inference capability.
  Conclusion: ConVibNet advances real-time needle detection in ultrasound-guided interventions by integrating temporal correlation modeling with a novel intersection-and-difference loss, thereby improving accuracy and robustness and demonstrating high potential for integration into autonomous insertion systems.

</details>


### [191] [Multi-Level Bidirectional Decoder Interaction for Uncertainty-Aware Breast Ultrasound Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01295)
*Abdullah Al Shafi,Md Kawsar Mahmud Khan Zunayed,Safin Ahmmed,Sk Imran Hossain,Engelbert Mephu Nguifo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A multi-task breast ultrasound model introduces multi-level decoder interactions and uncertainty-aware attention to jointly segment lesions and classify tissue, adapting per-sample and per-scale; it achieves 74.5% IoU (segmentation) and 90.6% accuracy (classification) on BUSI with ablations validating the design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional multi-task learning for ultrasound suffers from task interference and inflexible, one-size-fits-all coordination (often limited to encoder sharing or single-level interaction), which fails to adapt to instance-specific difficulty and scale variability of lesions.

Method: A unified segmentation–classification framework with: (1) Task Interaction Modules at every decoder level enabling bidirectional communication during spatial reconstruction via attention-weighted pooling and multiplicative modulation; (2) Uncertainty-Proxy Attention that uses feature activation variance to adaptively weight base vs. enhanced features per level and per sample; (3) multi-scale context fusion to capture morphology across lesion sizes. This departs from encoder-only or single-level designs by leveraging scale-specific synergies across semantic-to-spatial stages.

Result: On public breast ultrasound datasets, the method is competitive; on BUSI it attains 74.5% lesion IoU and 90.6% classification accuracy. Ablations show multi-level decoder interaction significantly improves both tasks over encoder-only sharing and single-level interaction baselines.

Conclusion: Decoder-level, bidirectional, uncertainty-aware coordination mitigates task interference and better balances segmentation and classification across scales and instances, yielding state-of-the-art-competitive results. The design is practical (code released) and suggests decoder-centric interaction is more effective than traditional encoder-sharing in ultrasound multi-task learning.

Abstract: Breast ultrasound interpretation requires simultaneous lesion segmentation and tissue classification. However, conventional multi-task learning approaches suffer from task interference and rigid coordination strategies that fail to adapt to instance-specific prediction difficulty. We propose a multi-task framework addressing these limitations through multi-level decoder interaction and uncertainty-aware adaptive coordination. Task Interaction Modules operate at all decoder levels, establishing bidirectional segmentation-classification communication during spatial reconstruction through attention weighted pooling and multiplicative modulation. Unlike prior single-level or encoder-only approaches, this multi-level design captures scale specific task synergies across semantic-to-spatial scales, producing complementary task interaction streams. Uncertainty-Proxy Attention adaptively weights base versus enhanced features at each level using feature activation variance, enabling per-level and per-sample task balancing without heuristic tuning. To support instance-adaptive prediction, multi-scale context fusion captures morphological cues across varying lesion sizes. Evaluation on multiple publicly available breast ultrasound datasets demonstrates competitive performance, including 74.5% lesion IoU and 90.6% classification accuracy on BUSI dataset. Ablation studies confirm that multi-level task interaction provides significant performance gains, validating that decoder-level bidirectional communication is more effective than conventional encoder-only parameter sharing. The code is available at: https://github.com/C-loud-Nine/Uncertainty-Aware-Multi-Level-Decoder-Interaction.

</details>


### [192] [AG-VAS: Anchor-Guided Zero-Shot Visual Anomaly Segmentation with Large Multimodal Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01305)
*Zhen Qu,Xian Tao,Xiaoyi Bao,Dingrong Wang,ShiChen Qu,Zhengtao Zhang,Xingang Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AG-VAS equips LMMs with learnable semantic anchors and improved cross-modal alignment to localize anomalies in a zero-shot manner, achieving SOTA on multiple industrial and medical benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Zero-shot visual anomaly segmentation with LMMs is hindered by the abstract, context-dependent nature of anomalies (no stable visual prototypes) and weak alignment between high-level language semantics and pixel-level visual features, leading to poor localization.

Method: Introduce AG-VAS with three learnable anchor tokens: [SEG] (absolute anchor to ground abstract anomaly semantics into concrete, spatial visual entities) and [NOR]/[ANO] (relative anchors modeling normal vs. abnormal contrast). Propose SPAM to align language-level semantic embeddings with high-resolution visual features. Design AGMD for anchor-conditioned mask decoding to produce precise anomaly masks. Curate Anomaly-Instruct20K with structured descriptions (appearance, shape, spatial attributes) to train and integrate anchors within an expanded LMM vocabulary.

Result: Across six industrial and medical benchmarks, AG-VAS delivers consistent state-of-the-art zero-shot anomaly segmentation, indicating improved localization accuracy and robustness over prior LMM-based methods.

Conclusion: Semantic anchors plus explicit semantic–pixel alignment and an instruction dataset effectively bridge the gap between abstract anomaly concepts and pixel-level segmentation, providing a generalizable, zero-shot framework for anomaly localization across diverse domains.

Abstract: Large multimodal models (LMMs) exhibit strong task generalization capabilities, offering new opportunities for zero-shot visual anomaly segmentation (ZSAS). However, existing LMM-based segmentation approaches still face fundamental limitations: anomaly concepts are inherently abstract and context-dependent, lacking stable visual prototypes, and the weak alignment between high-level semantic embeddings and pixel-level spatial features hinders precise anomaly localization. To address these challenges, we present AG-VAS (Anchor-Guided Visual Anomaly Segmentation), a new framework that expands the LMM vocabulary with three learnable semantic anchor tokens-[SEG], [NOR], and [ANO], establishing a unified anchor-guided segmentation paradigm. Specifically, [SEG] serves as an absolute semantic anchor that translates abstract anomaly semantics into explicit, spatially grounded visual entities (e.g., holes or scratches), while [NOR] and [ANO] act as relative anchors that model the contextual contrast between normal and abnormal patterns across categories. To further enhance cross-modal alignment, we introduce a Semantic-Pixel Alignment Module (SPAM) that aligns language-level semantic embeddings with high-resolution visual features, along with an Anchor-Guided Mask Decoder (AGMD) that performs anchor-conditioned mask prediction for precise anomaly localization. In addition, we curate Anomaly-Instruct20K, a large-scale instruction dataset that organizes anomaly knowledge into structured descriptions of appearance, shape, and spatial attributes, facilitating effective learning and integration of the proposed semantic anchors. Extensive experiments on six industrial and medical benchmarks demonstrate that AG-VAS achieves consistent state-of-the-art performance in the zero-shot setting.

</details>


### [193] [BeautyGRPO: Aesthetic Alignment for Face Retouching via Dynamic Path Guidance and Fine-Grained Preference Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01163)
*Jiachen Yang,Xianhui Lin,Yi Dong,Zebiao Zheng,Xing Liu,Hong Gu,Yanmei Fang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BeautyGRPO is a preference-aligned face retouching framework that trains on a new fine-grained human preference dataset and uses a Dynamic Path Guidance sampler to curb stochastic drift, yielding higher-fidelity, more aesthetically preferred edits than prior retouching and general editing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Face retouching must simultaneously remove imperfections and preserve identity while matching subjective human aesthetics. Supervised methods overfit to pixel-level labels and miss nuanced preferences; standard online RL captures preferences but its stochastic exploration introduces noise and fidelity loss. A method is needed that aligns with human preferences without sacrificing high-fidelity details.

Method: (1) Build FRPref-10K, a fine-grained human preference dataset across five retouching dimensions. (2) Train a reward model to evaluate subtle perceptual differences. (3) Optimize a retouching policy/model via RL guided by this reward. (4) Introduce Dynamic Path Guidance (DPG): at each sampling timestep, compute an anchor-based ODE path and replan a guided trajectory to stabilize the stochastic sampling process, correcting drift while allowing controlled exploration.

Result: Across extensive experiments, BeautyGRPO outperforms specialized face retouching systems and general image editing models, delivering superior texture quality, more accurate blemish removal, and results that align better with human aesthetic preferences.

Conclusion: By combining a fine-grained preference reward with DPG-stabilized RL, BeautyGRPO reconciles preference alignment and fidelity in face retouching. The approach demonstrates that carefully guided exploration can enable RL to handle delicate, high-fidelity image editing tasks.

Abstract: Face retouching requires removing subtle imperfections while preserving unique facial identity features, in order to enhance overall aesthetic appeal. However, existing methods suffer from a fundamental trade-off. Supervised learning on labeled data is constrained to pixel-level label mimicry, failing to capture complex subjective human aesthetic preferences. Conversely, while online reinforcement learning (RL) excels at preference alignment, its stochastic exploration paradigm conflicts with the high-fidelity demands of face retouching and often introduces noticeable noise artifacts due to accumulated stochastic drift. To address these limitations, we propose BeautyGRPO, a reinforcement learning framework that aligns face retouching with human aesthetic preferences. We construct FRPref-10K, a fine-grained preference dataset covering five key retouching dimensions, and train a specialized reward model capable of evaluating subtle perceptual differences. To reconcile exploration and fidelity, we introduce Dynamic Path Guidance (DPG). DPG stabilizes the stochastic sampling trajectory by dynamically computing an anchor-based ODE path and replanning a guided trajectory at each sampling timestep, effectively correcting stochastic drift while maintaining controlled exploration. Extensive experiments show that BeautyGRPO outperforms both specialized face retouching methods and general image editing models, achieving superior texture quality, more accurate blemish removal, and overall results that better align with human aesthetic preferences.

</details>


### [194] [You Only Need One Stage: Novel-View Synthesis From A Single Blind Face Image](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01328)
*Taoyue Wang,Xiang Zhang,Xiaotian Li,Huiyuan Yang,Lijun Yin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: NVB-Face is a one-stage diffusion-based framework that generates high-quality, consistent novel facial views directly from a single degraded (blind) face image by learning 3D-aware multi-view latents without an explicit restoration step.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Two-stage pipelines (restore then synthesize views) depend heavily on the restoration quality, often propagating errors and causing view inconsistencies. A direct method that handles degraded inputs while maintaining 3D consistency is needed.

Method: Extract single-view features from the blind face; pass them through a feature manipulator to obtain 3D-aware, multi-view latent representations; use a diffusion model to synthesize novel views directly from these latents, ensuring cross-view consistency and high fidelity.

Result: Empirically outperforms conventional two-stage approaches on both consistency across views and image fidelity, producing higher-quality and more reliable novel-view face images from degraded inputs.

Conclusion: A single-stage, 3D-aware diffusion framework can bypass fragile restoration-first pipelines, delivering more consistent and faithful novel-view synthesis from blind face images.

Abstract: We propose a novel one-stage method, NVB-Face, for generating consistent Novel-View images directly from a single Blind Face image. Existing approaches to novel-view synthesis for objects or faces typically require a high-resolution RGB image as input. When dealing with degraded images, the conventional pipeline follows a two-stage process: first restoring the image to high resolution, then synthesizing novel views from the restored result. However, this approach is highly dependent on the quality of the restored image, often leading to inaccuracies and inconsistencies in the final output. To address this limitation, we extract single-view features directly from the blind face image and introduce a feature manipulator that transforms these features into 3D-aware, multi-view latent representations. Leveraging the powerful generative capacity of a diffusion model, our framework synthesizes high-quality, consistent novel-view face images. Experimental results show that our method significantly outperforms traditional two-stage approaches in both consistency and fidelity.

</details>


### [195] [FREE-Edit: Using Editing-aware Injection in Rectified Flow Models for Zero-shot Image-Driven Video Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01164)
*Maomao Li,Yunfei Liu,Yu Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FREE-Edit introduces an editing-aware feature injection (REE) that modulates how much source-video information is injected during video reconstruction, avoiding conflicts with user edits while preserving motion/layout. Using rectified-Flow models, it achieves zero-shot image-driven video editing with higher-quality results than prior methods, without training or fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Attention injection in current image-to-video editing either overwhelms edits (over-injection) or loses source structure/motion (under-injection). The authors aim to balance edit fidelity with preservation of the original video’s motion and layout.

Method: 1) Compute a pixel-difference mask between the source first frame and its edited version to localize edits. 2) Propagate this mask across the video via optical flow warping to track edited regions. 3) Use the propagated mask to produce per-token injection intensities: suppress injection in edited areas while retaining it elsewhere. 4) Implement the approach within rectified-Flow (FREE-Edit) for zero-shot editing, requiring no additional training or fine-tuning.

Result: Across diverse image-driven video editing tasks, FREE-Edit yields qualitatively higher-quality outputs than existing methods, maintaining motion/layout while respecting edits, all in a zero-shot setting. A project page with demos is provided.

Conclusion: Editing-aware, per-token modulation of feature injection resolves the over/under-injection dilemma, enabling training-free, robust image-driven video editing. Rectified-Flow serves as an effective backbone for zero-shot video editing with improved consistency and edit fidelity.

Abstract: Image-driven video editing aims to propagate edit contents from the modified first frame to the rest frames. The existing methods usually invert the source video to noise using a pre-trained image-to-video (I2V) model and then guide the sampling process using the edited first frame. Generally, a popular choice for maintaining motion and layout from the source video is intervening in the denoising process by injecting attention during reconstruction. However, such injection often leads to unsatisfactory results, where excessive injection leads to conflicting semantics from the source video while insufficient injection brings limited source representation. Recognizing this, we propose an Editing-awaRE (REE) injection method to modulate injection intensity of each token. Specifically, we first compute the pixel difference between the source and edited first frame to form a corresponding editing mask. Next, we track the editing area throughout the entire video by using optical flow to warp the first-frame mask. Then, editing-aware feature injection intensity for each token is generated accordingly, where injection is not conducted on editing areas. Building upon REE injection, we further propose a zero-shot image-driven video editing framework with recent-emerging rectified-Flow models, dubbed FREE-Edit. Without fine-tuning or training, our FREE-Edit demonstrates effectiveness in various image-driven video editing scenarios, showing its capability to produce higher-quality outputs compared with existing techniques. Project page: https://free-edit.github.io/page/.

</details>


### [196] [VP-Hype: A Hybrid Mamba-Transformer Framework with Visual-Textual Prompting for Hyperspectral Image Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01174)
*Abdellah Zakaria Sellam,Fadi Abdeladhim Zidi,Salah Eddine Bekhouche,Ihssen Houhou,Marouane Tliba,Cosimo Distante,Abdenour Hadid*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VP-Hype is a hybrid Mamba–Transformer model with a 3D-CNN front-end and dual visual/text prompts that achieves state-of-the-art hyperspectral image classification in low-label settings while cutting Transformer complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HSI classification faces two linked challenges: extremely high spectral dimensionality and very limited labeled data. While hierarchical Transformers help, their quadratic attention makes scaling costly. The authors seek a model that retains long-range dependency modeling and strong accuracy but with linear-time efficiency and better sample efficiency.

Method: The framework begins with a robust 3D-CNN to extract spectral–spatial features. It replaces standard attention blocks with a Hybrid Mamba–Transformer backbone, leveraging state-space models (SSMs) to capture long-range dependencies in linear time while preserving relational modeling capacity. To combat label scarcity, it injects dual-modal prompts—visual and textual—to provide context-aware guidance during feature extraction and fine-tuning. The design builds on ideas from local/windowed attention and parameter-efficient adaptation but shifts to an SSM–Transformer hybrid to reduce computation.

Result: In low-data regimes with only 2% labeled samples, VP-Hype attains new SOTA: 99.69% OA on Salinas and 99.45% on Longkou, alongside reduced computational overhead versus standard Transformers.
