Conclusion: Combining linear-time SSMs with Transformer-style relations and multi-modal prompting yields a sample-efficient, scalable approach for HSI classification. The results indicate a promising direction for high performance under tight label and compute budgets.

Abstract: Accurate classification of hyperspectral imagery (HSI) is often frustrated by the tension between high-dimensional spectral data and the extreme scarcity of labeled training samples. While hierarchical models like LoLA-SpecViT have demonstrated the power of local windowed attention and parameter-efficient fine-tuning, the quadratic complexity of standard Transformers remains a barrier to scaling. We introduce VP-Hype, a framework that rethinks HSI classification by unifying the linear-time efficiency of State-Space Models (SSMs) with the relational modeling of Transformers in a novel hybrid architecture. Building on a robust 3D-CNN spectral front-end, VP-Hype replaces conventional attention blocks with a Hybrid Mamba-Transformer backbone to capture long-range dependencies with significantly reduced computational overhead. Furthermore, we address the label-scarcity problem by integrating dual-modal Visual and Textual Prompts that provide context-aware guidance for the feature extraction process. Our experimental evaluation demonstrates that VP-Hype establishes a new state of the art in low-data regimes. Specifically, with a training sample distribution of only 2\%, the model achieves Overall Accuracy (OA) of 99.69\% on the Salinas dataset and 99.45\% on the Longkou dataset. These results suggest that the convergence of hybrid sequence modeling and multi-modal prompting provides a robust path forward for high-performance, sample-efficient remote sensing.

</details>


### [197] [MixerCSeg: An Efficient Mixer Architecture for Crack Segmentation via Decoupled Mamba Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01361)
*Zilong Zhao,Zhengming Ding,Pei Niu,Wenhao Sun,Feng Guo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MixerCSeg is a lightweight crack-segmentation encoder that mixes CNN-like local texture paths, Transformer-style global dependency paths, and Mamba-inspired sequential context within a unified TransMixer, plus edge- and scale-aware refinements, achieving state-of-the-art results with only 2.05 GFLOPs and 2.54M parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CNN-, Transformer-, and Mamba-based encoders each emphasize only a subset of the spatial/structural cues needed to capture fine, thin, and irregular crack patterns, leaving gaps in modeling complex crack geometries while maintaining efficiency.

Method: Design a mixer encoder (TransMixer) with dedicated pathways: CNN-like for locality, Transformer-style for global relationships, and Mamba-inspired flows for sequential/latent attention context. Add spatial block processing, a Direction-guided Edge Gated Convolution (DEGConv) to boost edge sensitivity under irregular geometry with minimal overhead, and a Spatial Refinement Multi-Level Fusion (SRF) module for multi-scale detail refinement—all within a lightweight design.

Result: Across multiple crack-segmentation benchmarks, MixerCSeg delivers state-of-the-art performance with 2.05 GFLOPs and 2.54M parameters, indicating strong representation quality at low computational cost.

Conclusion: Coordinating complementary pathways for locality, global awareness, and sequential context—augmented by edge- and scale-aware refinements—yields an efficient, accurate crack segmentation encoder; public code supports reproducibility.

Abstract: Feature encoders play a key role in pixel-level crack segmentation by shaping the representation of fine textures and thin structures. Existing CNN-, Transformer-, and Mamba-based models each capture only part of the required spatial or structural information, leaving clear gaps in modeling complex crack patterns. To address this, we present MixerCSeg, a mixer architecture designed like a coordinated team of specialists, where CNN-like pathways focus on local textures, Transformer-style paths capture global dependencies, and Mamba-inspired flows model sequential context within a single encoder. At the core of MixerCSeg is the TransMixer, which explores Mamba's latent attention behavior while establishing dedicated pathways that naturally express both locality and global awareness. To further enhance structural fidelity, we introduce a spatial block processing strategy and a Direction-guided Edge Gated Convolution (DEGConv) that strengthens edge sensitivity under irregular crack geometries with minimal computational overhead. A Spatial Refinement Multi-Level Fusion (SRF) module is then employed to refine multi-scale details without increasing complexity. Extensive experiments on multiple crack segmentation benchmarks show that MixerCSeg achieves state-of-the-art performance with only 2.05 GFLOPs and 2.54 M parameters, demonstrating both efficiency and strong representational capability. The code is available at https://github.com/spiderforest/MixerCSeg.

</details>


### [198] [RnG: A Unified Transformer for Complete 3D Modeling from Partial Observations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01194)
*Mochu Xiang,Zhelun Shen,Xuesong Li,Jiahui Ren,Jing Zhang,Chen Zhao,Shanshan Liu,Haocheng Feng,Jingdong Wang,Yuchao Dai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RnG is a feed‑forward Transformer that unifies 3D reconstruction and novel view generation by using reconstruction‑guided causal attention and an attention KV‑cache as an implicit, complete 3D scene representation, enabling fast querying to render high‑fidelity RGBD views and achieving state‑of‑the‑art performance in real time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalizable 3D reconstruction from sparse 2D images often only models observed regions; unseen geometry remains unrepresented. The fundamental question is whether complete 3D structure can be inferred from partial 2D observations.

Method: Introduce a feed‑forward Transformer with reconstruction‑guided causal attention that explicitly separates reconstruction (from observed inputs) and generation (for unobserved content) at the attention level. Treat the attention KV‑cache as an implicit 3D representation that arbitrary camera poses can query to render novel RGBD views efficiently.

Result: Accurate recovery of visible geometry and plausibly coherent synthesis of unseen geometry and appearance; high‑fidelity novel‑view RGBD rendering; state‑of‑the‑art performance on both generalizable 3D reconstruction and novel view generation benchmarks; efficiency suitable for real‑time, interactive use.

Conclusion: By coupling reconstruction‑guided causal attention with a KV‑cache as an implicit 3D memory, RnG unifies reconstruction and generation, infers complete scene representations from sparse views, and delivers SOTA quality and speed for real‑time applications.

Abstract: Human perceive the 3D world through 2D observations from limited viewpoints. While recent feed-forward generalizable 3D reconstruction models excel at recovering 3D structures from sparse images, their representations are often confined to observed regions, leaving unseen geometry un-modeled. This raises a key, fundamental challenge: Can we infer a complete 3D structure from partial 2D observations? We present RnG (Reconstruction and Generation), a novel feed-forward Transformer that unifies these two tasks by predicting an implicit, complete 3D representation. At the core of RnG, we propose a reconstruction-guided causal attention mechanism that separates reconstruction and generation at the attention level, and treats the KV-cache as an implicit 3D representation. Then, arbitrary poses can efficiently query this cache to render high-fidelity, novel-view RGBD outputs. As a result, RnG not only accurately reconstructs visible geometry but also generates plausible, coherent unseen geometry and appearance. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in both generalizable 3D reconstruction and novel view generation, while operating efficiently enough for real-time interactive applications. Project page: https://npucvr.github.io/RnG

</details>


### [199] [CoSMo3D: Open-World Promptable 3D Semantic Part Segmentation through LLM-Guided Canonical Spatial Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01205)
*Li Jin,Weikai Chen,Yujie Wang,Yingda Yin,Zeyu Hu,Runze Zhang,Keyang Luo,Shengju Qian,Xin Wang,Xueying Qin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They learn a latent canonical reference frame and embedding for 3D objects to decouple semantics from input pose, yielding more stable, transferable part understanding and state‑of‑the‑art open‑world promptable 3D segmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open‑world promptable 3D segmentation is brittle because parts are inferred in sensor‑centric coordinates; humans instead reason in canonical object frames (e.g., wings lateral, handles protrude), suggesting that canonicalization could stabilize semantics across pose and symmetry.

Method: By construction: assemble a unified canonical dataset via LLM‑guided intra‑ and cross‑category alignment to expose canonical spatial regularities across 200 categories. By induction: a dual‑branch model with canonical map anchoring and canonical box calibration learns a latent canonical reference frame, collapsing pose variation and symmetries into a stable canonical embedding for part semantics.

Result: Shifting from input pose space to a canonical embedding produces far more stable and transferable part semantics and achieves new state of the art on open‑world promptable 3D segmentation benchmarks.

Conclusion: Learning and enforcing canonical object frames—via dataset alignment and a dual‑branch architecture—substantially improves robustness and generalization of 3D part semantics across poses and categories, making promptable open‑world segmentation more reliable.

Abstract: Open-world promptable 3D semantic segmentation remains brittle as semantics are inferred in the input sensor coordinates. Yet, humans, in contrast, interpret parts via functional roles in a canonical space -- wings extend laterally, handles protrude to the side, and legs support from below. Psychophysical evidence shows that we mentally rotate objects into canonical frames to reveal these roles. To fill this gap, we propose \methodName{}, which attains canonical space perception by inducing a latent canonical reference frame learned directly from data. By construction, we create a unified canonical dataset through LLM-guided intra- and cross-category alignment, exposing canonical spatial regularities across 200 categories. By induction, we realize canonicality inside the model through a dual-branch architecture with canonical map anchoring and canonical box calibration, collapsing pose variation and symmetry into a stable canonical embedding. This shift from input pose space to canonical embedding yields far more stable and transferable part semantics. Experimental results show that \methodName{} establishes new state of the art in open-world promptable 3D segmentation.

</details>


### [200] [VidDoS: Universal Denial-of-Service Attack on Video-based Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01454)
*Duoxun Tang,Dasen Dai,Jiyao Wang,Xiao Yang,Jianyu Wang,Siqi Cai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VidDoS is a universal Energy‑Latency Attack on Video‑LLMs that uses training-time, instance‑agnostic triggers to force extremely long, costly generations, causing >205× token expansion and >15× latency increases, leading to safety‑critical failures in real‑time settings like autonomous driving.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video‑LLMs are entering safety‑critical domains but are vulnerable to computational Denial‑of‑Service. Image‑based attack methods don’t transfer because temporal aggregation dilutes per‑frame perturbations, and real‑time streaming precludes per‑instance gradient attacks. A universal, low‑overhead attack tailored to temporal models is needed to expose and study this risk.

Method: Design a universal ELA via offline optimization to learn instance‑agnostic triggers. Apply masked teacher forcing to steer the model toward long, computation‑heavy target sequences, while adding a refusal penalty and early‑termination suppression to counteract conciseness and refusal behaviors. No inference‑time gradient computation is required.

Result: Across three mainstream Video‑LLMs and three datasets (covering video QA and autonomous driving), VidDoS drastically increases output length and compute: >205× token expansion and >15× inference latency relative to clean runs. In simulated real‑time autonomous driving streams, the induced delays translate into critical safety violations.

Conclusion: Video‑LLMs face severe DoS‑style vulnerabilities from universal ELAs. VidDoS demonstrates practical, trigger‑based attacks that bypass temporal aggregation and real‑time constraints, urging urgent mitigation research and defenses for safety‑critical deployments.

Abstract: Video-LLMs are increasingly deployed in safety-critical applications but are vulnerable to Energy-Latency Attacks (ELAs) that exhaust computational resources. Current image-centric methods fail because temporal aggregation mechanisms dilute individual frame perturbations. Additionally, real-time demands make instance-wise optimization impractical for continuous video streams. We introduce VidDoS, which is the first universal ELA framework tailored for Video-LLMs. Our method leverages universal optimization to create instance-agnostic triggers that require no inference-time gradient calculation. We achieve this through $\textit{masked teacher forcing}$ to steer models toward expensive target sequences, combined with a $\textit{refusal penalty}$ and $\textit{early-termination suppression}$ to override conciseness priors. Testing across three mainstream Video-LLMs and three video datasets, which include video question answering and autonomous driving scenarios, shows extreme degradation. VidDoS induces a token expansion of more than 205$\times$ and inflates the inference latency by more than 15$\times$ relative to clean baselines. Simulations of real-time autonomous driving streams further reveal that this induced latency leads to critical safety violations. We urge the community to recognize and mitigate these high-hazard ELA in Video-LLMs.

</details>


### [201] [Towards Policy-Adaptive Image Guardrail: Benchmark and Method](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01228)
*Caiyong Piao,Zhiyuan Yan,Haoming Xu,Yunzhen Zhao,Kaiqing Lin,Feiyang Xu,Shuigeng Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They propose a policy-adaptive visual safety guardrail system for vision-language models, introducing a new benchmark (SafeEditBench) and a reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards method (SafeGuard-VL) that improves cross-policy generalization beyond standard supervised fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety policies and harmful content evolve across domains and time, but fixed-category classifiers and VLM guardrails trained on a single policy fail to generalize and can degrade instruction-following and general knowledge. A flexible, policy-aware approach is needed.

Method: Two parts: (1) SafeEditBench—an evaluation suite that uses image-editing to create safe counterparts to unsafe images, keeping visual similarity except localized violations; human annotators label images under five distinct policies for fine-grained, cross-policy assessment. (2) SafeGuard-VL—an RL-based training framework with verifiable, policy-grounded rewards (RLVR) to optimize VLM guardrails for adherence across evolving policies, going beyond SFT on fixed policies.

Result: Benchmarking shows existing VLM-based guardrails overfit to seen policies and generalize poorly. SafeGuard-VL achieves more robust unsafe-image rejection across multiple, previously unseen policies compared with SFT-trained baselines.

Conclusion: VLM guardrails can be made adaptable to evolving safety policies by optimizing with policy-grounded, verifiable rewards; SafeEditBench offers a rigorous way to evaluate cross-policy generalization, and SafeGuard-VL provides a practical path to robust, policy-aware safeguards.

Abstract: Accurate rejection of sensitive or harmful visual content, i.e., harmful image guardrail, is critical in many application scenarios. This task must continuously adapt to the evolving safety policies and content across various domains and over time. However, traditional classifiers, confined to fixed categories, require frequent retraining when new policies are introduced. Vision-language models (VLMs) offer a more adaptable and generalizable foundation for dynamic safety guardrails. Despite this potential, existing VLM-based safeguarding methods are typically trained and evaluated under only a fixed safety policy. We find that these models are heavily overfitted to the seen policy, fail to generalize to unseen policies, and even lose the basic instruction-following ability and general knowledge. To address this issue, in this paper we make two key contributions. First, we benchmark the cross-policy generalization performance of existing VLMs with SafeEditBench, a new evaluation suite. SafeEditBench leverages image-editing models to convert unsafe images into safe counterparts, producing policy-aligned datasets where each safe-unsafe image pair remains visually similar except for localized regions violating specific safety rules. Human annotators then provide accurate safe/unsafe labels under five distinct policies, enabling fine-grained assessment of policy-aware generalization. Second, we introduce SafeGuard-VL, a reinforcement learning-based method with verifiable rewards (RLVR) for robust unsafe-image guardrails. Instead of relying solely on supervised fine-tuning (SFT) under fixed policies, SafeGuard-VL explicitly optimizes the model with policy-grounded rewards, promoting verifiable adaptation across evolving policies. Extensive experiments verify the effectiveness of our method for unsafe image guardrails across various policies.

</details>


### [202] [ATA: Bridging Implicit Reasoning with Attention-Guided and Action-Guided Inference for Vision-Language Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01490)
*Cheng Yang,Jianhao Jiao,Lingyi Huang,Jinqi Xiao,Zhexiang Tang,Yu Gong,Yibiao Ying,Yang Sui,Jintian Lin,Wen Huang,Bo Yuan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ATA is a training-free, plug-and-play framework that injects implicit reasoning into Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models by combining attention maps with action-guided regions of interest to refine visual inputs, boosting task success, robustness, and even efficiency without extra annotations or retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explicit reasoning approaches for VLA (e.g., Chain-of-Thought step decomposition and explicit visual grounding) demand costly annotations and retraining, slow down inference with long reasoning chains, and require time-consuming dataset construction—hindering practical deployment.

Method: Introduce implicit reasoning at inference via two complementary strategies: (1) attention-guided refinement using model attention maps to emphasize relevant visual regions; (2) action-guided refinement using action-based regions of interest derived from predicted actions. Fuse these signals to adaptively refine visual inputs. The approach is training-free, annotation-free, and model-agnostic (plug-and-play).

Result: Extensive experiments report consistent improvements in task success rates and robustness across settings, while maintaining or improving inference efficiency (shorter or unchanged inference sequences), all without additional training data or grounding labels.

Conclusion: Implicit reasoning via attention- and action-guided visual refinement offers a lightweight, practical way to enhance VLA models, sidestepping the costs and inefficiencies of CoT-style reasoning and explicit grounding, and yielding better performance and efficiency in practice.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models rely on current observations, including images, language instructions, and robot states, to predict actions and complete tasks. While accurate visual perception is crucial for precise action prediction and execution, recent work has attempted to further improve performance by introducing explicit reasoning during inference. However, such approaches face significant limitations. They often depend on data-intensive resources such as Chain-of-Thought (CoT) style annotations to decompose tasks into step-by-step reasoning, and in many cases require additional visual grounding annotations (e.g., bounding boxes or masks) to highlight relevant image regions. Moreover, they involve time-consuming dataset construction, labeling, and retraining, which ultimately results in longer inference sequences and reduced efficiency. To address these challenges, we propose ATA, a novel training-free framework that introduces implicit reasoning into VLA inference through complementary attention-guided and action-guided strategies. Unlike CoT or explicit visual-grounding methods, ATA formulates reasoning implicitly by integrating attention maps with an action-based region of interest (RoI), thereby adaptively refining visual inputs without requiring extra training or annotations. ATA is a plug-and-play implicit reasoning approach for VLA models, lightweight yet effective. Extensive experiments show that it consistently improves task success and robustness while preserving, and even enhancing, inference efficiency.

</details>


### [203] [AgilePruner: An Empirical Study of Attention and Diversity for Adaptive Visual Token Pruning in Large Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01236)
*Changwoo Baek,Jouwon Song,Sohyeon Kim,Kyeongbo Kong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Empirical study of visual token pruning in LVLMs shows attention-based pruning suits simple images, diversity-based pruning suits complex ones; diversity pruning often preserves less true diversity than intended and correlates with more hallucinations. An image-aware adaptive hybrid pruning improves both accuracy and hallucination robustness across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LVLMs face high compute from long visual token sequences, and existing pruning (attention- vs diversity-based) lacks clear understanding of trade-offs and when to use which, especially regarding hallucinations and feature coverage.

Method: Analyze pruning via effective rank (erank) to quantify feature diversity and attention-score entropy to study focus distribution; evaluate hallucinations using CHAIR; compare attention-based, diversity-based, and hybrid pruning; derive insights and incorporate image-aware adjustments; implement a simple adaptive pruning policy as a minimal instantiation.

Result: (1) Diversity-oriented pruning often retains less actual feature diversity than expected; retained “diversity” correlates with higher hallucination rates than attention-based pruning (CHAIR). (2) Attention-based pruning excels on simple, concentrated-evidence images; diversity-based pruning helps on complex, distributed-evidence images. Image-aware adjustments to hybrid pruning consistently improve performance; a lightweight adaptive mechanism achieves strong, reliable results across standard and hallucination-focused benchmarks.

Conclusion: Pruning should be adaptive to image complexity. Erank and attention entropy expose why fixed diversity or attention heuristics fail. Image-aware hybrid pruning offers better accuracy-efficiency trade-offs and reduces hallucinations; future work should refine complexity estimation and generalize across LVLM architectures.

Abstract: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have adopted visual token pruning strategies to mitigate substantial computational overhead incurred by extensive visual token sequences. While prior works primarily focus on either attention-based or diversity-based pruning methods, in-depth analysis of these approaches' characteristics and limitations remains largely unexplored. In this work, we conduct thorough empirical analysis using effective rank (erank) as a measure of feature diversity and attention score entropy to investigate visual token processing mechanisms and analyze the strengths and weaknesses of each approach. Our analysis reveals two insights: (1) Our erank-based quantitative analysis shows that many diversity-oriented pruning methods preserve substantially less feature diversity than intended; moreover, analysis using the CHAIR dataset reveals that the diversity they do retain is closely tied to increased hallucination frequency compared to attention-based pruning. (2) We further observe that attention-based approaches are more effective on simple images where visual evidence is concentrated, while diversity-based methods better handle complex images with distributed features. Building on these empirical insights, we show that incorporating image-aware adjustments into existing hybrid pruning strategies consistently improves their performance. We also provide a minimal instantiation of our empirical findings through a simple adaptive pruning mechanism, which achieves strong and reliable performance across standard benchmarks as well as hallucination-specific evaluations. Our project page available at https://cvsp-lab.github.io/AgilePruner.

</details>


### [204] [Retrieval, Refinement, and Ranking for Text-to-Video Generation via Prompt Optimization and Test-Time Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01509)
*Zillur Rahman,Alex Sheng,Cristian Meo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: 3R is a plug-and-play, training-free prompt optimization framework for text-to-video generation that combines RAG-based prompt enrichment, diffusion-driven preference alignment, and temporal frame interpolation to improve visual fidelity and temporal coherence across T2V models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern T2V models are highly sensitive to prompts; improving outputs via post-editing risks artifacts and via fine-tuning is costly and limits scalability. A lightweight, general method is needed to reliably boost video quality without retraining core generators.

Method: 3R integrates: (1) RAG-based modifier extraction to retrieve and inject contextual details into prompts; (2) diffusion-based Preference Optimization to steer generations toward human-preferred attributes using a vision-language model as feedback; and (3) temporal frame interpolation to enhance temporal consistency. It operates on top of state-of-the-art T2V diffusion models and VLMs without additional model training, remaining model-agnostic.

Result: Experiments show that 3R improves static visual fidelity and dynamic coherence of generated videos, yielding more accurate, efficient, and context-aligned outputs compared to baseline prompting or editing approaches. Quantitative details are not provided in the abstract.

Conclusion: Prompt optimization via 3R can significantly enhance T2V outputs without retraining or complex post-editing. Its model-agnostic, training-free design underscores the value of retrieval-enhanced prompting and preference alignment for practical, scalable T2V generation.

Abstract: While large-scale datasets have driven significant progress in Text-to-Video (T2V) generative models, these models remain highly sensitive to input prompts, demonstrating that prompt design is critical to generation quality. Current methods for improving video output often fall short: they either depend on complex, post-editing models, risking the introduction of artifacts, or require expensive fine-tuning of the core generator, which severely limits both scalability and accessibility. In this work, we introduce 3R, a novel RAG based prompt optimization framework. 3R utilizes the power of current state-of-the-art T2V diffusion model and vision language model. It can be used with any T2V model without any kind of model training. The framework leverages three key strategies: RAG-based modifiers extraction for enriched contextual grounding, diffusion-based Preference Optimization for aligning outputs with human preferences, and temporal frame interpolation for producing temporally consistent visual contents. Together, these components enable more accurate, efficient, and contextually aligned text-to-video generation. Experimental results demonstrate the efficacy of 3R in enhancing the static fidelity and dynamic coherence of generated videos, underscoring the importance of optimizing user prompts.

</details>


### [205] [Pri4R: Learning World Dynamics for Vision-Language-Action Models with Privileged 4D Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01549)
*Jisoo Kim,Jungbin Cho,Sanghyeok Chu,Ananya Bal,Jinhyung Kim,Gunhee Lee,Sihaeng Lee,Seung Hwan Kim,Bohyung Han,Hyunmin Lee,Laszlo A. Jeni,Seungryong Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Pri4R equips Vision-Language-Action models with implicit physical dynamics by training them to predict 3D point tracks using privileged 4D supervision, yielding large manipulation gains with zero inference overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA models understand semantics but struggle with spatiotemporal dynamics of physical interaction. The goal is to endow VLAs with physically-aware representations for precise control without increasing inference cost.

Method: Attach a lightweight point-track head to a VLA that predicts future 3D trajectories from injected VLA features, leveraging privileged 4D information during training. The head encourages the shared representation to capture evolving scene geometry. The head is used only during training; at inference the original VLA runs unchanged.

Result: Consistent improvements in simulation and real: +10% on LIBERO-Long and +40% on RoboCasa. Ablations show predicting 3D point tracks is an effective supervision target and validate architectural choices.

Conclusion: Privileged 4D point-track supervision teaches action–world dynamics within VLA representations, improving manipulation performance broadly while remaining architecture-compatible and incurring no inference-time cost.

Abstract: Humans learn not only how their bodies move, but also how the surrounding world responds to their actions. In contrast, while recent Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models exhibit impressive semantic understanding, they often fail to capture the spatiotemporal dynamics governing physical interaction. In this paper, we introduce Pri4R, a simple yet effective approach that endows VLA models with an implicit understanding of world dynamics by leveraging privileged 4D information during training. Specifically, Pri4R augments VLAs with a lightweight point track head that predicts 3D point tracks. By injecting VLA features into this head to jointly predict future 3D trajectories, the model learns to incorporate evolving scene geometry within its shared representation space, enabling more physically aware context for precise control. Due to its architectural simplicity, Pri4R is compatible with dominant VLA design patterns with minimal changes. During inference, we run the model using the original VLA architecture unchanged; Pri4R adds no extra inputs, outputs, or computational overhead. Across simulation and real-world evaluations, Pri4R significantly improves performance on challenging manipulation tasks, including a +10% gain on LIBERO-Long and a +40% gain on RoboCasa. We further show that 3D point track prediction is an effective supervision target for learning action-world dynamics, and validate our design choices through extensive ablations.

</details>


### [206] [Cross-Modal Guidance for Fast Diffusion-Based Computed Tomography](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01253)
*Timofey Efimov,Singanallur Venkatakrishnan,Maliha Hossain,Haley Duba-Sullivan,Amirkoushyar Ziabari*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They introduce a way to use an auxiliary imaging modality (e.g., X-ray CT) to guide diffusion-prior reconstructions for another costly modality (e.g., neutron CT) without retraining, markedly improving sparse-view reconstructions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparse measurements in modalities like neutron CT make high-quality reconstruction difficult even with diffusion priors. While a complementary, readily available modality could help, existing methods usually need large-scale retraining, which is costly and impractical.

Method: A cross-modal guidance scheme that incorporates an additional side modality during diffusion-based reconstruction without retraining the diffusion prior; they also analyze robustness to imperfect side-modal inputs.

Result: On sparse-view neutron CT, adding X-ray CT of the same samples substantially improves reconstruction quality compared to using the diffusion prior alone.

Conclusion: Cross-modal guidance of diffusion-prior reconstructions can accelerate imaging for expensive modalities, works without retraining, and remains beneficial under imperfect auxiliary data.

Abstract: Diffusion models have emerged as powerful priors for solving inverse problems in computed tomography (CT). In certain applications, such as neutron CT, it can be expensive to collect large amounts of measurements even for a single scan, leading to sparse data sets from which it is challenging to obtain high quality reconstructions even with diffusion models. One strategy to mitigate this challenge is to leverage a complementary, easily available imaging modality; however, such approaches typically require retraining the diffusion model with large datasets. In this work, we propose incorporating an additional modality without retraining the diffusion prior, enabling accelerated imaging of costly modalities. We further examine the impact of imperfect side modalities on cross-modal guidance. Our method is evaluated on sparse-view neutron computed tomography, where reconstruction quality is substantially improved by incorporating X-ray computed tomography of the same samples.

</details>


### [207] [FoSS: Modeling Long Range Dependencies and Multimodal Uncertainty in Trajectory Prediction via Fourier State Space Integration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01284)
*Yizhou Huang,Gengze Jiang,Yihua Cheng,Kezhi Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FoSS is a dual-branch, linear-time trajectory predictor that fuses frequency-domain features with time-domain state-space modeling to achieve state-of-the-art accuracy while reducing computation and parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing trajectory predictors trade off accuracy and efficiency: attention is powerful but scales quadratically with agents, and recurrent models miss long-range context and fine-grained local dynamics. A method is needed that captures both global intent and local variations with linear-time complexity.

Method: FoSS unifies spectral and temporal modeling. A frequency branch applies a discrete Fourier transform to split trajectories into amplitude (global intent) and phase (local variation), then uses a progressive helix reordering to preserve spectral order. Two selective state-space modules—Coarse2Fine-SSM and SpecEvolve-SSM—refine spectral features in O(N). In parallel, a time-domain dynamic selective SSM reconstructs self-attention behavior in linear time to maintain long-range temporal context. Cross-attention fuses spectral and temporal representations. Learnable queries produce multiple candidate trajectories, and a weighted fusion head models motion uncertainty.

Result: On Argoverse 1 and 2, FoSS attains state-of-the-art accuracy while cutting computation by 22.5% and parameters by over 40%. Ablations show each component contributes materially.

Conclusion: Combining frequency-domain reasoning with linear-time SSMs yields an efficient, scalable trajectory predictor that captures both global intent and local dynamics, offering a practical alternative to quadratic-attention models for autonomous driving.

Abstract: Accurate trajectory prediction is vital for safe autonomous driving, yet existing approaches struggle to balance modeling power and computational efficiency. Attention-based architectures incur quadratic complexity with increasing agents, while recurrent models struggle to capture long-range dependencies and fine-grained local dynamics. Building upon this, we present FoSS, a dual-branch framework that unifies frequency-domain reasoning with linear-time sequence modeling. The frequency-domain branch performs a discrete Fourier transform to decompose trajectories into amplitude components encoding global intent and phase components capturing local variations, followed by a progressive helix reordering module that preserves spectral order; two selective state-space submodules, Coarse2Fine-SSM and SpecEvolve-SSM, refine spectral features with O(N) complexity. In parallel, a time-domain dynamic selective SSM reconstructs self-attention behavior in linear time to retain long-range temporal context. A cross-attention layer fuses temporal and spectral representations, while learnable queries generate multiple candidate trajectories, and a weighted fusion head expresses motion uncertainty. Experiments on Argoverse 1 and Argoverse 2 benchmarks demonstrate that FoSS achieves state-of-the-art accuracy while reducing computation by 22.5% and parameters by over 40%. Comprehensive ablations confirm the necessity of each component.

</details>


### [208] [SkeleGuide: Explicit Skeleton Reasoning for Context-Aware Human-in-Place Image Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01579)
*Chuqiao Wu,Jin Song,Yiyun Fei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SkeleGuide introduces explicit skeletal reasoning to guide human image synthesis, yielding more structurally accurate and context-aware humans with fewer artifacts, plus an editable pose interface (PoseInverter) for control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current generative models struggle with human realism—producing distorted limbs and unnatural poses—because they lack explicit reasoning over human skeletal structure.

Method: A two-stage, jointly trained framework: (1) a reasoning stage that infers an internal latent pose acting as a strong structural prior; (2) a rendering stage that synthesizes the final human image conditioned on this prior. PoseInverter decodes the latent pose into an explicit, editable pose representation for fine-grained user control.

Result: Across extensive experiments, SkeleGuide surpasses both specialized and general-purpose baselines in fidelity, structural integrity, and contextual consistency of generated humans.

Conclusion: Explicit skeletal modeling is a crucial ingredient for robust, plausible human image synthesis; SkeleGuide demonstrates this and provides controllability via an editable pose interface.

Abstract: Generating realistic and structurally plausible human images into existing scenes remains a significant challenge for current generative models, which often produce artifacts like distorted limbs and unnatural poses. We attribute this systemic failure to an inability to perform explicit reasoning over human skeletal structure. To address this, we introduce SkeleGuide, a novel framework built upon explicit skeletal reasoning. Through joint training of its reasoning and rendering stages, SkeleGuide learns to produce an internal pose that acts as a strong structural prior, guiding the synthesis towards high structural integrity. For fine-grained user control, we introduce PoseInverter, a module that decodes this internal latent pose into an explicit and editable format. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SkeleGuide significantly outperforms both specialized and general-purpose models in generating high-fidelity, contextually-aware human images. Our work provides compelling evidence that explicitly modeling skeletal structure is a fundamental step towards robust and plausible human image synthesis.

</details>


### [209] [YCDa: YCbCr Decoupled Attention for Real-time Realistic Camouflaged Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01602)
*PeiHuang Zheng,Yunlong Zhao,Zheng Cui,Yang Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: YCDa is a plug-and-play, early-stage module that decouples chrominance and luminance and applies dynamic channel attention to emphasize luminance/texture cues when color is unreliable, boosting real-time camouflaged object detection with negligible overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human vision downweights color and leverages luminance/texture under camouflage. Many real-time detectors can be misled by chrominance cues in such settings. The work aims to replicate this adaptive mechanism to improve robustness in camouflaged object detection without sacrificing speed.

Method: At the input stage, YCDa separates color from luminance and uses dynamic channel attention to reweight these components so discriminative luminance/texture cues are amplified and misleading color noise is suppressed. The module replaces the first downsampling layer of existing real-time detectors, making it plug-and-play.

Result: Across multiple baselines, YCDa yields consistent gains with negligible computational overhead. The paper reports that YCDa-YOLO12s achieves a 112% relative improvement in mAP on COD10K-D and sets new state-of-the-art performance for real-time camouflaged object detection on COD-D datasets.

Conclusion: Embedding chrominance–luminance decoupling with dynamic attention into the earliest stage of detection networks is an effective, lightweight strategy for robust camouflaged object detection, delivering sizable accuracy improvements while maintaining real-time efficiency.

Abstract: Human vision exhibits remarkable adaptability in perceiving objects under camouflage. When color cues become unreliable, the visual system instinctively shifts its reliance from chrominance (color) to luminance (brightness and texture), enabling more robust perception in visually confusing environments. Drawing inspiration from this biological mechanism, we propose YCDa, an efficient early-stage feature processing strategy that embeds this "chrominance-luminance decoupling and dynamic attention" principle into modern real-time detectors. Specifically, YCDa separates color and luminance information in the input stage and dynamically allocates attention across channels to amplify discriminative cues while suppressing misleading color noise. The strategy is plug-and-play and can be integrated into existing detectors by simply replacing the first downsampling layer. Extensive experiments on multiple baselines demonstrate that YCDa consistently improves performance with negligible overhead as shown in Fig. Notably, YCDa-YOLO12s achieves a 112% improvement in mAP over the baseline on COD10K-D and sets new state-of-the-art results for real-time camouflaged object detection across COD-D datasets.

</details>


### [210] [When Does RL Help Medical VLMs? Disentangling Vision, SFT, and RL Gains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01301)
*Ahmadreza Jeddi,Kimia Shaban,Negin Baghbanzadeh,Natasha Sharan,Abhishek Moturu,Elham Dolatabadi,Babak Taati*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RL post-training on medical VLMs mostly sharpens output distributions when the model already has answer support from SFT/vision (high Pass@K), boosting Accuracy@1 and sampling efficiency; SFT is what grows support. A boundary-aware RL recipe built on OctoMed with a small, balanced PMC MC-VQA set yields strong performance across six medical VQA benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: It is unclear whether RL in medical VLMs truly improves visual reasoning or mainly refines behaviors induced by supervised fine-tuning. The field lacks a controlled analysis disentangling the roles of vision encoders, SFT, and RL, and metrics that separate answer support from sampling efficiency.

Method: Use MedMNIST as a multi-modality sandbox. Probe visual perception by benchmarking VLM vision towers versus vision-only baselines. Quantify support vs sampling via Accuracy@1 versus Pass@K. Analyze when RL closes the support gap and how gains transfer across modalities. Propose a boundary-aware RL training recipe and instantiate it by post-training an OctoMed-initialized model on a small, balanced subset of PMC multiple-choice VQA.

Result: RL is most effective when the model already exhibits non-trivial support (high Pass@K). It primarily sharpens the output distribution, improving Accuracy@1 and sampling efficiency, while SFT is what expands support and enables RL to be effective. The boundary-aware RL setup achieves strong average performance across six medical VQA benchmarks.

Conclusion: Treat RL as a distribution-sharpening stage contingent on sufficient pre-existing support; prioritize SFT (and capable vision towers) to build support first. A boundary-aware, small-data RL post-training strategy can transfer well across medical VQA modalities and yield strong average performance.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) is increasingly used to post-train medical Vision-Language Models (VLMs), yet it remains unclear whether RL improves medical visual reasoning or mainly sharpens behaviors already induced by supervised fine-tuning (SFT). We present a controlled study that disentangles these effects along three axes: vision, SFT, and RL. Using MedMNIST as a multi-modality testbed, we probe visual perception by benchmarking VLM vision towers against vision-only baselines, quantify reasoning support and sampling efficiency via Accuracy@1 versus Pass@K, and evaluate when RL closes the support gap and how gains transfer across modalities. We find that RL is most effective when the model already has non-trivial support (high Pass@K): it primarily sharpens the output distribution, improving Acc@1 and sampling efficiency, while SFT expands support and makes RL effective. Based on these findings, we propose a boundary-aware recipe and instantiate it by RL post-training an OctoMed-initialized model on a small, balanced subset of PMC multiple-choice VQA, achieving strong average performance across six medical VQA benchmarks.

</details>


### [211] [What Helps -- and What Hurts: Bidirectional Explanations for Vision Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01605)
*Qin Su,Tie Luo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BiCAM introduces signed (positive and negative) class activation maps for ViTs, yielding more faithful, contrastive explanations and enabling lightweight adversarial detection via a Positive-to-Negative Ratio, with improved localization and efficiency across datasets and ViT variants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision Transformers perform well but are hard to interpret; existing CAM methods largely ignore negative (suppressive) evidence, producing incomplete explanations and missing opportunities for robustness diagnostics.

Method: Propose BiCAM, a bidirectional CAM that preserves and visualizes both supportive (positive) and suppressive (negative) attributions in ViTs. Define a Positive-to-Negative Ratio (PNR) summarizing attribution balance, which can be used to detect adversarial inputs without retraining. Evaluate on ImageNet, VOC, and COCO, and test generalization to DeiT and Swin.

Result: BiCAM improves localization quality and explanation faithfulness over prior CAM-based methods while remaining computationally efficient. The PNR effectively flags adversarial examples. The approach generalizes across multiple ViT architectures (e.g., DeiT, Swin) and datasets (ImageNet, VOC, COCO).

Conclusion: Modeling both supportive and suppressive evidence is crucial for interpreting transformer-based vision models. BiCAM provides more complete, contrastive explanations and offers a simple, training-free signal (PNR) for adversarial detection, with broad applicability and efficiency.

Abstract: Vision Transformers (ViTs) achieve strong performance in visual recognition, yet their decision-making remains difficult to interpret. We propose BiCAM, a bidirectional class activation mapping method that captures both supportive (positive) and suppressive (negative) contributions to model predictions. Unlike prior CAM-based approaches that discard negative signals, BiCAM preserves signed attributions to produce more complete and contrastive explanations. BiCAM further introduces a Positive-to-Negative Ratio (PNR) that summarizes attribution balance and enables lightweight detection of adversarial examples without retraining. Across ImageNet, VOC, and COCO, BiCAM improves localization and faithfulness while remaining computationally efficient. It generalizes to multiple ViT variants, including DeiT and Swin. These results suggest the importance of modeling both supportive and suppressive evidence for interpreting transformer-based vision models.

</details>


### [212] [Open-Vocabulary vs Supervised Learning Methods for Post-Disaster Visual Scene Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01324)
*Anna Michailidou,Georgios Angelidis,Vasileios Argyriou,Panagiotis Sarigiannidis,Georgios Th. Papadopoulos*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They benchmark supervised vs. open-vocabulary/foundation vision models for post-disaster aerial imagery (segmentation and detection) across several datasets, finding that classic supervised training is still the most reliable—especially for small objects and precise boundaries—while open-vocabulary approaches offer flexibility but exhibit notable performance gaps and failure modes in cluttered, variable scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rapid, scalable post-disaster damage assessment is hindered by visual clutter, cross-event domain shift, and the cost/limited coverage of task-specific annotations. Open-vocabulary/foundation models promise reduced dependence on fixed labels and heavy annotation by leveraging large-scale vision-language pretraining—potentially valuable where concepts are ambiguous and data is scarce.

Method: A comparative evaluation of supervised learning and open-vocabulary/foundation vision models for post-disaster scene understanding. Tasks: semantic segmentation and object detection. Datasets: FloodNet+, RescueNet, DFire, LADD. They analyze performance trends, failure cases, and practical trade-offs across learning paradigms under real disaster imagery variability and domain shift.

Result: Across all evaluated benchmarks, supervised training remains most reliable when the label space is fixed and annotations exist, outperforming open-vocabulary methods particularly for small objects and fine boundary delineation in cluttered scenes. Open-vocabulary models show flexibility but suffer from notable failure modes under domain shift and visual variability.

Conclusion: For real-world disaster response, prefer supervised models when annotations and a stable label set are available, especially where small-object detection and precise segmentation matter. Open-vocabulary/foundation models are promising for flexibility and low-annotation settings but currently serve better as complementary tools rather than replacements; method choice should weigh label stability, annotation cost, object scale, and scene clutter.

Abstract: Aerial imagery is critical for large-scale post-disaster damage assessment. Automated interpretation remains challenging due to clutter, visual variability, and strong cross-event domain shift, while supervised approaches still rely on costly, task-specific annotations with limited coverage across disaster types and regions. Recent open-vocabulary and foundation vision models offer an appealing alternative, by reducing dependence on fixed label sets and extensive task-specific annotations. Instead, they leverage large-scale pretraining and vision-language representations. These properties are particularly relevant for post-disaster domains, where visual concepts are ambiguous and data availability is constrained. In this work, we present a comparative evaluation of supervised learning and open-vocabulary vision models for post-disaster scene understanding, focusing on semantic segmentation and object detection across multiple datasets, including FloodNet+, RescueNet, DFire, and LADD. We examine performance trends, failure modes, and practical trade-offs between different learning paradigms, providing insight into their applicability for real-world disaster response. The most notable remark across all evaluated benchmarks is that supervised training remains the most reliable approach (i.e., when the label space is fixed and annotations are available), especially for small objects and fine boundary delineation in cluttered scenes.

</details>


### [213] [Perspective-Equivariant Fine-tuning for Multispectral Demosaicing without Ground Truth](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01332)
*Andrew Wang,Mike Davies*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PEFD is a ground-truth-free multispectral demosaicing framework that enforces perspective equivariance and fine-tunes pretrained (1–3 channel) foundation models to reconstruct full-resolution spectral images from snapshot mosaics, recovering fine details and preserving spectral fidelity, with performance close to supervised methods on surgical and automotive data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time multispectral imaging needs accurate demosaicing of snapshot mosaics. Classical interpolation blurs details, and supervised learning demands costly, slow line-scan ground truth. A method that learns from mosaiced measurements alone while maintaining detail and spectral accuracy is needed.

Method: Perspective-Equivariant Fine-tuning (PEFD): leverage projective geometry to impose perspective (projective) equivariance, exploiting a richer transformation group than prior approaches to recover null-space information; adapt and fine-tune pretrained foundation models originally built for 1–3 channels to handle multispectral mosaics; learn without explicit ground truth by enforcing self-consistency under perspective transforms and the mosaic imaging model.

Result: On intraoperative and automotive datasets, PEFD reconstructs fine structures (e.g., blood vessels) and maintains spectral fidelity, substantially outperforming recent (unsupervised) baselines and approaching supervised performance.

Conclusion: By combining perspective-equivariant learning with foundation model adaptation, high-quality multispectral demosaicing can be achieved without ground truth, enabling practical real-time deployment in domains like neurosurgery and autonomous driving.

Abstract: Multispectral demosaicing is crucial to reconstruct full-resolution spectral images from snapshot mosaiced measurements, enabling real-time imaging from neurosurgery to autonomous driving. Classical methods are blurry, while supervised learning requires costly ground truth (GT) obtained from slow line-scanning systems. We propose Perspective-Equivariant Fine-tuning for Demosaicing (PEFD), a framework that learns multispectral demosaicing from mosaiced measurements alone. PEFD a) exploits the projective geometry of camera-based imaging systems to leverage a richer group structure than previous demosaicing methods to recover more null-space information, and b) learns efficiently without GT by adapting pretrained foundation models designed for 1-3 channel imaging. On intraoperative and automotive datasets, PEFD recovers fine details such as blood vessels and preserves spectral fidelity, substantially outperforming recent approaches, nearing supervised performance.

</details>


### [214] [MVR: Multi-view Video Reward Shaping for Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01694)
*Lirui Luo,Guoxi Zhang,Hongming Xu,Yaodong Yang,Cong Fang,Qing Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They introduce Multi-View Video Reward Shaping (MVR), which uses multi-view video–text similarity from a frozen VLM to learn a state relevance function and perform state-dependent reward shaping, improving RL performance on dynamic, visually complex tasks and automatically tapering VLM guidance once the target motion pattern emerges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior VLM-based reward augmentation often (1) linearly adds image-text scores to task rewards, risking policy distortion; (2) relies on single static images that mis-handle tasks requiring long, dynamic motions; and (3) uses single views that can occlude critical behavior. The authors aim to provide temporally-aware, multi-view, safer reward guidance that aligns with the true task objective.

Method: MVR builds multi-view video observations of agent behavior and computes video–text similarity with a frozen pre-trained VLM to learn a state relevance function that captures progress toward the target behavior over time and viewpoints. They then propose a state-dependent reward shaping that fuses the environment’s task/success rewards with VLM-derived guidance and automatically reduces the VLM influence once the desired motion pattern is detected, mitigating bias toward specific poses and preserving optimality.

Result: On humanoid locomotion (HumanoidBench) and manipulation (MetaWorld), MVR outperforms baselines that use static image VLM rewards or naive linear combinations. Ablations corroborate the need for multi-view inputs, video-based similarity, and the adaptive shaping schedule.

Conclusion: Video-based, multi-view VLM guidance paired with state-dependent shaping offers a more principled and robust way to shape rewards for complex, dynamic RL tasks, reducing reliance on brittle single-image cues and avoiding long-term policy distortion while generalizing across locomotion and manipulation domains.

Abstract: Reward design is of great importance for solving complex tasks with reinforcement learning. Recent studies have explored using image-text similarity produced by vision-language models (VLMs) to augment rewards of a task with visual feedback. A common practice linearly adds VLM scores to task or success rewards without explicit shaping, potentially altering the optimal policy. Moreover, such approaches, often relying on single static images, struggle with tasks whose desired behavior involves complex, dynamic motions spanning multiple visually different states. Furthermore, single viewpoints can occlude critical aspects of an agent's behavior. To address these issues, this paper presents Multi-View Video Reward Shaping (MVR), a framework that models the relevance of states regarding the target task using videos captured from multiple viewpoints. MVR leverages video-text similarity from a frozen pre-trained VLM to learn a state relevance function that mitigates the bias towards specific static poses inherent in image-based methods. Additionally, we introduce a state-dependent reward shaping formulation that integrates task-specific rewards and VLM-based guidance, automatically reducing the influence of VLM guidance once the desired motion pattern is achieved. We confirm the efficacy of the proposed framework with extensive experiments on challenging humanoid locomotion tasks from HumanoidBench and manipulation tasks from MetaWorld, verifying the design choices through ablation studies.

</details>


### [215] [TIMI: Training-Free Image-to-3D Multi-Instance Generation with Spatial Fidelity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01371)
*Xiao Cai,Lianli Gao,Pengpeng Zeng,Ji Zhang,Heng Tao Shen,Jingkuan Song*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TIMI is a training-free framework that boosts spatial fidelity in Image-to-3D multi‑instance generation by disentangling instances early and stabilizing geometry later, outperforming fine‑tuning approaches in layout accuracy, instance separation, and speed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-instance I23D needs precise spatial fidelity for real-world use, but current methods rely on costly fine-tuning and still suffer from instance entanglement. The authors observe unused spatial priors in pre-trained I23D models and aim to exploit them without retraining.

Method: Introduce two inference-time modules: (1) Instance-aware Separation Guidance (ISG) to disentangle instances during early denoising; (2) Spatial-stabilized Geometry-adaptive Update (SGU) to stabilize ISG’s effects, preserving each instance’s geometry and their relative spatial relationships. The approach is entirely training-free and plugs into pre-trained I23D pipelines.

Result: Across extensive experiments, TIMI achieves better global layout fidelity and clearer, more distinct local instances than prior multi-instance methods, while requiring no additional training and providing faster inference.

Conclusion: Leveraging latent spatial priors in pre-trained I23D models via training-free, guidance-based modules can deliver high-fidelity multi-instance 3D reconstruction. TIMI’s ISG and SGU effectively reduce entanglement and maintain geometry, making the method practical and efficient for downstream applications.

Abstract: Precise spatial fidelity in Image-to-3D multi-instance generation is critical for downstream real-world applications. Recent work attempts to address this by fine-tuning pre-trained Image-to-3D (I23D) models on multi-instance datasets, which incurs substantial training overhead and struggles to guarantee spatial fidelity. In fact, we observe that pre-trained I23D models already possess meaningful spatial priors, which remain underutilized as evidenced by instance entanglement issues. Motivated by this, we propose TIMI, a novel Training-free framework for Image-to-3D Multi-Instance generation that achieves high spatial fidelity. Specifically, we first introduce an Instance-aware Separation Guidance (ISG) module, which facilitates instance disentanglement during the early denoising stage. Next, to stabilize the guidance introduced by ISG, we devise a Spatial-stabilized Geometry-adaptive Update (SGU) module that promotes the preservation of the geometric characteristics of instances while maintaining their relative relationships. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method yields better performance in terms of both global layout and distinct local instances compared to existing multi-instance methods, without requiring additional training and with faster inference speed.

</details>


### [216] [Cross-modal Identity Mapping: Minimizing Information Loss in Modality Conversion via Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01696)
*Haonan Jia,Shichao Dong,Xin Dong,Zenghui Sun,Jin Wang,Jinsong Lan,Xiaoyong Zhu,Bo Zheng,Kaifu Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CIM uses retrieval-based reinforcement learning to minimize information loss in LVLM image captioning by making captions retrieve the right images, yielding more precise, detail-rich, and relation-aware captions without extra annotations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LVLMs often omit or misstate key visual details in captions, but quantifying cross-modal information loss is hard. The authors posit that better captions should retrieve more similar images, providing a measurable proxy for caption fidelity without human labels.

Method: Cross-modal Identity Mapping (CIM): RL fine-tuning guided by two retrieval-derived rewards—(1) Gallery Representation Consistency to keep representations of the target and retrieved gallery images aligned; (2) Query–gallery Image Relevance to ensure captions, used as text queries, retrieve the correct/closest images. This supervision encourages near-identity mapping from image to caption, requiring no additional annotations.

Result: CIM outperforms supervised fine-tuning on image captioning. On COCO-LN500, it delivers a ~20% improvement in relation reasoning with Qwen2.5-VL-7B, showing stronger detail grounding.

Conclusion: Retrieval-driven RL can effectively reduce cross-modal information loss, pushing LVLMs to focus on fine image details and produce more faithful captions. CIM is annotation-free and achieves state-of-the-art gains; code will be released upon acceptance.

Abstract: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) often omit or misrepresent critical visual content in generated image captions. Minimizing such information loss will force LVLMs to focus on image details to generate precise descriptions. However, measuring information loss during modality conversion is inherently challenging due to the modal gap between visual content and text output. In this paper, we argue that the quality of an image caption is positively correlated with the similarity between images retrieved via text search using that caption. Based on this insight, we further propose Cross-modal Identity Mapping (CIM), a reinforcement learning framework that enhances image captioning without requiring additional annotations. Specifically, the method quantitatively evaluates the information loss from two perspectives: Gallery Representation Consistency and Query-gallery Image Relevance. Supervised under these metrics, LVLM minimizes information loss and aims to achieve identity mapping from images to captions. The experimental results demonstrate the superior performance of our method in image captioning, even when compared with Supervised Fine-Tuning. Particularly, on the COCO-LN500 benchmark, CIM achieves a 20% improvement in relation reasoning on Qwen2.5-VL-7B.The code will be released when the paper is accepted.

</details>


### [217] [Continuous Exposure-Time Modeling for Realistic Atmospheric Turbulence Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01398)
*Junwei Zeng,Dong Liang,Sheng-Jun Huang,Kun Zhan,Songcan Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They introduce an exposure-time-dependent blur model (ET-MTF), derive a tilt-invariant PSF with a spatially varying blur-width field for realistic turbulence synthesis, and release ET-Turb, a large synthetic dataset that improves restoration realism and generalization to real data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Turbulence causes warping and exposure-time-dependent blur, but prior synthesis assumes fixed/binary exposure, producing unrealistic data and limiting trained models’ generalization. A physically grounded, continuous link between exposure time and blur is needed for better training data and performance.

Method: Revisit the modulation transfer function (MTF) to formulate an Exposure-Time-dependent MTF (ET-MTF) that models blur continuously with exposure time. From this, derive a tilt-invariant point spread function (PSF) and couple it with a spatially varying blur-width field to synthesize turbulence blur. Build ET-Turb, a large-scale dataset spanning diverse optical/atmospheric conditions and continuous exposure settings (5,083 videos; 2,005,835 frames; 3,988 train / 1,095 test).

Result: Models trained on ET-Turb yield more realistic restorations and superior generalization on real-world turbulence compared with models trained on existing datasets. Empirical evaluations across varied settings validate the benefits of continuous exposure-time modeling in synthesis.

Conclusion: Modeling blur as a continuous function of exposure time via ET-MTF enables physically accurate turbulence synthesis. The ET-Turb dataset operationalizes this modeling at scale and leads to improved restoration quality and generalization; it is publicly available for the community.

Abstract: Atmospheric turbulence significantly degrades long-range imaging by introducing geometric warping and exposure-time-dependent blur, which adversely affects both visual quality and the performance of high-level vision tasks. Existing methods for synthesizing turbulence effects often oversimplify the relationship between blur and exposure-time, typically assuming fixed or binary exposure settings. This leads to unrealistic synthetic data and limited generalization capability of trained models. To address this gap, we revisit the modulation transfer function (MTF) formulation and propose a novel Exposure-Time-dependent MTF (ET-MTF) that models blur as a continuous function of exposure-time. For blur synthesis, we derive a tilt-invariant point spread function (PSF) from the ET-MTF, which, when integrated with a spatially varying blur-width field, provides a comprehensive and physically accurate characterization of turbulence-induced blur. Building on this synthesis pipeline, we construct ET-Turb, a large-scale synthetic turbulence dataset that explicitly incorporates continuous exposure-time modeling across diverse optical and atmospheric conditions. The dataset comprises 5,083 videos (2,005,835 frames), partitioned into 3,988 training and 1,095 test videos. Extensive experiments demonstrate that models trained on ET-Turb produce more realistic restorations and achieve superior generalization on real-world turbulence data compared to those trained on other datasets. The dataset is publicly available at: github.com/Jun-Wei-Zeng/ET-Turb.

</details>


### [218] [Towards Principled Dataset Distillation: A Spectral Distribution Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01698)
*Ruixi Wu,Shaobo Wang,Jiahuan Chen,Zhiyuan Liu,Yicun Yang,Zhaorun Chen,Zekai Li,Kaixin Li,Xinming Wang,Hongzhu Yi,Kai Wang,Linfeng Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a class-aware, kernel-spectral approach to dataset distillation that substantially improves performance and stability on long-tailed data by aligning distributions in frequency space and emphasizing tail-class realism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dataset distillation methods struggle on long-tailed datasets due to (1) heuristic, potentially brittle discrepancy measures for aligning real and synthetic data distributions, and (2) treating all classes uniformly, which ignores imbalance and harms tail classes.

Method: Introduce Class-Aware Spectral Distribution Matching (CSDM). Reformulate distribution alignment using the spectrum of a well-behaved kernel, mapping samples into frequency space to define a Spectral Distribution Distance (SDD). Use a unified SDD form to perform amplitude–phase decomposition and adaptively emphasize realism of tail classes (class-aware weighting).

Result: On CIFAR-10-LT with 10 images per class, CSDM outperforms state-of-the-art DD by 14.0%. When tail-class images are reduced from 500 to 25, performance drops only 5.7%, indicating strong robustness to increased imbalance severity.

Conclusion: Spectral, class-aware distribution matching improves dataset distillation on long-tailed data, providing better alignment and resilience to class imbalance. The approach yields significant gains over prior methods and maintains stability as tail scarcity increases.

Abstract: Dataset distillation (DD) aims to compress large-scale datasets into compact synthetic counterparts for efficient model training. However, existing DD methods exhibit substantial performance degradation on long-tailed datasets. We identify two fundamental challenges: heuristic design choices for distribution discrepancy measure and uniform treatment of imbalanced classes. To address these limitations, we propose Class-Aware Spectral Distribution Matching (CSDM), which reformulates distribution alignment via the spectrum of a well-behaved kernel function. This technique maps the original samples into frequency space, resulting in the Spectral Distribution Distance (SDD). To mitigate class imbalance, we exploit the unified form of SDD to perform amplitude-phase decomposition, which adaptively prioritizes the realism in tail classes. On CIFAR-10-LT, with 10 images per class, CSDM achieves a 14.0% improvement over state-of-the-art DD methods, with only a 5.7% performance drop when the number of images in tail classes decreases from 500 to 25, demonstrating strong stability on long-tailed data.

</details>


### [219] [Token Reduction via Local and Global Contexts Optimization for Efficient Video Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01400)
*Jinlong Li,Liyuan Jiang,Haonan Zhang,Nicu Sebe*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AOT is a training-free token compression method for Video LLMs that builds intra- and inter-frame token anchors and uses optimal transport to aggregate information from pruned tokens, substantially cutting compute while preserving temporal and visual fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video LLMs are inefficient due to many redundant visual tokens. Existing pruning focuses mainly on spatial redundancy or shallow-layer LLM pruning, under-exploits long-context compressibility, and often discards subtle yet informative context.

Method: Under attention guidance, establish local- and global-aware token anchors within each frame. Use optimal transport to aggregate information from tokens slated for pruning into these anchors (intra-frame). For temporal redundancy, split video into clips and treat the first frame as keyframe anchors; apply optimal transport to absorb similar content from subsequent frames while retaining distinct tokens that capture motion dynamics. Entirely training-free.

Result: Across multiple short- and long-video benchmarks and on leading Video LLMs, AOT achieves competitive performance with substantial computational efficiency gains, while maintaining temporal and visual fidelity.

Conclusion: Local-global anchor formation plus optimal transport enables effective spatiotemporal token reduction for VLLMs without training, mitigating redundancy and preserving critical context, yielding practical speedups with minimal quality loss.

Abstract: Video Large Language Models (VLLMs) demonstrate strong video understanding but suffer from inefficiency due to redundant visual tokens. Existing pruning primary targets intra-frame spatial redundancy or prunes inside the LLM with shallow-layer overhead, yielding suboptimal spatiotemporal reduction and underutilizing long-context compressibility. All of them often discard subtle yet informative context from merged or pruned tokens. In this paper, we propose a new perspective that elaborates token \textbf{A}nchors within intra-frame and inter-frame to comprehensively aggregate the informative contexts via local-global \textbf{O}ptimal \textbf{T}ransport (\textbf{AOT}). Specifically, we first establish local- and global-aware token anchors within each frame under the attention guidance, which then optimal transport aggregates the informative contexts from pruned tokens, constructing intra-frame token anchors. Then, building on the temporal frame clips, the first frame within each clip will be considered as the keyframe anchors to ensemble similar information from consecutive frames through optimal transport, while keeping distinct tokens to represent temporal dynamics, leading to efficient token reduction in a training-free manner. Extensive evaluations show that our proposed AOT obtains competitive performances across various short- and long-video benchmarks on leading video LLMs, obtaining substantial computational efficiency while preserving temporal and visual fidelity. Project webpage: \href{https://tyroneli.github.io/AOT}{AOT}.

</details>


### [220] [An Analysis of Multi-Task Architectures for the Hierarchic Multi-Label Problem of Vehicle Model and Make Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01746)
*Alexandru Manole,Laura Diosan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They investigate whether multi-task learning that exploits the make→model hierarchy improves car classification, comparing parallel vs cascaded setups across CNNs and Transformers on StanfordCars and CompCars, tuning dropout and loss weights; they find consistent gains for CNNs and strong gains on CompCars for both model families.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world labels are often hierarchical (e.g., car make then model), but many deep learning models ignore such structure. Human learning benefits from hierarchies, suggesting models might too via multi-task learning. The work aims to quantify advantages and limits of leveraging label hierarchies in practice.

Method: Formulate hierarchical multi-label classification with two tasks: coarse (make) and fine (model). Compare parallel (joint prediction with shared features) and cascaded (parent influences child) multi-task architectures across CNNs and Transformers. Systematically vary dropout rates and loss-weighting between tasks to study sensitivity. Evaluate on two standard car datasets: StanfordCars and CompCars.

Result: Multi-task learning improves CNN performance in almost all tested configurations on both datasets. For Transformers, the benefits are pronounced on CompCars, yielding significant improvements; gains on StanfordCars are less emphasized. Overall, exploiting the hierarchy tends to help, but effects vary by model family and dataset.

Conclusion: Incorporating hierarchical structure via multi-task learning is generally effective for car make/model classification, especially for CNNs and on the CompCars dataset. The impact depends on architecture choices and hyperparameters, indicating that careful design and tuning matter. Dataset characteristics likely mediate the magnitude of gains, suggesting future work on broader domains and deeper hierarchies.

Abstract: Most information in our world is organized hierarchically; however, many Deep Learning approaches do not leverage this semantically rich structure. Research suggests that human learning benefits from exploiting the hierarchical structure of information, and intelligent models could similarly take advantage of this through multi-task learning. In this work, we analyze the advantages and limitations of multi-task learning in a hierarchical multi-label classification problem: car make and model classification. Considering both parallel and cascaded multi-task architectures, we evaluate their impact on different Deep Learning classifiers (CNNs, Transformers) while varying key factors such as dropout rate and loss weighting to gain deeper insight into the effectiveness of this approach. The tests are conducted on two established benchmarks: StanfordCars and CompCars. We observe the effectiveness of the multi-task paradigm on both datasets, improving the performance of the investigated CNN in almost all scenarios. Furthermore, the approach yields significant improvements on the CompCars dataset for both types of models.

</details>


### [221] [UETrack: A Unified and Efficient Framework for Single Object Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01412)
*Ben Kang,Jie Zhao,Xin Chen,Wanting Geng,Bin Zhang,Lu Zhang,Dong Wang,Huchuan Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UETrack is an efficient, versatile single-object tracker that handles multiple modalities (RGB, Depth, Thermal, Event, Language). It introduces a Token-Pooling Mixture-of-Experts and Target-aware Adaptive Distillation to boost accuracy while remaining lightweight, achieving state-of-the-art speed–accuracy trade-offs (e.g., 69.2% AUC on LaSOT at up to 163 FPS on GPU).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most trackers rely on RGB only and underperform in multi-modal settings. Existing multi-modal methods are complex and slow, limiting deployment on resource-constrained hardware. There is a need for a practical, unified, and efficient multi-modal tracker.

Method: UETrack proposes: (1) a Token-Pooling-based Mixture-of-Experts that aggregates features and allows expert specialization to better model diverse inputs; (2) a Target-aware Adaptive Distillation mechanism that selectively distills supervision based on sample characteristics, reducing redundant signals. The framework supports RGB, Depth, Thermal, Event, and Language inputs and is designed for efficiency across devices.
