Result: Extensive experiments on 12 benchmarks over 3 hardware platforms show superior speed–accuracy trade-offs versus prior work. Example: UETrack-B attains 69.2% AUC on LaSOT and runs at 163/56/60 FPS on GPU/CPU/AGX.

Conclusion: UETrack closes the gap in efficient multi-modal tracking with a practical, unified design and novel MoE + adaptive distillation components, delivering strong accuracy at high frame rates across platforms. Code is publicly available.

Abstract: With growing real-world demands, efficient tracking has received increasing attention. However, most existing methods are limited to RGB inputs and struggle in multi-modal scenarios. Moreover, current multi-modal tracking approaches typically use complex designs, making them too heavy and slow for resource-constrained deployment. To tackle these limitations, we propose UETrack, an efficient framework for single object tracking. UETrack demonstrates high practicality and versatility, efficiently handling multiple modalities including RGB, Depth, Thermal, Event, and Language, and addresses the gap in efficient multi-modal tracking. It introduces two key components: a Token-Pooling-based Mixture-of-Experts mechanism that enhances modeling capacity through feature aggregation and expert specialization, and a Target-aware Adaptive Distillation strategy that selectively performs distillation based on sample characteristics, reducing redundant supervision and improving performance. Extensive experiments on 12 benchmarks across 3 hardware platforms show that UETrack achieves a superior speed-accuracy trade-off compared to previous methods. For instance, UETrack-B achieves 69.2% AUC on LaSOT and runs at 163/56/60 FPS on GPU/CPU/AGX, demonstrating strong practicality and versatility. Code is available at https://github.com/kangben258/UETrack.

</details>


### [222] [UniTalking: A Unified Audio-Video Framework for Talking Portrait Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01418)
*Hebeizi Li,Zihao Liang,Benyuan Sun,Zihao Yin,Xiao Sha,Chenliang Wang,Yi Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UniTalking is an open, unified diffusion framework that generates high-fidelity, lip-synced talking portraits and speech, leveraging multi-modal transformers for tight audio–video alignment, priors from a pre-trained video generator for visual quality/efficiency, and a lightweight voice-cloning module; it outperforms existing open-source baselines on lip-sync, audio naturalness, and perceptual quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Closed-source leaders (e.g., Veo3, Sora2) limit access to architectures/training for audio–video generation. There is a need for an accessible, high-performing system that jointly models speech and synchronized facial video with personalization while maintaining strong fidelity and trainability.

Method: An end-to-end diffusion model with Multi-Modal Transformer Blocks that share self-attention across audio and video latent tokens to capture fine-grained temporal correspondence. It reuses priors from a pre-trained video generation model for visual fidelity and training efficiency, and adds a personalized voice cloning component that adapts to a target voice from a short reference.

Result: Qualitative and quantitative evaluations show highly realistic talking portraits with better lip-sync accuracy, more natural audio, and higher perceptual quality than existing open-source methods.

Conclusion: UniTalking closes part of the accessibility–performance gap by delivering a unified, efficient diffusion framework for synchronized speech–video generation with personalization, achieving state-of-the-art results among open-source approaches.

Abstract: While state-of-the-art audio-video generation models like Veo3 and Sora2 demonstrate remarkable capabilities, their closed-source nature makes their architectures and training paradigms inaccessible. To bridge this gap in accessibility and performance, we introduce UniTalking, a unified, end-to-end diffusion framework for generating high-fidelity speech and lip-synchronized video. At its core, our framework employs Multi-Modal Transformer Blocks to explicitly model the fine-grained temporal correspondence between audio and video latent tokens via a shared self-attention mechanism. By leveraging powerful priors from a pre-trained video generation model, our framework ensures state-of-the-art visual fidelity while enabling efficient training. Furthermore, UniTalking incorporates a personalized voice cloning capability, allowing the generation of speech in a target style from a brief audio reference. Qualitative and quantitative results demonstrate that our method produces highly realistic talking portraits, achieving superior performance over existing open-source approaches in lip-sync accuracy, audio naturalness, and overall perceptual quality.

</details>


### [223] [SeaVIS: Sound-Enhanced Association for Online Audio-Visual Instance Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01431)
*Yingjian Zhu,Ying Wang,Yuyang Hong,Ruohao Guo,Kun Ding,Xin Gu,Bin Fan,Shiming Xiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SeaVIS is the first online audio‑visual instance segmentation framework. It fuses current‑frame visuals with causal audio history and learns audio‑aware instance prototypes, enabling it to follow only sounding objects in streaming video. It achieves state‑of‑the‑art results on AVISeg with real‑time‑friendly speed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prevailing AVIS approaches are offline and cannot reliably associate instances across continuous video streams. Moreover, appearance‑based association cannot distinguish sounding from silent states, leading to incorrect segmentation/tracking of silent objects.

Method: Introduce SeaVIS with two key components: (1) Causal Cross Attention Fusion (CCAF) that integrates current‑frame visual features with the entire past audio under strict causality for efficient online inference; (2) Audio‑Guided Contrastive Learning (AGCL) to learn instance prototypes encoding both visual appearance and sounding activity, so silent instances are suppressed during association.

Result: On the AVISeg dataset, SeaVIS surpasses prior state‑of‑the‑art methods across multiple evaluation metrics while keeping a competitive inference speed suitable for real‑time processing.

Conclusion: Causal audio‑visual fusion plus audio‑guided contrastive prototypes enables effective online AVIS that tracks sounding instances and suppresses silent ones, making SeaVIS practical for real‑world streaming scenarios.

Abstract: Recently, an audio-visual instance segmentation (AVIS) task has been introduced, aiming to identify, segment and track individual sounding instances in videos. However, prevailing methods primarily adopt the offline paradigm, that cannot associate detected instances across consecutive clips, making them unsuitable for real-world scenarios that involve continuous video streams. To address this limitation, we introduce SeaVIS, the first online framework designed for audio-visual instance segmentation. SeaVIS leverages the Causal Cross Attention Fusion (CCAF) module to enable efficient online processing, which integrates visual features from the current frame with the entire audio history under strict causal constraints. A major challenge for conventional VIS methods is that appearance-based instance association fails to distinguish between an object's sounding and silent states, resulting in the incorrect segmentation of silent objects. To tackle this, we employ an Audio-Guided Contrastive Learning (AGCL) strategy to generate instance prototypes that encode not only visual appearance but also sounding activity. In this way, instances preserved during per-frame prediction that do not emit sound can be effectively suppressed during instance association process, thereby significantly enhancing the audio-following capability of SeaVIS. Extensive experiments conducted on the AVISeg dataset demonstrate that SeaVIS surpasses existing state-of-the-art models across multiple evaluation metrics while maintaining a competitive inference speed suitable for real-time processing.

</details>


### [224] [Non-verbal Real-time Human-AI Interaction in Constrained Robotic Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01804)
*Dragos Costea,Alina Marcu,Cristina Lazar,Marius Leordeanu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They introduce a real-time framework that generates AI nonverbal responses from 2D keypoints, show that synthetic pretraining lowers motion errors without slowing inference, and find a persistent “reality gap”: AI motion remains statistically distinguishable from human motion, with temporal coherence (more than image fidelity) driving downstream performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Settle whether AI-generated full-body motion merely mimics surface patterns or can participate in the expressive dynamics of human body language; provide a practical, closed-loop, real-time testbed to compare human and AI motion statistically and behaviorally.

Method: Build the first real-time Human–AI nonverbal interaction pipeline from 2D body keypoints, using four lightweight architectures that run up to 100 FPS on an NVIDIA Orin Nano. Train on 437 human video clips, pretrain on synthetic motion sequences, and evaluate motion errors and generalization by feeding keypoints extracted from state-of-the-art text-to-video outputs (SORA, VEO).

Result: Synthetic pretraining significantly reduces motion errors with no speed penalty. Despite this, a measurable gap between AI and human motion remains. When evaluated on keypoints from text-to-video models, performance drops notably on SORA-generated clips but far less on VEO, implicating temporal coherence—not image fidelity—as the main determinant of real-world performance.

Conclusion: Lightweight, real-time models can enable closed-loop nonverbal interaction, but current generative motion remains detectably different from human motion. Bridging the gap likely requires advancing temporal coherence and sequence-level consistency rather than focusing on image realism alone.

Abstract: We study the ongoing debate regarding the statistical fidelity of AI-generated data compared to human-generated data in the context of non-verbal communication using full body motion. Concretely, we ask if contemporary generative models move beyond surface mimicry to participate in the silent, but expressive dialogue of body language. We tackle this question by introducing the first framework that generates a natural non-verbal interaction between Human and AI in real-time from 2D body keypoints. Our experiments utilize four lightweight architectures which run at up to 100 FPS on an NVIDIA Orin Nano, effectively closing the perception-action loop needed for natural Human-AI interaction. We trained on 437 human video clips and demonstrated that pretraining on synthetically-generated sequences reduces motion errors significantly, without sacrificing speed. Yet, a measurable reality gap persists. When the best model is evaluated on keypoints extracted from cutting-edge text-to-video systems, such as SORA and VEO, we observe that performance drops on SORA-generated clips. However, it degrades far less on VEO, suggesting that temporal coherence, not image fidelity, drives real-world performance. Our results demonstrate that statistically distinguishable differences persist between Human and AI motion.

</details>


### [225] [DOCFORGE-BENCH: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Document Forgery Detection and Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01433)
*Zengqi Zhao,Weidi Xia,Peter Wei,Yan Zhang,Yiyi Zhang,Jane Mo,Tiannan Zhang,Yuanqin Dai,Zexi Chen,Simiao Ren*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DOCFORGE-BENCH is a unified zero-shot benchmark showing that current document forgery detectors largely fail out-of-the-box due to severe score miscalibration under extreme class imbalance; simple threshold adaptation on a handful of in-domain images recovers much of the lost F1, but no method is reliably deployable yet, and benchmarks lag behind modern AI-driven forgeries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate how pretrained forgery detectors perform in realistic settings where practitioners lack labeled, domain-specific data, and identify what truly limits deployment (representation vs. calibration), across diverse document types.

Method: Construct a zero-shot benchmark (DOCFORGE-BENCH) evaluating 14 published methods, applied strictly with released pretrained weights and no domain adaptation, on eight datasets (text tampering, receipts, identity documents). Measure pixel-level AUC, F1 with a fixed threshold (τ=0.5), and Oracle-F1; analyze score distributions under extreme sparsity of tampered pixels; run a controlled calibration study that learns a single threshold on N=10 domain images to quantify recoverable performance.

Result: Despite moderate discrimination (Pixel-AUC ≥ 0.76), Pixel-F1 is near zero because tampered regions cover only 0.27–4.17% of pixels, making τ=0.5 catastrophically miscalibrated. Oracle-F1 is 2–10× higher than fixed-threshold F1, and adapting one threshold using 10 images recovers 39–55% of the Oracle-F1 gap. No method works reliably out-of-the-box across document types.

Conclusion: Calibration—not representation—is the primary bottleneck in zero-shot document forgery detection; lightweight threshold adaptation is essential for practical deployment. The field remains unsolved, and existing datasets predate generative AI, underscoring the need for new benchmarks including diffusion/LLM-based forgeries.

Abstract: We present DOCFORGE-BENCH, the first unified zero-shot benchmark for document forgery detection, evaluating 14 methods across eight datasets spanning text tampering, receipt forgery, and identity document manipulation. Unlike fine-tuning-oriented evaluations such as ForensicHub [Du et al., 2025], DOCFORGE-BENCH applies all methods with their published pretrained weights and no domain adaptation -- a deliberate design choice that reflects the realistic deployment scenario where practitioners lack labeled document training data. Our central finding is a pervasive calibration failure invisible under single-threshold protocols: methods achieve moderate Pixel-AUC (>=0.76) yet near-zero Pixel-F1. This AUC-F1 gap is not a discrimination failure but a score-distribution shift: tampered regions occupy only 0.27-4.17% of pixels in document images -- an order of magnitude less than in natural image benchmarks -- making the standard tau=0.5 threshold catastrophically miscalibrated. Oracle-F1 is 2-10x higher than fixed-threshold Pixel-F1, confirming that calibration, not representation, is the bottleneck. A controlled calibration experiment validates this: adapting a single threshold on N=10 domain images recovers 39-55% of the Oracle-F1 gap, demonstrating that threshold adaptation -- not retraining -- is the key missing step for practical deployment. Overall, no evaluated method works reliably out-of-the-box on diverse document types, underscoring that document forgery detection remains an unsolved problem. We further note that all eight datasets predate the era of generative AI editing; benchmarks covering diffusion- and LLM-based document forgeries represent a critical open gap on the modern attack surface.

</details>


### [226] [Unifying Language-Action Understanding and Generation for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01441)
*Xinyang Wang,Qian Liu,Wenjie Ding,Zhao Yang,Wei Li,Chang Liu,Bailin Li,Kun Zhan,Xianpeng Lang,Wei Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LinkVLA aligns and accelerates Vision-Language-Action driving by unifying language and action tokens in one codebook, adding a trajectory-to-caption objective for bidirectional grounding, and introducing a coarse-to-fine two-step action decoder that cuts inference time by 86%, yielding better instruction following and driving performance in closed-loop benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA-based autonomous driving models often misalign natural-language instructions with produced actions and rely on slow autoregressive action decoding, limiting reliability and real-time deployment.

Method: (1) Structural link: unify language and action as discrete tokens in a shared codebook within a single multimodal model to enforce cross-modal consistency. (2) Semantic link: an auxiliary action-understanding task that generates captions from trajectories to promote a bidirectional mapping between language and actions. (3) Efficiency: replace step-by-step autoregressive decoding with a two-step coarse-to-fine (C2F) action generation scheme for faster inference.

Result: On closed-loop driving benchmarks, LinkVLA improves instruction-following accuracy and driving performance while reducing latency; the C2F decoding saves 86% of inference time.

Conclusion: Structurally and semantically linking language and actions, coupled with coarse-to-fine decoding, leads to better-aligned and more efficient end-to-end driving. The approach indicates shared tokenization plus bidirectional objectives can enhance VLA grounding and practical real-time performance.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are emerging as a promising paradigm for end-to-end autonomous driving, valued for their potential to leverage world knowledge and reason about complex driving scenes. However, existing methods suffer from two critical limitations: a persistent misalignment between language instructions and action outputs, and the inherent inefficiency of typical auto-regressive action generation. In this paper, we introduce LinkVLA, a novel architecture that directly addresses these challenges to enhance both alignment and efficiency. First, we establish a structural link by unifying language and action tokens into a shared discrete codebook, processed within a single multi-modal model. This structurally enforces cross-modal consistency from the ground up. Second, to create a deep semantic link, we introduce an auxiliary action understanding objective that trains the model to generate descriptive captions from trajectories, fostering a bidirectional language-action mapping. Finally, we replace the slow, step-by-step generation with a two-step coarse-to-fine generation method C2F that efficiently decodes the action sequence, saving 86% inference time. Experiments on closed-loop driving benchmarks show consistent gains in instruction following accuracy and driving performance, alongside reduced inference latency.

</details>


### [227] [Deepfake Forensics Adapter: A Dual-Stream Network for Generalizable Deepfake Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01450)
*Jianfeng Liao,Yichen Wei,Raymond Chan Ching Bon,Shulan Wang,Kam-Pui Chow,Kwok-Yan Lam*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DFA is a CLIP-based, dual-stream deepfake detector that fuses global and local facial cues via adapters and a transformer fusion head, achieving state-of-the-art generalization (notably on DFDC) without updating CLIP’s weights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing deepfake detectors overfit to known artifacts and fail to generalize to new forgery patterns. Leveraging vision–language foundation models’ broad visual priors while injecting targeted forensic inductive biases could improve robustness against evolving attacks.

Method: Freeze a pre-trained CLIP backbone and add: (1) Global Feature Adapter to capture image-wide inconsistencies; (2) Local Anomaly Stream using facial-structure priors to emphasize localized forgery cues; (3) Interactive Fusion Classifier (transformer encoder) to deeply fuse global and local signals for final classification. Dual-stream design aims to combine CLIP’s generality with explicit forensics cues.

Result: Across frame- and video-level benchmarks, DFA shows superior generalization. On DFDC, it reaches frame AUC/EER 0.816/0.256 and video AUC/EER 0.836/0.251, improving video AUC by 4.8% over prior SOTA.

Conclusion: Adapting frozen foundation models with lightweight forensic adapters and structured fusion yields a robust, generalizable deepfake detector. DFA sets new SOTA on DFDC and offers a practical direction for resilient detection as deepfakes evolve; code is publicly available.

Abstract: The rapid advancement of deepfake generation techniques poses significant threats to public safety and causes societal harm through the creation of highly realistic synthetic facial media. While existing detection methods demonstrate limitations in generalizing to emerging forgery patterns, this paper presents Deepfake Forensics Adapter (DFA), a novel dual-stream framework that synergizes vision-language foundation models with targeted forensics analysis. Our approach integrates a pre-trained CLIP model with three core components to achieve specialized deepfake detection by leveraging the powerful general capabilities of CLIP without changing CLIP parameters: 1) A Global Feature Adapter is used to identify global inconsistencies in image content that may indicate forgery, 2) A Local Anomaly Stream enhances the model's ability to perceive local facial forgery cues by explicitly leveraging facial structure priors, and 3) An Interactive Fusion Classifier promotes deep interaction and fusion between global and local features using a transformer encoder. Extensive evaluations of frame-level and video-level benchmarks demonstrate the superior generalization capabilities of DFA, particularly achieving state-of-the-art performance in the challenging DFDC dataset with frame-level AUC/EER of 0.816/0.256 and video-level AUC/EER of 0.836/0.251, representing a 4.8% video AUC improvement over previous methods. Our framework not only demonstrates state-of-the-art performance, but also points out a feasible and effective direction for developing a robust deepfake detection system with enhanced generalization capabilities against the evolving deepfake threats. Our code is available at https://github.com/Liao330/DFA.git

</details>


### [228] [UltraStar: Semantic-Aware Star Graph Modeling for Echocardiography Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01461)
*Teng Wang,Haojun Jiang,Chenxi Li,Diwen Wang,Yihang Tang,Zhenguo Sun,Yujiao Deng,Shiji Song,Gao Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UltraStar reframes automated echo probe navigation as anchor-based global localization using a Star Graph of historical keyframes and semantic sampling, yielding more accurate, scalable, and noise-robust guidance than sequential path models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Skilled sonographers are scarce and echocardiography probe operation is hard. Automated navigation must leverage historical feedback, but real-world scan trajectories are noisy; sequential models overfit these paths and degrade on long sequences. A more robust way to exploit history is needed.

Method: Reformulate navigation from path regression to global localization. Build a Star Graph that connects the current view directly to selected historical keyframes treated as spatial anchors, explicitly encoding geometric constraints for precise positioning. Introduce a semantic-aware sampling strategy to pick representative landmarks from large, noisy history logs to reduce redundancy and strengthen anchoring.

Result: On a dataset exceeding 1.31 million samples, UltraStar surpasses baseline methods and maintains/improves performance as input history grows, showing better robustness to noise and scalability than sequential history modeling.

Conclusion: An anchor-based, star-topology history model with semantic sampling is more effective than chain-based sequence models for probe navigation, delivering higher accuracy and better scaling under noisy exploration, and offering practical potential for clinical deployment.

Abstract: Echocardiography is critical for diagnosing cardiovascular diseases, yet the shortage of skilled sonographers hinders timely patient care, due to high operational difficulties. Consequently, research on automated probe navigation has significant clinical potential. To achieve robust navigation, it is essential to leverage historical scanning information, mimicking how experts rely on past feedback to adjust subsequent maneuvers. Practical scanning data collected from sonographers typically consists of noisy trajectories inherently generated through trial-and-error exploration. However, existing methods typically model this history as a sequential chain, forcing models to overfit these noisy paths, leading to performance degradation on long sequences. In this paper, we propose UltraStar, which reformulates probe navigation from path regression to anchor-based global localization. By establishing a Star Graph, UltraStar treats historical keyframes as spatial anchors connected directly to the current view, explicitly modeling geometric constraints for precise positioning. We further enhance the Star Graph with a semantic-aware sampling strategy that actively selects the representative landmarks from massive history logs, reducing redundancy for accurate anchoring. Extensive experiments on a dataset with over 1.31 million samples demonstrate that UltraStar outperforms baselines and scales better with longer input lengths, revealing a more effective topology for history modeling under noisy exploration.

</details>


### [229] [physfusion: A Transformer-based Dual-Stream Radar and Vision Fusion Framework for Open Water Surface Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01947)
*Yuting Wan,Liguo Sun,Jiuwu Hao,Zao Zhang,Pin LV*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PhysFusion is a physics-informed radar–camera fusion framework that converts raw radar attributes into scattering-aware, reliability-weighted features, fuses them with visual cues at the query level, and aggregates them temporally, yielding strong, efficient water-surface target detection under challenging maritime conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Water-surface perception for USVs is hard due to wave clutter, specular reflections, and weak long-range visual cues. While mmWave radar helps in poor lighting, maritime radar point clouds are sparse/intermittent and have heavy-tailed reflectivity from scattering and multipath, making standard fusion pipelines fail to exploit radar effectively.

Method: PhysFusion comprises: (1) Physics-Informed Radar (PIR) Encoder with an RCS Mapper and Quality Gate that map per-point radar attributes to compact scattering priors and predict point reliability; (2) Radar-guided Interactive Fusion Module (RIFM) that performs query-level radar–image fusion, using a dual-stream radar backbone (point-based local stream + transformer-based global stream with Scattering-Aware Self-Attention); (3) Temporal Query Aggregation (TQA) to fuse frame-wise queries over a short window for temporal consistency. The model is lightweight (5.6M params, 12.5G FLOPs) and can use T=5 radar history.

Result: On WaterScenes with T=5, PhysFusion achieves 59.7% mAP50:95 and 90.3% mAP50; on FLOW (radar+camera), it attains 94.8% mAP50 and 46.2% mAP50:95. Ablations show gains from PIR Encoder, SASA-based global reasoning, and RIFM.

Conclusion: Incorporating scattering physics and reliability estimation into radar encoding, coupled with radar-guided query fusion and short-term temporal aggregation, enables robust and accurate maritime target detection with low computation. This design effectively leverages sparse, heavy-tailed radar cues and complements vision under degraded conditions.

Abstract: Detecting water-surface targets for Unmanned Surface Vehicles (USVs) is challenging due to wave clutter,
  specular reflections, and weak appearance cues in long-range observations. Although 4D millimeter-wave
  radar complements cameras under degraded illumination, maritime radar point clouds are sparse and
  intermittent, with reflectivity attributes exhibiting heavy-tailed variations under scattering and
  multipath, making conventional fusion designs struggle to exploit radar cues effectively.
  We propose PhysFusion, a physics-informed radar-image detection framework for water-surface perception.
  The framework integrates: (1) a Physics-Informed Radar Encoder (PIR Encoder) with an RCS Mapper and
  Quality Gate, transforming per-point radar attributes into compact scattering priors and predicting
  point-wise reliability for robust feature learning under clutter; (2) a Radar-guided Interactive Fusion
  Module (RIFM) performing query-level radar-image fusion between semantically enriched radar features and
  multi-scale visual features, with the radar branch modeled by a dual-stream backbone including a
  point-based local stream and a transformer-based global stream using Scattering-Aware Self-Attention
  (SASA); and (3) a Temporal Query Aggregation module (TQA) aggregating frame-wise fused queries over a
  short temporal window for temporally consistent representations.
  Experiments on WaterScenes and FLOW demonstrate that PhysFusion achieves 59.7% mAP50:95 and 90.3% mAP50
  on WaterScenes (T=5 radar history) using 5.6M parameters and 12.5G FLOPs, and reaches 94.8% mAP50 and
  46.2% mAP50:95 on FLOW under radar+camera setting. Ablation studies quantify the contributions of PIR
  Encoder, SASA-based global reasoning, and RIFM.

</details>


### [230] [WildCross: A Cross-Modal Large Scale Benchmark for Place Recognition and Metric Depth Estimation in Natural Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01475)
*Joshua Knights,Joseph Reid,Kaushik Roy,David Hall,Mark Cox,Peyman Moghadam*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: WildCross introduces a large-scale, cross-modal benchmark focused on unstructured natural environments, providing 476K+ RGB frames with semi-dense depth and surface normals, accurate 6DoF poses, and synchronized dense lidar submaps, and demonstrates its utility for visual/lidar/cross-modal place recognition and metric depth estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robotics is moving into unstructured natural settings, but most available datasets are urban and structured. There is a need to bridge 2D–3D scene understanding and evaluate multimodal perception specifically in complex natural environments.

Method: Construct a dataset/benchmark collected in large-scale natural areas: sequential RGB video with semi-dense depth and surface normal annotations, per-frame accurate 6DoF poses, and synchronized dense lidar submaps. Define and run benchmark tasks for visual, lidar, and cross-modal place recognition, as well as metric depth estimation.

Result: Delivered a dataset of >476K frames with rich annotations and alignment across modalities. Comprehensive experiments establish baselines and show the benchmark is challenging and informative across visual, lidar, and cross-modal place recognition and metric depth tasks.

Conclusion: WildCross fills a key gap by enabling rigorous, multimodal evaluation in unstructured natural environments and serves as a strong, publicly available benchmark for place recognition and metric depth estimation across sensing modalities.

Abstract: Recent years have seen a significant increase in demand for robotic solutions in unstructured natural environments, alongside growing interest in bridging 2D and 3D scene understanding. However, existing robotics datasets are predominantly captured in structured urban environments, making them inadequate for addressing the challenges posed by complex, unstructured natural settings. To address this gap, we propose WildCross, a cross-modal benchmark for place recognition and metric depth estimation in large-scale natural environments. WildCross comprises over 476K sequential RGB frames with semi-dense depth and surface normal annotations, each aligned with accurate 6DoF poses and synchronized dense lidar submaps. We conduct comprehensive experiments on visual, lidar, and cross-modal place recognition, as well as metric depth estimation, demonstrating the value of WildCross as a challenging benchmark for multi-modal robotic perception tasks. We provide access to the code repository and dataset at https://csiro-robotics.github.io/WildCross.

</details>


### [231] [SCATR: Mitigating New Instance Suppression in LiDAR-based Tracking-by-Attention via Second Chance Assignment and Track Query Dropout](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01485)
*Brian Cheong,Letian Wang,Sandro Papais,Steven L. Waslander*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SCATR is a LiDAR tracking-by-attention model that introduces two architecture-agnostic training strategies—Second Chance Assignment and Track Query Dropout—to reduce false negatives and make TBA competitive with tracking-by-detection, achieving state-of-the-art results on nuScenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LiDAR-based tracking-by-attention (TBA) trails tracking-by-detection (TBD) due to high false negatives and a conflict between detection and tracking objectives. There is a need for training strategies tailored to LiDAR TBA that improve assignment to ground truth and robustness to missing/newborn tracks.

Method: Two training strategies, applicable across TBA architectures: (1) Second Chance Assignment (SCA) augments bipartite matching by appending unassigned track queries to proposal queries, giving them another opportunity to match ground-truth objects and alleviating competition between detection and tracking. (2) Track Query Dropout (TQD) varies the supervised object query set during training so the decoder learns to cope with differing track-query compositions, improving robustness to absent or newly born tracks.

Result: On the nuScenes tracking benchmark, SCATR sets a new state of the art among LiDAR-based TBA methods, improving AMOTA by 7.6% over prior work and closing the historical performance gap between LiDAR TBA and TBD. Ablations confirm the effectiveness and generalization of SCA and TQD. Code is released.

Conclusion: Strategic, architecture-agnostic training—via improved ground-truth assignment and query-set diversification—substantially lowers false negatives in LiDAR TBA, yielding state-of-the-art performance and narrowing the gap with TBD. The approaches appear general and practical for broader TBA models.

Abstract: LiDAR-based tracking-by-attention (TBA) frameworks inherently suffer from high false negative errors, leading to a significant performance gap compared to traditional LiDAR-based tracking-by-detection (TBD) methods. This paper introduces SCATR, a novel LiDAR-based TBA model designed to address this fundamental challenge systematically. SCATR leverages recent progress in vision-based tracking and incorporates targeted training strategies specifically adapted for LiDAR.
  Our work's core innovations are two architecture-agnostic training strategies for TBA methods: Second Chance Assignment and Track Query Dropout.
  Second Chance Assignment is a novel ground truth assignment that concatenates unassigned track queries to the proposal queries before bipartite matching, giving these track queries a second chance to be assigned to a ground truth object and effectively mitigating the conflict between detection and tracking tasks inherent in tracking-by-attention.
  Track Query Dropout is a training method that diversifies supervised object query configurations to efficiently train the decoder to handle different track query sets, enhancing robustness to missing or newborn tracks.
  Experiments on the nuScenes tracking benchmark demonstrate that SCATR achieves state-of-the-art performance among LiDAR-based TBA methods, outperforming previous works by 7.6\% AMOTA and successfully bridging the long-standing performance gap between LiDAR-based TBA and TBD methods.
  Ablation studies further validate the effectiveness and generalization of Second Chance Assignment and Track Query Dropout.
  Code can be found at the following link: \href{https://github.com/TRAILab/SCATR}{https://github.com/TRAILab/SCATR}

</details>


### [232] [MAP-Diff: Multi-Anchor Guided Diffusion for Progressive 3D Whole-Body Low-Dose PET Denoising](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02012)
*Peiyuan Jing,Chun-Wun Cheng,Liutao Yang,Zhenxuan Zhang,Thiago V. Lima,Klaus Strobel,Antoine Leimgruber,Angelica Aviles-Rivero,Guang Yang,Javier A. Montoya-Zegarra*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MAP-Diff is a diffusion-based, multi-anchor guided framework that uses clinically acquired intermediate-dose PET scans to constrain the reverse diffusion trajectory, producing progressive, dose-aligned 3D whole-body reconstructions from ultra-low-dose input and outperforming strong baselines while generalizing across scanners.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Lowering PET dose reduces radiation but introduces heavy noise and harms quantitative accuracy. Conventional diffusion models generate unconstrained reverse trajectories that do not reflect how image quality improves with increasing dose. There is a need for dose-consistent, progressive denoising that preserves quantitation and generalizes across scanners.

Method: Introduce multi-anchor guidance: incorporate clinically observed intermediate-dose PET scans as trajectory anchors and apply timestep-dependent supervision to align reverse diffusion with dose progression. Calibrate anchor timesteps by matching diffusion corruption levels to real multi-dose PET pairs. Use a timestep-weighted anchor loss to stabilize stage-wise learning. At inference, require only ultra-low-dose input and output progressive, dose-aligned intermediate restorations.

Result: Internal dataset (Siemens Biograph Vision Quadra): PSNR improves from 42.48 dB to 43.71 dB (+1.23 dB vs 3D DDPM), SSIM reaches 0.986, NMAE drops from 0.115 to 0.103. External dataset (United Imaging uEXPLORER): 34.42 dB PSNR and 0.141 NMAE, outperforming CNN-, Transformer-, GAN-, and diffusion-based competitors.

Conclusion: Constraining diffusion with real intermediate-dose anchors yields dose-consistent, progressive PET denoising from ultra-low-dose input, improving fidelity and quantitative metrics, with demonstrated cross-scanner generalization.

Abstract: Low-dose Positron Emission Tomography (PET) reduces radiation exposure but suffers from severe noise and quantitative degradation. Diffusion-based denoising models achieve strong final reconstructions, yet their reverse trajectories are typically unconstrained and not aligned with the progressive nature of PET dose formation. We propose MAP-Diff, a multi-anchor guided diffusion framework for progressive 3D whole-body PET denoising. MAP-Diff introduces clinically observed intermediate-dose scans as trajectory anchors and enforces timestep-dependent supervision to regularize the reverse process toward dose-aligned intermediate states. Anchor timesteps are calibrated via degradation matching between simulated diffusion corruption and real multi-dose PET pairs, and a timestep-weighted anchor loss stabilizes stage-wise learning. At inference, the model requires only ultra-low-dose input while enabling progressive, dose-consistent intermediate restoration. Experiments on internal (Siemens Biograph Vision Quadra) and cross-scanner (United Imaging uEXPLORER) datasets show consistent improvements over strong CNN-, Transformer-, GAN-, and diffusion-based baselines. On the internal dataset, MAP-Diff improves PSNR from 42.48 dB to 43.71 dB (+1.23 dB), increases SSIM to 0.986, and reduces NMAE from 0.115 to 0.103 (-0.012) compared to 3D DDPM. Performance gains generalize across scanners, achieving 34.42 dB PSNR and 0.141 NMAE on the external cohort, outperforming all competing methods.

</details>


### [233] [Radiometrically Consistent Gaussian Surfels for Inverse Rendering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01491)
*Kyu Beom Han,Jaeyoon Kim,Woo Jae Kim,Jinhwan Seo,Sung-eui Yoon*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RadioGS introduces radiometric consistency for Gaussian surfels to correctly disentangle material properties from indirect illumination, creating a self-correcting supervision loop that combines physically based rendering with novel-view synthesis, yielding fast, accurate inverse rendering and quick relighting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pre-trained Gaussian primitives used for indirect radiance are supervised only from limited training viewpoints, leading to poor modeling of indirect illumination from unobserved views and entangled material/lighting estimates.

Method: Impose a physically based constraint—radiometric consistency—by minimizing the residual between each Gaussian primitive’s learned radiance and its physically based rendered counterpart, including for unobserved views. Implement this with Gaussian surfels and efficient 2D Gaussian ray tracing; add a finetuning-based relighting step that adapts surfel radiances to new lights within minutes at low rendering cost.

Result: On inverse rendering benchmarks, RadioGS surpasses prior Gaussian-based methods in accuracy (especially for inter-reflections) while retaining efficiency; relighting adapts in minutes and renders in under ~10 ms per view.

Conclusion: Physically grounded radiometric consistency provides missing supervision for unobserved views, enabling better disentanglement of materials and global illumination. RadioGS delivers state-of-the-art inverse rendering with efficient rendering and practical, fast relighting.

Abstract: Inverse rendering with Gaussian Splatting has advanced rapidly, but accurately disentangling material properties from complex global illumination effects, particularly indirect illumination, remains a major challenge. Existing methods often query indirect radiance from Gaussian primitives pre-trained for novel-view synthesis. However, these pre-trained Gaussian primitives are supervised only towards limited training viewpoints, thus lack supervision for modeling indirect radiances from unobserved views. To address this issue, we introduce radiometric consistency, a novel physically-based constraint that provides supervision towards unobserved views by minimizing the residual between each Gaussian primitive's learned radiance and its physically-based rendered counterpart. Minimizing the residual for unobserved views establishes a self-correcting feedback loop that provides supervision from both physically-based rendering and novel-view synthesis, enabling accurate modeling of inter-reflection. We then propose Radiometrically Consistent Gaussian Surfels (RadioGS), an inverse rendering framework built upon our principle by efficiently integrating radiometric consistency by utilizing Gaussian surfels and 2D Gaussian ray tracing. We further propose a finetuning-based relighting strategy that adapts Gaussian surfel radiances to new illuminations within minutes, achieving low rendering cost (<10ms). Extensive experiments on existing inverse rendering benchmarks show that RadioGS outperforms existing Gaussian-based methods in inverse rendering, while retaining the computational efficiency.

</details>


### [234] [Tri-path DINO: Feature Complementary Learning for Remote Sensing Multi-Class Change Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01498)
*Kai Zheng,Hang-Cheng Dong,Zhenkai Wu,Fupeng Wei,Wei Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Tripath DINO is a three-path architecture for multi-class change detection that adapts a DINOv3 backbone for coarse semantics, adds a siamese auxiliary path for fine details, and uses a multi-scale attention decoder; it achieves state-of-the-art results on Gaza Change and SECOND, with Grad-CAM showing complementary coarse/fine focus for interpretable, rapid damage assessment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-class change detection in remote sensing is hard due to complex scene variations and limited fine-grained annotations; there is a need to rapidly adapt powerful foundation models to domain-specific tasks while capturing both coarse semantic shifts and fine structural changes.

Method: A complementary three-path design: (1) main path uses DINOv3 pretrained features for coarse-grained representations; (2) auxiliary siamese path aggregates intermediate encoder features to enhance fine-grained change cues; (3) a decoder with multi-scale attention via parallel convolutions to capture context at multiple receptive fields; outputs multi-class changes; interpretability via Grad-CAM.

Result: Claims optimal (state-of-the-art) performance on Gaza facility damage assessment (Gaza Change) and SECOND datasets; qualitative Grad-CAM indicates main vs. auxiliary paths emphasize semantic vs. structural changes, respectively.

Conclusion: The synergistic coarse–fine complementarity plus multi-scale attention yields a robust and interpretable MCD system, enabling rapid, accurate change/damage assessment and demonstrating effective adaptation of foundation models to complex remote-sensing domains.

Abstract: In remote sensing imagery, multi class change detection (MCD) is crucial for fine grained monitoring, yet it has long been constrained by complex scene variations and the scarcity of detailed annotations. To address this, we propose the Tripath DINO architecture, which adopts a three path complementary feature learning strategy to facilitate the rapid adaptation of pre trained foundation models to complex vertical domains. Specifically, we employ the DINOv3 pre trained model as the backbone feature extraction network to learn coarse grained features. An auxiliary path also adopts a siamese structure, progressively aggregating intermediate features from the siamese encoder to enhance the learning of fine grained features. Finally, a multi scale attention mechanism is introduced to augment the decoder network, where parallel convolutions adaptively capture and enhance contextual information under different receptive fields. The proposed method achieves optimal performance on the MCD task on both the Gaza facility damage assessment dataset (Gaza change) and the classic SECOND dataset. GradCAM visualizations further confirm that the main and auxiliary paths naturally focus on coarse grained semantic changes and fine grained structural details, respectively. This synergistic complementarity provides a robust and interpretable solution for advanced change detection tasks, offering a basis for rapid and accurate damage assessment.

</details>


### [235] [OMG-Avatar: One-shot Multi-LOD Gaussian Head Avatar](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01506)
*Jianqiang Ren,Lin Liu,Steven Hoi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OMG-Avatar is a one-shot, multi-LOD Gaussian-based system that reconstructs an animatable 3D head (with shoulders) from a single image in ~0.2s, fusing global and local cues via depth-guided integration and trained coarse-to-fine to deliver SOTA quality, reenactment fidelity, and efficiency across hardware tiers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable fast, high-quality, and hardware-adaptive 3D head avatar creation from a single image; overcome 3DMM limitations (especially non-head regions like shoulders); maintain occlusion plausibility and hierarchical detail across levels of detail (LOD).

Method: Use a unified Multi-LOD Gaussian representation for the avatar. Extract global facial features with a transformer and local details via projection-based sampling; fuse them with a depth buffer to respect occlusions. Train with a coarse-to-fine paradigm to realize LOD and enhance hierarchical detail. Decompose the model into head and shoulder regions, predicting them separately and integrating via cross-region combination to address 3DMM shortcomings.

Result: Achieves single-image reconstruction and animation in ~0.2 seconds. Extensive experiments show consistent improvements over prior methods in reconstruction quality, reenactment performance, and computational efficiency, with adaptable LOD for varying device capabilities and speed requirements.

Conclusion: A unified, multi-LOD Gaussian framework enables rapid, high-fidelity, and hardware-adaptive 3D head-and-shoulders avatars from a single image, improving over SOTA in accuracy and speed while addressing non-head modeling and occlusion handling.

Abstract: We propose OMG-Avatar, a novel One-shot method that leverages a Multi-LOD (Level-of-Detail) Gaussian representation for animatable 3D head reconstruction from a single image in 0.2s. Our method enables LOD head avatar modeling using a unified model that accommodates diverse hardware capabilities and inference speed requirements. To capture both global and local facial characteristics, we employ a transformer-based architecture for global feature extraction and projection-based sampling for local feature acquisition. These features are effectively fused under the guidance of a depth buffer, ensuring occlusion plausibility. We further introduce a coarse-to-fine learning paradigm to support Level-of-Detail functionality and enhance the perception of hierarchical details. To address the limitations of 3DMMs in modeling non-head regions such as the shoulders, we introduce a multi-region decomposition scheme in which the head and shoulders are predicted separately and then integrated through cross-region combination. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OMG-Avatar outperforms state-of-the-art methods in reconstruction quality, reenactment performance, and computational efficiency.

</details>


### [236] [Detection-Gated Glottal Segmentation with Zero-Shot Cross-Dataset Transfer and Clinical Feature Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02087)
*Harikrishnan Unnikrishnan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A detection-gated YOLOv8 + U-Net pipeline with temporal consistency delivers robust, real-time glottal segmentation in HSV, generalizing zero-shot from a small GIRAFE training set to BAGLS and enabling reliable biomarker extraction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate, artifact-free glottal segmentation is critical for deriving kinematic biomarkers, yet existing models yield false positives in non-glottal frames and struggle to generalize across clinics and devices.

Method: Integrate a YOLOv8 detector to gate a U-Net segmenter; add a temporal-consistency wrapper to suppress false positives during glottal closure and instrument occlusion. Train on 600 GIRAFE frames; evaluate zero-shot on BAGLS; validate biomarkers (Open Quotient, coefficient of variation) on a 65-subject clinical cohort.

Result: State-of-the-art on GIRAFE (DSC 0.81). Strong generalization to BAGLS without institutional fine-tuning (DSC 0.85). Downstream biomarkers matched clinical benchmarks; glottal area CV significantly differentiated healthy vs pathological voices (p=0.006).

Conclusion: A lightweight (~35 fps), deployment-ready approach that supports standardized, large-scale biomarker extraction across diverse HSV platforms; code and weights released for reproducibility.

Abstract: Background: Accurate glottal segmentation in high-speed videoendoscopy (HSV) is essential for extracting kinematic biomarkers of laryngeal function. However, existing deep learning models often produce spurious artifacts in non-glottal frames and fail to generalize across different clinical settings.
  Methods: We propose a detection-gated pipeline that integrates a YOLOv8-based detector with a U-Net segmenter. A temporal consistency wrapper ensures robustness by suppressing false positives during glottal closure and instrument occlusion. The model was trained on a limited subset of the GIRAFE dataset (600 frames) and evaluated via zero-shot transfer on the large-scale BAGLS dataset.
  Results: The pipeline achieved state-of-the-art performance on the GIRAFE benchmark (DSC 0.81) and demonstrated superior generalizability on BAGLS (DSC 0.85, in-distribution) without institutional fine-tuning. Downstream validation on a 65-subject clinical cohort confirmed that automated kinematic features (Open Quotient, coefficient of variation) remained consistent with established clinical benchmarks. The coefficient of variation (CV) of the glottal area was found to be a significant marker for distinguishing healthy from pathological vocal function (p=0.006).
  Conclusions: The detection-gated architecture provides a lightweight, computationally efficient solution (~35 frames/s) for real-time clinical use. By enabling robust zero-shot transfer, this framework facilitates the standardized, large-scale extraction of clinical biomarkers across diverse endoscopy platforms. Code, trained weights, and evaluation scripts are released at https://github.com/hari-krishnan/openglottal.

</details>


### [237] [FluxMem: Adaptive Hierarchical Memory for Streaming Video Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02096)
*Yiweng Xie,Bo He,Junke Wang,Xiangyu Zheng,Ziyi Ye,Zuxuan Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FluxMem is a training‑free, hierarchical token-compression framework for streaming video that adaptively prunes temporal redundancy and consolidates spatially repetitive regions, delivering state‑of‑the‑art accuracy with large reductions in latency and GPU memory, while preserving strong offline performance with far fewer visual tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Streaming video understanding suffers from heavy temporal and spatial redundancy, which inflates latency and GPU memory and often requires hand‑tuned compression heuristics or retraining to stay real‑time. The goal is a plug‑and‑play approach that automatically adapts compression to scene dynamics without sacrificing accuracy.

Method: A two‑stage, training‑free design: (1) Temporal Adjacency Selection (TAS) drops redundant visual tokens across adjacent frames; (2) Spatial Domain Consolidation (SDC) merges spatially repetitive regions within each frame. Both modules incorporate self‑adaptive token compression that sets rates from intrinsic scene statistics rather than manual tuning, yielding compact visual memory for online inference.

Result: On online video benchmarks, FluxMem reaches 76.4 on StreamingBench and 67.2 on OVO‑Bench in real‑time settings, while cutting latency by 69.9% and peak GPU memory by 34.5% on OVO‑Bench. It also maintains strong offline performance: 73.1 on MLVU using 65% fewer visual tokens.

Conclusion: Adaptive, hierarchical token compression can effectively eliminate temporal and spatial redundancy in streaming video without retraining, achieving state‑of‑the‑art accuracy with substantial efficiency gains and preserving performance in offline scenarios with significantly fewer tokens.

Abstract: This paper presents FluxMem, a training-free framework for efficient streaming video understanding. FluxMem adaptively compresses redundant visual memory through a hierarchical, two-stage design: (1) a Temporal Adjacency Selection (TAS) module removes redundant visual tokens across adjacent frames, and (2) a Spatial Domain Consolidation (SDC) module further merges spatially repetitive regions within each frame into compact representations. To adapt effectively to dynamic scenes, we introduce a self-adaptive token compression mechanism in both TAS and SDC, which automatically determines the compression rate based on intrinsic scene statistics rather than manual tuning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FluxMem achieves new state-of-the-art results on existing online video benchmarks, reaching 76.4 on StreamingBench and 67.2 on OVO-Bench under real-time settings, while reducing latency by 69.9% and peak GPU memory by 34.5% on OVO-Bench. Furthermore, it maintains strong offline performance, achieving 73.1 on MLVU while using 65% fewer visual tokens.

</details>


### [238] [FACE: A Face-based Autoregressive Representation for High-Fidelity and Efficient Mesh Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01515)
*Hanxiao Wang,Yuan-Chen Guo,Ying-Tian Liu,Zi-Xin Zou,Biao Zhang,Weize Quan,Ding Liang,Yan-Pei Cao,Dong-Ming Yan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FACE is an autoregressive autoencoder that models 3D meshes at the triangle-face level (one face = one token), cutting sequence length 9× and achieving 0.11 compression while maintaining or improving quality, enabling SOTA reconstruction and high-fidelity single-image-to-mesh via latent diffusion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Flattening meshes into long vertex-coordinate sequences makes autoregressive generation computationally prohibitive and semantically misaligned with mesh structure.

Method: Treat each triangle face as a single token in an ARAE: a face-level autoregressive decoder paired with a strong VecSet encoder. Learn a compact latent space and train a latent diffusion model on it for image-to-mesh generation.

Result: Sequence length reduced by factor of nine; compression ratio of 0.11 (about 2× better than prior SOTA). Achieves state-of-the-art mesh reconstruction on standard benchmarks and delivers high-fidelity single-image-to-mesh results using the latent diffusion model.

Conclusion: Operating at the face level yields a simple, scalable, and efficient paradigm for structured 3D content creation, removing computational bottlenecks without sacrificing quality.

Abstract: Autoregressive models for 3D mesh generation suffer from a fundamental limitation: they flatten meshes into long vertex-coordinate sequences. This results in prohibitive computational costs, hindering the efficient synthesis of high-fidelity geometry. We argue this bottleneck stems from operating at the wrong semantic level. We introduce FACE, a novel Autoregressive Autoencoder (ARAE) framework that reconceptualizes the task by generating meshes at the face level. Our one-face-one-token strategy treats each triangle face, the fundamental building block of a mesh, as a single, unified token. This simple yet powerful design reduces the sequence length by a factor of nine, leading to an unprecedented compression ratio of 0.11, halving the previous state-of-the-art. This dramatic efficiency gain does not compromise quality; by pairing our face-level decoder with a powerful VecSet encoder, FACE achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction quality on standard benchmarks. The versatility of the learned latent space is further demonstrated by training a latent diffusion model that achieves high-fidelity, single-image-to-mesh generation. FACE provides a simple, scalable, and powerful paradigm that lowers the barrier to high-quality structured 3D content creation.

</details>


### [239] [Better Matching, Less Forgetting: A Quality-Guided Matcher for Transformer-based Incremental Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01524)
*Qirui Wu,Shizhou Zhang,De Cheng,Yinghui Xing,Lingyan Ran,Dahu Shi,Peng Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Identifies a DETR-specific forgetting source—“background foregrounding” from the Hungarian matcher’s forced assignments—and proposes a Quality-guided Min-Cost Max-Flow (Q-MCMF) matcher that avoids harmful matches, yielding consistent SOTA gains on COCO in incremental object detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Incremental Object Detection suffers from catastrophic forgetting. While dense detectors use pseudo-labeling to mitigate background shift, DETR-like models face an additional issue: the Hungarian matcher’s exhaustiveness forces low-IoU, background-heavy predictions to be assigned to ground truths, supervising background as foreground and corrupting learned representations.

Method: Replace the exhaustive Hungarian matching with a Quality-guided Min-Cost Max-Flow (Q-MCMF) matcher. Build a flow graph between predictions and ground truths, prune implausible edges using geometric quality (e.g., IoU/box overlap), then solve a min-cost max-flow to both minimize assignment cost and maximize the number of valid, high-quality matches—explicitly allowing unmatched ground truths to prevent erroneous supervision.

Result: On COCO, across multiple incremental learning schedules, the proposed matcher consistently outperforms prior SOTA, indicating reduced forgetting and stronger learning of new classes. (Abstract does not report exact numbers.)

Conclusion: Avoiding forced, low-quality assignments in DETR-like architectures removes harmful supervision that accelerates forgetting. Q-MCMF preserves prior knowledge while enabling learning of new classes, offering a principled and effective replacement for Hungarian matching in incremental settings.

Abstract: Incremental Object Detection (IOD) aims to continuously learn new object classes without forgetting previously learned ones. A persistent challenge is catastrophic forgetting, primarily attributed to background shift in conventional detectors. While pseudo-labeling mitigates this in dense detectors, we identify a novel, distinct source of forgetting specific to DETR-like architectures: background foregrounding. This arises from the exhaustiveness constraint of the Hungarian matcher, which forcibly assigns every ground truth target to one prediction, even when predictions primarily cover background regions (i.e., low IoU). This erroneous supervision compels the model to misclassify background features as specific foreground classes, disrupting learned representations and accelerating forgetting. To address this, we propose a Quality-guided Min-Cost Max-Flow (Q-MCMF) matcher. To avoid forced assignments, Q-MCMF builds a flow graph and prunes implausible matches based on geometric quality. It then optimizes for the final matching that minimizes cost and maximizes valid assignments. This strategy eliminates harmful supervision from background foregrounding while maximizing foreground learning signals. Extensive experiments on the COCO dataset under various incremental settings demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches.

</details>


### [240] [LiftAvatar: Kinematic-Space Completion for Expression-Controlled 3D Gaussian Avatar Animation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02129)
*Hualiang Wei,Shunran Jia,Jialun Liu,Wenhui Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LiftAvatar is a plug-and-play video diffusion Transformer that “lifts” sparse monocular kinematic cues (expressions, head pose) into rich, temporally coherent control signals to drive higher-fidelity, more controllable 3D avatar animation—especially under extreme, unseen expressions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Monocular videos provide sparse, noisy kinematic cues that cause limited expressiveness and reconstruction artifacts in 3D Gaussian Splatting–based avatars. There is a need for precise, stable, and controllable expression driving and for leveraging priors from large video generative models in 3D avatar pipelines.

Method: A fine-grained, expression-controllable large-scale video diffusion Transformer that synthesizes coherent expression sequences from one or multiple reference images. Two key mechanisms: (i) multi-granularity expression control combining shading maps with expression coefficients for precise, stable driving; (ii) multi-reference conditioning that aggregates complementary cues across frames for strong 3D consistency and controllability. The model completes/expands sparse inputs in kinematic space and feeds them to downstream 3D avatar methods as enhanced control signals.

Result: Extensive experiments show consistent boosts in animation quality and quantitative metrics for state-of-the-art 3D avatar methods, with notable gains in temporal coherence, 3D consistency, and robustness to extreme or previously unseen expressions. It also enables effective prior distillation from large-scale video generative models into 3D pipelines.

Conclusion: Lifting sparse monocular cues into a richer kinematic representation via a controllable video diffusion Transformer substantially improves reconstruction and animation in 3D avatar pipelines. LiftAvatar serves as a general, plug-and-play enhancer that strengthens expressiveness, stability, and controllability, particularly in challenging expression regimes.

Abstract: We present LiftAvatar, a new paradigm that completes sparse monocular observations in kinematic space (e.g., facial expressions and head pose) and uses the completed signals to drive high-fidelity avatar animation. LiftAvatar is a fine-grained, expression-controllable large-scale video diffusion Transformer that synthesizes high-quality, temporally coherent expression sequences conditioned on single or multiple reference images. The key idea is to lift incomplete input data into a richer kinematic representation, thereby strengthening both reconstruction and animation in downstream 3D avatar pipelines. To this end, we introduce (i) a multi-granularity expression control scheme that combines shading maps with expression coefficients for precise and stable driving, and (ii) a multi-reference conditioning mechanism that aggregates complementary cues from multiple frames, enabling strong 3D consistency and controllability. As a plug-and-play enhancer, LiftAvatar directly addresses the limited expressiveness and reconstruction artifacts of 3D Gaussian Splatting-based avatars caused by sparse kinematic cues in everyday monocular videos. By expanding incomplete observations into diverse pose-expression variations, LiftAvatar also enables effective prior distillation from large-scale video generative models into 3D pipelines, leading to substantial gains. Extensive experiments show that LiftAvatar consistently boosts animation quality and quantitative metrics of state-of-the-art 3D avatar methods, especially under extreme, unseen expressions.

</details>


### [241] [Boosting AI Reliability with an FSM-Driven Streaming Inference Pipeline: An Industrial Case](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01528)
*Yutian Zhang,Zhongyi Pei,Yi Mao,Chen Wang,Lin Liu,Jianmin Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Hybrid streaming pipeline that fuses an object detector with a knowledge-encoded finite state machine to robustly count excavator workloads in surveillance video, outperforming a heuristic baseline on a 7k-image, 12-video real-world dataset (300+ workloads).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial AI systems often fail under scenarios not seen in training, causing biased or brittle predictions. The authors aim to boost robustness by injecting explicit prior operational knowledge into inference for a practical task: counting excavator workloads from video.

Method: A streaming inference pipeline integrates a data-driven object detection model with a Finite State Machine that encodes operational scenarios. The FSM guides and corrects per-frame detector outputs over time to track workload events in streaming video. Baseline is a manual heuristic-rule solution.

Result: On a real-world dataset (>7,000 images from 12 site videos; >300 workloads), the proposed approach achieves better accuracy and robustness than the manual heuristic baseline. Code is to be released at the provided GitHub URL.

Conclusion: Embedding prior knowledge via an FSM into streaming inference improves robustness and accuracy for industrial video analytics. The approach appears practical and potentially generalizable to similar temporal event-counting tasks in noisy real-world settings.

Abstract: The widespread adoption of AI in industry is often hampered by its limited robustness when faced with scenarios absent from training data, leading to prediction bias and vulnerabilities. To address this, we propose a novel streaming inference pipeline that enhances data-driven models by explicitly incorporating prior knowledge. This paper presents the work on an industrial AI application that automatically counts excavator workloads from surveillance videos. Our approach integrates an object detection model with a Finite State Machine (FSM), which encodes knowledge of operational scenarios to guide and correct the AI's predictions on streaming data. In experiments on a real-world dataset of over 7,000 images from 12 site videos, encompassing more than 300 excavator workloads, our method demonstrates superior performance and greater robustness compared to the original solution based on manual heuristic rules. We will release the code at https://github.com/thulab/video-streamling-inference-pipeline.

</details>


### [242] [Benchmarking Semantic Segmentation Models via Appearance and Geometry Attribute Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01535)
*Zijin Yin,Bing Li,Kongming Liang,Hao Sun,Zhongjiang He,Zhanyu Ma,Jun Guo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Gen4Seg is a diffusion-based pipeline that edits visual attributes of real images to stress-test semantic segmentation under controllable appearance and geometric changes, enabling label reuse. It yields two benchmarks (Pascal-EA, COCO-EA), reveals robustness gaps (especially for open-vocabulary models under geometry), shows limits of common augmentations, and improves ID/OOD performance when used for augmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deployed segmentation models face varied, complex conditions, yet existing evaluations largely cover only global style/weather changes and lack fine-grained object-level and geometric variations. Creating new labeled data is expensive; a scalable way to generate challenging, labeled test cases is needed.

Method: Use diffusion-based editing to modify object- and image-level attributes (color, material, size, position, weather, style) of real images while preserving structural information so original segmentation labels remain valid. Automatically generate diverse, challenging samples and assemble two evaluation suites (Pascal-EA and COCO-EA). Benchmark closed-set and open-vocabulary segmentation models; also use generated data as augmentation to assess training gains.

Result: Two benchmarks are introduced. Key findings: (1) Advanced open-vocabulary models are not more robust than closed-set models under geometric variations. (2) Popular augmentations (CutOut, CutMix) provide limited robustness gains for appearance shifts. (3) Using Gen4Seg-generated data as augmentation improves both in-distribution and out-of-distribution performance.

Conclusion: Generative diffusion models can systematically probe and strengthen segmentation models. Gen4Seg offers a practical route to robustness analysis and data augmentation, encouraging evaluations that include controlled appearance and geometric changes to build more reliable segmentation systems.

Abstract: Semantic segmentation takes pivotal roles in various applications such as autonomous driving and medical image analysis. When deploying segmentation models in practice, it is critical to test their behaviors in varied and complex scenes in advance. In this paper, we construct an automatic data generation pipeline Gen4Seg to stress-test semantic segmentation models by generating various challenging samples with different attribute changes. Beyond previous evaluation paradigms focusing solely on global weather and style transfer, we investigate variations in both appearance and geometry attributes at the object and image level. These include object color, material, size, position, as well as image-level variations such as weather and style. To achieve this, we propose to edit visual attributes of existing real images with precise control of structural information, empowered by diffusion models. In this way, the existing segmentation labels can be reused for the edited images, which greatly reduces the labor costs. Using our pipeline, we construct two new benchmarks, Pascal-EA and COCO-EA. We benchmark a wide variety of semantic segmentation models, spanning from closed-set models to open-vocabulary large models. We have several key findings: 1) advanced open-vocabulary models do not exhibit greater robustness compared to closed-set methods under geometric variations; 2) data augmentation techniques, such as CutOut and CutMix, are limited in enhancing robustness against appearance variations; 3) our pipeline can also be employed as a data augmentation tool and improve both in-distribution and out-of-distribution performances. Our work suggests the potential of generative models as effective tools for automatically analyzing segmentation models, and we hope our findings will assist practitioners and researchers in developing more robust and reliable segmentation models.

</details>


### [243] [Kiwi-Edit: Versatile Video Editing via Instruction and Reference Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02175)
*Yiqi Lin,Guoqiang Liang,Ziyun Zeng,Zechen Bai,Yanzhe Chen,Mike Zheng Shou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They build a scalable pipeline to synthesize high-quality reference-conditioned training data (forming RefVIE), introduce an evaluation benchmark (RefVIE-Bench), and propose Kiwi-Edit—a unified model using learnable queries and latent visual features with a progressive training curriculum—achieving state-of-the-art controllable, instruction- and reference-following video editing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Instruction-only video editing lacks precise visual control because natural language cannot capture fine-grained visual nuances. Reference-guided editing could fix this but is limited by the lack of large, high-quality paired training data.

Method: A data-generation pipeline converts existing video editing pairs into high-fidelity quadruplets by synthesizing reference scaffolds with image generative models. They curate the large-scale RefVIE dataset for instruction-reference following and create RefVIE-Bench for evaluation. They introduce Kiwi-Edit, which fuses instruction and reference via learnable queries and latent visual features, trained with a progressive multi-stage curriculum to improve instruction adherence and reference fidelity.

Result: Extensive experiments show significant gains in instruction following and reference fidelity, establishing new state-of-the-art performance in controllable video editing. All data, models, and code are released.

Conclusion: By removing the data bottleneck for reference-guided editing and introducing a unified, well-trained architecture, the work advances precise, controllable video editing and provides community resources (dataset, benchmark, code) that set a new performance bar.

Abstract: Instruction-based video editing has witnessed rapid progress, yet current methods often struggle with precise visual control, as natural language is inherently limited in describing complex visual nuances. Although reference-guided editing offers a robust solution, its potential is currently bottlenecked by the scarcity of high-quality paired training data. To bridge this gap, we introduce a scalable data generation pipeline that transforms existing video editing pairs into high-fidelity training quadruplets, leveraging image generative models to create synthesized reference scaffolds. Using this pipeline, we construct RefVIE, a large-scale dataset tailored for instruction-reference-following tasks, and establish RefVIE-Bench for comprehensive evaluation. Furthermore, we propose a unified editing architecture, Kiwi-Edit, that synergizes learnable queries and latent visual features for reference semantic guidance. Our model achieves significant gains in instruction following and reference fidelity via a progressive multi-stage training curriculum. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our data and architecture establish a new state-of-the-art in controllable video editing. All datasets, models, and code is released at https://github.com/showlab/Kiwi-Edit.

</details>


### [244] [RA-Det: Towards Universal Detection of AI-Generated Images via Robustness Asymmetry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01544)
*Xinchang Wang,Yunhao Chen,Yuechen Zhang,Congcong Bian,Zihao Guo,Xingjun Ma,Hui Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They detect AI-generated images by probing behavior instead of appearance: under small, structured perturbations, real images keep stable semantic features while generated ones drift more (“robustness asymmetry”). A simple detector (RA-Det) turns this drift into a decision signal and outperforms strong baselines across many generators (+7.81% average).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Appearance-based detectors are increasingly unreliable as modern generators produce photo-realistic outputs that erase forensic traces and confound high-level cues. The authors seek a general, stable signal that is agnostic to specific generators and persists across architectures and training data.

Method: 1) Empirically identify robustness asymmetry: real images preserve semantic representations under small, structured perturbations; generated images exhibit larger feature drift. 2) Provide theory linking this asymmetry to memorization tendencies in generative models via a lower bound. 3) Propose RA-Det: apply controlled perturbations to an image, measure semantic feature drift in a feature space, and convert the drift into a detection score. The approach is data- and model-agnostic and requires no generator fingerprints.

Result: Across 14 diverse generative models and against 10+ strong detectors, RA-Det achieves superior performance, improving average performance by 7.81%. It generalizes to unseen generators and does not rely on fingerprints or training on specific model outputs.

Conclusion: Robustness asymmetry is a stable, universal behavioral cue for synthetic-image detection. Carefully designed perturbation-based probing yields a practical, transferable, fingerprint-free detector; code is publicly available on GitHub.

Abstract: Recent image generators produce photo-realistic content that undermines the reliability of downstream recognition systems. As visual appearance cues become less pronounced, appearance-driven detectors that rely on forensic cues or high-level representations lose stability. This motivates a shift from appearance to behavior, focusing on how images respond to controlled perturbations rather than how they look. In this work, we identify a simple and universal behavioral signal. Natural images preserve stable semantic representations under small, structured perturbations, whereas generated images exhibit markedly larger feature drift. We refer to this phenomenon as robustness asymmetry and provide a theoretical analysis that establishes a lower bound connecting this asymmetry to memorization tendencies in generative models, explaining its prevalence across architectures. Building on this insight, we introduce Robustness Asymmetry Detection (RA-Det), a behavior-driven detection framework that converts robustness asymmetry into a reliable decision signal. Evaluated across 14 diverse generative models and against more than 10 strong detectors, RA-Det achieves superior performance, improving the average performance by 7.81 percent. The method is data- and model-agnostic, requires no generator fingerprints, and transfers across unseen generators. Together, these results indicate that robustness asymmetry is a stable, general cue for synthetic-image detection and that carefully designed probing can turn this cue into a practical, universal detector. The source code is publicly available at Github.

</details>


### [245] [Leveraging Model Soups to Classify Intangible Cultural Heritage Images from the Mekong Delta](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02181)
*Quoc-Khang Tran,Minh-Thien Nguyen,Nguyen-Khang Pham*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Hybrid CoAtNet plus model-soup checkpoint averaging (greedy/uniform) delivers SOTA on the low-resource ICH-17 dataset, reducing variance with minimal bias and outperforming strong CNN/ViT baselines, without added inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ICH image classification in the Mekong Delta suffers from scarce labels, high visual similarity among classes, and domain heterogeneity.
