Conventional deep models overfit or exhibit high variance, so a more robust, data-efficient approach is needed that generalizes better without heavier inference.

Method: Use CoAtNet (stage-wise fusion of convolution and self-attention) as backbone; collect checkpoints along a single training trajectory; construct model soups via uniform and greedy weight averaging; analyze effects via bias–variance decomposition and quantify checkpoint/output diversity using cross-entropy–based distances and MDS; compare against soft voting and strong baselines (ResNet-50, DenseNet-121, ViT).

Result: On ICH-17 (7,406 images, 17 classes), the approach achieves 72.36% top-1 accuracy and 69.28% macro F1, outperforming the listed baselines. Analyses indicate soups stabilize predictions (lower variance) with minimal added bias and that selected checkpoints are geometrically diverse, unlike soft voting which blends redundant, centrally clustered models.

Conclusion: Diversity-aware checkpoint averaging provides a principled, efficient way to reduce variance and enhance generalization in data-scarce, culturally rich image classification, leveraging CoAtNet and incurring no extra inference cost; selecting diverse checkpoints is key to gains over output-level ensembling.

Abstract: The classification of Intangible Cultural Heritage (ICH) images in the Mekong Delta poses unique challenges due to limited annotated data, high visual similarity among classes, and domain heterogeneity. In such low-resource settings, conventional deep learning models often suffer from high variance or overfit to spurious correlations, leading to poor generalization. To address these limitations, we propose a robust framework that integrates the hybrid CoAtNet architecture with model soups, a lightweight weight-space ensembling technique that averages checkpoints from a single training trajectory without increasing inference cost. CoAtNet captures both local and global patterns through stage-wise fusion of convolution and self-attention. We apply two ensembling strategies - greedy and uniform soup - to selectively combine diverse checkpoints into a final model. Beyond performance improvements, we analyze the ensembling effect through the lens of bias-variance decomposition. Our findings show that model soups reduces variance by stabilizing predictions across diverse model snapshots, while introducing minimal additional bias. Furthermore, using cross-entropy-based distance metrics and Multidimensional Scaling (MDS), we show that model soups selects geometrically diverse checkpoints, unlike Soft Voting, which blends redundant models centered in output space. Evaluated on the ICH-17 dataset (7,406 images across 17 classes), our approach achieves state-of-the-art results with 72.36% top-1 accuracy and 69.28% macro F1-score, outperforming strong baselines including ResNet-50, DenseNet-121, and ViT. These results underscore that diversity-aware checkpoint averaging provides a principled and efficient way to reduce variance and enhance generalization in culturally rich, data-scarce classification tasks.

</details>


### [246] [Training-Free Spatio-temporal Decoupled Reasoning Video Segmentation with Adaptive Object Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01545)
*Zhengtong Zhu,Jiaqing Fan,Zhixuan Liu,Fanzhang Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SDAM is a training-free, spatio-temporally decoupled framework for reasoning video object segmentation that uses an adaptive motion-driven object memory to localize targets spatially and propagate masks stably over time, reporting strong results across five benchmarks without fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior ReasonVOS approaches often require costly fine-tuning of MLLMs and entangle spatial and temporal reasoning, which can degrade temporal stability. The goal is to cut training cost while improving stability and robustness to implicit/complex text queries.

Method: Leverage only pre-trained models in a training-free pipeline. Introduce Adaptive Object Memory to select and memorize key objects using motion cues. Decouple spatial and temporal processing: (1) spatial—precise text-conditioned localization and segmentation; (2) temporal—use the memorized key-object information to drive stable cross-frame propagation.

Result: Demonstrates excellent performance on Ref-YouTubeVOS, Ref-DAVIS17, MeViS, ReasonVOS, and ReVOS. The abstract implies competitive or superior performance to methods that require fine-tuning, though no specific metrics are provided.

Conclusion: A training-free, spatio-temporally decoupled design with adaptive motion-based memory can deliver accurate and temporally stable ReasonVOS while avoiding the computational and data costs of fine-tuning, suggesting a practical and scalable alternative to MLLM-tuned pipelines.

Abstract: Reasoning Video Object Segmentation (ReasonVOS) is a challenging task that requires stable object segmentation across video sequences using implicit and complex textual inputs. Previous methods fine-tune Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to produce segmentation outputs, which demand substantial resources. Additionally, some existing methods are coupled in the processing of spatio-temporal information, which affects the temporal stability of the model to some extent. To address these issues, we propose Training-Free \textbf{S}patio-temporal \textbf{D}ecoupled Reasoning Video Segmentation with \textbf{A}daptive Object \textbf{M}emory (SDAM). We aim to design a training-free reasoning video segmentation framework that outperforms existing methods requiring fine-tuning, using only pre-trained models. Meanwhile, we propose an Adaptive Object Memory module that selects and memorizes key objects based on motion cues in different video sequences. Finally, we propose Spatio-temporal Decoupling for stable temporal propagation. In the spatial domain, we achieve precise localization and segmentation of target objects, while in the temporal domain, we leverage key object temporal information to drive stable cross-frame propagation. Our method achieves excellent results on five benchmark datasets, including Ref-YouTubeVOS, Ref-DAVIS17, MeViS, ReasonVOS, and ReVOS.

</details>


### [247] [Sketch2Colab: Sketch-Conditioned Multi-Human Animation via Controllable Flow Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02190)
*Divyanshu Daiya,Aniket Bera*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Sketch2Colab converts storyboard-like 2D sketches into precise, object-aware 3D multi-human motions by distilling a sketch-conditioned diffusion model into a fast rectified-flow generator, guided by differentiable constraints and a CTMC planner for discrete interaction events, achieving SOTA accuracy and faster inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion-based motion generators produce realistic motion but struggle to strictly satisfy complex, multi-entity interaction constraints (timing, contacts, joint-level control) without heavy training or expensive posterior guidance, and they often degrade under strong conditioning. There is a need for a method that is both constraint-faithful and fast/stable at inference.

Method: 1) Learn a sketch-driven diffusion prior. 2) Distill it into a latent-space rectified-flow student for efficient sampling. 3) Shape the student’s transport field with differentiable energies over keyframes, trajectories, and physics constraints to enforce storyboard and physical plausibility. 4) Add a continuous-time Markov chain (CTMC) planner to schedule discrete events (touch, grasp, handoff) that modulate motion dynamics for coordinated multi-human-object interactions.

Result: On CORE4D and InterHuman, Sketch2Colab achieves state-of-the-art adherence to constraints and perceptual quality while being significantly faster at inference than diffusion-only baselines. Quantitative details are not provided in the abstract.

Conclusion: Combining rectified-flow distillation with differentiable energy shaping and CTMC-based event planning yields fast, stable, and physically plausible 3D multi-human motions that closely follow storyboard sketches and handle discrete interaction phases, outperforming diffusion-only approaches in both fidelity and efficiency.

Abstract: We present Sketch2Colab, which turns storyboard-style 2D sketches into coherent, object-aware 3D multi-human motion with fine-grained control over agents, joints, timing, and contacts. Conventional diffusion-based motion generators have advanced realism; however, achieving precise adherence to rich interaction constraints typically demands extensive training and/or costly posterior guidance, and performance can degrade under strong multi-entity conditioning. Sketch2Colab instead first learns a sketch-driven diffusion prior and then distills it into an efficient rectified-flow student operating in latent space for fast, stable sampling. Differentiable energies over keyframes, trajectories, and physics-based constraints directly shape the student's transport field, steering samples toward motions that faithfully satisfy the storyboard while remaining physically plausible. To capture coordinated interaction, we augment the continuous flow with a continuous-time Markov chain (CTMC) planner that schedules discrete events such as touches, grasps, and handoffs, modulating the dynamics to produce crisp, well-phased human-object-human collaborations. Experiments on CORE4D and InterHuman show that Sketch2Colab achieves state-of-the-art constraint adherence and perceptual quality while offering significantly faster inference than diffusion-only baselines.

</details>


### [248] [PathMoE: Interpretable Multimodal Interaction Experts for Pediatric Brain Tumor Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01547)
*Jian Yu,Joakim Nguyen,Jinrui Fang,Awais Naeem,Zeyuan Cao,Sanjay Krishnan,Nicholas Konz,Tianlong Chen,Chandra Krishnan,Hairong Wang,Edward Castillo,Ying Ding,Ankita Shukla*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PathMoE is an interpretable multimodal mixture-of-experts that fuses H&E whole-slide images, pathology reports, and nuclei-level cell graphs to improve pediatric CNS tumor classification, delivering higher macro-F1 and sample-level modality attributions on internal PBT and external TCGA tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pediatric CNS tumor classification is hard due to complex histology and scarce labeled data. Existing pathology foundation models largely focus on images and overlook complementary clinical text and microarchitectural cues, limiting performance and transparency—especially problematic for rare subtypes where trust and validation are critical.

Method: An interaction-aware mixture-of-experts framework built atop state-of-the-art foundation encoders for three modalities (WSI, pathology text, nuclei-level cell graphs). Specialized experts capture modality uniqueness, redundancy, and synergy. An input-dependent gating mechanism dynamically weights expert interactions, yielding sample-level interpretability. Evaluated on PBT and TCGA classification tasks against image-only baselines.

Result: On PBT, integrating WSI+text+graph improves macro-F1 from 0.762 to 0.799 (+0.037). On TCGA, adding graph knowledge to WSI improves macro-F1 from 0.668 to 0.709 (+0.041). The model outperforms state-of-the-art image-only baselines and exposes which modality interactions drive each prediction.

Conclusion: Multimodal, interaction-aware fusion meaningfully boosts accuracy and provides transparent, sample-level reasoning, which is especially valuable for rare tumor subtypes. PathMoE demonstrates that leveraging complementary text and microarchitectural information alongside images enhances generalization and clinical interpretability.

Abstract: Accurate classification of pediatric central nervous system tumors remains challenging due to histological complexity and limited training data. While pathology foundation models have advanced whole-slide image (WSI) analysis, they often fail to leverage the rich, complementary information found in clinical text and tissue microarchitecture. To this end, we propose PathMoE, an interpretable multimodal framework that integrates H\&E slides, pathology reports, and nuclei-level cell graphs via an interaction-aware mixture-of-experts architecture built on state-of-the-art foundation models for each modality. By training specialized experts to capture modality uniqueness, redundancy, and synergy, PathMoE employs an input-dependent gating mechanism that dynamically weights these interactions, providing sample-level interpretability. We evaluate our framework on two dataset-specific classification tasks on an internal pediatric brain tumor dataset (PBT) and external TCGA datasets. PathMoE improves macro-F1 from 0.762 to 0.799 (+0.037) on PBT when integrating WSI, text, and graph modalities; on TCGA, augmenting WSI with graph knowledge improves macro-F1 from 0.668 to 0.709 (+0.041). These results demonstrate significant performance gains over state-of-the-art image-only baselines while revealing the specific modality interactions driving individual predictions. This interpretability is particularly critical for rare tumor subtypes, where transparent model reasoning is essential for clinical trust and diagnostic validation.

</details>


### [249] [Adaptive Confidence Regularization for Multimodal Failure Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02200)
*Moru Liu,Hao Dong,Olga Fink,Mario Trapp*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ACR is a training framework that detects multimodal failures by penalizing cases where the fused model is less confident than its strongest unimodal branch and by augmenting training with failure-like examples via multimodal feature swapping, yielding robust gains across datasets and modalities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-stakes multimodal applications (e.g., autonomous driving, medical diagnostics) require not just accuracy but dependable failure detection. Prior work underexplores failure detection specific to multimodal fusion. The authors observe a common failure signature—multimodal confidence often drops below at least one unimodal branch (confidence degradation)—and use it to guide training.

Method: Adaptive Confidence Regularization (ACR) with two components: (1) Adaptive Confidence Loss that penalizes confidence degradation of the fused prediction relative to the strongest unimodal branch, encouraging detection or rejection when degradation occurs; (2) Multimodal Feature Swapping, an outlier synthesis technique that swaps features across modalities to create hard, failure-aware training examples, improving the model’s ability to recognize uncertain or conflicting multimodal inputs.

Result: On four datasets spanning three modalities and various evaluation settings, ACR delivers consistent, robust improvements in failure detection and reliability over baselines (exact numbers not in abstract).

Conclusion: Leveraging confidence relationships between unimodal and multimodal branches and training with synthetic, failure-aware outliers improves the model’s ability to recognize and reject uncertain predictions, enhancing reliability of multimodal systems in safety-critical contexts.

Abstract: The deployment of multimodal models in high-stakes domains, such as self-driving vehicles and medical diagnostics, demands not only strong predictive performance but also reliable mechanisms for detecting failures. In this work, we address the largely unexplored problem of failure detection in multimodal contexts. We propose Adaptive Confidence Regularization (ACR), a novel framework specifically designed to detect multimodal failures. Our approach is driven by a key observation: in most failure cases, the confidence of the multimodal prediction is significantly lower than that of at least one unimodal branch, a phenomenon we term confidence degradation. To mitigate this, we introduce an Adaptive Confidence Loss that penalizes such degradations during training. In addition, we propose Multimodal Feature Swapping, a novel outlier synthesis technique that generates challenging, failure-aware training examples. By training with these synthetic failures, ACR learns to more effectively recognize and reject uncertain predictions, thereby improving overall reliability. Extensive experiments across four datasets, three modalities, and multiple evaluation settings demonstrate that ACR achieves consistent and robust gains. The source code will be available at https://github.com/mona4399/ACR.

</details>


### [250] [Align-cDAE: Alzheimer's Disease Progression Modeling with Attention-Aligned Conditional Diffusion Auto-Encoder](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01552)
*Ayantika Das,Keerthi Ram,Mohanasankar Sivaprakasam*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They introduce a diffusion autoencoder that enforces cross‑modal alignment and disentangles progression cues from subject identity, yielding more anatomically precise and controllable generation of Alzheimer’s disease progression images.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Longitudinal brain image synthesis can help track neurodegeneration, but current diffusion models with multi‑modal conditioning don’t ensure that non‑imaging attributes (e.g., clinical or demographic data) drive meaningful, region‑specific changes. Moreover, internal representations lack progression‑aware structure, limiting controllability and anatomical specificity.

Method: A diffusion autoencoder with two key innovations: (1) an explicit alignment objective that couples non‑imaging conditions to image features, steering attention toward regions exhibiting progression; (2) a structured latent space with separate subspaces—one for progression‑related conditions and one for subject‑specific identity—to enable controlled generation while preserving identity.

Result: Compared with prior approaches, the framework achieves more precise anatomical modulation of progression‑specific regions and better control over image generation, indicating improved alignment between conditioning signals and synthesized image changes. (No quantitative metrics provided in the abstract.)

Conclusion: Explicit cross‑modal alignment and a disentangled latent design in diffusion autoencoding improve the anatomical fidelity and controllability of Alzheimer’s progression modeling, suggesting a more reliable tool for disease tracking from multi‑modal data.

Abstract: Generative AI framework-based modeling and prediction of longitudinal human brain images offer an efficient mechanism to track neurodegenerative progression, essential for the assessment of diseases like Alzheimer's. Among the existing generative approaches, recent diffusion-based models have emerged as an effective alternative to generate disease progression images. Incorporating multi-modal and non-imaging attributes as conditional information into diffusion frameworks has been shown to improve controllability during such generations. However, existing methods do not explicitly ensure that information from non-imaging conditioning modalities is meaningfully aligned with image features to introduce desirable changes in the generated images, such as modulation of progression-specific regions. Further, more precise control over the generation process can be achieved by introducing progression-relevant structure into the internal representations of the model, lacking in the existing approaches. To address these limitations, we propose a diffusion autoencoder-based framework for disease progression modeling that explicitly enforces alignment between different modalities. The alignment is enforced by introducing an explicit objective function that enables the model to focus on the regions exhibiting progression-related changes. Further, we devise a mechanism to better structure the latent representational space of the diffusion auto-encoding framework. Specifically, we assign separate latent subspaces for integrating progression-related conditions and retaining subject-specific identity information, allowing better-controlled image generation. These results demonstrate that enforcing alignment and better structuring of the latent representational space of diffusion auto-encoding framework leads to more anatomically precise modeling of Alzheimer's disease progression.

</details>


### [251] [TopoMaskV3: 3D Mask Head with Dense Offset and Height Predictions for Road Topology Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01558)
*Muhammet Esat Kalfaoglu,Halil Ibrahim Ozturk,Ozsel Kilinc,Alptekin Temizel*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TopoMaskV3 turns mask-based road topology into a standalone 3D predictor using dense offset and height heads, fixes discretization errors, introduces geographic-leakage-safe evaluation with long-range testing, and achieves SOTA (28.5 OLS), showing masks generalize better than Bezier and LiDAR helps most at long range.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior mask-based methods (e.g., TopoMaskV2) were confined to 2D and suffered from grid discretization artifacts, requiring auxiliary parametric heads. Evaluation protocols also leaked geographic information due to overlapping train/test regions, inflating perceived performance. There is a need for a true 3D, robust, mask-based approach and fair, geography-disjoint benchmarks—especially at long range.

Method: Extend the dense BEV mask pipeline with two new dense heads: (1) a dense offset field to correct sub-grid discretization within the fixed BEV resolution; (2) a dense height map for direct 3D estimation of road centerlines. Complement this with a new evaluation setup featuring geographically distinct splits to prevent memorization and a +/-100 m long-range benchmark. Analyze robustness vs. Bezier representations and the effect of LiDAR fusion.

Result: On the new geographically disjoint benchmark, TopoMaskV3 attains state-of-the-art 28.5 OLS, outperforming previous methods. Empirically, mask representations are less prone to geographic overfitting than Bezier-based approaches. LiDAR fusion yields the largest relative gains at long range and shows even bigger benefits on overlapping splits, indicating overlap-induced memorization in prior setups.

Conclusion: Dense mask representations, upgraded with offset and height heads, provide accurate standalone 3D road topology. Fair, geography-disjoint evaluation reveals genuine generalization, where masks are more robust than Bezier curves. LiDAR fusion is most impactful at long distances, and prior overlapping splits likely overstated performance due to geographic memorization.

Abstract: Mask-based paradigms for road topology understanding, such as TopoMaskV2, offer a complementary alternative to query-based methods by generating centerlines via a dense rasterized intermediate representation. However, prior work was limited to 2D predictions and suffered from severe discretization artifacts, necessitating fusion with parametric heads. We introduce TopoMaskV3, which advances this pipeline into a robust, standalone 3D predictor via two novel dense prediction heads: a dense offset field for sub-grid discretization correction within the existing BEV resolution, and a dense height map for direct 3D estimation. Beyond the architecture, we are the first to address geographic data leakage in road topology evaluation by introducing (1) geographically distinct splits to prevent memorization and ensure fair generalization, and (2) a long-range (+/-100 m) benchmark. TopoMaskV3 achieves state-of-the-art 28.5 OLS on this geographically disjoint benchmark, surpassing all prior methods. Our analysis shows that the mask representation is more robust to geographic overfitting than Bezier, while LiDAR fusion is most beneficial at long range and exhibits larger relative gains on the overlapping original split, suggesting overlap-induced memorization effects.

</details>


### [252] [Cryo-Bench: Benchmarking Foundation Models for Cryosphere Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01576)
*Saurabh Kaushik,Lalit Maurya,Beth Tellman*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Cryo-Bench is a new benchmark to evaluate geo-foundation models (GFMs) for cryosphere mapping across four tasks and multiple sensors/regions; it shows UNet with frozen encoder leads on average, GFMs excel in few-shot and with tuned fine-tuning, and recommends encoder fine-tuning plus hyperparameter optimization for best results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GFMs have shown promise across Earth observation, but cryosphere-focused benchmarking is missing due to a lack of suitable datasets. The community needs a standardized way to assess domain transfer and data-efficiency of GFMs for key cryospheric tasks.

Method: Construct Cryo-Bench covering debris-covered glaciers, glacial lakes, sea ice, and calving fronts across diverse sensors and regions. Evaluate 14 GFMs against UNet and ViT baselines under multiple regimes: frozen encoders, few-shot (~10% data), and full fine-tuning, including learning-rate tuning. Report mIoU; analyze performance on representative datasets (GLID, CaFFe).

Result: - Frozen encoder: UNet achieves highest average mIoU 66.38; TerraMind 64.02 across five datasets. - Few-shot (10%): certain GFMs (e.g., DOFA ~59.53, TerraMind ~56.62) meet/beat UNet (~56.60). - Full fine-tuning: performance inconsistent across datasets/models; adding LR tuning yields substantial gains (avg relative +12.77% on GLID and CaFFe). - Despite limited cryosphere pretraining, GFMs adapt meaningfully across tasks.

Conclusion: Cryo-Bench enables systematic evaluation of GFMs for cryosphere applications. Best practice is encoder fine-tuning with hyperparameter search; use frozen encoders for fast, low-effort deployment. GFMs show promising domain adaptation under label scarcity. Resources available via the provided GitHub link.

Abstract: Geo-Foundation Models (GFMs) have been evaluated across diverse Earth observation task including multiple domains and have demonstrated strong potential of producing reliable maps even with sparse labels. However, benchmarking GFMs for Cryosphere applications has remained limited, primarily due to the lack of suitable evaluation datasets. To address this gap, we introduce \textbf{Cryo-Bench}, a benchmark compiled to evaluate GFM performance across key Cryospheric components. Cryo-Bench includes debris-covered glaciers, glacial lakes, sea ice, and calving fronts, spanning multiple sensors and broad geographic regions. We evaluate 14 GFMs alongside UNet and ViT baselines to assess their advantages, limitations, and optimal usage strategies. With a frozen encoder, UNet achieves the highest average mIoU of \textbf{66.38}, followed by TerraMind at \textbf{64.02} across five evluation dataset included in Cryo-Bench. In the few-shot setting (10\% input data), GFMs such as DOFA and TerraMind outperform UNet, achieving mIoU scores of \textbf{59.53}, \textbf{56.62}, and \textbf{56.60}, respectively, comapred to U-Net's 56.60. When fully finetuning GFMs, we observe inconsistent performance across datasets and models. However, tuning learning rate along with finetuning substantially improves GFM performance. For example, evaluation on two representative datasets (GLID and CaFFe) shows an average relative improvement of \textbf{12.77\%}. Despite having minimal Cryosphere representation in their pretraining data, GFMs exhibit notable domain adaptation capabilities and produce meaningful results across tasks. Based on our findings, We recommend encoder fine-tuning with hyperparameter optimization optimization to achieve the best possible performance, while using frozen encoders when users need quick results without extensive experimentation.(\href{https://github.com/Sk-2103/Cryo-Bench}{GitHub}).

</details>


### [253] [InterCoG: Towards Spatially Precise Image Editing with Interleaved Chain-of-Grounding Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01586)
*Yecong Wan,Fan Li,Chunwei Wang,Hao Wu,Mingwen Shao,Wangmeng Zuo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: InterCoG is a text–vision interleaved reasoning-and-grounding framework that performs textual spatial reasoning to identify targets, then visually grounds them with boxes/masks, and finally rewrites edit intents, yielding more precise fine-grained edits in complex multi-entity scenes. It adds specialized supervision/alignment losses and introduces a 45K grounding-oriented dataset and a benchmark, showing superior performance on grounding-aware, fine-grained editing tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unified image editors struggle with fine-grained edits when targets are small, not salient, or require spatial reasoning across multiple entities. The authors aim to explicitly reason about spatial relations and target identity before editing, improving localization accuracy and interpretability.

Method: Three-stage pipeline: (1) text-only spatial reasoning (“interleaved chain-of-grounding”) to deduce target identity and location from relational cues; (2) visual grounding via generated bounding boxes and segmentation masks to highlight edit targets in pixel space; (3) prompt rewriting to specify intended edit outcomes. Two auxiliary modules: multimodal grounding reconstruction supervision (to enforce accurate localization) and multimodal grounding reasoning alignment (to align reasoning with visual evidence). Additionally, they construct the GroundEdit-45K dataset with reasoning annotations and GroundEdit-Bench for evaluation.

Result: Across extensive experiments, InterCoG achieves superior precision in fine-grained edits under spatially intricate, multi-entity settings on the proposed benchmark and grounding-aware metrics, outperforming contemporary unified editing models. Specific numerical gains are not provided in the abstract.

Conclusion: Interleaving explicit textual spatial reasoning with visual grounding improves both the accuracy and interpretability of fine-grained image editing in complex scenes. The training objectives and new datasets further support this paradigm, advancing grounding-aware editing performance.

Abstract: Emerging unified editing models have demonstrated strong capabilities in general object editing tasks. However, it remains a significant challenge to perform fine-grained editing in complex multi-entity scenes, particularly those where targets are not visually salient and require spatial reasoning. To this end, we propose InterCoG, a novel text-vision Interleaved Chain-of-Grounding reasoning framework for fine-grained image editing in complex real-world scenes. The key insight of InterCoG is to first perform object position reasoning solely within text that includes spatial relation details to explicitly deduce the location and identity of the edited target. It then conducts visual grounding via highlighting the editing targets with generated bounding boxes and masks in pixel space, and finally rewrites the editing description to specify the intended outcomes. To further facilitate this paradigm, we propose two auxiliary training modules: multimodal grounding reconstruction supervision and multimodal grounding reasoning alignment to enforce spatial localization accuracy and reasoning interpretability, respectively. We also construct GroundEdit-45K, a dataset comprising 45K grounding-oriented editing samples with detailed reasoning annotations, and GroundEdit-Bench for grounding-aware editing evaluation. Extensive experiments substantiate the superiority of our approach in highly precise edits under spatially intricate and multi-entity scenes.

</details>


### [254] [PPEDCRF: Privacy-Preserving Enhanced Dynamic CRF for Location-Privacy Protection for Sequence Videos with Minimal Detection Degradation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01593)
*Bo Ma,Jinsong Wu,Weiqi Yan,Catherine Shi,Minh Nguyen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PPEDCRF perturbs only background regions inferred to leak location in dashcam videos, enforcing temporal consistency and utility-aware noise to thwart background-based retrieval while preserving detection/segmentation performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Removing GPS from shared driving videos is insufficient because backgrounds (buildings, road layouts) enable geo-localization via large-scale street-view retrieval. The goal is to reduce location-privacy leakage without degrading perception tasks needed for autonomy.

Method: A three-part framework: (i) a dynamic CRF discovers and temporally tracks location-sensitive background regions; (ii) a normalized control penalty (NCP) allocates perturbation strength using a hierarchical sensitivity model; (iii) a utility-preserving noise injection module calibrates perturbations to minimize harm to object detection and segmentation.

Result: On public driving datasets, PPEDCRF lowers Top-k geo-retrieval success versus baselines (global noise, white-noise masking, feature-based anonymization) while keeping competitive mAP and segmentation metrics. Code is available at the provided repo.

Conclusion: Selective, temporally consistent, utility-aware perturbation can materially reduce background-based geo-localization risk with limited utility loss. Remaining gaps include robustness to stronger/adaptive attackers, real-time feasibility, and validation across varied conditions and tasks.

Abstract: Dashcam videos collected by autonomous or assisted-driving systems are increasingly shared for safety auditing and model improvement. Even when explicit GPS metadata are removed, an attacker can still infer the recording location by matching background visual cues (e.g., buildings and road layouts) against large-scale street-view imagery. This paper studies location-privacy leakage under a background-based retrieval attacker, and proposes PPEDCRF, a privacy-preserving enhanced dynamic conditional random field framework that injects calibrated perturbations only into inferred location-sensitive background regions while preserving foreground detection utility. PPEDCRF consists of three components: (i) a dynamic CRF that enforces temporal consistency to discover and track location sensitive regions across frames, (ii) a normalized control penalty (NCP) that allocates perturbation strength according to a hierarchical sensitivity model, and (iii) a utility-preserving noise injection module that minimizes interference to object detection and segmentation. Experiments on public driving datasets demonstrate that PPEDCRF significantly reduces location-retrieval attack success (e.g., Top-k retrieval accuracy) while maintaining competitive detection performance (e.g., mAP and segmentation metrics) compared with common baselines such as global noise, white-noise masking, and feature-based anonymization. The source code is in https://github.com/mabo1215/PPEDCRF.git

</details>


### [255] [Preference Score Distillation: Leveraging 2D Rewards to Align Text-to-3D Generation with Human Preference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01594)
*Jiaqi Leng,Shuyuan Tu,Haidong Cao,Sicheng Xie,Daoguo Dong,Zuxuan Wu,Yu-Gang Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They introduce Preference Score Distillation (PSD), a CFG-style, optimization-based approach that uses pretrained 2D reward models to align text-to-3D diffusion outputs with human preferences—without any 3D training data—by avoiding unstable pixel-level reward gradients and co-optimizing negative text embeddings during score distillation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human preference alignment for text-to-3D diffusion is difficult because 3D data is scarce and current methods need task-specific fine-tuning. Directly using 2D reward gradients destabilizes denoising, leaving a gap for a stable, data-efficient alignment method.

Method: PSD reframes preference alignment as classifier-free guidance within score distillation. Instead of backpropagating pixel-level reward gradients from a 2D reward model (which conflicts with diffusion noise training), PSD uses an implicit reward model to provide CFG-like guidance. It further co-optimizes preference scores and negative text embeddings online during optimization, adapting the guidance while preserving the pretrained diffusion prior.

Result: Experiments report better aesthetic metrics, smooth drop-in compatibility with multiple text-to-3D pipelines, and strong extensibility compared to baselines that rely on finetuning or naive reward gradients.

Conclusion: By bridging human preference alignment with CFG theory under a score-distillation framework and avoiding noisy pixel-level reward gradients, PSD offers a data-efficient, stable, and flexible path to human-aligned text-to-3D generation, though specifics on generalization and failure modes will require further empirical validation.

Abstract: Human preference alignment presents a critical yet underexplored challenge for diffusion models in text-to-3D generation. Existing solutions typically require task-specific fine-tuning, posing significant hurdles in data-scarce 3D domains. To address this, we propose Preference Score Distillation (PSD), an optimization-based framework that leverages pretrained 2D reward models for human-aligned text-to-3D synthesis without 3D training data. Our key insight stems from the incompatibility of pixel-level gradients: due to the absence of noisy samples during reward model training, direct application of 2D reward gradients disturbs the denoising process. Noticing that similar issue occurs in the naive classifier guidance in conditioned diffusion models, we fundamentally rethink preference alignment as a classifier-free guidance (CFG)-style mechanism through our implicit reward model. Furthermore, recognizing that frozen pretrained diffusion models constrain performance, we introduce an adaptive strategy to co-optimize preference scores and negative text embeddings. By incorporating CFG during optimization, online refinement of negative text embeddings dynamically enhances alignment. To our knowledge, we are the first to bridge human preference alignment with CFG theory under score distillation framework. Experiments demonstrate the superiority of PSD in aesthetic metrics, seamless integration with diverse pipelines, and strong extensibility.

</details>


### [256] [Dehallu3D: Hallucination-Mitigated 3D Generation from Single Image via Cyclic View Consistency Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01601)
*Xiwen Wang,Shichao Zhang,Hailun Zhang,Ruowei Wang,Mao Li,Chenyu Zhou,Qijun Zhao,Ji-Zhe Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Dehallu3D is a plug-and-play optimization for 3D mesh generation that suppresses hallucinated structural outliers by enforcing balanced multi-view continuity and introduces an Outlier Risk Measure (ORM) to quantify geometric fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large 3D reconstruction models often hallucinate geometry (holes, protrusions) due to reconstructing from sparsely generated, discontinuous multi-view images, causing malformed prints and poor VR/AR immersion. There is little prior work specifically targeting these hallucinations.

Method: Introduce Dehallu3D, a plug-and-play post-optimization with a balanced multi-view continuity constraint across dense interpolated viewpoints. It combines (i) adjacent consistency to enforce geometric continuity across neighboring views and (ii) adaptive smoothness to avoid over-smoothing and preserve sharp features. Also propose ORM, a metric focused on measuring outlier risk in generated geometry.

Result: Across extensive experiments, Dehallu3D removes hallucinated outliers while preserving fine structural details, yielding higher-fidelity 3D meshes and improved scores on the proposed ORM (and presumably standard benchmarks) compared to baselines.

Conclusion: Targeting hallucinations in large 3D reconstruction, Dehallu3D provides an effective, general plug-and-play module that balances continuity and detail preservation, and introduces ORM to evaluate outlier suppression, resulting in more reliable, printable, and immersive 3D assets.

Abstract: Large 3D reconstruction models have revolutionized the 3D content generation field, enabling broad applications in virtual reality and gaming. Just like other large models, large 3D reconstruction models suffer from hallucinations as well, introducing structural outliers (e.g., odd holes or protrusions) that deviate from the input data. However, unlike other large models, hallucinations in large 3D reconstruction models remain severely underexplored, leading to malformed 3D-printed objects or insufficient immersion in virtual scenes. Such hallucinations majorly originate from that existing methods reconstruct 3D content from sparsely generated multi-view images which suffer from large viewpoint gaps and discontinuities. To mitigate hallucinations by eliminating the outliers, we propose Dehallu3D for 3D mesh generation. Our key idea is to design a balanced multi-view continuity constraint to enforce smooth transitions across dense intermediate viewpoints, while avoiding over-smoothing that could erase sharp geometric features. Therefore, Dehallu3D employs a plug-and-play optimization module with two key constraints: (i) adjacent consistency to ensure geometric continuity across views, and (ii) adaptive smoothness to retain fine details.We further propose the Outlier Risk Measure (ORM) metric to quantify geometric fidelity in 3D generation from the perspective of outliers. Extensive experiments show that Dehallu3D achieves high-fidelity 3D generation by effectively preserving structural details while removing hallucinated outliers.

</details>


### [257] [Sparse View Distractor-Free Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01603)
*Yi Gu,Zhaorui Wang,Jiahang Cao,Jiaxu Wang,Mingle Zhao,Dongjun Ye,Renjing Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Augment distractor-free 3D Gaussian Splatting with geometry and semantic priors (from VGGT and VLMs) to robustly handle transient objects under sparse-view inputs, yielding better novel-view synthesis than color-residual–driven baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing distractor-free 3DGS relies on color-residual heuristics to filter/transient-handle content, which breaks down when only few images are available. There is a need to inject stronger priors so sparse-view settings can still separate transient from static scene content.

Method: (1) Use the geometry foundation model VGGT to estimate cameras and seed a dense set of 3D points; (2) exploit VGGT attention maps for efficient, accurate semantic entity matching across views; (3) employ vision–language models to detect and preserve large static regions; (4) plug these priors into existing distractor-free 3DGS pipelines to guide optimization beyond color residual cues.

Result: Across extensive experiments, the proposed priors improve distractor mitigation and robustness for sparse-view 3DGS training, enabling higher-quality, distractor-free reconstructions and novel view synthesis compared to prior methods under limited observations.

Conclusion: Geometry- and semantics-aware priors reduce dependence on unreliable residual heuristics, making distractor-free 3DGS effective in sparse-view scenarios and broadly compatible with current 3DGS frameworks.

Abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) enables efficient training and fast novel view synthesis in static environments. To address challenges posed by transient objects, distractor-free 3DGS methods have emerged and shown promising results when dense image captures are available. However, their performance degrades significantly under sparse input conditions. This limitation primarily stems from the reliance on the color residual heuristics to guide the training, which becomes unreliable with limited observations. In this work, we propose a framework to enhance distractor-free 3DGS under sparse-view conditions by incorporating rich prior information. Specifically, we first adopt the geometry foundation model VGGT to estimate camera parameters and generate a dense set of initial 3D points. Then, we harness the attention maps from VGGT for efficient and accurate semantic entity matching. Additionally, we utilize Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to further identify and preserve the large static regions in the scene. We also demonstrate how these priors can be seamlessly integrated into existing distractor-free 3DGS methods. Extensive experiments confirm the effectiveness and robustness of our approach in mitigating transient distractors for sparse-view 3DGS training.

</details>


### [258] [Coarse-to-Fine Monocular Re-Localization in OpenStreetMap via Semantic Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01613)
*Yuchen Zou,Xiao Hu,Dexing Zhong,Yuqing Tang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They propose a privacy-preserving, scalable monocular re-localization method that localizes images on OpenStreetMap by aligning image semantics with map semantics and performing a coarse-to-fine hierarchical search, yielding higher accuracy and faster runtime than prior work.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dense-map monocular localization is accurate but hard to scale and raises privacy concerns. OSM is lightweight, globally available, and privacy-friendly, but using it is hard due to (1) cross-modal gaps between natural images and vector maps and (2) the high compute cost of global map-based matching.

Method: A hierarchical search framework with semantic alignment to OSM: (1) leverage DINO-ViT’s emergent semantic awareness to decompose visual scenes and associate them with OSM semantics; (2) replace global dense matching with a coarse-to-fine search that progressively narrows candidates and refines pose estimates for efficiency.

Result: Extensive experiments show both accuracy and speed improvements. Notably, when trained on a single dataset, their method’s 3° orientation recall exceeds the 5° recall of state-of-the-art methods, indicating stronger precision at stricter thresholds while being faster.

Conclusion: Semantic alignment between images and OSM plus hierarchical search enables efficient, accurate, and privacy-preserving monocular re-localization without dense maps, addressing cross-modal discrepancies and computational burden, and achieving SOTA-or-better orientation recall.

Abstract: Monocular re-localization plays a crucial role in enabling intelligent agents to achieve human-like perception. However, traditional methods rely on dense maps, which face scalability limitations and privacy risks. OpenStreetMap (OSM), as a lightweight map that protects privacy, offers semantic and geometric information with global scalability. Nonetheless, there are still challenges in using OSM for localization: the inherent cross-modal discrepancies between natural images and OSM, as well as the high computational cost of global map-based localization. In this paper, we propose a hierarchical search framework with semantic alignment for localization in OSM. First, the semantic awareness capability of DINO-ViT is utilised to deconstruct visual elements to establish semantic relationships with OSM. Second, a coarse-to-fine search paradigm is designed to replace global dense matching, enabling efficient progressive refinement. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method significantly improves both localization accuracy and speed. When trained on a single dataset, the 3° orientation recall of our method even outperforms the 5° recall of state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [259] [Adaptive Spectral Feature Forecasting for Diffusion Sampling Acceleration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01623)
*Jiaqi Han,Juntong Shi,Puheng Li,Haotian Ye,Qiushan Guo,Stefano Ermon*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Training-free spectral forecasting of denoiser features with Chebyshev polynomials enables long-range feature reuse in diffusion models, avoiding error compounding and delivering ~4.7× faster inference with better quality than prior caching methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models are slow at inference due to many iterative passes. Existing feature caching/reuse skips steps but relies on local approximations whose errors grow with larger skips, degrading sample quality at high speedups.

Method: Model latent denoiser features as functions of time and approximate them with Chebyshev polynomials. Fit basis coefficients via ridge regression from previously computed features, then forecast features for multiple future diffusion steps to skip network evaluations. Provide theory showing non-compounding error with step size and better long-horizon stability.

Result: Across SOTA image and video diffusion models, the approach consistently outperforms prior caching baselines. Reported speedups: up to 4.79× on FLUX.1 and 4.67× on Wan2.1-14B, while maintaining much higher sample quality.

Conclusion: Global, long-range feature reuse via spectral (Chebyshev-based) forecasting yields controlled-error acceleration for diffusion inference, preserving sample quality at high speedups and outperforming local approximation caching schemes.

Abstract: Diffusion models have become the dominant tool for high-fidelity image and video generation, yet are critically bottlenecked by their inference speed due to the numerous iterative passes of Diffusion Transformers. To reduce the exhaustive compute, recent works resort to the feature caching and reusing scheme that skips network evaluations at selected diffusion steps by using cached features in previous steps. However, their preliminary design solely relies on local approximation, causing errors to grow rapidly with large skips and leading to degraded sample quality at high speedups. In this work, we propose spectral diffusion feature forecaster (Spectrum), a training-free approach that enables global, long-range feature reuse with tightly controlled error. In particular, we view the latent features of the denoiser as functions over time and approximate them with Chebyshev polynomials. Specifically, we fit the coefficient for each basis via ridge regression, which is then leveraged to forecast features at multiple future diffusion steps. We theoretically reveal that our approach admits more favorable long-horizon behavior and yields an error bound that does not compound with the step size. Extensive experiments on various state-of-the-art image and video diffusion models consistently verify the superiority of our approach. Notably, we achieve up to 4.79$\times$ speedup on FLUX.1 and 4.67$\times$ speedup on Wan2.1-14B, while maintaining much higher sample quality compared with the baselines.

</details>


### [260] [DriveCombo: Benchmarking Compositional Traffic Rule Reasoning in Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01637)
*Enhui Ma,Jiahuan Zhang,Guantian Zheng,Tao Tang,Shengbo Eben Li,Yuhang Lu,Xia Zhou,Xueyang Zhang,Yifei Zhan,Kun Zhan,Zhihui Hao,Xianpeng Lang,Kaicheng Yu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DriveCombo introduces a multimodal benchmark and tooling to test whether MLLMs can understand, compose, and resolve conflicts among traffic rules, using a five-level cognitive ladder and a Rule2Scene agent to generate dynamic scenes. Evaluations show sharp performance drops as tasks require multi-rule integration/conflict resolution, but targeted fine-tuning yields substantial gains in both reasoning and planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current MLLM benchmarks for autonomous driving mostly test single-rule skills (e.g., sign recognition) and ignore realistic situations where multiple rules interact or conflict. This mismatch leads to models that excel on simple tests yet violate rules in complex, real-world scenarios. A benchmark that assesses compositional, conflict-aware traffic rule reasoning is needed.

Method: Propose DriveCombo, a text- and vision-based benchmark structured by a Five-Level Cognitive Ladder spanning from single-rule understanding to multi-rule integration and conflict resolution. Introduce a Rule2Scene Agent that converts language-defined traffic rules into dynamic driving scenes via rule crafting and scene generation, enabling scene-level visual reasoning. Evaluate 14 mainstream MLLMs, and conduct dataset splitting plus fine-tuning to measure improvements in reasoning and downstream planning.

Result: Across 14 MLLMs, performance degrades as task complexity increases, with pronounced failures in rule-conflict scenarios. After splitting the dataset and fine-tuning on the training portion, models show substantial improvements in compositional traffic rule reasoning and in downstream planning tasks.

Conclusion: DriveCombo provides a systematic, quantitative way to evaluate and improve MLLMs’ compositional traffic rule reasoning, especially under multi-rule concurrency and conflicts, and shows that targeted fine-tuning can enhance compliance and planning. The benchmark can help push autonomous driving MLLMs toward safer, more intelligent behavior.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are rapidly becoming the intelligence brain of end-to-end autonomous driving systems. A key challenge is to assess whether MLLMs can truly understand and follow complex real-world traffic rules. However, existing benchmarks mainly focus on single-rule scenarios like traffic sign recognition, neglecting the complexity of multi-rule concurrency and conflicts in real driving. Consequently, models perform well on simple tasks but often fail or violate rules in real world complex situations. To bridge this gap, we propose DriveCombo, a text and vision-based benchmark for compositional traffic rule reasoning. Inspired by human drivers' cognitive development, we propose a systematic Five-Level Cognitive Ladder that evaluates reasoning from single-rule understanding to multi-rule integration and conflict resolution, enabling quantitative assessment across cognitive stages. We further propose a Rule2Scene Agent that maps language-based traffic rules to dynamic driving scenes through rule crafting and scene generation, enabling scene-level traffic rule visual reasoning. Evaluations of 14 mainstream MLLMs reveal performance drops as task complexity grows, particularly during rule conflicts. After splitting the dataset and fine-tuning on the training set, we further observe substantial improvements in both traffic rule reasoning and downstream planning capabilities. These results highlight the effectiveness of DriveCombo in advancing compliant and intelligent autonomous driving systems.

</details>


### [261] [MSP-ReID: Hairstyle-Robust Cloth-Changing Person Re-Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01640)
*Xiangyang He,Lin Wan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MSP is a CC-ReID framework that reduces reliance on volatile hairstyle cues and clothing textures by combining hairstyle-oriented augmentation, clothing-region–aware random erasing that preserves body shape, and parsing-guided attention emphasizing stable face/limb cues, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Head-focused de-biasing in CC-ReID often conflates face and hair, making models brittle to hairstyle changes; meanwhile, suppressing clothing can discard structural/body-shape cues or induce texture bias. The paper aims to decouple hair from face features and preserve structural information while reducing apparel bias.

Method: MSP comprises three components: (1) Hairstyle-Oriented Augmentation (HSOA) to create intra-identity hairstyle diversity and reduce hair dependence; (2) Cloth-Preserved Random Erasing (CPRE) that restricts, ratio-controls erasing to clothing regions to suppress texture bias while keeping body shape/context; (3) Region-based Parsing Attention (RPA) that uses human parsing priors to upweight face/limb regions and downweight hair features.

Result: Across multiple CC-ReID benchmarks, MSP achieves new state-of-the-art performance and shows robustness to hairstyle and clothing changes (numbers not provided in the abstract).

Conclusion: Targeted augmentation and parsing-guided attention that separate hair from face features while preserving structural cues provide a robust, practical solution to long-term CC-ReID, outperforming prior methods.

Abstract: Cloth-Changing Person Re-Identification (CC-ReID) aims to match the same individual across cameras under varying clothing conditions. Existing approaches often remove apparel and focus on the head region to reduce clothing bias. However, treating the head holistically without distinguishing between face and hair leads to over-reliance on volatile hairstyle cues, causing performance degradation under hairstyle changes. To address this issue, we propose the Mitigating Hairstyle Distraction and Structural Preservation (MSP) framework. Specifically, MSP introduces Hairstyle-Oriented Augmentation (HSOA), which generates intra-identity hairstyle diversity to reduce hairstyle dependence and enhance attention to stable facial and body cues. To prevent the loss of structural information, we design Cloth-Preserved Random Erasing (CPRE), which performs ratio-controlled erasing within clothing regions to suppress texture bias while retaining body shape and context. Furthermore, we employ Region-based Parsing Attention (RPA) to incorporate parsing-guided priors that highlight face and limb regions while suppressing hair features. Extensive experiments on multiple CC-ReID benchmarks demonstrate that MSP achieves state-of-the-art performance, providing a robust and practical solution for long-term person re-identification.

</details>


### [262] [QCAgent: An agentic framework for quality-controllable pathology report generation from whole slide image](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01647)
*Rundong Wang,Wei Ba,Ying Zhou,Yingtai Li,Bowen Liu,Baizhi Wang,Yuhao Wang,Zhidong Yang,Kun Zhang,Rui Yan,S. Kevin Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: QCAgent is an agentic, checklist-driven framework that iteratively critiques and refines WSI pathology reports by retrieving evidence from informative regions, enabling controllable, evidence-grounded, and high-coverage report generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing WSI-to-report systems produce broad slide-level descriptions without grounding fine-grained claims in localized visual evidence and offer little control over which diagnostic details are included or how they’re verified. There’s a need for controllable, verifiable, and evidence-linked reporting aligned with pathologists’ selective field-of-view inspection.

Method: An agentic loop guided by a user-defined checklist. The system (i) generates a draft report, (ii) applies a customized critique against checklist requirements and constraints, and (iii) re-identifies informative WSI regions using text–patch semantic retrieval to gather corroborating evidence. This iterative critique–retrieve–refine process enriches and reconciles the report with localized findings.

Result: Experiments indicate that making requirements explicit and verifiable through evidence-grounded refinement yields more controllable, clinically meaningful, and higher-coverage pathology reports from WSIs compared to prior approaches. (Quantitative details are not provided in the abstract.)

Conclusion: QCAgent aligns with pathologists’ workflow and enables constraint-aware, evidence-grounded, and controllable WSI report generation, improving clinical relevance and coverage over prior methods.

Abstract: Recent methods for pathology report generation from whole-slide image (WSI) are capable of producing slide-level diagnostic descriptions but fail to ground fine-grained statements in localized visual evidence. Furthermore, they lack control over which diagnostic details to include and how to verify them. Inspired by emerging agentic analysis paradigms and the diagnostic workflow of pathologists,who selectively examine multiple fields of view, we propose QCAgent, an agentic framework for quality-controllable WSI report generation. The core innovations of this framework are as follows: (i) it incorporates a customized critique mechanism guided by a user-defined checklist specifying required diagnostic details and constraints; (ii) it re-identifies informative regions in the WSI based on the critique feedback and text-patch semantic retrieval, a process that iteratively enriches and reconciles the report. Experiments demonstrate that by making report requirements explicitly prompt-defined, constraint-aware, and verifiable through evidence-grounded refinement, QCAgent enables controllable generation of clinically meaningful and high-coverage pathology reports from WSI.

</details>


### [263] [PromptStereo: Zero-Shot Stereo Matching via Structure and Motion Prompts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01650)
*Xianqi Wang,Hao Yang,Hangtian Wang,Junda Cheng,Gangwei Xu,Min Lin,Xin Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They introduce Prompt Recurrent Unit (PRU), a prompt-guided iterative refinement module built on monocular depth foundation model decoders, to inject stereo-scale cues while preserving monocular priors, yielding state-of-the-art zero-shot stereo matching with competitive or faster speed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Zero-shot stereo methods often exploit monocular depth foundation models for features or initialization, but the iterative refinement phase is underexplored. GRU-based refiners have limited capacity to leverage monocular priors, leaving performance on unseen domains suboptimal.

Method: PromptStereo uses PRU, which repurposes the decoder of a monocular depth foundation model as an iterative refiner. It feeds monocular structural signals and stereo motion cues as prompts into the decoder, enriching latent representations with absolute stereo-scale information while retaining monocular depth priors, replacing conventional GRU-based refinement.

Result: Across multiple datasets, PromptStereo achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot generalization and maintains comparable or faster inference than prior methods.

Conclusion: Prompt-guided iterative refinement effectively fuses monocular priors with stereo cues, improving zero-shot stereo matching without sacrificing speed, and represents a promising direction for future stereo refiners.

Abstract: Modern stereo matching methods have leveraged monocular depth foundation models to achieve superior zero-shot generalization performance. However, most existing methods primarily focus on extracting robust features for cost volume construction or disparity initialization. At the same time, the iterative refinement stage, which is also crucial for zero-shot generalization, remains underexplored. Some methods treat monocular depth priors as guidance for iteration, but conventional GRU-based architectures struggle to exploit them due to the limited representation capacity. In this paper, we propose Prompt Recurrent Unit (PRU), a novel iterative refinement module based on the decoder of monocular depth foundation models. By integrating monocular structure and stereo motion cues as prompts into the decoder, PRU enriches the latent representations of monocular depth foundation models with absolute stereo-scale information while preserving their inherent monocular depth priors. Experiments demonstrate that our PromptStereo achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot generalization performance across multiple datasets, while maintaining comparable or faster inference speed. Our findings highlight prompt-guided iterative refinement as a promising direction for zero-shot stereo matching.

</details>


### [264] [A Diffusion-Driven Fine-Grained Nodule Synthesis Framework for Enhanced Lung Nodule Detection from Chest Radiographs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01659)
*Aryan Goyal,Shreshtha Singh,Ashish Mittal,Manoj Tadepalli,Piyush Kumar,Preetham Putha*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They propose a mask‑conditioned diffusion model with modular LoRA adapters—one per radiological attribute—and a new orthogonality loss for composing adapters, enabling fine‑grained, multi‑attribute control of synthetic lung nodules on CXRs. The generated data boosts downstream nodule detection and outperforms prior synthesis methods, with radiologist-validated realism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep learning CAD for lung nodule detection needs diverse, well‑labeled examples spanning size, texture, and boundary variations, but collecting such datasets is expensive and impractical. Existing synthetic nodule generators lack fine‑grained, attribute‑level control, limiting their ability to cover underrepresented characteristics.

Method: 1) Train a base diffusion model conditioned on nodule masks to control size and shape. 2) Train separate LoRA adapters, each targeting a specific radiological feature (e.g., texture, margin). 3) Compose multiple LoRAs at generation time to synthesize nodules with multiple characteristics. 4) Address composition issues—overlapping attention regions and non‑orthogonal parameter spaces—by revisiting merging strategies and introducing an orthogonality loss during LoRA composition training.

Result: Across in‑house and public CXR datasets, the synthetic nodules improve downstream nodule detection performance. Radiologists confirm fine‑grained attribute controllability and realism. Quantitatively, the approach surpasses existing CXR nodule generation baselines on multiple metrics (specific numbers not given in the abstract).

Conclusion: Attribute‑controlled diffusion with composable, orthogonalized LoRA modules enables realistic, multi‑characteristic nodule synthesis that strengthens CAD training. The framework offers practical, controllable data augmentation and sets a new state of the art for CXR nodule generation, with validated gains in detection.

Abstract: Early detection of lung cancer in chest radiographs (CXRs) is crucial for improving patient outcomes, yet nodule detection remains challenging due to their subtle appearance and variability in radiological characteristics like size, texture, and boundary. For robust analysis, this diversity must be well represented in training datasets for deep learning based Computer-Assisted Diagnosis (CAD) systems. However, assembling such datasets is costly and often impractical, motivating the need for realistic synthetic data generation. Existing methods lack fine-grained control over synthetic nodule generation, limiting their utility in addressing data scarcity. This paper proposes a novel diffusion-based framework with low-rank adaptation (LoRA) adapters for characteristic controlled nodule synthesis on CXRs. We begin by addressing size and shape control through nodule mask conditioned training of the base diffusion model. To achieve individual characteristic control, we train separate LoRA modules, each dedicated to a specific radiological feature. However, since nodules rarely exhibit isolated characteristics, effective multi-characteristic control requires a balanced integration of features. We address this by leveraging the dynamic composability of LoRAs and revisiting existing merging strategies. Building on this, we identify two key issues, overlapping attention regions and non-orthogonal parameter spaces. To overcome these limitations, we introduce a novel orthogonality loss term during LoRA composition training. Extensive experiments on both in-house and public datasets demonstrate improved downstream nodule detection. Radiologist evaluations confirm the fine-grained controllability of our generated nodules, and across multiple quantitative metrics, our method surpasses existing nodule generation approaches for CXRs.

</details>


### [265] [FastLightGen: Fast and Light Video Generation with Fewer Steps and Parameters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01685)
*Shao Shitong,Gu Yufei,Xie Zeke*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FastLightGen jointly compresses video generators by distilling both sampling steps and model size, achieving state-of-the-art efficiency; best trade-off found is 4-step sampling with ~30% parameter pruning, outperforming prior acceleration methods on major TI2V benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Powerful video models (e.g., Hunyuan, WanX, Veo3, Kling) are hard to deploy due to huge parameter counts and slow multi-step inference. Prior accelerations either cut steps or prune/compress size, but not both together. There is a need for a method that co-optimizes speed and compactness under real inference budgets.

Method: Introduce FastLightGen, a synergistic distillation framework that (1) constructs an “optimal teacher” tailored to maximize student performance and (2) jointly distills the diffusion sampling process (few-step generation) and model capacity (parameter pruning/compression). The student is trained to mimic the teacher while being pruned (~30%) and constrained to very few sampling steps (around 4).

Result: On HunyuanVideo-ATI2V and WanX-TI2V, the combination of 4 sampling steps and ~30% parameter pruning gives the best visual quality under a fixed compute budget. Across evaluations, FastLightGen surpasses baselines like LCM, DMD, MagicDistillation (step reduction) and ICMD (model compression), establishing new SOTA efficiency/quality trade-offs.

Conclusion: Co-distilling inference steps and model size via an optimized teacher yields a fast, lightweight video generator without sacrificing visual quality. This joint approach advances practical deployment and sets a new benchmark for efficient video generation.

Abstract: The recent advent of powerful video generation models, such as Hunyuan, WanX, Veo3, and Kling, has inaugurated a new era in the field. However, the practical deployment of these models is severely impeded by their substantial computational overhead, which stems from enormous parameter counts and the iterative, multi-step sampling process required during inference. Prior research on accelerating generative models has predominantly followed two distinct trajectories: reducing the number of sampling steps (e.g., LCM, DMD, and MagicDistillation) or compressing the model size for more efficient inference (e.g., ICMD). The potential of simultaneously compressing both to create a fast and lightweight model remains an unexplored avenue. In this paper, we propose FastLightGen, an algorithm that transforms large, computationally expensive models into fast, lightweight counterparts. The core idea is to construct an optimal teacher model, one engineered to maximize student performance, within a synergistic framework for distilling both model size and inference steps. Our extensive experiments on HunyuanVideo-ATI2V and WanX-TI2V reveal that a generator using 4-step sampling and 30\% parameter pruning achieves optimal visual quality under a constrained inference budget. Furthermore, FastLightGen consistently outperforms all competing methods, establishing a new state-of-the-art in efficient video generation.

</details>


### [266] [DiffusionXRay: A Diffusion and GAN-Based Approach for Enhancing Digitally Reconstructed Chest Radiographs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01686)
*Aryan Goyal,Ashish Mittal,Pranav Rao,Manoj Tadepalli,Preetham Putha*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DiffusionXRay is a two-stage CXR restoration pipeline that combines diffusion models and GANs to transform degraded/synthetic chest X-rays into higher-quality images that preserve subtle nodules, improving their diagnostic utility and usefulness for training, as validated by metrics and radiologist review.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep learning for lung cancer detection needs large, diverse, well-annotated CXR datasets, but subtle nodules are hard to label and DRR-synthesized images are often blurry and lose fine lung structures, limiting training effectiveness and generalization.

Method: Two-stage approach: (1) Create a low-quality CXR domain via style transfer, exploring two routes—DDPM-LQ and GAN-based MUNIT-LQ—to emulate degraded DRR-like images and obtain paired LQ–HQ data. (2) Train a DDPM-based restoration model on these pairs to learn denoising and structure recovery, leveraging complementary strengths of diffusion processes and adversarial learning.

Result: Restored CXRs show improved clarity and contrast while preserving subtle, clinically significant features; gains are supported by quantitative image-quality metrics and expert radiologist assessments.

Conclusion: DiffusionXRay effectively addresses synthetic CXR quality degradation and data scarcity, yielding higher-quality training data and potentially better-performing, more generalizable lung nodule detection models; it is a promising direction for CXR restoration and dataset augmentation.

Abstract: Deep learning-based automated diagnosis of lung cancer has emerged as a crucial advancement that enables healthcare professionals to detect and initiate treatment earlier. However, these models require extensive training datasets with diverse case-specific properties. High-quality annotated data is particularly challenging to obtain, especially for cases with subtle pulmonary nodules that are difficult to detect even for experienced radiologists. This scarcity of well-labeled datasets can limit model performance and generalization across different patient populations. Digitally reconstructed radiographs (DRR) using CT-Scan to generate synthetic frontal chest X-rays with artificially inserted lung nodules offers one potential solution. However, this approach suffers from significant image quality degradation, particularly in the form of blurred anatomical features and loss of fine lung field structures. To overcome this, we introduce DiffusionXRay, a novel image restoration pipeline for Chest X-ray images that synergistically leverages denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) and generative adversarial networks (GANs). DiffusionXRay incorporates a unique two-stage training process: First, we investigate two independent approaches, DDPM-LQ and GAN-based MUNIT-LQ, to generate low-quality CXRs, addressing the challenge of training data scarcity, posing this as a style transfer problem. Subsequently, we train a DDPM-based model on paired low-quality and high-quality images, enabling it to learn the nuances of X-ray image restoration. Our method demonstrates promising results in enhancing image clarity, contrast, and overall diagnostic value of chest X-rays while preserving subtle yet clinically significant artifacts, validated by both quantitative metrics and expert radiological assessment.

</details>


### [267] [CoopDiff: A Diffusion-Guided Approach for Cooperation under Corruptions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01688)
*Gong Chen,Chaokun Zhang,Pengcheng Lv*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CoopDiff is a diffusion-based, teacher–student cooperative perception framework that denoises corrupted multi-agent features via voxel-level quality-aware fusion and a dual-branch diffusion student with ego-guided cross-attention, achieving state-of-the-art robustness across diverse degradations on OPV2Vn and DAIR-V2Xn while enabling a precision–efficiency tradeoff.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world cooperative perception suffers from diverse, unpredictable environmental and sensor corruptions that degrade robustness and generalization; a unified, corruption-agnostic denoising approach is needed to reliably fuse multi-agent information.

Method: A teacher–student architecture: (1) Quality-Aware Teacher performs voxel-level early fusion using Quality of Interest weighting and semantic guidance, then applies a diffusion denoiser to produce clean supervision features; (2) Dual-Branch Diffusion Student encodes ego and cooperative streams separately to reconstruct the teacher’s clean targets and uses an Ego-Guided Cross-Attention decoder to adaptively integrate features under degradation.

Result: On two multi-degradation benchmarks (OPV2Vn and DAIR-V2Xn) with six corruption types, CoopDiff consistently outperforms prior methods across all degradations and reduces relative corruption error, while offering a tunable balance between accuracy and inference cost.

Conclusion: Leveraging diffusion’s inherent denoising within a quality-aware teacher–student design substantially improves robustness of cooperative perception to heterogeneous corruptions, providing a practical, adjustable solution for real-world deployment.

Abstract: Cooperative perception lets agents share information to expand coverage and improve scene understanding. However, in real-world scenarios, diverse and unpredictable corruptions undermine its robustness and generalization. To address these challenges, we introduce CoopDiff, a diffusion-based cooperative perception framework that mitigates corruptions via a denoising mechanism. CoopDiff adopts a teacher-student paradigm: the Quality-Aware Teacher performs voxel-level early fusion with Quality of Interest weighting and semantic guidance, then produces clean supervision features via a diffusion denoiser. The Dual-Branch Diffusion Student first separates ego and cooperative streams in encoding to reconstruct the teacher's clean targets. And then, an Ego-Guided Cross-Attention mechanism facilitates balanced decoding under degradation by adaptively integrating ego and cooperative features. We evaluate CoopDiff on two constructed multi-degradation benchmarks, OPV2Vn and DAIR-V2Xn, each incorporating six corruption types, including environmental and sensor-level distortions. Benefiting from the inherent denoising properties of diffusion, CoopDiff consistently outperforms prior methods across all degradation types and lowers the relative corruption error. Furthermore, it offers a tunable balance between precision and inference efficiency.

</details>


### [268] [Search Multilayer Perceptron-Based Fusion for Efficient and Accurate Siamese Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01706)
*Tianqi Shen,Huakao Lin,Ning An*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They replace the Siamese tracker’s fusion neck with a lightweight MLP-based module and use a tailored DNAS procedure that decouples channel-width from other design choices, yielding a low-complexity architecture with state-of-the-art accuracy–efficiency trade-offs and real-time speed on constrained GPUs/NPUs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Convolutional and Transformer-based fusion struggle to provide efficient pixel-level interactions on resource-limited hardware, causing an accuracy–efficiency imbalance. Purely stacking MLPs can be computationally prohibitive due to quadratic scaling with channel width.

Method: Redesign the Siamese neck using a simple MLP-based fusion enabling pixel-level interaction with minimal overhead. Build a hierarchical search space of MLP modules and introduce a customized relaxation strategy for differentiable NAS that decouples channel-width optimization from other architectural choices, automatically balancing width and depth.

Result: The searched architecture attains state-of-the-art accuracy–efficiency trade-offs, ranking among top performers on four general-purpose and three aerial tracking benchmarks, while running in real time on resource-constrained GPUs and NPUs.

Conclusion: A carefully searched, MLP-based fusion neck can surpass heavier conv/Transformer fusion in accuracy–efficiency balance, delivering practical real-time tracking on edge hardware via DNAS-guided width–depth optimization.

Abstract: Siamese visual trackers have recently advanced through increasingly sophisticated fusion mechanisms built on convolutional or Transformer architectures.
