However, both struggle to deliver pixel-level interactions efficiently on resource-constrained hardware, leading to a persistent accuracy-efficiency imbalance. Motivated by this limitation, we redesign the Siamese neck with a simple yet effective Multilayer Perception (MLP)-based fusion module that enables pixel-level interaction with minimal structural overhead. Nevertheless, naively stacking MLP blocks introduces a new challenge: computational cost can scale quadratically with channel width. To overcome this, we construct a hierarchical search space of carefully designed MLP modules and introduce a customized relaxation strategy that enables differentiable neural architecture search (DNAS) to decouple channel-width optimization from other architectural choices. This targeted decoupling automatically balances channel width and depth, yielding a low-complexity architecture. The resulting tracker achieves state-of-the-art accuracy-efficiency trade-offs. It ranks among the top performers on four general-purpose and three aerial tracking benchmarks, while maintaining real-time performance on both resource-constrained Graphics Processing Units (GPUs) and Neural Processing Units (NPUs).

</details>


### [269] [WhisperNet: A Scalable Solution for Bandwidth-Efficient Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01708)
*Gong Chen,Chaokun Zhang,Xinyan Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: WhisperNet is a receiver-centric, bandwidth-aware collaborative perception framework that requests only the most informative features from agents based on lightweight saliency metadata, aligns them before fusion, and achieves SOTA accuracy with a tiny fraction of bandwidth.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Collaborative perception in autonomous driving is bottlenecked by tight and variable bandwidth. Prior fixed-rate compression adapts poorly to changing scenes, and sender-side spatial selection is object-centric, losing global context. A method that adaptively and globally coordinates what/where to share is needed.

Method: A request–response, receiver-centric pipeline: (1) senders transmit lightweight saliency metadata; (2) the receiver formulates a global request plan that dynamically allocates bandwidth across agents and feature regions/channels; (3) it retrieves only high-value features; (4) a collaborative feature routing module aligns related messages to ensure structural consistency before fusion. The framework is plug-and-play and bandwidth-aware.

Result: On OPV2V, it improves AP@0.7 by 2.4% while using only 0.5% of the communication cost, boosts strong baselines with just 5% of full bandwidth, and remains robust under localization noise.

Conclusion: Receiver-driven, globally coordinated allocation over both what and where to share—paired with alignment before fusion—enables highly efficient, robust collaborative perception under severe bandwidth constraints.

Abstract: Collaborative perception is vital for autonomous driving yet remains constrained by tight communication budgets. Earlier work reduced bandwidth by compressing full feature maps with fixed-rate encoders, which adapts poorly to a changing environment, and it further evolved into spatial selection methods that improve efficiency by focusing on salient regions, but this object-centric approach often sacrifices global context, weakening holistic scene understanding. To overcome these limitations, we introduce \textit{WhisperNet}, a bandwidth-aware framework that proposes a novel, receiver-centric paradigm for global coordination across agents. Senders generate lightweight saliency metadata, while the receiver formulates a global request plan that dynamically budgets feature contributions across agents and features, retrieving only the most informative features. A collaborative feature routing module then aligns related messages before fusion to ensure structural consistency. Extensive experiments show that WhisperNet achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving AP@0.7 on OPV2V by 2.4\% with only 0.5\% of the communication cost. As a plug-and-play component, it boosts strong baselines with merely 5\% of full bandwidth while maintaining robustness under localization noise. These results demonstrate that globally-coordinated allocation across \textit{what} and \textit{where} to share is the key to achieving efficient collaborative perception.

</details>


### [270] [Dual Distillation for Few-Shot Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01713)
*Le Dong,Qinzhong Tan,Chunlei Li,Jingliang Hu,Yilei Shi,Weisheng Dong,Xiao Xiang Zhu,Lichao Mou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: D^2 4FAD is a dual-distillation, few-shot anomaly detection framework for medical imaging that uses a small set of normal reference images and a learn-to-weight module to generalize to unseen tasks, achieving state-of-the-art results on a new multi-organ, multi-modality benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unsupervised anomaly detection methods often need large normal datasets and fail to generalize across anatomical contexts; clinicians need methods that work with limited normal references and transfer to new organs/modalities.

Method: Use a pre-trained encoder (teacher) to extract multi-scale features from support (normal) and query images; a student decoder distills knowledge from teacher on query images and self-distills on support images. Introduce a learn-to-weight mechanism that dynamically weights each support image conditioned on the query to improve reference effectiveness. Evaluate on a curated benchmark of 13,084 images spanning four organs, four imaging modalities, and five disease categories.

Result: Across extensive experiments, D^2 4FAD significantly outperforms prior approaches and sets a new state of the art for few-shot medical anomaly detection on the curated benchmark (no specific metrics reported in the abstract).

Conclusion: Dual distillation plus adaptive support weighting enables effective few-shot anomaly detection that generalizes to previously unseen tasks with minimal normal data; the approach is validated on a comprehensive benchmark and released with code.

Abstract: Anomaly detection is a critical task in computer vision with profound implications for medical imaging, where identifying pathologies early can directly impact patient outcomes. While recent unsupervised anomaly detection approaches show promise, they require substantial normal training data and struggle to generalize across anatomical contexts. We introduce D$^2$4FAD, a novel dual distillation framework for few-shot anomaly detection that identifies anomalies in previously unseen tasks using only a small number of normal reference images. Our approach leverages a pre-trained encoder as a teacher network to extract multi-scale features from both support and query images, while a student decoder learns to distill knowledge from the teacher on query images and self-distill on support images. We further propose a learn-to-weight mechanism that dynamically assesses the reference value of each support image conditioned on the query, optimizing anomaly detection performance. To evaluate our method, we curate a comprehensive benchmark dataset comprising 13,084 images across four organs, four imaging modalities, and five disease categories. Extensive experiments demonstrate that D$^2$4FAD significantly outperforms existing approaches, establishing a new state-of-the-art in few-shot medical anomaly detection. Code is available at https://github.com/ttttqz/D24FAD.

</details>


### [271] [Preoperative-to-intraoperative Liver Registration for Laparoscopic Surgery via Latent-Grounded Correspondence Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01720)
*Ruize Cui,Jialun Pei,Haiqiao Wang,Jun Zhou,Jeremy Yuen-Chun Teoh,Pheng-Ann Heng,Jing Qin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Land-Reg, a correspondence-driven 2D–3D registration framework for AR-guided laparoscopic liver surgery that learns latent-grounded landmarks to enable interpretable and robust rigid and non-rigid alignment, outperforming baselines on P2ILF.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AR registration methods for overlaying 3D liver models onto 2D laparoscopic views lack explicit, trustworthy 2D–3D geometric correspondences, reducing interpretability and causing unstable alignment in the OR. The authors aim to make registration both interpretable and robust by explicitly modeling cross-modal correspondences supported by latent evidence.

Method: Land-Reg comprises: (1) Cross-modal Latent Alignment to map CT/MRI (3D) and laparoscopic (2D) features into a unified latent space; (2) an Uncertainty-enhanced Overlap Landmark Detector with similarity matching to extract reliable, explicit 2D–3D landmark correspondences; and (3) for non-rigid registration, a shape-constrained supervision scheme that anchors deformation to matched landmarks via reprojection consistency, adds local-isometric regularization to mitigate 2D–3D depth ambiguity, and uses rendered-mask alignment for global shape consistency.

Result: On the P2ILF dataset, Land-Reg achieves superior performance to existing methods for both rigid pose estimation and non-rigid deformation; qualitative and quantitative improvements are reported. Code will be released at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: Explicitly learning latent-grounded 2D–3D correspondences yields a more interpretable and stable registration pipeline for intraoperative AR in laparoscopic liver surgery. The framework improves both rigid and deformable alignment and offers a principled way to handle cross-modal, depth-ambiguous scenarios.

Abstract: In laparoscopic liver surgery, augmented reality technology enhances intraoperative anatomical guidance by overlaying 3D liver models from preoperative CT/MRI onto laparoscopic 2D views. However, existing registration methods lack explicit modeling of reliable 2D-3D geometric correspondences supported by latent evidence, leading to limited interpretability and potentially unstable alignment in clinical scenarios. In this work, we introduce Land-Reg, a correspondence-driven deformable registration framework that explicitly learns latent-grounded 2D-3D landmark correspondences as an interpretable intermediate representation to bridge cross-modal alignment. For rigid registration, Land-Reg embraces a Cross-modal Latent Alignment module to map multi-modal features into a unified latent space. Further, an Uncertainty-enhanced Overlap Landmark Detector with similarity matching is proposed to robustly estimate explicit 2D-3D landmark correspondences. For non-rigid registration, we design a novel shape-constrained supervision strategy that anchors shape deformation to matched landmarks through reprojection consistency and incorporates local-isometric regularization to alleviate inherent 2D-3D depth ambiguity, while a rendered-mask alignment enforces global shape consistency. Experimental results on the P2ILF dataset demonstrate the superiority of our method on both rigid pose estimation and non-rigid deformation. Our code will be available at https://github.com/cuiruize/Land-Reg.

</details>


### [272] [Learning Domain-Aware Task Prompt Representations for Multi-Domain All-in-One Image Restoration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01725)
*Guanglu Dong,Chunlei Li,Chao Ren,Jingliang Hu,Yilei Shi,Xiao Xiang Zhu,Lichao Mou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: First multi-domain, all-in-one image restoration model (DATPRL-IR) that composes task and domain prompt representations per image—drawing domain priors from multimodal LLMs—and fuses them to guide restoration, outperforming SOTA with strong generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AiOIR models largely target a single domain (natural, medical, or remote sensing), limiting scalability and cross-domain utility. A unified approach that leverages both shared and domain/task-specific priors could reduce fragmentation, improve generalization, and simplify deployment.

Method: Build two learnable prompt pools: (1) task prompts encoding task-related knowledge; (2) domain prompts infused with priors distilled from multimodal LLMs. For each input, a prompt composition mechanism (PCM) adaptively selects and composes relevant task prompts into an instance-level task representation and, analogously, composes domain prompts into a domain representation. These are fused into a domain-aware task prompt representation that conditions the restoration backbone.

Result: Across extensive experiments, DATPRL-IR achieves superior performance to state-of-the-art restoration models and shows strong cross-domain generalization. Code is publicly available.

Conclusion: Domain-aware task prompt representation learning enables a single model to exploit both shared and specialized priors across tasks and domains, delivering improved restoration quality and generalization, and establishing a first multi-domain AiOIR baseline.

Abstract: Recently, significant breakthroughs have been made in all-in-one image restoration (AiOIR), which can handle multiple restoration tasks with a single model. However, existing methods typically focus on a specific image domain, such as natural scene, medical imaging, or remote sensing. In this work, we aim to extend AiOIR to multiple domains and propose the first multi-domain all-in-one image restoration method, DATPRL-IR, based on our proposed Domain-Aware Task Prompt Representation Learning. Specifically, we first construct a task prompt pool containing multiple task prompts, in which task-related knowledge is implicitly encoded. For each input image, the model adaptively selects the most relevant task prompts and composes them into an instance-level task representation via a prompt composition mechanism (PCM). Furthermore, to endow the model with domain awareness, we introduce another domain prompt pool and distill domain priors from multimodal large language models into the domain prompts. PCM is utilized to combine the adaptively selected domain prompts into a domain representation for each input image. Finally, the two representations are fused to form a domain-aware task prompt representation which can make full use of both specific and shared knowledge across tasks and domains to guide the subsequent restoration process. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our DATPRL-IR significantly outperforms existing SOTA image restoration methods, while exhibiting strong generalization capabilities. Code is available at https://github.com/GuangluDong0728/DATPRL-IR.

</details>


### [273] [Action-Guided Attention for Video Action Anticipation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01743)
*Tsung-Ming Tai,Sofia Casarin,Andrea Pilzer,Werner Nutt,Oswald Lanz*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Action-Guided Attention (AGA), which uses predicted action sequences to steer attention over past video frames, improving action anticipation and interpretability on EPIC-Kitchens-100.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard transformer attention over pixel features often overfits to visible past cues and lacks high-level semantics needed to infer latent intentions for future action prediction, leading to poor generalization.

Method: Introduce AGA: use predicted action tokens as queries/keys to focus attention on past moments relevant to the upcoming action and fuse them with the current frame embedding via a gating mechanism; the design supports post-hoc inspection of learned dependencies and counterfactual evidence.

Result: On EPIC-Kitchens-100, the method generalizes well from validation to unseen test sets, outperforming baselines that rely on pixel-level attention (exact numbers not provided in the abstract).

Conclusion: Guiding attention with action-level semantics improves anticipation performance and yields transparent, interpretable insights into modeled action dependencies and counterfactual cues.

Abstract: Anticipating future actions in videos is challenging, as the observed frames provide only evidence of past activities, requiring the inference of latent intentions to predict upcoming actions. Existing transformer-based approaches, which rely on dot-product attention over pixel representations, often lack the high-level semantics necessary to model video sequences for effective action anticipation. As a result, these methods tend to overfit to explicit visual cues present in the past frames, limiting their ability to capture underlying intentions and degrading generalization to unseen samples. To address this, we propose Action-Guided Attention (AGA), an attention mechanism that explicitly leverages predicted action sequences as queries and keys to guide sequence modeling. Our approach fosters the attention module to emphasize relevant moments from the past based on the upcoming activity and combine this information with the current frame embedding via a dedicated gating function. The design of AGA enables post-training analysis of the knowledge discovered from the training set. Experiments on the widely adopted EPIC-Kitchens-100 benchmark demonstrate that AGA generalizes well from validation to unseen test sets. Post-training analysis can further examine the action dependencies captured by the model and the counterfactual evidence it has internalized, offering transparent and interpretable insights into its anticipative predictions.

</details>


### [274] [NeuroSymb-MRG: Differentiable Abductive Reasoning with Active Uncertainty Minimization for Radiology Report Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01756)
*Rong Fu,Yiqing Lyu,Chunlei Meng,Muge Qi,Yabin Jin,Qi Zhao,Li Bao,Juntao Gao,Fuqian Shi,Nilanjan Dey,Wei Luo,Simon Fong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: NeuroSymb-MRG is a neuro-symbolic framework for radiology report generation that maps images to probabilistic clinical concepts, reasons with differentiable logic chains, drafts templated clauses, and refines them via retrieval and constrained LLM editing; an uncertainty-driven, clinician-in-the-loop loop improves reliability. It outperforms baselines on factuality and standard language metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automate radiology reporting to reduce workload and improve consistency while overcoming known issues of encoder–decoder and retrieval-augmented models: visual–linguistic bias, factual errors/hallucinations, and lack of explicit multi-hop clinical reasoning.

Method: (1) Extract image features and project them to probabilistic clinical concepts. (2) Compose differentiable, logic-based abductive reasoning chains over these concepts. (3) Decode reasoning outcomes into templated report clauses. (4) Refine text via retrieval and constrained language-model editing. (5) Use an active sampling loop driven by rule-level uncertainty/diversity to prioritize clinician adjudication and refine a promptbook/rule set.

Result: On standard benchmarks, the method shows consistent gains in factual consistency and typical language metrics relative to representative baselines.

Conclusion: A unified neuro-symbolic plus uncertainty-aware, clinician-in-the-loop approach yields more structured and clinically grounded radiology reports, mitigating biases and inconsistency while maintaining fluency; it offers a promising path to safer, more reliable report generation.

Abstract: Automatic generation of radiology reports seeks to reduce clinician workload while improving documentation consistency. Existing methods that adopt encoder-decoder or retrieval-augmented pipelines achieve progress in fluency but remain vulnerable to visual-linguistic biases, factual inconsistency, and lack of explicit multi-hop clinical reasoning. We present NeuroSymb-MRG, a unified framework that integrates NeuroSymbolic abductive reasoning with active uncertainty minimization to produce structured, clinically grounded reports. The system maps image features to probabilistic clinical concepts, composes differentiable logic-based reasoning chains, decodes those chains into templated clauses, and refines the textual output via retrieval and constrained language-model editing. An active sampling loop driven by rule-level uncertainty and diversity guides clinician-in-the-loop adjudication and promptbook refinement. Experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements in factual consistency and standard language metrics compared to representative baselines.

</details>


### [275] [StepVAR: Structure-Texture Guided Pruning for Visual Autoregressive Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01757)
*Keli Liu,Zhendong Wang,Wengang Zhou,Houqiang Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: StepVAR is a training‑free token pruning framework for Visual AutoRegressive next‑scale models that jointly scores tokens by texture (high‑pass filter) and structure (PCA), then reconstructs dense features via nearest‑neighbor propagation, yielding large inference speedups while preserving image/video quality and outperforming prior pruning/acceleration methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Next‑scale VAR generation becomes quadratically expensive at high resolutions. Later scales mainly refine high‑frequency textures and contain spatial redundancy. Prior pruning focuses on high‑frequency cues, which can disrupt global structure and semantics. A method is needed that accelerates inference while preserving both fine texture and global composition, without retraining.

Method: A training‑free dual‑criterion token pruning: (1) a lightweight high‑pass filter to estimate local textural importance; (2) PCA to capture and preserve tokens important for global structural information. Tokens are selected by combining these scores. To keep next‑scale prediction valid after pruning, a nearest‑neighbor feature propagation reconstructs dense feature maps from the sparse tokens. The approach plugs into state‑of‑the‑art text‑to‑image and text‑to‑video VAR models.

Result: Across multiple VAR architectures, StepVAR achieves substantial inference speedups while maintaining generation fidelity. Both quantitative metrics and qualitative assessments indicate it consistently outperforms existing acceleration/pruning baselines, demonstrating robust gains and generality. (Exact numbers not provided in the abstract.)

Conclusion: Considering both structural and textural importance in token pruning, coupled with sparse‑to‑dense feature reconstruction, enables efficient, training‑free acceleration of VAR inference without sacrificing global semantics or fine details. The method is broadly applicable and superior to prior acceleration approaches.

Abstract: Visual AutoRegressive (VAR) models based on next-scale prediction enable efficient hierarchical generation, yet the inference cost grows quadratically at high resolutions. We observe that the computationally intensive later scales predominantly refine high-frequency textures and exhibit substantial spatial redundancy, in contrast to earlier scales that determine the global structural layout. Existing pruning methods primarily focus on high-frequency detection for token selection, often overlooking structural coherence and consequently degrading global semantics. To address this limitation, we propose StepVAR, a training-free token pruning framework that accelerates VAR inference by jointly considering structural and textural importance. Specifically, we employ a lightweight high-pass filter to capture local texture details, while leveraging Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to preserve global structural information. This dual-criterion design enables the model to retain tokens critical for both fine-grained fidelity and overall composition. To maintain valid next-scale prediction under sparse tokens, we further introduce a nearest neighbor feature propagation strategy to reconstruct dense feature maps from pruned representations. Extensive experiments on state-of-the-art text-to-image and text-to-video VAR models demonstrate that StepVAR achieves substantial inference speedups while maintaining generation quality. Quantitative and qualitative evaluations consistently show that our method outperforms existing acceleration approaches, validating its effectiveness and general applicability across diverse VAR architectures.

</details>


### [276] [Unifying Heterogeneous Multi-Modal Remote Sensing Detection Via Language-Pivoted Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01758)
*Yuxuan Li,Yuming Chen,Yunheng Li,Ming-Ming Cheng,Xiang Li,Jian Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BabelRS is a language-pivoted pretraining framework for heterogeneous multi-modal remote sensing detection that decouples modality alignment from task learning via CSIA and LVSA, stabilizing training and surpassing state of the art.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Late-alignment methods entangle cross-modality alignment with downstream detection optimization, leading to unstable training and weak generalization across diverse sensors (RGB, SAR, IR). A clearer, more robust alignment mechanism is needed, as well as finer-grained semantics for dense detection.

Method: Use a unified language pivot during pretraining. (1) Concept-Shared Instruction Aligning (CSIA) aligns each sensor’s visual features to a shared set of linguistic concepts, creating a common semantic space across modalities. (2) Layerwise Visual-Semantic Annealing (LVSA) progressively injects/aggregates multi-scale visual features with language guidance to bridge the granularity gap between high-level language and dense detection features. After pretraining, fine-tune detection heads with alignment decoupled from task learning.

Result: Across extensive experiments, BabelRS yields more stable training and consistently outperforms prior state-of-the-art heterogeneous multi-modal detectors without extra tricks. Code is available at https://github.com/zcablii/SM3Det.

Conclusion: Language-pivoted, decoupled alignment plus layerwise semantic refinement effectively unifies heterogeneous sensors and better supports dense detection, improving robustness and generalization over existing late-alignment approaches.

Abstract: Heterogeneous multi-modal remote sensing object detection aims to accurately detect objects from diverse sensors (e.g., RGB, SAR, Infrared). Existing approaches largely adopt a late alignment paradigm, in which modality alignment and task-specific optimization are entangled during downstream fine-tuning. This tight coupling complicates optimization and often results in unstable training and suboptimal generalization. To address these limitations, we propose BabelRS, a unified language-pivoted pretraining framework that explicitly decouples modality alignment from downstream task learning. BabelRS comprises two key components: Concept-Shared Instruction Aligning (CSIA) and Layerwise Visual-Semantic Annealing (LVSA). CSIA aligns each sensor modality to a shared set of linguistic concepts, using language as a semantic pivot to bridge heterogeneous visual representations. To further mitigate the granularity mismatch between high-level language representations and dense detection objectives, LVSA progressively aggregates multi-scale visual features to provide fine-grained semantic guidance. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BabelRS stabilizes training and consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods without bells and whistles. Code: https://github.com/zcablii/SM3Det.

</details>


### [277] [Efficient Test-Time Optimization for Depth Completion via Low-Rank Decoder Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01765)
*Minseok Seo,Wonjun Lee,Jaehyuk Jang,Changick Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They introduce a fast, lightweight test-time adaptation method for zero-shot depth completion that updates only a low-dimensional decoder subspace (using sparse depth), avoiding costly diffusion-based optimization and full-network prompt tuning, and achieving state-of-the-art accuracy–efficiency trade-offs across diverse datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Zero-shot depth completion needs to generalize without sensor-specific training, but current methods are slow: diffusion-based test-time optimization requires iterative denoising; visual-prompt methods still perform repeated full-network forward–backward passes. A faster, practical adaptation mechanism is needed.

Method: Empirically observe that depth foundation models encode most depth-relevant information in a low-dimensional decoder subspace. During test-time, adapt only this decoder subspace using sparse depth supervision, leaving the rest of the model frozen. This reduces compute versus diffusion or full-network/prompt optimization while retaining task-specific adaptation.

Result: Across five indoor and outdoor benchmarks, the approach delivers state-of-the-art zero-shot depth completion with consistent gains over prior methods and sets a new Pareto frontier balancing accuracy and efficiency (i.e., faster inference for equal or better accuracy).

Conclusion: Confining test-time updates to a low-dimensional decoder subspace is both sufficient and efficient for zero-shot depth completion, making fast, practical deployment feasible while maintaining or improving accuracy over heavier optimization schemes.

Abstract: Zero-shot depth completion has gained attention for its ability to generalize across environments without sensor-specific datasets or retraining. However, most existing approaches rely on diffusion-based test-time optimization, which is computationally expensive due to iterative denoising. Recent visual-prompt-based methods reduce training cost but still require repeated forward--backward passes through the full frozen network to optimize input-level prompts, resulting in slow inference. In this work, we show that adapting only the decoder is sufficient for effective test-time optimization, as depth foundation models concentrate depth-relevant information within a low-dimensional decoder subspace. Based on this insight, we propose a lightweight test-time adaptation method that updates only this low-dimensional subspace using sparse depth supervision. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance, establishing a new Pareto frontier between accuracy and efficiency for test-time adaptation. Extensive experiments on five indoor and outdoor datasets demonstrate consistent improvements over prior methods, highlighting the practicality of fast zero-shot depth completion.

</details>


### [278] [Downstream Task Inspired Underwater Image Enhancement: A Perception-Aware Study from Dataset Construction to Network Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01767)
*Bosen Lin,Feng Gao,Yanwei Yu,Junyu Dong,Qian Du*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DTI-UIE is a task-inspired underwater image enhancement framework that uses a two-branch, attention-guided network, multi-stage training, and a task-driven perceptual loss (plus an automatically built dataset) to better recover task-relevant details, yielding consistent gains for segmentation, detection, and instance segmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Underwater images often suffer from blur and color casts that degrade downstream recognition. Existing UIE methods optimize for human visual appeal rather than task-relevant high-frequency details, leading to suboptimal performance in semantic/instance segmentation and detection. The authors aim to align enhancement with machine vision needs.

Method: They propose DTI-UIE: (1) an efficient two-branch network whose features are fused via a task-aware attention module; (2) guidance from a human visual perception model to focus enhancement on perceptually and task-relevant cues; (3) a multi-stage training strategy; (4) a task-driven perceptual loss to encourage recovery of high-frequency, discriminative details; and (5) an automatically constructed Task-Inspired UIE Dataset (TI-UIED) using outputs from various task-specific networks.

Result: Across multiple benchmarks and tasks, images preprocessed by DTI-UIE lead to significant performance improvements in semantic segmentation, object detection, and instance segmentation compared to conventional UIE approaches. Code is released for reproducibility.

Conclusion: Task-aligned enhancement outperforms human-visual-only UIE for underwater vision. DTI-UIE and TI-UIED effectively restore task-critical details and serve as practical preprocessing for diverse downstream tasks, with publicly available code enabling adoption and extension.

Abstract: In real underwater environments, downstream image recognition tasks such as semantic segmentation and object detection often face challenges posed by problems like blurring and color inconsistencies. Underwater image enhancement (UIE) has emerged as a promising preprocessing approach, aiming to improve the recognizability of targets in underwater images. However, most existing UIE methods mainly focus on enhancing images for human visual perception, frequently failing to reconstruct high-frequency details that are critical for task-specific recognition. To address this issue, we propose a Downstream Task-Inspired Underwater Image Enhancement (DTI-UIE) framework, which leverages human visual perception model to enhance images effectively for underwater vision tasks. Specifically, we design an efficient two-branch network with task-aware attention module for feature mixing. The network benefits from a multi-stage training framework and a task-driven perceptual loss. Additionally, inspired by human perception, we automatically construct a Task-Inspired UIE Dataset (TI-UIED) using various task-specific networks. Experimental results demonstrate that DTI-UIE significantly improves task performance by generating preprocessed images that are beneficial for downstream tasks such as semantic segmentation, object detection, and instance segmentation. The codes are publicly available at https://github.com/oucailab/DTIUIE.

</details>


### [279] [Neural Operator-Grounded Continuous Tensor Function Representation and Its Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01812)
*Ruoyang Su,Xi-Le Zhao,Sheng Liu,Wei-Hao Wu,Yisi Luo,Michael K. Ng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They replace the discrete, linear mode-n product in tensor models with learned, continuous, nonlinear neural operators, yielding a neural-operator-grounded continuous tensor representation (NO-CTR) that better models data on and off grids, comes with a universality guarantee, and achieves superior performance on multi-dimensional data completion across images, videos, satellite data at multiple resolutions, and point clouds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discrete tensor operations (mode-n products) limit expressivity and induce discretization artifacts, which is problematic for representing complex real-world signals that may live on irregular domains or require resolution-agnostic modeling. A truly continuous representation is needed to unify grid and non-grid data and to reduce artifacts.

Method: Introduce neural-operator-grounded mode-n operators that map a continuous core tensor function directly to a continuous target tensor function, replacing the discrete linear mode-n product with a continuous nonlinear alternative. Build NO-CTR by composing these operators to represent high-order data. Provide a universal approximation theorem for continuous tensor functions. Instantiate NO-CTR in a multi-dimensional data completion model.

Result: Across datasets on regular grids (multispectral images, color videos), grids with varying resolutions (Sentinel-2), and non-gridded domains (point clouds), NO-CTR achieves superior performance to classic discrete tensor and existing continuous tensor function baselines, indicating reduced discretization artifacts and improved fidelity.

Conclusion: Neural-operator-grounded mode-n operators enable a genuinely continuous, resolution-agnostic tensor representation (NO-CTR) that can approximate any continuous tensor function and empirically outperforms prior methods across diverse modalities and domains, suggesting strong potential for modeling complex real-world data beyond regular grids.

Abstract: Recently, continuous tensor functions have attracted increasing attention, because they can unifiedly represent data both on mesh grids and beyond mesh grids. However, since mode-$n$ product is essentially discrete and linear, the potential of current continuous tensor function representations is still locked. To break this bottleneck, we suggest neural operator-grounded mode-$n$ operators as a continuous and nonlinear alternative of discrete and linear mode-$n$ product. Instead of mapping the discrete core tensor to the discrete target tensor, proposed mode-$n$ operator directly maps the continuous core tensor function to the continuous target tensor function, which provides a genuine continuous representation of real-world data and can ameliorate discretization artifacts. Empowering with continuous and nonlinear mode-$n$ operators, we propose a neural operator-grounded continuous tensor function representation (abbreviated as NO-CTR), which can more faithfully represent complex real-world data compared with classic discrete tensor representations and continuous tensor function representations. Theoretically, we also prove that any continuous tensor function can be approximated by NO-CTR. To examine the capability of NO-CTR, we suggest an NO-CTR-based multi-dimensional data completion model. Extensive experiments across various data on regular mesh grids (multi-spectral images and color videos), on mesh girds with different resolutions (Sentinel-2 images) and beyond mesh grids (point clouds) demonstrate the superiority of NO-CTR.

</details>


### [280] [Affine Correspondences in Stereo Vision: Theory, Practice, and Limitations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01836)
*Levente Hajder*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper studies stereo from local affine transformations: it reviews the theory, proposes ways to estimate local affine maps from image directions (optionally using the fundamental matrix), analyzes how affine accuracy affects 3D recovery, and shows that surface normals can be reconstructed within a few degrees on synthetic and real data, including challenging poses and plane orientations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Affine correspondences are increasingly used for stereo tasks (normals, homographies, F/E matrices, and even full 3D), but practical guidance is missing on (i) how to reliably estimate local affine transformations from limited image cues and known epipolar geometry, and (ii) how errors in these transforms propagate to 3D accuracy. The work aims to fill these gaps and provide quantitative evidence.

Method: 1) Summarize fundamentals linking local affine transformations to epipolar geometry. 2) Derive new estimators for local affine transformations from corresponding image directions, optionally constrained by the fundamental matrix. 3) Evaluate on synthetic and real data by reconstructing surface normals. 4) Build a calibration object with three orthogonal chessboard planes to obtain ground truth. 5) Analyze performance across special stereo poses and plane orientations.

Result: - New techniques can estimate local affine transformations from directional correspondences, further improved by leveraging the fundamental matrix. - Error propagation analysis shows how affine estimation accuracy controls 3D reconstruction quality. - Quantitative experiments demonstrate surface-normal errors of only a few degrees under realistic conditions, validated on synthetic datasets and a custom real setup with three perpendicular chessboard planes. - Behavior characterized for special stereo geometries and plane orientations.

Conclusion: Local-affine-driven stereo is practical and accurate: with the proposed estimators and use of the fundamental matrix, one can achieve few-degree errors in surface normal reconstruction. The accuracy of the local affine estimates is the key driver of 3D quality, and the methods are robust across a range of stereo poses and plane orientations.

Abstract: Affine transformations have been recently used for stereo vision. They can be exploited in various computer vision application, e.g., when estimating surface normals, homographies, fundamental and essential matrices. Even full 3D reconstruction can be obtained by using affine correspondences. First, this paper overviews the fundamental statements for affine transformations and epipolar geometry. Then it is investigated how the transformation accuracy influences the quality of the 3D reconstruction. Besides, we propose novel techniques for estimating the local affine transformation from corresponding image directions; moreover, the fundamental matrix, related to the processed image pair, can also be exploited. Both synthetic and real quantitative evaluations are implemented based on the accuracy of the reconstructed surface normals. For the latter one, a special object, containing three perpendicular planes with chessboard patterns, is constructed. The quantitative evaluations are based on the accuracy of the reconstructed surface normals and it is concluded that the estimation accuracy is around a few degrees for realistic test cases. Special stereo poses and plane orientations are also evaluated in detail.

</details>


### [281] [LEAR: Learning Edge-Aware Representations for Event-to-LiDAR Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01839)
*Kuangyi Chen,Jun Zhang,Yuxi Hu,Yi Zhou,Friedrich Fraundorfer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LEAR is a dual-task framework that jointly estimates edges and dense event–depth flow from event cameras; via cross-modal fusion and iterative refinement it produces edge-aware, depth-aligned flows that enable robust PnP-based pose estimation against LiDAR maps, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Event cameras remain reliable under high-speed motion and challenging lighting, making them appealing for localization where GPS and conventional vision fail. But aligning sparse, asynchronous events with dense LiDAR point clouds is ill-posed due to a strong sensing-modality gap, so direct correspondence estimation is unreliable.

Method: LEAR jointly learns edge structures and dense event–depth flow fields. A cross-modal fusion module injects modality-invariant geometric cues into the motion representation, while an iterative refinement strategy enforces mutual consistency between edge and flow estimates over multiple update steps. The resulting depth-aligned, edge-aware flows are used with a PnP solver for pose recovery.

Result: On several popular and challenging datasets, LEAR outperforms the prior best method in robustness and accuracy for localization; code, trained models, and demos are publicly released.

Conclusion: Coupling edge extraction with event–depth flow through cross-modal fusion and iterative refinement bridges the event–LiDAR modality gap, enabling more accurate and robust pose estimation in GPS-denied and visually degraded environments; resources are available for reproduction.

Abstract: Event cameras offer high-temporal-resolution sensing that remains reliable under high-speed motion and challenging lighting, making them promising for localization from LiDAR point clouds in GPS-denied and visually degraded environments. However, aligning sparse, asynchronous events with dense LiDAR maps is fundamentally ill-posed, as direct correspondence estimation suffers from modality gaps. We propose LEAR, a dual-task learning framework that jointly estimates edge structures and dense event-depth flow fields to bridge the sensing-modality divide. Instead of treating edges as a post-hoc aid, LEAR couples them with flow estimation through a cross-modal fusion mechanism that injects modality-invariant geometric cues into the motion representation, and an iterative refinement strategy that enforces mutual consistency between the two tasks over multiple update steps. This synergy produces edge-aware, depth-aligned flow fields that enable more robust and accurate pose recovery via Perspective-n-Point (PnP) solvers. On several popular and challenging datasets, LEAR achieves superior performance over the best prior method. The source code, trained models, and demo videos are made publicly available online.

</details>


### [282] [FireRed-OCR Technical Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01840)
*Hao Wu,Haoran Lou,Xinyue Li,Zuodong Zhong,Zhaojun Sun,Phellon Chen,Xuanhe Zhou,Kai Zuo,Yibo Chen,Xu Tang,Yao Hu,Boxiang Zhou,Jian Wu,Yongji Wu,Wenxin Yu,Yingmiao Liu,Yuhao Huang,Manjie Xu,Gang Liu,Yidong Ma,Zhichao Sun,Changhao Qiao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FireRed-OCR turns a general VLM (Qwen3-VL) into a pixel-precise, structure-aware OCR expert via a curated “Geometry + Semantics” dataset and a three-stage training curriculum, achieving state-of-the-art results (92.94% on OmniDocBench v1.5).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: General VLMs show strong broad abilities but often hallucinate document structures on complex layouts. Industrial OCR needs reliable, pixel-aligned parsing with correct syntax for tables, formulas, and reading order, yet high-quality structured training data—especially for long-tail layouts—is scarce.

Method: 1) Data: A “Geometry + Semantics” Data Factory that clusters geometric features and applies multi-dimensional tagging to synthesize/curate a balanced dataset covering rare layouts and document types. 2) Training: A Three-Stage Progressive Training pipeline—(i) Multi-task Pre-alignment to ground structural understanding; (ii) Specialized SFT to standardize full-page Markdown outputs; (iii) Format-Constrained GRPO (RL) to enforce strict syntactic validity and structural integrity (e.g., table closure, formula syntax). Built on Qwen3-VL.

Result: On OmniDocBench v1.5, FireRed-OCR sets a new SOTA with an overall 92.94%, outperforming strong baselines like DeepSeek-OCR 2 and OCRVerse across text, formula, table, and reading order metrics.

Conclusion: A targeted data+training strategy can reliably specialize general VLMs into structural OCR experts, reducing hallucinations and improving pixel-precise parsing. The open-sourced framework supports a broader paradigm of converting general VLMs into domain-specialized structural parsers.

Abstract: We present FireRed-OCR, a systematic framework to specialize general VLMs into high-performance OCR models. Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have demonstrated impressive general capabilities but frequently suffer from ``structural hallucination'' when processing complex documents, limiting their utility in industrial OCR applications. In this paper, we introduce FireRed-OCR, a novel framework designed to transform general-purpose VLMs (based on Qwen3-VL) into pixel-precise structural document parsing experts. To address the scarcity of high-quality structured data, we construct a ``Geometry + Semantics'' Data Factory. Unlike traditional random sampling, our pipeline leverages geometric feature clustering and multi-dimensional tagging to synthesize and curate a highly balanced dataset, effectively handling long-tail layouts and rare document types. Furthermore, we propose a Three-Stage Progressive Training strategy that guides the model from pixel-level perception to logical structure generation. This curriculum includes: (1) Multi-task Pre-alignment to ground the model's understanding of document structure; (2) Specialized SFT for standardizing full-image Markdown output; and (3) Format-Constrained Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), which utilizes reinforcement learning to enforce strict syntactic validity and structural integrity (e.g., table closure, formula syntax). Extensive evaluations on OmniDocBench v1.5 demonstrate that FireRed-OCR achieves state-of-the-art performance with an overall score of 92.94\%, significantly outperforming strong baselines such as DeepSeek-OCR 2 and OCRVerse across text, formula, table, and reading order metrics. We open-source our code and model weights to facilitate the ``General VLM to Specialized Structural Expert'' paradigm.

</details>


### [283] [GroupEnsemble: Efficient Uncertainty Estimation for DETR-based Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01847)
*Yutong Yang,Katarina Popović,Julian Wiederer,Markus Braun,Vasileios Belagiannis,Bin Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GroupEnsemble partitions DETR queries into multiple masked, non-interacting groups so the shared decoder produces several independent detection sets in one parallel forward pass, enabling efficient ensemble-style spatial (and semantic) uncertainty; a hybrid with MC-Dropout beats Deep Ensembles on Cityscapes/COCO at far lower cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DETR-like detectors output confidence reflecting mainly semantic uncertainty and under-represent spatial uncertainty, limiting reliability for autonomous systems. Deep Ensembles yield strong spatial uncertainty but are memory-heavy; MC-Dropout is cheaper in memory but slow due to multiple stochastic passes. A single-pass, low-overhead alternative is needed.

Method: At inference, inject multiple groups of object queries into the DETR decoder and apply attention masks to block inter-group interactions. Each group independently predicts a complete detection set using the shared decoder weights. Aggregating across groups yields ensemble-based uncertainty estimates. The approach exploits transformer parallelism to keep latency low; a hybrid further combines this with MC-Dropout.

Result: On Cityscapes (driving) and COCO (general), GroupEnsemble and especially its hybrid with MC-Dropout outperform Deep Ensembles on several uncertainty metrics while using a fraction of the memory and runtime, demonstrating better cost–quality trade-offs for uncertainty estimation.

Conclusion: GroupEnsemble provides a practical, single-forward-pass way to estimate spatial uncertainty for DETR-like models. By enforcing independence between query groups, it approximates an ensemble without duplicating models; combined with MC-Dropout it achieves state-of-the-art uncertainty–efficiency trade-offs, making it attractive for real-time, resource-constrained applications.

Abstract: Detection Transformer (DETR) and its variants show strong performance on object detection, a key task for autonomous systems. However, a critical limitation of these models is that their confidence scores only reflect semantic uncertainty, failing to capture the equally important spatial uncertainty. This results in an incomplete assessment of the detection reliability. On the other hand, Deep Ensembles can tackle this by providing high-quality spatial uncertainty estimates. However, their immense memory consumption makes them impractical for real-world applications. A cheaper alternative, Monte Carlo (MC) Dropout, suffers from high latency due to the need of multiple forward passes during inference to estimate uncertainty.
  To address these limitations, we introduce GroupEnsemble, an efficient and effective uncertainty estimation method for DETR-like models. GroupEnsemble simultaneously predicts multiple individual detection sets by feeding additional diverse groups of object queries to the transformer decoder during inference. Each query group is transformed by the shared decoder in isolation and predicts a complete detection set for the same input. An attention mask is applied to the decoder to prevent inter-group query interactions, ensuring each group detects independently to achieve reliable ensemble-based uncertainty estimation. By leveraging the decoder's inherent parallelism, GroupEnsemble efficiently estimates uncertainty in a single forward pass without sequential repetition. We validated our method under autonomous driving scenes and common daily scenes using the Cityscapes and COCO datasets, respectively. The results show that a hybrid approach combining MC-Dropout and GroupEnsemble outperforms Deep Ensembles on several metrics at a fraction of the cost. The code is available at https://github.com/yutongy98/GroupEnsemble.

</details>


### [284] [Streaming Real-Time Trajectory Prediction Using Endpoint-Aware Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01864)
*Alexander Prutsch,David Schinagl,Horst Possegger*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A lightweight streaming trajectory forecasting method reuses endpoints from prior predictions to guide scene context encoding, delivering low-latency, temporally consistent, state-of-the-art results on Argoverse 2 with fewer resources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional trajectory prediction treats each frame independently, ignoring temporal continuity. Real autonomous driving requires continuous, low-latency inference with stable predictions across timesteps.

Method: Introduce an endpoint-aware streaming model that propagates temporal context by using previously predicted trajectory endpoints as anchors to extract targeted scenario encodings, guiding the scene encoder without multi-stage refinement or segment-wise decoding.

Result: Effectively carries information across timesteps, markedly lowers inference latency versus refinement-heavy methods, and achieves state-of-the-art performance on Argoverse 2 multi-agent and single-agent streaming benchmarks while using substantially fewer resources.

Conclusion: Endpoint-anchored temporal context propagation enables accurate, consistent, and efficient streaming trajectory forecasting suited for real-world deployment, avoiding expensive refinement stages.

Abstract: Future trajectories of neighboring traffic agents have a significant influence on the path planning and decision-making of autonomous vehicles. While trajectory forecasting is a well-studied field, research mainly focuses on snapshot-based prediction, where each scenario is treated independently of its global temporal context. However, real-world autonomous driving systems need to operate in a continuous setting, requiring real-time processing of data streams with low latency and consistent predictions over successive timesteps. We leverage this continuous setting to propose a lightweight yet highly accurate streaming-based trajectory forecasting approach. We integrate valuable information from previous predictions with a novel endpoint-aware modeling scheme. Our temporal context propagation uses the trajectory endpoints of the previous forecasts as anchors to extract targeted scenario context encodings. Our approach efficiently guides its scene encoder to extract highly relevant context information without needing refinement iterations or segment-wise decoding. Our experiments highlight that our approach effectively relays information across consecutive timesteps. Unlike methods using multi-stage refinement processing, our approach significantly reduces inference latency, making it well-suited for real-world deployment. We achieve state-of-the-art streaming trajectory prediction results on the Argoverse~2 multi-agent and single-agent benchmarks, while requiring substantially fewer resources.

</details>


### [285] [CTForensics: A Comprehensive Dataset and Method for AI-Generated CT Image Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01878)
*Yiheng Li,Zichang Tan,Guoqing Xu,Yijun Ye,Yang Yang,Zhen Lei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces CTForensics, a multi-generator CT forgery dataset, and ESF-CTFD, a lightweight CNN that fuses wavelet-enhanced multi‑scale spatial features with frequency cues to detect synthetic CT; it outperforms prior methods and generalizes better to unseen generators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative models can create realistic synthetic CTs useful for augmentation and diagnosis, but they pose security risks. Existing detectors often adapt natural-image methods and lack CT-specific artifacts modeling and robust cross-generator evaluation datasets—hindering real-world deployment.

Method: 1) Build CTForensics: a benchmark covering ten diverse CT generative methods for systematic generalization testing. 2) Propose ESF-CTFD: transform an input CT into three scales; use a Wavelet-Enhanced Central Stem to extract features per scale; apply a Spatial Process Block to progressively fuse from large to small scales; finish with a Frequency Process Block to learn frequency-domain cues for final prediction.

Result: Across experiments, ESF-CTFD consistently surpasses existing detectors and shows stronger generalization across different CT generative models (i.e., cross-model robustness), with efficiency implied by its CNN design.

Conclusion: CT-specific, multi-domain feature fusion (wavelet, spatial, frequency) plus a broad benchmark enables more reliable CT forgery detection. The work advances practical robustness, addressing gaps in datasets and CT-tailored detection; it suggests that domain-aware designs are critical for medical-image forensics.

Abstract: With the rapid development of generative AI in medical imaging, synthetic Computed Tomography (CT) images have demonstrated great potential in applications such as data augmentation and clinical diagnosis, but they also introduce serious security risks. Despite the increasing security concerns, existing studies on CT forgery detection are still limited and fail to adequately address real-world challenges. These limitations are mainly reflected in two aspects: the absence of datasets that can effectively evaluate model generalization to reflect the real-world application requirements, and the reliance on detection methods designed for natural images that are insensitive to CT-specific forgery artifacts. In this view, we propose CTForensics, a comprehensive dataset designed to systematically evaluate the generalization capability of CT forgery detection methods, which includes ten diverse CT generative methods. Moreover, we introduce the Enhanced Spatial-Frequency CT Forgery Detector (ESF-CTFD), an efficient CNN-based neural network that captures forgery cues across the wavelet, spatial, and frequency domains. First, it transforms the input CT image into three scales and extracts features at each scale via the Wavelet-Enhanced Central Stem. Then, starting from the largest-scale features, the Spatial Process Block gradually performs feature fusion with the smaller-scale ones. Finally, the Frequency Process Block learns frequency-domain information for predicting the final results. Experiments demonstrate that ESF-CTFD consistently outperforms existing methods and exhibits superior generalization across different CT generative models.

</details>


### [286] [Resolving Blind Inverse Problems under Dynamic Range Compression via Structured Forward Operator Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01890)
*Muyu Liu,Xuanyu Tian,Chenhe Du,Qing Wu,Hongjiang Wei,Yuyao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They enforce monotonicity via a Cascaded Monotonic Bernstein (CaMB) operator to model unknown compression in UDRC and pair it with a plug‑and‑play diffusion prior (CaMB‑Diff), enabling zero‑shot recovery that outperforms state of the art in fidelity and physical consistency across low‑light, low‑field MRI, and HDR tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Blind recovery from unknown dynamic range compression (UDRC) is hard because the forward mapping is unknown and lossy, making existing methods unstable and physically inconsistent. Many UDRC processes share a monotonic intensity mapping, suggesting a universal, physically grounded constraint to regularize operator estimation.

Method: Introduce CaMB, a cascaded monotonic Bernstein operator that parameterizes the unknown forward (compression) function with hard monotonicity constraints, narrowing the solution space to physically valid mappings. Integrate CaMB into a plug‑and‑play diffusion framework (CaMB‑Diff): the diffusion model supplies a strong geometric/semantic prior while CaMB explicitly models and inverts radiometric distortions. Trained/fit in a zero‑shot setting per instance/task.

Result: Across zero‑shot UDRC tasks—including low‑light enhancement, low‑field MRI enhancement, and HDR reconstruction—CaMB‑Diff substantially surpasses contemporary zero‑shot baselines in both signal fidelity and physical consistency. Empirical studies indicate the CaMB parameterization accurately captures the unknown forward operator.

Conclusion: Encoding monotonicity as a hard inductive bias via CaMB enables robust estimation of unknown compression curves; combined with diffusion priors, it yields a physically consistent and superior zero‑shot solution for diverse UDRC problems.

Abstract: Recovering radiometric fidelity from unknown dynamic range compression (UDRC), such as low-light enhancement and HDR reconstruction, is a challenging blind inverse problem, due to the unknown forward model and irreversible information loss introduced by compression. To address this challenge, we first identify monotonicity as the fundamental physical invariant shared across UDRC tasks. Leveraging this insight, we introduce the \textbf{cascaded monotonic Bernstein} (CaMB) operator to parameterize the unknown forward model. CaMB enforces monotonicity as a hard architectural inductive bias, constraining optimization to physically consistent mappings and enabling robust and stable operator estimation. We further integrate CaMB with a plug-and-play diffusion framework, proposing \textbf{CaMB-Diff}. Within this framework, the diffusion model serves as a powerful geometric prior for structural and semantic recovery, while CaMB explicitly models and corrects radiometric distortions through a physically grounded forward operator. Extensive experiments on a variety of zero-shot UDRC tasks, including low-light enhancement, low-field MRI enhancement, and HDR reconstruction, demonstrate that CaMB-Diff significantly outperforms state-of-the-art zero-shot baselines in terms of both signal fidelity and physical consistency. Moreover, we empirically validate the effectiveness of the proposed CaMB parameterization in accurately modeling the unknown forward operator.

</details>


### [287] [Generative Visual Chain-of-Thought for Image Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01893)
*Zijin Yin,Tiankai Hang,Yiji Cheng,Shiyi Zhang,Runze He,Yu Xu,Chunyu Wang,Bing Li,Zheng Chang,Kongming Liang,Qinglin Lu,Zhanyu Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GVCoT introduces an end-to-end visual chain-of-thought for image editing that first localizes target regions via generated spatial cues and then performs edits, trained with SFT+RL on a new 1.8M-sample dataset and evaluated on a new benchmark, achieving state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current image editing models often fail to accurately identify where to edit in complex scenes with fine-grained spatial instructions. Text-only CoT and tool-dependent visual CoT approaches lack native, learnable visual reasoning, motivating a method that natively reasons in the visual domain to achieve precise, interpretable localization before editing.

Method: GVCoT generates visual reasoning traces (spatial cues) to localize target regions, then executes edits, jointly optimizing reasoning and editing visual tokens end-to-end. Training is progressive: supervised fine-tuning to build localization ability in the reasoning trace, followed by reinforcement learning to enhance both reasoning and editing. A new dataset (GVCoT-Edit-Instruct, 1.8M samples across 19 tasks) supports training. A new benchmark (SREdit-Bench) stress-tests complex scenes and fine-grained referring expressions.

Result: GVCoT consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods on SREdit-Bench and ImgEdit, indicating improved spatial reasoning and edit precision. The approach demonstrates emergent spatial reasoning ability and better use of visual-domain cues.

Conclusion: End-to-end, native visual chain-of-thought enables more interpretable and precise image editing. The released large-scale dataset and specialized benchmark facilitate further research. Empirical results suggest GVCoT advances the state of the art on challenging, fine-grained editing tasks.

Abstract: Existing image editing methods struggle to perceive where to edit, especially under complex scenes and nuanced spatial instructions. To address this issue, we propose Generative Visual Chain-of-Thought (GVCoT), a unified framework that performs native visual reasoning by first generating spatial cues to localize the target region and then executing the edit. Unlike prior text-only CoT or tool-dependent visual CoT paradigms, GVCoT jointly optimizes visual tokens generated during the reasoning and editing phases in an end-to-end manner. This way fosters the emergence of innate spatial reasoning ability and enables more effective utilization of visual-domain cues. The main challenge of training GCVoT lies in the scarcity of large-scale editing data with precise edit region annotations; to this end, we construct GVCoT-Edit-Instruct, a dataset of 1.8M high-quality samples spanning 19 tasks. We adopt a progressive training strategy: supervised fine-tuning to build foundational localization ability in reasoning trace before final editing, followed by reinforcement learning to further improve reasoning and editing quality. Finally, we introduce SREdit-Bench, a new benchmark designed to comprehensively stress-test models under sophisticated scenes and fine-grained referring expressions. Experiments demonstrate that GVCoT consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models on SREdit-Bench and ImgEdit. We hope our GVCoT will inspire future research toward interpretable and precise image editing.

</details>


### [288] [Zero-shot Low-Field MRI Enhancement via Diffusion-Based Adaptive Contrast Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01913)
*Muyu Liu,Chenhe Du,Xuanyu Tian,Qing Wu,Xiao Wang,Haonan Zhang,Hongjiang Wei,Yuyao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a zero-shot framework (DACT) that upgrades low-field MRI to high-field–quality images by coupling a pre-trained high-field diffusion prior with an adaptive, physics-informed contrast transport via Sinkhorn optimal transport, achieving state-of-the-art structural fidelity and tissue contrast without paired data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-field MRI is cheaper and more accessible but suffers from low SNR and altered tissue contrast due to field-dependent relaxation, making direct recovery of authentic high-field contrast difficult. Paired LF–HF data are scarce and the LF→HF contrast mapping is nonlinear and unknown, so existing zero-shot methods with linear-degradation assumptions fail to restore true tissue contrast.

Method: DACT combines: (1) a pre-trained high-field diffusion model to enforce anatomical realism; (2) a differentiable, physics-informed forward model; and (3) a Sinkhorn optimal transport module embedded in reverse diffusion to explicitly match LF-to-HF intensity distributions. This enables dynamic learning of the nonlinear contrast mapping while preserving topology and structure, in a fully zero-shot setting (no paired supervision).

Result: On simulated and real clinical low-field datasets, DACT outperforms prior zero-shot approaches, delivering reconstructions with better structural detail and more faithful tissue contrast, establishing new state-of-the-art performance.

Conclusion: Integrating OT-based adaptive contrast transport with a HF diffusion prior effectively bridges LF–HF domain gaps without paired data, recovering anatomically plausible and contrast-correct HF-quality images. This approach is promising for democratizing MRI quality on LF systems, though broader clinical validation and robustness studies will be important next steps.

Abstract: Low-field (LF) magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) democratizes access to diagnostic imaging but is fundamentally limited by low signal-to-noise ratio and significant tissue contrast distortion due to field-dependent relaxation dynamics. Reconstructing high-field (HF) quality images from LF data is a blind inverse problem, severely challenged by the scarcity of paired training data and the unknown, non-linear contrast transformation operator. Existing zero-shot methods, which assume simplified linear degradation, often fail to recover authentic tissue contrast. In this paper, we propose DACT(Diffusion-Based Adaptive Contrast Transport), a novel zero-shot framework that restores HF-quality images without paired supervision. DACT synergizes a pre-trained HF diffusion prior to ensure anatomical fidelity with a physically-informed adaptive forward model. Specifically, we introduce a differentiable Sinkhorn optimal transport module that explicitly models and corrects the intensity distribution shift between LF and HF domains during the reverse diffusion process. This allows the framework to dynamically learn the intractable contrast mapping while preserving topological consistency. Extensive experiments on simulated and real clinical LF datasets demonstrate that DACT achieves state-of-the-art performance, yielding reconstructions with superior structural detail and correct tissue contrast.

</details>


### [289] [LaST-VLA: Thinking in Latent Spatio-Temporal Space for Vision-Language-Action in Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01928)
*Yuechen Luo,Fang Li,Shaoqing Xu,Yang Ji,Zehan Zhang,Bing Wang,Yuannan Shen,Jianwei Cui,Long Chen,Guang Chen,Hangjun Ye,Zhi-Xin Yang,Fuxi Wen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LaST-VLA replaces explicit textual reasoning with a physically grounded latent spatio-temporal chain-of-thought, aligning latent features to 3D geometry and dynamics, trained progressively and safety-refined with GRPO, achieving state-of-the-art driving benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explicit text Chain-of-Thought in VLA driving stacks decouples semantics from perception and causes symbolic–perceptual conflicts. Pure latent CoT avoids text but is often physics-agnostic, lacking constraints needed for reliable planning. The goal is to couple perception, reasoning, and action in a physics-aware latent space.

Method: Introduce a Latent Spatio-Temporal VLA (LaST-VLA) that grounds reasoning in a latent spatio-temporal CoT. Use a dual-feature alignment mechanism to distill (1) geometric constraints from 3D foundation models and (2) dynamic foresight from world models directly into the latent space. Apply a progressive SFT schedule transitioning from feature alignment to trajectory generation, then refine with reinforcement learning via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to enforce safety and traffic-rule compliance.

Result: Sets new records on NAVSIM v1 (91.3 PDMS) and NAVSIM v2 (87.1 EPDMS), and shows superior spatio-temporal reasoning on SURDS and NuDynamics benchmarks.

Conclusion: Grounding latent reasoning in 3D geometry and dynamics mitigates semantic–perceptual decoupling, leading to safer, rule-compliant, and more accurate planning. The latent spatio-temporal CoT paradigm advances VLA-based autonomous driving and reduces reliance on brittle textual intermediates.

Abstract: While Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have revolutionized autonomous driving by unifying perception and planning, their reliance on explicit textual Chain-of-Thought (CoT) leads to semantic-perceptual decoupling and perceptual-symbolic conflicts. Recent shifts toward latent reasoning attempt to bypass these bottlenecks by thinking in continuous hidden space. However, without explicit intermediate constraints, standard latent CoT often operates as a physics-agnostic representation. To address this, we propose the Latent Spatio-Temporal VLA (LaST-VLA), a framework shifting the reasoning paradigm from discrete symbolic processing into a physically grounded Latent Spatio-Temporal CoT. By implementing a dual-feature alignment mechanism, we distill geometric constraints from 3D foundation models and dynamic foresight from world models directly into the latent space. Coupled with a progressive SFT training strategy that transitions from feature alignment to trajectory generation, and refined via Reinforcement Learning with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to ensure safety and rule compliance. \method~setting a new record on NAVSIM v1 (91.3 PDMS) and NAVSIM v2 (87.1 EPDMS), while excelling in spatial-temporal reasoning on SURDS and NuDynamics benchmarks.

</details>


### [290] [BAWSeg: A UAV Multispectral Benchmark for Barley Weed Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01932)
*Haitian Wang,Xinyu Wang,Muhammad Ibrahim,Dustin Severtson,Ajmal Mian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces VISA, a two‑stream UAV multispectral segmentation model that separates raw reflectance and vegetation‑index cues and fuses them with attention mechanisms, plus BAWSeg, a four‑year calibrated barley dataset. VISA improves weed mapping accuracy and robustness across fields and years.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Weed segmentation pipelines based on thresholded vegetation indices are brittle under radiometric drift and mixed crop–weed pixels. Single‑stream CNN/Transformer models that stack bands and indices cause interference between radiance and normalized cues, reducing sensitivity to small, sparse weeds under crop canopies. Reliable, cross‑season/field, pixel‑level mapping is needed for practical deployment.

Method: VISA is a two‑stream network. The radiance stream ingests calibrated five‑band reflectance and applies residual spectral–spatial attention to preserve fine textures and row boundaries. The index stream ingests vegetation‑index maps and combines windowed self‑attention for efficient local structure, state‑space layers to propagate field‑scale context without quadratic cost, and Slot Attention to form stable region descriptors for sparse weed discrimination. Streams are fused at native resolution. The authors also contribute BAWSeg: a four‑year UAV multispectral dataset (blue/green/red/red‑edge/NIR), derived indices, dense crop/weed/other labels, and leakage‑free block splits for supervised training and deployment‑oriented evaluation.

Result: On BAWSeg, VISA achieves 75.6% mIoU and 63.5% weed IoU with 22.8M parameters, outperforming a multispectral SegFormer‑B1 by +1.2 mIoU and +1.9 weed IoU. Under cross‑plot and cross‑year protocols, VISA maintains 71.2% and 69.2% mIoU, respectively.

Conclusion: Decoupling radiance and index cues with tailored attention improves weed segmentation accuracy and generalization, particularly for small, canopy‑mixed weeds, while remaining efficient. BAWSeg, code, and trained models will be released to support further research and deployment.

Abstract: Accurate weed mapping in cereal fields requires pixel-level segmentation from UAV imagery that remains reliable across fields, seasons, and illumination. Existing multispectral pipelines often depend on thresholded vegetation indices, which are brittle under radiometric drift and mixed crop--weed pixels, or on single-stream CNN and Transformer backbones that ingest stacked bands and indices, where radiance cues and normalized index cues interfere and reduce sensitivity to small weed clusters embedded in crop canopies. We propose VISA (Vegetation-Index and Spectral Attention), a two-stream segmentation network that decouples these cues and fuses them at native resolution. The radiance stream learns from calibrated five-band reflectance using residual spectral-spatial attention to preserve fine textures and row boundaries that are attenuated by ratio indices. The index stream operates on vegetation-index maps with windowed self-attention to model local structure efficiently, state-space layers to propagate field-scale context without quadratic attention cost, and Slot Attention to form stable region descriptors that improve discrimination of sparse weeds under canopy mixing. To support supervised training and deployment-oriented evaluation, we introduce BAWSeg, a four-year UAV multispectral dataset collected over commercial barley paddocks in Western Australia, providing radiometrically calibrated blue, green, red, red edge, and near-infrared orthomosaics, derived vegetation indices, and dense crop, weed, and other labels with leakage-free block splits. On BAWSeg, VISA achieves 75.6% mIoU and 63.5% weed IoU with 22.8M parameters, outperforming a multispectral SegFormer-B1 baseline by 1.2 mIoU and 1.9 weed IoU. Under cross-plot and cross-year protocols, VISA maintains 71.2% and 69.2% mIoU, respectively. The BAWSeg data, VISA code, and trained models will be released upon publication.

</details>


### [291] [MobileMold: A Smartphone-Based Microscopy Dataset for Food Mold Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01944)
*Dinh Nam Pham,Leonard Prokisch,Bennet Meyer,Jonas Thumbs*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MobileMold is an open smartphone-microscopy dataset for detecting food mold and classifying food types; the authors benchmark multiple deep models, achieve ~99.5% accuracy/F1, and provide saliency explanations, demonstrating low-cost, portable food-safety sensing with clip-on microscopes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clip-on smartphone microscopes can reveal fungal structures invisible to the naked eye, enabling affordable, portable mold inspection, but there is a lack of open, standardized datasets to train and evaluate ML models for food spoilage detection in real-world mobile conditions.

Method: Assemble MobileMold with 4,941 handheld microscopy images spanning 11 food types, 4 smartphones, 3 microscope attachments, and varied real-world settings. Establish baselines for (i) mold detection and (ii) food-type classification, plus a multi-task model predicting both. Evaluate multiple pretrained deep architectures with data augmentation and add saliency-based visual explanations to highlight mold-relevant regions.

Result: Across architectures and augmentations, the models reach near-ceiling performance for detecting food spoilage (accuracy = 0.9954, F1 = 0.9954, MCC = 0.9907). Saliency maps emphasize image regions associated with mold that drive predictions.

Conclusion: MobileMold provides a valuable, open benchmark for accessible food-safety sensing and mobile imaging. Results suggest smartphone-plus-attachment microscopy can reliably detect mold, and the inclusion of saliency improves transparency, encouraging further research on smartphone-enhanced sensing.

Abstract: Smartphone clip-on microscopes turn everyday devices into low-cost, portable imaging systems that can even reveal fungal structures at the microscopic level, enabling mold inspection beyond unaided visual checks. In this paper, we introduce MobileMold, an open smartphone-based microscopy dataset for food mold detection and food classification. MobileMold contains 4,941 handheld microscopy images spanning 11 food types, 4 smartphones, 3 microscopes, and diverse real-world conditions. Beyond the dataset release, we establish baselines for (i) mold detection and (ii) food-type classification, including a multi-task setting that predicts both attributes. Across multiple pretrained deep learning architectures and augmentation strategies, we obtain near-ceiling performance (accuracy = 0.9954, F1 = 0.9954, MCC = 0.9907), validating the utility of our dataset for detecting food spoilage. To increase transparency, we complement our evaluation with saliency-based visual explanations highlighting mold regions associated with the model's predictions.
