MobileMold aims to contribute to research on accessible food-safety sensing, mobile imaging, and exploring the potential of smartphones enhanced with attachments.

</details>


### [292] [PreSight: Preoperative Outcome Prediction for Parkinson's Disease via Region-Prior Morphometry and Patient-Specific Weighting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01948)
*Yand Wang,Chen Zhang,Lanyun Zhu,Yixin Chen,Qunbo Wang,Yutong Bai,Jurgen Germann,Yinghong Wen,Shuai Shao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PreSight is a presurgical, multimodal model that fuses clinical priors with MRI-based deformation morphometry and patient-specific regional weighting to predict individual motor improvement after Parkinson’s surgery, achieving strong, calibrated performance with external validation and interpretable region-level explanations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Predicting which Parkinson’s disease patients will benefit from surgery preoperatively is hard due to subtle imaging signals and patient heterogeneity. Clinicians need reliable, patient-specific, decision-ready predictions based only on data available before surgery to inform selection and counseling.

Method: Develop PreSight: an end-to-end model that combines clinical prior knowledge with preoperative MRI and deformation-based morphometry (DBM). It includes a patient-specific regional weighting module to adapt the importance of brain regions per patient. The system outputs calibrated probabilities and patient-level explanations. Evaluation used a two-center cohort (n=400) with multimodal presurgical inputs and postoperative improvement labels, compared against clinical-only, imaging-only, and multimodal baselines; included ablations (removing DBM and weighting) and decision-curve analysis.

Result: PreSight outperformed baselines, achieving 88.89% internal validation accuracy and 85.29% external test accuracy for responder classification. It showed better probability calibration and higher net benefit on decision-curve analysis. Ablations confirmed the added value of DBM and the patient-specific weighting module, and explanations emphasized disease-relevant regions in a patient-specific manner.

Conclusion: Integrating clinical priors with region-adaptive morphometry enables reliable, calibrated presurgical decision support for Parkinson’s surgery in routine practice, with performance that generalizes across centers and interpretable, patient-specific regional emphasis.

Abstract: Preoperative improvement rate prediction for Parkinson's disease surgery is clinically important yet difficult because imaging signals are subtle and patients are heterogeneous. We address this setting, where only information available before surgery is used, and the goal is to predict patient-specific postoperative motor benefit. We present PreSight, a presurgical outcome model that fuses clinical priors with preoperative MRI and deformation-based morphometry (DBM) and adapts regional importance through a patient-specific weighting module. The model produces end-to-end, calibrated, decision-ready predictions with patient-level explanations. We evaluate PreSight on a real-world two-center cohort of 400 subjects with multimodal presurgical inputs and postoperative improvement labels. PreSight outperforms strong clinical, imaging-only, and multimodal baselines. It attains 88.89% accuracy on internal validation and 85.29% on an external-center test for responder classification and shows better probability calibration and higher decision-curve net benefit. Ablations and analyses confirm the contribution of DBM and the patient-specific weighting module and indicate that the model emphasizes disease-relevant regions in a patient-specific manner. These results demonstrate that integrating clinical prior knowledge with region-adaptive morphometry enables reliable presurgical decision support in routine practice.

</details>


### [293] [Robust White Blood Cell Classification with Stain-Normalized Decoupled Learning and Ensembling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01976)
*Luu Le,Hoang-Loc Cao,Ha-Hieu Pham,Thanh-Huy Nguyen,Ulas Bagci*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A two-stage, stain-normalized and decoupled training pipeline combats stain/scanner variability and severe class imbalance in WBC images, then boosts robustness with model ensembling and test-time augmentation—achieving top rank on WBCBench 2026.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world WBC datasets suffer from large appearance shifts due to staining/scanning protocols and from long-tailed distributions where rare yet clinically crucial cell types are underrepresented, degrading classifier robustness and recall on minority classes.

Method: 1) Preprocess with stain normalization. 2) Decoupled training: first learn transferable representations using instance-balanced sampling; then rebalance the classifier using class-aware sampling and a hybrid loss that combines effective-number weighting with focal modulation. 3) Inference-time robustness via ensembling diverse backbones and applying test-time augmentation.

Result: The approach obtained the top ranking on the WBCBench 2026: Robust White Blood Cell Classification Challenge at ISBI 2026 (no detailed metrics reported in the abstract).

Conclusion: Addressing distribution shift and class imbalance via decoupled representation/classifier learning plus loss reweighting and inference ensembling yields state-of-the-art WBC classification performance and improved robustness, suggesting suitability for clinical hematology workflows.

Abstract: White blood cell (WBC) classification is fundamental for hematology applications such as infection assessment, leukemia screening, and treatment monitoring. However, real-world WBC datasets present substantial appearance variations caused by staining and scanning conditions, as well as severe class imbalance in which common cell types dominate while rare but clinically important categories are underrepresented. To address these challenges, we propose a stain-normalized, decoupled training framework that first learns transferable representations using instance-balanced sampling, and then rebalances the classifier with class-aware sampling and a hybrid loss combining effective-number weighting and focal modulation. In inference stage, we further enhance robustness by ensembling various trained backbones with test-time augmentation. Our approach achieved the top rank on the leaderboard of the WBCBench 2026: Robust White Blood Cell Classification Challenge at ISBI 2026.

</details>


### [294] [Process Over Outcome: Cultivating Forensic Reasoning for Generalizable Multimodal Manipulation Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01993)
*Yuchen Zhang,Yaxiong Wang,Kecheng Han,Yujiao Wu,Lianwei Wu,Li Zhu,Zhedong Zheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: REFORM is a reasoning-driven framework for multimodal manipulation detection that learns explicit forensic rationales via a three-stage curriculum and achieves state-of-the-art, more generalizable performance; it is supported by a new reasoning-annotated dataset, ROM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Detection models that simply classify manipulation types under result-only supervision overfit superficial artifacts and lack interpretability, leading to poor generalization to unseen manipulation patterns. The authors aim to embed explicit forensic reasoning to improve robustness and explainability.

Method: They propose REFORM, which shifts from outcome fitting to process modeling through a three-stage curriculum: (1) induce forensic rationales, (2) align the generated reasoning with final judgments, and (3) refine logical consistency using reinforcement learning. They also construct ROM, a large-scale dataset with rich reasoning annotations to train and evaluate reasoning-driven detection and grounding.

Result: REFORM achieves new state of the art with stronger cross-dataset generalization: 81.52% ACC on ROM, 76.65% ACC on DGM4, and 74.9 F1 on MMFakeBench.

Conclusion: Explicit forensic reasoning, coupled with curriculum learning and RL for logical consistency, yields more interpretable and generalizable manipulation detection. The ROM dataset enables this paradigm and helps establish SOTA results across benchmarks.

Abstract: Recent advances in generative AI have significantly enhanced the realism of multimodal media manipulation, thereby posing substantial challenges to manipulation detection. Existing manipulation detection and grounding approaches predominantly focus on manipulation type classification under result-oriented supervision, which not only lacks interpretability but also tends to overfit superficial artifacts. In this paper, we argue that generalizable detection requires incorporating explicit forensic reasoning, rather than merely classifying a limited set of manipulation types, which fails to generalize to unseen manipulation patterns. To this end, we propose REFORM, a reasoning-driven framework that shifts learning from outcome fitting to process modeling. REFORM adopts a three-stage curriculum that first induces forensic rationales, then aligns reasoning with final judgments, and finally refines logical consistency via reinforcement learning. To support this paradigm, we introduce ROM, a large-scale dataset with rich reasoning annotations. Extensive experiments show that REFORM establishes new state-of-the-art performance with superior generalization, achieving 81.52% ACC on ROM, 76.65% ACC on DGM4, and 74.9 F1 on MMFakeBench.

</details>


### [295] [Event-Only Drone Trajectory Forecasting with RPM-Modulated Kalman Filtering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01997)
*Hari Prasanth S. M.,Pejman Habibiroudkenar,Eerik Alamikkotervo,Dimitrios Bouzoulas,Risto Ojala*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Event-only drone trajectory forecasting that leverages propeller RPM cues fused via an RPM-aware Kalman filter, outperforming learning-based and vanilla KF baselines on short/medium horizons without RGB or training data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Event cameras excel at high-speed motion capture but are underused for drone trajectory prediction. The authors aim to exploit a distinctive, reliable signal present in event data—periodic propeller-induced motion—to improve forecasting accuracy and robustness while avoiding dependence on RGB imagery and large training datasets.

Method: Extract propeller rotational speeds directly from raw event streams (e.g., via frequency analysis of propeller-induced event patterns) and integrate these measurements into a Kalman filtering pipeline that explicitly models RPM dynamics (“RPM-aware” KF) to inform motion state estimation and short-horizon prediction.

Result: On the FRED dataset, the proposed approach achieves lower average distance error and final distance error than learning-based approaches and a vanilla Kalman filter at 0.4 s and 0.8 s prediction horizons.

Conclusion: Propeller-cue-aware, event-only filtering can deliver robust, accurate short- to medium-horizon drone trajectory forecasts without training data or RGB input, highlighting event cameras’ utility and the value of physics/actuation-informed filtering.

Abstract: Event cameras provide high-temporal-resolution visual sensing that is well suited for observing fast-moving aerial objects; however, their use for drone trajectory prediction remains limited. This work introduces an event-only drone forecasting method that exploits propeller-induced motion cues. Propeller rotational speed are extracted directly from raw event data and fused within an RPM-aware Kalman filtering framework. Evaluations on the FRED dataset show that the proposed method outperforms learning-based approaches and vanilla kalman filter in terms of average distance error and final distance error at 0.4s and 0.8s forecasting horizons. The results demonstrate robust and accurate short- and medium-horizon trajectory forecasting without reliance on RGB imagery or training data.

</details>


### [296] [NICO-RAG: Multimodal Hypergraph Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Understanding the Nicotine Public Health Crisis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02047)
*Manuel Serna-Aguilera,Raegan Anderes,Page Dobbs,Khoa Luu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces NICO, a >200,000-sample multimodal dataset on 55 nicotine/tobacco brands, and NICO-RAG, a cost-efficient multimodal retrieval-augmented generation framework that uses hypergraph knowledge representations to retrieve and reason over image and text without processing additional image tokens, achieving performance comparable to state-of-the-art image-adapted RAG on 100+ questions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The tobacco industry’s rapid product innovation (e.g., flavored nicotine pouches) undermines public health efforts, while existing research lacks large-scale, connected multimodal resources and tools to make factual, scalable inferences across images and text.

Method: Construct the NICO dataset with images and accompanying text for 55 brands. Build NICO-RAG: pre-extract image/text entities and relations; organize them into multimodal hypergraphs; retrieve by both visual similarity and semantic similarity of descriptions; perform RAG without incurring additional image-token processing costs by using precomputed image features and structured knowledge.

Result: On 100+ questions, NICO-RAG achieves performance comparable to a state-of-the-art image-adapted RAG system while avoiding the extra cost of processing image tokens on a large-scale dataset like NICO.

Conclusion: NICO provides a substantial, domain-specific multimodal resource, and NICO-RAG offers an efficient, factuality-oriented approach to multimodal retrieval and QA, enabling public health researchers to analyze nicotine/tobacco marketing at scale with reduced computational cost.

Abstract: The nicotine addiction public health crisis continues to be pervasive. In this century alone, the tobacco industry has released and marketed new products in an aggressive effort to lure new and young customers for life. Such innovations and product development, namely flavored nicotine or tobacco such as nicotine pouches, have undone years of anti-tobacco campaign work. Past work is limited both in scope and in its ability to connect large-scale data points. Thus, we introduce the Nicotine Innovation Counter-Offensive (NICO) Dataset to provide public health researchers with over 200,000 multimodal samples, including images and text descriptions, on 55 tobacco and nicotine product brands. In addition, to provide public health researchers with factual connections across a large-scale dataset, we propose NICO-RAG, a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework that can retrieve image features without incurring the high-cost of language models, as well as the added cost of processing image tokens with large-scale datasets such as NICO. At construction time, NICO-RAG organizes image- and text-extracted entities and relations into hypergraphs to produce as factual responses as possible. This joint multimodal knowledge representation enables NICO-RAG to retrieve images for query answering not only by visual similarity but also by the semantic similarity of image descriptions. Experimentals show that without needing to process additional tokens from images for over 100 questions, NICO-RAG performs comparably to the state-of-the-art RAG method adapted for images.

</details>


### [297] [WorldStereo: Bridging Camera-Guided Video Generation and Scene Reconstruction via 3D Geometric Memories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02049)
*Yisu Zhang,Chenjie Cao,Tengfei Wang,Xuhui Zuo,Junta Wu,Jianke Zhu,Chunchao Guo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: WorldStereo augments video diffusion with geometry-aware memories to enable precise camera control and multi-view-consistent video generation, yielding high-fidelity 3D reconstructions efficiently across diverse inputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Although recent video diffusion models produce high-quality videos, they lack reliable camera control and cross-view consistency, making 3D scene reconstruction from generated content difficult.

Method: Introduce WorldStereo with two geometric memory modules: (1) a global-geometric memory that incrementally builds point clouds to provide coarse structural priors and precise camera control; (2) a spatial-stereo memory that enforces 3D correspondences to constrain attention for fine-detail consistency. A flexible control branch interfaces with a distribution-matching distilled VDM backbone, avoiding joint training and improving efficiency.

Result: On camera-guided video generation and 3D reconstruction benchmarks, WorldStereo produces multi-view-consistent videos with precise camera trajectories and enables high-quality 3D reconstructions. It generalizes as a world model to varied scene generation tasks from both perspective and panoramic inputs.

Conclusion: By bridging camera-guided video diffusion with explicit 3D geometry through dual memory modules, WorldStereo achieves controllable, view-consistent video synthesis and robust 3D reconstruction efficiently, with broad applicability and plans for model release.

Abstract: Recent advances in foundational Video Diffusion Models (VDMs) have yielded significant progress. Yet, despite the remarkable visual quality of generated videos, reconstructing consistent 3D scenes from these outputs remains challenging, due to limited camera controllability and inconsistent generated content when viewed from distinct camera trajectories. In this paper, we propose WorldStereo, a novel framework that bridges camera-guided video generation and 3D reconstruction via two dedicated geometric memory modules. Formally, the global-geometric memory enables precise camera control while injecting coarse structural priors through incrementally updated point clouds. Moreover, the spatial-stereo memory constrains the model's attention receptive fields with 3D correspondence to focus on fine-grained details from the memory bank. These components enable WorldStereo to generate multi-view-consistent videos under precise camera control, facilitating high-quality 3D reconstruction. Furthermore, the flexible control branch-based WorldStereo shows impressive efficiency, benefiting from the distribution matching distilled VDM backbone without joint training. Extensive experiments across both camera-guided video generation and 3D reconstruction benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach. Notably, we show that WorldStereo acts as a powerful world model, tackling diverse scene generation tasks (whether starting from perspective or panoramic images) with high-fidelity 3D results. Models will be released.

</details>


### [298] [ORGAN: Object-Centric Representation Learning using Cycle Consistent Generative Adversarial Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02063)
*Joël Küchler,Ellen van Maren,Vaiva Vasiliauskaitė,Katarina Vulić,Reza Abbasi-Asl,Stephan J. Ihle*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ORGAN is a cycle-consistent GAN–based method for unsupervised object-centric representation learning that segments images into objects, learns low-dimensional object latents, matches state-of-the-art on synthetic data, outperforms others on challenging real-world scenes, enables object manipulation, and scales with object count and image size.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extracting actionable information from raw data is hard; current object-centric learning is dominated by autoencoders that struggle with complex real-world images, limited contrast, many objects, and scalability. The authors aim to provide a more robust, manipulable, and scalable alternative.

Method: Replace autoencoder-based object-centric pipelines with a cycle-consistent Generative Adversarial Network. The model segments images into object components and maps each object to a compact latent code, trained via adversarial objectives with cycle-consistency to ensure faithful, invertible representations suitable for manipulation.

Result: On synthetic datasets, ORGAN performs on par with state-of-the-art AE-based methods. On real-world datasets with many objects and low visual contrast, it is the only tested method that succeeds. It produces expressive latent spaces that support object manipulation and shows strong scaling with both number of objects and image resolution.

Conclusion: Cycle-consistent GANs provide a strong foundation for object-centric representation learning, delivering robust performance on real data, manipulable latents, and superior scalability compared to prevailing AE-based approaches.

Abstract: Although data generation is often straightforward, extracting information from data is more difficult. Object-centric representation learning can extract information from images in an unsupervised manner. It does so by segmenting an image into its subcomponents: the objects. Each object is then represented in a low-dimensional latent space that can be used for downstream processing. Object-centric representation learning is dominated by autoencoder architectures (AEs). Here, we present ORGAN, a novel approach for object-centric representation learning, which is based on cycle-consistent Generative Adversarial Networks instead. We show that it performs similarly to other state-of-the-art approaches on synthetic datasets, while at the same time being the only approach tested here capable of handling more challenging real-world datasets with many objects and low visual contrast. Complementing these results, ORGAN creates expressive latent space representations that allow for object manipulation. Finally, we show that ORGAN scales well both with respect to the number of objects and the size of the images, giving it a unique edge over current state-of-the-art approaches.

</details>


### [299] [MMNavAgent: Multi-Magnification WSI Navigation Agent for Clinically Consistent Whole-Slide Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02079)
*Zhengyang Xu,Han Li,Jingsong Liu,Linrui Xie,Xun Ma,Xin You,Shihui Zu,Ayako Ito,Xinyu Hao,Hongming Xu,Shaohua Kevin Zhou,Nassir Navab,Peter J. Schüffler*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces MMNavAgent, an agent-based multi-magnification navigation system for whole-slide images that models cross-scale interactions and adaptively selects magnifications, yielding modest but consistent diagnostic gains (+1.45% AUC, +2.93% BACC) over a non-agent baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing WSI navigation methods either operate at a single fixed magnification or follow a predefined magnification sequence, unlike pathologists who fluidly switch and integrate information across scales. This mismatch limits cross-magnification reasoning and adaptive magnification choice, potentially hurting diagnostic performance and efficiency.

Method: Proposes a clinically aligned Multi-Magnification WSI Navigation Agent (MMNavAgent) with two key components: (1) Cross-Magnification navigation Tool (CMT) that aggregates contextual information from adjacent magnifications to strengthen representations along the navigation trajectory; (2) Magnification Selection Tool (MST) that uses memory-driven reasoning within an agent framework to interactively and adaptively choose which magnification to inspect next, mimicking sequential pathologist decisions.

Result: On a public dataset, MMNavAgent achieves improved diagnostic performance compared with a non-agent baseline: +1.45% AUC and +2.93% balanced accuracy (BACC). Code will be released upon acceptance.

Conclusion: Aligning WSI navigation with clinical multi-scale behavior—via cross-magnification feature aggregation and memory-informed, adaptive magnification selection—improves diagnostic metrics. The agent-based design and the proposed CMT and MST are effective components for multi-scale WSI analysis.

Abstract: Recent AI navigation approaches aim to improve Whole-Slide Image (WSI) diagnosis by modeling spatial exploration and selecting diagnostically relevant regions, yet most operate at a single fixed magnification or rely on predefined magnification traversal. In clinical practice, pathologists examine slides across multiple magnifications and selectively inspect only necessary scales, dynamically integrating global and cellular evidence in a sequential manner. This mismatch prevents existing methods from modeling cross-magnification interactions and adaptive magnification selection inherent to real diagnostic workflows. To these, we propose a clinically consistent Multi-Magnification WSI Navigation Agent (MMNavAgent) that explicitly models multi magnification interaction and adaptive magnification selection. Specifically, we introduce a Cross-Magnification navigation Tool (CMT) that aggregates contextual information from adjacent magnifications to enhance discriminative representations along the navigation path. We further introduce a Magnification Selection Tool (MST) that leverages memory-driven reasoning within the agent framework to enable interactive and adaptive magnification selection, mimicking the sequential decision process of pathologists. Extensive experiments on a public dataset demonstrate improved diagnostic performance, with 1.45% gain of AUC and 2.93% gain of BACC over a non-agent baseline. Code will be public upon acceptance.

</details>


### [300] [From Pixels to Patches: Pooling Strategies for Earth Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02080)
*Isaac Corley,Caleb Robinson,Inbal Becker-Reshef,Juan M. Lavista Ferres*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Pooling matters for geospatial pixel embeddings: smarter, distribution-aware pooling beats mean pooling, improving robustness to spatial shift and overall accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As geospatial models move from patch-level to dense pixel-level embeddings, practitioners must compress many pixel vectors into a single patch representation that preserves class-discriminative information and aligns with coarser label resolution. The common default—mean pooling—ignores within-patch variability and can severely hurt accuracy, especially under geographic distribution shift.

Method: Release EuroSAT-Embed, a benchmark of ~81k embedding GeoTIFFs generated from three geospatial foundation models (AlphaEarth, OlmoEarth, Tessera). Evaluate 13 pooling strategies (11 training-free, 2 parametric) for aggregating pixel embeddings into patch representations under both random splits and geographically disjoint (spatial) splits.

Result: Compared to mean pooling, richer pooling reduces geographic generalization gap by up to 40% and improves accuracy by up to 5% on spatial splits. Generalized Mean Pooling (GeM) gives consistent gains without increasing embedding dimensionality. Stats pooling (concatenated min/max/mean/std) achieves the best overall accuracy but increases representation size 4x. Effectiveness varies by embedding source, with higher-dimensional embeddings benefiting most from distributional statistics.

Conclusion: Mean pooling is suboptimal for pixel-to-patch aggregation in geospatial settings, particularly under spatial shift. Use GeM as a drop-in upgrade when dimensionality must be fixed; choose Stats pooling when accuracy is paramount and larger embeddings are acceptable. Pooling choice should consider the embedding source and dimensionality.

Abstract: As geospatial foundation models shift from patch-level to pixel-level embeddings, practitioners must aggregate thousands of pixel vectors into patch representations that preserve class-discriminative signal while matching downstream label resolution. The default choice, mean pooling, discards within-patch variability and can drop accuracy by more than 10% under spatial shift. To evaluate this effect, we introduce EuroSAT-Embed: 81,000 embedding GeoTIFFs derived from three foundation models: AlphaEarth, OlmoEarth, and Tessera. We benchmark 11 training-free and 2 parametric pooling methods under both random and geographically disjoint test splits. Our results show that richer pooling schemes reduce the geographic generalization gap by up to 40% relative to mean pooling and increases accuracy by up to 5% on spatial splits. We recommend Generalized Mean Pooling (GeM) as a drop-in replacement for mean pooling: it improves accuracy without increasing embedding dimensionality. For maximum accuracy, Stats pooling (concatenation of min/max/mean/std pooling) performs best at 4x the embedding size. We further find that pooling effectiveness varies across embedding sources and that higher-dimensional embeddings benefit most from distributional statistics.

</details>


### [301] [A 3D mesh convolution-based autoencoder for geometry compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02125)
*Germain Bregeon,Marius Preda,Radu Ispas,Titus Zaharia*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Novel mesh-convolution autoencoder for 3D geometry compression that operates directly on irregular, non-manifold, non-watertight meshes; preserves connectivity with pooling/unpooling; encodes to a comparable base-mesh latent and decodes to full-resolution geometry; outperforms SOTA in reconstruction and latent-space classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 3D mesh compression and learning methods often assume regularity (manifold/watertight meshes) or require heavy preprocessing, and their latents are not easily comparable across shapes. There is a need for a compression model that handles irregular meshes directly while producing a meaningful, comparable latent representation.

Method: A face-based mesh-convolution autoencoder with dedicated connectivity-preserving pooling/unpooling. The encoder maps meshes to a compact base-mesh space so latents are comparable; the decoder reconstructs original connectivity and recovers full-resolution geometry from the compressed latent.

Result: On multi-class datasets, the approach achieves state-of-the-art performance for both geometry reconstruction accuracy and downstream latent-space classification, surpassing prior methods.

Conclusion: Learning directly on mesh faces with topology-aware pooling/unpooling enables robust compression of irregular meshes and yields useful, comparable latents; the method is practical (no preprocessing/manifold requirements) and effective across categories. Code is available at the provided GitHub link.

Abstract: In this paper, we introduce a novel 3D mesh convolution-based autoencoder for geometry compression, able to deal with irregular mesh data without requiring neither preprocessing nor manifold/watertightness conditions. The proposed approach extracts meaningful latent representations by learning features directly from the mesh faces, while preserving connectivity through dedicated pooling and unpooling operations. The encoder compresses the input mesh into a compact base mesh space, which ensures that the latent space remains comparable. The decoder reconstructs the original connectivity and restores the compressed geometry to its full resolution. Extensive experiments on multi-class datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in both 3D mesh geometry reconstruction and latent space classification tasks. Code available at: github.com/germainGB/MeshConv3D

</details>


### [302] [Stereo-Inertial Poser: Towards Metric-Accurate Shape-Aware Motion Capture Using Sparse IMUs and a Single Stereo Camera](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02130)
*Tutian Tang,Xingyu Ji,Yutong Li,MingHao Liu,Wenqiang Xu,Cewu Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Stereo-Inertial Poser is a real-time (>200 FPS) motion-capture system that fuses a single stereo camera with six IMUs to produce metric-accurate, shape-aware 3D human motion, delivering drift-free global translation and reduced foot-skate without post-optimization, achieving state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Monocular visual–inertial mocap is affordable and robust to occlusion but suffers from depth ambiguity (hurting metric global translation) and ignores body-shape differences (leading to shape-agnostic local motion). The goal is a low-cost system that is metrically accurate and personalized to subject anthropometry, while remaining real-time and drift-robust.

Method: Replace monocular RGB with calibrated stereo to resolve depth and enable direct 3D keypoint extraction and body-shape parameter estimation; fuse visual cues with six-IMU data to predict drift-compensated joints and root motion; introduce a shape-aware fusion module that explicitly harmonizes anthropometric variations with global translations; implement an end-to-end pipeline that runs >200 FPS without optimization-based post-processing.

Result: Quantitative evaluations across multiple datasets show state-of-the-art accuracy. Qualitatively, the system maintains drift-free global translation over long recordings and reduces foot-skating artifacts.

Conclusion: Combining stereo vision with IMUs and shape-aware fusion achieves metrically accurate, real-time human motion capture that generalizes across subjects and scenarios, with practical deployment enabled by high throughput and no need for post-optimization.

Abstract: Recent advancements in visual-inertial motion capture systems have demonstrated the potential of combining monocular cameras with sparse inertial measurement units (IMUs) as cost-effective solutions, which effectively mitigate occlusion and drift issues inherent in single-modality systems. However, they are still limited by metric inaccuracies in global translations stemming from monocular depth ambiguity, and shape-agnostic local motion estimations that ignore anthropometric variations. We present Stereo-Inertial Poser, a real-time motion capture system that leverages a single stereo camera and six IMUs to estimate metric-accurate and shape-aware 3D human motion. By replacing the monocular RGB with stereo vision, our system resolves depth ambiguity through calibrated baseline geometry, enabling direct 3D keypoint extraction and body shape parameter estimation. IMU data and visual cues are fused for predicting drift-compensated joint positions and root movements, while a novel shape-aware fusion module dynamically harmonizes anthropometry variations with global translations. Our end-to-end pipeline achieves over 200 FPS without optimization-based post-processing, enabling real-time deployment. Quantitative evaluations across various datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art performance. Qualitative results show our method produces drift-free global translation under a long recording time and reduces foot-skating effects.

</details>


### [303] [SimRecon: SimReady Compositional Scene Reconstruction from Real Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02133)
*Chong Xia,Kai Zhu,Zizhuo Wang,Fangfu Liu,Zhizheng Zhang,Yueqi Duan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SimRecon is a three-stage Perception–Generation–Simulation framework for compositional, object-centric scene reconstruction from video, augmented by Active Viewpoint Optimization and a Scene Graph Synthesizer to boost visual fidelity and physical plausibility, achieving state-of-the-art results on ScanNet.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing compositional reconstruction methods emphasize visual appearance and struggle to generalize to real-world, cluttered scenes. Simply chaining perception, object generation, and simulation often yields visually inaccurate assets and physically implausible scenes—limitations that hinder downstream simulation and interaction.

Method: 1) Perception: perform scene-level semantic reconstruction from video to obtain object masks, poses, and coarse geometry. 2) Generation: conduct single-object completion/generation, conditioned on optimal 2D projections selected via Active Viewpoint Optimization that searches 3D viewpoints to maximize informative observations. 3) Simulation: assemble generated assets in a simulator using a Scene Graph Synthesizer that infers and enforces spatial/physical relations (e.g., support, contact, adjacency) to construct scenes from scratch in a physically consistent manner. Two bridging modules—AVO (Perception→Generation) and SGS (Generation→Simulation)—address fidelity and plausibility gaps between stages.

Result: On the ScanNet dataset, SimRecon surpasses previous state-of-the-art methods, demonstrating higher visual fidelity of reconstructed objects and improved physical plausibility and consistency when scenes are instantiated in 3D simulators.

Conclusion: Bridging perception, generation, and simulation with targeted modules enables robust, object-centric reconstruction of cluttered real-world scenes that are both visually faithful and physically plausible, producing simulation-ready assets and layouts that generalize better than prior approaches.

Abstract: Compositional scene reconstruction seeks to create object-centric representations rather than holistic scenes from real-world videos, which is natively applicable for simulation and interaction. Conventional compositional reconstruction approaches primarily emphasize on visual appearance and show limited generalization ability to real-world scenarios. In this paper, we propose SimRecon, a framework that realizes a "Perception-Generation-Simulation" pipeline towards cluttered scene reconstruction, which first conducts scene-level semantic reconstruction from video input, then performs single-object generation, and finally assembles these assets in the simulator. However, naively combining these three stages leads to visual infidelity of generated assets and physical implausibility of the final scene, a problem particularly severe for complex scenes. Thus, we further propose two bridging modules between the three stages to address this problem. To be specific, for the transition from Perception to Generation, critical for visual fidelity, we introduce Active Viewpoint Optimization, which actively searches in 3D space to acquire optimal projected images as conditions for single-object completion. Moreover, for the transition from Generation to Simulation, essential for physical plausibility, we propose a Scene Graph Synthesizer, which guides the construction from scratch in 3D simulators, mirroring the native, constructive principle of the real world. Extensive experiments on the ScanNet dataset validate our method's superior performance over previous state-of-the-art approaches.

</details>


### [304] [OnlineX: Unified Online 3D Reconstruction and Understanding with Active-to-Stable State Evolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02134)
*Chong Xia,Fangfu Liu,Yule Wang,Yize Pang,Yueqi Duan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OnlineX is a feed-forward, real-time system that reconstructs 3D appearance and language-aware fields from streaming images by decoupling short-term “active” memory from long-term “stable” memory and fusing them to avoid drift, outperforming prior generalizable 3DGS methods in view synthesis and semantics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalizable 3D Gaussian Splatting enables fast per-scene-free reconstruction, but existing approaches are offline and suffer from cumulative drift when extended to continuous, streaming inputs—limiting use in robotics and VR/AR where online, stable, and accurate updates are required.

Method: Introduce a decoupled active-to-stable state evolution: split memory into an active state for rapidly capturing high-frequency local details and a persistent stable state for maintaining global structure; periodically fuse active into stable to balance fidelity and stability. Jointly model visual appearance and language (semantic) fields and employ an implicit Gaussian fusion module to improve reconstruction quality—all in a feed-forward pipeline processing streaming images.

Result: On standard benchmarks, OnlineX consistently surpasses prior work in novel view synthesis and semantic understanding, remains robust across varied input sequence lengths, and achieves real-time inference speeds.

Conclusion: Decoupling memory into active and stable states, combined with implicit Gaussian fusion and joint appearance-language modeling, enables reliable, drift-resistant, real-time online 3DGS reconstruction, broadening applicability to time-critical settings like robotics and AR/VR.

Abstract: Recent advances in generalizable 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) have enabled rapid 3D scene reconstruction within seconds, eliminating the need for per-scene optimization. However, existing methods primarily follow an offline reconstruction paradigm, lacking the capacity for continuous reconstruction, which limits their applicability to online scenarios such as robotics and VR/AR. In this paper, we introduce OnlineX, a feed-forward framework that reconstructs both 3D visual appearance and language fields in an online manner using only streaming images. A key challenge in online formulation is the cumulative drift issue, which is rooted in the fundamental conflict between two opposing roles of the memory state: an active role that constantly refreshes to capture high-frequency local geometry, and a stable role that conservatively accumulates and preserves the long-term global structure. To address this, we introduce a decoupled active-to-stable state evolution paradigm. Our framework decouples the memory state into a dedicated active state and a persistent stable state, and then cohesively fuses the information from the former into the latter to achieve both fidelity and stability. Moreover, we jointly model visual appearance and language fields and incorporate an implicit Gaussian fusion module to enhance reconstruction quality. Experiments on mainstream datasets demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms prior work in novel view synthesis and semantic understanding, showcasing robust performance across input sequences of varying lengths with real-time inference speed.

</details>


### [305] [OmniLottie: Generating Vector Animations via Parameterized Lottie Tokens](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02138)
*Yiying Yang,Wei Cheng,Sijin Chen,Honghao Fu,Xianfang Zeng,Yujun Cai,Gang Yu,Xingjun Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OmniLottie introduces a tokenizer for Lottie JSON and a VLM-based framework that turns multimodal instructions into high-quality, controllable vector animations, supported by a new large-scale dataset (MMLottie-2M).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Raw Lottie JSON contains extensive invariant metadata and formatting tokens that hinder learning effective vector animation generation. There is a need for fine-grained control over motion and visuals from multimodal instructions and for substantial training data to advance this field.

Method: Design a Lottie tokenizer that converts JSON animations into structured command–parameter sequences for shapes, animation functions, and control parameters. Build a generation framework (OmniLottie) on top of pretrained vision–language models to follow interleaved multimodal instructions. Curate MMLottie-2M, a large dataset of professionally designed Lottie animations paired with textual and visual annotations, and train/validate the system on it.

Result: Extensive experiments show OmniLottie generates vivid, high-quality, and semantically aligned vector animations that closely follow multimodal human instructions, outperforming unspecified baselines qualitatively (quantitative details not provided in the abstract).

Conclusion: Tokenizing Lottie into learnable sequences and leveraging VLMs enables controllable, instruction-following vector animation synthesis. The introduced dataset and framework advance research and practice in vector animation generation.

Abstract: OmniLottie is a versatile framework that generates high quality vector animations from multi-modal instructions. For flexible motion and visual content control, we focus on Lottie, a light weight JSON formatting for both shapes and animation behaviors representation. However, the raw Lottie JSON files contain extensive invariant structural metadata and formatting tokens, posing significant challenges for learning vector animation generation. Therefore, we introduce a well designed Lottie tokenizer that transforms JSON files into structured sequences of commands and parameters representing shapes, animation functions and control parameters. Such tokenizer enables us to build OmniLottie upon pretrained vision language models to follow multi-modal interleaved instructions and generate high quality vector animations. To further advance research in vector animation generation, we curate MMLottie-2M, a large scale dataset of professionally designed vector animations paired with textual and visual annotations. With extensive experiments, we validate that OmniLottie can produce vivid and semantically aligned vector animations that adhere closely to multi modal human instructions.

</details>


### [306] [Is Bigger Always Better? Efficiency Analysis in Resource-Constrained Small Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02142)
*Kwame Mbobda-Kuate,Gabriel Kasmi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: In data-scarce Earth observation, scaling laws break: a small YOLO11N at high resolution outperforms larger models in both efficiency and absolute accuracy (mAP50=0.617), with resolution the dominant lever; adding data at low resolution yields little benefit.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Test whether the common “bigger model + more data = better” assumption holds under EO’s compute/data constraints, and determine where to invest resources (model size, dataset size, or input resolution) for rooftop PV detection.

Method: Conduct a systematic scaling study on rooftop PV detection in Madagascar, varying three axes—model size (YOLO11 variants), dataset size, and input resolution. Measure efficiency as mAP50 per unit model size and analyze accuracy-throughput Pareto front across 44 configurations.

Result: Observed an efficiency inversion: YOLO11N (small) achieved both the highest efficiency (24× higher than YOLO11X) and the highest mAP50 (0.617). Resolution was the dominant lever (+120% efficiency); additional data provided negligible returns at low resolution. Small high-resolution setups were Pareto-dominant in accuracy-throughput with no tradeoff to resolve.

Conclusion: In data-scarce EO, prioritize small models and higher input resolution. Scaling model size or dataset size offers limited or even negative returns; “bigger” can be worse for both efficiency and absolute accuracy, and findings are robust to deployment objectives.

Abstract: Scaling laws assume larger models trained on more data consistently outperform smaller ones -- an assumption that drives model selection in computer vision but remains untested in resource-constrained Earth observation (EO). We conduct a systematic efficiency analysis across three scaling dimensions: model size, dataset size, and input resolution, on rooftop PV detection in Madagascar. Optimizing for model efficiency (mAP$_{50}$ per unit of model size), we find a consistent efficiency inversion: YOLO11N achieves both the highest efficiency ($24\times$ higher than YOLO11X) and the highest absolute mAP$_{50}$ (0.617). Resolution is the dominant resource allocation lever ($+$120% efficiency gain), while additional data yields negligible returns at low resolution. These findings are robust to the deployment objective: small high-resolution configurations are Pareto-dominant across all 44 setups in the joint accuracy-throughput space, leaving no tradeoff to resolve. In data-scarce EO, bigger is not just unnecessary: it can be worse.

</details>


### [307] [3D Field of Junctions: A Noise-Robust, Training-Free Structural Prior for Volumetric Inverse Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02149)
*Namhoon Kim,Narges Moeini,Justin Romberg,Sara Fridovich-Keil*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They introduce a training-free, fully volumetric 3D Field of Junctions (3D FoJ) prior that models each 3D patch as an optimized junction of wedges, enforces patch consistency, preserves sharp edges/corners, and serves as a plug-in denoiser or regularizer via projected/proximal steps, outperforming classical and neural baselines on low-dose CT, cryo-ET, and noisy lidar point clouds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D imaging modalities often operate at very low SNR, making robust volume denoising crucial. Existing learned denoisers risk hallucinations and can oversmooth sharp 3D structures. A general, data-free structural prior that preserves edges/corners and can be integrated into diverse inverse problems is needed.

Method: Extend 2D Field of Junctions to a fully volumetric 3D representation. For each 3D patch, optimize a junction of 3D wedges that best explains the local structure, while enforcing consistency across overlapping patches. Use this representation directly for denoising and as a structural prior within projected/proximal gradient descent for arbitrary volumetric inverse problems under low SNR.

Result: Across low-SNR settings in three domains—low-dose CT, cryo-electron tomography, and lidar point cloud denoising—the 3D FoJ prior enables successful volume reconstruction/denoising and surpasses a range of classical and neural baselines.

Conclusion: 3D FoJ provides an effective, training-free structural prior for volumetric imaging that reduces hallucination risk, preserves and enhances sharp 3D features, integrates easily into optimization frameworks, and consistently improves reconstruction quality across diverse low-SNR 3D tasks.

Abstract: Volume denoising is a foundational problem in computational imaging, as many 3D imaging inverse problems face high levels of measurement noise. Inspired by the strong 2D image denoising properties of Field of Junctions (ICCV 2021), we propose a novel, fully volumetric 3D Field of Junctions (3D FoJ) representation that optimizes a junction of 3D wedges that best explain each 3D patch of a full volume, while encouraging consistency between overlapping patches. In addition to direct volume denoising, we leverage our 3D FoJ representation as a structural prior that: (i) requires no training data, and thus precludes the risk of hallucination, (ii) preserves and enhances sharp edge and corner structures in 3D, even under low signal to noise ratio (SNR), and (iii) can be used as a drop-in denoising representation via projected or proximal gradient descent for any volumetric inverse problem with low SNR. We demonstrate successful volume reconstruction and denoising with 3D FoJ across three diverse 3D imaging tasks with low-SNR measurements: low-dose X-ray computed tomography (CT), cryogenic electron tomography (cryo-ET), and denoising point clouds such as those from lidar in adverse weather. Across these challenging low-SNR volumetric imaging problems, 3D FoJ outperforms a mixture of classical and neural methods.

</details>


### [308] [Bridging the gap between Performance and Interpretability: An Explainable Disentangled Multimodal Framework for Cancer Survival Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02162)
*Aniek Eijpe,Soufyan Lakbir,Melis Erdal Cesur,Sara P. Oliveira,Angelos Chatzimparmpas,Sanne Abeln,Wilson Silva*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DIMAFx is an explainable multimodal survival model that disentangles modality-shared and modality-specific signals from histopathology whole-slide images and transcriptomics, achieving state-of-the-art performance while exposing biologically meaningful drivers of risk.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal survival predictors are growing in accuracy but remain black boxes, obscuring how each data source contributes to risk estimates. There is a need for models that keep high performance yet provide interpretable, biologically grounded insights to support precision oncology.

Method: Introduce DIMAFx, a framework that learns disentangled latent representations separating modality-shared from modality-specific factors using histopathology WSIs and transcriptomics. The model’s interpretable design is coupled with SHAP to quantify feature importance and reveal multimodal interactions and biological signals linked to survival risk across cancer cohorts.

Result: Across multiple cancer datasets, DIMAFx matches or surpasses prior methods and shows improved disentanglement of representations. In breast cancer, the top predictors are largely modality-shared, including a factor capturing solid tumor morphology contextualized by late estrogen response; higher-grade morphology with pathway upregulation correlates with increased risk. Modality-specific features capture microenvironmental cues from adipose and stromal morphologies.

Conclusion: Multimodal models need not trade off performance for explainability. DIMAFx provides competitive survival prediction while yielding interpretable, biologically consistent insights, supporting deployment in precision medicine.

Abstract: While multimodal survival prediction models are increasingly more accurate, their complexity often reduces interpretability, limiting insight into how different data sources influence predictions. To address this, we introduce DIMAFx, an explainable multimodal framework for cancer survival prediction that produces disentangled, interpretable modality-specific and modality-shared representations from histopathology whole-slide images and transcriptomics data. Across multiple cancer cohorts, DIMAFx achieves state-of-the-art performance and improved representation disentanglement. Leveraging its interpretable design and SHapley Additive exPlanations, DIMAFx systematically reveals key multimodal interactions and the biological information encoded in the disentangled representations. In breast cancer survival prediction, the most predictive features contain modality-shared information, including one capturing solid tumor morphology contextualized primarily by late estrogen response, where higher-grade morphology aligned with pathway upregulation and increased risk, consistent with known breast cancer biology. Key modality-specific features capture microenvironmental signals from interacting adipose and stromal morphologies. These results show that multimodal models can overcome the traditional trade-off between performance and explainability, supporting their application in precision medicine.

</details>


### [309] [GeoDiT: Point-Conditioned Diffusion Transformer for Satellite Image Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02172)
*Srikumar Sastry,Dan Cher,Brian Wei,Aayush Dhakal,Subash Khanal,Dev Gupta,Nathan Jacobs*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GeoDiT is a diffusion transformer for controllable text-to-satellite image synthesis that uses sparse geolocated points with textual tags and an adaptive local attention mechanism, achieving state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pixel-level control inputs for satellite image generation are costly to annotate and semantically narrow. The field needs a more flexible, annotation-light control signal that still conveys rich semantics and spatial specificity.

Method: Introduce point-based conditioning where each control point provides a geographic location and a natural-language description. Build a diffusion transformer (DiT) that incorporates these point queries via an adaptive local attention module that regularizes attention near specified locations. Explore domain-specific training choices, including satellite image representation/alignment and geolocation encodings for conditioning.

Result: Experiments show strong controllability and image quality, with GeoDiT outperforming prior remote sensing generative models. The model benefits from the proposed attention regularization and chosen representations/encodings.

Conclusion: Sparse, semantically rich point prompts combined with adaptive local attention enable efficient, flexible control for satellite image generation, reducing annotation burden while improving state-of-the-art performance.

Abstract: We introduce GeoDiT, a diffusion transformer designed for text-to-satellite image generation with point-based control. Existing controlled satellite image generative models often require pixel-level maps that are time-consuming to acquire, yet semantically limited. To address this limitation, we introduce a novel point-based conditioning framework that controls the generation process through the spatial location of the points and the textual description associated with each point, providing semantically rich control signals. This approach enables flexible, annotation-friendly, and computationally simple inference for satellite image generation. To this end, we introduce an adaptive local attention mechanism that effectively regularizes the attention scores based on the input point queries. We systematically evaluate various domain-specific design choices for training GeoDiT, including the selection of satellite image representation for alignment and geolocation representation for conditioning. Our experiments demonstrate that GeoDiT achieves impressive generation performance, surpassing the state-of-the-art remote sensing generative models.

</details>


### [310] [From Leaderboard to Deployment: Code Quality Challenges in AV Perception Repositories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02194)
*Mateus Karvat,Bram Adams,Sidney Givigi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: They audit 178 AV perception repositories from top 3D detection leaderboards with static analysis and find only 7.3% are minimally production-ready; security problems are highly concentrated, CI/CD correlates with better maintainability, and leaderboard scores don’t reflect deployability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between benchmark-focused research and the software quality, safety, and maintainability requirements needed for real-world AV deployment under safety standards.

Method: Large-scale empirical study of 178 KITTI and NuScenes leaderboard repos using Pylint (code errors), Bandit (security), and Radon (maintainability/complexity). Defined basic production readiness as zero critical errors and no high-severity vulnerabilities; measured prevalence/concentration of issues and examined correlation of CI/CD adoption with maintainability; produced actionable security guidelines.

Result: Only 7.3% of repositories met the production-readiness bar. The top five security issues made up ~80% of occurrences. Repos with CI/CD showed better maintainability. Performance on leaderboards was decoupled from software quality indicators.

Conclusion: Benchmark performance is a poor proxy for deployment readiness in safety-critical AV perception. Targeted practices—addressing common security issues, eliminating critical errors, and adopting CI/CD—could materially improve code quality and safety; the community should integrate such standards into research workflows.

Abstract: Autonomous vehicle (AV) perception models are typically evaluated solely on benchmark performance metrics, with limited attention to code quality, production readiness and long-term maintainability. This creates a significant gap between research excellence and real-world deployment in safety-critical systems subject to international safety standards. To address this gap, we present the first large-scale empirical study of software quality in AV perception repositories, systematically analyzing 178 unique models from the KITTI and NuScenes 3D Object Detection leaderboards. Using static analysis tools (Pylint, Bandit, and Radon), we evaluated code errors, security vulnerabilities, maintainability, and development practices. Our findings revealed that only 7.3% of the studied repositories meet basic production-readiness criteria, defined as having zero critical errors and no high-severity security vulnerabilities. Security issues are highly concentrated, with the top five issues responsible for almost 80% of occurrences, which prompted us to develop a set of actionable guidelines to prevent them. Additionally, the adoption of Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment pipelines was correlated with better code maintainability. Our findings highlight that leaderboard performance does not reflect production readiness and that targeted interventions could substantially improve the quality and safety of AV perception code.

</details>


### [311] [HiFi-Inpaint: Towards High-Fidelity Reference-Based Inpainting for Generating Detail-Preserving Human-Product Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02210)
*Yichen Liu,Donghao Zhou,Jie Wang,Xin Gao,Guisheng Liu,Jiatong Li,Quanwei Zhang,Qiang Lyu,Lanqing Guo,Shilei Wen,Weiqiang Wang,Pheng-Ann Heng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HiFi-Inpaint is a reference-based inpainting framework for generating human–product images that preserves fine product details via a new attention module (SEA), a detail-aware loss (DAL) using high-frequency maps, and a curated 40K-sample dataset, achieving state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human–product images are crucial for advertising and e-commerce, but existing generation methods struggle to retain product details due to limited diverse training data, weak focus on fine-grained features, and coarse supervision signals.

Method: Propose HiFi-Inpaint, a high-fidelity reference-based inpainting approach tailored to human–product composition. It introduces Shared Enhancement Attention (SEA) to refine and propagate fine-grained product features, and Detail-Aware Loss (DAL) that leverages high-frequency maps for precise pixel-level supervision. A new dataset, HP-Image-40K, is assembled via self-synthesis and automatic filtering to improve data coverage and quality.

Result: Experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, with improved detail preservation in generated human–product images compared to prior methods.

Conclusion: Combining SEA, DAL, and the HP-Image-40K dataset enables precise guidance and faithful product detail reproduction in reference-based inpainting, establishing a new benchmark for human–product image generation.

Abstract: Human-product images, which showcase the integration of humans and products, play a vital role in advertising, e-commerce, and digital marketing. The essential challenge of generating such images lies in ensuring the high-fidelity preservation of product details. Among existing paradigms, reference-based inpainting offers a targeted solution by leveraging product reference images to guide the inpainting process. However, limitations remain in three key aspects: the lack of diverse large-scale training data, the struggle of current models to focus on product detail preservation, and the inability of coarse supervision for achieving precise guidance. To address these issues, we propose HiFi-Inpaint, a novel high-fidelity reference-based inpainting framework tailored for generating human-product images. HiFi-Inpaint introduces Shared Enhancement Attention (SEA) to refine fine-grained product features and Detail-Aware Loss (DAL) to enforce precise pixel-level supervision using high-frequency maps. Additionally, we construct a new dataset, HP-Image-40K, with samples curated from self-synthesis data and processed with automatic filtering. Experimental results show that HiFi-Inpaint achieves state-of-the-art performance, delivering detail-preserving human-product images.

</details>


<div id='cs.CL'></div>

# cs.CL [[Back]](#toc)

### [312] [From Global to Local: Learning Context-Aware Graph Representations for Document Classification and Summarization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00021)
*Ruangrin Ldallitsakool,Margarita Bugueño,Gerard de Melo*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They build sentence-level document graphs automatically using a dynamic sliding‑window attention mechanism, then apply Graph Attention Networks on these learned graphs to classify documents efficiently; preliminary tests also probe extractive summarization, with code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Document tasks need representations that capture local and mid‑range sentence dependencies and structural relations without the quadratic cost of full attention or the brittleness of heuristic graph construction. Leveraging recent sliding‑window attention helps model useful context while staying efficient.

Method: 1) Segment documents into sentences. 2) Use a dynamic sliding‑window attention module (per Bugueño & de Melo, 2025) to compute semantic/structural affinities among nearby sentences, yielding a weighted graph. 3) Train Graph Attention Networks over these graphs for document classification. 4) Exploratorily apply the constructed graphs to extractive summarization (e.g., ranking/selecting sentences based on graph signals).

Result: On document classification, GATs over the learned graphs deliver competitive accuracy with lower compute than prior approaches. On extractive summarization, results indicate promise but reveal current limitations. Implementation is available on GitHub.

Conclusion: Automatically learned, attention‑induced document graphs are effective and more resource‑friendly for classification; their use for summarization is promising but not yet mature, warranting further refinement and evaluation.

Abstract: This paper proposes a data-driven method to automatically construct graph-based document representations. Building upon the recent work of Bugueño and de Melo (2025), we leverage the dynamic sliding-window attention module to effectively capture local and mid-range semantic dependencies between sentences, as well as structural relations within documents. Graph Attention Networks (GATs) trained on our learned graphs achieve competitive results on document classification while requiring lower computational resources than previous approaches. We further present an exploratory evaluation of the proposed graph construction method for extractive document summarization, highlighting both its potential and current limitations. The implementation of this project can be found on GitHub.

</details>


### [313] [Noise reduction in BERT NER models for clinical entity extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00022)
*Kuldeep Jiwani,Yash K Jeengar,Ayush Dhaka*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They augment a clinical BERT-based NER with a supervised Noise Removal (NR) post-processor that exploits sequence-level probability patterns (a Probability Density Map capturing a “semantic-pull” effect) to distinguish weak vs. strong entity predictions, cutting false positives by 50–90%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clinical NLP often prioritizes very high precision. Fine-tuned encoder NERs tend to be overconfident due to softmax, making simple probability thresholding unreliable; thus a smarter way is needed to suppress spurious extractions without hurting recall excessively.

Method: 1) Pretrain in-house clinical BERT; 2) fine-tune for token-level NER; 3) collect token probability sequences; 4) train a supervised NR classifier using advanced features, notably a Probability Density Map that encodes sequence-wise probability distributions reflecting a semantic-pull phenomenon; 5) classify each predicted span as weak or strong and filter weak ones.

Result: Across multiple clinical NER models, the NR post-processor reduces false positives by 50–90%, and classifies weak vs. strong predictions with higher accuracy than naive thresholding. (Recall/overall F1 changes are not reported.)

Conclusion: A supervised, sequence-aware calibration/filtering layer on top of NER—leveraging PDM features—effectively mitigates softmax overconfidence and substantially improves precision in clinical extraction. Future work should quantify recall/F1 trade-offs and test generalization across datasets and entity types.

Abstract: Precision is of utmost importance in the realm of clinical entity extraction from clinical notes and reports. Encoder Models fine-tuned for Named Entity Recognition (NER) are an efficient choice for this purpose, as they don't hallucinate. We pre-trained an in-house BERT over clinical data and then fine-tuned it for NER. These models performed well on recall but could not close upon the high precision range, needed for clinical models. To address this challenge, we developed a Noise Removal model that refines the output of NER. The NER model assigns token-level entity tags along with probability scores for each token. Our Noise Removal (NR) model then analyzes these probability sequences and classifies predictions as either weak or strong. A naïve approach might involve filtering predictions based on low probability values; however, this method is unreliable. Owing to the characteristics of the SoftMax function, Transformer based architectures often assign disproportionately high confidence scores even to uncertain or weak predictions, making simple thresholding ineffective. To address this issue, we adopted a supervised modeling strategy in which the NR model leverages advanced features such as the Probability Density Map (PDM). The PDM captures the Semantic-Pull effect observed within Transformer embeddings, an effect that manifests in the probability distributions of NER class predictions across token sequences. This approach enables the model to classify predictions as weak or strong with significantly improved accuracy. With these NR models we were able to reduce False Positives across various clinical NER models by 50\% to 90\%.

</details>


### [314] [Personalization Increases Affective Alignment but Has Role-Dependent Effects on Epistemic Independence in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00024)
*Sean W. Kelley,Christoph Riedl*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Personalization changes how LLMs “agree” with users: it reliably boosts affective alignment (validation, hedging), while its effect on epistemic alignment depends on the model’s role—strengthening independence in advice settings but weakening it in peer-like social settings. The authors introduce role-sensitive metrics and a benchmark, showing effects stem from personalization itself, not token length or demographics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs can be sycophantic, especially when conditioned on user-specific context. As personalization becomes common, we need to know whether it amplifies uncritical agreement or can improve judgment, and how this varies by interaction role and context. Existing evaluations are piecemeal and lack systematic, role-aware analysis across models and tasks.

Method: Evaluate nine frontier LLMs on five datasets covering advice, moral judgment, and debate. Distinguish affective vs. epistemic alignment and manipulate role (advisor vs. social peer) and degree of personalization. Measure belief adoption, position stability, resistance to influence, emotional validation, and hedging/deference. Run robustness checks controlling for extra tokens and demographic-only conditioning.

Result: Personalization consistently increases affective alignment. Its impact on epistemic alignment is role-dependent: as advisors, models become more epistemically independent (more likely to challenge user presuppositions); as social peers, they become less independent and more liable to adopt user beliefs, abandoning prior positions more often under personalized user challenges. Robustness indicates effects are due to personalized conditioning rather than token length or demographics.

Conclusion: Personalization’s effect on sycophancy is nuanced and role-contingent. Evaluations of personalized AI must be role-sensitive. The paper offers measurement frameworks and a new benchmark for assessing goal alignment, informing safer deployment: personalization can aid truth-seeking in advisory roles but risks conformity in peer-like interactions.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are prone to sycophantic behavior, uncritically conforming to user beliefs. As models increasingly condition responses on user-specific context (personality traits, preferences, conversation history), they gain information to tailor agreement more effectively. Understanding how personalization modulates sycophancy is critical, yet systematic evaluation across models and contexts remains limited. We present a rigorous evaluation of personalization's impact on LLM sycophancy across nine frontier models and five benchmark datasets spanning advice, moral judgment, and debate contexts. We find that personalization generally increases affective alignment (emotional validation, hedging/deference), but affects epistemic alignment (belief adoption, position stability, resistance to influence) with context-dependent role modulation. When the LLM's role is to give advice, personalization strengthens epistemic independence (models challenge user presuppositions). When its role is that of a social peer, personalization decreases epistemic independence. In this role, extensively personalized user challenges causing LLMs to abandon their position at significantly higher rates. Robustness tests confirm that the effects are driven by personalized conditioning, not by additional input tokens per se or demographic information alone. Our work provides measurement frameworks for evaluating personalized AI systems, demonstrates the necessity of role-sensitive evaluation, and establishes a novel benchmark to assess goal alignment.

</details>


### [315] [TAB-PO: Preference Optimization with a Token-Level Adaptive Barrier for Token-Critical Structured Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00025)
*Samah Fodeh,Linhai Ma,Ganesh Puthiaraju,Srivani Talakokkul,Afshan Khan,Ashley Hagaman,Sarah R. Lowe,Aimee Kendall Roundtree*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: TAB-PO is a DPO variant that reweights token importance and adds a token-level barrier to prevent margin collapse, improving token-critical structured prediction; it yields ~4% relative micro-F1 gains on medical communication annotation and beats preference-optimization baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard DPO underperforms in structured tasks where preferred and rejected outputs differ by only a few tokens and where rare semantic tokens matter far more than frequent structural tokens. In such regimes, DPO faces margin collapse, likelihood squeezing, and gradient dilution, harming learning on the sparse, high-value tokens needed for accurate medical annotation.

Method: Token-Adaptive Barrier Preference Optimization augments DPO with token-weighted, reference-adjusted advantages to upweight high-value semantic tokens (e.g., labels, evidence spans) and introduces a conditional token-level barrier regularizer to shore up under-confident tokens, balancing SFT-likelihood anchoring with preference-driven separation in low-separation, importance-skewed settings.

Result: On medical communication annotation (joint hierarchical labels + evidence spans), TAB-PO delivers ~4% relative micro-F1 over SFT and consistently outperforms recent preference-optimization baselines.

Conclusion: Making preference optimization token-aware and adding barrier regularization addresses DPO’s brittleness on token-critical structured prediction, improving alignment and accuracy in low-separation, importance-skewed regimes.

Abstract: Direct Preference Optimization is an offline post-SFT method for aligning language models from preference pairs, with strong results in instruction following and summarization. However, DPO's sequence-level implicit reward can be brittle for token-critical structured prediction settings such as medical annotation, which often exhibit (i) low-separation preference pairs, where chosen and rejected completions differ by minimal edit distance (often 1-3 tokens), and (ii) token-importance skew, where sparse semantic tokens (hierarchical labels and evidence Spans) carry disproportionate task importance relative to high-frequency structural tokens (JSON scaffolding). In this regime, standard DPO suffers from margin collapse (insufficient log-probability separation between near-identical preferences), likelihood squeezing (the margin objective shifts the absolute likelihoods of both completions together), and gradient dilution, where uniform sequence-level weighting diffuses learning signal across shared scaffolding while rare, confusable label tokens receive weak, noisy updates. We introduce Token-Adaptive Barrier Preference Optimization (TAB-PO), which augments DPO with token-weighted, reference-adjusted advantages that prioritize high-value semantic tokens, and a conditional token-level barrier that regularizes under-confident tokens balancing SFT-anchored likelihood and preference-driven separation in low-separation, importance-skewed regimes. We evaluate TAB-PO on medical communication annotation, a task requiring joint prediction of hierarchical labels and evidence Spans from patient-provider messages. TAB-PO achieves a ~ 4% relative improvement in micro-F1 over SFT and consistently outperforms recent preference-optimization baselines.

</details>


### [316] [ActMem: Bridging the Gap Between Memory Retrieval and Reasoning in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00026)
*Xiaohui Zhang,Zequn Sun,Chengyuan Yang,Yaqin Jin,Yazhong Zhang,Wei Hu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ActMem is an actionable memory framework that converts dialogue history into a causal/semantic graph and uses counterfactual and commonsense reasoning to detect conflicts and guide decisions, with a new evaluation set (ActMemEval); it outperforms prior memory methods on complex, memory-dependent tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents often act as passive memory recorders and struggle with conflict detection and reasoning about implicit constraints, leading to failures in long-term, logic-heavy interactions.

Method: Transform unstructured dialogue into a structured causal + semantic graph; integrate retrieval with active causal reasoning; apply counterfactual reasoning and commonsense completion to infer implicit constraints and resolve conflicts; introduce ActMemEval for logic-driven evaluation beyond fact retrieval.

Result: Experiments show significant improvements over state-of-the-art baselines on complex, memory-dependent tasks, indicating better consistency and decision-making.

Conclusion: Coupling memory with causal, counterfactual reasoning enables more reliable, consistent LLM agents over long horizons; ActMem and ActMemEval push evaluation and capability beyond simple recall toward actionable reasoning.

Abstract: Effective memory management is essential for large language model (LLM) agents handling long-term interactions. Current memory frameworks typically treat agents as passive "recorders" and retrieve information without understanding its deeper implications. They may fail in scenarios requiring conflict detection and complex decision-making. To bridge this critical gap, we propose a novel actionable memory framework called ActMem that integrates memory retrieval with active causal reasoning. ActMem transforms unstructured dialogue history into a structured causal and semantic graph. By leveraging counterfactual reasoning and commonsense completion, it enables agents to deduce implicit constraints and resolve potential conflicts between past states and current intentions. Furthermore, we introduce a comprehensive dataset ActMemEval to evaluate agent reasoning capabilities in logic-driven scenarios, moving beyond the fact-retrieval focus of existing memory benchmarks.
