Experiments demonstrate that ActMem significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in handling complex, memory-dependent tasks, paving the way for more consistent and reliable intelligent assistants.

</details>


### [317] [EPPCMinerBen: A Novel Benchmark for Evaluating Large Language Models on Electronic Patient-Provider Communication via the Patient Portal](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00028)
*Samah Fodeh,Yan Wang,Linhai Ma,Srivani Talakokkul,Jordan M. Alpert,Sarah Schellhorn*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: EPPCMinerBen is a new benchmark to evaluate LLMs on understanding patient-provider secure messages via three tasks—code classification, subcode classification, and evidence extraction—using 1,933 expert-annotated sentences from 752 messages. Large, instruction-tuned models perform best (notably Llama-3.1-70B for evidence extraction, Llama-3.3-70b for code classification, and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B for subcodes), few-shot prompting helps, and smaller models struggle with fine-grained reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As clinical communication shifts to secure messaging, extracting communicative intent and supporting evidence from EPPC is crucial for care quality, adherence, and workflow optimization. There is no dedicated, publicly available benchmark to systematically evaluate LLMs on discourse-level understanding in this setting.

Method: Construct a benchmark (EPPCMinerBen) with expert-annotated EPPC sentences from a hospital portal (YNHH). Define three subtasks: (1) Code Classification, (2) Subcode Classification (finer-grained labels), and (3) Evidence Extraction (span selection). Evaluate multiple LLMs under zero-shot and few-shot prompts; report F1 scores; plan data release via NCI Cancer Data Service.

Result: Performance varied by task and model. Llama-3.1-70B led evidence extraction (F1 82.84%) and was strong in classification. Llama-3.3-70b-Instruct was best for code classification (F1 67.03%). DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B topped subcode classification (F1 48.25%). sdoh-llama-3-70B showed consistent performance. Smaller models underperformed, especially on subcodes (>30% F1). Few-shot prompting generally improved results.

Conclusion: Large, instruction-tuned LLMs are currently better suited for EPPC discourse tasks—particularly evidence extraction—while smaller models struggle with fine-grained categorization. EPPCMinerBen fills a benchmarking gap and can drive research on generalization, fine-grained reasoning, and practical analysis of patient-provider messaging.

Abstract: Effective communication in health care is critical for treatment outcomes and adherence. With patient-provider exchanges shifting to secure messaging, analyzing electronic patient-communication (EPPC) data is both essential and challenging. We introduce EPPCMinerBen, a benchmark for evaluating LLMs in detecting communication patterns and extracting insights from electronic patient-provider messages. EPPCMinerBen includes three sub-tasks: Code Classification, Subcode Classification, and Evidence Extraction. Using 1,933 expert annotated sentences from 752 secure messages of the patient portal at Yale New Haven Hospital, it evaluates LLMs on identifying communicative intent and supportive text. Benchmarks span various LLMs under zero-shot and few-shot settings, with data to be released via the NCI Cancer Data Service. Model performance varied across tasks and settings. Llama-3.1-70B led in evidence extraction (F1: 82.84%) and performed well in classification. Llama-3.3-70b-Instruct outperformed all models in code classification (F1: 67.03%). DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B excelled in subcode classification (F1: 48.25%), while sdoh-llama-3-70B showed consistent performance. Smaller models underperformed, especially in subcode classification (>30% F1). Few-shot prompting improved most tasks. Our results show that large, instruction-tuned models generally perform better in EPPCMinerBen tasks, particularly evidence extraction while smaller models struggle with fine-grained reasoning. EPPCMinerBen provides a benchmark for discourse-level understanding, supporting future work on model generalization and patient-provider communication analysis. Keywords: Electronic Patient-Provider Communication, Large language models, Data collection, Prompt engineering

</details>


### [318] [Embracing Anisotropy: Turning Massive Activations into Interpretable Control Knobs for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00029)
*Youngji Roh,Hyunjin Cho,Jaehyung Kim*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LLM “massive activation” directions are not just artifacts; they are interpretable, domain-critical units that can be identified by magnitude alone and selectively steered to control model behavior, outperforming whole-vector steering for domain adaptation and jailbreak-related tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prevailing views treat extreme activation dimensions as nuisances to suppress. The authors argue these dimensions embody specialized, interpretable features tied to domains, and that exploiting them could yield finer control and better transfer/adaptation without retraining.

Method: 1) Define Domain-Critical Dimensions (DCDs) via a simple, training-free magnitude-based selection criterion. 2) Show these DCDs act as semantic detectors (symbolic/quantitative patterns, domain-specific terms). 3) Propose Critical Dimension Steering: apply activation steering only along the identified DCDs rather than the whole representation.

Result: Identified dimensions align with interpretable semantics and, when selectively steered, deliver better performance than whole-dimension steering on benchmarks involving domain adaptation and jailbreaking, indicating more precise and effective control.

Conclusion: Massive-activation anisotropy reflects meaningful, specialized functional units. A magnitude-based DCD selection plus targeted steering yields more interpretable and effective control of LLM behavior than conventional full-representation steering.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit highly anisotropic internal representations, often characterized by massive activations, a phenomenon where a small subset of feature dimensions possesses magnitudes significantly larger than the rest. While prior works view these extreme dimensions primarily as artifacts to be managed, we propose a distinct perspective: these dimensions serve as intrinsic interpretable functional units arising from domain specialization. Specifically, we propose a simple magnitude-based criterion to identify Domain-Critical Dimensions in a training-free manner. Our analyses reveal that such dimensions behave as interpretable semantic detectors for symbolic/quantitative patterns or domain-specific terms. In addition, we introduce Critical Dimension Steering, which applies activation steering exclusively to the identified dimensions. Empirical results show that this approach outperforms conventional whole-dimension steering in domain adaptation and jailbreaking scenarios.

</details>


### [319] [SimpleTool: Parallel Decoding for Real-Time LLM Function Calling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00030)
*Xiaoxin Shi,Jiaxin Wan,Linkang Dong,Wei Jiang,Yue Liu,Zengfeng Huang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SimpleTool adds special tokens that both compress structured function-call tokens and enable parallel generation of function name and arguments, yielding 3–6x (up to 9.6x) speedups with minimal overhead and competitive accuracy, achieving ~61 ms P50 latency (16 Hz) at 4B scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autoregressive decoding is too slow for real-time tool use (e.g., 10 Hz control) in embodied agents, game AI, and avatars. Function calling differs from free-form text: outputs have redundant structure and arguments with weak causal dependencies—properties that can be exploited to cut latency.

Method: Introduce special tokens playing a dual role: (1) compress low-entropy structured tokens (delimiters, parameter names) by 4–6x; (2) act as mode selectors enabling independent, parallel generation of the function name and its arguments. Implement across Qwen models (0.5B–14B), with parallelization incurring ~+8.2% overhead and compatibility with quantization on consumer GPUs.

Result: Across five benchmarks, achieve 3–6x end-to-end speedups (up to 9.6x) while maintaining or improving accuracy. On Mobile Actions, ST-Qwen-0.5B surpasses Google’s FunctionGemma in accuracy and latency consistency. With quantization, attain 61.2 ms P50 latency at 4B scale, enabling 16 Hz real-time control.

Conclusion: Joint compression of structured tokens and parallel generation for function calling effectively overcomes autoregressive latency limits, enabling real-time, latency-critical deployments and narrowing the gap between LLM tool-use and practical control applications.

Abstract: LLM-based function calling enables intelligent agents to interact with external tools and environments, yet autoregressive decoding imposes a fundamental latency bottleneck that limits real-time applications such as embodied intelligence, game AI, and interactive avatars (e.g., 10 Hz control frequency). We observe that function calling differs fundamentally from free-form text generation: structured outputs exhibit substantial token redundancy (delimiters, parameter names), and arguments exhibit weak causal dependencies. Crucially, these two properties must be exploited jointly to achieve real-time performance. We present SimpleTool, which introduces special tokens that serve a dual role: compressing low-entropy tokens (4-6x reduction) while acting as mode selectors that enable independent parallel generation of function name and arguments. This synergistic design achieves 3-6x end-to-end speedup (up to 9.6x) with only +8.2% parallelization overhead. Experiments on five benchmarks across Qwen-series models (0.5B-14B) demonstrate substantial speedup while maintaining competitive or improved accuracy. On Mobile Actions, ST-Qwen-0.5B outperforms Google's FunctionGemma in both accuracy and latency consistency. With quantization on consumer-grade GPU, SimpleTool achieves 61.2ms P50 latency, enabling 16 Hz real-time control at 4B model scale, bridging the gap between LLM function calling and latency-critical real-world deployment.

</details>


### [320] [GRIP: Geometric Refinement and Adaptive Information Potential for Data Efficiency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00031)
*Changhao Wang,Jiaolong Yang,Xinhao Yao,Yunfei Yu,Peng Jiao,Lu Yu,Junpeng Fang,Riccardo Cantoro,Qing Cui,Jun Zhou*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: GRIP is an adaptive, geometry-driven data curation framework that reallocates sampling toward underrepresented semantic regions (via RAP) and performs length-aware intra-cluster selection, yielding LLMs that outperform SOTA and even models trained on 3× larger uncurated corpora.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM performance is now constrained more by data efficiency than sheer volume. Existing selection pipelines split global distribution balancing from local instance choice, breaking hierarchical structure and missing long-tail value. A unified approach is needed to preserve both corpus-wide coverage and within-cluster quality.

Method: Model the corpus as an information-dense geometric space. Use a Rapid Adaptation Probe (RAP) to estimate each semantic cluster’s information potential and dynamically shift sampling budgets toward clusters with representation deficits. Within each cluster, apply a length-rectified geometric prior to mitigate embedding density artifacts and retain long-tail, logically rich sequences. Evaluated on Mixture-of-Experts pretraining up to 300B tokens.

Result: Across extensive evaluations, GRIP consistently beats state-of-the-art data selection baselines and surpasses the performance of models trained on 3× larger uncurated datasets, on MoE models up to 300B tokens.

Conclusion: Unifying global balancing and local selection via a geometric, information-potential view improves data efficiency and preserves hierarchical integrity in large-scale pretraining, providing a robust foundation for adaptive data curation beyond brute-force scaling.

Abstract: The performance of Large Language Models (LLMs) is increasingly governed by data efficiency rather than raw scaling volume. However, existing selection methods often decouple global distribution balancing from local instance selection, compromising the hierarchical integrity of the training set. We introduce \textbf{GRIP} (Geometric Refinement and Adaptive Information Potential), a framework that unifies these dimensions by modeling the corpus as an information-dense geometric space. GRIP employs a \textbf{Rapid Adaptation Probe (RAP)} to quantify the information potential of semantic clusters, dynamically re-allocating the sampling budget to regions with the highest representation deficits. Subsequently, we perform Intra-Cluster Selection using a \textbf{length-rectified geometric prior} to counteract embedding density artifacts and preserve long-tail logical sequences. Extensive evaluations on Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models up to 300B tokens demonstrate that GRIP consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, \textbf{surpassing the performance of models trained on $3\times$ larger uncurated datasets}. Our work establishes a robust geometric foundation for adaptive data curation in large-scale pre-training.

</details>


### [321] [Autorubric: A Unified Framework for Rubric-Based LLM Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00077)
*Delip Rao,Chris Callison-Burch*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces Autorubric, a unified, open-source framework for rubric-based LLM evaluation that supports multiple criterion types, ensemble judging, calibration, bias mitigations, and psychometric reliability metrics; validated on three benchmarks and releases the mixed-criteria CHARM-100 dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rubric-based LLM evaluation is widely used but fragmented across papers with inconsistent terminology and partial, ad-hoc techniques. A unified, reliable, and production-ready approach is needed to standardize practices and improve comparability and robustness.

Method: Define a unified evaluation framework and implement it as Autorubric. Key features: binary/ordinal/nominal criteria with weights; single- and multi-judge ensembles with majority/weighted/unanimous/any-vote aggregation; few-shot calibration using verdict-balanced sampling; mitigations for position bias (option shuffling), verbosity bias (length penalties), and criterion conflation (per-criterion atomic evaluation with natural-language justifications); psychometric reliability metrics (Cohen’s κ, weighted κ, correlations, distributional tests); and production tooling (caching, checkpointing/resumability, multi-provider rate limiting, cost tracking). Evaluate across RiceChem (per-criterion binary + few-shot), ResearcherBench (multi-judge ensembles across judge models), and CHARM-100 (mixed criteria). Also contribute CHARM-100 dataset with ground-truth labels for binary/ordinal/nominal criteria.

Result: Autorubric reproduces results consistent with prior benchmarks while showcasing its capabilities on three tasks: accurate per-criterion binary evaluation with few-shot calibration (RiceChem), robust multi-judge ensemble evaluation (ResearcherBench), and handling heterogeneous criteria (CHARM-100). CHARM-100 is released as a 100-sample chatbot dataset with ground truth across all three criterion types for stress-testing frameworks.

Conclusion: Autorubric standardizes rubric-based LLM evaluation, offering a flexible, reliable, and practical toolkit that addresses common biases and reports psychometric reliability, suitable for both research and production. The new CHARM-100 dataset enables rigorous testing on mixed-criteria settings and supports broader adoption of consistent evaluation practices.

Abstract: Rubric-based evaluation with large language models (LLMs) has become standard practice for assessing text generation at scale, yet the underlying techniques are scattered across papers with inconsistent terminology and partial solutions. We present a unified framework: each identified technique is paired with its realization in Autorubric, an open-source Python framework proposed in this paper. Autorubric supports binary, ordinal, and nominal criteria with configurable weights; single-judge and multi-judge ensemble evaluation with majority, weighted, unanimous, and any-vote aggregation; few-shot calibration with verdict-balanced sampling; and mitigations for position bias (option shuffling), verbosity bias (length penalties), and criterion conflation (per-criterion atomic evaluation with natural language explanations). The framework provides reliability metrics drawn from psychometrics (Cohen's $κ$, weighted $κ$, correlation coefficients, and distribution-level tests) alongside production infrastructure including response caching, checkpointing with resumable runs, multi-provider rate limiting, and cost tracking. We evaluate Autorubric on three benchmarks spanning educational assessment, deep research evaluation, and chatbot quality assessment, demonstrating that it produces results consistent with published benchmarks while exercising the framework's key capabilities: per-criterion binary evaluation with few-shot calibration (RiceChem), multi-judge ensemble evaluation across judge models (ResearcherBench), and mixed criterion types combining binary, ordinal, and nominal scales (CHARM-100). We also contribute CHARM-100, a 100-sample chatbot evaluation dataset with per-sample ground truth labels across all three criterion types, designed to stress-test rubric evaluation frameworks on heterogeneous criteria.

</details>


### [322] [Iterative LLM-based improvement for French Clinical Interview Transcription and Speaker Diarization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00086)
*Ambre Marie,Thomas Bertin,Guillaume Dardenne,Gwenolé Quellec*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A multi-pass LLM post-processor that alternates speaker and word recognition improves French clinical ASR, significantly reducing WDER on suicide-prevention calls while remaining stable on neurosurgery data, with zero failures and acceptable offline cost (RTF 0.32).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: French medical conversations are hard for ASR, with spontaneous speech pushing WER above 30% and complicating speaker attribution. There is a need to boost transcription and diarization accuracy without retraining ASR systems and to validate feasibility for clinical deployment.

Method: Introduce an alternating multi-pass LLM pipeline with Speaker Recognition and Word Recognition passes. Conduct ablations on four design choices—model selection, prompting strategy, pass ordering, and iteration depth—using two French clinical datasets (suicide-prevention telephone counseling; awake neurosurgery consultations). Evaluate primarily with WDER, apply Wilcoxon signed-rank tests for significance, and report computational cost via real-time factor (RTF). The strongest results are obtained with Qwen3-Next-80B.

Result: Significant WDER reductions on suicide-prevention conversations (p<0.05, n=18); stable performance (no significant degradation) on awake neurosurgery consultations (n=10); zero output failures; acceptable computational overhead for offline use (RTF 0.32).

Conclusion: An alternating speaker/word LLM post-processing architecture is effective and robust for French clinical ASR, offering domain-dependent gains (notably in counseling calls) and is practically deployable offline. Findings support iterative LLM passes as a viable path to improve word accuracy and speaker attribution without changing the base ASR.

Abstract: Automatic speech recognition for French medical conversations remains challenging, with word error rates often exceeding 30% in spontaneous clinical speech. This study proposes a multi-pass LLM post-processing architecture alternating between Speaker Recognition and Word Recognition passes to improve transcription accuracy and speaker attribution. Ablation studies on two French clinical datasets (suicide prevention telephone counseling and preoperative awake neurosurgery consultations) investigate four design choices: model selection, prompting strategy, pass ordering, and iteration depth. Using Qwen3-Next-80B, Wilcoxon signed-rank tests confirm significant WDER reductions on suicide prevention conversations (p < 0.05, n=18), while maintaining stability on awake neurosurgery consultations (n=10), with zero output failures and acceptable computational cost (RTF 0.32), suggesting feasibility for offline clinical deployment.

</details>


### [323] [Stepwise Penalization for Length-Efficient Chain-of-Thought Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00296)
*Xintong Li,Sha Li,Rongmei Lin,Hongye Jin,Linwei Li,Hejie Cui,Sarah Zhang,Chia-Yuan Chang,Kewei Cheng,Besnik Fetahu,Priyanka Nigam,Jingbo Shang,Bing Yin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SWAP is a step-level RL framework that trims redundant reasoning by penalizing low-importance steps (measured via on‑policy log‑probability gains toward the correct answer), cutting chain length by 64.3% while boosting accuracy by 5.7%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large reasoning models often overthink: longer chains raise compute cost without accuracy gains. Existing RL uses coarse trajectory-level length penalties or indirect step signals, failing to distinguish essential from redundant steps. A fine-grained, step-level objective is needed to compress reasoning without harming correctness.

Method: Estimate each step’s importance by its on-policy improvement in log-probability toward the correct answer. Treat excess length as a penalty mass and reallocate it to penalize low-importance steps more than high-importance ones. Train with a unified outcome–process advantage within group-relative policy optimization to jointly optimize final correctness and per-step efficiency.

Result: Across extensive experiments, SWAP reduces average reasoning length by 64.3% and increases accuracy by 5.7% compared to the base model, indicating that it removes redundancy while preserving or enhancing critical reasoning.

Conclusion: Making reasoning length an explicit step-level RL objective via adaptive penalties yields fine-grained compression and better accuracy. SWAP effectively curbs overthinking by preserving high-value steps and pruning low-value ones, outperforming blunt trajectory-level penalties.

Abstract: Large reasoning models improve with more test-time computation, but often overthink, producing unnecessarily long chains-of-thought that raise cost without improving accuracy. Prior reinforcement learning approaches typically rely on a single outcome reward with trajectory-level length penalties, which cannot distinguish essential from redundant reasoning steps and therefore yield blunt compression. Although recent work incorporates step-level signals, such as offline pruning, supervised data construction, or verifier-based intermediate rewards, reasoning length is rarely treated as an explicit step-level optimization objective during RL. We propose Step-wise Adaptive Penalization (SWAP), a fine-grained framework that allocates length reduction across steps based on intrinsic contribution. We estimate step importance from the model's on-policy log-probability improvement toward the correct answer, then treat excess length as a penalty mass redistributed to penalize low-importance steps more heavily while preserving high-importance reasoning. We optimize with a unified outcome-process advantage within group-relative policy optimization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SWAP reduces reasoning length by 64.3% on average while improving accuracy by 5.7% relative to the base model.

</details>


### [324] [From Prerequisites to Predictions: Validating a Geometric Hallucination Taxonomy Through Controlled Induction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00307)
*Matic Korun*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: In GPT-2 (124M), only “coverage-gap” hallucinations show robust geometric separability—and mainly via vector magnitude (norm) rather than direction—while “center-drift” and “wrong-well” types do not; token-level analyses spuriously inflate significance, so prompt-level inference is crucial.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether a proposed geometric taxonomy of hallucinations (Type 1: center-drift, Type 2: wrong-well, Type 3: coverage-gap) actually distinguishes failure modes in model representations, and evaluate statistical robustness and pitfalls (e.g., pseudoreplication) when measuring such effects.

Method: Controlled induction of the three hallucination types in GPT-2 (124M) with a two-level design using prompts (N=15 per group) as the unit of inference; each experiment repeated 20 times with different generation seeds. Compare separability in static embeddings vs contextual hidden states, focusing on norm (magnitude) and direction effects; apply significance testing with Holm correction; contrast prompt-level vs token-level tests.

Result: Static embeddings: Type 3 shows robust norm separation (significant in 18/20 runs; Holm-corrected in 14/20; median r=+0.61). Contextual hidden states: Type 3 effect direction is stable (19/20 runs) but underpowered at N=15 (significant in 4/20; median r=-0.28). Types 1 and 2 fail to separate in either space (≤3/20 runs). Token-level tests inflate significance by 4–16× due to pseudoreplication, replicated in all 20 runs.

Conclusion: Coverage-gap hallucinations are the most geometrically distinctive at this scale, distinguished by magnitude rather than direction; Type 1/2 non-separation appears genuine in GPT-2 (124M). Reliable inference requires prompt-level analysis to avoid pseudoreplication; the taxonomy is partially validated, chiefly for Type 3.

Abstract: We test whether a geometric hallucination taxonomy -- classifying failures as center-drift (Type~1), wrong-well convergence (Type~2), or coverage gaps (Type~3) -- can distinguish hallucination types through controlled induction in GPT-2. Using a two-level statistical design with prompts ($N = 15$/group) as the unit of inference, we run each experiment 20 times with different generation seeds to quantify result stability. In static embeddings, Type~3 norm separation is robust (significant in 18/20 runs, Holm-corrected in 14/20, median $r = +0.61$). In contextual hidden states, the Type~3 norm effect direction is stable (19/20 runs) but underpowered at $N = 15$ (significant in 4/20, median $r = -0.28$). Types~1 and~2 do not separate in either space (${\leq}\,3/20$ runs). Token-level tests inflate significance by 4--16$\times$ through pseudoreplication -- a finding replicated across all 20 runs. The results establish coverage-gap hallucinations as the most geometrically distinctive failure mode, carried by magnitude rather than direction, and confirm the Type~1/2 non-separation as genuine at 124M parameters.

</details>


### [325] [When Metrics Disagree: Automatic Similarity vs. LLM-as-a-Judge for Clinical Dialogue Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00314)
*Bian Sun,Zhenjian Wang,Orvill de la Torre,Zirui Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Fine-tune Llama 2–7B on real patient–doctor transcripts to improve medical responses; text-similarity metrics show gains, but GPT‑4-as-judge disagrees; authors urge human medical expert evaluation before any clinical use.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs often underperform and can be unsafe in medical contexts (e.g., LIU findings). The work aims to improve accuracy/precision for medical queries and examine deployment implications of more accurate LLMs in healthcare.

Method: Select Llama 2–7B (decoder-only) as baseline and apply supervised fine-tuning on transcripts of real patient–doctor interactions to capture domain-specific nuances. Evaluate mainly with text similarity metrics due to resource limits and compare against a GPT‑4 evaluation.

Result: Significant improvements across most quantitative text-similarity dimensions after fine-tuning; however, GPT‑4’s qualitative evaluation did not align with these gains.

Conclusion: Domain-specific supervised fine-tuning can raise surface-level alignment but may not guarantee clinically sound answers. LLM-as-judge can conflict with quantitative metrics; therefore, human medical expert review is essential before deployment, and care is needed in healthcare integration.

Abstract: This paper details the baseline model selection, fine-tuning process, evaluation methods, and the implications of deploying more accurate LLMs in healthcare settings. As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly employed to address diverse problems, including medical queries, concerns about their reliability have surfaced. A recent study by Long Island University highlighted that LLMs often perform poorly in medical contexts, potentially leading to harmful misguidance for users. To address this, our research focuses on fine-tuning the Llama 2 7B, a transformer-based, decoder-only model, using transcripts from real patient-doctor interactions. Our objective was to enhance the model's accuracy and precision in responding to medical queries. We fine-tuned the model using a supervised approach, emphasizing domain-specific nuances captured in the training data. In the best scenario, the model results should be reviewed and evaluated by real medical experts. Due to resource constraints, the performance of the fine-tuned model was evaluated using text similarity metrics. The fine-tuned model demonstrated significant improvements across all key dimensions except GPT-4's evaluation. The evaluations of ChatGPT4 are quite different from the quantitative results; here, we not only suggest, but also propose that the result should be evaluated by human medical experts.

</details>


### [326] [How Large Language Models Get Stuck: Early structure with persistent errors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00359)
*Alokesh Manna,William Snyder,Whitney Tabor*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Training OPT on the 100M-word BabyLM corpus and evaluating on BLiMP, the model fails on ~1/3 of linguistic phenomena to prefer grammatical over ungrammatical sentences, with early, persistent mis-separation; authors hypothesize early bigram-driven bias causes entrenched errors (Bigram Hypothesis) and outline tests in progress.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Use linguistic diagnostics to understand and improve the efficiency and reliability of LLM training, especially why models persistently mis-handle certain syntactic/semantic contrasts and how early training dynamics create entrenched mistakes.

Method: Train Meta’s OPT on the BabyLM (100M tokens) dataset; evaluate across training iterations on BLiMP’s 67 minimal-pair classes by comparing likelihoods assigned to grammatical vs. ungrammatical sentences. Analyze error trajectories qualitatively (linguistic theory + deep learning theory) and quantitatively (numerical testing). Identify which BLiMP tests are diagnostically meaningful. Propose the Bigram Hypothesis and outline an approach to test it on selected BLiMP classes.

Result: In about one-third of BLiMP classes, OPT does not consistently assign higher likelihood to grammatical sentences, even after extensive training. When failing, the model often establishes an erroneous likelihood gap early and maintains it through training. Some BLiMP tests do not provide meaningful guidance. These findings suggest costly entrenched biases that require later reversal.

Conclusion: They propose the Bigram Hypothesis: early bigram statistics can bias learning toward incorrect distinctions, yielding entrenched errors. They advocate testing this on carefully chosen BLiMP classes and imply training strategies that account for early bigram effects to avoid persistent mis-generalizations.

Abstract: Linguistic insights may help make Large Language Model (LLM) training more efficient. We trained Meta's OPT model on the 100M word BabyLM dataset, and evaluated it on the BLiMP benchmark, which consists of 67 classes, each defined by sentence pairs that differ in a targeted syntactic or semantic rule violation. We tested the model's preference for grammatical over ungrammatical sentences across training iterations and grammatical types. In nearly one-third of the BLiMP classes, OPT fails to consistently assign a higher likelihood to grammatical sentences, even after extensive training. When it fails, it often establishes a clear (erroneous) separation of the likelihoods at an early stage of processing and sustains this to the end of our training phase. We hypothesize that this mis-categorization is costly because it creates entrenched biases that must, eventually, be reversed in order for the model to perform well. We probe this phenomenon using a mixture of qualitative (based on linguistic theory and the theory of Deep Learning) and quantitative (based on numerical testing) assessments. Our qualitative assessments indicate that only some BLiMP tests are meaningful guides. We conclude by articulating a hypothesis, the Bigram Hypothesis, which claims that the learning process will exhibit erroneous entrenchment if bigram statistics bias the model toward wrong distinctions early in training, and we describe a method (in progress) of testing the hypothesis on appropriately selected BLiMP classes.

</details>


### [327] [Distribution-Aware Companding Quantization of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00364)
*Athul Radhakrishnan,Siddhant Mohan,Mahima Sachdeva*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Train LLMs to predict multiple future tokens per position (with n parallel heads on a shared trunk) to improve sample efficiency, downstream performance—especially for code—and enable faster inference, all without added training time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard next-token prediction may underuse available supervision at each position and be sample-inefficient, limiting generative performance and the emergence of algorithmic reasoning. The authors seek a simple modification that extracts more learning signal per token and accelerates inference.

Method: At each sequence position, add n independent output heads to predict the next n tokens simultaneously from a shared backbone. Treat multi-token prediction as an auxiliary objective alongside standard training. Evaluate across model sizes, epochs, and domains (code and natural language).

Result: Consistent downstream gains with no training-time overhead; benefits grow with model size and persist over multiple epochs. Strong improvements in code generation: a 13B model solves 12% more HumanEval and 17% more MBPP problems than comparable next-token baselines. Small algorithmic tasks show accelerated emergence of induction heads and reasoning skills. With 4-token prediction, inference can be up to 3× faster even at large batch sizes.

Conclusion: Multi-token prediction is a simple, scalable training change that boosts efficiency and quality, particularly for larger models and coding tasks, while also enabling multi-token emission for substantial inference speedups. It likely encourages helpful inductive biases (e.g., induction heads) and offers a practical path to better generative LLMs without extra training cost.

Abstract: Large language models such as GPT and Llama are trained with a next-token prediction loss. In this work, we suggest that training language models to predict multiple future tokens at once results in higher sample efficiency. More specifically, at each position in the training corpus, we ask the model to predict the following n tokens using n independent output heads, operating on top of a shared model trunk. Considering multi-token prediction as an auxiliary training task, we measure improved downstream capabilities with no overhead in training time for both code and natural language models. The method is increasingly useful for larger model sizes and keeps its appeal when training for multiple epochs. Gains are especially pronounced on generative benchmarks like coding, where our models consistently outperform strong baselines by several percentage points. Our 13B parameter models solves 12 % more problems on HumanEval and 17 % more on MBPP than comparable next-token models. Experiments on small algorithmic tasks demonstrate that multi-token prediction is favorable for the development of induction heads and algorithmic reasoning capabilities. As an additional benefit, models trained with 4-token prediction are up to 3X times faster at inference, even with large batch sizes.

</details>


### [328] [Policy Compliance of User Requests in Natural Language for AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00369)
*Pedro Cisneros-Velarde*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces the first industry-relevant benchmark of policy compliance for natural-language user requests to AI systems and uses it to evaluate LLMs; results show uneven performance, underscoring the task’s difficulty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Organizations must ensure user prompts comply with diverse internal policies to guarantee safe, reliable AI use, but lack a standardized benchmark to assess and compare compliance-checking approaches.

Method: Construct an annotated dataset of user requests labeled for compliance across multiple policies (rooted in tech-industry applications). Evaluate various LLMs on the compliance-assessment task under different solution methods and compare performance metrics.

Result: Substantial performance variation across models and solution strategies; no uniformly superior approach. Metrics highlight that accurate policy compliance assessment remains challenging.

Conclusion: The benchmark enables systematic, apples-to-apples evaluation of policy compliance assessment. Current LLMs are not consistently reliable for this task, motivating further research and specialized techniques.

Abstract: Consider an organization whose users send requests in natural language to an AI system that fulfills them by carrying out specific tasks. In this paper, we consider the problem of ensuring such user requests comply with a list of diverse policies determined by the organization with the purpose of guaranteeing the safe and reliable use of the AI system. We propose, to the best of our knowledge, the first benchmark consisting of annotated user requests of diverse compliance with respect to a list of policies. Our benchmark is related to industrial applications in the technology sector. We then use our benchmark to evaluate the performance of various LLM models on policy compliance assessment under different solution methods. We analyze the differences on performance metrics across the models and solution methods, showcasing the challenging nature of our problem.

</details>


### [329] [LLM-Bootstrapped Targeted Finding Guidance for Factual MLLM-based Medical Report Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00426)
*Cunyuan Yang,Dejuan Song,Xiaotao Pang,Qianqian Shen,Wenjie Nie,Yifan Huang,Lei Wu,Wei Han,Haishuai Wang,Jiajun Bu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Fact-Flow is a two-stage framework for medical report generation that first extracts clinical findings from images and then conditions an MLLM on these facts to produce reports, improving factual accuracy without sacrificing language quality. It also auto-constructs a labeled findings dataset using an LLM, reducing manual annotation costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end MLLM report generation often hallucinates or omits findings because it conditions directly on image features without an explicit factual backbone, limiting clinical reliability.

Method: (1) Visual fact identification: predict structured clinical findings from images. (2) Fact-guided reporting: feed predicted findings to an MLLM to generate the report. A complementary LLM-based pipeline auto-labels medical findings to build training data, avoiding expensive expert annotations. Evaluated on two disease-focused datasets.

Result: Across two medical imaging datasets, the approach yields substantially higher factual accuracy than state-of-the-art baselines while maintaining strong overall text quality, per the paper’s experiments.

Conclusion: Decoupling fact extraction from language generation and bootstrapping labels via an LLM yields more reliable, clinically grounded reports, suggesting a practical path to safer deployment of MLLM-based reporting systems.

Abstract: The automatic generation of medical reports utilizing Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) frequently encounters challenges related to factual instability, which may manifest as the omission of findings or the incorporation of inaccurate information, thereby constraining their applicability in clinical settings. Current methodologies typically produce reports based directly on image features, which inherently lack a definitive factual basis. In response to this limitation, we introduce Fact-Flow, an innovative framework that separates the process of visual fact identification from the generation of reports. This is achieved by initially predicting clinical findings from the image, which subsequently directs the MLLM to produce a report that is factually precise. A pivotal advancement of our approach is a pipeline that leverages a Large Language Model (LLM) to autonomously create a dataset of labeled medical findings, effectively eliminating the need for expensive manual annotation. Extensive experimental evaluations conducted on two disease-focused medical datasets validate the efficacy of our method, demonstrating a significant enhancement in factual accuracy compared to state-of-the-art models, while concurrently preserving high standards of text quality.

</details>


### [330] [A Typologically Grounded Evaluation Framework for Word Order and Morphology Sensitivity in Multilingual Masked LMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00432)
*Anna Feldman,Libby Barak,Jing Peng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose a typology-aware stress test for multilingual masked LMs showing that models rely heavily on surface word order; morphological cues do not compensate when order is disrupted, and lemmatization particularly hurts inflectional languages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To pinpoint what linguistic signals multilingual masked language models actually use across typologically diverse languages—specifically, to disentangle reliance on word order vs. inflectional morphology during masked word reconstruction.

Method: Apply inference-time, linguistically-motivated perturbations to Universal Dependencies sentences: (1) full token scrambling, (2) content-word scrambling with function words fixed, (3) dependency head–dependent swaps, and (4) sentence-level lemma substitution (+L) that lemmatizes both context and masked targets. Evaluate mBERT and XLM-R on English, Chinese, German, Spanish, and Russian using top-1 and top-5 masked-word reconstruction accuracy; provide balanced subsets; report Turkish (strict reconstruction) in appendix.

Result: Full scrambling collapses reconstruction accuracy to near zero in all languages; partial scrambling and head–dependent swaps still cause large drops. Lemmatization has minimal effect in Chinese but substantially lowers accuracy in German/Spanish/Russian and does not alleviate the impact of scrambling. Top-5 accuracy follows the same pattern—the gold token rarely appears among the top five under full scrambling.

Conclusion: Multilingual masked LMs depend strongly on linear order and are not robustly leveraging inflectional morphology to recover masked tokens when order cues are degraded. Typological differences emerge (lemmatization harms inflectional languages more). The authors release code and evaluation resources.

Abstract: We introduce a typology-aware diagnostic for multilingual masked language models that tests reliance on word order versus inflectional form. Using Universal Dependencies, we apply inference-time perturbations: full token scrambling, content-word scrambling with function words fixed, dependency-based head--dependent swaps, and sentence-level lemma substitution (+L), which lemmatizes both the context and the masked target label. We evaluate mBERT and XLM-R on English, Chinese, German, Spanish, and Russian. Full scrambling drives word-level reconstruction accuracy near zero in all languages; partial and head--dependent perturbations cause smaller but still large drops. +L has little effect in Chinese but substantially lowers accuracy in German/Spanish/Russian, and it does not mitigate the impact of scrambling. Top-5 word accuracy shows the same pattern: under full scrambling, the gold word rarely appears among the five highest-ranked reconstructions. We release code, sampling scripts, and balanced evaluation subsets; Turkish results under strict reconstruction are reported in the appendix.

</details>


### [331] [CIRCUS: Circuit Consensus under Uncertainty via Stability Ensembles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00523)
*Swapnil Parekh*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: CIRCUS ensembles multiple pruned views of a single attribution run to score edge stability and extract a strict-consensus “core” circuit that is compact, robust to analyst choices, and causally validated, all with negligible overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mechanistic circuit discovery is brittle and highly sensitive to arbitrary analytic choices (e.g., pruning thresholds, feature dictionaries), yielding one-shot explanations without principled uncertainty; a method is needed to quantify uncertainty, surface alternatives, and report reliable structure.

Method: From one raw attribution pass, produce many pruned attribution graphs under varied configurations; assign each edge a stability score equal to the fraction of configurations that retain it; extract a strict-consensus circuit of edges present in all configurations, while surfacing contingent alternatives and rejecting low-agreement edges. Requires no retraining and adds negligible computational overhead by aggregating already-computed pruned graphs.

Result: On Gemma-2-2B and Llama-3.2-1B, strict-consensus circuits are ~40× smaller than the union of all configurations yet retain comparable influence-flow explanatory power; they outperform a same-edge-budget baseline (union pruned to the consensus size). Activation patching shows consensus-identified nodes outperform matched non-consensus controls (p=0.0004).

Conclusion: CIRCUS provides a practical, uncertainty-aware framework for trustworthy, auditable mechanistic circuits with an explicit core/contingent/noise decomposition and robustness to pruning thresholds and feature choices.

Abstract: Mechanistic circuit discovery is notoriously sensitive to arbitrary analyst choices, especially pruning thresholds and feature dictionaries, often yielding brittle "one-shot" explanations with no principled notion of uncertainty. We reframe circuit discovery as an uncertainty-quantification problem over these analytic degrees of freedom. Our method, CIRCUS, constructs an ensemble of attribution graphs by pruning a single raw attribution run under multiple configurations, assigns each edge a stability score (the fraction of configurations that retain it), and extracts a strict-consensus circuit consisting only of edges that appear in all views. This produces a threshold-robust "core" circuit while explicitly surfacing contingent alternatives and enabling rejection of low-agreement structure. CIRCUS requires no retraining and adds negligible overhead, since it aggregates structure across already-computed pruned graphs. On Gemma-2-2B and Llama-3.2-1B, strict consensus circuits are ~40x smaller than the union of all configurations while retaining comparable influence-flow explanatory power, and they outperform a same-edge-budget baseline (union pruned to match the consensus size). We further validate causal relevance with activation patching, where consensus-identified nodes consistently beat matched non-consensus controls (p=0.0004). Overall, CIRCUS provides a practical, uncertainty-aware framework for reporting trustworthy, auditable mechanistic circuits with an explicit core/contingent/noise decomposition.

</details>


### [332] [CoMoL: Efficient Mixture of LoRA Experts via Dynamic Core Space Merging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00573)
*Jie Cao,Zhenxuan Fan,Zhuonan Wang,Tianwei Lin,Ziyuan Zhao,Rolan Yan,Wenqiao Zhang,Feifei Shao,Hongwei Wang,Jun Xiao,Siliang Tang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces CoMoL, a core-space mixture-of-LoRA framework that token-wise routes to compact "core" experts, softly merges them, and pairs with a shared LoRA, yielding MoE-level adaptability with near-LoRA parameter cost and superior performance across tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PEFT—especially MoE-LoRA—incurs growing parameters from many experts and relies on coarse, instance-level routing, limiting fine-grained adaptation and efficiency. There is a need to keep expert diversity and token-level adaptivity while controlling parameter growth to match LoRA-like efficiency.

Method: CoMoL defines compact core-space experts stored as small core matrices. A core-space routing network selects and activates appropriate core experts per token (fine-grained, input-adaptive). Activated experts are softly merged into a single core expert and combined with a shared LoRA to build a specialized LoRA module. The routing network itself is projected into the same low-rank space as LoRA to reduce parameter overhead.

Result: Across extensive experiments, CoMoL maintains the adaptability of MoE-LoRA while achieving parameter efficiency comparable to standard LoRA and consistently outperforms prior PEFT and MoE-LoRA methods on multiple tasks.

Conclusion: Core-space experts plus token-level core-space routing and soft merging provide a parameter-efficient yet expressive PEFT approach. CoMoL mitigates MoE-LoRA’s parameter bloat and coarse routing, delivering fine-grained adaptation and strong, consistent empirical gains with LoRA-like efficiency.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) achieve remarkable performance on diverse downstream and domain-specific tasks via parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT). However, existing PEFT methods, particularly MoE-LoRA architectures, suffer from limited parameter efficiency and coarse-grained adaptation due to the proliferation of LoRA experts and instance-level routing. To address these issues, we propose Core Space Mixture of LoRA (\textbf{CoMoL}), a novel MoE-LoRA framework that incorporates expert diversity, parameter efficiency, and fine-grained adaptation. Specifically, CoMoL introduces two key components: core space experts and core space routing. Core space experts store each expert in a compact core matrix, preserving diversity while controlling parameter growth. Core space routing dynamically selects and activates the appropriate core experts for each token, enabling fine-grained, input-adaptive routing. Activated core experts are then merged via a soft-merging strategy into a single core expert, which is combined with a shared LoRA to form a specialized LoRA module. Besides, the routing network is projected into the same low-rank space as the LoRA matrices, further reducing parameter overhead without compromising expressiveness. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CoMoL retains the adaptability of MoE-LoRA architectures while achieving parameter efficiency comparable to standard LoRA, consistently outperforming existing methods across multiple tasks.

</details>


### [333] [Super Research: Answering Highly Complex Questions with Large Language Models through Super Deep and Super Wide Research](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00582)
*Yubo Dong,Nianhao You,Yuxuan Hou,Zixun Sun,Yue Zhang,Liang Zhang,Siyuan Zhao,Hehe Fan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces Super Research, a benchmarked task and auditing protocol for ultra-complex autonomous research, combining structured planning with super-wide and super-deep retrieval to produce verifiable, citation-rich reports; includes a 300-question dataset and a graph-anchored evaluation across five dimensions with a public leaderboard.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Although LLMs can handle deep research or wide search in isolation, their ability to tackle super-complex questions that need long-horizon planning and reconciliation of heterogeneous, conflicting evidence is underexplored. The field needs a ceiling evaluation and stress test that correlates with real research competence.

Method: Define the Super Research task with three components: (i) structured decomposition into a research plan; (ii) super-wide retrieval for diverse perspectives; (iii) super-deep, iterative querying to resolve uncertainties. Curate 300 expert-written, multi-domain questions that can require 100+ retrieval steps and 1,000+ web pages. Require outputs as verifiable reports with fine-grained citations and intermediate artifacts (outlines, tables). Propose a graph-anchored auditing protocol scoring Coverage, Logical Consistency, Report Utility, Objectivity, and Citation Health; host a leaderboard.

Result: Deliverables are a challenging benchmark, a traceable-report generation framework, a graph-anchored multi-dimensional auditing protocol, and a public leaderboard. The abstract does not report specific model performance numbers.

Conclusion: Super Research serves as a rigorous ceiling evaluation and stress test. Strong performance on this benchmark is intended to proxy general research competence, indicating robustness for a wide range of subordinate research tasks.

Abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated proficiency in Deep Research or Wide Search, their capacity to solve highly complex questions-those requiring long-horizon planning, massive evidence gathering, and synthesis across heterogeneous sources-remains largely unexplored. We introduce Super Research, a task for complex autonomous research tasks that integrates (i) structured decomposition into a research plan, (ii) super wide retrieval for diverse perspectives, and (iii) super deep investigation to resolve uncertainties through iterative queries. To evaluate this capability, we curated a benchmark of 300 expert-written questions across diverse domains, each requiring up to 100+ retrieval steps and 1,000+ web pages to reconcile conflicting evidence. Super Research produces verifiable reports with fine-grained citations and intermediate artifacts (e.g., outlines and tables) to ensure traceable reasoning. Furthermore, we present a graph-anchored auditing protocol that evaluates Super Research along five dimensions: Coverage, Logical Consistency, Report Utility, Objectivity and Citation Health. While super-complex questions may be infrequent in standard applications, Super Research serves as a critical ceiling evaluation and stress test for LLM capabilities. A model's proficiency within Super Research acts as a powerful proxy for its general research competence; success here suggests the robustness necessary to navigate nearly any subordinate research task. Leaderboard is available at: https://cnsdqd-dyb.github.io/Super-Research-Benchmark/

</details>


### [334] [From Literature to Hypotheses: An AI Co-Scientist System for Biomarker-Guided Drug Combination Hypothesis Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00612)
*Raneen Younis,Suvinava Basak,Lukas Chavez,Zahra Ahmadi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: CoDHy is a human-in-the-loop system that builds a task-specific biomedical knowledge graph from structured databases and literature, then uses graph embeddings plus agent-based reasoning to generate, evidence-grounded, ranked hypotheses for biomarker-guided cancer drug combinations via an interactive web interface.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exploding volumes of biomedical literature and databases make it hard to systematically link biomarker mechanisms to actionable, evidence-backed drug combination hypotheses in oncology; researchers need transparent, steerable tools that surface and connect mechanistic evidence to candidate therapies.

Method: Construct a task-specific knowledge graph by integrating structured biomedical resources with unstructured literature evidence; compute knowledge graph embeddings; apply agent-based reasoning to generate, validate, and rank candidate drug combinations; explicitly ground each hypothesis in retrievable evidence; provide a web UI for configuring context, inspecting intermediates, and iteratively refining hypotheses with a human-in-the-loop workflow.

Result: A working system is demonstrated, including design, interaction workflow, and practical translational oncology use cases that show exploratory hypothesis generation and decision support; the abstract does not report quantitative benchmarks but emphasizes transparency and researcher steerability.

Conclusion: CoDHy enables transparent, evidence-grounded, and interactive hypothesis generation for biomarker-guided drug combinations in cancer, supporting researcher-driven exploration and decision support rather than full automation.

Abstract: The rapid growth of biomedical literature and curated databases has made it increasingly difficult for researchers to systematically connect biomarker mechanisms to actionable drug combination hypotheses. We present AI Co-Scientist (CoDHy), an interactive, human-in-the-loop system for biomarker-guided drug combination hypothesis generation in cancer research. CoDHy integrates structured biomedical databases and unstructured literature evidence into a task-specific knowledge graph, which serves as the basis for graph-based reasoning and hypothesis construction. The system combines knowledge graph embeddings with agent-based reasoning to generate, validate, and rank candidate drug combinations, while explicitly grounding each hypothesis in retrievable evidence. Through a web-based interface, users can configure the scientific context, inspect intermediate results, and iteratively refine hypotheses, enabling transparent and researcher-steerable exploration rather than automated decision-making. We demonstrate CoDHy as a system for exploratory hypothesis generation and decision support in translational oncology, highlighting its design, interaction workflow, and practical use cases.

</details>


### [335] [QQ: A Toolkit for Language Identifiers and Metadata](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00620)
*Wessel Poelman,Yiyi Chen,Miryam de Lhoneux*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: QwanQwa (QQ) is a lightweight Python toolkit that unifies language metadata and identifier mapping (e.g., BCP‑47, ISO 639, Glottocodes) and offers a graph-based interface to traverse languages by families, regions, scripts, and other attributes, easing multilingual NLP workflows and exploration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multilingual NLP now spans thousands of languages and datasets that use incompatible identifiers. Manually mapping identifiers and consolidating metadata is error-prone and does not scale, hindering reproducible reporting and cross-dataset work.

Method: Build a lightweight Python library that aggregates multiple language resources behind a single API, normalizes and maps between common language identifiers, and represents metadata in a graph structure to enable traversal across linguistic families, regions, writing systems, and related attributes.

Result: QQ provides convenient, scalable normalization and crosswalks among identifiers, plus graph-based queries to find related languages by shared scripts, regions, or lineage. It functions as a practical glue layer for multilingual experiments and an exploration tool for language relationships.

Conclusion: A unified, graph-backed metadata toolkit like QQ streamlines consistent language reporting and large-scale multilingual experimentation, and facilitates intuitive discovery of related languages via shared metadata.

Abstract: The growing number of languages considered in multilingual NLP, including new datasets and tasks, poses challenges regarding properly and accurately reporting which languages are used and how. For example, datasets often use different language identifiers; some use BCP-47 (e.g. en_Latn), others use ISO 639-1 (en), and more linguistically oriented datasets use Glottocodes (stan1293). Mapping between identifiers is manageable for a few dozen languages, but becomes unscalable when dealing with thousands. We introduce QwanQwa, a light-weight Python toolkit for unified language metadata management. QQ integrates multiple language resources into a single interface, provides convenient normalization and mapping between language identifiers, and affords a graph-based structure that enables traversal across families, regions, writing systems, and other linguistic attributes. QQ serves both as (1) a simple "glue" library in multilingual NLP research to make working with many languages easier, and (2) as an intuitive way for exploring languages, such as finding related ones through shared scripts, regions or other metadata.

</details>


### [336] [Piecing Together Cross-Document Coreference Resolution Datasets: Systematic Dataset Analysis and Unification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00621)
*Anastasia Zhukova,Terry Ruas,Jan Philip Wahle,Bela Gipp*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: uCDCR unifies multiple English CDCR corpora into a single, standardized dataset with harmonized annotations for both entity and event coreference, standardized metrics/protocols, and analysis tools. It reveals ECB+ has relatively low lexical diversity and mid-level complexity, shows that training/evaluating across all uCDCR datasets should improve generalization, and finds entity and event coreference are similarly challenging.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CDCR research is fragmented by incompatible formats, inconsistent annotation standards, and an event-centric framing (ECR), which hinder fair, reproducible, and comparable evaluations across datasets and domains.

Method: Consolidate diverse public English CDCR datasets into a consistent schema; fix known inconsistencies; augment with missing attributes; provide standardized metrics and evaluation protocols. Analyze lexical properties (mention composition, diversity, ambiguity) and dataset distributions. Use a same-head-lemma baseline to estimate dataset complexity. Compare event vs entity coreference performance under identical baselines. Release dataset and open-source code for parsing, analysis, and scoring.

Result: - ECB+ exhibits among the lowest lexical diversity and mid-range complexity per the same-head-lemma baseline.
- Using all uCDCR datasets for training/evaluation is expected to improve CDCR model generalizability due to broader document/mention distributions.
- Nearly identical baseline performance for events and entities indicates both tasks are comparably difficult; focusing solely on events is unwarranted.

Conclusion: uCDCR offers a unified benchmark suite and tooling for fair, interpretable, cross-dataset CDCR research. Findings recommend moving beyond ECB+ as the singular benchmark, evaluating on the unified suite, and addressing both entity and event coreference. Resources are publicly available for reproducible research.

Abstract: Research in CDCR remains fragmented due to heterogeneous dataset formats, varying annotation standards, and the predominance of the CDCR definition as the event coreference resolution (ECR). To address these challenges, we introduce uCDCR, a unified dataset that consolidates diverse publicly available English CDCR corpora across various domains into a consistent format, which we analyze with standardized metrics and evaluation protocols. uCDCR incorporates both entity and event coreference, corrects known inconsistencies, and enriches datasets with missing attributes to facilitate reproducible research. We establish a cohesive framework for fair, interpretable, and cross-dataset analysis in CDCR and compare the datasets on their lexical properties, e.g., lexical composition of the annotated mentions, lexical diversity and ambiguity metrics, discuss the annotation rules and principles that lead to high lexical diversity, and examine how these metrics influence performance on the same-head-lemma baseline. Our dataset analysis shows that ECB+, the state-of-the-art benchmark for CDCR, has one of the lowest lexical diversities, and its CDCR complexity, measured by the same-head-lemma baseline, lies in the middle among all uCDCR datasets. Moreover, comparing document and mention distributions between ECB+ and uCDCR shows that using all uCDCR datasets for model training and evaluation will improve the generalizability of CDCR models. Finally, the almost identical performance on the same-head-lemma baseline, separately applied to events and entities, shows that resolving both types is a complex task and should not be steered toward ECR alone. The uCDCR dataset is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/AnZhu/uCDCR, and the code for parsing, analyzing, and scoring the dataset is available at https://github.com/anastasia-zhukova/uCDCR.

</details>


### [337] [BLUFF: Benchmarking the Detection of False and Synthetic Content across 58 Low-Resource Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00634)
*Jason Lucas,Matt Murtagh-White,Adaku Uchendu,Ali Al-Lawati,Michiharu Yamashita,Dominik Macko,Ivan Srba,Robert Moro,Dongwon Lee*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: BLUFF is a large-scale, multilingual benchmark (79 languages, 202K+ samples) for detecting false and synthetic content, coupling human fact-checked and LLM-generated texts, with diverse manipulations and translations, plus new generation (AXL-CoI) and filtering (mPURIFY) pipelines. It reveals substantial performance drops of current detectors on low-resource languages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most misinformation detection resources focus on English or a few high-resource languages, leaving low-resource communities underserved and obscuring cross-lingual robustness gaps. A unified, controlled, and diverse multilingual dataset is needed to evaluate and improve detectors across language resource levels and content types (human vs. synthetic).

Method: Assemble BLUFF by combining human-written, fact-checked content across 57 languages with LLM-generated content across 71 languages. Include four content types (human, LLM-generated, LLM-translated, hybrid), bidirectional English↔X translation, 39 textual modification/editing techniques at varying intensities, generated with 19 LLMs. Introduce AXL-CoI, a multi-agent framework for controlled fake/real news generation, and mPURIFY, a quality filtering pipeline. Evaluate state-of-the-art detectors across languages and resource levels.

Result: BLUFF spans 79 languages (20 high-resource, 59 low-resource) with 202K+ samples (122K+ human, 79K+ LLM). Experiments show up to 25.3% F1 degradation on low-resource languages compared to high-resource ones. The work releases dataset, documentation, and open-source tools for comprehensive multilingual evaluation.

Conclusion: BLUFF fills a critical gap by enabling rigorous, linguistically diverse evaluation of falsehood detection and exposing inequities in detector performance for low-resource languages. The released resources can catalyze research toward more equitable, robust, and cross-lingual misinformation detection systems.

Abstract: Multilingual falsehoods threaten information integrity worldwide, yet detection benchmarks remain confined to English or a few high-resource languages, leaving low-resource linguistic communities without robust defense tools. We introduce BLUFF, a comprehensive benchmark for detecting false and synthetic content, spanning 79 languages with over 202K samples, combining human-written fact-checked content (122K+ samples across 57 languages) and LLM-generated content (79K+ samples across 71 languages). BLUFF uniquely covers both high-resource "big-head" (20) and low-resource "long-tail" (59) languages, addressing critical gaps in multilingual research on detecting false and synthetic content. Our dataset features four content types (human-written, LLM-generated, LLM-translated, and hybrid human-LLM text), bidirectional translation (English$\leftrightarrow$X), 39 textual modification techniques (36 manipulation tactics for fake news, 3 AI-editing strategies for real news), and varying edit intensities generated using 19 diverse LLMs. We present AXL-CoI (Adversarial Cross-Lingual Agentic Chainof-Interactions), a novel multi-agentic framework for controlled fake/real news generation, paired with mPURIFY, a quality filtering pipeline ensuring dataset integrity. Experiments reveal state-of-theart detectors suffer up to 25.3% F1 degradation on low-resource versus high-resource languages. BLUFF provides the research community with a multilingual benchmark, extensive linguistic-oriented benchmark evaluation, comprehensive documentation, and opensource tools to advance equitable falsehood detection. Dataset and code are available at: https://jsl5710.github.io/BLUFF/

</details>


### [338] [SSKG Hub: An Expert-Guided Platform for LLM-Empowered Sustainability Standards Knowledge Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00669)
*Chaoyue He,Xin Zhou,Xinjia Yu,Lei Zhang,Yan Zhang,Yi Wu,Lei Xiao,Liangyue Li,Di Wang,Hong Xu,Xiaoqiao Wang,Wei Liu,Chunyan Miao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SSKG Hub turns complex sustainability disclosure standards into auditable, provenance-rich knowledge graphs via an LLM-centered, expert-governed pipeline, enabling exploration, evidence tracing, cross-KG fusion, and downstream tasks; feasibility is shown through an expert-led curation case study and a public web app.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sustainability standards are long, jargon-heavy, and highly cross-referential, which impedes structured analysis, comparison, and reuse. There is a need to convert these texts into machine-actionable representations with strong provenance and governance to support reliable analytics and decision-making.

Method: A research prototype and interactive web platform that: (1) automatically identifies standards and chunks text; (2) uses standard-specific prompting for LLM extraction; (3) performs robust triple parsing; (4) stores triples in Neo4j with fine-grained, provenance-aware audit metadata; (5) produces a provenance-linked Draft KG that undergoes expert review with CRUD operations; (6) promotes to a Certified KG via meta-expert adjudication, all under a role-based governance framework. The platform also supports graph exploration, triple-level evidence tracing, cross-KG fusion, KG-driven tasks, and curated insights/resources.

Result: A comprehensive, expert-led KG review case study demonstrates end-to-end curation and quality assurance. The platform and prototype are publicly accessible at www.sskg-hub.com. Quantitative metrics are not reported in the abstract.

Conclusion: SSKG Hub demonstrates a practical, auditable approach to structuring sustainability standards as knowledge graphs, combining LLM extraction with human governance to ensure traceability and accountability, and enabling downstream analytical tasks across multiple standards.

Abstract: Sustainability disclosure standards (e.g., GRI, SASB, TCFD, IFRS S2) are comprehensive yet lengthy, terminology-dense, and highly cross-referential, hindering structured analysis and downstream use. We present SSKG Hub (Sustainability Standards Knowledge Graph Hub), a research prototype and interactive web platform that transforms standards into auditable knowledge graphs (KGs) through an LLM-centered, expert-guided pipeline. The system integrates automatic standard identification, configurable chunking, standard-specific prompting, robust triple parsing, and provenance-aware Neo4j storage with fine-grained audit metadata. LLM extraction produces a provenance-linked Draft KG, which is reviewed, curated, and formally promoted to a Certified KG through meta-expert adjudication. A role-based governance framework covering read-only guest access, expert review and CRUD operations, meta-expert certification, and administrative oversight ensures traceability and accountability across draft and certified states. Beyond graph exploration and triple-level evidence tracing, SSKG Hub supports cross-KG fusion, KG-driven tasks, and dedicated modules for insights and curated resources. We validate the platform through a comprehensive expert-led KG review case study that demonstrates end-to-end curation and quality assurance. The web application is publicly available at www.sskg-hub.com.

</details>


### [339] [Polynomial Mixing for Efficient Self-supervised Speech Encoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00683)
*Eva Feillet,Ryan Whetten,David Picard,Alexandre Allauzen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Proposes Polynomial Mixer (PoM) as a linear-time, drop‑in replacement for multi‑head self‑attention in speech encoders, attaining competitive WER while reducing time and memory costs when integrated into BEST‑RQ pretraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformer self‑attention is quadratic in sequence length, limiting scalability for long speech sequences and large‑batch training. The work seeks a token‑mixing mechanism that preserves accuracy but scales linearly to improve efficiency.

Method: Introduce PoM, which forms a polynomial representation over input tokens to mix information with O(n) complexity. Replace attention blocks with PoM inside a self‑supervised speech representation learner based on BEST‑RQ, then fine‑tune/evaluate on downstream ASR tasks.

Result: Across downstream speech recognition benchmarks, PoM matches or closely approaches full self‑attention and outperforms/competes with other linear‑complexity alternatives, yielding better time/memory efficiency and a favorable accuracy–efficiency trade‑off.

Conclusion: Polynomial mixing can substitute for attention in speech encoders without large accuracy loss, enabling more scalable and memory‑efficient training/inference; it is a promising direction for efficient ASR architectures.

Abstract: State-of-the-art speech-to-text models typically employ Transformer-based encoders that model token dependencies via self-attention mechanisms. However, the quadratic complexity of self-attention in both memory and computation imposes significant constraints on scalability. In this work, we propose a novel token-mixing mechanism, the Polynomial Mixer (PoM), as a drop-in replacement for multi-head self-attention. PoM computes a polynomial representation of the input with linear complexity with respect to the input sequence length. We integrate PoM into a self-supervised speech representation learning framework based on BEST-RQ and evaluate its performance on downstream speech recognition tasks. Experimental results demonstrate that PoM achieves a competitive word error rate compared to full self-attention and other linear-complexity alternatives, offering an improved trade-off between performance and efficiency in time and memory.

</details>


### [340] [RAVEL: Reasoning Agents for Validating and Evaluating LLM Text Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00686)
*Andrew Zhuoer Feng,Cunxiang Wang,Yu Luo,Bosi Wen,Yidong Wang,Lin Fan,Yilin Zhou,Zikang Wang,Wenbo Yu,Lindong Wu,Hongning Wang,Minlie Huang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: RAVEL is an agentic evaluation framework for process-level text synthesis (outline, draft, review, refine), paired with C3EBench (1,258 pro-writing samples) and four reverse-engineered tasks (Cloze, Edit, Expand, End-to-End). Testing 14 LLMs shows that reasoning—especially under sparse/underspecified instructions—dominates performance; strong reasoners can steer weaker generators, but not vice versa.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations largely score final outputs and overlook the intermediate operations of text synthesis (outlining, drafting, editing). This misses critical capabilities like contextual integration and instruction disambiguation, especially under limited guidance. The goal is to evaluate these operational competencies directly and reveal where LLMs actually fail during long-horizon writing.

Method: Introduce RAVEL, an autonomous agentic testing framework that plans and executes typical synthesis steps. Construct C3EBench with 1,258 samples from professional writings. Use a reverse-engineering pipeline to isolate capabilities across four tasks: Cloze (fill missing content), Edit (revise/repair), Expand (extend content), and End-to-End (full synthesis). Evaluate 14 LLMs and run operator-augmented setups where a strong reasoning model guides other models to test the interplay of reasoning vs generation.

Result: Most models underperform when instructions are sparse or underspecified, indicating weak contextual grasp. Performance in agentic synthesis correlates more with reasoning capability than raw generative fluency. A strong reasoning operator can coordinate a weaker generator to produce higher-quality text, whereas a strong generator cannot compensate for weak reasoning.

Conclusion: Reliable evaluation of long-horizon text synthesis must target the process and reasoning, not just final text quality. RAVEL+C3EBench expose reasoning as the primary bottleneck and suggest practical systems where a strong reasoner orchestrates more economical generators. The resources are released for reproducibility and further research.

Abstract: Large Language Models have evolved from single-round generators into long-horizon agents, capable of complex text synthesis scenarios. However, current evaluation frameworks lack the ability to assess the actual synthesis operations, such as outlining, drafting, and editing. Consequently, they fail to evaluate the actual and detailed capabilities of LLMs. To bridge this gap, we introduce RAVEL, an agentic framework that enables the LLM testers to autonomously plan and execute typical synthesis operations, including outlining, drafting, reviewing, and refining. Complementing this framework, we present C3EBench, a comprehensive benchmark comprising 1,258 samples derived from professional human writings. We utilize a "reverse-engineering" pipeline to isolate specific capabilities across four tasks: Cloze, Edit, Expand, and End-to-End. Through our analysis of 14 LLMs, we uncover that most LLMs struggle with tasks that demand contextual understanding under limited or under-specified instructions. By augmenting RAVEL with SOTA LLMs as operators, we find that such agentic text synthesis is dominated by the LLM's reasoning capability rather than raw generative capacity. Furthermore, we find that a strong reasoner can guide a weaker generator to yield higher-quality results, whereas the inverse does not hold. Our code and data are available at this link: https://github.com/ZhuoerFeng/RAVEL-Reasoning-Agents-Text-Eval.

</details>


### [341] [DRIV-EX: Counterfactual Explanations for Driving LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00696)
*Amaia Cardiel,Eloi Zablocki,Elias Ramzi,Eric Gaussier*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: DRIV-EX generates minimal, fluent counterfactual scene edits that flip an LLM driving planner’s decision by optimizing in embedding space and using the result only to bias a controlled re-decoding of the original text.
