It outperforms baselines on highD, revealing latent biases and offering actionable diagnostics for robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based planners for autonomous driving make opaque decisions. Practitioners need faithful, human-readable counterfactuals that show what small semantic changes would alter a plan, but naive continuous optimization tends to yield incoherent or invalid text. The goal is to obtain interpretable, minimal changes while preserving fluency and domain plausibility.

Method: Define counterfactuals as the smallest semantic perturbations to a scene description that change the planner’s action. Perform gradient-based optimization in the continuous embedding space to find shifts that would flip the decision. Use these optimized embeddings only as a semantic guide to bias a constrained/controlled decoding process that re-generates the scene text, thereby steering toward the counterfactual while enforcing fluency, domain validity, and proximity to the original. Evaluate with the LC-LLM planner on textual transcriptions of the highD dataset.

Result: Compared to baselines, DRIV-EX more reliably produces valid and fluent counterfactuals on highD. It surfaces latent biases in the planner and provides concrete insights that can be used to improve robustness.

Conclusion: Embedding-guided, controlled decoding enables faithful, interpretable counterfactual explanations for LLM driving planners. DRIV-EX both explains and stress-tests planner behavior, offering a practical tool for diagnosing and improving LLM-based autonomous driving systems.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as reasoning engines in autonomous driving, yet their decision-making remains opaque. We propose to study their decision process through counterfactual explanations, which identify the minimal semantic changes to a scene description required to alter a driving plan.
  We introduce DRIV-EX, a method that leverages gradient-based optimization on continuous embeddings to identify the input shifts required to flip the model's decision. Crucially, to avoid the incoherent text typical of unconstrained continuous optimization, DRIV-EX uses these optimized embeddings solely as a semantic guide: they are used to bias a controlled decoding process that re-generates the original scene description. This approach effectively steers the generation toward the counterfactual target while guaranteeing the linguistic fluency, domain validity, and proximity to the original input, essential for interpretability.
  Evaluated using the LC-LLM planner on a textual transcription of the highD dataset, DRIV-EX generates valid, fluent counterfactuals more reliably than existing baselines. It successfully exposes latent biases and provides concrete insights to improve the robustness of LLM-based driving agents.

</details>


### [342] [SkillCraft: Can LLM Agents Learn to Use Tools Skillfully?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00718)
*Shiqi Chen,Jingze Gai,Ruochen Zhou,Jinghan Zhang,Tongyao Zhu,Junlong Li,Kangrui Wang,Zihan Wang,Zhengyu Chen,Klara Kaleb,Ning Miao,Siyang Gao,Cong Lu,Manling Li,Junxian He,Yee Whye Teh*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SkillCraft is a benchmark and protocol to test whether tool-using agents can compose, cache, and reuse higher-level tool sequences ("Skills"), yielding large efficiency gains and linking success to compositional ability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations focus on single-instance success with fixed tools, missing the long-horizon, recurring, and compositional nature of real-world tool use where agents must abstract and reuse higher-level procedures.

Method: Design a benchmark of realistic, highly compositional tool-use scenarios with difficulty scaled both quantitatively and structurally; provide an evaluation protocol that lets agents auto-compose atomic tools into executable Skills, cache and reuse them within and across tasks, and persist a growing skill library.

Result: State-of-the-art agents evaluated on SkillCraft show up to 80% token savings from skill saving/reuse; test-time success rates strongly correlate with tool-composition ability.

Conclusion: Reusable, compositional skill acquisition is a core capability for tool-using agents; SkillCraft offers a targeted way to measure and encourage it, showing that caching and reusing composed Skills markedly improves efficiency and performance.

Abstract: Real-world tool-using agents operate over long-horizon workflows with recurring structure and diverse demands, where effective behavior requires not only invoking atomic tools but also abstracting, and reusing higher-level tool compositions. However, existing benchmarks mainly measure instance-level success under static tool sets, offering limited insight into agents' ability to acquire such reusable skills. We address this gap by introducing SkillCraft, a benchmark explicitly stress-test agent ability to form and reuse higher-level tool compositions, where we call Skills. SkillCraft features realistic, highly compositional tool-use scenarios with difficulty scaled along both quantitative and structural dimensions, designed to elicit skill abstraction and cross-task reuse. We further propose a lightweight evaluation protocol that enables agents to auto-compose atomic tools into executable Skills, cache and reuse them inside and across tasks, thereby improving efficiency while accumulating a persistent library of reusable skills. Evaluating state-of-the-art agents on SkillCraft, we observe substantial efficiency gains, with token usage reduced by up to 80% by skill saving and reuse. Moreover, success rate strongly correlates with tool composition ability at test time, underscoring compositional skill acquisition as a core capability.

</details>


### [343] [RLAR: An Agentic Reward System for Multi-task Reinforcement Learning on Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00724)
*Andrew Zhuoer Feng,Cunxiang Wang,Bosi Wen,Yidong Wang,Yu Luo,Hongning Wang,Minlie Huang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: RLAR is an agent-driven approach that dynamically constructs and selects per-query reward functions—by retrieving reward models online and generating programmatic verifiers—yielding better generalization and performance than static reward models in RL for LLM alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static, domain-specific reward models are expensive to train and often fail to generalize to the shifting, out-of-distribution data encountered during RL training, limiting alignment quality.

Method: Formulate reward acquisition as a tool-using agent task: an LLM agent (i) retrieves suitable reward models from the Internet, (ii) synthesizes code-based verifiers, and (iii) orchestrates which reward tool to apply per query. This enables the reward mechanism to adapt and “self-evolve” as the training distribution changes.

Result: Across math, coding, translation, and dialogue, RLAR delivers consistent gains reported as 10–60 (units not specified). On RewardBench-V2, it surpasses static baselines and approaches an upper-bound performance, indicating improved OOD generalization via dynamic reward orchestration.

Conclusion: Dynamic, agent-orchestrated reward construction can overcome limitations of static reward models, improving alignment and robustness across tasks. Open-source code/data are provided, suggesting practical applicability and extensibility.

Abstract: Large language model alignment via reinforcement learning depends critically on reward function quality. However, static, domain-specific reward models are often costly to train and exhibit poor generalization in out-of-distribution scenarios encountered during RL iterations. We present RLAR (Reinforcement Learning from Agent Rewards), an agent-driven framework that dynamically assigns tailored reward functions to individual queries. Specifically, RLAR transforms reward acquisition into a dynamic tool synthesis and invocation task. It leverages LLM agents to autonomously retrieve optimal reward models from the Internet and synthesize programmatic verifiers through code generation. This allows the reward system to self-evolve with the shifting data distributions during training. Experimental results demonstrate that RLAR yields consistent performance gains ranging from 10 to 60 across mathematics, coding, translation, and dialogue tasks. On RewardBench-V2, RLAR significantly outperforms static baselines and approaches the performance upper bound, demonstrating superior generalization through dynamic reward orchestration. The data and code are available on this link: https://github.com/ZhuoerFeng/RLAR.

</details>


### [344] [LaSTR: Language-Driven Time-Series Segment Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00725)
*Kota Dohi,Harsh Purohit,Tomoya Nishida,Takashi Endo,Yusuke Ohtsubo,Koichiro Yawata,Koki Takeshita,Tatsuya Sasaki,Yohei Kawaguchi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LaSTR introduces language-driven retrieval of local segments from large time-series by aligning text and time-series in a shared embedding space; trained on TV2-segmented LOTSA windows with GPT-5.2 captions, a Conformer-based contrastive retriever outperforms random and CLIP baselines across pool sizes, improving ranking and semantic agreement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Time-series search typically depends on hand-crafted similarity metrics or whole-series descriptors, which miss fine-grained, semantically meaningful events. The goal is to enable natural-language queries to find relevant local segments within vast time-series repositories.

Method: Construct large-scale segment–caption pairs by applying TV2-based segmentation to LOTSA windows and generating descriptions via GPT-5.2. Train a Conformer-based contrastive retriever to map text and time-series segments into a shared embedding space. Evaluate single-positive retrieval and caption-side consistency using SBERT and a vision–language model as a judge under varying candidate pool sizes.

Result: Across all candidate pool sizes and metrics, LaSTR beats random and CLIP baselines, yielding superior ranking quality and higher semantic alignment (per SBERT and VLM-as-a-judge) between retrieved segments and query intent.

Conclusion: Natural-language, segment-level retrieval in time-series is feasible and effective with contrastive alignment and synthetic captions. LaSTR’s pipeline improves retrieval quality over strong baselines, indicating shared text–time-series embeddings are a promising direction for time-series search.

Abstract: Effectively searching time-series data is essential for system analysis, but existing methods often require expert-designed similarity criteria or rely on global, series-level descriptions. We study language-driven segment retrieval: given a natural language query, the goal is to retrieve relevant local segments from large time-series repositories. We build large-scale segment--caption training data by applying TV2-based segmentation to LOTSA windows and generating segment descriptions with GPT-5.2, and then train a Conformer-based contrastive retriever in a shared text--time-series embedding space. On a held-out test split, we evaluate single-positive retrieval together with caption-side consistency (SBERT and VLM-as-a-judge) under multiple candidate pool sizes. Across all settings, LaSTR outperforms random and CLIP baselines, yielding improved ranking quality and stronger semantic agreement between retrieved segments and query intent.

</details>


### [345] [Qwen3-Coder-Next Technical Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00729)
*Ruisheng Cao,Mouxiang Chen,Jiawei Chen,Zeyu Cui,Yunlong Feng,Binyuan Hui,Yuheng Jing,Kaixin Li,Mingze Li,Junyang Lin,Zeyao Ma,Kashun Shum,Xuwu Wang,Jinxi Wei,Jiaxi Yang,Jiajun Zhang,Lei Zhang,Zongmeng Zhang,Wenting Zhao,Fan Zhou*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Open-weight coding-focused MoE model that activates only ~3B of 80B parameters and is trained agentically on verifiable, executable coding tasks with environment feedback (mid-training + RL), delivering competitive agent performance with efficient inference; base and instruction-tuned checkpoints released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Push the capability frontier of coding agents while keeping inference costs low by testing how far strong training recipes and agentic learning can take models with small active parameter footprints. Enable open research and practical deployment via open weights.

Method: Architecturally, an 80B-parameter sparse MoE that activates ~3B experts per token. Training-wise, large-scale synthesis of verifiable coding tasks bundled with executable environments; agentic learning from environment feedback through mid-training and reinforcement learning; plus instruction tuning for usability.

Result: On agent-centric coding benchmarks (e.g., SWE-Bench, Terminal-Bench), the model achieves competitive performance relative to its 3B active parameter count, indicating strong efficiency–performance trade-offs. Efficient inference is emphasized; specific scores are not provided in the abstract.

Conclusion: Carefully designed agentic training with verifiable, executable tasks and environment-feedback RL can yield coding agents that perform competitively despite small active compute. Open-weight base and instruction-tuned models aim to catalyze research and real-world coding-agent applications.

Abstract: We present Qwen3-Coder-Next, an open-weight language model specialized for coding agents. Qwen3-Coder-Next is an 80-billion-parameter model that activates only 3 billion parameters during inference, enabling strong coding capability with efficient inference. In this work, we explore how far strong training recipes can push the capability limits of models with small parameter footprints. To achieve this, we perform agentic training through large-scale synthesis of verifiable coding tasks paired with executable environments, allowing learning directly from environment feedback via mid-training and reinforcement learning. Across agent-centric benchmarks including SWE-Bench and Terminal-Bench, Qwen3-Coder-Next achieves competitive performance relative to its active parameter count. We release both base and instruction-tuned open-weight versions to support research and real-world coding agent development.

</details>


### [346] [A Comprehensive Evaluation of LLM Unlearning Robustness under Multi-Turn Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00823)
*Ruihao Pan,Suhang Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Static, single-turn evaluations overstate the effectiveness of LLM unlearning: interactive prompting (self-correction, dialogue-conditioned queries) can resurrect ostensibly forgotten knowledge. Stronger unlearning often yields rigid behaviors rather than genuine erasure, underscoring the need for interaction-robust forgetting metrics and methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ensure that unlearning in LLMs truly removes the influence of sensitive or problematic data to meet safety, privacy, and legal requirements—not just in static tests but in real-world, interactive use.

Method: Evaluate unlearning robustness under two realistic interaction patterns: (1) self-correction (models revising their own outputs) and (2) dialogue-conditioned querying (information primed by prior turns). Compare different strengths of unlearning and measure whether knowledge deemed forgotten in static, single-turn tests re-emerges during multi-turn interactions.

Result: Knowledge that appears forgotten in static evaluations frequently reappears through interactive prompting. Increasing unlearning strength improves surface-level robustness but often manifests as inflexible refusals or rigid behavior, rather than actual removal of internal knowledge.

Conclusion: Current static benchmarks overestimate unlearning success. Effective unlearning must ensure stable forgetting under interactive settings; evaluations and methods should target interaction-robust erasure rather than superficial behavioral suppression.

Abstract: Machine unlearning aims to remove the influence of specific training data from pre-trained models without retraining from scratch, and is increasingly important for large language models (LLMs) due to safety, privacy, and legal concerns. Although prior work primarily evaluates unlearning in static, single-turn settings, forgetting robustness under realistic interactive use remains underexplored. In this paper, we study whether unlearning remains stable in interactive environments by examining two common interaction patterns: self-correction and dialogue-conditioned querying. We find that knowledge appearing forgotten in static evaluation can often be recovered through interaction. Although stronger unlearning improves apparent robustness, it often results in behavioral rigidity rather than genuine knowledge erasure. Our findings suggest that static evaluation may overestimate real-world effectiveness and highlight the need for ensuring stable forgetting under interactive settings.

</details>


### [347] [Constitutional Black-Box Monitoring for Scheming in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00829)
*Simon Storf,Rich Barton-Cooper,James Peters-Gill,Marius Hobbhahn*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They study LLM-based, constitutional black-box monitors trained only on synthetic agent trajectories (via STRIDE and Gloom) to detect scheming; these monitors generalize to more realistic environments but quickly hit a performance ceiling—simple prompt sweeps match heavier optimization, which tends to overfit.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous LLM agents may covertly pursue misaligned objectives (“scheming”), so reliable oversight is needed. Real-world labeled data for scheming is scarce and internal states are hard to access, motivating black-box monitors and synthetic data generation from behavior specs.

Method: Generate two 1k-sample synthetic datasets of agent trajectories using STRIDE (iterative refinement from natural-language specs) and Gloom (agent–environment simulation). Train/select prompted classifier monitors (frontier LLMs) via prompt sweeps, human refinement, and automated prompt optimization. Evaluate on 7.5k held-out trajectories from ControlArena, a grounded multi-environment benchmark.

Result: Monitors chosen solely using synthetic data detect a meaningful scheming signal and transfer to ControlArena. However, gains saturate: simple prompt sweeps perform as well as extensive human/automated prompt optimization; pushing optimization further yields no improvement and induces overfitting.

Conclusion: Constitutional black-box LLM monitoring trained on synthetic data is viable but limited by a performance ceiling. Heavy prompt optimization gives diminishing returns and risks overfitting; further progress likely requires richer data, stronger supervision, or alternative monitoring modalities beyond black-box prompts.

Abstract: Safe deployment of Large Language Model (LLM) agents in autonomous settings requires reliable oversight mechanisms. A central challenge is detecting scheming, where agents covertly pursue misaligned goals. One approach to mitigating such risks is LLM-based monitoring: using language models to examine agent behaviors for suspicious actions. We study constitutional black-box monitors: prompted classifiers that detect scheming using only externally observable inputs and outputs, optimized on synthetic data generated from natural-language behavior specifications. We introduce two pipelines for generating synthetic agent trajectories, STRIDE (iterative refinement) and Gloom (agent-environment simulation), from which we generate 1,000 samples each. We optimize frontier LLM monitors on these datasets via prompt sweeps, human refinement, and automated prompt optimization, and evaluate performance on 7,500 held-out trajectories from ControlArena, a suite of grounded environments where agents operate in more realistic contexts. Our results demonstrate that monitors selected purely on synthetic data can generalize to more realistic environments, capturing a meaningful scheming signal. However, we find that performance saturates quickly in our setting, with simple prompt sweeps matching the results of more extensive optimization. Pushing beyond this limit yields no further improvements and instead leads to overfitting.

</details>


### [348] [Learning Nested Named Entity Recognition from Flat Annotations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00840)
*Igor Rozhkov,Natalia Loukachevitch*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Can nested NER be learned from only flat annotations? Using four simple strategies and a hybrid FT+LLM pipeline, the authors recover part of the nested structure and reach 26.37% inner F1 on NEREL, closing ~40% of the gap to full nested supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Nested NER needs costly multilayer labels, while flat NER data is plentiful. The paper seeks to exploit flat annotations to infer nested entities and reduce dependence on scarce, expensive nested corpora.

Method: Evaluate four ways to induce nested structure from flat labels: (1) string inclusions (substring matching of entity spans), (2) entity corruption to synthesize pseudo-nested data, (3) flat neutralization to lessen false-negative signals during training, and (4) a hybrid pipeline that combines a fine-tuned model with an LLM. Methods are assessed on the Russian NEREL benchmark (29 types, 21% nested).

Result: On NEREL, the best combined approach attains 26.37% inner F1, recovering about 40% of the performance gap compared to models trained with full nested supervision.

Conclusion: It is feasible to learn part of nested structure from flat annotations alone, and combining simple heuristics with a hybrid FT+LLM pipeline yields meaningful gains, though performance still lags behind fully supervised nested NER. Code is released for reproducibility.

Abstract: Nested named entity recognition identifies entities contained within other entities, but requires expensive multi-level annotation. While flat NER corpora exist abundantly, nested resources remain scarce. We investigate whether models can learn nested structure from flat annotations alone, evaluating four approaches: string inclusions (substring matching), entity corruption (pseudo-nested data), flat neutralization (reducing false negative signal), and a hybrid fine-tuned + LLM pipeline. On NEREL, a Russian benchmark with 29 entity types where 21% of entities are nested, our best combined method achieves 26.37% inner F1, closing 40% of the gap to full nested supervision. Code is available at https://github.com/fulstock/Learning-from-Flat-Annotations.

</details>


### [349] [MedGPT-oss: Training a General-Purpose Vision-Language Model for Biomedicine](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00842)
*Kai Zhang,Zhengqing Yuan,Cheng Peng,Songlin Zhao,Mengxian Lyu,Ziyi Chen,Yanfang Ye,Wei Liu,Ying Zhang,Kaleb E Smith,Lifang He,Lichao Sun,Yonghui Wu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Open-weight 20B vision–language medical assistant (MEDGPT-OSS) trained with a three-stage, domain-adaptive curriculum that unifies radiology/pathology/images and clinical text, outperforming larger open medical models on OOD multimodal reasoning and complex text-only tasks, while being deployable on commodity GPUs; full recipe, checkpoints, and evaluation harness released for privacy-preserving on-prem research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Top-performing biomedical multimodal systems are closed-source or too compute-heavy for on-prem deployment required for PHI/privacy. There is a need for an open, efficient, generalist model that can handle multiple clinical modalities under one interface without prohibitive infrastructure.

Method: Pair a GPT-oss language backbone with a visual encoder front-end, trained via an optimized three-stage curriculum. Progressively domain-adapt both modules using rigorous data curation and long-context multimodal alignment. Present a single instruction-following interface that is parameter-efficient and compatible with commodity GPUs.

Result: A 20B-parameter model that bridges the capacity gap, outperforming larger open medical models on out-of-distribution multimodal reasoning and difficult text-only clinical tasks, while maintaining deployability on standard GPUs.

Conclusion: Releases open weights, complete training recipe, and a rigorous evaluation harness to enable verifiable, privacy-preserving, institution-specific clinical AI research, offering a practical path to unified multimodal clinical assistants on-prem.

Abstract: Biomedical multimodal assistants have the potential to unify radiology, pathology, and clinical-text reasoning, yet a critical deployment gap remains: top-performing systems are either closed-source or computationally prohibitive, precluding the on-premises deployment required for patient privacy and PHI compliance. We introduce MEDGPT-OSS, an open-weight, 20B-parameter generalist vision-language model designed to facilitate open research in clinical AI. Rather than relying on architectural complexity, MEDGPT-OSS pairs the GPT-oss language backbone with a visual front-end via a optimized, three-stage training curriculum. By progressively domain-adapting these modules through rigorous data curation and long-context multimodal alignment, we demonstrate that a 20B model can bridge the capacity gap. It successfully outperforms larger open medical models on out-of-distribution (OOD) multimodal reasoning and complex text-only clinical tasks. By unifying diverse modalities under a single instruction-following interface, MEDGPT-OSS maintains a parameter-efficient footprint fully compatible with commodity GPUs. We release the complete training recipe, open-weight checkpoints, and a rigorous evaluation harness to serve as a verifiable foundation for privacy-preserving, institution-specific clinical AI research.

</details>


### [350] [CHIMERA: Compact Synthetic Data for Generalizable LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00889)
*Xinyu Zhu,Yihao Feng,Yanchao Sun,Xianzhi Du,Pingzhi Li,Olli Saarikivi,Yun Zhu,Yu Meng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: CHIMERA is a 9K-sample, synthetic, long-CoT, cross-domain reasoning dataset with automated validation that, when used to post-train a 4B Qwen3 model, yields performance on tough reasoning benchmarks approaching much larger models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open, scalable reproduction of strong LLM reasoning is blocked by lack of long-CoT seed data (cold start), narrow domain coverage (math-heavy datasets), and costly/unreliable human labels for hard reasoning tasks.

Method: Synthesize rich, long chain-of-thought trajectories using top reasoning models; organize problems via a model-generated hierarchical taxonomy covering 8 scientific disciplines and >1K topics; automatically validate problem soundness and answers via cross-checking with strong models; post-train a 4B Qwen3 model on this dataset.

Result: Despite only 9K samples, the post-trained Qwen3-4B attains strong scores on GPQA-Diamond, AIME 24/25/26, HMMT 25, and Humanity’s Last Exam, approaching or matching the reasoning of much larger models like DeepSeek-R1 and Qwen3-235B.

Conclusion: A compact, high-quality, synthetically generated, broadly covered, and automatically validated long-CoT dataset can effectively bootstrap small models’ reasoning across domains, mitigating cold-start, coverage, and annotation bottlenecks and offering a scalable path to strong reasoning performance.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have recently exhibited remarkable reasoning capabilities, largely enabled by supervised fine-tuning (SFT)- and reinforcement learning (RL)-based post-training on high-quality reasoning data. However, reproducing and extending these capabilities in open and scalable settings is hindered by three fundamental data-centric challenges: (1) the cold-start problem, arising from the lack of seed datasets with detailed, long Chain-of-Thought (CoT) trajectories needed to initialize reasoning policies; (2) limited domain coverage, as most existing open-source reasoning datasets are concentrated in mathematics, with limited coverage of broader scientific disciplines; and (3) the annotation bottleneck, where the difficulty of frontier-level reasoning tasks makes reliable human annotation prohibitively expensive or infeasible. To address these challenges, we introduce CHIMERA, a compact synthetic reasoning dataset comprising 9K samples for generalizable cross-domain reasoning. CHIMERA is constructed with three key properties: (1) it provides rich, long CoT reasoning trajectories synthesized by state-of-the-art reasoning models; (2) it has broad and structured coverage, spanning 8 major scientific disciplines and over 1K fine-grained topics organized via a model-generated hierarchical taxonomy; and (3) it employs a fully automated, scalable evaluation pipeline that uses strong reasoning models to cross-validate both problem validity and answer correctness. We use CHIMERA to post-train a 4B Qwen3 model. Despite the dataset's modest size, the resulting model achieves strong performance on a suite of challenging reasoning benchmarks, including GPQA-Diamond, AIME 24/25/26, HMMT 25, and Humanity's Last Exam, approaching or matching the reasoning performance of substantially larger models such as DeepSeek-R1 and Qwen3-235B.

</details>


### [351] [KVSlimmer: Theoretical Insights and Practical Optimizations for Asymmetric KV Merging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00907)
*Lianjun Liu,Hongli An,Weiqi Yan,Xin Du,Shengchuan Zhang,Huazhong Liu,Yunshan Zhong*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: KVSlimmer provides a theory-backed, gradient-free KV-cache merging method for LLMs, using spectral analysis of projection weights and a closed-form, exact-Hessian formulation derived from forward-pass variables, yielding lower memory/latency and better task scores than prior SOTA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KV caches dominate memory and compute in LLM decoding. Prior KV-merging approaches lean on heuristics (asymmetry observations) or costly/approximate gradient-based Hessian estimates, lacking rigorous theory and incurring overhead. A principled, efficient method is needed to compress KV caches without hurting quality.

Method: 1) Build a theoretical framework linking KV asymmetry to spectral energy distributions of projection matrices: concentrated spectra in Query/Key induce feature homogeneity; dispersed spectra in Value preserve heterogeneity. 2) Design KVSlimmer: an algorithm that captures exact Hessian information via a mathematically exact formulation and derives a closed-form solution relying only on forward-pass quantities—hence gradient-free, memory- and time-efficient for KV merging.

Result: Across multiple models/benchmarks, KVSlimmer outperforms SOTA KV-merging. On Llama3.1-8B-Instruct, it boosts LongBench average by +0.92 while reducing memory by 29% and latency by 28%.

Conclusion: A principled spectral view explains KV asymmetry in LLM attention, and KVSlimmer exploits it to deliver superior KV-cache compression with lower inference overhead and improved accuracy. The approach offers a practical, theory-grounded path to scaling LLM inference efficiency.

Abstract: The growing computational and memory demands of the Key-Value (KV) cache significantly limit the ability of Large Language Models (LLMs). While KV merging has emerged as a promising solution, existing methods that rely on empirical observations of KV asymmetry and gradient-based Hessian approximations lack a theoretical foundation and incur suboptimal compression and inference overhead. To bridge these gaps, we establish a theoretical framework that characterizes this asymmetry through the spectral energy distribution of projection weights, demonstrating that concentrated spectra in Query/Key weights induce feature homogeneity, whereas dispersed spectra in Value weights preserve heterogeneity. Then, we introduce KVSlimmer, an efficient algorithm that captures exact Hessian information through a mathematically exact formulation, and derives a closed-form solution utilizing only forward-pass variables, resulting in a gradient-free approach that is both memory- and time-efficient. Extensive experiments across various models and benchmarks demonstrate that KVSlimmer consistently outperforms SOTA methods. For instance, on Llama3.1-8B-Instruct, it improves the LongBench average score by 0.92 while reducing memory costs and latency by 29% and 28%, respectively.

</details>


### [352] [Prompt Sensitivity and Answer Consistency of Small Open-Source Large Language Models on Clinical Question Answering: Implications for Low-Resource Healthcare Deployment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00917)
*Shravani Hariprasad*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Small open-source LMs for clinical QA show that prompt phrasing strongly affects behavior; consistency and accuracy diverge. Gemma 2 is most consistent but least accurate; Llama 3.2 is most accurate with decent consistency. Roleplay prompts hurt accuracy; domain pretraining without instruction tuning (Meditron-7B) fails instruction adherence. Safe deployment must jointly assess accuracy, consistency, and instruction following.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether small, locally-runnable open-source LMs are reliable for healthcare QA in low-resource settings, and how different prompt styles affect accuracy, consistency, and instruction adherence. Test the assumption that more consistent models are more correct and whether domain pretraining alone suffices for structured clinical tasks.

Method: Evaluate five open-source LMs (Gemma 2 2B, Phi-3 Mini 3.8B, Llama 3.2 3B, Mistral 7B, Meditron-7B without instruction tuning) on three clinical QA datasets (MedQA, MedMCQA, PubMedQA) under five prompt styles (original, formal, simplified, roleplay, direct). Compute consistency scores across prompt variants, accuracy, and instruction-following failure rates. All inference ran locally on consumer CPUs; no fine-tuning performed.

Result: Consistency and accuracy were largely independent. Gemma 2 had the highest consistency (0.845–0.888) but lowest accuracy (33.0–43.5%). Llama 3.2 had moderate consistency (0.774–0.807) and the highest accuracy (49.0–65.0%). Roleplay prompts reduced accuracy for all models (e.g., Phi-3 Mini dropped 21.5 pp on MedQA). Meditron-7B showed near-complete instruction-following failure on PubMedQA (99% UNKNOWN), indicating domain pretraining alone is insufficient for structured QA.

Conclusion: High consistency does not guarantee correctness; models can be reliably wrong, posing clinical risk. Avoid roleplay prompts in healthcare contexts. For low-resource deployment, Llama 3.2 offers the best accuracy–reliability balance among tested models. Robust clinical AI evaluation must jointly consider accuracy, consistency, and instruction adherence; domain pretraining without instruction tuning is inadequate.

Abstract: Small open-source language models are gaining attention for low-resource healthcare settings, but their reliability under different prompt phrasings remains poorly understood. We evaluated five open-source models (Gemma 2 2B, Phi-3 Mini 3.8B, Llama 3.2 3B, Mistral 7B, and Meditron-7B domain-pretrained without instruction tuning) across three clinical QA datasets (MedQA, MedMCQA, PubMedQA) using five prompt styles (original, formal, simplified, roleplay, direct). We measured consistency scores, accuracy, and instruction-following failure rates. All inference ran locally on consumer CPU hardware without fine-tuning.
  Consistency and accuracy were largely independent. Gemma 2 achieved the highest consistency (0.845-0.888) but lowest accuracy (33.0-43.5%), while Llama 3.2 showed moderate consistency (0.774-0.807) with the highest accuracy (49.0-65.0%). Roleplay prompts consistently reduced accuracy across all models, with Phi-3 Mini dropping 21.5 percentage points on MedQA. Meditron-7B exhibited near-complete instruction-following failure on PubMedQA (99.0% UNKNOWN rate), showing domain pretraining alone is insufficient for structured clinical QA.
  High consistency does not imply correctness. Models can be reliably wrong, a dangerous failure mode in clinical AI. Roleplay prompts should be avoided in healthcare applications. Llama 3.2 showed the strongest balance of accuracy and reliability for low-resource deployment. Safe clinical AI requires joint evaluation of consistency, accuracy, and instruction adherence.

</details>


### [353] [Hybrid Neural-LLM Pipeline for Morphological Glossing in Endangered Language Documentation: A Case Study of Jungar Tuvan](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00923)
*Siyu Liang,Talant Mawkanuli,Gina-Anne Levow*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Hybrid BiLSTM‑CRF + LLM post‑correction significantly improves automatic interlinear glossing for a low‑resource, morphologically rich language (Jungar Tuvan), with retrieval‑augmented prompting outperforming random examples and few‑shot gains following a logarithmic curve; morpheme dictionaries often hurt, not help; overall workload meaningfully reduced.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interlinear glossed text creation is a time‑consuming bottleneck in documenting low‑resource, morphologically complex languages; practitioners need accurate, lightweight automation that works under severe data/resource constraints.

Method: A two‑stage pipeline: (1) a BiLSTM‑CRF sequence labeler produces initial glossing outputs; (2) a large language model performs post‑correction using few‑shot prompting. Systematic ablations test retrieval‑augmented prompting vs. random example selection, inclusion of morpheme dictionaries, and scaling with the number of few‑shot examples. Evaluated on Jungar Tuvan.

Result: Retrieval‑augmented prompting yields substantial improvements over random examples; morpheme dictionaries generally degrade performance versus providing no dictionary; performance scales approximately logarithmically with the number of few‑shot examples; the BiLSTM‑CRF + LLM post‑correction pipeline delivers substantial gains across most models and reduces human annotation workload.

Conclusion: Hybrid architectures that pair structured predictors with LLM reasoning are effective and computationally light for automatic linguistic annotation in endangered language documentation. The paper distills design principles for integrating these components in morphologically complex field settings.

Abstract: Interlinear glossed text (IGT) creation remains a major bottleneck in linguistic documentation and fieldwork, particularly for low-resource morphologically rich languages. We present a hybrid automatic glossing pipeline that combines neural sequence labeling with large language model (LLM) post-correction, evaluated on Jungar Tuvan, a low-resource Turkic language. Through systematic ablation studies, we show that retrieval-augmented prompting provides substantial gains over random example selection. We further find that morpheme dictionaries paradoxically hurt performance compared to providing no dictionary at all in most cases, and that performance scales approximately logarithmically with the number of few-shot examples. Most significantly, our two-stage pipeline combining a BiLSTM-CRF model with LLM post-correction yields substantial gains for most models, achieving meaningful reductions in annotation workload. Drawing on these findings, we establish concrete design principles for integrating structured prediction models with LLM reasoning in morphologically complex fieldwork contexts. These principles demonstrate that hybrid architectures offer a promising direction for computationally light solutions to automatic linguistic annotation in endangered language documentation.

</details>


### [354] [Conformal Prediction for Risk-Controlled Medical Entity Extraction Across Clinical Domains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00924)
*Manil Shrestha,Edward Kim*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They apply conformal prediction to LLM-based medical entity extraction and show that calibration quality flips across domains (underconfident on structured FDA labels, overconfident on free-text radiology). With domain-specific thresholds, they achieve ≥90% coverage at ~9–13% rejection, arguing calibration must be tailored to document structure, category, and model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM confidence is often miscalibrated, undermining safety in clinical extraction tasks where reliable uncertainty is critical. The authors aim to provide finite-sample coverage guarantees and understand how calibration behaves across distinct clinical document types.

Method: Use GPT-4.1 (and Llama-4-Maverick) to extract entities from two settings: (1) 1,000 FDA drug labels across eight sections; correctness verified via FactScore atomic statement evaluation. (2) MIMIC-CXR radiology reports using RadGraph schema; evaluated against physician annotations. Apply conformal prediction with a scalar threshold τ to control acceptance/rejection and target coverage.

Result: On FDA labels, models are underconfident and require small conformal thresholds (τ≈0.06). On radiology free text, models are overconfident, requiring strict thresholds (τ up to 0.99). Despite heterogeneity, conformal prediction attains ≥90% target coverage with manageable rejection rates (9–13%). Reported base performance: FactScore accuracy 97.7% over 128,906 entities; RadGraph entity F1 0.81–0.84.

Conclusion: Calibration is not a global property; it depends on document structure, extraction category, and model architecture. Domain-specific conformal calibration is necessary for safe deployment of LLM-based clinical extraction systems.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used for medical entity extraction, yet their confidence scores are often miscalibrated, limiting safe deployment in clinical settings. We present a conformal prediction framework that provides finite-sample coverage guarantees for LLM-based extraction across two clinical domains. First, we extract structured entities from 1,000 FDA drug labels across eight sections using GPT-4.1, verified via FactScore-based atomic statement evaluation (97.7\% accuracy over 128,906 entities). Second, we extract radiological entities from MIMIC-CXR reports using the RadGraph schema with GPT-4.1 and Llama-4-Maverick, evaluated against physician annotations (entity F1: 0.81 to 0.84). Our central finding is that miscalibration direction reverses across domains: on well-structured FDA labels, models are underconfident, requiring modest conformal thresholds ($τ\approx 0.06$), while on free-text radiology reports, models are overconfident, demanding strict thresholds ($τ$ up to 0.99). Despite this heterogeneity, conformal prediction achieves target coverage ($\geq 90\%$) in both settings with manageable rejection rates (9--13\%). These results demonstrate that calibration is not a global model property but depends on document structure, extraction category, and model architecture, motivating domain-specific conformal calibration for safe clinical deployment.

</details>


### [355] [The Aftermath of DrawEduMath: Vision Language Models Underperform with Struggling Students and Misdiagnose Errors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00925)
*Li Lucy,Albert Zhang,Nathan Anderson,Ryan Knight,Kyle Lo*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Evaluating 11 vision-language models on real students’ handwritten math work (DrawEduMath) shows they struggle most with diagnosing student errors—especially on lower-proficiency students—implying current math-optimized VLMs are ill-suited for pedagogical use without new training incentives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In math education, timely identification and interpretation of student mistakes are central to effective teaching. For AI to be pedagogically useful, it must robustly understand work from students at varying proficiency levels, not just solve problems. The study probes whether current VLMs meet this need.

Method: A year-long evaluation of 11 VLMs on DrawEduMath, a QA benchmark built from real students’ handwritten and hand-drawn math responses. Performance is analyzed across question types, with particular focus on items that require assessing student error and across strata reflecting students’ need for pedagogical help.

Result: All VLMs underperform when interpreting work from students who need more help; across all QA categories, the most difficult items are those requiring assessment of student error. Weaknesses cluster around diagnosing and describing student mistakes rather than solving canonical math problems.

Conclusion: Current VLMs, while strong at problem solving, are not reliable at pedagogically critical tasks like error diagnosis. To be useful in classrooms, they need different objectives, data, and evaluation protocols that emphasize understanding and explaining student errors and supporting lower-proficiency students.

Abstract: Effective mathematics education requires identifying and responding to students' mistakes. For AI to support pedagogical applications, models must perform well across different levels of student proficiency. Our work provides an extensive, year-long snapshot of how 11 vision-language models (VLMs) perform on DrawEduMath, a QA benchmark involving real students' handwritten, hand-drawn responses to math problems. We find that models' weaknesses concentrate on a core component of math education: student error. All evaluated VLMs underperform when describing work from students who require more pedagogical help, and across all QA, they struggle the most on questions related to assessing student error. Thus, while VLMs may be optimized to be math problem solving experts, our results suggest that they require alternative development incentives to adequately support educational use cases.

</details>


### [356] [Towards Orthographically-Informed Evaluation of Speech Recognition Systems for Indian Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00941)
*Kaushal Santosh Bhogale,Tahir Javed,Greeshma Susan John,Dhruv Rathi,Akshayasree Padmanaban,Niharika Parasa,Mitesh M. Khapra*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces OIWER, an orthography-aware ASR evaluation for Indian languages using LLM-derived permissible variants, yielding lower, more human-aligned error rates and smaller model gaps than standard WER/WER-SN.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard WER penalizes legitimate orthographic variability common in Indian languages (spelling variants, flexible suffix splitting, non-standard code-mixed spellings), making systems look worse than users perceive—especially problematic for under-resourced languages lacking normalization resources.

Method: Leverage LLMs to construct evaluation benchmarks that enumerate or accept permissible orthographic variations, then compute OIWER, a WER-style metric that credits those variants as correct. Validate via extensive experiments across Indian-language ASR outputs and compare against WER and WER-SN, including human-judgment alignment studies.

Result: OIWER reduces pessimistic error rates by an average of 6.3 points, shrinks inflated model performance gaps (e.g., Gemini–Canary from 18.1 to 11.5 points), and aligns with human perception better than WER-SN by 4.9 points.

Conclusion: Orthography-aware evaluation meaningfully improves fidelity of ASR assessment for Indian languages, producing scores closer to user perception and fairer cross-model comparisons; LLM-assisted benchmark construction makes this feasible for under-resourced settings.

Abstract: Evaluating ASR systems for Indian languages is challenging due to spelling variations, suffix splitting flexibility, and non-standard spellings in code-mixed words. Traditional Word Error Rate (WER) often presents a bleaker picture of system performance than what human users perceive. Better aligning evaluation with real-world performance requires capturing permissible orthographic variations, which is extremely challenging for under-resourced Indian languages. Leveraging recent advances in LLMs, we propose a framework for creating benchmarks that capture permissible variations. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that OIWER, by accounting for orthographic variations, reduces pessimistic error rates (an average improvement of 6.3 points), narrows inflated model gaps (e.g., Gemini-Canary performance difference drops from 18.1 to 11.5 points), and aligns more closely with human perception than prior methods like WER-SN by 4.9 points.

</details>


### [357] [S-VoCAL: A Dataset and Evaluation Framework for Inferring Speaking Voice Character Attributes in Literature](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00958)
*Abigail Berthe-Pardo,Gaspard Michel,Elena V. Epure,Christophe Cerisara*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces S-VoCAL, a dataset and evaluation framework for inferring voice-related character attributes from literature to support character-aware TTS; 8 sociophonetic attributes over 952 character–book pairs, with an LLM-embedding similarity metric and a RAG baseline that works well for Age/Gender but poorly for Origin/Physical Health.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Synthetic audiobook TTS sounds natural but still struggles to convincingly portray distinct fictional characters and nuanced prosody. Assigning plausible voices requires inferring character traits (e.g., age, gender, origin, health) from long book contexts, yet there is no benchmark to evaluate such inference systems.

Method: Curate S-VoCAL: 952 character–book pairs from Project Gutenberg annotated for 8 sociophonetic, voice-relevant attributes. Provide an evaluation framework tailored per attribute, including a new LLM-embedding-based similarity metric. Demonstrate utility with a simple Retrieval-Augmented Generation pipeline that extracts evidence from text and predicts attributes.

Result: The RAG baseline reliably infers straightforward attributes like Age and Gender but performs poorly on more nuanced ones such as Origin and Physical Health, indicating current limitations in attribute extraction from long-form text.

Conclusion: S-VoCAL fills a key benchmarking gap for character voice inference, enabling systematic evaluation and progress toward character-aware TTS. Public release of data/code lowers barriers for research; results highlight the need for improved methods for subtle, context-dependent attributes and prosodic cues.

Abstract: With recent advances in Text-to-Speech (TTS) systems, synthetic audiobook narration has seen increased interest, reaching unprecedented levels of naturalness. However, larger gaps remain in synthetic narration systems' ability to impersonate fictional characters, and convey complex emotions or prosody. A promising direction to enhance character identification is the assignment of plausible voices to each fictional characters in a book. This step typically requires complex inference of attributes in book-length contexts, such as a character's age, gender, origin or physical health, which in turns requires dedicated benchmark datasets to evaluate extraction systems' performances. We present S-VoCAL (Speaking Voice Character Attributes in Literature), the first dataset and evaluation framework dedicated to evaluate the inference of voice-related fictional character attributes. S-VoCAL entails 8 attributes grounded in sociophonetic studies, and 952 character-book pairs derived from Project Gutenberg. Its evaluation framework addresses the particularities of each attribute, and includes a novel similarity metric based on recent Large Language Models embeddings. We demonstrate the applicability of S-VoCAL by applying a simple Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline to the task of inferring character attributes. Our results suggest that the RAG pipeline reliably infers attributes such as Age or Gender, but struggles on others such as Origin or Physical Health. The dataset and evaluation code are available at https://github.com/AbigailBerthe/S-VoCAL .

</details>


### [358] [Qayyem: A Real-time Platform for Scoring Proficiency of Arabic Essays](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01009)
*Hoor Elbahnasawi,Marwan Sayed,Sohaila Eltanbouly,Fatima Brahamia,Tamer Elsayed*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Qayyem is a web platform that streamlines Arabic Automated Essay Scoring (AES), offering an end-to-end workflow for instructors and deploying multiple state-of-the-art Arabic AES models with per-trait evaluation, while hiding API/engineering complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Arabic AES lags behind other languages due to linguistic complexity and the scarcity of large, publicly annotated datasets. Educators need scalable, consistent writing assessment but lack accessible tools and resources to apply advanced AES methods to Arabic essays.

Method: Design and implement a web-based system that: (1) supports assignment creation and batch essay uploads; (2) offers configurable scoring and per-trait evaluation; (3) abstracts interactions with scoring server APIs through a user-friendly interface; and (4) hosts several state-of-the-art Arabic essay scoring models with varying effectiveness–efficiency trade-offs.

Result: A functioning platform that enables non-technical instructors to access advanced Arabic AES services, compare multiple deployed models, and obtain per-trait scores. The abstract implies availability of models and trade-offs but does not report empirical metrics.

Conclusion: Qayyem lowers the barrier to Arabic AES adoption by integrating workflow management with accessible model deployment, potentially improving consistency and scalability of Arabic writing assessment. Further quantitative validation and dataset expansion are likely needed.

Abstract: Over the past years, Automated Essay Scoring (AES) systems have gained increasing attention as scalable and consistent solutions for assessing the proficiency of student writing. Despite recent progress, support for Arabic AES remains limited due to linguistic complexity and scarcity of large publicly-available annotated datasets. In this work, we present Qayyem, a Web-based platform designed to support Arabic AES by providing an integrated workflow for assignment creation, batch essay upload, scoring configuration, and per-trait essay evaluation. Qayyem abstracts the technical complexity of interacting with scoring server APIs, allowing instructors to access advanced scoring services through a user-friendly interface. The platform deploys a number of state-of-the-art Arabic essay scoring models with different effectiveness and efficiency figures.

</details>


### [359] [Thoth: Mid-Training Bridges LLMs to Time Series Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01042)
*Jiafeng Lin,Yuxuan Wang,Jialong Wu,Huakun Luo,Zhongyi Pei,Jianmin Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Thoth introduces a mid-training stage and a time-series–centric corpus (Book-of-Thoth) to align time series with natural language, enabling bidirectional TS↔text generation and substantially improving LLM performance on time-series QA; it also releases a knowledge-intensive benchmark (KnoTS) and shows strong data-efficient fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: General-purpose LLMs struggle with temporal pattern understanding and reasoning, which limits their usefulness for decision-making tasks dependent on time series dynamics. The paper aims to endow LLMs with general time series understanding in a task- and domain-agnostic way.

Method: Introduce “mid-training” as an intermediate alignment phase between pretraining and task fine-tuning. Construct Book-of-Thoth, a high-quality corpus centered on time series to support both TS-to-text and text-to-TS generation. Train a family of models (Thoth) using this corpus. Propose KnoTS, a benchmark targeting knowledge-intensive time series understanding that requires joint reasoning over temporal patterns and domain knowledge. Evaluate on diverse time-series QA tasks and under data-scarce fine-tuning settings.

Result: Mid-training with Book-of-Thoth enables Thoth to significantly outperform its base model and strong LLM baselines on multiple time-series QA benchmarks. Thoth also shows superior performance when fine-tuned with limited data, indicating improved data efficiency.

Conclusion: A dedicated mid-training alignment stage with a time-series corpus effectively imparts general time series reasoning capabilities to LLMs, leading to better benchmark performance and strong data efficiency; KnoTS offers a relevant evaluation suite for knowledge-intensive temporal reasoning.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable success in general-purpose reasoning. However, they still struggle to understand and reason about time series data, which limits their effectiveness in decision-making scenarios that depend on temporal dynamics. In this paper, we propose Thoth, the first family of mid-trained LLMs with general-purpose time series understanding capabilities. As a pivotal intermediate stage, mid-training achieves task- and domain-agnostic alignment between time series and natural language, for which we construct Book-of-Thoth, a high-quality, time-series-centric mid-training corpus. Book-of-Thoth enables both time-series-to-text and text-to-time-series generation, equipping LLMs with a foundational grasp of temporal patterns. To better evaluate advanced reasoning capabilities, we further present KnoTS, a novel benchmark of knowledge-intensive time series understanding, designed for joint reasoning over temporal patterns and domain knowledge. Extensive experiments demonstrate that mid-training with Book-of-Thoth enables Thoth to significantly outperform its base model and advanced LLMs across a range of time series question answering benchmarks. Moreover, Thoth exhibits superior capabilities when fine-tuned under data scarcity, underscoring the effectiveness of mid-training for time series understanding. Code is available at: https://github.com/thuml/Thoth.

</details>


### [360] [GroupGPT: A Token-efficient and Privacy-preserving Agentic Framework for Multi-User Chat Assistant](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01059)
*Zhuokang Shen,Yifan Wang,Hanyu Chen,Wenxuan Huang,Shaohui Lin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: GroupGPT is a multimodal, token-efficient, privacy-preserving framework for group-chat assistants that separates when to intervene from what to say, and introduces MUIR, a 2,500-sample benchmark for intervention reasoning. It achieves strong timing and response quality with up to 3× fewer tokens than baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most LLM chatbots are designed for single-user dialogues and struggle in multi-user group chats that demand proactive, well-timed interventions under shifting context. Prior methods use large models for both reasoning and generation, causing high token costs, scalability issues, and privacy risks when sending raw messages to the cloud.

Method: GroupGPT uses a small–large model collaboration: a lightweight model monitors context and determines intervention timing, while a larger model handles response generation only when needed. The system supports multimodal inputs (memes, images, videos, voice) and sanitizes private data before cloud transmission. The authors also release MUIR—a benchmark of 2,500 annotated group-chat segments with intervention labels and rationales—to evaluate timing accuracy and response quality.

Result: Across diverse group-chat scenarios, GroupGPT delivers accurate, well-timed responses, scoring 4.72/5.0 in LLM-based evaluations and receiving positive user feedback. It reduces token usage by up to 3× relative to baselines, indicating improved efficiency. Models of varying sizes are evaluated on MUIR to validate generality.

Conclusion: Decoupling intervention timing from response generation enables efficient, accurate, and privacy-conscious group-chat assistance. With multimodal support and the new MUIR benchmark, GroupGPT provides a practical, scalable path for multi-user assistants; code is publicly available.

Abstract: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have enabled increasingly capable chatbots. However, most existing systems focus on single-user settings and do not generalize well to multi-user group chats, where agents require more proactive and accurate intervention under complex, evolving contexts. Existing approaches typically rely on LLMs for both reasoning and generation, leading to high token consumption, limited scalability, and potential privacy risks. To address these challenges, we propose GroupGPT, a token-efficient and privacy-preserving agentic framework for multi-user chat assistant. GroupGPT adopts a small-large model collaborative architecture to decouple intervention timing from response generation, enabling efficient and accurate decision-making. The framework also supports multimodal inputs, including memes, images, videos, and voice messages. We further introduce MUIR, a benchmark dataset for multi-user chat assistant intervention reasoning. MUIR contains 2,500 annotated group chat segments with intervention labels and rationales, supporting evaluation of timing accuracy and response quality. We evaluate a range of models on MUIR, from large language models to smaller counterparts. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GroupGPT produces accurate and well-timed responses, achieving an average score of 4.72/5.0 in LLM-based evaluation, and is well received by users across diverse group chat scenarios. Moreover, GroupGPT reduces token usage by up to 3 times compared to baseline methods, while providing privacy sanitization of user messages before cloud transmission. Code is available at: https://github.com/Eliot-Shen/GroupGPT .

</details>


### [361] [How RL Unlocks the Aha Moment in Geometric Interleaved Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01070)
*Xiangxiang Zhang,Caijun Jia,Siyuan Li,Dingyu He,Xiya Xiong,Zheng Sun,Honghao He,Yuchen Wu,Bihui Yu,Linzhuang Sun,Cheng Tan,Jingxuan Wei*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SFT on interleaved plot–reason data hurts reasoning because it learns surface format, not causal dependencies; Faire, an RL framework with causal constraints, functionally aligns plotting with reasoning and improves geometric reasoning performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interleaved geometric reasoning demands tight back-and-forth between constructing diagrams and deducing facts. Despite strong visual skills, MLLMs fine-tuned with standard SFT on interleaved data degrade in reasoning, implying distributional alignment is insufficient. The goal is to train models to internalize the causal role of plots in reasoning, not just mimic format.

Method: Faire: a reinforcement-learning training scheme that enforces three causal constraints linking the generated plot to subsequent reasoning steps, moving beyond imitation to functional alignment. Rewards encourage plots that support deductions and reasoning that correctly references plot geometry; robustness to interventions and consistency between visual and textual states are promoted.

Result: Experiments show a qualitative shift—models use plots as functional tools rather than decorative outputs—and achieve competitive results on challenging geometric reasoning benchmarks, outperforming SFT interleaved baselines and recovering or exceeding text-only baselines.

Conclusion: SFT alone is inadequate for interleaved multimodal reasoning. Functional alignment via RL with causal constraints enables MLLMs to internalize plotting as part of the reasoning loop, yielding more robust and accurate geometric problem solving and pointing to better training paradigms for multimodal reasoning.

Abstract: Solving complex geometric problems inherently requires interleaved reasoning: a tight alternation between constructing diagrams and performing logical deductions. Although recent Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in visual generation and plotting, we identify a counter-intuitive and underexplored phenomenon. Naively applying Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) on interleaved plot-solution data leads to a substantial degradation in reasoning performance compared to text-only baselines. We argue that this failure stems from a fundamental limitation of SFT, which primarily induces distributional alignment: the model learns to reproduce the surface format of interleaved plotting but fails to internalize the causal dependency between the generated plot and reasoning steps. To overcome this limitation, we propose Faire (Functional alignment for interleaved reasoning), a reinforcement learning framework that enforces three casual constraints to move beyond superficial imitation toward functional alignment. Extensive experiments show that Faire induces a qualitative shift in model behavior in which the plotting is effectively internalized, yielding competitive performance on challenging geometric reasoning benchmarks.

</details>


### [362] [CARD: Towards Conditional Design of Multi-agent Topological Structures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01089)
*Tongtong Wu,Yanming Li,Ziye Tang,Chen Jiang,Linhao Luo,Guilin Qi,Shirui Pan,Gholamreza Haffari*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: CARD is a conditional graph-generation framework that adapts multi‑agent LLM communication topologies to environmental signals (e.g., model/tool changes) using a conditional variational graph encoder and environment‑aware optimization, yielding higher accuracy and robustness on HumanEval, MATH, and MMLU than static or prompt-based baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed or statically learned communication topologies in LLM multi‑agent systems break down when real‑world conditions change (model upgrades, API/tool variability, shifting knowledge sources). There is a need for communication structures that adapt at training and inference time to maintain effectiveness and robustness.

Method: Introduce CARD, which instantiates AMACP (an adaptive multi‑agent communication protocol). CARD conditions graph construction on dynamic environmental signals and learns via a conditional variational graph encoder with environment‑aware objectives, enabling topology adaptation during training and runtime.

Result: Across HumanEval, MATH, and MMLU, CARD consistently outperforms static and prompt-engineered baselines, providing higher accuracy and improved robustness under shifts in model capability or resource availability.

Conclusion: Adaptive, condition-aware communication topology design is crucial for resilient multi‑agent LLM systems. CARD provides a principled mechanism to generate such topologies and demonstrates superior, robust performance across diverse benchmarks; code is released for reproducibility.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM)-based multi-agent systems have shown strong capabilities in tasks such as code generation and collaborative reasoning. However, the effectiveness and robustness of these systems critically depend on their communication topology, which is often fixed or statically learned, ignoring real-world dynamics such as model upgrades, API (or tool) changes, or knowledge source variability. To address this limitation, we propose CARD (Conditional Agentic Graph Designer), a conditional graph-generation framework that instantiates AMACP, a protocol for adaptive multi-agent communication. CARD explicitly incorporates dynamic environmental signals into graph construction, enabling topology adaptation at both training and runtime. Through a conditional variational graph encoder and environment-aware optimization, CARD produces communication structures that are both effective and resilient to shifts in model capability or resource availability. Empirical results on HumanEval, MATH, and MMLU demonstrate that CARD consistently outperforms static and prompt-based baselines, achieving higher accuracy and robustness across diverse conditions. The source code is available at: https://github.com/Warma10032/CARD.

</details>


### [363] [DEP: A Decentralized Large Language Model Evaluation Protocol](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01167)
*Jianxiang Peng,Junhao Li,Hongxiang Wang,Haocheng Lyu,Hui Guo,Siyi Hao,Zhen Wang,Chuang Liu,Shaowei Zhang,Bojian Xiong,Yue Chen,Zhuowen Han,Ling Shi,Tianyu Dong,Juesi Xiao,Lei Yang,Yuqi Ren,Deyi Xiong*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: DEP is a decentralized, standardized evaluation protocol and toolkit for LLM benchmarks that keeps datasets/answers server-side to prevent leakage, enables plug-and-play evaluation, and reduces deployment costs; already adapted to 60+ benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM benchmarks lack unified standards, rely on manual custom scripts that hinder consistency and reproducibility, and centralized evaluation frameworks expose datasets/answers, increasing leakage risk.

Method: Propose a Decentralized Evaluation Protocol centered on a reusable matching server that decouples users, LLMs, and benchmarks. Benchmark data and evaluation logic stay exclusively on the server (local or remote). Provide a DEP Toolkit supporting breakpoint resume, concurrent requests, congestion control, and documentation to adapt new benchmarks, enabling modular, plug-and-play evaluation without exposing ground truth.

Result: DEP was used to evaluate multiple LLMs across benchmarks. Experiments verify its effectiveness and show reduced deployment costs. As of Feb 2026, over 60 benchmarks have been adapted to the protocol.

Conclusion: DEP enables unified, reproducible, leak-resistant, and modular LLM evaluation across diverse tasks/domains, with long-term reusability and community co-construction to scale coverage.

Abstract: With the rapid development of Large Language Models (LLMs), a large number of benchmarks have been proposed. However, most benchmarks lack unified evaluation standard and require the manual implementation of custom scripts, making results hard to ensure consistency and reproducibility. Furthermore, mainstream evaluation frameworks are centralized, with datasets and answers, which increases the risk of benchmark leakage. To address these issues, we propose a Decentralized Evaluation Protocol (DEP), a decentralized yet unified and standardized evaluation framework through a matching server without constraining benchmarks. The server can be mounted locally or deployed remotely, and once adapted, it can be reused over the long term. By decoupling users, LLMs, and benchmarks, DEP enables modular, plug-and-play evaluation: benchmark files and evaluation logic stay exclusively on the server side. In remote setting, users cannot access the ground truth, thereby achieving data isolation and leak-proof evaluation. To facilitate practical adoption, we develop DEP Toolkit, a protocol-compatible toolkit that supports features such as breakpoint resume, concurrent requests, and congestion control. We also provide detailed documentation for adapting new benchmarks to DEP. Using DEP toolkit, we evaluate multiple LLMs across benchmarks. Experimental results verify the effectiveness of DEP and show that it reduces the cost of deploying benchmark evaluations. As of February 2026, we have adapted over 60 benchmarks and continue to promote community co-construction to support unified evaluation across various tasks and domains.

</details>


### [364] [Token-level Data Selection for Safe LLM Fine-tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01185)
*Yanping Li,Zhening Liu,Zijian Li,Zehong Lin,Jun Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They diagnose why safety degrades during LLM fine-tuning at the token level and introduce TOSS/TOSS-Pro, which score and remove unsafe tokens based on loss differences between a safety-degraded and a utility-oriented model, yielding better safety-utility trade-offs than sample-level defenses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fine-tuning on custom data often weakens LLM safety. Current defenses filter or modify data at the sample level, frequently harming task utility or missing fine-grained unsafe content. A more precise, token-level approach could preserve utility while mitigating safety risks.

Method: Token-level data selection (TOSS) computes a per-token safety risk by measuring the loss difference between a safety-degraded model and a utility-oriented model; tokens with high risk are removed to keep task-relevant content. TOSS-Pro adds progressive refinement: iteratively improving the safety-degraded model’s ability to flag unsafe tokens and re-filtering the data.

Result: Across extensive experiments, TOSS/TOSS-Pro maintain or improve downstream task performance while increasing safety, and they significantly outperform prior sample-level defense baselines on the safety–utility trade-off. (No specific numbers are given in the abstract.)

Conclusion: Token-level diagnosis and filtering is an effective strategy for safe LLM fine-tuning. TOSS and its iterative variant TOSS-Pro better preserve utility while mitigating safety risks, offering a practical path to safer domain adaptation; code is publicly available.

Abstract: Fine-tuning large language models (LLMs) on custom datasets has become a standard approach for adapting these models to specific domains and applications. However, recent studies have shown that such fine-tuning can lead to significant degradation in the model's safety. Existing defense methods operate at the sample level and often suffer from an unsatisfactory trade-off between safety and utility. To address this limitation, we perform a systematic token-level diagnosis of safety degradation during fine-tuning. Based on this, we propose token-level data selection for safe LLM fine-tuning (TOSS), a novel framework that quantifies the safety risk of each token by measuring the loss difference between a safety-degraded model and a utility-oriented model. This token-level granularity enables accurate identification and removal of unsafe tokens, thereby preserving valuable task-specific information. In addition, we introduce a progressive refinement strategy, TOSS-Pro, which iteratively enhances the safety-degraded model's ability to identify unsafe tokens. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach robustly safeguards LLMs during fine-tuning while achieving superior downstream task performance, significantly outperforming existing sample-level defense methods. Our code is available at https://github.com/Polly-LYP/TOSS.

</details>


### [365] [Reasoning or Rationalization? The Role of Justifications in Masked Diffusion Models for Fact Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01190)
*Jacob Devasier*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: For masked diffusion language models (MDLMs) on fact verification, the verdict is settled early and acts as a global anchor; forcing “reasoning first” harms accuracy because noisy, evolving justifications feed back and override initially correct verdicts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Chain-of-Thought improves autoregressive models via step-by-step reasoning, but MDLMs update all positions in parallel. It is unclear whether justifications are true reasoning or post‑hoc and how imposing reasoning‑first affects MDLM decision quality.

Method: Analyze diffusion-time dynamics on fact verification: (1) measure when verdict tokens stabilize; (2) enforce delayed verdict unmasking to force reasoning-first; (3) intervene by forcing incorrect verdicts to test rationalization; (4) corrupt vs provide ground-truth justifications to assess causal influence on verdicts.

Result: Verdicts typically converge early. Forcing delayed verdict unmasking drops accuracy from 86.2% to 71.9%. The model rationalizes incorrect forced verdicts in 56% of cases. Verdict accuracy falls to 57.3% with corrupted justifications but rises to 97.1% with ground-truth justifications, indicating strong causal dependence on justification quality.

Conclusion: MDLMs condition verdicts on concurrently generated justification tokens; as noisy justifications accrue, they can overwrite correct early assessments. Hence, extended deliberation and reasoning-first constraints can be counterproductive for MDLM fact verification; better strategies should decouple or stabilize verdicts while generating explanations.

Abstract: Unlike autoregressive models, which generate tokens sequentially and benefit from reasoning-before-answering strategies such as Chain-of-Thought, Masked Diffusion Language Models (MDLMs) refine all sequence positions simultaneously, raising questions about how these models handle tasks requiring justified verdicts. In this work, we investigate the dynamics of MDLM reasoning on fact verification, examining whether justifications serve as genuine reasoning or post-hoc rationalization. We observe that MDLMs typically converge on a verdict early in the diffusion process, treating it as a global anchor that is resolved before the justification is complete. Crucially, enforcing a reasoning-first constraint via delayed verdict unmasking actively degrades performance, dropping accuracy from 86.2% to 71.9% as accumulating justification tokens introduce inconsistencies that override initially correct predictions. Interventional experiments reveal that the model rationalizes incorrect forced verdicts in 56% of cases, and that verdicts are strongly causally dependent on justification quality (57.3% accuracy with corrupted justifications vs. 97.1% with ground-truth). This causal dependence explains the degradation under forced deliberation: as the model generates noisy justification tokens, it conditions on them, gradually overriding its initially correct assessment. Our findings suggest that for fact verification with MDLMs, extended deliberation can be counterproductive, risking the dilution of accurate early predictions with noise introduced during justification generation.

</details>


### [366] [XAI-enhanced Comparative Opinion Mining via Aspect-based Scoring and Semantic Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01212)
*Ngoc-Quang Le,T. Thanh-Lam Nguyen,Quoc-Trung Phu,Thi-Phuong Le,Duy-Cat Can,Hoang-Quynh Le*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: XCom is an explainable transformer for comparative opinion mining that couples aspect-based rating prediction with semantic comparison and uses SHAP to expose decision rationales, achieving state-of-the-art performance over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformer models for comparative opinion mining are accurate but opaque, which undermines user trust and adoption. The work aims to add transparency without sacrificing performance.

Method: Design a two-module transformer: (1) aspect-based rating prediction to capture per-aspect sentiment/ratings; (2) semantic analysis for comparative judgments across products. Integrate Shapley Additive Explanations (SHAP) to attribute predictions to input features, yielding post-hoc interpretability. Provide implementation and experiments against baselines.

Result: XCom attains leading scores versus competing models on comparative opinion mining benchmarks and produces human-interpretable explanations via SHAP. The abstract claims superior accuracy and meaningful attributions; code is released for reproducibility.
