Conclusion: Evaluation and decoding for radiology VLMs must move beyond overlap metrics. Incorporating CAD/WAE and diversity-aware assessments can reduce metric gaming and better balance clinical specificity with fairness, prompting a rethink of what constitutes “optimal” reporting.

Abstract: Reliable deployment of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) in radiology requires validation metrics that go beyond surface-level text similarity to ensure clinical fidelity and demographic fairness. This paper investigates a critical blind spot in current model evaluation: the use of decoding strategies that lead to high aggregate token-overlap scores despite succumbing to template collapse, in which models generate only repetitive, safe generic text and omit clinical terminology. Unaddressed, this blind spot can lead to metric gaming, where models that perform well on benchmarks prove clinically uninformative. Instead, we advocate for lexical diversity measures to check model generations for clinical specificity. We introduce Clinical Association Displacement (CAD), a vocabulary-level framework that quantifies shifts in demographic-based word associations in generated reports. Weighted Association Erasure (WAE) aggregates these shifts to measure the clinical signal loss across demographic groups. We show that deterministic decoding produces high levels of semantic erasure, while stochastic sampling generates diverse outputs but risks introducing new bias, motivating a fundamental rethink of how "optimal" reporting is defined.

</details>


### [392] [Learning to Draft: Adaptive Speculative Decoding with Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01639)
*Jiebin Zhang,Zhenghan Yu,Liang Wang,Nan Yang,Eugene J. Yu,Zheng Li,Yifan Song,Dawei Zhu,Xingxing Zhang,Furu Wei,Sujian Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LTD is a reinforcement-learning approach to speculative decoding that jointly optimizes drafting and verification to maximize end-to-end throughput, yielding 2.24–4.32× speedups and up to 36.4% over Eagle3 across multiple LLMs and tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing speculative decoding methods either use static time allocation or optimize proxy metrics (e.g., acceptance length) that don’t reflect true wall-clock cost, and they treat drafting and verification as separate, non-coordinated phases. There is a need to adaptively balance and coordinate both phases to directly optimize real-time throughput.

Method: Formulate speculative decoding as an RL problem and train two co-adaptive policies that dynamically coordinate drafting and verification within each cycle. The policies are optimized to maximize throughput (tokens per unit time) rather than proxy acceptance metrics, enabling end-to-end efficiency gains.

Result: On five diverse LLMs and four tasks, LTD achieves 2.24×–4.32× speedups and outperforms state-of-the-art Eagle3 by up to 36.4%.

Conclusion: Directly optimizing end-to-end throughput with co-adaptive RL policies for drafting and verification improves speculative decoding efficiency and generalizes across models and tasks, surpassing prior static and proxy-optimized approaches.

Abstract: Speculative decoding accelerates large language model (LLM) inference by using a small draft model to generate candidate tokens for a larger target model to verify. The efficacy of this technique hinges on the trade-off between the time spent on drafting candidates and verifying them. However, current state-of-the-art methods rely on a static time allocation, while recent dynamic approaches optimize for proxy metrics like acceptance length, often neglecting the true time cost and treating the drafting and verification phases in isolation. To address these limitations, we introduce Learning to Draft (LTD), a novel method that directly optimizes for throughput of each draft-and-verify cycle. We formulate the problem as a reinforcement learning environment and train two co-adaptive policies to dynamically coordinate the draft and verification phases. This encourages the policies to adapt to each other and explicitly maximize decoding efficiency. We conducted extensive evaluations on five diverse LLMs and four distinct tasks. Our results show that LTD achieves speedup ratios ranging from 2.24x to 4.32x, outperforming the state-of-the-art method Eagle3 up to 36.4%.

</details>


### [393] [LexChronos: An Agentic Framework for Structured Event Timeline Extraction in Indian Jurisprudence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01651)
*Anka Chandrahas Tummepalli,Preethu Rose Anish*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LexChronos is a dual-agent system that extracts structured event timelines from Indian Supreme Court judgments, trained with LoRA and refined via a confidence-feedback loop, supported by a 2,000-sample synthetic event dataset; it reports 0.8751 BERT-based F1 versus synthetic labels and yields 75% GPT-4 preference for timeline-based summaries over unstructured text.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Legal judgments are long, complex, and temporally dense. Treating them as unstructured text limits LLMs in summarization, argument construction, and outcome prediction. There is also a scarcity of Indian legal event datasets, hindering model development tailored to this jurisdiction.

Method: A dual-agent, agentic framework: (1) a LoRA-instruct-tuned extraction agent identifies candidate legal events; (2) a pre-trained feedback agent scores and refines them in a confidence-driven loop. To compensate for data scarcity, the authors synthesize a 2,000-sample corpus using reverse-engineering with DeepSeek-R1 and GPT-4 to generate gold event annotations. Evaluation uses a BERT-based F1 metric against the synthetic ground truth and downstream LLM preference tests for summarization.

Result: Event extraction achieves 0.8751 BERT-based F1 on the synthetic test set. In downstream summarization, GPT-4 preferred structured timelines over unstructured baselines in 75% of comparisons, suggesting improved comprehension and reasoning for Indian legal texts.

Conclusion: Structured event timelines materially assist legal NLP for Indian jurisprudence. The agentic extraction-plus-feedback loop, backed by synthetic supervision, shows promise and could underpin applications like precedent mapping, argument synthesis, and judgment prediction. Future work should validate on human-annotated data and expand downstream evaluations.

Abstract: Understanding and predicting judicial outcomes demands nuanced analysis of legal documents. Traditional approaches treat judgments and proceedings as unstructured text, limiting the effectiveness of large language models (LLMs) in tasks such as summarization, argument generation, and judgment prediction. We propose LexChronos, an agentic framework that iteratively extracts structured event timelines from Supreme Court of India judgments. LexChronos employs a dual-agent architecture: a LoRA-instruct-tuned extraction agent identifies candidate events, while a pre-trained feedback agent scores and refines them through a confidence-driven loop. To address the scarcity of Indian legal event datasets, we construct a synthetic corpus of 2000 samples using reverse-engineering techniques with DeepSeek-R1 and GPT-4, generating gold-standard event annotations. Our pipeline achieves a BERT-based F1 score of 0.8751 against this synthetic ground truth. In downstream evaluations on legal text summarization, GPT-4 preferred structured timelines over unstructured baselines in 75% of cases, demonstrating improved comprehension and reasoning in Indian jurisprudence. This work lays a foundation for future legal AI applications in the Indian context, such as precedent mapping, argument synthesis, and predictive judgment modelling, by harnessing structured representations of legal events.

</details>


### [394] [Beyond the Grid: Layout-Informed Multi-Vector Retrieval with Parsed Visual Document Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01666)
*Yibo Yan,Mingdong Ou,Yi Cao,Xin Zou,Shuliang Liu,Jiahao Huo,Yu Huang,James Kwok,Xuming Hu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ColParse compresses multi-vector visual document retrieval by parsing pages into a few layout-aware sub-image embeddings fused with a global page vector, cutting storage by >95% while improving retrieval accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-vector VDR captures fine-grained layout but is storage-heavy; existing compression (merging, pruning, abstract tokens) degrades performance or loses layout information. There is a need for a compact representation that preserves structural cues for scalable deployment.

Method: Use a document parsing model to identify salient layout regions and generate a small set of layout-informed sub-image embeddings, then fuse them with a global page-level embedding to create a compact, structurally-aware multi-vector representation.

Result: Extensive experiments show >95% storage reduction with significant performance gains across multiple VDR benchmarks and base models compared to existing approaches.

Conclusion: ColParse bridges the gap between fine-grained multi-vector accuracy and large-scale practicality, enabling efficient, interpretable multimodal retrieval with drastically reduced storage.

Abstract: Harnessing the full potential of visually-rich documents requires retrieval systems that understand not just text, but intricate layouts, a core challenge in Visual Document Retrieval (VDR). The prevailing multi-vector architectures, while powerful, face a crucial storage bottleneck that current optimization strategies, such as embedding merging, pruning, or using abstract tokens, fail to resolve without compromising performance or ignoring vital layout cues. To address this, we introduce ColParse, a novel paradigm that leverages a document parsing model to generate a small set of layout-informed sub-image embeddings, which are then fused with a global page-level vector to create a compact and structurally-aware multi-vector representation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method reduces storage requirements by over 95% while simultaneously yielding significant performance gains across numerous benchmarks and base models. ColParse thus bridges the critical gap between the fine-grained accuracy of multi-vector retrieval and the practical demands of large-scale deployment, offering a new path towards efficient and interpretable multimodal information systems.

</details>


### [395] [Surgical Post-Training: Cutting Errors, Keeping Knowledge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01683)
*Wenye Lin,Kai Han*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SPoT is a post-training recipe that minimally edits model-generated reasoning traces (“surgical” rectification) and trains with a reward-based binary cross-entropy objective, leveraging DPO’s implicit regularization to boost reasoning accuracy efficiently while mitigating catastrophic forgetting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-training to improve LLM reasoning often requires costly data/compute and risks catastrophic forgetting. Prior work leans on on-policy data (e.g., DPO) to reduce forgetting but overlooks the regularization effect embedded in DPO’s reward estimate. The authors aim to exploit this mechanism to get targeted reasoning gains without erasing prior knowledge.

Method: SPoT has two parts: (1) Data rectification with an Oracle that minimally corrects erroneous intermediate steps in the model’s own outputs, keeping training examples close to the model’s distribution; (2) A reward-based binary cross-entropy loss that treats step/task correctness as a binary classification problem with decoupled supervision, in contrast to DPO’s pairwise ranking loss. Theoretical and empirical analysis link performance/forgetting behavior to implicit regularization from the reward estimate.

Result: Using only 4k rectified math pairs, SPoT lifts Qwen3-8B accuracy by 6.2% on average across in-domain and out-of-distribution tasks, trained in ~28 minutes on 8×H800 GPUs. Experiments and theory support the claim that the reward-estimate regularization mitigates forgetting.

Conclusion: Aligning training data closely to the model’s own generations and replacing relative-ranking losses with a binary reward objective yields efficient reasoning gains while preserving prior knowledge. SPoT offers a practical, compute-light alternative for reasoning post-training that maintains general capabilities.

Abstract: Enhancing the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) via post-training is often constrained by the trade-off between efficiency and catastrophic forgetting. While prior research emphasizes the role of on-policy data in mitigating forgetting, we uncover--and validate both theoretically and empirically--an overlooked yet critical mechanism: the implicit regularization inherent in Direct Preference Optimization's (DPO) reward estimate. This motivates our Surgical Post-Training (SPoT), a new paradigm designed to optimize reasoning efficiently while preserving learned prior knowledge. SPoT consists of: (1) a data rectification pipeline that employs an Oracle to surgically correct erroneous steps via minimal edits, generating data proximal to the model's distribution; and (2) a reward-based binary cross-entropy objective. Unlike the relative ranking in DPO, this objective treats reasoning correctness as a binary classification problem, enforcing decoupled supervision signals. Empirically, with only 4k rectified math data pairs, SPoT improves Qwen3-8B's accuracy by 6.2% on average across in-domain and OOD tasks, requiring merely 28 minutes of training on 8x H800 GPUs. Code: https://github.com/Visual-AI/SPoT

</details>


### [396] [QIME: Constructing Interpretable Medical Text Embeddings via Ontology-Grounded Questions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01690)
*Yixuan Tang,Zhenghong Lin,Yandong Sun,Wynne Hsu,Mong Li Lee,Anthony K. H. Tung*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: QIME is an ontology-grounded, question-based interpretable embedding method for biomedical text that maps each dimension to a clinically meaningful yes/no question, generates fine-grained, semantically atomic questions via cluster-specific concept signatures, and offers a training-free embedding construction; it outperforms prior interpretable methods and narrows the gap to black-box encoders across similarity, clustering, and retrieval, while yielding concise clinical explanations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dense biomedical embeddings perform well but are opaque, limiting trust and utility in clinical decision-making. Existing question-based interpretable embeddings often depend on heuristic, surface-level contrasts and neglect specialized medical knowledge, motivating a domain-grounded, clinically meaningful, and higher-performing interpretable alternative.

Method: Introduce QIME, which: (1) grounds each embedding dimension in a natural-language yes/no clinical question tied to a medical ontology; (2) conditions question generation on cluster-specific medical concept signatures to create semantically atomic, fine-grained distinctions; and (3) provides a training-free embedding construction strategy that removes the need for per-question classifier training.

Result: Across biomedical semantic similarity, clustering, and retrieval benchmarks, QIME consistently surpasses prior interpretable embedding approaches and substantially closes the performance gap with strong black-box biomedical encoders, while also offering concise, clinically informative explanations.

Conclusion: Ontology-grounded, question-based interpretable embeddings can deliver both transparency and competitive performance for biomedical NLP. QIME’s cluster-conditioned, semantically atomic questions and training-free construction make it practical and effective, advancing interpretable clinical text representation.

Abstract: While dense biomedical embeddings achieve strong performance, their black-box nature limits their utility in clinical decision-making. Recent question-based interpretable embeddings represent text as binary answers to natural-language questions, but these approaches often rely on heuristic or surface-level contrastive signals and overlook specialized domain knowledge. We propose QIME, an ontology-grounded framework for constructing interpretable medical text embeddings in which each dimension corresponds to a clinically meaningful yes/no question. By conditioning on cluster-specific medical concept signatures, QIME generates semantically atomic questions that capture fine-grained distinctions in biomedical text. Furthermore, QIME supports a training-free embedding construction strategy that eliminates per-question classifier training while further improving performance. Experiments across biomedical semantic similarity, clustering, and retrieval benchmarks show that QIME consistently outperforms prior interpretable embedding methods and substantially narrows the gap to strong black-box biomedical encoders, while providing concise and clinically informative explanations.

</details>


### [397] [Building a Strong Instruction Language Model for a Less-Resourced Language](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01691)
*Domen Vreš,Tjaša Arčon,Timotej Petrič,Dario Vajda,Marko Robnik-Šikonja,Iztok Lebar Bajec*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: GaMS3-12B is a 12B-parameter Slovene-focused LLM built via multi-stage continual pretraining of Gemma 3 and two-stage SFT on 140B multi-lingual tokens and 200k+ SFT examples; it outperforms Gemma 3 (12B) on Slovene tasks and competes with GPT-4o in a Slovene arena (>60% win rate).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-source LLMs are predominantly English-centric, leading to weaker performance in less-resourced languages. The work aims to provide a concrete methodology to adapt strong base models to such languages, demonstrated on Slovene.

Method: Start from Gemma 3 and apply three-stage continual pretraining using 140B tokens spanning Slovene, English, and closely related South Slavic languages (Bosnian, Serbian, Croatian), followed by two-stage supervised fine-tuning with 200k+ English and Slovene examples; evaluate on Slovenian-LLM-Eval, English→Slovene translation, and a Slovene LLM arena.

Result: GaMS3-12B is the best open-source Slovene model in its parameter class; it surpasses Gemma 3 (12B) across all evaluated scenarios and achieves >60% win rate in the Slovene LLM arena, performing competitively with much larger commercial GPT-4o.

Conclusion: A structured pipeline—multi-stage continual pretraining augmented with related-language data plus staged SFT—effectively adapts an LLM to a less-resourced language. GaMS3-12B establishes strong Slovene performance among open-source 12B models and shows that careful cross-lingual training can narrow the gap with state-of-the-art commercial systems.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have become an essential tool for natural language processing and artificial intelligence in general. Current open-source models are primarily trained on English texts, resulting in poorer performance on less-resourced languages and cultures. We present a set of methodological approaches necessary for the successful adaptation of an LLM to a less-resourced language, and demonstrate them using the Slovene language. We present GaMS3-12B, a generative model for Slovene with 12 billion parameters, and demonstrate that it is the best-performing open-source model for Slovene within its parameter range. We adapted the model to the Slovene language using three-stage continual pre-training of the Gemma 3 model, followed by two-stage supervised fine-tuning (SFT). We trained the model on a combination of 140B Slovene, English, Bosnian, Serbian, and Croatian pretraining tokens, and over 200 thousand English and Slovene SFT examples. We evaluate GaMS3-12B on the Slovenian-LLM-Eval datasets, English-to-Slovene translation, and the Slovene LLM arena. We show that the described model outperforms 12B Gemma 3 across all three scenarios and performs comparably to much larger commercial GPT-4o in the Slovene LLM arena, achieving a win rate of over 60 %.

</details>


### [398] [Legal RAG Bench: an end-to-end benchmark for legal RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01710)
*Abdur-Rahman Butler,Umar Butler*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Legal RAG Bench is a domain-specific benchmark and evaluation methodology for legal retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), showing that retrieval quality is the dominant determinant of performance; a specific embedder (Kanon 2) yields large gains, and many “hallucinations” stem from retrieval failures rather than LLM reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current legal RAG systems lack a rigorous, domain-grounded way to disentangle and quantify the contributions of retrieval versus generation. Existing evaluations often conflate hallucinations with retrieval errors and lack expert-level, long-form questions with provenance. The authors aim to create a standardized, reproducible benchmark and methodology that enables apples-to-apples comparisons and a clearer error taxonomy in legal RAG.

Method: Build a benchmark from 4,876 passages in the Victorian Criminal Charge Book and 100 complex, expert-authored questions with long-form reference answers and supporting passages. Propose an evaluation methodology using a full factorial design to systematically swap retrieval and reasoning components, coupled with a hierarchical error decomposition to attribute errors to retrieval vs. generation. Evaluate three embedding models (Kanon 2 Embedder, Gemini Embedding 001, OpenAI Text Embedding 3 Large) and two LLMs (Gemini 3.1 Pro, GPT-5.2) on correctness, groundedness, and retrieval accuracy.

Result: Retrieval quality is the primary driver of legal RAG performance; LLM choice has a smaller yet noticeable effect on correctness and groundedness. Kanon 2 Embedder delivers the largest improvements: +17.5 points in average correctness, +4.5 in groundedness, and +34 in retrieval accuracy. Many outputs labeled as hallucinations are actually downstream of retrieval failure. The ceiling of performance is set by retrieval effectiveness.

Conclusion: Improving retrievers—especially domain-adapted embedding models—is the most impactful lever for boosting legal RAG quality. The provided benchmark and hierarchical error analysis enable fair component-wise comparisons and better diagnosis of failure modes. The authors release code and data to support reproducibility and further research.

Abstract: We introduce Legal RAG Bench, a benchmark and evaluation methodology for assessing the end-to-end performance of legal RAG systems. As a benchmark, Legal RAG Bench consists of 4,876 passages from the Victorian Criminal Charge Book alongside 100 complex, hand-crafted questions demanding expert knowledge of criminal law and procedure. Both long-form answers and supporting passages are provided. As an evaluation methodology, Legal RAG Bench leverages a full factorial design and novel hierarchical error decomposition framework, enabling apples-to-apples comparisons of the contributions of retrieval and reasoning models in RAG. We evaluate three state-of-the-art embedding models (Isaacus' Kanon 2 Embedder, Google's Gemini Embedding 001, and OpenAI's Text Embedding 3 Large) and two frontier LLMs (Gemini 3.1 Pro and GPT-5.2), finding that information retrieval is the primary driver of legal RAG performance, with LLMs exerting a more moderate effect on correctness and groundedness. Kanon 2 Embedder, in particular, had the largest positive impact on performance, improving average correctness by 17.5 points, groundedness by 4.5 points, and retrieval accuracy by 34 points. We observe that many errors attributed to hallucinations in legal RAG systems are in fact triggered by retrieval failures, concluding that retrieval sets the ceiling for the performance of many modern legal RAG systems. We document why and how we built Legal RAG Bench alongside the results of our evaluations. We also openly release our code and data to assist with reproduction of our findings.

</details>


### [399] [Bootstrapping Embeddings for Low Resource Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01732)
*Merve Basoz,Andrew Horne,Mattia Opper*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They evaluate LLM-based strategies to synthesize triplet data for training multilingual embedding models and find that adapter composition and an XL-LoRA cross-lingual finetuning approach substantially improve performance across tasks and languages, whereas plain in-context generation lags behind strong non-synthetic baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-quality embedding models depend on supervised triplet data that is plentiful in English but scarce in many other languages. The work asks whether LLMs can bridge this data gap by generating effective synthetic training data.

Method: Compare three synthetic triplet-generation strategies for optimizing embedding models: (1) in-context learning (prompt-only generation); (2) a novel adapter-composition approach to steer the LLM; and (3) XL-LoRA, a cross-lingual finetuning of the LLM generator. Use the generated triplets to train embeddings and evaluate across multiple tasks and languages.

Result: In-context learning underperforms relative to strong non-synthetic baselines. Both adapter composition and XL-LoRA deliver strong, consistent gains across a wide range of tasks and languages.

Conclusion: LLM-generated synthetic data, when produced via adapter composition or XL-LoRA, provides a scalable, effective route to high-quality multilingual embedding models, helping address data scarcity beyond English.

Abstract: Embedding models are crucial to modern NLP. However, the creation of the most effective models relies on carefully constructed supervised finetuning data. For high resource languages, such as English, such datasets are readily available. However, for hundreds of other languages, they are simply non-existent. We investigate whether the advent of large language models can help to bridge this gap. We test three different strategies for generating synthetic triplet data used to optimise embedding models. These include in-context learning as well as two novel approaches, leveraging adapter composition and cross lingual finetuning of the LLM generator (XL-LoRA) respectively. We find that while in-context learning still falls short of strong non-synthetic baselines, adapter composition and XL-LoRA yield strong performance gains across a wide array of tasks and languages, offering a clear, scalable pathway to producing performant embedding models for a wide variety of languages.

</details>


### [400] [AnnoABSA: A Web-Based Annotation Tool for Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis with Retrieval-Augmented Suggestions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01773)
*Nils Constantin Hellwig,Jakob Fehle,Udo Kruschwitz,Christian Wolff*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: AnnoABSA is an open-source, customizable web tool for full-spectrum ABSA annotation that offers optional RAG-augmented LLM suggestions using the top-10 most similar previously annotated examples as few-shot prompts, improving suggestion accuracy over time while keeping humans in control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current ABSA annotation tools do not comprehensively cover all ABSA subtasks, are often hard to customize, and rarely integrate controllable LLM assistance. There is a need for a flexible, extensible platform that supports diverse ABSA schemas and leverages modern LLMs without relinquishing human oversight.

Method: Implement a web-based, highly configurable annotation interface for ABSA tasks. Provide optional LLM-based retrieval-augmented generation: retrieve the ten most similar, already-annotated instances and insert them as few-shot examples into the prompt to produce context-aware suggestions. Maintain a human-in-the-loop workflow where annotators can accept or edit suggestions. Release the tool under the MIT License for easy adoption and extension.

Result: Delivers context-aware LLM suggestions that reportedly become more accurate as more annotated data accumulates due to retrieval over an expanding pool of examples. The system and code are released as open-source. The abstract does not report quantitative evaluations or user studies.

Conclusion: AnnoABSA offers a practical, extensible solution for ABSA annotation with LLM-assisted RAG that adapts and improves during use while preserving annotator control, making it well-suited for research and real-world deployments.

Abstract: We introduce AnnoABSA, the first web-based annotation tool to support the full spectrum of Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA) tasks. The tool is highly customizable, enabling flexible configuration of sentiment elements and task-specific requirements. Alongside manual annotation, AnnoABSA provides optional Large Language Model (LLM)-based retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) suggestions that offer context-aware assistance in a human-in-the-loop approach, keeping the human annotator in control. To improve prediction quality over time, the system retrieves the ten most similar examples that are already annotated and adds them as few-shot examples in the prompt, ensuring that suggestions become increasingly accurate as the annotation process progresses. Released as open-source software under the MIT License, AnnoABSA is freely accessible and easily extendable for research and practical applications.

</details>


### [401] [Beyond the Resumé: A Rubric-Aware Automatic Interview System for Information Elicitation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01775)
*Harry Stuart,Masahiro Kaneko,Timothy Baldwin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose an LLM-driven interviewer that adaptively elicits role-specific evidence and updates calibrated beliefs about a candidate’s rubric-aligned latent traits; in simulations, the inferred abilities converge to ground truth. Code, data, and a demo are released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Expert interviews are effective but expensive and don’t scale; current automated screening relies on shallow signals from resumes. The authors aim to cheaply capture nuanced, role-specific information early in the funnel to make better hiring decisions.

Method: Build a system where an LLM acts as a subject-matter-expert interviewer, asking targeted questions and updating a probabilistic belief over rubric-oriented latent traits. Belief calibration is emphasized. Evaluate using simulated interviews with applicants that have known (artificial) latent ability levels; include calibration tests. Provide a small public/anonymized resume dataset and tooling.

Result: On simulated candidates, the posterior beliefs over traits converge toward the true, constructed ability levels, indicating successful elicitation and calibrated updating. Artifacts (code, dataset, calibration tests, demo) are publicly released.

Conclusion: LLM-led interviewing can improve early-stage screening by extracting richer, role-specific signals and updating trait estimates in a calibrated way. Results are promising in simulation, but real-world validation, fairness/bias auditing, and robustness checks will be necessary before deployment.

Abstract: Effective hiring is integral to the success of an organisation, but it is very challenging to find the most suitable candidates because expert evaluation (e.g.\ interviews conducted by a technical manager) are expensive to deploy at scale. Therefore, automated resume scoring and other applicant-screening methods are increasingly used to coarsely filter candidates, making decisions on limited information. We propose that large language models (LLMs) can play the role of subject matter experts to cost-effectively elicit information from each candidate that is nuanced and role-specific, thereby improving the quality of early-stage hiring decisions. We present a system that leverages an LLM interviewer to update belief over an applicant's rubric-oriented latent traits in a calibrated way. We evaluate our system on simulated interviews and show that belief converges towards the simulated applicants' artificially-constructed latent ability levels. We release code, a modest dataset of public-domain/anonymised resumes, belief calibration tests, and simulated interviews, at \href{https://github.com/mbzuai-nlp/beyond-the-resume}{https://github.com/mbzuai-nlp/beyond-the-resume}. Our demo is available at \href{https://btr.hstu.net}{https://btr.hstu.net}.

</details>


### [402] [FreeAct: Freeing Activations for LLM Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01776)
*Xiaohao Liu,Xiaobo Xia,Manyi Zhang,Ji-Fu Li,Xianzhi Yu,Fei Shen,Xiu Su,See-Kiong Ng,Tat-Seng Chua*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: FreeAct is a token-aware, dynamic activation transformation framework for LLM quantization that decouples activation transforms from weight transforms by exploiting low-rank (rank-deficient) activations, yielding up to 5.3% performance gains on diffusion and multimodal LLMs over static, one-to-one transform baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing transformation-based quantization methods use fixed, orthogonal, one-to-one mappings that ignore heterogeneous, dynamic activation distributions—especially pronounced in diffusion and multimodal LLMs where different token types (e.g., vision vs text, masked tokens) behave differently—leading to suboptimal quantization fidelity.

Method: Leverage the rank-deficient structure of activations to enlarge the space of valid transformation solutions beyond strict inverses, enabling decoupling of activation-side transforms from weight transforms. Learn/assign multiple activation transformation matrices conditioned on token type (vision, text, masked), while keeping a single static transform for weights.

Result: Across dLLMs and MLLMs, FreeAct consistently outperforms prior quantization approaches, achieving up to 5.3% performance improvement, supported by extensive experiments and analyses.

Conclusion: Relaxing the static one-to-one constraint and introducing token-specific activation transforms substantially improves quantization for models with heterogeneous activations. The framework is practical—weights retain a unified transform—and generalizes across diffusion and multimodal settings; code to be released.

Abstract: Quantization is pivotal for mitigating the significant memory and computational overhead of Large Language Models (LLMs). While emerging transformation-based methods have successfully enhanced quantization by projecting feature spaces onto smoother manifolds using orthogonal matrices, they typically enforce a rigid one-to-one transformation constraint. This static approach fails to account for the dynamic patterns inherent in input activations, particularly within diffusion LLMs (dLLMs) and Multimodal LLMs (MLLMs), where varying token types exhibit distinct distributions. To advance this, we propose FreeAct, a novel quantization framework that relaxes the static one-to-one constraint to accommodate dynamic activation disparities. Theoretically, we leverage the rank-deficient nature of activations to derive a solution space that extends beyond simple inverse matrices, enabling the decoupling of activation transformations from weights. Methodologically, FreeAct identifies token-specific dynamics (i.e., vision v.s. text, or masked tokens) and allocates distinct transformation matrices to the activation side, while maintaining a unified, static transformation for the weights. Extensive experiments across dLLMs and MLLMs demonstrate that FreeAct significantly outperforms baselines, up to 5.3% performance improvement, with in-depth analyses. Our code will be publicly released.

</details>


### [403] [LLM-as-an-Annotator: Training Lightweight Models with LLM-Annotated Examples for Aspect Sentiment Tuple Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01778)
*Nils Constantin Hellwig,Jakob Fehle,Udo Kruschwitz,Christian Wolff*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LA-ABSA uses LLM-generated pseudo-labels (guided by a small set of human-labeled ICL examples) to fine-tune lightweight models for ABSA, achieving near-LLM prompting performance with far lower compute and energy costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual annotation for ABSA (TASD/ASQP) is expensive and slow, while direct LLM prompting is accurate but computationally costly at inference time. The goal is to obtain competitive accuracy in low-resource settings without ongoing LLM inference costs.

Method: Provide ~50 human-labeled examples for in-context learning to an LLM, use it to annotate large unlabeled corpora for TASD/ASQP, then fine-tune compact models on these synthetic labels. Evaluate on five ABSA datasets, comparing against prior augmentation strategies and LLM prompting baselines, and report energy/compute efficiency.

Result: Across five datasets, LA-ABSA outperforms prior augmentation approaches and is competitive with LLM prompting in low-resource scenarios. Example: on SemEval Rest16 ASQP, LA-ABSA reaches F1 49.85 using 50 ICL examples, close to Gemma-3-27B prompting at 51.10, while using substantially fewer computational resources.

Conclusion: LLM-assisted labeling can effectively bootstrap small ABSA models that deliver near-LLM performance with markedly reduced compute/energy. This approach is a practical trade-off for low-resource ABSA, though future work should address pseudo-label quality control, robustness across domains, and scaling behavior.

Abstract: Training models for Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA) tasks requires manually annotated data, which is expensive and time-consuming to obtain. This paper introduces LA-ABSA, a novel approach that leverages Large Language Model (LLM)-generated annotations to fine-tune lightweight models for complex ABSA tasks. We evaluate our approach on five datasets for Target Aspect Sentiment Detection (TASD) and Aspect Sentiment Quad Prediction (ASQP). Our approach outperformed previously reported augmentation strategies and achieved competitive performance with LLM-prompting in low-resource scenarios, while providing substantial energy efficiency benefits. For example, using 50 annotated examples for in-context learning (ICL) to guide the annotation of unlabeled data, LA-ABSA achieved an F1 score of 49.85 for ASQP on the SemEval Rest16 dataset, closely matching the performance of ICL prompting with Gemma-3-27B (51.10), while requiring significantly lower computational resources.

</details>


### [404] [nchellwig at SemEval-2026 Task 3: Self-Consistent Structured Generation (SCSG) for Dimensional Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis using Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01788)
*Nils Constantin Hellwig,Jakob Fehle,Udo Kruschwitz,Christian Wolff*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose a self-consistency scheme for Dimensional Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis: run a LoRA-adapted LLM many times per input and keep only tuples agreed upon by a majority; accelerate multi-pass inference with vLLM’s PagedAttention. Across multilingual benchmarks, 15 runs beat single-pass prompting and the system ranks near the top, including first on Tatar-Restaurant.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single-pass LLM extraction for DimASTE is noisy and unstable, especially across languages and domains. The authors aim to improve reliability and accuracy of structured tuple predictions while keeping multi-pass costs manageable for a competitive shared task (SemEval-2026 Task 3, Track A).

Method: Self-Consistent Structured Generation (SCSG): (1) Adapt a base LLM with LoRA; (2) Execute the model multiple times per instance; (3) Aggregate outputs and retain only tuples with majority agreement across runs; (4) Reduce overhead using vLLM’s PagedAttention for key–value cache reuse. Evaluate with 15 executions, using Gemma 3 as the backbone.

Result: Self-consistency with 15 runs yields statistically significant gains over single-inference prompting across 6 languages and 8 language–domain settings. The system places in the top seven in all settings, achieves second place on three of four English subsets, and first on Tatar-Restaurant for DimASTE.

Conclusion: Majority-vote self-consistency combined with efficient multi-pass decoding improves robustness and accuracy for multilingual DimASTE. The approach is practical for shared-task settings and delivers competitive, often state-of-the-art, performance while controlling inference costs via cache reuse.

Abstract: We present Self-Consistent Structured Generation (SCSG) for Dimensional Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis in SemEval-2026 Task 3 (Track A). SCSG enhances prediction reliability by executing a LoRA-adapted large language model multiple times per instance, retaining only tuples that achieve a majority consensus across runs. To mitigate the computational overhead of multiple forward passes, we leverage vLLM's PagedAttention mechanism for efficient key--value cache reuse. Evaluation across 6 languages and 8 language--domain combinations demonstrates that self-consistency with 15 executions yields statistically significant improvements over single-inference prompting, with our system (leveraging Gemma 3) ranking in the top seven across all settings, achieving second place on three out of four English subsets and first place on Tatar-Restaurant for DimASTE.

</details>


### [405] [Semantic Novelty Trajectories in 80,000 Books: A Cross-Corpus Embedding Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01791)
*Fred Zimmerman*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Corpus-scale test of Schmidhuber’s compression-progress notion of interestingness shows modern English books explore semantic space with higher paragraph-level novelty and more circuitous trajectories than pre-1920 works, while novelty is independent of reader-rated quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate whether a formal, compressor-based theory of interestingness manifests in historical literary practice, and how narrative novelty dynamics have shifted from pre-1920 to modern publishing. Also test whether computational novelty aligns with perceived literary merit.

Method: Compute paragraph embeddings (sentence-transformers) and define novelty as distance to a running centroid along each book’s paragraph sequence. Compare two large corpora (PG19 vs. Books3). Quantify mean novelty, trajectory circuitousness (path length/net displacement), prevalence of convergent curves (declining novelty), and correlation with reader ratings. Cluster paragraph-level novelty trajectories using PAA-16 to recover archetypal narrative shapes and compare distributions across eras.

Result: Modern books exhibit ~10% higher mean novelty (0.503 vs 0.459). Circuitousness is ~+67% higher. Convergent curves are 2.3× more common in pre-1920 books. Novelty and reader ratings are orthogonal (r = -0.002). Eight trajectory archetypes emerge from PAA-16 clustering, with substantial era-dependent shifts in their frequencies.

Conclusion: Over two centuries, English-language prose has shifted toward more exploratory, less convergent semantic movement at the paragraph level. Schmidhuber-style novelty appears to be a structural property of texts that does not predict perceived quality. Narrative-shape archetypes have rebalanced across eras. Code and an interactive toolkit are publicly released.

Abstract: I apply Schmidhuber's compression progress theory of interestingness at corpus scale, analyzing semantic novelty trajectories in more than 80,000 books spanning two centuries of English-language publishing. Using sentence-transformer paragraph embeddings and a running-centroid novelty measure, I compare 28,730 pre-1920 Project Gutenberg books (PG19) against 52,796 modern English books (Books3, approximately 1990-2010). The principal findings are fourfold. First, mean paragraph-level novelty is roughly 10% higher in modern books (0.503 vs. 0.459). Second, trajectory circuitousness -- the ratio of cumulative path length to net displacement in embedding space -- nearly doubles in the modern corpus (+67%). Third, convergent narrative curves, in which novelty declines toward a settled semantic register, are 2.3x more common in pre-1920 literature. Fourth, novelty is orthogonal to reader quality ratings (r = -0.002), suggesting that interestingness in Schmidhuber's sense is structurally independent of perceived literary merit. Clustering paragraph-level trajectories via PAA-16 representations reveals eight distinct narrative-shape archetypes whose distribution shifts substantially between eras. All analysis code and an interactive exploration toolkit are publicly available at https://bigfivekiller.online/novelty_hub.

</details>


### [406] [ALTER: Asymmetric LoRA for Token-Entropy-Guided Unlearning of LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01792)
*Xunlei Chen,Jinyu Guo,Yuang Li,Zhaokun Wang,Yi Gong,Jie Zou,Jiwei Wei,Wenhong Tian*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ALTER introduces a lightweight, token-level unlearning method for LLMs using an asymmetric LoRA setup to isolate and forget targeted knowledge while preserving general utility and efficiency, achieving >95% forgetting with >90% retained utility on multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs accumulate broad knowledge, but safely deploying them requires removing specific undesired knowledge. Conventional unlearning is hard due to entangled parameters, fuzzy retention–forgetting boundaries, collateral damage from aggressive methods, and prohibitive compute for billion-parameter updates.

Method: Two-phase framework: (I) identify and capture high-entropy tokens, learned via a shared A matrix in LoRA to concentrate volatile knowledge; (II) apply an asymmetric LoRA architecture with parameter isolation to unlearn tokens in target subdomains while preserving foundational tokens—decoupling unlearning from full model parameters and enabling token-level isolation.

Result: State-of-the-art unlearning on TOFU, WMDP, and MUSE benchmarks: >95% forget quality with minimal side effects; retains >90% overall utility, outperforming baselines that retain 47.8–83.6%; improved efficiency by avoiding full-parameter optimization.

Conclusion: Token-level isolation with asymmetric LoRA is an effective and efficient path for LLM unlearning, mitigating knowledge entanglement and preserving utility, and offers a promising direction for safe, controllable knowledge removal in large models.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have advanced to encompass extensive knowledge across diverse domains. Yet controlling what a LLMs should not know is important for ensuring alignment and thus safe use. However, effective unlearning in LLMs is difficult due to the fuzzy boundary between knowledge retention and forgetting. This challenge is exacerbated by entangled parameter spaces from continuous multi-domain training, often resulting in collateral damage, especially under aggressive unlearning strategies. Furthermore, the computational overhead required to optimize State-of-the-Art (SOTA) models with billions of parameters poses an additional barrier. In this work, we present ALTER, a lightweight unlearning framework for LLMs to address both the challenges of knowledge entanglement and unlearning efficiency. ALTER operates through two phases: (I) high entropy tokens are captured and learned via the shared A matrix in LoRA, followed by (II) an asymmetric LoRA architecture that achieves a specified forgetting objective by parameter isolation and unlearning tokens within the target subdomains. Serving as a new research direction for achieving unlearning via token-level isolation in the asymmetric framework. ALTER achieves SOTA performance on TOFU, WMDP, and MUSE benchmarks with over 95% forget quality and shows minimal side effects through preserving foundational tokens. By decoupling unlearning from LLMs' billion-scale parameters, this framework delivers excellent efficiency while preserving over 90% of model utility, exceeding baseline preservation rates of 47.8-83.6%.

</details>


### [407] [OpenAutoNLU: Open Source AutoML Library for NLU](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01824)
*Grigory Arshinov,Aleksandr Boriskin,Sergey Senichev,Ayaz Zaripov,Daria Galimzianova,Daniil Karpov,Leonid Sanochkin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: OpenAutoNLU is an open-source AutoML library for NLU (text classification, NER) that introduces data-aware training-regime selection requiring no manual configuration, and bundles data-quality diagnostics, configurable OOD detection, and LLM integrations in a minimal low-code API (demo: https://openautonlu.dev).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automate and simplify NLU development so users can achieve strong performance without expert configuration, while addressing common pain points in real-world deployments: data quality issues, out-of-distribution inputs, and leveraging LLM capabilities within a unified, low-code workflow.

Method: Provide a library that (1) inspects data to automatically pick training strategies (data-aware training-regime selection), (2) integrates data-quality diagnostics, (3) offers configurable OOD detection, and (4) exposes LLM-powered features, all behind a minimal low-code API and demo app.

Result: Release of an open-source library and demo app that supports text classification and NER with zero/low manual configuration, plus built-in diagnostics, OOD detection, and LLM integrations. No quantitative benchmarks are reported in the abstract.

Conclusion: OpenAutoNLU aims to reduce manual effort and improve robustness for NLU tasks by coupling AutoML with data-aware decisions and practical tooling (diagnostics, OOD, LLMs). If empirically validated, it could streamline production-grade NLU pipelines for a wide range of users.

Abstract: OpenAutoNLU is an open-source automated machine learning library for natural language understanding (NLU) tasks, covering both text classification and named entity recognition (NER). Unlike existing solutions, we introduce data-aware training regime selection that requires no manual configuration from the user. The library also provides integrated data quality diagnostics, configurable out-of-distribution (OOD) detection, and large language model (LLM) features, all within a minimal lowcode API. The demo app is accessible here https://openautonlu.dev.

</details>


### [408] [Let the Agent Search: Autonomous Exploration Beats Rigid Workflows in Temporal Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01853)
*Xufei Lv,Jiahui Yang,Yifu Gao,Linbo Qiao,Houde Liu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: AT2QA is a training-free, autonomous LLM agent for temporal KG question answering that iteratively searches the graph and achieves new SOTA on MultiTQ, highlighting the power of agentic autonomy over fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Temporal KG QA requires multi-hop reasoning under time constraints. Prior LLM-based methods depend on rigid, hand-crafted retrieval pipelines or expensive supervised fine-tuning, which limit generalization and zero-shot usability. The work explores whether granting an off-the-shelf LLM autonomy can surpass these constraints.

Method: Give an off-the-shelf LLM autonomy to plan and act: the agent (AT2QA) iteratively interacts with a temporal knowledge graph through a general search tool, performing dynamic retrieval and reasoning over multiple hops without training or task-specific fine-tuning.

Result: On the MultiTQ benchmark, AT2QA reaches 88.7% Hits@1, +10.7% over prior SOTA, with a +20.1% improvement on challenging multi-target queries. The authors release code and sampled trajectories.

Conclusion: Agentic autonomy plus dynamic retrieval over temporal KGs can decisively outperform supervised fine-tuning for TKGQA in zero-shot settings, suggesting a shift from rigid pipelines to autonomous LLM agents for temporal reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Temporal Knowledge Graph Question Answering (TKGQA) demands multi-hop reasoning under temporal constraints. Prior approaches based on large language models (LLMs) typically rely on rigid, hand-crafted retrieval workflows or costly supervised fine-tuning. We show that simply granting an off-the-shelf LLM autonomy, that is, letting it decide what to do next, already yields substantial gains even in a strict zero-shot setting. Building on this insight, we propose AT2QA, an autonomous, training-free agent for temporal question answering that iteratively interacts with the temporal knowledge graph via a general search tool for dynamic retrieval. Experiments on MultiTQ demonstrate large improvements: AT2QA achieves 88.7% Hits@1 (+10.7% over prior SOTA), including a +20.1% gain on challenging multi-target queries, showing that agentic autonomy can decisively outperform fine-tuning for temporal question answering. Code and the full set of sampled trajectories are available on https://github.com/AT2QA-Official-Code/AT2QA-Official-Code

</details>


### [409] [CyclicJudge: Mitigating Judge Bias Efficiently in LLM-based Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01865)
*Ziyi Zhu,Olivier Tieleman,Alexey Bukhtiyarov,Jinghong Chen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LLM-as-judge benchmarks suffer from judge bias comparable to actual model performance gaps. The paper decomposes score variance and proposes CyclicJudge, a round-robin judge assignment that exactly cancels judge bias at the same cost as single-judge evaluation, validated on MT-Bench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-ended LLM evaluation increasingly relies on LLM (or other) judges, but systematic judge biases persist even when scaling the number of scenarios or generations, leading to unreliable rankings. A principled framework is needed to quantify where variance comes from and to remove judge-induced bias without increasing evaluation cost.

Method: Introduce a variance decomposition that partitions benchmark score variance into scenario, generation, judge, and residual components. Using this analysis, design CyclicJudge: a round-robin allocation using each judge exactly once per cycle to balance judge effects. Provide theoretical proof that this allocation is optimal for eliminating judge bias while remaining cost-equivalent to single-judge evaluations.

Result: The decomposition reveals substantial judge-driven variance, often as large as differences between models. CyclicJudge is proven to precisely eliminate judge bias under the given constraints and requires no extra per-comparison cost. Empirical results on MT-Bench confirm the theoretical predictions and improved reliability.

Conclusion: Single-judge evaluations can produce unreliable rankings due to persistent judge bias. Employing the proposed variance decomposition with CyclicJudge yields unbiased, more reliable benchmark outcomes without added cost, whereas simply adding scenarios or generations does not address judge bias.

Abstract: LLM-as-judge evaluation has become standard practice for open-ended model assessment; however, judges exhibit systematic biases that cannot be eliminated by increasing the number of scenarios or generations. These biases are often similar in magnitude to the model differences that benchmarks are designed to detect, resulting in unreliable rankings when single-judge evaluations are used. This work introduces a variance decomposition that partitions benchmark score variance into scenario, generation, judge, and residual components. Based on this analysis, CyclicJudge, a round-robin assignment of judges, is demonstrated to be the optimal allocation strategy. It eliminates bias precisely while requiring each judge only once per cycle, maintaining the cost of single-judge evaluation. Empirical validation on MT-Bench supports all theoretical predictions.

</details>


### [410] [Sovereign AI-based Public Services are Viable and Affordable](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01869)
*António Branco,Luís Gomes,Rodrigo Santos,Eduardo Santos,João Silva,Nuno Marques,Madalena Rodrigues*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper argues that governments need not depend on a small oligopoly of general-purpose AI providers; practical experiments show that sovereign, on‑premise AI public services are technically feasible and economically sustainable on modest resources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rising dependence on a concentrated set of global AI providers creates risks for availability, reliability, cultural fit, and geopolitical autonomy—especially for always-on public services. The authors seek to reduce structural dependency and enhance digital and cultural sovereignty.

Method: Practical experimentation and deployment of AI-enabled public service systems operated on premises with limited compute and budget, assessing viability and drawing technical and operational lessons. The work challenges the assumption that general-purpose commercial architectures are optimal for all contexts.

Result: They demonstrate viable, cost-effective alternatives to oligopoly cloud AI offerings. These alternatives can run effectively on modest local infrastructure while preserving digital and cultural autonomy, indicating both technical feasibility and economic sustainability.

Conclusion: Sovereign AI-based public services are achievable without relying on dominant commercial providers. Governments and agencies can deploy on-premise, resource-efficient systems that align with sovereignty principles; the paper offers technical insights and deployment guidance to support wider adoption.

Abstract: The rapid expansion of AI-based remote services has intensified debates about the long-term implications of growing structural concentration in infrastructure and expertise. As AI capabilities become increasingly intertwined with geopolitical interests, the availability and reliability of foundational AI services can no longer be taken for granted. This issue is particularly pressing for AI-enabled public services for citizens, as governments and public agencies are progressively adopting 24/7 AI-driven support systems typically operated through commercial offerings from a small oligopoly of global technology providers. This paper challenges the prevailing assumption that general-purpose architectures, offered by these providers, are the optimal choice for all application contexts. Through practical experimentation, we demonstrate that viable and cost-effective alternatives exist. Alternatives that align with principles of digital and cultural sovereignty. Our findings provide an empirical illustration that sovereign AI-based public services are both technically feasible and economically sustainable, capable of operating effectively on premises with modest computational and financial resources while maintaining cultural and digital autonomy. The technical insights and deployment lessons reported here are intended to inform the adoption of similar sovereign AI public services by national agencies and governments worldwide.

</details>


### [411] [KDFlow: A User-Friendly and Efficient Knowledge Distillation Framework for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01875)
*Songming Zhang,Xue Zhang,Tong Zhang,Bojie Hu,Yufeng Chen,Jinan Xu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: KDFlow is a decoupled LLM distillation framework that pairs FSDP2-based student training with SGLang-based teacher inference, transmits teacher hidden states (zero-copy) instead of full logits, and supports off-/on-policy and cross-tokenizer KD, yielding 1.44×–6.36× speedups over existing KD systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most KD pipelines run teacher and student with the same training backend, underutilizing hardware and causing communication/computation inefficiencies. The goal is to exploit specialized systems for inference vs. training and reduce inter-process transfer overhead without hurting KD quality.

Method: Decouple teacher and student into separate backends: SGLang for high-throughput teacher inference and FSDP2 for scalable student training. Communicate only teacher hidden states via zero-copy, then recompute logits on the student side to reduce bandwidth while preserving KD signal. Provide APIs supporting off-policy/on-policy KD and cross-tokenizer KD techniques.

Result: Across experiments, KDFlow achieves 1.44× to 6.36× end-to-end speedups compared to current KD frameworks, while maintaining KD performance, with minimal engineering overhead for users.

Conclusion: Combining inference-optimized and training-optimized stacks with efficient hidden-state transfer is an effective route to accelerate LLM KD. KDFlow offers a practical, extensible system to rapidly prototype and scale distillation with better hardware utilization.

Abstract: Knowledge distillation (KD) is an essential technique to compress large language models (LLMs) into smaller ones. However, despite the distinct roles of the student model and the teacher model in KD, most existing frameworks still use a homogeneous training backend (e.g., FSDP and DeepSpeed) for both models, leading to suboptimal training efficiency. In this paper, we present a novel framework for LLM distillation, termed \textbf{KDFlow}, which features a decoupled architecture and employs SGLang for teacher inference. By bridging the training efficiency of FSDP2 and the inference efficiency of SGLang, KDFlow achieves full utilization of both advantages in a unified system. Moreover, instead of transferring full logits across different processes, our framework only transmits the teacher's hidden states using zero-copy data transfer and recomputes the logits on the student side, effectively balancing the communication cost and KD performance. Furthermore, our framework supports both off-policy and on-policy distillation and incorporates KD algorithms for cross-tokenizer KD through highly extensible and user-friendly APIs. Experiments show that KDFlow can achieve \textbf{1.44$\times$ to 6.36$\times$} speedup compared to current KD frameworks, enabling researchers to rapidly prototype and scale LLM distillation with minimal engineering overhead. Code is available at: https://github.com/songmzhang/KDFlow

</details>


### [412] [FLANS at SemEval-2026 Task 7: RAG with Open-Sourced Smaller LLMs for Everyday Knowledge Across Diverse Languages and Cultures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01910)
*Liliia Bogdanova,Shiran Sun,Lifeng Han,Natalia Amat Lefort,Flor Miriam Plaza-del-Arco*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: System paper for SemEval-2025 Task 7: builds a multilingual QA system (SAQ and MCQ) using Retrieval-Augmented Generation with open-source small LLMs on Ollama, powered by a custom culturally aware Wikipedia-derived knowledge base and optional live DuckDuckGo search; prompts and code are shared; no explicit performance metrics in the abstract.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address culturally grounded everyday-knowledge QA across languages while emphasizing privacy and sustainability by using smaller, open-source LLMs and culture-specific knowledge sources tailored to the SemEval task.

Method: Construct custom cultural knowledge bases (CulKBs) by extracting Wikipedia content via curated keyword lists (both culturally-aware text and country-specific summaries); integrate RAG pipelines with open-source small LLMs deployed on Ollama; add a variant with live online retrieval via DuckDuckGo; develop and iteratively refine prompts; evaluate on English, Spanish, and Chinese for both SAQ and MCQ tracks.

Result: Participation in both tracks with systems tested in EN/ES/ZH; availability of prompts, code, and a reported prompt learning curve; the abstract does not provide quantitative leaderboard results or ablation outcomes.

Conclusion: A practical, privacy- and sustainability-oriented approach that combines sLLM-based RAG with tailored cultural KBs and optional live search can address multilingual culturally-aware QA; resources are released to enable reproduction and further study.

Abstract: This system paper describes our participation in the SemEval-2025 Task-7 ``Everyday Knowledge Across Diverse Languages and Cultures''. We attended two subtasks, i.e., Track 1: Short Answer Questions (SAQ), and Track 2: Multiple-Choice Questions (MCQ). The methods we used are retrieval augmented generation (RAGs) with open-sourced smaller LLMs (OS-sLLMs). To better adapt to this shared task, we created our own culturally aware knowledge base (CulKBs) by extracting Wikipedia content using keyword lists we prepared. We extracted both culturally-aware wiki-text and country-specific wiki-summary. In addition to the local CulKBs, we also have one system integrating live online search output via DuckDuckGo. Towards better privacy and sustainability, we aimed to deploy smaller LLMs (sLLMs) that are open-sourced on the Ollama platform. We share the prompts we developed using refinement techniques and report the learning curve of such prompts. The tested languages are English, Spanish, and Chinese for both tracks. Our resources and codes are shared via https://github.com/aaronlifenghan/FLANS-2026

</details>


### [413] [Demonstrating ViviDoc: Generating Interactive Documents through Human-Agent Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01912)
*Yinghao Tang,Yupeng Xie,Yingchaojie Feng,Tingfeng Lan,Wei Chen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ViviDoc is a human-in-the-loop, multi-agent system that converts a single topic into interactive educational documents using a structured intermediate spec (DocSpec). It improves controllability and verifiability over naive LLM agents and yields better quality with an intuitive editing flow.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Creating interactive articles is costly and demands both subject-matter and web development expertise. While LLM agents can automate parts of this process, their outputs are often opaque, hard to control, and difficult to verify pedagogically and technically. A method is needed to bridge educators’ intentions and executable, reliable artifacts.

Method: Introduce ViviDoc: a collaborative pipeline with three LLM agents—Planner, Executor, Evaluator—coordinated through a human-readable Document Specification (DocSpec). DocSpec decomposes each interactive visualization into State, Render, Transition, and Constraint components, enabling educators to review and refine plans before code generation, ensuring alignment with goals and constraints.

Result: Expert evaluation and a user study show ViviDoc substantially outperforms naive agentic generation approaches and offers an intuitive editing experience for educators.

Conclusion: A DocSpec-centered, multi-agent, human-in-the-loop workflow provides controllable, verifiable, and higher-quality generation of interactive educational documents, effectively linking pedagogical intent to executable implementations.

Abstract: Interactive articles help readers engage with complex ideas through exploration, yet creating them remains costly, requiring both domain expertise and web development skills. Recent LLM-based agents can automate content creation, but naively applying them yields uncontrollable and unverifiable outputs. We present ViviDoc, a human-agent collaborative system that generates interactive educational documents from a single topic input. ViviDoc introduces a multi-agent pipeline (Planner, Executor, Evaluator) and the Document Specification (DocSpec), a human-readable intermediate representation that decomposes each interactive visualization into State, Render, Transition, and Constraint components. The DocSpec enables educators to review and refine generation plans before code is produced, bridging the gap between pedagogical intent and executable output. Expert evaluation and a user study show that ViviDoc substantially outperforms naive agentic generation and provides an intuitive editing experience. Our project homepage is available at https://vividoc-homepage.vercel.app/.

</details>


### [414] [AdaPonderLM: Gated Pondering Language Models with Token-Wise Adaptive Depth](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01914)
*Shixiang Song,He Li,Zitong Wang,Boyi Zeng,Feichen Song,Yixuan Wang,Zhiqin John Xu,Ziwei He,Zhouhan Lin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: AdaPonderLM adds learned, token-wise early exit to recurrent/iterative Transformers and reuses halted tokens’ KV states, cutting ~10% inference compute while preserving perplexity and downstream accuracy; it allocates more compute to hard tokens and outperforms fixed pruning under equal FLOPs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recurrent LMs enable test-time scaling but typically run a fixed number of iterations, wasting compute on easy tokens and lacking token-level adaptivity. The aim is to learn, without supervision or manual pruning schedules, when to stop recurring per token while keeping train–test behavior consistent for real speedups.

Method: A self-supervised recurrent LM with iteration-specific MLP gates and a monotonic halting mask to decide token-wise early exit during pretraining. Halted tokens reuse cached key/value (KV) states across iterations to ensure consistency and acceleration. No hand-tuned per-layer/per-token pruning ratios are required.

Result: On Pythia backbones (70M–410M for pretraining; up to 2.8B for continued pretraining), AdaPonderLM reduces inference compute by ≈10% with comparable language-model perplexity and competitive downstream accuracy. Gates learn to spend more computation on high-NLL (hard) tokens, and under iso-FLOPs the learned halting policy consistently beats fixed pruning.

Conclusion: Learned, self-supervised token-wise halting enables adaptive computation time in recurrent LMs with practical speedups and no quality loss. The policy targets hard tokens effectively and is superior to uniform pruning, suggesting a better compute–performance trade-off at inference.

Abstract: Test-time scaling via recurrent/iterative Transformers enables large language models to spend more computation at inference, but most pretrained recurrent LMs run a fixed number of iterations, wasting compute on easy tokens and lacking token-wise adaptivity. Following the core idea of Adaptive Computation Time(ACT) and Early Exit(EE), we propose AdaPonderLM, a self-supervised recurrent language model that learns token-wise early exiting during pretraining without manually tuned per-token/per-layer pruning ratios. AdaPonderLM uses iteration-specific MLP gates with a monotonic halting mask to decide when each token stops recurring, and introduces a KV reuse mechanism that reuses cached key/value states for halted tokens, ensuring train--test consistency and practical acceleration. Across Pythia backbones from 70M to 410M (pretraining) and up to 2.8B (continued pretraining), AdaPonderLM reduces inference compute at about 10% while maintaining comparable language modeling perplexity and competitive downstream accuracy. Our analysis shows the learned gates allocate more computation to high-NLL (hard) tokens, exhibiting adaptive computation time behavior in a fully self-supervised setting. Meanwhile, under iso-FLOPs, the learned halting policy consistently outperforms fixed pruning, showing AdaPonderLM allocates compute to the right tokens rather than just reducing average depth.

</details>


### [415] [From Variance to Invariance: Qualitative Content Analysis for Narrative Graph Annotation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01930)
*Junbo Huang,Max Weinig,Ulrich Fritsche,Ricardo Usbeck*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose a QCA-informed framework to annotate news narratives about inflation as causal event graphs, release a DAG dataset and a graph-based Krippendorff’s α implementation, and show that lenient overlap metrics inflate agreement while locally constrained representations reduce annotator variability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Narratives in news shape public understanding of economic phenomena (e.g., inflation), but reliably annotating such narratives is hard due to human label variation and a lack of structured, high-quality, graph-based annotation protocols and validity checks.

Method: Introduce a narrative graph annotation framework guided by qualitative content analysis (QCA) to reduce annotation errors; annotate inflation narratives as DAGs with events as nodes and causal edges; evaluate annotation quality via a 6×3 factorial design varying narrative representations (six levels) and distance metrics (three types) to compute graph-based Krippendorff’s α and quantify human label variation.

Result: Empirically, (1) overlap/lenient distance metrics overestimate inter-annotator reliability, and (2) locally constrained representations (e.g., one-hop neighborhoods) reduce annotation variability. They open-source the annotated graphs and their graph-based Krippendorff’s α implementation.

Conclusion: For graph-based narrative annotation under human label variation, stricter distance metrics and locally scoped representations yield more trustworthy agreement estimates; the released framework, dataset, and α implementation offer practical guidance and resources for future NLP narrative analysis.

Abstract: Narratives in news discourse play a critical role in shaping public understanding of economic events, such as inflation. Annotating and evaluating these narratives in a structured manner remains a key challenge for Natural Language Processing (NLP). In this work, we introduce a narrative graph annotation framework that integrates principles from qualitative content analysis (QCA) to prioritize annotation quality by reducing annotation errors. We present a dataset of inflation narratives annotated as directed acyclic graphs (DAGs), where nodes represent events and edges encode causal relations. To evaluate annotation quality, we employed a $6\times3$ factorial experimental design to examine the effects of narrative representation (six levels) and distance metric type (three levels) on inter-annotator agreement (Krippendorrf's $α$), capturing the presence of human label variation (HLV) in narrative interpretations. Our analysis shows that (1) lenient metrics (overlap-based distance) overestimate reliability, and (2) locally-constrained representations (e.g., one-hop neighbors) reduce annotation variability. Our annotation and implementation of graph-based Krippendorrf's $α$ are open-sourced. The annotation framework and evaluation results provide practical guidance for NLP research on graph-based narrative annotation under HLV.

</details>


### [416] [When Numbers Tell Half the Story: Human-Metric Alignment in Topic Model Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01945)
*Thibault Prouteau,Francis Lareau,Nicolas Dugué,Jean-Charles Lamirel,Christophe Malaterre*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Proposes Topic Word Mixing (TWM), a human evaluation task targeting inter‑topic distinctness, shows that in a domain‑specific corpus coherence and word‑intrusion can diverge, while TWM aligns with diversity metrics; evaluates six topic models, releases data/code, and argues for hybrid human+automated evaluation frameworks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated topic quality metrics (e.g., coherence, diversity) often fail to reflect human judgments, especially in specialized domains, and existing human tasks (e.g., word intrusion) mainly test intra‑topic coherence and were validated on general‑domain corpora. There is a need for human‑grounded measures of inter‑topic distinctness for domain‑specific evaluations.

Method: Introduce Topic Word Mixing (TWM): present annotators with word sets drawn from a single topic or mixed from two topics; ask them to decide if the set is mixed or single, operationalizing inter‑topic separability. Compare six models (LDA, NMF, Top2Vec, BERTopic, CFMF, CFMF‑emb) on a philosophy‑of‑science corpus using ~4,000 human annotations; contrast TWM and word‑intrusion with automated coherence and diversity metrics. Release annotated dataset and task‑generation code.

Result: Coherence metrics and word‑intrusion scores can diverge in specialized domains. TWM captures human‑perceived inter‑topic distinctness and appears to track diversity metrics better than coherence. Model rankings differ across metrics (details not specified in the abstract).

Conclusion: Evaluation of topic models should combine automated metrics with human tasks that jointly assess intra‑topic coherence and inter‑topic separability. TWM complements word intrusion and provides a human‑grounded counterpart to diversity, which is especially important for domain‑specific corpora; shared resources enable further study.

Abstract: Topic models uncover latent thematic structures in text corpora, yet evaluating their quality remains challenging, particularly in specialized domains. Existing methods often rely on automated metrics like topic coherence and diversity, which may not fully align with human judgment. Human evaluation tasks, such as word intrusion, provide valuable insights but are costly and primarily validated on general-domain corpora. This paper introduces Topic Word Mixing (TWM), a novel human evaluation task assessing inter-topic distinctness by testing whether annotators can distinguish between word sets from single or mixed topics. TWM complements word intrusion's focus on intra-topic coherence and provides a human-grounded counterpart to diversity metrics. We evaluate six topic models - both statistical and embedding-based (LDA, NMF, Top2Vec, BERTopic, CFMF, CFMF-emb) - comparing automated metrics with human evaluation methods based on nearly 4,000 annotations from a domain-specific corpus of philosophy of science publications. Our findings reveal that word intrusion and coherence metrics do not always align, particularly in specialized domains, and that TWM captures human-perceived distinctness while appearing to align with diversity metrics. We release the annotated dataset and task generation code. This work highlights the need for evaluation frameworks bridging automated and human assessments, particularly for domain-specific corpora.

</details>


### [417] [AMemGym: Interactive Memory Benchmarking for Assistants in Long-Horizon Conversations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01966)
*Cheng Jiayang,Dongyu Ru,Lin Qiu,Yiyang Li,Xuezhi Cao,Yangqiu Song,Xunliang Cai*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: AMemGym is an interactive, on-policy environment that uses structured user-state evolution and LLM role-play to reliably evaluate and optimize memory-driven personalization in conversational agents, exposing weaknesses in current memory approaches (RAG, long-context, agentic memory) and guiding better strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon assistant-user interactions require robust memory, but existing benchmarks use static, off-policy contexts that undermine reliable, scalable evaluation and make training/optimization difficult. The field lacks an on-policy, diagnostically rich setup to measure and improve memory behavior during live interactions.

Method: AMemGym constructs structured user profiles and latent state trajectories, with state-dependent questions and evolution rules. LLM-simulated users role-play to reveal latent states while remaining consistent with the structured ground truth. The framework enables on-policy interaction, and defines comprehensive, structure-grounded metrics to assess and optimize memory mechanisms. Experiments cover common memory systems (RAG, long-context models, agentic memory).
