Result: Experiments show clear performance gaps among popular memory approaches and identify reasons behind their failures under on-policy, evolving-state conditions. The environment supports principled selection among competing methods and can be used to iteratively improve (self-evolve) memory strategies.

Conclusion: By aligning structured state evolution with free-form dialogue, AMemGym provides a scalable, cost-effective, and diagnostically rich testbed for developing and benchmarking memory in LLM assistants, improving reliability of evaluation and enabling data-driven optimization of memory strategies.

Abstract: Long-horizon interactions between users and LLM-based assistants necessitate effective memory management, yet current approaches face challenges in training and evaluation of memory. Existing memory benchmarks rely on static, off-policy data as context, limiting evaluation reliability and scalability. To address these gaps, we introduce AMemGym, an interactive environment enabling on-policy evaluation and optimization for memory-driven personalization. AMemGym employs structured data sampling to predefine user profiles, state-dependent questions, and state evolution trajectories, enabling cost-effective generation of high-quality, evaluation-aligned interactions. LLM-simulated users expose latent states through role-play while maintaining structured state consistency. Comprehensive metrics based on structured data guide both assessment and optimization of assistants. Extensive experiments reveal performance gaps in existing memory systems (e.g., RAG, long-context LLMs, and agentic memory) and corresponding reasons. AMemGym not only enables effective selection among competing approaches but also can potentially drive the self-evolution of memory management strategies. By bridging structured state evolution with free-form interactions, our framework provides a scalable, diagnostically rich environment for advancing memory capabilities in conversational agents.

</details>


### [418] [CharacterFlywheel: Scaling Iterative Improvement of Engaging and Steerable LLMs in Production](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01973)
*Yixin Nie,Lin Guan,Zhongyao Ma,Anchit Gupta,Yipin Zhou,Xiao Li,Zhengping Zhou,Raymond Zeng,Gelin Zhou,Shigan Chu,Ajay Thampi,Wancen Mu,Nathan Shuster,Ketong Wang,Lin Chen,Jason Brewer,Derek Hao Hu,Alexander McCauley,Jason Weston,Sem Park,Na Zhang,Kevin Tang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: CharacterFlywheel is an iterative, production-oriented flywheel that combines data curation, reward modeling, SFT, RL, and rigorous offline/online evaluation to steadily improve LLM engagement and steerability for social chat apps; 15 generations delivered consistent A/B-tested gains across Instagram, WhatsApp, and Messenger.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve real-world LLM performance at scale in social chat, optimizing engagement and steerability while avoiding overfitting and coping with production dynamics; provide a scientifically rigorous, repeatable path from user data to better deployed models.

Method: Start from LLaMA 3.1 and iterate through 15 generations. Curate internal/external real-user traffic data; build reward models to estimate/interpolate engagement metrics; apply supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning; use offline metrics and controlled 7-day online A/B tests; implement overfitting prevention and operational practices for large-scale deployments.

Result: Across July 2024–April 2025, 7/8 deployed models outperformed baselines. Best gains: up to +8.8% engagement breadth and +19.4% engagement depth. Steerability improved: instruction following 59.2%→84.8%, violations 26.6%→5.8%. Reliable progress observed at each optimization step.

Conclusion: An end-to-end flywheel can reliably lift engagement and steerability for LLMs in production social chat. Integrating reward modeling, SFT, RL, and rigorous evaluations mitigates overfitting and scales to millions of users, advancing practical and scientific understanding of LLM deployment.

Abstract: This report presents CharacterFlywheel, an iterative flywheel process for improving large language models (LLMs) in production social chat applications across Instagram, WhatsApp, and Messenger. Starting from LLaMA 3.1, we refined models across 15 generations using data from both internal and external real-user traffic. Through continuous deployments from July 2024 to April 2025, we conducted controlled 7-day A/B tests showing consistent engagement improvements: 7 of 8 newly deployed models demonstrated positive lift over the baseline, with the strongest performers achieving up to 8.8% improvement in engagement breadth and 19.4% in engagement depth. We also observed substantial gains in steerability, with instruction following increasing from 59.2% to 84.8% and instruction violations decreasing from 26.6% to 5.8%. We detail the CharacterFlywheel process which integrates data curation, reward modeling to estimate and interpolate the landscape of engagement metrics, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), reinforcement learning (RL), and both offline and online evaluation to ensure reliable progress at each optimization step. We also discuss our methods for overfitting prevention and navigating production dynamics at scale. These contributions advance the scientific rigor and understanding of LLMs in social applications serving millions of users.

</details>


### [419] [PonderLM-3: Adaptive Token-Wise Pondering with Differentiable Masking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02023)
*He Li,Feichen Song,Boyi Zeng,Shixiang Song,Zhiqin John Xu,Ziwei He,Zhouhan Lin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: PonderLM-3 learns, per token, when to spend extra inference compute via differentiable masking at train time and hard pruning at test time, delivering better quality–FLOP tradeoffs and similar downstream quality with fewer FLOPs than fixed-step baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Test-time scaling helps generation, but uniformly adding compute to every token is inefficient. The goal is to selectively allocate extra inference computation only where it benefits tokens, while keeping training and inference behavior consistent and learnable under self-supervision.

Method: An end-to-end differentiable, self-supervised pretraining framework atop PonderLM-2 that treats extra compute as a per-token resource. It injects a differentiable attention mask during pretraining to learn which tokens warrant additional pondering, paired with a hard pruning rule at inference to mirror the learned allocation.

Result: Compared to recursive/adaptive baselines, it achieves lower pretraining perplexity at the same inference FLOPs (a stronger Pareto frontier). On downstream tasks, it matches fixed-step PonderLM-2 performance under the same max extra steps while using fewer inference FLOPs in practice.

Conclusion: Token-wise adaptive pondering aligns training and inference and allocates extra compute where most useful, improving compute efficiency without sacrificing downstream performance.

Abstract: Test-time scaling has shown that allocating more additional computation at inference can improve generation quality, motivating a natural follow-up question: where should this computation be spent? Building on this insight, we introduce PonderLM-3, a pretraining framework for token-wise adaptive pondering that learns to selectively allocate additional computation under purely self-supervised objectives, built on top of the PonderLM-2 backbone. This makes additional inference computation an allocatable per-token resource, so tokens receive more computation only when it is beneficial, rather than paying a uniform extra cost. To make this allocation learnable while maintaining train-inference consistency, PonderLM-3 injects a differentiable attention mask during pretraining and pairs it with a matching hard pruning rule at inference. PonderLM-3 defines a stronger Pareto frontier: compared with existing recursive or adaptive baselines, it achieves lower pretraining perplexity at equal inference FLOPs. On downstream benchmarks, PonderLM-3 attains comparable performance to fixed-step PonderLM-2 under the same maximum number of additional computation steps, while using fewer inference FLOPs in practice. Overall, PonderLM-3 provides an end-to-end differentiable and train-inference consistent framework for token-wise adaptive computation, enabling additional inference compute to be allocated where it is most useful rather than paid uniformly by every token.

</details>


### [420] [MMR-Life: Piecing Together Real-life Scenes for Multimodal Multi-image Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02024)
*Jiachun Li,Shaoping Huang,Zhuoran Jin,Chenlong Zhang,Pengfei Cao,Yubo Chen,Kang Liu,Jun Zhao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: MMR-Life is a challenging, comprehensive benchmark for evaluating multimodal multi-image reasoning of MLLMs across seven reasoning types using real-life images; across 37 models, even top systems reach only ~58% accuracy, revealing large gaps and variability by reasoning type.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reasoning abilities of MLLMs in real-life, multi-image scenarios are underexplored and lack standardized, broad, domain-agnostic benchmarks. The field needs a way to systematically assess diverse reasoning skills and understand how factors like reasoning style and length affect performance.

Method: Construct a benchmark of 2,646 multiple-choice questions derived from 19,108 real-world images covering seven reasoning types (abductive, analogical, causal, deductive, inductive, spatial, temporal). Evaluate 37 advanced MLLMs and analyze performance patterns with respect to thinking length, reasoning method, and reasoning type.

Result: All evaluated models find the benchmark difficult; the best-performing model (e.g., GPT-5) achieves about 58% accuracy and exhibits significant variation across reasoning categories. Analyses reveal how reasoning length and strategy correlate with outcomes across types.

Conclusion: MMR-Life provides a robust foundation for standardized evaluation and analysis of multimodal multi-image reasoning, highlighting current limitations and guiding improvements for next-generation MLLMs.

Abstract: Recent progress in the reasoning capabilities of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) has empowered them to address more complex tasks such as scientific analysis and mathematical reasoning. Despite their promise, MLLMs' reasoning abilities across different scenarios in real life remain largely unexplored and lack standardized benchmarks for evaluation. To address this gap, we introduce MMR-Life, a comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate the diverse multimodal multi-image reasoning capabilities of MLLMs across real-life scenarios. MMR-Life consists of 2,646 multiple-choice questions based on 19,108 images primarily sourced from real-world contexts, comprehensively covering seven reasoning types: abductive, analogical, causal, deductive, inductive, spatial, and temporal. Unlike existing reasoning benchmarks, MMR-Life does not rely on domain-specific expertise but instead requires models to integrate information across multiple images and apply diverse reasoning abilities. The evaluation of 37 advanced models highlights the substantial challenge posed by MMR-Life. Even top models like GPT-5 achieve only 58% accuracy and display considerable variance in performance across reasoning types. Moreover, we analyze the reasoning paradigms of existing MLLMs, exploring how factors such as thinking length, reasoning method, and reasoning type affect their performance. In summary, MMR-Life establishes a comprehensive foundation for evaluating, analyzing, and improving the next generation of multimodal reasoning systems.

</details>


### [421] [EstLLM: Enhancing Estonian Capabilities in Multilingual LLMs via Continued Pretraining and Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02041)
*Aleksei Dorkin,Taido Purason,Emil Kalbaliyev,Hele-Andra Kuulmets,Marii Ojastu,Mark Fišel,Tanel Alumäe,Eleri Aedmaa,Krister Kruusmaa,Kairit Sirts*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They continue-pretrain Llama 3.1 8B on an Estonian-boosted but balanced data mix (with English replay, code, math, and instruction-like data), then align it via SFT, preference optimization, and chat-vector merging. This markedly improves Estonian competence across tasks while keeping English performance competitive.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs trained mostly on English perform poorly for smaller languages. The authors ask whether focused continued pretraining can substantially raise a single low-/mid-resource language’s capability without sacrificing English/general reasoning.

Method: Start from a pretrained multilingual Llama 3.1 8B. Do continued pretraining on a mixture that increases Estonian content while approximating the original distribution via English replay and inclusion of code, math, and instruction-like data. After CPT, apply supervised fine-tuning, preference optimization, and merge a chat vector to strengthen instruction-following. Evaluate on diverse Estonian benchmarks and standard English benchmarks.

Result: Compared to the base and its instruction-tuned variant, the CPT+aligned model shows consistent gains in Estonian linguistic competence, knowledge, reasoning, translation, and instruction-following. English performance remains competitive, indicating limited forgetting.

Conclusion: A carefully balanced CPT regimen plus post-training alignment can significantly enhance a target language in a multilingual LLM without materially harming English, offering a practical recipe for improving smaller-language capabilities.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are predominantly trained on English-centric data, resulting in uneven performance for smaller languages. We study whether continued pretraining (CPT) can substantially improve Estonian capabilities in a pretrained multilingual LLM while preserving its English and general reasoning performance. Using Llama 3.1 8B as the main base model, we perform CPT on a mixture that increases Estonian exposure while approximating the original training distribution through English replay and the inclusion of code, mathematics, and instruction-like data. We subsequently apply supervised fine-tuning, preference optimization, and chat vector merging to introduce robust instruction-following behavior. Evaluation on a comprehensive suite of Estonian benchmarks shows consistent gains in linguistic competence, knowledge, reasoning, translation quality, and instruction-following compared to the original base model and its instruction-tuned variant, while maintaining competitive performance on English benchmarks. These findings indicate that CPT, with an appropriately balanced data mixture, together with post-training alignment, can substantially improve single-language capabilities in pretrained multilingual LLMs.

</details>


### [422] [What Exactly do Children Receive in Language Acquisition? A Case Study on CHILDES with Automated Detection of Filler-Gap Dependencies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02082)
*Zhenghao Herbert Zhou,William Dai,Maya Viswanathan,Simon Charlow,R. Thomas McCoy,Robert Frank*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They build and validate an automated parser-based pipeline that detects core filler–gap constructions and extraction sites in child-directed English speech, apply it to 57 CHILDES corpora to quantify children’s input and production over development, and show its utility for acquisition research and language modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Debate in language acquisition: are children’s filler–gap dependencies learned from distributional input or require innate grammar? Progress is blocked by the lack of scalable, fine-grained quantification of the relevant constructions in naturalistic child-directed speech.

Method: Develop a system that combines constituency and dependency parsing to (a) identify three constructions—matrix wh-questions, embedded wh-questions, and relative clauses—and (b) label extraction sites (subject, object, adjunct). Validate on human-annotated data; then apply to 57 English CHILDES corpora to derive frequencies, extraction-site asymmetries, and developmental trajectories. Demonstrate downstream use via filtered-corpus training of language models.

Result: The system attains strong validation performance across most categories. When run on CHILDES, it yields detailed statistics about children’s filler–gap input and their production over time, including construction-specific rates and extraction-site asymmetries. The fine-grained labels support further analyses and modeling, illustrated with a language-model case study.

Conclusion: Automated, fine-grained identification of filler–gap constructions in large-scale child-directed speech is feasible and accurate enough to support empirical study. The resulting resource enables more direct tests of distributional sufficiency in acquisition and facilitates computational experiments, helping move the innateness vs. input sufficiency debate forward.

Abstract: Children's acquisition of filler-gap dependencies has been argued by some to depend on innate grammatical knowledge, while others suggest that the distributional evidence available in child-directed speech suffices. Unfortunately, the relevant input is difficult to quantify at scale with fine granularity, making this question difficult to resolve. We present a system that identifies three core filler-gap constructions in spoken English corpora -- matrix wh-questions, embedded wh-questions, and relative clauses -- and further identifies the extraction site (i.e., subject vs. object vs. adjunct). Our approach combines constituency and dependency parsing, leveraging their complementary strengths for construction classification and extraction site identification. We validate the system on human-annotated data and find that it scores well across most categories. Applying the system to 57 English CHILDES corpora, we are able to characterize children's filler-gap input and their filler-gap production trajectories over the course of development, including construction-specific frequencies and extraction-site asymmetries. The resulting fine-grained labels enable future work in both acquisition and computational studies, which we demonstrate with a case study using filtered corpus training with language models.

</details>


### [423] [Modeling Grammatical Hypothesis Testing in Young Learners: A Sequence-Based Learning Analytics Study of Morphosyntactic Reasoning in an Interactive Game](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02084)
*Thierry Geoffre,Trystan Geoffre*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Action-level analytics from a French morphosyntax game show that primary learners often anchor on verbs first, then adjust determiners; Hamming-distance trajectories expose difficulty hotspots and convergence dynamics, enabling prospects for real-time scaffolding and teacher tools.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Final-answer assessments obscure the process of grammatical reasoning. The study seeks fine-grained, in-situ evidence of how children test and revise hypotheses during sentence construction, to better support learning in diverse classrooms.

Method: Analyze 9,783 slider actions from 597 sessions by 100 students (ages 8–11). Treat each slider move as a hypothesis-testing step. Compute Hamming distance to the nearest valid grammatical solution over time and compare convergence patterns across exercises with differing solution-space sizes/difficulties.

Result: Determinants and verbs are principal difficulty loci. Action order departs from left-to-right: students often stabilize the verb first and then revise preceding elements. Tasks with fewer valid solutions yield slower, more erratic convergence. Switching of the nearest valid solution over time indicates dynamic hypothesis revision.

Conclusion: Sequence-based learning analytics reveal hidden reasoning strategies and difficulty hotspots in morphosyntactic agreement. The approach can inform real-time scaffolding and teacher dashboards and suggests designing tasks with attention to solution-space structure; it is likely transferable beyond this context.

Abstract: This study investigates grammatical reasoning in primary school learners through a sequence-based learning analytics approach, leveraging fine-grained action sequences from an interactive game targeting morphosyntactic agreement in French. Unlike traditional assessments that rely on final answers, we treat each slider movement as a hypothesis-testing action, capturing real-time cognitive strategies during sentence construction. Analyzing 597 gameplay sessions (9,783 actions) from 100 students aged 8-11 in authentic classroom settings, we introduce Hamming distance to quantify proximity to valid grammatical solutions and examine convergence patterns across exercises with varying levels of difficulty. Results reveal that determiners and verbs are key sites of difficulty, with action sequences deviating from left-to-right usual treatment. This suggests learners often fix the verb first and adjust preceding elements. Exercises with fewer solutions exhibit slower and more erratic convergence, while changes in the closest valid solution indicate dynamic hypothesis revision. Our findings demonstrate how sequence-based analytics can uncover hidden dimensions of linguistic reasoning, offering a foundation for real-time scaffolding and teacher-facing tools in linguistically diverse classrooms.

</details>


### [424] [ClinConsensus: A Consensus-Based Benchmark for Evaluating Chinese Medical LLMs across Difficulty Levels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02097)
*Xiang Zheng,Han Li,Wenjie Luo,Weiqi Zhai,Yiyuan Li,Chuanmiao Yan,Tianyi Tang,Yubo Ma,Kexin Yang,Dayiheng Liu,Hu Wei,Bing Zhao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ClinConsensus is a clinician-curated Chinese benchmark of 2,500 open-ended, longitudinal cases spanning prevention-to-follow-up across 36 specialties and 12 task types. It introduces rubric-based scoring with a Clinically Applicable Consistency Score (CACS@k) and a dual-judge evaluation (strong LLM-as-judge + distilled local judge). Evaluations show top LLMs have similar overall scores but differ substantially in reasoning, evidence use, longitudinal follow-up, and especially actionable treatment planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing medical LLM benchmarks are static, narrow, and task-isolated, failing to reflect open-ended, longitudinal, safety-critical clinical workflows. There is a need for a realistic, expert-validated benchmark and a scalable, physician-aligned evaluation protocol.

Method: Curate 2,500 open-ended clinical cases (36 specialties; 12 task types) covering the full care continuum. Use clinician-designed rubrics and propose CACS@k for reliability on complex tasks. Implement a dual-judge framework: a high-capability LLM-as-judge and a distilled, locally deployable SFT judge to scale and stabilize evaluations.

Result: A comprehensive assessment of leading LLMs reveals marked heterogeneity across task themes, care stages, and specialties. Although top models have comparable aggregate scores, they vary in reasoning quality, evidence grounding, and longitudinal management; clinically actionable treatment planning remains a key weakness.

Conclusion: ClinConsensus provides an extensible, expert-grounded benchmark and a scalable, reproducible evaluation pipeline aimed at advancing robust, clinically applicable medical LLMs suitable for real-world deployment.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied to health management, showing promise across disease prevention, clinical decision-making, and long-term care. However, existing medical benchmarks remain largely static and task-isolated, failing to capture the openness, longitudinal structure, and safety-critical complexity of real-world clinical workflows. We introduce ClinConsensus, a Chinese medical benchmark curated, validated, and quality-controlled by clinical experts. ClinConsensus comprises 2500 open-ended cases spanning the full continuum of care--from prevention and intervention to long-term follow-up--covering 36 medical specialties, 12 common clinical task types, and progressively increasing levels of complexity. To enable reliable evaluation of such complex scenarios, we adopt a rubric-based grading protocol and propose the Clinically Applicable Consistency Score (CACS@k). We further introduce a dual-judge evaluation framework, combining a high-capability LLM-as-judge with a distilled, locally deployable judge model trained via supervised fine-tuning, enabling scalable and reproducible evaluation aligned with physician judgment. Using ClinConsensus, we conduct a comprehensive assessment of several leading LLMs and reveal substantial heterogeneity across task themes, care stages, and medical specialties. While top-performing models achieve comparable overall scores, they differ markedly in reasoning, evidence use, and longitudinal follow-up capabilities, and clinically actionable treatment planning remains a key bottleneck. We release ClinConsensus as an extensible benchmark to support the development and evaluation of medical LLMs that are robust, clinically grounded, and ready for real-world deployment.

</details>


### [425] [Recursive Think-Answer Process for LLMs and VLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02099)
*Byung-Kwan Lee,Youngchae Chee,Yong Man Ro*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: R-TAP introduces a confidence-guided, recursive Think-Answer process with dual rewards that iteratively refines outputs, reducing self-reflective “Oops”-style corrections and improving accuracy and stability for both LLMs and VLMs while speeding up inference-time reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite interpretable internal reasoning, single-pass Think-Answer models (e.g., DeepSeek-R1) still produce output errors and exhibit unstable self-reflection cues (e.g., “Oops!”). There is a need for a mechanism to refine answers iteratively, calibrate certainty, and decide when to stop to improve reliability and efficiency.

Method: R-TAP enables iterative reasoning cycles steered by a confidence generator that evaluates the certainty of interim and final answers. Training/optimization uses two complementary rewards: (1) Recursively Confidence Increase Reward to encourage monotonic confidence gains across steps, and (2) Final Answer Confidence Reward to promote confident, accurate final outputs. The approach is applied to both LLMs and VLMs.

Result: Across evaluated tasks, R-TAP-enhanced models outperform conventional single-pass methods. They show fewer “Oops”-like expressions, indicating more stable chains of thought, and achieve faster inference-time reasoning alongside higher answer quality for both language-only and vision-language settings.

Conclusion: Confidence-guided recursive reasoning effectively reduces unstable self-corrections and improves accuracy and efficiency. R-TAP is a promising direction for refining the reasoning processes of future AI systems across modalities.

Abstract: Think-Answer reasoners such as DeepSeek-R1 have made notable progress by leveraging interpretable internal reasoning. However, despite the frequent presence of self-reflective cues like "Oops!", they remain vulnerable to output errors during single-pass inference. To address this limitation, we propose an efficient Recursive Think-Answer Process (R-TAP) that enables models to engage in iterative reasoning cycles and generate more accurate answers, going beyond conventional single-pass approaches. Central to this approach is a confidence generator that evaluates the certainty of model responses and guides subsequent improvements. By incorporating two complementary rewards-Recursively Confidence Increase Reward and Final Answer Confidence Reward-we show that R-TAP-enhanced models consistently outperform conventional single-pass methods for both large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs). Moreover, by analyzing the frequency of "Oops"-like expressions in model responses, we find that R-TAP-applied models exhibit significantly fewer self-reflective patterns, resulting in more stable and faster inference-time reasoning. We hope R-TAP pave the way evolving into efficient and elaborated methods to refine the reasoning processes of future AI.

</details>


### [426] [LLMs as Strategic Actors: Behavioral Alignment, Risk Calibration, and Argumentation Framing in Geopolitical Simulations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02128)
*Veronika Solopova,Viktoria Skorik,Maksym Tereshchenko,Alina Haidun,Ostap Vykhopen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Six state-of-the-art LLMs were tested in four multi-round geopolitical crisis simulations against human baselines. Models initially match human actions but diverge over time, showing distinct strategy shifts. Their justifications consistently favor cooperative, stability- and risk-mitigation framing with little adversarial reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are being proposed as autonomous or assistive agents in high-stakes strategic settings, but their behavior in structured geopolitical simulations is poorly understood. Policymakers and researchers need to know how closely LLMs align with human decision patterns, how they calibrate risk, and what international-relations framings they employ.

Method: Run four real-world crisis simulation scenarios over multiple rounds. For each round, have six popular SOTA LLMs choose from predefined actions and provide justifications. Compare to human results using: (1) action alignment with humans, (2) risk calibration via action severity, and (3) argumentative framing coded with international relations theory. Track dynamics across rounds.

Result: LLMs approximate human choices in initial rounds but diverge later, exhibiting distinct behavioral profiles and strategy updates. Across models, explanations strongly emphasize normative-cooperative reasoning—stability, coordination, and risk mitigation—with limited adversarial framing.

Conclusion: LLMs can superficially mirror human decisions at the outset but display different temporal dynamics and a cooperative bias in reasoning. This raises caution for deploying LLM agents in evolving geopolitical contexts and suggests the need to account for framing biases and behavior drift over time.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly proposed as agents in strategic decision environments, yet their behavior in structured geopolitical simulations remains under-researched. We evaluate six popular state-of-the-art LLMs alongside results from human results across four real-world crisis simulation scenarios, requiring models to select predefined actions and justify their decisions across multiple rounds. We compare models to humans in action alignment, risk calibration through chosen actions' severity, and argumentative framing grounded in international relations theory. Results show that models approximate human decision patterns in base simulation rounds but diverge over time, displaying distinct behavioural profiles and strategy updates. LLM explanations for chosen actions across all models exhibit a strong normative-cooperative framing centered on stability, coordination, and risk mitigation, with limited adversarial reasoning.

</details>


### [427] [LongRLVR: Long-Context Reinforcement Learning Requires Verifiable Context Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02146)
*Guanzheng Chen,Michael Qizhe Shieh,Lidong Bing*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Outcome-only rewards in RLVR make long-context grounding intractable; LongRLVR adds a dense, verifiable context reward that directly incentivizes correct evidence selection, yielding large gains on long-context benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard RLVR optimizes LLMs for factual final answers but relies on internal knowledge and provides sparse feedback, which fails when tasks require retrieving and reasoning over external, long-context evidence. The authors aim to provide learning signals that guide evidence selection and grounding, not just final correctness.

Method: They formally show that outcome-only rewards cause vanishing gradients for the grounding (evidence selection) process. They propose LongRLVR, which augments the sparse answer reward with a dense, verifiable context reward that checks whether the model selects correct grounding information. This auxiliary reward supplies robust gradients to train evidence selection and reasoning jointly. Implemented on Qwen and LLaMA for long-context tasks.

Result: Across multiple long-context benchmarks, LongRLVR consistently outperforms RLVR. For example, on RULER-QA with a 14B model, scores rise from 73.17 to 88.90; on LongBench v2, from 39.8 to 46.5. Improvements hold across both Qwen and LLaMA backbones.

Conclusion: Explicitly rewarding the grounding step is crucial for long-context reasoning. By providing a dense, verifiable context reward, LongRLVR overcomes optimization issues of outcome-only RLVR and unlocks stronger performance on long-context tasks; code is released for reproducibility.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has significantly advanced the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) by optimizing them against factual outcomes. However, this paradigm falters in long-context scenarios, as its reliance on internal parametric knowledge is ill-suited for tasks requiring contextual grounding--the ability to find and reason over externally provided information. We identify a key reason for this failure: a reward based solely on the final answer is too sparse to effectively guide the model for identifying relevant evidence. We formally prove that the outcome-only reward leads to significant vanishing gradients for the context grounding process, rendering learning intractable. To overcome this bottleneck, we introduce LongRLVR to augment the sparse answer reward with a dense and verifiable context reward. This auxiliary signal directly incentivizes the model for selecting the correct grounding information, providing a robust learning gradient that solves the underlying optimization challenge. We validate our method on challenging long-context benchmarks using Qwen and LLaMA models. LongRLVR consistently and significantly outperforms the standard RLVR across all models and benchmarks, e.g., boosting a 14B model's scores on RULER-QA from 73.17 to 88.90 and on LongBench v2 from 39.8 to 46.5. Our work demonstrates that explicitly rewarding the grounding process is a critical and effective strategy for unlocking the full reasoning potential of LLMs in long-context applications. Our code is available at https://github.com/real-absolute-AI/LongRLVR.

</details>


### [428] [Zero- and Few-Shot Named-Entity Recognition: Case Study and Dataset in the Crime Domain (CrimeNER)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02150)
*Miguel Lopez-Duran,Julian Fierrez,Aythami Morales,Daniel DeAlcala,Gonzalo Mancera,Javier Irigoyen,Ruben Tolosana,Oscar Delgado,Francisco Jurado,Alvaro Ortigosa*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces CrimeNER, a zero-/few-shot case study for crime-related NER, and CrimeNERdb, a >1.5k-document annotated dataset with 5 coarse and 22 fine-grained entity types; evaluates with SOTA NER models and general LLMs under zero/few-shot settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Law enforcement needs structured extraction of key information from crime documents, but there is a shortage of well-annotated, real-world crime NER data.

Method: Construct a crime-focused NER corpus (CrimeNERdb) from public terrorist attack reports and U.S. DOJ press releases; define a hierarchical entity schema (5 coarse, 22 fine-grained types); run zero- and few-shot experiments using state-of-the-art NER models and general-purpose LLMs to assess data and task quality.

Result: Delivers a publicly described dataset of 1.5k+ annotated documents and presents zero-/few-shot evaluation results across SOTA NER and general LLMs; the abstract does not report specific metrics but indicates empirical assessment of quality.

Conclusion: CrimeNER and CrimeNERdb provide foundational resources and a benchmark for crime-domain NER, facilitating research in low-annotation settings and supporting better information extraction for law enforcement.

Abstract: The extraction of critical information from crime-related documents is a crucial task for law enforcement agencies. Named-Entity Recognition (NER) can perform this task in extracting information about the crime, the criminal, or law enforcement agencies involved. However, there is a considerable lack of adequately annotated data on general real-world crime scenarios. To address this issue, we present CrimeNER, a case-study of Crime-related zero- and Few-Shot NER, and a general Crime-related Named-Entity Recognition database (CrimeNERdb) consisting of more than 1.5k annotated documents for the NER task extracted from public reports on terrorist attacks and the U.S. Department of Justice's press notes. We define 5 types of coarse crime entity and a total of 22 types of fine-grained entity. We address the quality of the case-study and the annotated data with experiments on Zero and Few-Shot settings with State-of-the-Art NER models as well as generalist and commonly used Large Language Models.

</details>


### [429] [Organizing, Orchestrating, and Benchmarking Agent Skills at Ecosystem Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02176)
*Hao Li,Chunjiang Mu,Jianhao Chen,Siyue Ren,Zhiyao Cui,Yiqun Zhang,Lei Bai,Shuyue Hu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: AgentSkillOS is a two-stage framework that organizes large agent-skill ecosystems into a capability tree and executes tasks via DAG-based skill pipelines, achieving near-oracle skill retrieval and substantially better outcomes than flat invocation on a 30-task, five-domain benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: With the rapid growth of agent skills, practitioners lack a principled way to discover, select, orchestrate, and manage skills at ecosystem scale (hundreds to hundreds of thousands). The paper aims to address fragmentation, inefficiency, and poor composition quality in current flat tool-calling approaches.

Method: Two stages: (1) Manage Skills—construct a capability tree by recursive categorization to enable efficient retrieval/discovery; (2) Solve Tasks—retrieve relevant skills and orchestrate them through DAG pipelines for multi-step execution. A benchmark of 30 artifact-rich tasks across five categories is evaluated via LLM-based pairwise comparisons, aggregated using a Bradley–Terry model. Experiments span ecosystems from 200 to 200K skills.

Result: Tree-based retrieval effectively approximates oracle skill selection, and DAG-based orchestration significantly outperforms flat, single-step invocation given the same skill set. Unified quality scores from the Bradley–Terry aggregation corroborate consistent gains across domains and ecosystem scales.

Conclusion: Structured composition and ecosystem-level management are key to unlocking the potential of large skill ecosystems. AgentSkillOS offers a scalable path for skill discovery and multi-skill execution, with released code enabling reproduction and extension.

Abstract: The rapid proliferation of Claude agent skills has raised the central question of how to effectively leverage, manage, and scale the agent skill ecosystem. In this paper, we propose AgentSkillOS, the first principled framework for skill selection, orchestration, and ecosystem-level management. AgentSkillOS comprises two stages: (i) Manage Skills, which organizes skills into a capability tree via node-level recursive categorization for efficient discovery; and (ii) Solve Tasks, which retrieves, orchestrates, and executes multiple skills through DAG-based pipelines. To evaluate the agent's ability to invoke skills, we construct a benchmark of 30 artifact-rich tasks across five categories: data computation, document creation, motion video, visual design, and web interaction. We assess the quality of task outputs using LLM-based pairwise evaluation, and the results are aggregated via a Bradley-Terry model to produce unified quality scores. Experiments across three skill ecosystem scales (200 to 200K skills) show that tree-based retrieval effectively approximates oracle skill selection, and that DAG-based orchestration substantially outperforms native flat invocation even when given the identical skill set. Our findings confirm that structured composition is the key to unlocking skill potential. Our GitHub repository is available at:https://github.com/ynulihao/AgentSkillOS.

</details>


### [430] [Reasoning Core: A Scalable Procedural Data Generation Suite for Symbolic Pre-training and Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.02208)
*Valentin Lacombe,Valentin Quesnel,Damien Sileo*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Reasoning Core is a scalable, solver-verified generator of symbolic reasoning data across five formal domains (planning, first-order logic, CFG parsing/generation, causal Bayesian networks, and systems of equations) with controllable difficulty, optional reasoning traces, and RL reward functions; mixing this data into pre-training improves downstream reasoning without degrading language modeling, and the tasks remain challenging even for frontier models like GPT-5.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs trained on standard corpora struggle with robust symbolic reasoning. Existing procedural datasets are narrow, template-driven, and lack verification and scalability. The field needs broad, rigorously verifiable, curriculum-friendly data (and rewards) to extend the reasoning capabilities of language models during pre-training and RL.

Method: Build a suite of procedural generators spanning five core formal domains and pair each with external solvers for correctness verification and continuous difficulty control. Optionally output solver-derived reasoning traces for supervised training and expose the same interface as verifiable reward functions for reinforcement learning. Pre-train by mixing this data with standard corpora and evaluate downstream reasoning and zero-shot performance.

Result: Adding Reasoning Core data to pre-training improves downstream reasoning while preserving or slightly improving general language modeling quality. Zero-shot evaluations indicate the suite’s tasks are still difficult for strong models (e.g., GPT-5). Code and data are MIT-licensed.

Conclusion: Scalable, solver-backed synthetic data can expand LLM reasoning without sacrificing general language performance. Reasoning Core provides a practical, open-source foundation for curriculum design and RL training with verifiable signals across multiple symbolic domains.

Abstract: Training on verifiable symbolic data is a promising way to expand the reasoning frontier of language models beyond what standard pre-training corpora provide. Yet existing procedural generators often rely on fixed puzzles or templates and do not deliver the distributional breadth needed at scale. We introduce Reasoning Core, a scalable suite that procedurally generates verifiable symbolic reasoning data across core formal domains: PDDL planning over randomized domains, first-order logic with equality, context-free grammar parsing and generation, causal reasoning over random Bayesian networks, and systems of equations. Each task is paired with an external solver for rigorous verification and admits continuous difficulty control for curriculum design. Examples can optionally include solver-derived reasoning traces, enabling supervised training from the earliest pre-training stages, and the same interface provides verifiable reward functions for reinforcement learning. Our experiments show that mixing Reasoning Core data into pre-training improves downstream reasoning while preserving, or slightly improving, language modeling quality. Zero-shot evaluations confirm these tasks challenge frontier models such as GPT-5. The code and data are publicly available under the MIT license.

</details>


### [431] [MemeIntel: Explainable Detection of Propagandistic and Hateful Memes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.16612)
*Mohamed Bayan Kmainasi,Abul Hasnat,Md Arid Hasan,Ali Ezzat Shahroor,Firoj Alam*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They introduce MemeXplain, a large-scale, explanation-augmented multimodal meme dataset (Arabic propaganda, English hateful memes) and a multi-stage training strategy for VLMs that jointly improves label prediction and rationale generation, achieving modest but consistent SOTA gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Moderating multimodal misinformation/propaganda/hate is hard, especially when models must both classify and explain; joint training typically degrades classification and there is a lack of large-scale, explanation-rich datasets—particularly for Arabic propagandistic memes.

Method: Build MemeXplain with labels and explanation rationales for Arabic propagandistic and English hateful memes. Train Vision-Language Models using a multi-stage optimization pipeline (stage-wise/decoupled objectives) to better balance classification and explanation generation.

Result: Their multi-stage VLMs significantly improve both tasks over base models and surpass prior SOTA, with ~+1.4% accuracy on ArMeme and ~+2.2% accuracy on Hateful Memes, along with higher-quality explanations (qualitatively/quantitatively, per abstract).

Conclusion: Coupling an explanation-augmented dataset with staged optimization yields better joint classification and rationale generation for multimodal memes; the released data and code aim to facilitate reproducibility and further research.

Abstract: The proliferation of multimodal content on social media presents significant challenges in understanding and moderating complex, context-dependent issues such as misinformation, hate speech, and propaganda. While efforts have been made to develop resources and propose new methods for automatic detection, limited attention has been given to jointly modeling label detection and the generation of explanation-based rationales, which often leads to degraded classification performance when trained simultaneously. To address this challenge, we introduce MemeXplain, an explanation-enhanced dataset for propagandistic memes in Arabic and hateful memes in English, making it the first large-scale resource for these tasks. To solve these tasks, we propose a multi-stage optimization approach and train Vision-Language Models (VLMs). Our results show that this strategy significantly improves both label detection and explanation generation quality over the base model, outperforming the current state-of-the-art with an absolute improvement of ~1.4% (Acc) on ArMeme and ~2.2% (Acc) on Hateful Memes. For reproducibility and future research, we aim to make the MemeXplain dataset and scripts publicly available (https://github.com/MohamedBayan/MemeIntel).

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [432] [Multi-Sourced, Multi-Agent Evidence Retrieval for Fact-Checking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00267)
*Shuzhi Gong,Richard O. Sinnott,Jianzhong Qi,Cecile Paris,Preslav Nakov,Zhuohan Xie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: WKGFC is a fact-checking framework that replaces text-similarity RAG with an agentic LLM that retrieves and reasons over knowledge-graph subgraphs (augmented by web content), modeling retrieval/verification as an MDP and using prompt optimization to better capture multi-hop, subtle factual relations for improved veracity judgments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Text/RAG systems overfit to training distributions and rely on shallow semantic similarity, missing multi-hop links and subtle factual correlations, which harms evidence retrieval and veracity prediction in misinformation settings.

Method: Use an authorized open knowledge graph as primary evidence; have an LLM assess a claim and retrieve the most relevant KG subgraphs as structured evidence; augment with web content when KG is incomplete; orchestrate the process as an automatic MDP where a reasoning LLM agent selects actions (e.g., retrieve, expand, verify) based on current evidence and the claim; adapt the agent via prompt optimization to tailor policies for fact-checking.

Result: The abstract does not report quantitative results; it asserts that the approach addresses multi-hop retrieval limitations and reduces missed factual correlations, aiming for more accurate fact verification.

Conclusion: Positioned to generalize better than text-similarity RAG by grounding verification in structured KG evidence and agentic planning, with web augmentation; claims improved retrieval quality and veracity prediction, pending empirical validation and analysis of scalability and KG coverage.

Abstract: Misinformation spreading over the Internet poses a significant threat to both societies and individuals, necessitating robust and scalable fact-checking that relies on retrieving accurate and trustworthy evidence. Previous methods rely on semantic and social-contextual patterns learned from training data, which limits their generalization to new data distributions. Recently, Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) based methods have been proposed to utilize the reasoning capability of LLMs with retrieved grounding evidence documents. However, these methods largely rely on textual similarity for evidence retrieval and struggle to retrieve evidence that captures multi-hop semantic relations within rich document contents. These limitations lead to overlooking subtle factual correlations between the evidence and the claims to be fact-checked during evidence retrieval, thus causing inaccurate veracity predictions.
  To address these issues, we propose WKGFC, which exploits authorized open knowledge graph as a core resource of evidence. LLM-enabled retrieval is designed to assess the claims and retrieve the most relevant knowledge subgraphs, forming structured evidence for fact verification. To augment the knowledge graph evidence, we retrieve web contents for completion. The above process is implemented as an automatic Markov Decision Process (MDP): A reasoning LLM agent decides what actions to take according to the current evidence and the claims. To adapt the MDP for fact-checking, we use prompt optimization to fine-tune the agentic LLM.

</details>


### [433] [TraderBench: How Robust Are AI Agents in Adversarial Capital Markets?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00285)
*Xiaochuang Yuan,Hui Xu,Silvia Xu,Cui Zou,Jing Xiong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TraderBench is a performance-grounded benchmark for AI trading agents that combines expert-verified static tasks with adversarial, refreshable trading simulations scored purely by realized risk/return metrics—removing LLM-judge variance. Across 13 models, many exhibit fixed, non-adaptive behavior; chain-of-thought improves retrieval but not trading.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current finance-agent evaluations rely on static, annotation-heavy datasets that miss dynamic decision-making, or on LLM judges that add variance and bias in domain-specific tasks. The field needs an evaluation that (a) directly measures market performance and risk, (b) minimizes subjective judging, and (c) resists contamination via refreshable data.

Method: Design TraderBench with two components: (1) expert-verified static tasks (knowledge retrieval, analytical reasoning); (2) adversarial trading simulations scored by Sharpe ratio, returns, and drawdown—no LLM judges. Two tracks: crypto trading with four progressive market-manipulation transforms; options derivatives with scoring over P&L accuracy, Greeks, and risk management. Benchmarks are refreshable with new market data. Evaluate 13 models (8B open-source to frontier) on ~50 tasks; analyze impact of extended thinking on both static tasks and trading.

Result: Eight of 13 models cluster around a crypto score of ~33 with <1-point variation across adversarial conditions, indicating fixed, non-adaptive strategies. Extended thinking substantially improves retrieval (+26 points) but yields essentially no improvement in trading performance (+0.3 crypto, −0.1 options).

Conclusion: Current LLM-based agents lack genuine market adaptation; improvements in deliberative reasoning do not translate into better trading control. Performance-grounded, refreshable evaluations are necessary to diagnose and drive progress toward adaptive, risk-aware trading agents.

Abstract: Evaluating AI agents in finance faces two key challenges: static benchmarks require costly expert annotation yet miss the dynamic decision-making central to real-world trading, while LLM-based judges introduce uncontrolled variance on domain-specific tasks. We introduce TraderBench, a benchmark that addresses both issues. It combines expert-verified static tasks (knowledge retrieval, analytical reasoning) with adversarial trading simulations scored purely on realized performance-Sharpe ratio, returns, and drawdown-eliminating judge variance entirely. The framework features two novel tracks: crypto trading with four progressive market-manipulation transforms, and options derivatives scoring across P&L accuracy, Greeks, and risk management. Trading scenarios can be refreshed with new market data to prevent benchmark contamination. Evaluating 13 models (8B open-source to frontier) on ~50 tasks, we find: (1) 8 of 13 models score ~33 on crypto with <1-point variation across adversarial conditions, exposing fixed non-adaptive strategies; (2) extended thinking helps retrieval (+26 points) but has zero impact on trading (+0.3 crypto, -0.1 options). These findings reveal that current agents lack genuine market adaptation, underscoring the need for performance-grounded evaluation in finance.

</details>


### [434] [DIG to Heal: Scaling General-purpose Agent Collaboration via Explainable Dynamic Decision Paths](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00309)
*Hanqing Yang,Hyungwoo Lee,Yuhang Yao,Zhiwei Liu,Kay Liu,Jingdi Chen,Carlee Joe-Wong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces Dynamic Interaction Graph (DIG), a time-evolving causal graph to make emergent collaboration among unrestricted LLM agents observable, explainable, and correctable in real time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agentic multi-LLM systems without predefined roles can suffer from redundant work, cascading failures, and poor interpretability; there is a need to understand and steer emergent collaboration without constraining it.

Method: Study general-purpose LLM agents operating without roles, control flow, or communication constraints; propose DIG to represent agent activations and interactions as a temporal causal network; use DIG to trace collaboration paths and surface, explain, and correct error patterns in real time.

Result: DIG renders emergent collaboration observable/explainable and enables real-time identification, explanation, and correction of collaboration-induced errors directly from agents’ interaction paths; demonstrates how agents solve problems together in unconstrained settings.

Conclusion: DIG fills a key gap in understanding and managing truly agentic multi-agent systems by providing a causal, time-aware lens that improves transparency and facilitates intervention without imposing predefined structures.

Abstract: The increasingly popular agentic AI paradigm promises to harness the power of multiple, general-purpose large language model (LLM) agents to collaboratively complete complex tasks. While many agentic AI systems utilize predefined workflows or agent roles in order to reduce complexity, ideally these agents would be truly autonomous, able to achieve emergent collaboration even as the number of collaborating agents increases. Yet in practice, such unstructured interactions can lead to redundant work and cascading failures that are difficult to interpret or correct. In this work, we study multi-agent systems composed of general-purpose LLM agents that operate without predefined roles, control flow, or communication constraints, relying instead on emergent collaboration to solve problems. We introduce the Dynamic Interaction Graph (DIG), which captures emergent collaboration as a time-evolving causal network of agent activations and interactions. DIG makes emergent collaboration observable and explainable for the first time, enabling real-time identification, explanation, and correction of collaboration-induced error patterns directly from agents' collaboration paths. Thus, DIG fills a critical gap in understanding how general LLM agents solve problems together in truly agentic multi-agent systems. The project webpage can be found at: https://happyeureka.github.io/dig.

</details>


### [435] [How Well Do Multimodal Models Reason on ECG Signals?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00312)
*Maxwell A. Xu,Harish Haresumadram,Catherine W. Liu,Patrick Langer,Jathurshan Pradeepkumar,Wanting Mao,Sunita J. Ferns,Aradhana Verma,Jimeng Sun,Paul Schmiedmayer,Xin Liu,Daniel McDuff,Emily B. Fox,James M. Rehg*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a scalable, reproducible framework to evaluate multimodal LLM “reasoning” on ECGs by separating and verifying two parts: (1) Perception—whether the model correctly identifies temporal patterns in the raw signal via agent-generated code that checks the trace; and (2) Deduction—whether the clinical logic applied to those perceived patterns aligns with a structured database of established criteria using retrieval. This dual check targets the semantic validity of reasoning beyond proxy QA metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interpretable reasoning traces from health AI/MLLMs are promising but unverified; current checks are either manual and unscalable or rely on proxies (e.g., QA accuracy) that miss semantic correctness of clinical logic. There is a need for an automated, clinically grounded way to verify both signal understanding and logical application, with ECG as a testbed.

Method: Decompose reasoning into two components: Perception (pattern identification in ECG) and Deduction (application of domain knowledge). For Perception, use an agentic system that autowrites code to test temporal structures described in the reasoning trace against the ECG signal. For Deduction, retrieve relevant items from a structured knowledge base of ECG clinical criteria and assess alignment between the model’s stated logic and those criteria. The framework is designed to be reproducible and scalable.

Result: Claims a dual-verification pipeline that enables scalable assessment of true reasoning capabilities in ECG analysis. The abstract does not report quantitative metrics or empirical outcomes; it emphasizes feasibility and methodological contribution.

Conclusion: Separating and independently verifying perception and deduction offers a principled, scalable alternative to manual review and proxy QA for evaluating clinical reasoning traces in ECG tasks. If implemented with high-quality criteria and robust code-verification, it can better assess semantic correctness and potentially generalize to other biosignals.

Abstract: While multimodal large language models offer a promising solution to the "black box" nature of health AI by generating interpretable reasoning traces, verifying the validity of these traces remains a critical challenge. Existing evaluation methods are either unscalable, relying on manual clinician review, or superficial, utilizing proxy metrics (e.g. QA) that fail to capture the semantic correctness of clinical logic. In this work, we introduce a reproducible framework for evaluating reasoning in ECG signals. We propose decomposing reasoning into two distinct, components: (i) Perception, the accurate identification of patterns within the raw signal, and (ii) Deduction, the logical application of domain knowledge to those patterns. To evaluate Perception, we employ an agentic framework that generates code to empirically verify the temporal structures described in the reasoning trace. To evaluate Deduction, we measure the alignment of the model's logic against a structured database of established clinical criteria in a retrieval-based approach. This dual-verification method enables the scalable assessment of "true" reasoning capabilities.

</details>


### [436] [EmCoop: A Framework and Benchmark for Embodied Cooperation Among LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00349)
*Hanqing Yang,Shiyu Chen,Narjes Nourzad,Marie Siew,Jingdi Chen,Carlee Joe-Wong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EmCoop is a benchmark framework to study, quantify, and diagnose cooperation dynamics in LLM-driven embodied multi-agent systems via a layered design, process-level metrics, and scalable environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many real-world tasks exceed a single agent’s abilities and require coordinated multi-agent behavior under embodied constraints. While LLMs can provide high-level reasoning, planning, and communication, existing benchmarks lack fine-grained, temporal analyses of how collaboration emerges, unfolds, and affects task outcomes, making it hard to diagnose success and failure modes.

Method: Introduce EmCoop, which cleanly separates a high-level cognitive layer (LLM-based reasoning/planning/communication) from a low-level embodied interaction layer. Characterize cooperation through interleaved dynamics over time and propose generalizable, process-level metrics that go beyond final task success. Instantiate the framework in two embodied environments that scale to arbitrary team sizes and support diverse communication topologies.

Result: Across the two instantiated environments, EmCoop enables systematic analysis of cooperation dynamics across team sizes and task settings. The proposed process-level metrics reveal collaboration quality and failure modes that final success metrics miss, and the environments demonstrate scalability to many agents and varied communication structures.

Conclusion: EmCoop provides a principled, scalable benchmark to evaluate and improve cooperation in LLM-based embodied multi-agent systems. By separating cognition from embodiment and focusing on process-level diagnostics, it supports deeper understanding, comparison, and debugging of multi-agent collaboration. Project page: https://happyeureka.github.io/emcoop.

Abstract: Real-world scenarios increasingly require multiple embodied agents to collaborate in dynamic environments under embodied constraints, as many tasks exceed the capabilities of any single agent. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) enable high-level cognitive coordination through reasoning, planning, and natural language communication. However, fine-grained analyses of how such collaboration emerges, unfolds, and contributes to task success in embodied multi-agent systems are difficult to conduct with existing benchmarks. In this paper, we introduce EmCoop, a benchmark framework for studying cooperation in LLM-based embodied multi-agent systems. Our framework separates a high-level cognitive layer from a low-level embodied interaction layer, allowing us to characterize agent cooperation through their interleaved dynamics over time. Given a cooperation-constrained embodied task, we propose generalizable, process-level metrics that diagnose collaboration quality and failure modes, beyond final task success. We instantiate our framework in two embodied environments that scale to arbitrary numbers of agents and support diverse communication topologies, and use these instantiations to demonstrate how EmCoop enables systematic analysis of cooperation dynamics across team sizes and task settings. The project web page can be found at: https://happyeureka.github.io/emcoop.

</details>


### [437] [Monotropic Artificial Intelligence: Toward a Cognitive Taxonomy of Domain-Specialized Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00350)
*Antonio de Sousa Leitão Filho,Allan Kardec Duailibe Barros Filho,Fabrício Saul Lima,Selby Mykael Lima dos Santos,Rejani Bandeira Vieira Sousa*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes “Monotropic AI”: deliberately ultra-specialized language models that trade generality for extreme accuracy in narrow domains; validated with a 37.5M-parameter model excelling at Timoshenko beam analysis while failing elsewhere, arguing for a complementary ecosystem of specialists and generalists.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling general-purpose models emphasizes breadth over depth, which can be misaligned with safety-critical needs that demand reliability and precision. The authors challenge the implicit norm that more general, larger models are inherently better and question AGI as the singular goal, highlighting an epistemological trade-off between breadth and depth.

Method: The paper formalizes defining properties of monotropic models and contrasts them with conventional (polytropic) architectures. It implements a proof-of-concept, Mini-Enedina (37.5M parameters), trained/tuned narrowly for Timoshenko beam analysis, and designed to be intentionally incompetent outside its scope. Performance is evaluated within and outside the target domain.

Result: Mini-Enedina achieves near-perfect performance on Timoshenko beam analysis while remaining deliberately poor outside that domain, demonstrating the feasibility and controllability of specialization. This empirically supports the proposed trade-off and framework.

Conclusion: Highly specialized, monotropic models can offer superior precision and safety characteristics for narrowly defined tasks. The authors advocate for a cognitive ecology where specialized and generalist systems coexist, rather than pursuing AGI as the sole or paramount objective.

Abstract: The prevailing paradigm in artificial intelligence research equates progress with scale: larger models trained on broader datasets are presumed to yield superior capabilities. This assumption, while empirically productive for general-purpose applications, obscures a fundamental epistemological tension between breadth and depth of knowledge. We introduce the concept of \emph{Monotropic Artificial Intelligence} -- language models that deliberately sacrifice generality to achieve extraordinary precision within narrowly circumscribed domains. Drawing on the cognitive theory of monotropism developed to understand autistic cognition, we argue that intense specialization represents not a limitation but an alternative cognitive architecture with distinct advantages for safety-critical applications. We formalize the defining characteristics of monotropic models, contrast them with conventional polytropic architectures, and demonstrate their viability through Mini-Enedina, a 37.5-million-parameter model that achieves near-perfect performance on Timoshenko beam analysis while remaining deliberately incompetent outside its domain. Our framework challenges the implicit assumption that artificial general intelligence constitutes the sole legitimate aspiration of AI research, proposing instead a cognitive ecology in which specialized and generalist systems coexist complementarily.

</details>


### [438] [Conservative Equilibrium Discovery in Offline Game-Theoretic Multiagent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00374)
*Austin A. Nguyen,Michael P. Wellman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They introduce COffeE-PSRO, an offline variant of PSRO for mixed-motive multiagent games that models uncertainty from limited datasets and biases learning toward strategies more likely to have low regret, yielding lower-regret solutions than prior offline methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In offline multiagent settings, data cover only a small portion of game dynamics, making it impractical to verify equilibria or safely improve policies. There is a need to choose among candidate equilibria using limited evidence while avoiding overestimation and distribution shift—limitations of standard online PSRO and naive offline adaptations.

Method: Extend PSRO to the offline regime by: (1) quantifying uncertainty over game dynamics/payoffs from fixed datasets; (2) modifying the RL objective with conservative (offline RL–inspired) principles to favor strategies that are more likely to have low regret under uncertainty; and (3) introducing a meta-strategy solver tailored for offline data to guide exploration within PSRO. The overall framework, COffeE-PSRO, performs strategy generation and selection with uncertainty-aware, conservative updates.

Result: Empirically, COffeE-PSRO finds strategies with lower regret than state-of-the-art offline baselines. Experiments also expose how different components and the fidelity of the empirical game influence performance.

Conclusion: Conservative, uncertainty-aware extensions of PSRO enable effective offline game solving in mixed-motive settings. COffeE-PSRO offers a practical way to select robust, low-regret strategies from fixed datasets and outperforms existing offline approaches; results suggest careful meta-strategy design and uncertainty modeling are key drivers of performance.

Abstract: Offline learning of strategies takes data efficiency to its extreme by restricting algorithms to a fixed dataset of state-action trajectories. We consider the problem in a mixed-motive multiagent setting, where the goal is to solve a game under the offline learning constraint. We first frame this problem in terms of selecting among candidate equilibria. Since datasets may inform only a small fraction of game dynamics, it is generally infeasible in offline game-solving to even verify a proposed solution is a true equilibrium. Therefore, we consider the relative probability of low regret (i.e., closeness to equilibrium) across candidates based on the information available. Specifically, we extend Policy Space Response Oracles (PSRO), an online game-solving approach, by quantifying game dynamics uncertainty and modifying the RL objective to skew towards solutions more likely to have low regret in the true game. We further propose a novel meta-strategy solver, tailored for the offline setting, to guide strategy exploration in PSRO. Our incorporation of Conservatism principles from Offline reinforcement learning approaches for strategy Exploration gives our approach its name: COffeE-PSRO. Experiments demonstrate COffeE-PSRO's ability to extract lower-regret solutions than state-of-the-art offline approaches and reveal relationships between algorithmic components empirical game fidelity, and overall performance.

</details>


### [439] [NeuroHex: Highly-Efficient Hex Coordinate System for Creating World Models to Enable Adaptive AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00376)
*Quinn Jacobson,Joe Luo,Jingfei Xu,Shanmuga Venkatachalam,Kevin Wang,Dingchao Rong,John Paul Shen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NeuroHex is a brain-inspired hexagonal coordinate system with 60° symmetry that enables very cheap translation/rotation/distance ops, fast point-in-shape tests, and spatial matching. Via an OSM-to-NeuroHex pipeline, it compresses OpenStreetMap geometry by 90–99% while preserving navigational structure, yielding an efficient substrate for adaptive, online-learning world models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional (Cartesian) spatial representations make rotation, matching, and point-in-shape computations costly, impeding real-time, online-adaptive AI. Biological grid cells suggest hexagonal tilings are efficient for spatial coding. Practical deployment also needs to tame the complexity of real-world map data (e.g., OSM) without losing navigationally relevant structure.

Method: Introduce a cubic isometric hex coordinate system with full 60° rotational symmetry; develop ring indexing, quantized angular encoding, and a hierarchical library of geometric shape primitives (foundational→simple→complex). These enable low-overhead point-in-shape tests and spatial matching. Provide an OSM2Hex conversion pipeline to transform OSM data into NeuroHex abstractions.

Result: On city/neighborhood-scale OSM datasets, OSM2Hex reduces geometric complexity by 90–99% while retaining relevant spatial structure for navigation. Initial experiments indicate efficient construction of dynamic world models using the NeuroHex substrate.

Conclusion: NeuroHex offers a computationally efficient, hex-based spatial reference frame that supports adaptive spatial reasoning and continuous online learning in autonomous AI. Its hex coordinates and abstraction pipeline substantially simplify real-world map data while preserving navigational utility.

Abstract: NeuroHex is a hexagonal coordinate system designed to support highly efficient world models and reference frames for online adaptive AI systems. Inspired by the hexadirectional firing structure of grid cells in the human brain, NeuroHex adopts a cubic isometric hexagonal coordinate formulation that provides full 60° rotational symmetry and low-cost translation, rotation and distance computation. We develop a mathematical framework that incorporates ring indexing, quantized angular encoding, and a hierarchical library of foundational, simple, and complex geometric shape primitives. These constructs allow low-overhead point-in-shape tests and spatial matching operations that are expensive in Cartesian coordinate systems. To support realistic settings, the NeuroHex framework can process OpenStreetMap (OSM) data sets using an OSM-to-NeuroHex (OSM2Hex) conversion tool. The OSM2Hex spatial abstraction processing pipeline can achieve a reduction of 90-99% in geometric complexity while maintaining the relevant spatial structure map for navigation. Our initial results, based on actual city and neighborhood scale data sets, demonstrate that NeuroHex offers a highly efficient substrate for building dynamic world models to enable adaptive spatial reasoning in autonomous AI systems with continuous online learning capability.

</details>


### [440] [Confusion-Aware Rubric Optimization for LLM-based Automated Grading](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00451)
*Yucheng Chu,Hang Li,Kaiqi Yang,Yasemin Copur-Gencturk,Joseph Krajcik,Namsoo Shin,Jiliang Tang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CARO is a confusion-aware framework that optimizes grading rubrics for LLM-based graders by decomposing errors via a confusion matrix and applying targeted, non-conflicting fixes, yielding higher accuracy and efficiency than prior prompt-optimization methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual rubric/prompt crafting for LLM graders is brittle and often misinterpreted; existing automated prompt optimization aggregates heterogeneous errors into a single update, causing rule dilution and conflicting constraints. A method is needed to preserve specificity, avoid conflicts, and improve computational efficiency.

Method: Use the confusion matrix to partition errors into distinct modes (misclassification patterns), diagnose each mode separately, and synthesize targeted "fixing patches" per mode. Apply a diversity-aware selection mechanism to choose complementary patches and avoid guidance conflict, thus eliminating nested refinement loops.

Result: On teacher education and STEM grading datasets, CARO significantly outperforms state-of-the-art prompt/rubric optimization baselines in accuracy while being more computationally efficient.

Conclusion: Replacing mixed-error aggregation with mode-specific, confusion-aware repairs leads to more precise, scalable, and robust automated assessment. CARO demonstrates that structurally separating error signals and applying targeted fixes improves LLM grading performance and efficiency.

Abstract: Accurate and unambiguous guidelines are critical for large language model (LLM) based graders, yet manually crafting these prompts is often sub-optimal as LLMs can misinterpret expert guidelines or lack necessary domain specificity. Consequently, the field has moved toward automated prompt optimization to refine grading guidelines without the burden of manual trial and error. However, existing frameworks typically aggregate independent and unstructured error samples into a single update step, resulting in "rule dilution" where conflicting constraints weaken the model's grading logic. To address these limitations, we introduce Confusion-Aware Rubric Optimization (CARO), a novel framework that enhances accuracy and computational efficiency by structurally separating error signals. CARO leverages the confusion matrix to decompose monolithic error signals into distinct modes, allowing for the diagnosis and repair of specific misclassification patterns individually. By synthesizing targeted "fixing patches" for dominant error modes and employing a diversity-aware selection mechanism, the framework prevents guidance conflict and eliminates the need for resource-heavy nested refinement loops. Empirical evaluations on teacher education and STEM datasets demonstrate that CARO significantly outperforms existing SOTA methods. These results suggest that replacing mixed-error aggregation with surgical, mode-specific repair yields robust improvements in automated assessment scalability and precision.

</details>


### [441] [MED-COPILOT: A Medical Assistant Powered by GraphRAG and Similar Patient Case Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00460)
*Shuheng Chen,Namratha Patil,Haonan Pan,Angel Hsing-Chi Hwang,Yao Du,Ruishan Liu,Jieyu Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MED-COPILOT is an interpretable clinical decision-support system that grounds LLM reasoning in guideline-derived GraphRAG and similar-patient retrieval, outperforming standard LLM and RAG baselines on note completion and medical QA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clinicians must integrate heterogeneous evidence (guidelines, patient histories, and analog cases), but LLMs hallucinate and struggle with long, structured medical content; a transparent, evidence-aware approach is needed.

Method: Construct a knowledge graph from WHO/NICE guidelines with community-level summarization; perform guideline-grounded GraphRAG retrieval; pair with hybrid semantic+keyword retrieval over a 36,000-case similar-patient database built from SOAP-normalized MIMIC-IV notes and Synthea records; provide an interactive UI to inspect retrieved evidence and token-level similarity and enable guided follow-up analysis.

Result: Across clinical note completion and medical question answering, the system consistently beats parametric LLM baselines and standard RAG, yielding higher generation fidelity and better clinical reasoning accuracy; a public demo is available.

Conclusion: Combining structured guideline knowledge with patient-level analogical evidence in an interpretable retrieval framework offers a practical path to safer, more transparent clinical LLM decision support.

Abstract: Clinical decision-making requires synthesizing heterogeneous evidence, including patient histories, clinical guidelines, and trajectories of comparable cases. While large language models (LLMs) offer strong reasoning capabilities, they remain prone to hallucinations and struggle to integrate long, structured medical documents.
