We present MED-COPILOT, an interactive clinical decision-support system designed for clinicians and medical trainees, which combines guideline-grounded GraphRAG retrieval with hybrid semantic-keyword similar-patient retrieval to support transparent and evidence-aware clinical reasoning. The system builds a structured knowledge graph from WHO and NICE guidelines, applies community-level summarization for efficient retrieval, and maintains a 36,000-case similar-patient database derived from SOAP-normalized MIMIC-IV notes and Synthea-generated records.
  We evaluate our framework on clinical note completion and medical question answering, and demonstrate that it consistently outperforms parametric LLM baselines and standard RAG, improving both generation fidelity and clinical reasoning accuracy. The full system is available at https://huggingface.co/spaces/Cryo3978/Med_GraphRAG , enabling users to inspect retrieved evidence, visualize token-level similarity contributions, and conduct guided follow-up analysis. Our results demonstrate a practical and interpretable approach to integrating structured guideline knowledge with patient-level analogical evidence for clinical LLMs.

</details>


### [442] [Optimizing In-Context Demonstrations for LLM-based Automated Grading](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00465)
*Yucheng Chu,Hang Li,Kaiqi Yang,Yasemin Copur-Gencturk,Kevin Haudek,Joseph Krajcik,Jiliang Tang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GUIDE proposes an iterative, boundary-focused way to pick and refine few-shot exemplars and generate discriminative rationales for LLM-based grading, yielding stronger rubric adherence and especially better performance on borderline cases across multiple domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM graders via in-context learning are brittle: standard similarity-based retrieval misses subtle decision boundaries in rubrics, and hand-written expert rationales are costly. The paper seeks reliable, scalable grading that respects fine-grained rubric distinctions without intensive manual effort.

Method: GUIDE reframes exemplar construction as boundary optimization. It repeatedly (1) selects contrastive “boundary pairs” of responses that are semantically similar but differ in grade, using novel contrastive operators; (2) augments exemplars with discriminative rationales that explicitly justify the assigned score versus adjacent scores; and (3) refines the exemplar set in a continuous loop to better delineate rubric edges for ICL prompting.

Result: Across datasets in physics, chemistry, and pedagogical content knowledge, GUIDE significantly outperforms standard similarity-based retrieval baselines, showing especially large gains on borderline cases and improved rubric adherence.

Conclusion: Focusing LLM attention on decision boundaries and supplying discriminative rationales enables more trustworthy, scalable automated assessment that aligns with human grading standards.

Abstract: Automated assessment of open-ended student responses is a critical capability for scaling personalized feedback in education. While large language models (LLMs) have shown promise in grading tasks via in-context learning (ICL), their reliability is heavily dependent on the selection of few-shot exemplars and the construction of high-quality rationales. Standard retrieval methods typically select examples based on semantic similarity, which often fails to capture subtle decision boundaries required for rubric adherence. Furthermore, manually crafting the expert rationales needed to guide these models can be a significant bottleneck. To address these limitations, we introduce GUIDE (Grading Using Iteratively Designed Exemplars), a framework that reframes exemplar selection and refinement in automated grading as a boundary-focused optimization problem. GUIDE operates on a continuous loop of selection and refinement, employing novel contrastive operators to identify "boundary pairs" that are semantically similar but possess different grades. We enhance exemplars by generating discriminative rationales that explicitly articulate why a response receives a specific score to the exclusion of adjacent grades. Extensive experiments across datasets in physics, chemistry, and pedagogical content knowledge demonstrate that GUIDE significantly outperforms standard retrieval baselines. By focusing the model's attention on the precise edges of rubric, our approach shows exceptionally robust gains on borderline cases and improved rubric adherence. GUIDE paves the way for trusted, scalable assessment systems that align closely with human pedagogical standards.

</details>


### [443] [Why Not? Solver-Grounded Certificates for Explainable Mission Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00469)
*Najeeb Khan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Model-derived explanation certificates for satellite scheduling provide causal, stable, and scalable justifications, clearly outperforming post‑hoc explainers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Operators of Earth observation satellites must trust and act on schedule decisions; post‑hoc explanations can be non‑causal, omit interacting constraints, and vary with solver pathways, undermining accountability and operability.

Method: Adopt a faithfulness‑first approach that extracts explanations directly from the optimization model: minimal infeasible subsets (for rejected requests), tight constraints and contrastive trade‑offs (for selected requests), and inverse solves for what‑if queries. Evaluate soundness against the solver’s constraint model, counterfactual validity, stability across seeds, compare to a post‑hoc baseline, and assess scalability (up to 200 orders, 30 satellites).

Result: Certificates achieve perfect soundness (15/15 constraint checks), perfect counterfactual validity (7/7), and perfect stability (Jaccard 1.0 over 28 seed pairs). The post‑hoc baseline yields 29% non‑causal attributions and misses all constraint conjunctions in multi‑cause rejections. Extraction times remain practical at tested scales.

Conclusion: Certificate‑based, model‑faithful explanations deliver reliable, causal, and stable justifications and actionable what‑if guidance for satellite scheduling, with practical scalability and clear advantages over post‑hoc approaches.

Abstract: Operators of Earth observation satellites need justifications for scheduling decisions: why a request was selected, rejected, or what changes would make it schedulable. Existing approaches construct post-hoc reasoning layers independent of the optimizer, risking non-causal attributions, incomplete constraint conjunctions, and solver-path dependence. We take a faithfulness-first approach: every explanation is a certificate derived from the optimization model itself: minimal infeasible subsets for rejections, tight constraints and contrastive trade-offs for selections, and inverse solves for what-if queries. On a scheduling instance with structurally distinct constraint interactions, certificates achieve perfect soundness with respect to the solver's constraint model (15/15 cited-constraint checks), counterfactual validity (7/7), and stability (Jaccard = 1.0 across 28 seed-pairs), while a post-hoc baseline produces non-causal attributions in 29% of cases and misses constraint conjunctions in every multi-cause rejection. A scalability analysis up to 200 orders and 30 satellites confirms practical extraction times for operational batches.

</details>


### [444] [From Goals to Aspects, Revisited: An NFR Pattern Language for Agentic AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00472)
*Yijun Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Adapts the classic goals-to-aspects method to agentic AI and delivers a 12-pattern AOP-based toolkit (in Rust) for security, reliability, observability, and cost, including four agent-specific patterns; extends the V-graph and validates via a case study, arguing this improves modularization and production readiness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agentic AI systems frequently fail to reach production because non-functional, crosscutting concerns (security, observability, cost, fault tolerance) are not systematically identified or modularized early in design.

Method: Revisits RE 2004 goals-to-aspects using i* goal/soft-goal modeling to systematically discover crosscutting concerns. Proposes a pattern language of 12 reusable NFR patterns and maps each from i* goals to concrete Aspect-Oriented Programming implementations (Rust AOP). Extends the V-graph to show joint contribution of agent tasks to functional and non-functional goals. Validates with a case study on an open-source autonomous agent framework.

Result: - A catalog of 12 patterns across four categories (security, reliability, observability, cost). - Four agent-specific patterns: tool-scope sandboxing, prompt injection detection, token budget management, action audit trails. - An extended V-graph representation. - Case-study evidence that goal-driven aspect discovery can systematically identify and modularize crosscutting concerns in an agent framework.

Conclusion: A principled, early-stage, goal-driven approach can modularize critical crosscutting concerns in agentic AI via AOP, potentially improving reliability and production success. Broader empirical validation, performance impact, and language/framework generality remain future work.

Abstract: Agentic AI systems exhibit numerous crosscutting concerns -- security, observability, cost management, fault tolerance -- that are poorly modularized in current implementations, contributing to the high failure rate of AI projects in reaching production. The goals-to-aspects methodology proposed at RE 2004 demonstrated that aspects can be systematically discovered from i* goal models by identifying non-functional soft-goals that crosscut functional goals. This paper revisits and extends that methodology to the agentic AI domain. We present a pattern language of 12 reusable patterns organized across four NFR categories (security, reliability, observability, cost management), each mapping an i* goal model to a concrete aspect implementation using an AOP framework for Rust. Four patterns address agent-specific crosscutting concerns absent from traditional AOP literature: tool-scope sandboxing, prompt injection detection, token budget management, and action audit trails. We extend the V-graph model to capture how agent tasks simultaneously contribute to functional goals and non-functional soft-goals. We validate the pattern language through a case study analyzing an open-source autonomous agent framework, demonstrating how goal-driven aspect discovery systematically identifies and modularizes crosscutting concerns. The pattern language offers a principled approach for engineering reliable agentic AI systems through early identification of crosscutting concerns.

</details>


### [445] [LifeEval: A Multimodal Benchmark for Assistive AI in Egocentric Daily Life Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00490)
*Hengjian Gao,Kaiwei Zhang,Shibo Wang,Mingjie Chen,Qihang Cao,Xianfeng Wang,Yucheng Zhu,Xiongkuo Min,Wei Sun,Dandan Zhu,Guangtao Zhai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LifeEval is a new multimodal benchmark that evaluates real-time, task-oriented, egocentric human–AI collaboration. With 4,075 annotated QA pairs across six capability dimensions and tests on 26 MLLMs, it reveals major gaps in timely, effective, and adaptive assistance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video and multimodal benchmarks mainly test passive, retrospective understanding or isolated perception, not interactive, real-time assistance. There is a need to assess human-centered, adaptive collaboration in dynamic everyday settings from a first-person perspective.

Method: Design a benchmark centered on (1) holistic, task-oriented evaluation, (2) egocentric, real-time perception from continuous first-person streams, and (3) natural dialogue-based human–assistant interaction. Build a high-quality dataset (4,075 QA pairs) via a rigorous annotation pipeline, covering six core capability dimensions, and evaluate 26 state-of-the-art MLLMs on it.

Result: Across the 26 MLLMs, performance indicates substantial difficulties in timely responsiveness, effectiveness, and adaptivity within continuous, real-time, egocentric scenarios, exposing weaknesses across the benchmark’s capability dimensions.

Conclusion: LifeEval fills a critical evaluation gap for real-time, human-centered interactive intelligence. Current MLLMs are not yet adequate for dynamic, egocentric assistance; the benchmark surfaces essential research directions to improve adaptive, collaborative capabilities.

Abstract: The rapid progress of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) marks a significant step toward artificial general intelligence, offering great potential for augmenting human capabilities. However, their ability to provide effective assistance in dynamic, real-world environments remains largely underexplored. Existing video benchmarks predominantly assess passive understanding through retrospective analysis or isolated perception tasks, failing to capture the interactive and adaptive nature of real-time user assistance. To bridge this gap, we introduce LifeEval, a multimodal benchmark designed to evaluate real-time, task-oriented human-AI collaboration in daily life from an egocentric perspective. LifeEval emphasizes three key aspects: task-oriented holistic evaluation, egocentric real-time perception from continuous first-person streams, and human-assistant collaborative interaction through natural dialogues. Constructed via a rigorous annotation pipeline, the benchmark comprises 4,075 high-quality question-answer pairs across 6 core capability dimensions. Extensive evaluations of 26 state-of-the-art MLLMs on LifeEval reveal substantial challenges in achieving timely, effective and adaptive interaction, highlighting essential directions for advancing human-centered interactive intelligence.

</details>


### [446] [AI Runtime Infrastructure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00495)
*Christopher Cruz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces an active execution-time runtime layer for AI agents that monitors, reasons, and intervenes during runs to optimize success, efficiency, reliability, and safety over long workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Model-level tuning and passive logging do not adequately handle long-horizon, real-time challenges (memory, failures, safety) in autonomous agents; an adaptive layer is needed to manage and optimize execution as it unfolds.

Method: Define an AI Runtime Infrastructure situated between application and model that treats execution as an optimization surface: continuous observation and reasoning about agent behavior, with capabilities for adaptive memory management, failure detection and recovery, and policy enforcement during runtime.

Result: Presents a conceptual framework/system design and argues it can improve task success, latency, token efficiency, reliability, and safety via active runtime interventions across extended agent workflows.

Conclusion: Positioning runtime as a first-class optimization target can yield more robust, efficient, and safer agent systems; calls for building active orchestration layers that adaptively manage agent execution.

Abstract: We introduce AI Runtime Infrastructure, a distinct execution-time layer that operates above the model and below the application, actively observing, reasoning over, and intervening in agent behavior to optimize task success, latency, token efficiency, reliability, and safety while the agent is running. Unlike model-level optimizations or passive logging systems, runtime infrastructure treats execution itself as an optimization surface, enabling adaptive memory management, failure detection, recovery, and policy enforcement over long-horizon agent workflows.

</details>


### [447] [DenoiseFlow: Uncertainty-Aware Denoising for Reliable LLM Agentic Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00532)
*Yandong Yan,Junwei Peng,Shijie Li,Chenxi Li,Yifei Shang,Can Deng,Ruiting Dai,Yongqiang Zhao,Jiaqi Zhu,Yu Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DenoiseFlow is a closed-loop framework for long-horizon agent workflows that mitigates error accumulation from semantic ambiguity by estimating per-step uncertainty, adaptively branching computation, and performing targeted corrections, yielding higher accuracy and lower cost across math, code, and multi-hop QA benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long, multi-step reasoning by autonomous agents suffers from compounding misinterpretations in natural-language instructions—accumulated semantic ambiguity—which degrades reliability. Existing methods are either static (fixed exploration budgets), purely reactive, or single-path and uncertainty-agnostic. A runtime-adaptive, uncertainty-aware process is needed to maintain correctness without excessive cost.

Method: They formalize multi-step reasoning as a Noisy MDP and introduce DenoiseFlow, a progressive denoising loop with three coordinated stages: (1) Sensing estimates stepwise semantic uncertainty; (2) Regulating adaptively routes between single-path execution and parallel exploration based on estimated risk to allocate compute efficiently; (3) Correcting performs targeted recovery via influence-based root-cause localization. An online self-calibration module uses verifier feedback to align decision thresholds without ground-truth labels.

Result: On six benchmarks (math reasoning, code generation, multi-hop QA), DenoiseFlow achieves the best accuracy on all tasks with an 83.3% average, improving +1.3% over the strongest baseline, while reducing cost by 40–56% via adaptive branching. Ablations indicate robustness and generality of the framework.

Conclusion: Closed-loop, uncertainty-aware progressive denoising improves both reliability and efficiency for long-horizon agentic workflows. By sensing uncertainty, adaptively regulating exploration, and correcting root causes—self-calibrated online without labels—DenoiseFlow mitigates accumulated semantic ambiguity and generalizes across domains; code is available for reproduction.

Abstract: Autonomous agents are increasingly entrusted with complex, long-horizon tasks, ranging from mathematical reasoning to software generation. While agentic workflows facilitate these tasks by decomposing them into multi-step reasoning chains, reliability degrades significantly as the sequence lengthens. Specifically, minor interpretation errors in natural-language instructions tend to compound silently across steps. We term this failure mode accumulated semantic ambiguity. Existing approaches to mitigate this often lack runtime adaptivity, relying instead on static exploration budgets, reactive error recovery, or single-path execution that ignores uncertainty entirely. We formalize the multi-step reasoning process as a Noisy MDP and propose DenoiseFlow, a closed-loop framework that performs progressive denoising through three coordinated stages: (1)Sensing estimates per-step semantic uncertainty; (2)Regulating adaptively allocates computation by routing between fast single-path execution and parallel exploration based on estimated risk; and (3)Correcting performs targeted recovery via influence-based root-cause localization. Online self-calibration continuously aligns decision boundaries with verifier feedback, requiring no ground-truth labels. Experiments on six benchmarks spanning mathematical reasoning, code generation, and multi-hop QA show that DenoiseFlow achieves the highest accuracy on every benchmark (83.3% average, +1.3% over the strongest baseline) while reducing cost by 40--56% through adaptive branching. Detailed ablation studies further confirm framework-level's robustness and generality. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/DenoiseFlow-21D3/.

</details>


### [448] [LOGIGEN: Logic-Driven Generation of Verifiable Agentic Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00540)
*Yucheng Zeng,Weipeng Lu,Linyun Liu,Shupeng Li,Zitian Qu,Chenghao Zhu,Shaofei Li,Zhengdong Tan,Mengyue Liu,Haotian Zhao,Zhe Zhou,Jianmin Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LOGIGEN is a logic-driven framework that generates verifiable, stateful training data and uses verification-based SFT+RL to produce agents that reliably follow hard policies and achieve long-horizon goals, yielding large gains on a challenging benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents must operate in complex, stateful environments with strict policy constraints, but current data-generation pipelines are scarce and fail to encode rigorous, verifiable logic, limiting the training of causally valid trajectories.

Method: Three pillars: (1) Hard-Compiled Policy Grounding—compile natural-language policies into database constraints; (2) Logic-Driven Forward Synthesis—Triple-Agent Orchestration where the Architect compiles rules, the Set Designer seeds boundary-edge states to elicit conflicts, and the Explorer searches for causal solution paths; (3) Deterministic State Verification—validate outcomes via exact state equivalence. They build a 20k-task, 8-domain dataset and train with a verification-based protocol: SFT on verified trajectories for policy compliance, then RL with dense state rewards for long-horizon performance.

Result: A dataset of 20,000 complex, policy-intensive tasks across 8 domains with strict correctness guarantees. On τ^2-Bench, LOGIGEN-32B (with RL) attains a 79.5% success rate, versus 40.7% for the base model.

Conclusion: Logic-grounded synthesis coupled with deterministic verification and SFT+RL produces causally valid, policy-compliant trajectories and substantially improves agent reliability and success on long-horizon, stateful tasks.

Abstract: The evolution of Large Language Models (LLMs) from static instruction-followers to autonomous agents necessitates operating within complex, stateful environments to achieve precise state-transition objectives. However, this paradigm is bottlenecked by data scarcity, as existing tool-centric reverse-synthesis pipelines fail to capture the rigorous logic of real-world applications. We introduce \textbf{LOGIGEN}, a logic-driven framework that synthesizes verifiable training data based on three core pillars: \textbf{Hard-Compiled Policy Grounding}, \textbf{Logic-Driven Forward Synthesis}, and \textbf{Deterministic State Verification}. Specifically, a Triple-Agent Orchestration is employed: the \textbf{Architect} compiles natural-language policy into database constraints to enforce hard rules; the \textbf{Set Designer} initializes boundary-adjacent states to trigger critical policy conflicts; and the \textbf{Explorer} searches this environment to discover causal solution paths. This framework yields a dataset of 20,000 complex tasks across 8 domains, where validity is strictly guaranteed by checking exact state equivalence. Furthermore, we propose a verification-based training protocol where Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) on verifiable trajectories establishes compliance with hard-compiled policy, while Reinforcement Learning (RL) guided by dense state-rewards refines long-horizon goal achievement. On $τ^2$-Bench, LOGIGEN-32B(RL) achieves a \textbf{79.5\% success rate}, substantially outperforming the base model (40.7\%). These results demonstrate that logic-driven synthesis combined with verification-based training effectively constructs the causally valid trajectories needed for next-generation agents.

</details>


### [449] [Advancing Multimodal Judge Models through a Capability-Oriented Benchmark and MCTS-Driven Data Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00546)
*Zeyu Chen,Huanjin Yao,Ziwang Zhao,Min Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They introduce M-JudgeBench, a 10-dimensional, capability-oriented benchmark for evaluating multimodal LLMs acting as judges, and a Judge-MCTS data-generation framework to create varied reasoning trajectories. Trained on this data, their M-Judger models outperform prior judge systems on both existing benchmarks and M-JudgeBench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs are increasingly used as automated judges, but current benchmarks are organized by task type and miss core judgment skills, risking unreliable and biased evaluations. A capability-focused assessment is needed to ensure trustworthy, consistent judging.

Method: 1) Build M-JudgeBench that decomposes judgment into three task families—pairwise Chain-of-Thought comparison, length-bias avoidance, and process error detection—covering 10 fine-grained subtasks to probe reliability across reasoning styles, response lengths, and cross-model outputs. 2) Propose Judge-MCTS, a data-construction pipeline that generates pairwise reasoning trajectories with diverse correctness and lengths via MCTS, yielding an augmented dataset. 3) Train M-Judger models on this dataset and evaluate them extensively against baselines.

Result: Benchmarking exposes systematic weaknesses in existing MLLM-as-a-judge systems. Models trained with the MCTS-augmented data (M-Judger) achieve superior performance on prior judge benchmarks and on M-JudgeBench.

Conclusion: A capability-driven benchmark (M-JudgeBench) and a principled data-generation/training framework (Judge-MCTS → M-Judger) together lay a stronger foundation for reliable MLLM judging, guiding future research on judge evaluation and training.

Abstract: Using Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) as judges to achieve precise and consistent evaluations has gradually become an emerging paradigm across various domains. Evaluating the capability and reliability of MLLM-as-a-judge systems is therefore essential for ensuring trustworthy assessment. Existing judge benchmarks categorize samples by task types but fail to capture the fundamental judgment capabilities required for reliable evaluation. In this work, we introduce M-JudgeBench, a ten-dimensional capability-oriented benchmark designed to comprehensively assess the judgment abilities of MLLMs. Our benchmark decomposes evaluation into pairwise Chain-of-Thought (CoT) comparison, length bias avoidance, and process error detection tasks, jointly covering ten fine-grained subtasks. This design enables diagnosis of model reliability across reasoning styles, response lengths, and cross-model variations. Systematic evaluation uncovers the systematic weaknesses in existing MLLM-as-a-judge systems. To address this issue, we further propose Judge-MCTS, a data construction framework generating pairwise reasoning trajectories with various correctness and length. Using Judge-MCTS, we construct an MCTS-augmented dataset and train M-Judger, a series of strong judge models. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of M-Judger on existing judge benchmarks as well as M-JudgeBench. Overall, our work establishes a more principled foundation for evaluating MLLM-as-a-judge through M-JudgeBench and Judge-MCTS framework, paving the way for future research on judge model evaluation and capability-driven judge training.

</details>


### [450] [EMPA: Evaluating Persona-Aligned Empathy as a Process](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00552)
*Shiya Zhang,Yuhan Zhan,Ruixi Su,Ruihan Sun,Ziyi Song,Zhaohan Chen,Xiaofan Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EMPA is a process-oriented framework to evaluate and optimize persona-aligned empathy in LLM dialogue agents by simulating psychologically grounded scenarios, running them in an open-ended multi-agent sandbox, and scoring full dialogue trajectories in a latent psychological space for alignment, cumulative impact, and stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Turn-level empathy metrics miss whether support remains aligned with a user’s persona over time. Real users’ states are latent, feedback is sparse and hard to verify, and superficially supportive turns can still drift from needs—making long-horizon evaluation unreliable and irreproducible.

Method: Distill real interactions into controllable, psychologically grounded scenarios; pair them with an open-ended multi-agent sandbox to elicit strategic adaptation and failure; evaluate entire trajectories in a latent psychological space using three axes—directional alignment to persona needs, cumulative impact, and stability—and use these signals for comparison and optimization.

Result: Provides reproducible, trajectory-level metrics and signals that surface adaptation and failure modes and enable fair comparison and optimization of long-horizon empathic behavior; the approach extends to other agent settings with latent dynamics and weak, hard-to-verify feedback.

Conclusion: Evaluating empathy as sustained intervention via latent-space trajectory scoring yields robust, reproducible assessment of persona-aligned support and offers actionable signals for improving long-horizon behavior, with utility beyond empathy-focused agents.

Abstract: Evaluating persona-aligned empathy in LLM-based dialogue agents remains challenging. User states are latent, feedback is sparse and difficult to verify in situ, and seemingly supportive turns can still accumulate into trajectories that drift from persona-specific needs. We introduce EMPA, a process-oriented framework that evaluates persona-aligned support as sustained intervention rather than isolated replies. EMPA distills real interactions into controllable, psychologically grounded scenarios, couples them with an open-ended multi-agent sandbox that exposes strategic adaptation and failure modes, and scores trajectories in a latent psychological space by directional alignment, cumulative impact, and stability. The resulting signals and metrics support reproducible comparison and optimization of long-horizon empathic behavior, and they extend to other agent settings shaped by latent dynamics and weak, hard-to-verify feedback.

</details>


### [451] [SWE-Hub: A Unified Production System for Scalable, Executable Software Engineering Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00575)
*Yucheng Zeng,Shupeng Li,Daxiang Dong,Ruijie Xu,Zimo Chen,Liwei Zheng,Yuxuan Li,Zhe Zhou,Haotian Zhao,Lun Tian,Heng Xiao,Tianshu Zhu,Longkun Hao,Jianmin Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SWE-Hub is an end-to-end, reproducible “data factory” for software-engineering agents that automates environment setup and scales the synthesis of realistic repair and creation tasks across multiple languages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Progress of SE agents is limited by a lack of executable, scalable, and realistic data. Current pipelines suffer from brittle, non-reproducible environments across languages, high cost of synthesizing realistic system-level bugs, and datasets biased toward short-horizon fixes that miss long-horizon skills like architectural consistency.

Method: Unify a production stack with four components: (1) Env Agent converts raw repos into reproducible, multi-language container environments with standard interfaces; (2) SWE-Scale uses cross-language code analysis and cluster-scale validation to mass-produce localized bug–fix instances; (3) Bug Agent creates high-fidelity, system-level regression tasks with cross-module dependencies and user-like symptom reports; (4) SWE-Architect turns natural-language requirements into repository-scale build-a-repo creation tasks. These are integrated into a continuous pipeline.

Result: Delivers a unified, executable task pipeline covering repair and creation, with high-throughput generation, cross-language support, and system-level realism. The abstract emphasizes capabilities and integration rather than quantitative benchmarks.

Conclusion: By operationalizing a data-factory abstraction, SWE-Hub addresses reproducibility, scalability, and realism gaps in SE-agent datasets, enabling continuous generation of long- and short-horizon, executable tasks across the software engineering lifecycle.

Abstract: Progress in software-engineering agents is increasingly constrained by the scarcity of executable, scalable, and realistic data for training and evaluation. This scarcity stems from three fundamental challenges in existing pipelines: environments are brittle and difficult to reproduce across languages; synthesizing realistic, system-level bugs at scale is computationally expensive; and existing data predominantly consists of short-horizon repairs, failing to capture long-horizon competencies like architectural consistency. We introduce \textbf{SWE-Hub}, an end-to-end system that operationalizes the data factory abstraction by unifying environment automation, scalable synthesis, and diverse task generation into a coherent production stack. At its foundation, the \textbf{Env Agent} establishes a shared execution substrate by automatically converting raw repository snapshots into reproducible, multi-language container environments with standardized interfaces. Built upon this substrate, \textbf{SWE-Scale} engine addresses the need for high-throughput generation, combining cross-language code analysis with cluster-scale validation to synthesize massive volumes of localized bug-fix instances. \textbf{Bug Agent} generates high-fidelity repair tasks by synthesizing system-level regressions involving cross-module dependencies, paired with user-like issue reports that describe observable symptoms rather than root causes. Finally, \textbf{SWE-Architect} expands the task scope from repair to creation by translating natural-language requirements into repository-scale build-a-repo tasks. By integrating these components, SWE-Hub establishes a unified production pipeline capable of continuously delivering executable tasks across the entire software engineering lifecycle.

</details>


### [452] [Draft-Thinking: Learning Efficient Reasoning in Long Chain-of-Thought LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00578)
*Jie Cao,Tianwei Lin,Zhenxuan Fan,Bo Yuan,Ziyuan Zhao,Rolan Yan,Wenqiao Zhang,Siliang Tang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Draft-Thinking trains models to produce concise, draft-style chains of thought via curriculum learning and adaptive prompting, slashing reasoning tokens with minimal accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long chains of thought boost reasoning but waste tokens and induce overthinking; prior fixes mostly compress or truncate outputs post hoc rather than teaching efficient reasoning itself. The goal is to decouple reasoning skill from reasoning cost.

Method: 1) Teach a compact, draft-style reasoning structure that keeps only critical steps; 2) use progressive curriculum learning so the model internalizes this efficiency as it scales; 3) add adaptive prompting so reasoning depth becomes a model-selectable behavior at inference.

Result: Across benchmarks, reasoning budget drops substantially while performance largely holds. On MATH500, token usage falls by 82.6% with only a 2.6% drop in accuracy.

Conclusion: Guiding models to learn concise internal reasoning and to adapt depth on demand mitigates overthinking, preserving most performance while markedly reducing cost.

Abstract: Long chain-of-thought~(CoT) has become a dominant paradigm for enhancing the reasoning capability of large reasoning models~(LRMs); however, the performance gains often come with a substantial increase in reasoning budget. Recent studies show that existing CoT paradigms tend to induce systematic overthinking, unnecessarily coupling reasoning capability with reasoning cost. Most prior approaches reduce token usage through post hoc techniques such as token compression, truncation, or length penalties, without explicitly addressing the core mechanisms of reasoning. We propose \textbf{Draft-Thinking}, which guides models to first learn a concise \textit{draft-style} reasoning structure that retains only the critical reasoning steps. Through a \textit{progressive curriculum learning}, the model stably internalizes this efficient reasoning pattern as its capability scales. Moreover, Draft-Thinking introduces adaptive prompting, which elevates reasoning depth to a flexible, model-selectable behavior. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Draft-Thinking substantially reduces reasoning budget while largely preserving reasoning performance; for example, on MATH500, it achieves an 82.6\% reduction in reasoning budget at the cost of only a 2.6\% performance drop.

</details>


### [453] [MicroVerse: A Preliminary Exploration Toward a Micro-World Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00585)
*Rongsheng Wang,Minghao Wu,Hongru Zhou,Zhihan Yu,Zhenyang Cai,Junying Chen,Benyou Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They introduce the first comprehensive benchmark, dataset, and specialized video-generation model for simulating biological processes at microscale, show current SOTA models fail these tasks, and demonstrate a tailored model (MicroVerse) trained on a new dataset (MicroSim-10K) performs substantially better; all resources are released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video generation has advanced for macroscopic scenes but lacks tools and models for faithfully simulating microscopic biological phenomena. Such simulations would aid drug discovery, organ-on-chip studies, and education, necessitating rigorous, expert-grounded evaluation and domain-specific models.

Method: 1) Build MicroWorldBench: a multi-level, rubric-based benchmark with 459 expert-annotated criteria spanning organ-level, cellular, and subcellular tasks and dimensions like scientific fidelity, visual quality, and instruction following. 2) Diagnose SOTA video generators on this benchmark. 3) Create MicroSim-10K: an expert-verified microscale simulation dataset. 4) Train MicroVerse: a video generation model specialized for microscale simulation using the new dataset.

Result: Benchmarking shows current SOTA video generators violate physical laws, exhibit temporal inconsistency, and fail to meet expert criteria. Training on MicroSim-10K yields MicroVerse, which can accurately reproduce complex microscale mechanisms (per the benchmark and expert verification).

Conclusion: They formalize “Micro-World Simulation,” provide a proof-of-concept pipeline (benchmark + dataset + model), and show domain-tailored training enables faithful microscale simulations, with potential impact in biology education and scientific visualization; data and code are publicly available.

Abstract: Recent advances in video generation have opened new avenues for macroscopic simulation of complex dynamic systems, but their application to microscopic phenomena remains largely unexplored. Microscale simulation holds great promise for biomedical applications such as drug discovery, organ-on-chip systems, and disease mechanism studies, while also showing potential in education and interactive visualization. In this work, we introduce MicroWorldBench, a multi-level rubric-based benchmark for microscale simulation tasks. MicroWorldBench enables systematic, rubric-based evaluation through 459 unique expert-annotated criteria spanning multiple microscale simulation task (e.g., organ-level processes, cellular dynamics, and subcellular molecular interactions) and evaluation dimensions (e.g., scientific fidelity, visual quality, instruction following). MicroWorldBench reveals that current SOTA video generation models fail in microscale simulation, showing violations of physical laws, temporal inconsistency, and misalignment with expert criteria. To address these limitations, we construct MicroSim-10K, a high-quality, expert-verified simulation dataset. Leveraging this dataset, we train MicroVerse, a video generation model tailored for microscale simulation. MicroVerse can accurately reproduce complex microscale mechanism. Our work first introduce the concept of Micro-World Simulation and present a proof of concept, paving the way for applications in biology, education, and scientific visualization. Our work demonstrates the potential of educational microscale simulations of biological mechanisms. Our data and code are publicly available at https://github.com/FreedomIntelligence/MicroVerse

</details>


### [454] [Fair in Mind, Fair in Action? A Synchronous Benchmark for Understanding and Generation in UMLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00590)
*Yiran Zhao,Lu Zhou,Xiaogang Xu,Zhe Liu,Jiafei Wu,Liming Fang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: IRIS is a benchmark for unified fairness evaluation of multimodal LLMs that synchronizes understanding and generation assessment by aggregating 60 normalized metrics into a 3D “fairness space” (Ideal Fairness, Real-world Fidelity, Bias Inertia & Steerability), powered by a demographic classifier (ARES) and four large-scale datasets. It surfaces systemic fairness phenomena and offers diagnostics to guide model optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fairness research suffers from fragmented, sometimes conflicting metrics—the “Tower of Babel” problem—made worse in unified multimodal LLMs where biases propagate across tasks. A standardized, extensible, and synchronous evaluation across understanding and generation is needed to compare models and guide improvements.

Method: Construct the IRIS benchmark: (1) build/curate four datasets; (2) use ARES, a demographic classifier, to label sensitive attributes; (3) compute 60 granular fairness-related metrics; (4) normalize and embed them into a high-dimensional fairness space summarized along three axes—Ideal Fairness, Real-world Fidelity, and Bias Inertia & Steerability; (5) evaluate leading UMLLMs and analyze patterns/diagnostics.

Result: IRIS reveals systemic and individual-level phenomena in UMLLMs, including a generation-versus-understanding “generation gap,” intra-model “personality splits,” and a “counter-stereotype reward.” It provides diagnostic breakdowns that can inform targeted fairness optimization and demonstrates the feasibility of integrating heterogeneous fairness metrics.

Conclusion: IRIS offers a novel, extensible framework to harmonize fairness evaluation in UMLLMs, potentially alleviating the metric-fragmentation impasse. Its synchronized, multidimensional view aims to guide benchmarking, model selection, and iterative fairness improvements as metrics and tasks evolve.

Abstract: As artificial intelligence (AI) is increasingly deployed across domains, ensuring fairness has become a core challenge. However, the field faces a "Tower of Babel'' dilemma: fairness metrics abound, yet their underlying philosophical assumptions often conflict, hindering unified paradigms-particularly in unified Multimodal Large Language Models (UMLLMs), where biases propagate systemically across tasks. To address this, we introduce the IRIS Benchmark, to our knowledge the first benchmark designed to synchronously evaluate the fairness of both understanding and generation tasks in UMLLMs. Enabled by our demographic classifier, ARES, and four supporting large-scale datasets, the benchmark is designed to normalize and aggregate arbitrary metrics into a high-dimensional "fairness space'', integrating 60 granular metrics across three dimensions-Ideal Fairness, Real-world Fidelity, and Bias Inertia & Steerability (IRIS). Through this benchmark, our evaluation of leading UMLLMs uncovers systemic phenomena such as the "generation gap'', individual inconsistencies like "personality splits'', and the "counter-stereotype reward'', while offering diagnostics to guide the optimization of their fairness capabilities. With its novel and extensible framework, the IRIS benchmark is capable of integrating evolving fairness metrics, ultimately helping to resolve the "Tower of Babel'' impasse. Project Page: https://iris-benchmark-web.vercel.app/

</details>


### [455] [Heterophily-Agnostic Hypergraph Neural Networks with Riemannian Local Exchanger](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00599)
*Li Sun,Ming Zhang,Wenxin Jin,Zhongtian Sun,Zhenhao Huang,Hao Peng,Sen Su,Philip Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HealHGNN reframes hypergraph message passing as Riemannian heat flow and introduces an adaptive local heat-exchanger to mitigate bottlenecks/oversquashing, enabling heterophily-agnostic, long-range dependency modeling with linear complexity and state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional HGNNs inherit homophily assumptions from standard GNNs and thus underperform on heterophilic hypergraphs, where nodes connected via hyperedges can have dissimilar labels/features and long-range interactions are crucial. Moreover, message passing suffers from oversquashing due to structural bottlenecks in hypergraphs.

Method: Model message passing via heat flow on a Riemannian manifold, exposing a link between oversquashing and hypergraph bottlenecks. Propose locally adaptive control of these bottlenecks across subhypergraphs using a heat-exchanger mechanism: Robin boundary conditions to capture rich long-range dependencies and source terms to preserve representation distinguishability. Implement a node–hyperedge bidirectional architecture with linear complexity in numbers of nodes and hyperedges.

Result: Across both homophilic and heterophilic benchmarks, HealHGNN achieves state-of-the-art performance, indicating superior ability to propagate long-range information without losing class separability.

Conclusion: By leveraging Riemannian heat-flow insights, HealHGNN provides theoretically grounded, heterophily-agnostic message passing that scales linearly and empirically outperforms prior HGNNs on diverse hypergraph regimes.

Abstract: Hypergraphs are the natural description of higher-order interactions among objects, widely applied in social network analysis, cross-modal retrieval, etc. Hypergraph Neural Networks (HGNNs) have become the dominant solution for learning on hypergraphs. Traditional HGNNs are extended from message passing graph neural networks, following the homophily assumption, and thus struggle with the prevalent heterophilic hypergraphs that call for long-range dependence modeling. In this paper, we achieve heterophily-agnostic message passing through the lens of Riemannian geometry. The key insight lies in the connection between oversquashing and hypergraph bottleneck within the framework of Riemannian manifold heat flow. Building on this, we propose the novel idea of locally adapting the bottlenecks of different subhypergraphs. The core innovation of the proposed mechanism is the design of an adaptive local (heat) exchanger. Specifically, it captures the rich long-range dependencies via the Robin condition, and preserves the representation distinguishability via source terms, thereby enabling heterophily-agnostic message passing with theoretical guarantees. Based on this theoretical foundation, we present a novel Heat-Exchanger with Adaptive Locality for Hypergraph Neural Network (HealHGNN), designed as a node-hyperedge bidirectional systems with linear complexity in the number of nodes and hyperedges. Extensive experiments on both homophilic and heterophilic cases show that HealHGNN achieves the state-of-the-art performance.

</details>


### [456] [Machine Learning Grade Prediction Using Students' Grades and Demographics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00608)
*Mwayi Sonkhanani,Symon Chibaya,Clement N. Nyirenda*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Unified ML framework predicts both pass/fail and continuous grades for secondary students, achieving up to 96% classification accuracy and R^2=0.70 on 4,424 students; aims to enable early, personalized interventions to reduce repetition and optimize resources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Student repetition is costly, especially where resources are constrained. Prior studies treat classification (pass/fail) and regression (grade) separately, limiting holistic insight. The study seeks an integrated approach to better identify at-risk students early and inform targeted interventions.

Method: Evaluate six supervised models: Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, Random Forest (classification) and Linear Regression, Decision Tree Regressor, Random Forest Regressor (regression). Hyperparameters tuned via exhaustive grid search. Train on academic and demographic features from 4,424 secondary students; compare to baselines.

Result: Best classification accuracy reaches 96%; best regression achieves R^2=0.70, both outperforming baseline methods. Evidence suggests combined classification–regression use offers richer insights than using either alone.

Conclusion: A dual-task prediction framework is feasible and effective for early risk detection, supporting timely, personalized interventions to reduce grade repetition and better allocate scarce educational resources.

Abstract: Student repetition in secondary education imposes significant resource burdens, particularly in resource-constrained contexts. Addressing this challenge, this study introduces a unified machine learning framework that simultaneously predicts pass/fail outcomes and continuous grades, a departure from prior research that treats classification and regression as separate tasks. Six models were evaluated: Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, and Random Forest for classification, and Linear Regression, Decision Tree Regressor, and Random Forest Regressor for regression, with hyperparameters optimized via exhaustive grid search. Using academic and demographic data from 4424 secondary school students, classification models achieved accuracies of up to 96%, while regression models attained a coefficient of determination of 0.70, surpassing baseline approaches. These results confirm the feasibility of early, data-driven identification of at-risk students and highlight the value of integrating dual-task prediction for more comprehensive insights. By enabling timely, personalized interventions, the framework offers a practical pathway to reducing grade repetition and optimizing resource allocation.

</details>


### [457] [TraceSIR: A Multi-Agent Framework for Structured Analysis and Reporting of Agentic Execution Traces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00623)
*Shu-Xun Yang,Cunxiang Wang,Haoke Zhang,Wenbo Yu,Lindong Wu,Jiayi Gui,Dayong Yang,Yukuo Cen,Zhuoer Feng,Bosi Wen,Yidong Wang,Lucen Zhong,Jiamin Ren,Linfeng Zhang,Jie Tang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TraceSIR is a multi-agent framework that structures, diagnoses, and reports on agentic LLM execution traces using a compact TraceFormat, yielding more coherent and actionable analyses than baselines; it also introduces TraceBench and an evaluation protocol (ReportEval).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agentic LLM systems have long, complex execution traces that make failure diagnosis and root-cause analysis difficult. Manual inspection doesn’t scale; raw-trace LLM analysis hits context limits and unreliable reasoning; focusing only on final outcomes discards critical behavioral signals needed for accurate issue localization.

Method: Three coordinated agents: (1) StructureAgent compresses traces into a new abstraction, TraceFormat, preserving essential behavioral information; (2) InsightAgent performs fine-grained diagnosis (issue localization, root-cause analysis, optimization suggestions); (3) ReportAgent aggregates insights across tasks to produce comprehensive reports. The authors also build TraceBench (three real-world scenarios) and define ReportEval for report quality/usability assessment.

Result: Across TraceBench, TraceSIR consistently generates coherent, informative, actionable reports and significantly outperforms existing approaches on all reported evaluation dimensions per ReportEval.

Conclusion: Structured trace abstraction plus specialized analysis and reporting agents enables scalable, accurate diagnosis of agentic LLM behaviors; the framework aligns with industry needs and is released with code and a demo.

Abstract: Agentic systems augment large language models with external tools and iterative decision making, enabling complex tasks such as deep research, function calling, and coding. However, their long and intricate execution traces make failure diagnosis and root cause analysis extremely challenging. Manual inspection does not scale, while directly applying LLMs to raw traces is hindered by input length limits and unreliable reasoning. Focusing solely on final task outcomes further discards critical behavioral information required for accurate issue localization. To address these issues, we propose TraceSIR, a multi-agent framework for structured analysis and reporting of agentic execution traces. TraceSIR coordinates three specialized agents: (1) StructureAgent, which introduces a novel abstraction format, TraceFormat, to compress execution traces while preserving essential behavioral information; (2) InsightAgent, which performs fine-grained diagnosis including issue localization, root cause analysis, and optimization suggestions; (3) ReportAgent, which aggregates insights across task instances and generates comprehensive analysis reports. To evaluate TraceSIR, we construct TraceBench, covering three real-world agentic scenarios, and introduce ReportEval, an evaluation protocol for assessing the quality and usability of analysis reports aligned with industry needs. Experiments show that TraceSIR consistently produces coherent, informative, and actionable reports, significantly outperforming existing approaches across all evaluation dimensions. Our project and video are publicly available at https://github.com/SHU-XUN/TraceSIR.

</details>


### [458] [LiTS: A Modular Framework for LLM Tree Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00631)
*Xinzhe Li,Yaguang Tao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LiTS is a modular, plug-and-play Python framework that decomposes LLM tree search into Policy, Transition, and RewardModel components, works with algorithms like MCTS and BFS across multiple domains, and finds that policy diversity—not reward quality—is the key bottleneck in infinite action spaces. Open-sourced with demos and examples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM reasoning via tree search lacks a unified, reusable infrastructure. Researchers and practitioners need a way to swap components and algorithms across domains, accelerate experimentation, and diagnose true performance bottlenecks (e.g., policy diversity vs. reward quality).

Method: Design three orthogonal interfaces—Policy, Transition, RewardModel—registered via decorators for easy extensibility. Implement generic search algorithms (e.g., MCTS, BFS) that consume these interfaces. Demonstrate composability on MATH500 (language reasoning), Crosswords (environment planning), and MapEval (tool use). Analyze behavior in infinite action spaces to identify mode collapse and bottlenecks.

Result: Components were interoperable across algorithms within each task type, and algorithms transferred across components and domains, supporting orthogonality. Empirically, in infinite action spaces, limited LLM policy diversity constrained tree search effectiveness more than reward model quality. The package, runnable examples, and a demo video are released under Apache 2.0.

Conclusion: LiTS offers a clean separation of concerns for LLM tree search, enabling rapid reuse and customization across domains and algorithms. Findings emphasize improving policy diversity as a priority over solely enhancing reward models. The open-source release supports reproducibility and adoption.

Abstract: LiTS is a modular Python framework for LLM reasoning via tree search. It decomposes tree search into three reusable components (Policy, Transition, and RewardModel) that plug into algorithms like MCTS and BFS. A decorator-based registry enables domain experts to extend to new domains by registering components, and algorithmic researchers to implement custom search algorithms. We demonstrate composability on MATH500 (language reasoning), Crosswords (environment planning), and MapEval (tool use), showing that components and algorithms are orthogonal: components are reusable across algorithms within each task type, and algorithms work across all components and domains. We also report a mode-collapse finding: in infinite action spaces, LLM policy diversity (not reward quality) is the bottleneck for effective tree search. A demonstration video is available at https://youtu.be/nRGX43YrR3I. The package is released under the Apache 2.0 license at https://github.com/xinzhel/lits-llm, including installation instructions and runnable examples that enable users to reproduce the demonstrated workflows.

</details>


### [459] [InfoPO: Information-Driven Policy Optimization for User-Centric Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00656)
*Fanqi Kong,Jiayi Zhang,Mingyi Deng,Chenglin Wu,Yuyu Luo,Bang Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: InfoPO is a multi‑turn RL method for LLM agents that assigns fine‑grained, turn‑level credit using an information‑gain signal (change in action distribution vs a masked‑feedback counterfactual) fused with task outcome rewards, yielding consistent gains over prompting and GRPO‑style baselines across diverse interactive tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real user requests are often underspecified, so agents must ask the right questions and integrate feedback. Existing multi‑turn GRPO approaches compute trajectory‑level rewards, causing poor credit assignment and weak within‑group advantages, which hampers learning which turns/queries were truly useful.

Method: Frame interaction as active uncertainty reduction. For each turn, compute an information‑gain reward by measuring how observed feedback changes the agent’s subsequent action distribution relative to a masked‑feedback counterfactual. Fuse this signal with task outcome via an adaptive, variance‑gated mechanism to emphasize informative turns while keeping task alignment. Optimize the policy with this composite signal.

Result: Across intent clarification, collaborative coding, and tool‑augmented decision‑making, InfoPO consistently outperforms prompting and multi‑turn RL baselines, remains robust under user‑simulator shifts, and generalizes to environment‑interactive settings.

Conclusion: Turn‑level, information‑driven credit assignment provides a principled and scalable way to train interactive LLM agents, improving decision quality and robustness in complex user–agent collaboration. Code is publicly available for reproducibility.

Abstract: Real-world user requests to LLM agents are often underspecified. Agents must interact to acquire missing information and make correct downstream decisions. However, current multi-turn GRPO-based methods often rely on trajectory-level reward computation, which leads to credit assignment problems and insufficient advantage signals within rollout groups. A feasible approach is to identify valuable interaction turns at a fine granularity to drive more targeted learning. To address this, we introduce InfoPO (Information-Driven Policy Optimization), which frames multi-turn interaction as a process of active uncertainty reduction and computes an information-gain reward that credits turns whose feedback measurably changes the agent's subsequent action distribution compared to a masked-feedback counterfactual. It then combines this signal with task outcomes via an adaptive variance-gated fusion to identify information importance while maintaining task-oriented goal direction. Across diverse tasks, including intent clarification, collaborative coding, and tool-augmented decision making, InfoPO consistently outperforms prompting and multi-turn RL baselines. It also demonstrates robustness under user simulator shifts and generalizes effectively to environment-interactive tasks. Overall, InfoPO provides a principled and scalable mechanism for optimizing complex agent-user collaboration. Code is available at https://github.com/kfq20/InfoPO.

</details>


### [460] [K^2-Agent: Co-Evolving Know-What and Know-How for Hierarchical Mobile Device Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00676)
*Zhe Wu,Donglin Mo,Hongjin Lu,Junliang Xing,Jianheng Liu,Yuheng Jing,Kai Li,Kun Shao,Jianye Hao,Yuanchun Shi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: K2-Agent is a hierarchical mobile-control agent that separates and co-evolves declarative (what) and procedural (how) knowledge: a high-level SRLR loop distills task know-how from one demo per task, and a low-level C-GRPO learner acquires skills via balanced rewards and dynamic demo injection, achieving 76.1% on AndroidWorld and showing cross-model/task generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mobile agents struggle with long-horizon planning and precise UI manipulation because they lack task-specific experience and robust skill execution; a human-like split between planning knowledge and motor skills could improve reliability and generalization with minimal supervision.

Method: Hierarchical framework with two components: (1) High-level reasoner bootstrapped from a single demonstration per task and refined via a Summarize-Reflect-Locate-Revise (SRLR) self-evolution loop to learn transferable declarative plans; (2) Low-level executor trained by curriculum-guided Group Relative Policy Optimization (C-GRPO), which forms a balanced sample pool using decoupled reward signals and uses dynamic demonstration injection to elicit successful trajectories. Operates from raw screenshots using open-source backbones.

Result: Achieves 76.1% success on the AndroidWorld benchmark from raw screenshots; declarative knowledge transfers across different base models; procedural skills generalize competitively to unseen tasks in ScreenSpot-v2 and Android-in-the-Wild (AitW).

Conclusion: Decoupling and co-evolving planning (declarative) and execution (procedural) with SRLR and C-GRPO yields strong performance and dual generalization while relying on minimal demonstrations, suggesting a scalable path for robust mobile device control.

Abstract: Existing mobile device control agents often perform poorly when solving complex tasks requiring long-horizon planning and precise operations, typically due to a lack of relevant task experience or unfamiliarity with skill execution. We propose K2-Agent, a hierarchical framework that models human-like cognition by separating and co-evolving declarative (knowing what) and procedural (knowing how) knowledge for planning and execution. K2-Agent's high level reasoner is bootstrapped from a single demonstration per task and runs a Summarize-Reflect-Locate-Revise (SRLR) loop to distill and iteratively refine task-level declarative knowledge through self-evolution. The low-level executor is trained with our curriculum-guided Group Relative Policy Optimization (C-GRPO), which (i) constructs a balanced sample pool using decoupled reward signals and (ii) employs dynamic demonstration injection to guide the model in autonomously generating successful trajectories for training. On the challenging AndroidWorld benchmark, K2-Agent achieves a 76.1% success rate using only raw screenshots and open-source backbones. Furthermore, K2-Agent shows powerful dual generalization: its high-level declarative knowledge transfers across diverse base models, while its low-level procedural skills achieve competitive performance on unseen tasks in ScreenSpot-v2 and Android-in-the-Wild (AitW).

</details>


### [461] [MemPO: Self-Memory Policy Optimization for Long-Horizon Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00680)
*Ruoran Li,Xinghua Zhang,Haiyang Yu,Shitong Duan,Xiang Li,Wenxin Xiang,Chonghua Liao,Xudong Guo,Yongbin Li,Jinli Suo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MemPO is a self-managed memory optimization algorithm for long-horizon agents that lets the policy summarize and curate its own memory via credit assignment on memory effectiveness, boosting F1 while cutting token usage substantially.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon agents accumulate ever-growing context, harming stability and performance. Prior work relies on external memory and retrieval, which separates memory control from the policy and can misalign with task objectives. The authors seek an approach where the agent proactively manages memory aligned with its goals.

Method: Introduce Self-Memory Policy Optimization (MemPO): during interaction, the policy autonomously summarizes and manages its memory. A revised credit assignment mechanism evaluates memory effectiveness, allowing the policy to selectively retain crucial information and discard the rest, thereby reducing token consumption without sacrificing task performance.

Result: Across extensive experiments, MemPO yields absolute F1 gains of 25.98% over the base model and 7.1% over a prior SOTA baseline, while reducing token usage by 67.58% and 73.12%, respectively.

Conclusion: Letting the policy self-curate memory via effectiveness-based credit assignment aligns memory with task objectives, improving performance and efficiency for long-horizon agents. MemPO offers a more stable and token-efficient alternative to externally managed memory systems.

Abstract: Long-horizon agents face the challenge of growing context size during interaction with environment, which degrades the performance and stability. Existing methods typically introduce the external memory module and look up the relevant information from the stored memory, which prevents the model itself from proactively managing its memory content and aligning with the agent's overarching task objectives. To address these limitations, we propose the self-memory policy optimization algorithm (MemPO), which enables the agent (policy model) to autonomously summarize and manage their memory during interaction with environment. By improving the credit assignment mechanism based on memory effectiveness, the policy model can selectively retain crucial information, significantly reducing token consumption while preserving task performance. Extensive experiments and analyses confirm that MemPO achieves absolute F1 score gains of 25.98% over the base model and 7.1% over the previous SOTA baseline, while reducing token usage by 67.58% and 73.12%.

</details>


### [462] [AIoT-based Continuous, Contextualized, and Explainable Driving Assessment for Older Adults](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00691)
*Yimeng Liu,Fangwei Zhang,Maolin Gan,Jialuo Du,Jingkai Lin,Yawen Wang,Fei Sun,Honglei Chen,Linda Hill,Ruofeng Liu,Tianxing Li,Zhichao Cao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes AURA, an AIoT framework that continuously and privately assesses older adults’ real-world driving via rich in-vehicle sensing and context-aware, multi-scale behavioral modeling, aiming to separate age-related changes from situational effects and enable proactive, individualized safety support.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Aging populations depend on driving for independence, yet age-related declines (vision, attention, reaction, control) quietly increase crash risk. Existing clinic-based or brief screenings are infrequent and do not reflect actual on-road behavior, creating a gap in timely, actionable safety assessment.

Method: Design of AURA: integrate richer in-vehicle sensors with edge AI to capture fine-grained driving actions; aggregate them into longer behavioral trajectories; apply context-aware models to disentangle age-related performance from situational factors (traffic, road design, weather); preserve privacy via on-device processing; provide interpretable indicators of driving performance over time.

Result: Conceptual framework and design principles for continuous, real-world monitoring; demonstrates how to extract detailed indicators of driving performance from routine trips and organize them into interpretable trajectories while accounting for context. No specific empirical evaluation is reported in the abstract.

Conclusion: Continuous, privacy-preserving, context-aware monitoring like AURA can underpin proactive, individualized interventions that help older adults drive safely. The paper charts challenges and research opportunities needed to realize reliable, real-world AIoT systems for safer aging behind the wheel.

Abstract: The world is undergoing a major demographic shift as older adults become a rapidly growing share of the population, creating new challenges for driving safety. In car-dependent regions such as the United States, driving remains essential for independence, access to services, and social participation. At the same time, aging can introduce gradual changes in vision, attention, reaction time, and driving control that quietly reduce safety. Today's assessment methods rely largely on infrequent clinic visits or simple screening tools, offering only a brief snapshot and failing to reflect how an older adult actually drives on the road. Our work starts from the observation that everyday driving provides a continuous record of functional ability and captures how a driver responds to traffic, navigates complex roads, and manages routine behavior. Leveraging this insight, we propose AURA, an Artificial Intelligence of Things (AIoT) framework for continuous, real-world assessment of driving safety among older adults. AURA integrates richer in-vehicle sensing, multi-scale behavioral modeling, and context-aware analysis to extract detailed indicators of driving performance from routine trips. It organizes fine-grained actions into longer behavioral trajectories and separates age-related performance changes from situational factors such as traffic, road design, or weather. By integrating sensing, modeling, and interpretation within a privacy-preserving edge architecture, AURA provides a foundation for proactive, individualized support that helps older adults drive safely. This paper outlines the design principles, challenges, and research opportunities needed to build reliable, real-world monitoring systems that promote safer aging behind the wheel.

</details>


### [463] [MO-MIX: Multi-Objective Multi-Agent Cooperative Decision-Making With Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00730)
*Tianmeng Hu,Biao Luo,Chunhua Yang,Tingwen Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MO-MIX is a CTDE-based multi-objective multi-agent RL method that conditions decentralized value functions on a preference (weight) vector and uses a parallel mixing network plus an exploration guide to produce a well-covered Pareto set, outperforming baselines with lower compute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many real-world cooperative tasks involve multiple agents and conflicting objectives. Prior RL work typically addresses either multi-agent single-objective settings or single-agent multi-objective settings, leaving their intersection underexplored.

Method: Within CTDE, inject a weight vector encoding objective preferences into each agent to condition local action-value estimation; employ a parallel-architecture mixing network to estimate the joint action-value; add an exploration-guidance mechanism to improve coverage/uniformity of the resulting non-dominated (Pareto) solutions.

Result: Across experiments, MO-MIX effectively solves cooperative MOMARL tasks, generates an approximation to the Pareto set, significantly outperforms a baseline on four evaluation metrics, and uses less computational resources.

Conclusion: Conditioning on objective preferences and leveraging a parallel mixing network within CTDE, augmented by guided exploration, is an effective and efficient approach to cooperative MOMARL, yielding superior Pareto coverage and performance at reduced computational cost.

Abstract: Deep reinforcement learning (RL) has been applied extensively to solve complex decision-making problems. In many real-world scenarios, tasks often have several conflicting objectives and may require multiple agents to cooperate, which are the multi-objective multi-agent decision-making problems. However, only few works have been conducted on this intersection. Existing approaches are limited to separate fields and can only handle multi-agent decision-making with a single objective, or multi-objective decision-making with a single agent. In this paper, we propose MO-MIX to solve the multi-objective multi-agent reinforcement learning (MOMARL) problem. Our approach is based on the centralized training with decentralized execution (CTDE) framework. A weight vector representing preference over the objectives is fed into the decentralized agent network as a condition for local action-value function estimation, while a mixing network with parallel architecture is used to estimate the joint action-value function. In addition, an exploration guide approach is applied to improve the uniformity of the final non-dominated solutions. Experiments demonstrate that the proposed method can effectively solve the multi-objective multi-agent cooperative decision-making problem and generate an approximation of the Pareto set. Our approach not only significantly outperforms the baseline method in all four kinds of evaluation metrics, but also requires less computational cost.

</details>


### [464] [The Synthetic Web: Adversarially-Curated Mini-Internets for Diagnosing Epistemic Weaknesses of Language Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00801)
*Shrey Shah,Levent Ozgur*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They introduce a controlled, synthetic web environment to causally test how web-browsing language agents handle adversarial search ranking. By inserting a single high-plausibility misinformation article at various top ranks, they show six frontier models suffer large accuracy collapses, poor calibration, and little search escalation despite access to truthful sources. The benchmark provides a reproducible testbed to study and mitigate these failures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Web-enabled language agents must navigate unreliable or adversarial content. Existing evaluations emphasize navigation skills or static factuality and cannot isolate the specific vulnerability of adversarial ranking (misleading items placed high in results). There is a need for a benchmark with ground truth, control over exposure, and contamination filtering to measure robustness and test mitigations rigorously.

Method: Build a procedurally generated, large hyperlinked corpus with per-article ground-truth labels for credibility and factuality, plus process-level interaction traces and contamination filtering. Inject a single, highly plausible misinformation document at controllable search ranks. Evaluate six frontier models’ task accuracy, search behavior (escalation/depth), and confidence calibration under varying adversarial exposure, holding access to truthful sources constant to estimate causal effects.

Result: Across models, placing one plausible misinformation item near the top of results causes catastrophic accuracy drops, even though truthful sources remain available. Agents rarely escalate search or seek more evidence and are severely miscalibrated (high confidence in wrong answers). These behaviors reveal difficulty reconciling conflicting evidence and susceptibility to ranking manipulation.

Conclusion: Current frontier language agents are not robust to adversarial ranking and exhibit insufficient epistemic humility, posing risks in high-stakes deployments. The Synthetic Web Benchmark offers a reproducible baseline and controlled testbed for analyzing these failure modes and for developing and evaluating mitigation strategies aimed at search-robustness and better calibration.

Abstract: Language agents increasingly act as web-enabled systems that search, browse, and synthesize information from diverse sources. However, these sources can include unreliable or adversarial content, and the robustness of agents to adversarial ranking - where misleading information appears prominently in search results - remains poorly understood. Existing benchmarks evaluate functional navigation or static factuality but cannot causally isolate this vulnerability, and current mitigation strategies for retrieval-augmented generation remain largely untested under such conditions. We introduce Synthetic Web Benchmark, a procedurally generated environment comprising thousands of hyperlinked articles with ground-truth labels for credibility and factuality, process-level interaction traces, and contamination filtering to eliminate training-data leakage. By injecting a single high-plausibility misinformation article into a controllable search rank, we measure the causal effect of adversarial exposure in six frontier models. The results reveal catastrophic failures: accuracy collapses despite unlimited access to truthful sources, with minimal search escalation and severe miscalibration. These findings expose fundamental limitations in how current frontier models handle conflicting information, with immediate implications for deployment in high-stakes domains. Our benchmark enables systematic analysis of these failure modes and provides a controlled testbed for evaluating mitigation strategies under adversarial ranking - a gap in current research. This work establishes a reproducible baseline for developing search-robust and epistemically humble agents capable of resisting manipulation in high-stakes domains.

</details>


### [465] [MetaMind: General and Cognitive World Models in Multi-Agent Systems by Meta-Theory of Mind](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00808)
*Lingyi Wang,Rashed Shelim,Walid Saad,Naren Ramakrishna*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MetaMind is a cognitive world model for multi‑agent systems that learns a meta-theory of mind: agents self-supervise by inferring goals/beliefs from their own behavior, then analogically transfer this metacognition to infer others’ intentions from sparse observations, enabling zero-shot coordination and long-horizon planning without communication; simulations show superior performance and few-shot generalization over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: World models for multi-agent settings struggle with interdependent dynamics, interactive trajectory prediction, and long-horizon planning under decentralized, communication-free conditions. There is a need for agents to acquire theory-of-mind-like abilities to reason about others’ beliefs and goals for better coordination and generalization.

Method: Proposes MetaMind with a Meta-Theory of Mind (Meta-ToM) framework. Each agent runs a self-reflective, bidirectional inference loop: (1) forward prediction/planning over its beliefs; (2) inverse inference to recover its own latent goals/beliefs from its behavior trajectories, yielding metacognitive representations learned self-supervised. These first-person metacognitive models are then generalized to third-person via analogical reasoning so agents can infer other agents’ intentions from limited observed trajectories and adapt plans to emergent collective intentions, all without explicit communication or centralized supervision.

Result: Across diverse simulated multi-agent tasks, MetaMind achieves higher task performance and stronger few-shot generalization than baselines, demonstrating zero-shot reasoning about others from limited observations. Details of tasks/metrics are not provided in the abstract, but results are described as extended and consistent.

Conclusion: A general, communication-free, metacognitive world model enables decentralized agents to infer others’ beliefs/goals and plan collectively over long horizons. MetaMind advances multi-agent world modeling with zero/few-shot generalization and improved coordination in simulation, suggesting a path toward scalable, ToM-enabled multi-agent systems.
