Abstract: A major challenge for world models in multi-agent systems is to understand interdependent agent dynamics, predict interactive multi-agent trajectories, and plan over long horizons with collective awareness, without centralized supervision or explicit communication. In this paper, MetaMind, a general and cognitive world model for multi-agent systems that leverages a novel meta-theory of mind (Meta-ToM) framework, is proposed. Through MetaMind, each agent learns not only to predict and plan over its own beliefs, but also to inversely reason goals and beliefs from its own behavior trajectories. This self-reflective, bidirectional inference loop enables each agent to learn a metacognitive ability in a self-supervised manner. Then, MetaMind is shown to generalize the metacognitive ability from first-person to third-person through analogical reasoning. Thus, in multi-agent systems, each agent with MetaMind can actively reason about goals and beliefs of other agents from limited, observable behavior trajectories in a zero-shot manner, and then adapt to emergent collective intention without an explicit communication mechanism. Extended simulation results on diverse multi-agent tasks demonstrate that MetaMind can achieve superior task performance and outperform baselines in few-shot multi-agent generalization.

</details>


### [466] [MC-Search: Evaluating and Enhancing Multimodal Agentic Search with Structured Long Reasoning Chains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00873)
*Xuying Ning,Dongqi Fu,Tianxin Wei,Mengting Ai,Jiaru Zou,Ting-Wei Li,Hanghang Tong,Yada Zhu,Hendrik Hamann,Jingrui He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MC-Search introduces a benchmark and training framework for agentic multimodal RAG, offering long, step-wise verified reasoning chains, new process-level metrics, a unified evaluation pipeline, and a process-supervised fine-tuning method (Search-Align) that improves planning and retrieval in MLLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current MLLM evaluation largely centers on short, simplified QA and fixed retrieve-then-generate pipelines, which fail to capture emerging needs for adaptive, step-wise, cross-modal, knowledge-grounded reasoning and planning. There is a lack of benchmarks and metrics that assess and supervise the reasoning process itself, especially across modalities.

Method: (1) Construct MC-Search, a benchmark of 3,333 examples averaging 3.7 hops, covering five reasoning structures with explicit sub-questions, retrieval modalities, supporting facts, and intermediate answers; ensure fidelity using HAVE (Hop-wise Attribution and Verification of Evidence). (2) Propose process-level metrics evaluating reasoning quality, stepwise retrieval, and planning. (3) Build a unified agentic MM-RAG pipeline to benchmark six leading MLLMs. (4) Introduce Search-Align, a process-supervised fine-tuning framework that leverages verified reasoning chains for training.

Result: Benchmarking reveals systematic errors in state-of-the-art MLLMs, including over-/under-retrieval and modality-misaligned planning. The dataset contains 3,333 carefully verified examples with an average of 3.7 hops. Search-Align, trained on these verified chains, improves planning and retrieval fidelity in open-source MLLMs beyond baseline performance.

Conclusion: MC-Search enables faithful, process-level evaluation of agentic multimodal RAG and provides supervision signals that enhance MLLMs’ agentic planning and retrieval via Search-Align. It fills a critical gap in multimodal reasoning benchmarks by emphasizing long-horizon, cross-modal reasoning and offering both evaluation tools and a pathway to model improvement.

Abstract: With the increasing demand for step-wise, cross-modal, and knowledge-grounded reasoning, multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are evolving beyond the traditional fixed retrieve-then-generate paradigm toward more sophisticated agentic multimodal retrieval-augmented generation (MM-RAG). Existing benchmarks, however, mainly focus on simplified QA with short retrieval chains, leaving adaptive planning and multimodal reasoning underexplored. We present MC-Search, the first benchmark for agentic MM-RAG with long, step-wise annotated reasoning chains spanning five representative reasoning structures. Each example specifies sub-questions, retrieval modalities, supporting facts, and intermediate answers, with fidelity ensured by HAVE (Hop-wise Attribution and Verification of Evidence), resulting in 3,333 high-quality examples averaging 3.7 hops. Beyond answer accuracy, MC-Search introduces new process-level metrics for reasoning quality, stepwise retrieval and planning accuracy. By developing a unified agentic MM-RAG pipeline, we benchmark six leading MLLMs and reveal systematic issues such as over- and under-retrieval and modality-misaligned planning. Finally, we introduce Search-Align, a process-supervised fine-tuning framework leveraging verified reasoning chains, showing that our data not only enables faithful evaluation but also improves planning and retrieval fidelity in open-source MLLMs.

</details>


### [467] [BioProAgent: Neuro-Symbolic Grounding for Constrained Scientific Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00876)
*Yuyang Liu,Jingya Wang,Liuzhenghao Lv,Yonghong Tian*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BioProAgent is a neuro-symbolic agent for wet-lab automation that anchors LLM planning in a deterministic finite state machine, adds a Design–Verify–Rectify workflow, and compresses device schemas via semantic symbol grounding, yielding ~6x lower context use and 95.6% physical compliance on BioProBench (vs 21.0% for ReAct).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs can reason about scientific procedures but often hallucinate or violate hardware constraints, which in irreversible wet-lab settings leads to experiment failure or equipment damage. There is also a context bottleneck when handling complex device schemas. A reliable bridge from high-level planning to safe physical execution is needed.

Method: Propose a neuro-symbolic framework, BioProAgent, that (1) anchors probabilistic LLM planning in a deterministic Finite State Machine to constrain allowable actions; (2) introduces State-Augmented Planning enforcing a Design–Verify–Rectify loop to ensure hardware compliance pre-execution; and (3) applies Semantic Symbol Grounding to abstract device schemas and reduce token/context usage. Evaluation is done on an extended BioProBench, comparing to ReAct-style baselines.

Result: On BioProBench, BioProAgent attains 95.6% physical compliance, far exceeding ReAct’s 21.0%, and reduces token consumption by about 6x via symbolic abstraction.

Conclusion: Neuro-symbolic constraints that ground LLMs in deterministic state machines and enforce pre-execution verification are key to safe, reliable autonomy in irreversible physical environments. Symbolic grounding also mitigates context limits. The approach substantially improves hardware compliance; code and project resources are released.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated significant reasoning capabilities in scientific discovery but struggle to bridge the gap to physical execution in wet-labs. In these irreversible environments, probabilistic hallucinations are not merely incorrect, but also cause equipment damage or experimental failure. To address this, we propose \textbf{BioProAgent}, a neuro-symbolic framework that anchors probabilistic planning in a deterministic Finite State Machine (FSM). We introduce a State-Augmented Planning mechanism that enforces a rigorous \textit{Design-Verify-Rectify} workflow, ensuring hardware compliance before execution. Furthermore, we address the context bottleneck inherent in complex device schemas by \textit{Semantic Symbol Grounding}, reducing token consumption by $\sim$6$\times$ through symbolic abstraction. In the extended BioProBench benchmark, BioProAgent achieves 95.6\% physical compliance (compared to 21.0\% for ReAct), demonstrating that neuro-symbolic constraints are essential for reliable autonomy in irreversible physical environments. \footnote{Code at https://github.com/YuyangSunshine/bioproagent and project at https://yuyangsunshine.github.io/BioPro-Project/}

</details>


### [468] [HiMAC: Hierarchical Macro-Micro Learning for Long-Horizon LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00977)
*Hongbo Jin,Rongpeng Zhu,Jiayu Ding,Wenhao Zhang,Ge Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HiMAC is a hierarchical RL framework for LLM agents that separates macro-level planning (structured blueprints) from micro-level execution (goal-conditioned actions). Using critic-free hierarchical optimization and an alternating co-evolution of planner and executor, it achieves SOTA and better sample efficiency on ALFWorld, WebShop, and Sokoban.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents struggle on long-horizon tasks because flat autoregressive policies entangle high-level reasoning with low-level actions, causing inefficient exploration and compounding errors. Prior methods often scale model size or prompting tricks but lack explicit structure for planning and execution. The field needs a robust way to plan over long horizons while executing reliably and learning efficiently.

Method: HiMAC decomposes decision-making into two levels: a macro planner that produces a structured blueprint (e.g., goals/subgoals) and a micro executor that performs goal-conditioned actions to realize the plan. Reasoning is modeled as blueprint generation followed by conditioned action execution, enabling separation of concerns and reduced error propagation. Training uses a critic-free hierarchical policy optimization that extends group-based RL to a bi-level setup via hierarchical relative advantage estimation, avoiding learned critics. To stabilize learning despite hierarchy-induced non-stationarity, HiMAC alternates between planner exploration and executor adaptation in an iterative co-evolution scheme. The framework is implemented with LLM-based components and applied across both text-only and visually grounded environments.

Result: Across ALFWorld, WebShop, and Sokoban, HiMAC consistently outperforms strong prompting and RL baselines, achieving state-of-the-art returns with substantially improved sample efficiency. It demonstrates more robust long-horizon planning and execution compared to flat autoregressive policies. The empirical gains hold across diverse settings, including text-based and visual tasks.

Conclusion: Introducing explicit hierarchical structure—rather than merely increasing LLM scale—enables more reliable long-horizon agentic behavior. HiMAC’s blueprint-plus-executor design with critic-free bi-level optimization and co-evolution provides a practical path to robust planning and execution in LLM agents.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents have recently demonstrated strong capabilities in interactive decision-making, yet they remain fundamentally limited in long-horizon tasks that require structured planning and reliable execution. Existing approaches predominantly rely on flat autoregressive policies, where high-level reasoning and low-level actions are generated within a single token sequence, leading to inefficient exploration and severe error propagation over extended trajectories. In this work, we propose HiMAC, a hierarchical agentic RL framework that explicitly decomposes long-horizon decision-making into macro-level planning and micro-level execution. HiMAC models reasoning as a structured blueprint generation process followed by goal-conditioned action execution, enabling robust long-horizon planning within LLM-based agents. To train this hierarchy efficiently, we introduce a critic-free hierarchical policy optimization paradigm that extends group-based reinforcement learning to bi-level structures through hierarchical relative advantage estimation. Furthermore, we propose an iterative co-evolution training strategy that alternates between planner exploration and executor adaptation, mitigating the non-stationarity inherent in hierarchical learning. Extensive experiments on ALFWorld, WebShop, and Sokoban demonstrate that HiMAC consistently outperforms strong prompting and reinforcement learning baselines, achieving state-of-the-art performance and substantially improved sample efficiency across both text-based and visually grounded environments. Our results show that introducing structured hierarchy, rather than increasing model scale alone, is a key factor for enabling robust long-horizon agentic intelligence.

</details>


### [469] [Tracking Capabilities for Safer Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00991)
*Martin Odersky,Yaoyu Zhao,Yichen Xu,Oliver Bračevac,Cao Nguyen Pham*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Use a programming-language safety harness: have agents emit capability-safe Scala 3 code (with capture checking) instead of calling tools directly, so the type system enforces effect limits and local purity, blocking leaks and harmful side effects without hurting task performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tool-using AI agents risk privacy leaks, unintended side effects, and prompt injection. The authors seek principled, enforceable constraints on what agents can do, beyond ad‑hoc filters, by leveraging static guarantees from programming languages.

Method: Interpose a capability-safe layer: agents express intentions as Scala 3 code whose type system tracks capabilities and enforces local purity via capture checking. Capabilities (as values) gate access to effects/resources; static typing ensures only authorized effects occur and lets sub-computations be side-effect-free, preventing information flow from classified data. The harness is designed to be extensible; evaluation tests whether LLMs can generate such code and whether the type system blocks unsafe behaviors.

Result: Agents successfully generate capability-safe code with no significant performance degradation on tasks, while the type system consistently prevents unsafe behaviors like information leakage and malicious side effects.

Conclusion: Strong, capability-tracking type systems can serve as effective, extensible safety harnesses for tool-using AI agents, offering fine-grained, static control of effects and local purity to mitigate key safety risks without sacrificing performance.

Abstract: AI agents that interact with the real world through tool calls pose fundamental safety challenges: agents might leak private information, cause unintended side effects, or be manipulated through prompt injection. To address these challenges, we propose to put the agent in a programming-language-based "safety harness": instead of calling tools directly, agents express their intentions as code in a capability-safe language: Scala 3 with capture checking. Capabilities are program variables that regulate access to effects and resources of interest. Scala's type system tracks capabilities statically, providing fine-grained control over what an agent can do. In particular, it enables local purity, the ability to enforce that sub-computations are side-effect-free, preventing information leakage when agents process classified data. We demonstrate that extensible agent safety harnesses can be built by leveraging a strong type system with tracked capabilities. Our experiments show that agents can generate capability-safe code with no significant loss in task performance, while the type system reliably prevents unsafe behaviors such as information leakage and malicious side effects.

</details>


### [470] [CollabEval: Enhancing LLM-as-a-Judge via Multi-Agent Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00993)
*Yiyue Qian,Shinan Zhang,Yun Zhou,Haibo Ding,Diego Socolinsky,Yi Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CollabEval is a three-phase, multi-agent collaborative LLM-judge framework that improves reliability and robustness over single-LLM evaluators while remaining efficient via strategic consensus checks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single-LLM evaluators yield inconsistent, biased judgments due to stochasticity and pretraining data biases; practitioners need more reliable, fair, and robust AI-generated content evaluation.

Method: A collaborative multi-agent process with three phases: (1) initial independent evaluations by multiple agents, (2) multi-round discussion to reconcile and refine reasoning, and (3) final judgment using strategic consensus checking for efficiency. Emphasizes cooperation over competitive debate and supports multiple evaluation criteria.

Result: Across extensive experiments, CollabEval outperforms single-LLM evaluators on multiple dimensions and remains robust even when individual models underperform, while keeping evaluation efficient through its collaborative design. (Abstract provides claims but no quantitative details.)

Conclusion: Collaborative, consensus-driven multi-agent evaluation is a promising, more reliable alternative to single-LLM or debate-based judging, offering broader criteria coverage and robustness with manageable cost; specifics on metrics, datasets, and cost–quality trade-offs are needed for full assessment.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized AI-generated content evaluation, with the LLM-as-a-Judge paradigm becoming increasingly popular. However, current single-LLM evaluation approaches face significant challenges, including inconsistent judgments and inherent biases from pre-training data. To address these limitations, we propose CollabEval, a novel multi-agent evaluation framework that implements a three-phase Collaborative Evaluation process: initial evaluation, multi-round discussion, and final judgment. Unlike existing approaches that rely on competitive debate or single-model evaluation, CollabEval emphasizes collaboration among multiple agents with strategic consensus checking for efficiency. Our extensive experiments demonstrate that CollabEval consistently outperforms single-LLM approaches across multiple dimensions while maintaining robust performance even when individual models struggle. The framework provides comprehensive support for various evaluation criteria while ensuring efficiency through its collaborative design.

</details>


### [471] [MMCOMET: A Large-Scale Multimodal Commonsense Knowledge Graph for Contextual Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01055)
*Eileen Wang,Hiba Arnaout,Dhita Pratama,Shuo Yang,Dangyang Liu,Jie Yang,Josiah Poon,Jeff Pan,Caren Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MMCOMET augments ATOMIC2020 with a visual dimension to create ~900K multimodal commonsense triples, enabling richer, more coherent, and grounded visual storytelling than text-only knowledge sources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal knowledge graphs struggle to support complex reasoning tasks (e.g., image captioning, storytelling) because they lack integrated physical, social, and eventive commonsense with visual grounding. There is a need for a holistic resource that ties textual commonsense to images to better support narrative generation and reasoning.

Method: Extend the ATOMIC2020 commonsense KG by associating its textual triples with images via an efficient image retrieval pipeline, yielding a multimodal KG (MMCOMET) that unifies physical, social, and eventive knowledge. Evaluate its utility through a standard visual storytelling experiment, comparing against text-only knowledge usage.

Result: MMCOMET supports the generation of stories that are richer, more coherent, and more contextually grounded than those produced using text-only commonsense knowledge, as shown in the visual storytelling evaluation.

Conclusion: By providing large-scale, visually grounded commonsense across multiple dimensions, MMCOMET establishes a new foundation for multimodal commonsense reasoning and narrative generation, addressing limitations of prior MMKGs in complex reasoning tasks.

Abstract: We present MMCOMET, the first multimodal commonsense knowledge graph (MMKG) that integrates physical, social, and eventive knowledge. MMCOMET extends the ATOMIC2020 knowledge graph to include a visual dimension, through an efficient image retrieval process, resulting in over 900K multimodal triples. This new resource addresses a major limitation of existing MMKGs in supporting complex reasoning tasks like image captioning and storytelling. Through a standard visual storytelling experiment, we show that our holistic approach enables the generation of richer, coherent, and contextually grounded stories than those produced using text-only knowledge. This resource establishes a new foundation for multimodal commonsense reasoning and narrative generation.

</details>


### [472] [Alien Science: Sampling Coherent but Cognitively Unavailable Research Directions from Idea Atoms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01092)
*Alejandro H. Artiles,Martin Weiss,Levin Brinkmann,Anirudh Goyal,Nasim Rahaman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose a system that composes “alien” research directions—ideas that are coherent yet unlikely to be suggested by typical researchers—by learning a vocabulary of idea atoms and jointly modeling coherence and community availability over ~7,500 LLM papers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are good at remixing known material but struggle to produce the kind of creativity valued in research: coherent ideas that are non-obvious to the current community. The paper aims to formalize and overcome this gap.

Method: 1) Decompose papers into fine-grained conceptual units. 2) Cluster recurring units into shared “idea atoms.” 3) Train two models: a coherence model to judge whether sets of atoms form viable directions, and an availability model to estimate how likely such directions would be proposed by typical researchers. 4) Sample directions that are high-coherence, low-availability (“alien” ideas).

Result: On ~7,500 NeurIPS/ICLR/ICML LLM papers, they show: (a) conceptual units reconstruct paper content well; (b) idea atoms generalize across papers rather than memorize phrasing; (c) the Alien sampler yields more diverse directions than LLM baselines while preserving coherence.

Conclusion: Modeling coherence and community availability over learned idea atoms can systematically surface coherent yet non-obvious research directions, improving diversity without sacrificing feasibility and offering a practical tool for research ideation.

Abstract: Large language models are adept at synthesizing and recombining familiar material, yet they often fail at a specific kind of creativity that matters most in research: producing ideas that are both coherent and non-obvious to the current community. We formalize this gap through cognitive availability, the likelihood that a research direction would be naturally proposed by a typical researcher given what they have worked on. We introduce a pipeline that (i) decomposes papers into granular conceptual units, (ii) clusters recurring units into a shared vocabulary of idea atoms, and (iii) learns two complementary models: a coherence model that scores whether a set of atoms constitutes a viable direction, and an availability model that scores how likely that direction is to be generated by researchers drawn from the community. We then sample "alien" directions that score high on coherence but low on availability. On a corpus of $\sim$7,500 recent LLM papers from NeurIPS, ICLR and ICML, we validate that (a) conceptual units preserve paper content under reconstruction, (b) idea atoms generalize across papers rather than memorizing paper-specific phrasing, and (c) the Alien sampler produces research directions that are more diverse than LLM baselines while maintaining coherence.

</details>


### [473] [DIVA-GRPO: Enhancing Multimodal Reasoning through Difficulty-Adaptive Variant Advantage](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01106)
*Haowen Gao,Zhenyu Zhang,Liang Pang,Fangda Guo,Hongjian Dou,Guannan Lv,Shaoguo Liu,Tingting Gao,Huawei Shen,Xueqi Cheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DIVA-GRPO is a difficulty-aware variant of GRPO that adaptively samples problem variants and scales advantages across local/global groups to avoid sparse rewards and advantage vanishing, yielding more stable training and better reasoning performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GRPO, while enabling long-chain reasoning without a critic, often collapses on very easy or very hard items: rewards within groups become too uniform (advantage vanishes) or too sparse (no learning signal). Prior fixes (more samples, selective usage, indirect rewards) fail to preserve sufficient within-group reward variance.

Method: DIVA-GRPO dynamically estimates task difficulty, samples variant problems at appropriate difficulty levels, and computes advantages with difficulty-weighted and normalized scaling across both local and global groups. This maintains informative reward variance and stabilizes optimization.

Result: Across six reasoning benchmarks, DIVA-GRPO shows higher training efficiency and superior reasoning accuracy compared to existing GRPO-based methods.

Conclusion: Global, difficulty-adaptive sampling and advantage scaling effectively mitigate GRPO’s sparsity and vanishing-advantage issues, improving stability and performance; the approach appears broadly applicable to MLLM reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) with group relative policy optimization (GRPO) has become a widely adopted approach for enhancing the reasoning capabilities of multimodal large language models (MLLMs). While GRPO enables long-chain reasoning without a critic, it often suffers from sparse rewards on difficult problems and advantage vanishing when group-level rewards are too consistent for overly easy or hard problems. Existing solutions (sample expansion, selective utilization, and indirect reward design) often fail to maintain enough variance in within-group reward distributions to yield clear optimization signals. To address this, we propose DIVA-GRPO, a difficulty-adaptive variant advantage method that adjusts variant difficulty distributions from a global perspective. DIVA-GRPO dynamically assesses problem difficulty, samples variants with appropriate difficulty levels, and calculates advantages across local and global groups using difficulty-weighted and normalized scaling. This alleviates reward sparsity and advantage vanishing while improving training stability. Extensive experiments on six reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that DIVA-GRPO outperforms existing approaches in training efficiency and reasoning performance. Code: https://github.com/Siaaaaaa1/DIVA-GRPO

</details>


### [474] [HVR-Met: A Hypothesis-Verification-Replaning Agentic System for Extreme Weather Diagnosis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01121)
*Shuo Tang,Jiadong Zhang,Jian Xu,Gengxian Zhou,Qizhao Jin,Qinxuan Wang,Yi Hu,Ning Hu,Hongchang Ren,Lingli He,Jiaolan Fu,Jingtao Ding,Shiming Xiang,Chenglin Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HVR-Met is a multi-agent meteorological diagnostic system that embeds expert knowledge and uses a Hypothesis–Verification–Replanning (HVR) closed loop to iteratively reason about anomalous signals in extreme weather; a new atomic-subtask benchmark is introduced, and experiments show strong performance in complex diagnostics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite advances in deep learning for forecasting, extreme weather diagnosis lags because it requires multi-step logical reasoning, dynamic tool use, and expert judgment. Existing agent systems lack deep expert-knowledge integration, robust iterative reasoning loops, and fine-grained validation/evaluation for complex, high-stakes conditions.

Method: Design a multi-agent system (HVR-Met) with deep expert-knowledge integration and an HVR closed-loop mechanism that iteratively forms hypotheses, verifies them with tools/data, and replans. Complement this with a new benchmark targeting atomic-level subtasks to assess complex diagnostic workflows under extreme weather.

Result: Experimental evidence indicates HVR-Met performs well and excels in complex diagnostic scenarios involving extreme events; specifics (datasets, metrics, and quantitative gains) are not detailed in the abstract.

Conclusion: Embedding expert knowledge into a multi-agent, closed-loop HVR framework improves extreme weather diagnostics and addresses evaluation gaps via an atomic-subtask benchmark.

Abstract: While deep learning-based weather forecasting paradigms have made significant strides, addressing extreme weather diagnostics remains a formidable challenge. This gap exists primarily because the diagnostic process demands sophisticated multi-step logical reasoning, dynamic tool invocation, and expert-level prior judgment. Although agents possess inherent advantages in task decomposition and autonomous execution, current architectures are still hampered by critical bottlenecks: inadequate expert knowledge integration, a lack of professional-grade iterative reasoning loops, and the absence of fine-grained validation and evaluation systems for complex workflows under extreme conditions. To this end, we propose HVR-Met, a multi-agent meteorological diagnostic system characterized by the deep integration of expert knowledge. Its central innovation is the ``Hypothesis-Verification-Replanning'' closed-loop mechanism, which facilitates sophisticated iterative reasoning for anomalous meteorological signals during extreme weather events. To bridge gaps within existing evaluation frameworks, we further introduce a novel benchmark focused on atomic-level subtasks. Experimental evidence demonstrates that the system excels in complex diagnostic scenarios.

</details>


### [475] [FCN-LLM: Empower LLM for Brain Functional Connectivity Network Understanding via Graph-level Multi-task Instruction Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01135)
*Xingcan Hu,Wei Wang,Li Xiao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FCN-LLM aligns resting‑state fMRI functional connectivity networks with large language models via a multi-scale graph encoder and graph-level, multi-task instruction tuning, enabling zero-shot understanding of brain networks and outperforming prior supervised and foundation baselines on unseen datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Although LLMs and their multimodal variants excel at reasoning across text, images, audio, and video, brain functional connectivity networks (FCNs) from rs-fMRI are not aligned with text, preventing LLMs from directly understanding or reasoning over FCNs for clinical and neuroscientific tasks.

Method: A multi-scale FCN encoder extracts region-, subnetwork-, and whole-brain features and projects them into the LLM’s semantic space. The model is trained with graph-level, multi-paradigm instruction tasks spanning 19 subject-specific attributes (demographics, phenotypes, psychiatric conditions). Training follows a multi-stage strategy: first align FCN embeddings to the LLM, then jointly fine-tune the full model to capture higher-level semantics.

Result: On a large, multi-site FCN corpus, the approach shows strong zero-shot generalization to unseen datasets and surpasses conventional supervised methods and prior foundation models across evaluated tasks.

Conclusion: Integrating FCNs with LLMs via instruction-tuned, multi-scale graph encoding establishes a flexible and interpretable framework for neuroscience, offering improved generalization and potential clinical utility.

Abstract: Large Language Models have achieved remarkable success in language understanding and reasoning, and their multimodal extensions enable comprehension of images, video, and audio. Inspired by this, foundation models for brain functional connectivity networks derived from resting-state fMRI have shown promise in clinical tasks. However, existing methods do not align FCNs with the text modality, limiting the ability of LLMs to directly understand FCNs. To address this, we propose FCN-LLM, a framework that enables LLMs to understand FCNs through graph-level, multi-task instruction tuning. Our approach employs a multi-scale FCN encoder capturing brain-region, functional subnetwork, and whole-brain features, projecting them into the semantic space of LLM. We design multi-paradigm instruction tasks covering 19 subject-specific attributes across demographics, phenotypes, and psychiatric conditions. A multi-stage learning strategy first aligns FCN embeddings with the LLM and then jointly fine-tunes the entire model to capture high-level semantic information. Experiments on a large-scale, multi-site FCN database show that FCN-LLM achieves strong zero-shot generalization on unseen datasets, outperforming conventional supervised and foundation models. This work introduces a new paradigm for integrating brain functional networks with LLMs, offering a flexible and interpretable framework for neuroscience.

</details>


### [476] [AutoSkill: Experience-Driven Lifelong Learning via Skill Self-Evolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01145)
*Yutao Yang,Junsong Li,Qianjun Pan,Bihao Zhan,Yuxuan Cai,Lin Du,Jie Zhou,Kai Chen,Qin Chen,Xin Li,Bo Zhang,Liang He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AutoSkill is a model-agnostic plugin layer that turns users’ interaction histories into explicit, reusable, and shareable “skills,” dynamically injecting them into future tasks to achieve lifelong personalization without retraining the base LLM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Users exhibit stable, recurring preferences (e.g., reduce hallucinations, follow institutional styles, avoid overly technical language) that current LLM agents fail to retain across sessions, leading to lost experience and inconsistent personalization.

Method: Abstract skills from dialogue/interaction traces; represent them in a standardized, shareable format; manage a full skill lifecycle (derivation, maintenance, continual self-evolution); retrieve and inject relevant skills at inference time; operate as a model-agnostic plugin so existing LLMs can use these skills without fine-tuning.

Result: Provides the AutoSkill framework, architecture, lifecycle, and implementation details; shows how ephemeral interactions can be consolidated into explicit, composable skills that are portable across agents, users, and tasks; compatible with existing LLMs and positioned relative to memory, retrieval, personalization, and agentic systems.

Conclusion: AutoSkill offers a practical, scalable route to lifelong personalized LLM agents and digital surrogates by converting experience into persistent, transferable capabilities without modifying or retraining the underlying models.

Abstract: In practical LLM applications, users repeatedly express stable preferences and requirements, such as reducing hallucinations, following institutional writing conventions, or avoiding overly technical wording, yet such interaction experience is seldom consolidated into reusable knowledge. Consequently, LLM agents often fail to accumulate personalized capabilities across sessions. We present AutoSkill, an experience-driven lifelong learning framework that enables LLM agents to automatically derive, maintain, and reuse skills from dialogue and interaction traces. AutoSkill abstracts skills from user experience, supports their continual self-evolution, and dynamically injects relevant skills into future requests without retraining the underlying model. Designed as a model-agnostic plugin layer, it is compatible with existing LLMs and introduces a standardized skill representation for sharing and transfer across agents, users, and tasks. In this way, AutoSkill turns ephemeral interaction experience into explicit, reusable, and composable capabilities. This paper describes the motivation, architecture, skill lifecycle, and implementation of AutoSkill, and positions it with respect to prior work on memory, retrieval, personalization, and agentic systems. AutoSkill highlights a practical and scalable path toward lifelong personalized agents and personal digital surrogates.

</details>


### [477] [DeepResearch-9K: A Challenging Benchmark Dataset of Deep-Research Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01152)
*Tongzhou Wu,Yuhao Wang,Xinyu Ma,Xiuqiang He,Shuaiqiang Wang,Dawei Yin,Xiangyu Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They introduce DeepResearch-9K, a large, challenging dataset and DeepResearch-R1, an open-source RL training framework for deep-research web agents, yielding state-of-the-art results on tough benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep-research agents are strong but held back by (1) a shortage of large, realistic, hard datasets and (2) a lack of accessible, open-source frameworks for scalable data synthesis and agent training.

Method: Build DeepResearch-9K via a low-cost autonomous pipeline from open-source multi-hop QA sources, providing 9,000 questions across three difficulty levels (L1–L3), search trajectories with reasoning chains from Tongyi-DeepResearch-30B-A3B, and verifiable answers. Develop DeepResearch-R1, an open-source training framework supporting multi-turn web interactions, multiple RL algorithms, and reward models (rule-based outcome rewards and LLM-as-judge feedback).

Result: Agents trained on DeepResearch-9K within DeepResearch-R1 achieve state-of-the-art performance on challenging deep-research benchmarks in empirical evaluations.

Conclusion: Providing both a challenging dataset and a flexible, open-source RL training stack addresses key bottlenecks in training deep-research agents, enabling stronger performance and reproducible research; all data and code are released for the community.

Abstract: Deep-research agents are capable of executing multi-step web exploration, targeted retrieval, and sophisticated question answering. Despite their powerful capabilities, deep-research agents face two critical bottlenecks: (1) the lack of large-scale, challenging datasets with real-world difficulty, and (2) the absence of accessible, open-source frameworks for data synthesis and agent training. To bridge these gaps, we first construct DeepResearch-9K, a large-scale challenging dataset specifically designed for deep-research scenarios built from open-source multi-hop question-answering (QA) datasets via a low-cost autonomous pipeline. Notably, it consists of (1) 9000 questions spanning three difficulty levels from L1 to L3 (2) high-quality search trajectories with reasoning chains from Tongyi-DeepResearch-30B-A3B, a state-of-the-art deep-research agent, and (3) verifiable answers. Furthermore, we develop an open-source training framework DeepResearch-R1 that supports (1) multi-turn web interactions, (2) different reinforcement learning (RL) approaches, and (3) different reward models such as rule-based outcome reward and LLM-as-judge feedback. Finally, empirical results demonstrate that agents trained on DeepResearch-9K under our DeepResearch-R1 achieve state-of-the-art results on challenging deep-research benchmarks. We release the DeepResearch-9K dataset on https://huggingface.co/datasets/artillerywu/DeepResearch-9K and the code of DeepResearch-R1 on https://github.com/Applied-Machine-Learning-Lab/DeepResearch-R1.

</details>


### [478] [Semantic XPath: Structured Agentic Memory Access for Conversational AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01160)
*Yifan Simon Liu,Ruifan Wu,Liam Gallagher,Jiazhou Liang,Armin Toroghi,Scott Sanner*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces Semantic XPath, a tree-structured memory module for conversational AI that enables efficient, structured access and updates to long-term dialogue memory, outperforming flat RAG and dramatically reducing token usage versus in-context memory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In-context memory scales poorly due to context window limits, and common RAG approaches assume flat, unstructured memory, losing relational information needed for long-term, task-oriented conversations.

Method: Represent conversational memory as a tree and provide an XPath-like semantic query mechanism to retrieve and update nodes relevant to the current request; integrate this module into a ConvAI stack and release a demo (SemanticXPath Chat) that visualizes memory structure and query execution.

Result: Reported 176.7% performance improvement over flat-RAG baselines while using only 9.1% of the tokens required by in-context memory.

Conclusion: Tree-structured, queryable memory is a strong candidate for next-generation long-term, task-oriented ConvAI, offering better effectiveness and efficiency than flat retrieval or full in-context histories.

Abstract: Conversational AI (ConvAI) agents increasingly maintain structured memory to support long-term, task-oriented interactions. In-context memory approaches append the growing history to the model input, which scales poorly under context-window limits. RAG-based methods retrieve request-relevant information, but most assume flat memory collections and ignore structure. We propose Semantic XPath, a tree-structured memory module to access and update structured conversational memory. Semantic XPath improves performance over flat-RAG baselines by 176.7% while using only 9.1% of the tokens required by in-context memory. We also introduce SemanticXPath Chat, an end-to-end ConvAI demo system that visualizes the structured memory and query execution details. Overall, this paper demonstrates a candidate for the next generation of long-term, task-oriented ConvAI systems built on structured memory.

</details>


### [479] [Incremental LTLf Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01201)
*Giuseppe De Giacomo,Yves Lespérance,Gianmarco Parretti,Fabio Patrizi,Moshe Y. Vardi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Defines and tackles incremental LTLf synthesis, proposing an automata-based reuse method for efficient on-the-fly re-synthesis under new goals; analyzes a progression-based alternative with favorable automata-size bounds but poor performance if implemented naively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reactive systems often receive new objectives at runtime. In finite-horizon settings (LTLf), an agent must adapt its strategy mid-execution to satisfy both the original and newly arriving goals from the current state onward. Existing synthesis approaches assume fixed goals and do not exploit previously computed artifacts, motivating a formalization and efficient solution for incremental goal addition.

Method: 1) Formally define incremental LTLf synthesis. 2) Provide an automata-based approach that reuses auxiliary data structures created during standard synthesis to incrementally incorporate new goals without rebuilding everything from scratch. 3) Study an alternative approach via LTLf formula progression, analyzing the size of progressed formulas and their automata.

Result: - Theoretical: Although progression can blow up formula size exponentially, the minimal automata for the progressed formulas are bounded by the size of the original formula’s automaton. - Empirical: The structure-reuse automata-based method is efficient for multiple incremental goals; a naive progression-based implementation that recomputes automata from scratch is not competitive.

Conclusion: Incremental LTLf synthesis is both well-defined and practically solvable. Reusing automata-based artifacts yields efficient incremental re-synthesis, while progression has appealing theoretical properties but requires non-naive implementation strategies to be practical.

Abstract: In this paper, we study incremental LTLf synthesis -- a form of reactive synthesis where the goals are given incrementally while in execution. In other words, the protagonist agent is already executing a strategy for a certain goal when it receives a new goal: at this point, the agent has to abandon the current strategy and synthesize a new strategy still fulfilling the original goal, which was given at the beginning, as well as the new goal, starting from the current instant. In this paper, we formally define the problem of incremental synthesis and study its solution. We propose a solution technique that efficiently performs incremental synthesis for multiple LTLf goals by leveraging auxiliary data structures constructed during automata-based synthesis. We also consider an alternative solution technique based on LTLf formula progression. We show that, in spite of the fact that formula progression can generate formulas that are exponentially larger than the original ones, their minimal automata remain bounded in size by that of the original formula. On the other hand, we show experimentally that, if implemented naively, i.e., by actually computing the automaton of the progressed LTLf formulas from scratch every time a new goal arrives, the solution based on formula progression is not competitive.

</details>


### [480] [How Well Does Agent Development Reflect Real-World Work?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01203)
*Zora Zhiruo Wang,Sanidhya Vijayvargiya,Aspen Chen,Hanmo Zhang,Venu Arvind Arangarajan,Jett Chen,Valerie Chen,Diyi Yang,Daniel Fried,Graham Neubig*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Systematic audit of AI-agent benchmarks shows they overemphasize programming tasks and underrepresent where most human labor and economic value actually lie; authors propose principles to make benchmarks more representative and useful.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI agents are increasingly tested on benchmarks tied to human work, but it is unclear whether these benchmarks reflect the breadth and value distribution of real-world jobs. This uncertainty risks misdirecting research and overstating practical impact.

Method: Map benchmark instances to work domains and skills, then compare their coverage to U.S. labor-market data. Concretely: analyze 43 benchmarks comprising 72,342 tasks; align them with employment and capital allocation across 1,016 occupations; within targeted domains, assess agent utility via measured autonomy levels to inform interaction strategies.

Result: Significant mismatch: current agent development is programming-centric, while human labor and economic value are concentrated elsewhere. Within covered areas, agent autonomy varies, enabling guidance on when agents can operate independently versus require human-in-the-loop support.

Conclusion: Benchmarking today does not adequately represent socially important or technically challenging work. The authors recommend three measurable design principles—coverage, realism, and granular evaluation—to steer benchmarks toward better alignment with real labor-market needs and more actionable assessments of agent capability.

Abstract: AI agents are increasingly developed and evaluated on benchmarks relevant to human work, yet it remains unclear how representative these benchmarking efforts are of the labor market as a whole. In this work, we systematically study the relationship between agent development efforts and the distribution of real-world human work by mapping benchmark instances to work domains and skills. We first analyze 43 benchmarks and 72,342 tasks, measuring their alignment with human employment and capital allocation across all 1,016 real-world occupations in the U.S. labor market. We reveal substantial mismatches between agent development that tends to be programming-centric, and the categories in which human labor and economic value are concentrated. Within work areas that agents currently target, we further characterize current agent utility by measuring their autonomy levels, providing practical guidance for agent interaction strategies across work scenarios. Building on these findings, we propose three measurable principles for designing benchmarks that better capture socially important and technically challenging forms of work: coverage, realism, and granular evaluation.

</details>


### [481] [Agents Learn Their Runtime: Interpreter Persistence as Training-Time Semantics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01209)
*Victor May,Aaditya Salgarkar,Yishan Wang,Diganta Misra,Huu Nguyen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Training-agent traces should encode whether interpreter state persists: it doesn’t change final solution quality but strongly affects efficiency and robustness. Aligning training with deployment (persistent vs stateless) prevents errors and excess tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deployed CodeAct-style agents rely on runtime state across steps, but common fine-tuning ignores such execution semantics by treating traces as plain token sequences. The authors ask whether models can learn to use state persistence when training data makes it explicit, and what happens when training/runtime semantics mismatch.

Method: They isolate interpreter persistence as the only changing variable. Using Opaque Knapsack—a procedurally generated, partially observable optimization task that forces multi-turn tool use and iterative state updates—they create paired trajectories that differ only by whether interpreter state persists or resets each step. Holding tasks, prompts, tools, base model (Qwen3-8B), and supervision fixed, they fine-tune on each variant and run a 2×2 cross-evaluation across train/runtime persistence settings.

Result: Solution quality remains statistically indistinguishable across all conditions. But behavior diverges: a persistent-trained model in a stateless runtime throws missing-variable errors in ~80% of episodes; a stateless-trained model in a persistent runtime redundantly recomputes retained state, using ~3.5× more tokens. So execution semantics affect how solutions are reached, not whether they’re reached.

Conclusion: Interpreter persistence is a first-class semantic of agent traces. Fine-tuning with the same persistence semantics as deployment reduces brittleness and token overhead, whereas mismatches induce avoidable errors or inefficiency.

Abstract: Tool-augmented LLMs are increasingly deployed as agents that interleave natural-language reasoning with executable Python actions, as in CodeAct-style frameworks. In deployment, these agents rely on runtime state that persists across steps. By contrast, common training pipelines treat agent traces as token sequences, with execution semantics left implicit. This raises a data-centric question: Is state persistence merely an inference-time scaffold, or can models learn to exploit it when training data exposes the corresponding execution semantics?
  We isolate state persistence as a training-time variable. We introduce Opaque Knapsack, a procedurally generated family of partially observable optimization tasks designed to prevent one-shot solutions. Item attributes and constraints are hidden behind budgeted tool calls, forcing multi-turn control flow and iterative state revision. Holding task instances, prompts, tools, model, and supervision fixed, we generate paired trajectories differing only in whether interpreter state persists across steps or resets after each action. We then fine-tune identical base models (Qwen3-8B) on each trace variant and evaluate all four train-runtime combinations.
  Our 2x2 cross-evaluation shows that execution semantics primarily affect how agents reach solutions, not whether they do: solution quality is statistically indistinguishable across conditions, but token cost and stability differ substantially. A persistent-trained model in a stateless runtime triggers missing-variable errors in roughly 80% of episodes; a stateless-trained model in a persistent runtime redundantly re-derives retained state, using roughly 3.5x more tokens.
  Interpreter persistence should be treated as a first-class semantic of agent traces. Aligning fine-tuning data with deployment runtimes improves efficiency and reduces brittle train-runtime mismatches.

</details>


### [482] [A Unified Framework to Quantify Cultural Intelligence of AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01211)
*Sunipa Dev,Vinodkumar Prabhakaran,Rutledge Chin Feman,Aida Davani,Remi Denton,Charu Kalia,Piyawat L Kumjorn,Madhurima Maji,Rida Qadri,Negar Rostamzadeh,Renee Shelby,Romina Stella,Hayk Stepanyan,Erin van Liemt,Aishwarya Verma,Oscar Wahltinez,Edem Wornyo,Andrew Zaldivar,Saška Mojsilović*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a psychometrics-grounded, unified framework to assess AI systems’ cultural intelligence by defining core cultural domains, modeling cultural capabilities, and operationalizing them via measurable indicators, along with guidance on data, probing, and metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing cultural benchmarks for AI are fragmented and narrow, making it hard to comprehensively and reliably assess AI competence across diverse cultural contexts at scale. A principled, standardized approach is needed.

Method: Drawing on measurement validity theory, the authors decouple the construct (cultural intelligence) from its measurement. They: (1) offer a working definition of culture and identify its core domains; (2) conceptualize cultural intelligence as a suite of capabilities spanning those domains; (3) operationalize capabilities through indicators designed for reliable measurement; and (4) outline practical considerations for data collection, probing strategies, and evaluation metrics.

Result: A broad-purpose, systematic, and extensible assessment framework is articulated, including a domain taxonomy for culture, a capability-to-indicator mapping for measurement, and methodological guidance for building reliable evaluations. The paper surfaces key challenges and research pathways rather than reporting empirical performance results.

Conclusion: A unified, validity-aware framework can enable comprehensive, scalable measurement of AI cultural intelligence. Success depends on careful indicator design, robust data and probing practices, and ongoing research to address validity, bias, and contextual dynamism.

Abstract: As generative AI technologies are increasingly being launched across the globe, assessing their competence to operate in different cultural contexts is exigently becoming a priority. While recent years have seen numerous and much-needed efforts on cultural benchmarking, these efforts have largely focused on specific aspects of culture and evaluation. While these efforts contribute to our understanding of cultural competence, a unified and systematic evaluation approach is needed for us as a field to comprehensively assess diverse cultural dimensions at scale. Drawing on measurement theory, we present a principled framework to aggregate multifaceted indicators of cultural capabilities into a unified assessment of cultural intelligence. We start by developing a working definition of culture that includes identifying core domains of culture. We then introduce a broad-purpose, systematic, and extensible framework for assessing cultural intelligence of AI systems. Drawing on theoretical framing from psychometric measurement validity theory, we decouple the background concept (i.e., cultural intelligence) from its operationalization via measurement. We conceptualize cultural intelligence as a suite of core capabilities spanning diverse domains, which we then operationalize through a set of indicators designed for reliable measurement. Finally, we identify the considerations, challenges, and research pathways to meaningfully measure these indicators, specifically focusing on data collection, probing strategies, and evaluation metrics.

</details>


### [483] [The Lattice Representation Hypothesis of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01227)
*Bo Xiong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes that LLM embeddings instantiate a concept lattice: linear attribute directions with thresholds carve half-spaces whose intersections form FCA lattices, enabling geometric meet/join reasoning; WordNet experiments support this bridge between continuous geometry and symbolic abstraction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain how logical structure and hierarchies could be grounded in embedding spaces, unifying the Linear Representation Hypothesis with a rigorous symbolic framework (Formal Concept Analysis) to account for compositional concepts and reasoning in LLMs.

Method: Theoretical: show that linearly separable attribute directions with thresholds induce FCA concept lattices via half-space intersections; define geometric meet (intersection) and join (union) operations; prove a canonical form under linear independence. Empirical: probe LLM embeddings on WordNet sub-hierarchies to detect attribute directions, reconstruct lattices, and test meet/join correspondences.

Result: Derive a mapping from embedding geometry to FCA lattices and demonstrate, on WordNet subsets, that attribute-aligned directions and thresholds can reconstruct concept lattices; empirical probes indicate meet/join operations align with geometric intersections/unions and support aspects of logical structure in embeddings.

Conclusion: LLM embeddings can encode concept lattices that support symbolic reasoning through simple geometric operations, offering a principled bridge between continuous vector spaces and discrete abstraction; when attributes are independent a canonical representation emerges, lending support to the Lattice Representation Hypothesis.

Abstract: We propose the Lattice Representation Hypothesis of large language models: a symbolic backbone that grounds conceptual hierarchies and logical operations in embedding geometry. Our framework unifies the Linear Representation Hypothesis with Formal Concept Analysis (FCA), showing that linear attribute directions with separating thresholds induce a concept lattice via half-space intersections. This geometry enables symbolic reasoning through geometric meet (intersection) and join (union) operations, and admits a canonical form when attribute directions are linearly independent. Experiments on WordNet sub-hierarchies provide empirical evidence that LLM embeddings encode concept lattices and their logical structure, revealing a principled bridge between continuous geometry and symbolic abstraction.

</details>


### [484] [Extended Empirical Validation of the Explainability Solution Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01235)
*Antoni Mestre,Manoli Albert,Miriam Gil,Vicente Pelechano*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Cross-domain validation shows the Explainability Solution Space (ESS) generalizes beyond employee attrition to an urban resource allocation system, quantitatively ranking XAI method families and demonstrating that rankings adapt to governance roles, risk profiles, and stakeholder configurations rather than the problem domain itself.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a domain-agnostic, operational way to choose explainability strategies that align with governance roles, risk levels, and multi-stakeholder needs. Initial ESS validation was single-domain, so broader evidence was needed to establish generality across socio-technical systems and data modalities.

Method: Use two contrasting case studies: (1) employee attrition prediction and (2) a heterogeneous intelligent urban resource allocation system integrating tabular, temporal, and geospatial data under multi-stakeholder governance. For each context, quantitatively position representative XAI families within the ESS and compare how rankings vary with governance roles, risk profiles, and stakeholder configurations.

Result: ESS yields structured, quantitative rankings of XAI families in both domains. The rankings are not fixed by domain; they systematically shift according to governance roles, risk profiles, and stakeholder setups, confirming domain-independent applicability with context-sensitive adaptation.

Conclusion: ESS operates as a generalizable, practical decision-support framework for XAI strategy design across socio-technical systems, helping select explainability approaches across diverse data types and governance conditions.

Abstract: This technical report provides an extended validation of the Explainability Solution Space (ESS) through cross-domain evaluation. While initial validation focused on employee attrition prediction, this study introduces a heterogeneous intelligent urban resource allocation system to demonstrate the generality and domain-independence of the ESS framework. The second case study integrates tabular, temporal, and geospatial data under multi-stakeholder governance conditions. Explicit quantitative positioning of representative XAI families is provided for both contexts. Results confirm that ESS rankings are not domain-specific but adapt systematically to governance roles, risk profiles, and stakeholder configurations. The findings reinforce ESS as a generalizable operational decision-support instrument for explainable AI strategy design across socio-technical systems.

</details>


### [485] [Beyond Reward: A Bounded Measure of Agent Environment Coupling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01283)
*Wael Hafez,Cameron Reid,Amit Nazeri*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a principled, information-theoretic monitor (bipredictability P) for real-time health of RL control loops, showing earlier and more reliable detection of perturbations than reward-based monitoring on MuJoCo HalfCheetah.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reward- or task-metric monitoring detects failures only after performance drops and misses early interaction/coupling degradations when distribution shifts occur in closed-loop RL systems.

Method: Define bipredictability P as the ratio of shared information within the observation–action–outcome loop to total available information, with provable bounds and diagnostic components. Implement an auxiliary monitor, the Information Digital Twin (IDT), that estimates P online from the interaction stream. Evaluate on SAC and PPO agents in MuJoCo HalfCheetah under eight agent- and environment-side perturbations (168 trials), comparing detection to reward-based monitoring.

Result: Under nominal operation, agents have P ≈ 0.33 ± 0.02, below a theoretical 0.5 bound, suggesting an intrinsic informational cost of action selection. The IDT detects 89.3% of perturbations vs 44.0% for reward monitoring, with 4.4× lower median detection latency.

Conclusion: Bipredictability provides a task-comparable, theoretically grounded signal that enables earlier detection of closed-loop degradation than reward metrics and could serve as a prerequisite for self-regulation in deployed RL systems.

Abstract: Real-world reinforcement learning (RL) agents operate in closed-loop systems where actions shape future observations, making reliable deployment under distribution shifts a persistent challenge. Existing monitoring relies on reward or task metrics, capturing outcomes but missing early coupling failures. We introduce bipredictability (P) as the ratio of shared information in the observation, action, outcome loop to the total available information, a principled, real time measure of interaction effectiveness with provable bounds, comparable across tasks. An auxiliary monitor, the Information Digital Twin (IDT), computes P and its diagnostic components from the interaction stream. We evaluate SAC and PPO agents on MuJoCo HalfCheetah under eight agent, and environment-side perturbations across 168 trials. Under nominal operation, agents exhibit P = 0.33 plus minus 0.02, below the classical bound of 0.5, revealing an informational cost of action selection. The IDT detects 89.3% of perturbations versus 44.0% for reward based monitoring, with 4.4x lower median latency. Bipredictability enables early detection of interaction degradation before performance drops and provides a prerequisite signal for closed loop self regulation in deployed RL systems.

</details>


### [486] [Information-Theoretic Framework for Self-Adapting Model Predictive Controllers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01286)
*Wael Hafez,Amir Nazeri*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose an information-theoretic add-on to MPC—an Information Digital Twin that tracks mutual information among MPC inputs, control actions, and UAV dynamics—to detect shifts and adapt MPC parameters online, aiming for proactive, robust control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical MPC is optimized for a nominal model and relies mainly on error-based feedback, which can lag or fail under dynamic obstacles or changing system dynamics. The paper seeks a principled, self-monitoring mechanism to detect when the closed-loop dependencies change and to adapt MPC before performance degrades.

Method: Introduce Entanglement Learning (EL) using an Information Digital Twin (IDT) that computes new “entanglement” metrics—information-theoretic quantities (e.g., mutual information) between optimizer inputs, control outputs, and observed UAV behavior. The IDT monitors changes in these dependencies and issues adaptive signals to retune MPC parameters in real time, creating a dual-feedback loop (traditional error feedback + information-flow feedback). Designed to be scalable and to leverage existing MPC infrastructure.

Result: The abstract claims that the framework can detect performance deviations early, preserve stability via real-time parameter recalibration, and improve reliability and robustness across varying scenarios. The approach is positioned as broadly applicable beyond UAVs to general MPC deployments.

Conclusion: Augmenting MPC with information-flow monitoring may enable proactive, adaptive control under evolving conditions. If the proposed entanglement metrics can be computed reliably and used to safely retune MPC online, the method could generalize across domains and improve robustness; empirical validation and stability guarantees will be key.

Abstract: Model Predictive Control (MPC) is a vital technique for autonomous systems, like Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs), enabling optimized motion planning. However, traditional MPC struggles to adapt to real-time changes such as dynamic obstacles and shifting system dynamics, lacking inherent mechanisms for self-monitoring and adaptive optimization. Here, we introduce Entanglement Learning (EL), an information-theoretic framework that enhances MPC adaptability through an Information Digital Twin (IDT). The IDT monitors and quantifies, in bits, the information flow between MPC inputs, control actions, and UAV behavior. By introducing new information-theoretic metrics we call entanglement metrics, it tracks variations in these dependencies. These metrics measure the mutual information between the optimizer's input, its control actions, and the resulting UAV dynamics, enabling a deeper understanding of their interrelationships. This allows the IDT to detect performance deviations and generate real-time adaptive signals to recalibrate MPC parameters, preserving stability. Unlike traditional MPC, which relies on error-based feedback, this dual-feedback approach leverages information flow for proactive adaptation to evolving conditions. Scalable and leveraging existing infrastructure, this framework improves MPC reliability and robustness across diverse scenarios, extending beyond UAV control to any MPC implementation requiring adaptive performance.

</details>


### [487] [Opponent State Inference Under Partial Observability: An HMM-POMDP Framework for 2026 Formula 1 Energy Strategy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01290)
*Kalliopi Kleisarchaki*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Two-layer opponent-model + decision system for 2026 F1 energy strategy: an HMM infers rivals’ ERS/MOM/tyre state from public telemetry, and a DQN uses that belief to choose deployment; formally analyzes a “counter-harvest trap.” In synthetic races it infers ERS with 92.3% accuracy and detects traps with 95.7% recall; real-world test pre-registered for the 2026 Australian GP.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 2026 regs (50/50 ICE/battery, unlimited regeneration, driver-controlled override) make optimal deployment depend on hidden opponent states. This turns racecraft into a partially observable multi-agent problem where single-agent heuristics/thresholds can fail, motivating probabilistic inference over rivals and decision-making on beliefs.

Method: - Model the interaction as a partially observable stochastic game.  - Layer 1: 30-state HMM per rival to infer ERS charge, Override Mode (MOM) status, and tyre degradation from five public telemetry streams.  - Layer 2: a DQN consumes the belief state to pick among energy deployment strategies.  - Formal definition of a “counter-harvest trap” and proof that detection requires belief-state inference rather than reactive thresholds.  - Evaluation on synthetic races generated under the paper’s own assumptions; pre-registration for on-track validation.

Result: - ERS inference accuracy: 92.3% (vs. 33.3% random baseline).  - Counter-harvest trap detection recall: 95.7%.  - Formal characterization showing necessity of belief-state reasoning to detect deception.  - Claims of tractability in real time (implied), pending real-world validation.

Conclusion: Belief-state opponent modeling coupled with RL-based policy selection is promising for 2026-style energy strategy and can expose deceptive opponent tactics that beat threshold rules. Results are strong in-silico but rely on self-generated data; true value will hinge on on-track validation and robustness to modeling misspecification and adversarial opponents.

Abstract: The 2026 Formula 1 technical regulations introduce a fundamental change to energy strategy: under a 50/50 internal combustion engine / battery power split with unlimited regeneration and a driver-controlled Override Mode (abbreviated MOM throughout), the optimal energy deployment policy depends not only on a driver's own state but on the hidden state of rival cars. This creates a Partially Observable Stochastic Game that cannot be solved by single-agent optimisation methods. We present a tractable two-layer inference and decision framework. The first layer is a 30-state Hidden Markov Model (HMM) that infers a probability distribution over each rival's ERS charge level, Override Mode status, and tyre degradation state from five publicly observable telemetry signals. The second layer is a Deep Q-Network (DQN) policy that takes the HMM belief state as input and selects between energy deployment strategies. We formally characterise the counter-harvest trap -- a deceptive strategy in which a car deliberately suppresses observable deployment signals to induce a rival into a failed attack -- and show that detecting it requires belief-state inference rather than reactive threshold rules. On synthetic races generated from the model's own assumptions, the HMM achieves 92.3% ERS inference accuracy (random baseline: 33.3%) and detects counter-harvest trap conditions with 95.7% recall. Pre-registration -- empirical validation begins Australian Grand Prix, 8 March 2026.

</details>


### [488] [ASTRA-bench: Evaluating Tool-Use Agent Reasoning and Action Planning with Personal User Context](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01357)
*Zidi Xiu,David Q. Sun,Kevin Cheng,Maitrik Patel,Josh Date,Yizhe Zhang,Jiarui Lu,Omar Attia,Raviteja Vemulapalli,Oncel Tuzel,Meng Cao,Samy Bengio*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ASTRA-bench is a benchmark for evaluating tool-using, plan-executing AI assistants in evolving personal contexts. It generates 2,413 life-event-driven scenarios with complexity annotations and an execution environment. State-of-the-art models degrade sharply under high complexity, with argument (tool-parameter) generation as the main failure point.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most agent benchmarks are context-free, single-turn, and ignore the messy, time-evolving personal data and tool orchestration real assistants must handle. The paper aims to provide a realistic, diagnostic setting to test grounding, planning, and tool use together.

Method: An event-driven pipeline builds longitudinal narratives for four protagonists and produces interactive tasks tied to an external toolbox. Scenarios are annotated along three axes—referential, functional, and informational complexity—and run in a provided execution environment with evaluation scripts. Multiple state-of-the-art models are evaluated under varying complexity conditions.

Result: Across models (e.g., Claude-4.5-Opus, DeepSeek-V3.2), performance drops substantially as task complexity rises; the dominant error source is argument generation for tools, rather than high-level plan selection alone.

Conclusion: Today’s agents struggle to ground reasoning in rich, evolving personal contexts and to execute reliable multi-step plans. ASTRA-bench offers a unified, diagnostic testbed intended to spur development of truly context-aware assistants by exposing failure modes and enabling targeted improvements.

Abstract: Next-generation AI must manage vast personal data, diverse tools, and multi-step reasoning, yet most benchmarks remain context-free and single-turn. We present ASTRA-bench (Assistant Skills in Tool-use, Reasoning \& Action-planning), a benchmark that uniquely unifies time-evolving personal context with an interactive toolbox and complex user intents. Our event-driven pipeline generates 2,413 scenarios across four protagonists, grounded in longitudinal life events and annotated by referential, functional, and informational complexity. Evaluation of state-of-the-art models (e.g., Claude-4.5-Opus, DeepSeek-V3.2) reveals significant performance degradation under high-complexity conditions, with argument generation emerging as the primary bottleneck. These findings expose critical limitations in current agents' ability to ground reasoning within messy personal context and orchestrate reliable multi-step plans. We release ASTRA-bench with a full execution environment and evaluation scripts to provide a diagnostic testbed for developing truly context-aware AI assistants.

</details>


### [489] [Words & Weights: Streamlining Multi-Turn Interactions via Co-Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01375)
*Chenxing Wei,Hong Wang,Ying He,Zhongxiang Dai,Bo Jiang,F. Richard Yu,Yao Shu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ROSA2 jointly adapts both prompts (words) and model parameters (weights) at inference to fix interaction failures, showing large gains on MATH and fewer dialogue turns, with theory showing less parameter shift needed for convergence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Test-time policy adaptation for multi-turn LLM use often treats failures as either ambiguity (prompting) or incapacity (weights) alone. The paper argues these are coupled: clearer semantics enable more effective parameter updates, so both should be adapted together.

Method: Reformulate interaction as a joint optimization over heterogeneous spaces of words and weights. Decompose the error signal into components addressing intent ambiguity (via “textual gradients” guiding context/prompt refinement) and capability gaps (via test-time parameter updates). Provide theory that co-adaptation preconditions optimization and reduces required parameter movement.

Result: On MATH, ROSA2 beats strong baselines by ~30% and reduces required interaction turns by ~40%.

Conclusion: Co-adapting prompts and weights at test time synergistically improves alignment and efficiency: refining context unlocks the efficacy of parameter updates, yielding faster convergence with smaller weight changes.

Abstract: Test-time policy adaptation for multi-turn interactions (T2PAM) is essential for aligning Large Language Models (LLMs) with dynamic user needs during inference time. However, existing paradigms commonly treat test-time adaptation as a single-axis problem, either purely refining instructions (Prompt Engineering) or only adjusting weights (Test-Time Training), ignoring that interaction failures stem from a coupled mix of ambiguity and incapacity. We argue that these two optimization paths are not merely additive but synergistic: semantic clarity acts as a pre-conditioner for effective parameter updates. To this end, we propose ROSA2, a framework that reformulates interaction as a joint optimization problem over the heterogeneous space of Words and Weights. By mathematically decomposing the error signal, ROSA2 utilizes textual gradients to rectify intent ambiguity and parameter updates to bridge capability gaps. Theoretically, we prove that this co-adaptation strictly reduces the required parameter shift for convergence. Empirically, ROSA2 outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by 30% on MATH while reducing interaction turns by 40%, demonstrating that refining the context unlocks the true potential of parameter updates.

</details>


### [490] [HarmonyCell: Automating Single-Cell Perturbation Modeling under Semantic and Distribution Shifts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01396)
*Wenxuan Huang,Mingyu Tsoi,Yanhao Huang,Xinjie Mao,Xue Xia,Hao Wu,Jiaqi Wei,Yuejin Yang,Lang Yu,Cheng Tan,Xiang Zhang,Zhangyang Gao,Siqi Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HarmonyCell is an agentic framework that unifies heterogeneous single‑cell perturbation metadata with an LLM and adapts model architectures via MCTS to handle distribution shifts, achieving high execution reliability and state‑of‑the‑art out‑of‑distribution performance without dataset‑specific engineering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single‑cell perturbation datasets encode the same biological concepts under incompatible metadata schemas (semantic heterogeneity) and exhibit distribution shifts due to biological variation (statistical heterogeneity). These issues break automation and require bespoke modeling per dataset.

Method: Two coordinated components: (1) an LLM‑driven Semantic Unifier that autonomously maps disparate dataset metadata into a canonical interface; (2) an adaptive Monte Carlo Tree Search over a hierarchical action space to automatically synthesize architectures with inductive biases tailored to each dataset’s distribution. The system forms an end‑to‑end agent that ingests raw heterogeneous inputs and outputs executable modeling pipelines.

Result: Across varied perturbation tasks with both semantic and statistical shifts, HarmonyCell attains a 95% valid execution rate on heterogeneous datasets (vs 0% for generic agents) and matches or surpasses expert‑designed baselines in out‑of‑distribution tests.

Conclusion: By jointly solving metadata normalization and adaptive model design, HarmonyCell enables scalable, automated virtual cell modeling across datasets without manual, dataset‑specific engineering.

Abstract: Single-cell perturbation studies face dual heterogeneity bottlenecks: (i) semantic heterogeneity--identical biological concepts encoded under incompatible metadata schemas across datasets; and (ii) statistical heterogeneity--distribution shifts from biological variation demanding dataset-specific inductive biases. We propose HarmonyCell, an end-to-end agent framework resolving each challenge through a dedicated mechanism: an LLM-driven Semantic Unifier autonomously maps disparate metadata into a canonical interface without manual intervention; and an adaptive Monte Carlo Tree Search engine operates over a hierarchical action space to synthesize architectures with optimal statistical inductive biases for distribution shifts. Evaluated across diverse perturbation tasks under both semantic and distribution shifts, HarmonyCell achieves a 95% valid execution rate on heterogeneous input datasets (versus 0% for general agents) while matching or even exceeding expert-designed baselines in rigorous out-of-distribution evaluations.
