This dual-track orchestration enables scalable automatic virtual cell modeling without dataset-specific engineering.

</details>


### [491] [The Observer-Situation Lattice: A Unified Formal Basis for Perspective-Aware Cognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01407)
*Saad Alqithami*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a unified, finite complete lattice (Observer-Situation Lattice, OSL) to model perspective-aware beliefs in multi-agent settings, with efficient update and contradiction-handling algorithms, theoretical guarantees, and empirical evidence on Theory-of-Mind tasks, outperforming or matching ATMS-style baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fragmented handling of agent, time, and context leads to brittle belief reasoning, especially for Theory of Mind; a single semantic space is needed to coherently integrate perspectives and manage contradictions at scale.

Method: Define OSL, where each lattice element is an observer–situation pair; develop (i) Relativized Belief Propagation for incremental, perspective-aware information updates, and (ii) Minimal Contradiction Decomposition to isolate contradiction components via a graph-based procedure; prove soundness of the framework.

Result: Benchmarks on classic Theory-of-Mind tasks and comparisons with assumption-based truth maintenance systems indicate OSL is computationally efficient and expressive, enabling robust perspective-aware reasoning; theoretical soundness is established.

Conclusion: OSL offers a principled and scalable foundation for managing beliefs across perspectives, supporting efficient updates and contradiction localization, thus enabling more robust autonomous agents in complex multi-agent environments.

Abstract: Autonomous agents operating in complex, multi-agent environments must reason about what is true from multiple perspectives. Existing approaches often struggle to integrate the reasoning of different agents, at different times, and in different contexts, typically handling these dimensions in separate, specialized modules. This fragmentation leads to a brittle and incomplete reasoning process, particularly when agents must understand the beliefs of others (Theory of Mind). We introduce the Observer-Situation Lattice (OSL), a unified mathematical structure that provides a single, coherent semantic space for perspective-aware cognition. OSL is a finite complete lattice where each element represents a unique observer-situation pair, allowing for a principled and scalable approach to belief management. We present two key algorithms that operate on this lattice: (i) Relativized Belief Propagation, an incremental update algorithm that efficiently propagates new information, and (ii) Minimal Contradiction Decomposition, a graph-based procedure that identifies and isolates contradiction components. We prove the theoretical soundness of our framework and demonstrate its practical utility through a series of benchmarks, including classic Theory of Mind tasks and a comparison with established paradigms such as assumption-based truth maintenance systems. Our results show that OSL provides a computationally efficient and expressive foundation for building robust, perspective-aware autonomous agents.

</details>


### [492] [MIST-RL: Mutation-based Incremental Suite Testing via Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01409)
*Sicheng Zhu,Jiajun Wang,Jiawei Ai,Xin Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MIST-RL reframes LLM unit-test generation as a reinforcement learning problem that maximizes utility rather than quantity, using mutation-based incremental rewards and dynamic penalties to reduce redundancy; it raises mutation score by 28.5% with 19.3% fewer tests and improves reranking accuracy by 3.05% on HumanEval+.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs often fail to produce correct code on first attempt, so auto-generated tests are used as verifiers. Current methods scale by generating many tests, yielding diminishing fault-detection returns and heavy redundancy. The goal is to create fewer, higher-utility tests that better expose faults and verify code.

Method: Formulate test generation as a sequential decision process and optimize with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). Introduce an incremental mutation reward that credits discovering new faults and dynamic penalties that suppress functionally equivalent or redundant assertions, shifting from quantity to utility. Use mutation testing feedback to shape rewards during training.

Result: On HumanEval+ and MBPP+, MIST-RL surpasses SOTA baselines: +28.5% mutation score with 19.3% fewer test cases. As verifiers, these compact suites improve downstream code reranking accuracy on HumanEval+ by 3.05% using 10 candidate samples.

Conclusion: Utility-focused, RL-driven test generation yields compact, high-utility test suites that detect more unique faults and act as superior verifiers, outperforming brute-force scaling approaches; resources are available in the supplementary material.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) often fail to generate correct code on the first attempt, which requires using generated unit tests as verifiers to validate the solutions. Despite the success of recent verification methods, they remain constrained by a "scaling-by-quantity" paradigm. This brute-force approach suffers from a critical limitation: it yields diminishing returns in fault detection while causing severe test redundancy. To address this, we propose MIST-RL (Mutation-based Incremental Suite Testing via Reinforcement Learning), a framework that shifts the focus to "scaling-by-utility". We formulate test generation as a sequential decision process optimized via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). Specifically, we introduce a novel incremental mutation reward combined with dynamic penalties, which incentivizes the model to discover new faults while it suppresses functionally equivalent assertions. Experiments on HumanEval+ and MBPP+ demonstrate that MIST-RL outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. It achieves a +28.5% higher mutation score while reducing the number of test cases by 19.3%. Furthermore, we show that these compact, high-utility tests serve as superior verifiers, which improves downstream code reranking accuracy on HumanEval+ by 3.05% over the SOTA baseline with 10 candidate samples. The source code and data are provided in the supplementary material.

</details>


### [493] [GraphScout: Empowering Large Language Models with Intrinsic Exploration Ability for Agentic Graph Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01410)
*Yuchen Ying,Weiqi Jiang,Tongya Zheng,Yu Wang,Shunyu Liu,Kaixuan Chen,Mingli Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GraphScout is a training-centric agentic framework that equips LLMs with flexible graph-exploration tools to autonomously generate structured training data from knowledge graphs, enabling post-training that internalizes graph reasoning. It outperforms stronger LLM baselines by 16.7% on average across five KG domains while using fewer inference tokens and transfers well across domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GraphRAG approaches rely on hand-crafted guidance and a small set of predefined tools, limiting exploration of knowledge graphs and requiring labor-intensive curation. There is a need to improve factual grounding and reasoning over KGs without heavy manual design or high inference-time costs.

Method: Introduce GraphScout, an agentic graph reasoning framework with richer, more flexible graph-exploration tools. The agent autonomously interacts with KGs to synthesize structured training data, which are then used to post-train LLMs, thereby internalizing graph reasoning capabilities. The approach shifts effort to training rather than inference-time tooling. Evaluated across five KG domains against strong LLM baselines.

Result: A small model (e.g., Qwen3-4B) post-trained via GraphScout surpasses leading LLM baselines (e.g., Qwen-Max) by an average of 16.7% while consuming significantly fewer inference tokens. The method also shows robust cross-domain transfer.

Conclusion: Training-centric, agentic interaction with KGs can effectively imbue smaller LLMs with strong graph reasoning, reducing inference costs and dependence on manual guidance. Flexible graph tools improve exploration and reasoning, and the approach generalizes across domains; code release is planned.

Abstract: Knowledge graphs provide structured and reliable information for many real-world applications, motivating increasing interest in combining large language models (LLMs) with graph-based retrieval to improve factual grounding. Recent Graph-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation (GraphRAG) methods therefore introduce iterative interaction between LLMs and knowledge graphs to enhance reasoning capability. However, existing approaches typically depend on manually designed guidance and interact with knowledge graphs through a limited set of predefined tools, which substantially constrains graph exploration. To address these limitations, we propose GraphScout, a training-centric agentic graph reasoning framework equipped with more flexible graph exploration tools. GraphScout enables models to autonomously interact with knowledge graphs to synthesize structured training data which are then used to post-train LLMs, thereby internalizing agentic graph reasoning ability without laborious manual annotation or task curation. Extensive experiments across five knowledge-graph domains show that a small model (e.g., Qwen3-4B) augmented with GraphScout outperforms baseline methods built on leading LLMs (e.g., Qwen-Max) by an average of 16.7\% while requiring significantly fewer inference tokens. Moreover, GraphScout exhibits robust cross-domain transfer performance. Our code will be made publicly available~\footnote{https://github.com/Ying-Yuchen/_GraphScout_}.

</details>


### [494] [Securing the Floor and Raising the Ceiling: A Merging-based Paradigm for Multi-modal Search Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01416)
*Zhixiang Wang,Jingxuan Xu,Dajun Chen,Yunfang Wu,Wei Jiang,Yong Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Training-free cross‑modal model merging fuses a text-only search agent into a base VLM via Optimal Brain Merging (OBM), enabling zero-shot multi-modal search and providing a stronger warm-start that converges faster and achieves higher accuracy on search-heavy benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-modal search agents depend on costly supervised trajectories or unstable RL, causing high training cost, cold-start issues, and data demands. The goal is to endow standard VLMs with autonomous tool-use/search without additional multi-modal training data.

Method: Cross-modal model merging: combine parameters of a text-based search agent with a base VLM. Introduce Optimal Brain Merging (OBM), a saliency-aware algorithm that selects and merges task-critical parameters by estimating their impact on model loss using a small calibration set, mitigating parameter interference. No extra multi-modal training is required.

Result: On InfoSeek and MMSearch, simple model merging yields a solid zero-shot performance floor; OBM further improves search rates. When used as a warm-start, OBM leads to faster convergence and higher peak accuracy versus standard VLM initialization.

Conclusion: OBM-enabled, training-free cross-modal merging is an effective and practical way to equip VLMs with multi-step, tool-augmented search, alleviating cold-start and improving efficiency while boosting both zero-shot performance and downstream trainability.

Abstract: Recent advances in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have motivated the development of multi-modal search agents that can actively invoke external search tools and integrate retrieved evidence through multi-step reasoning. While promising, existing approaches typically rely on large-scale supervised trajectories or expensive reinforcement learning (RL), leading to high training cost, instability, and a severe cold-start problem for standard VLMs. We propose a training-free paradigm to empower VLMs with autonomous search capabilities via cross-modal model merging. By fusing a text-based search agent with a base VLM, we show that multi-modal search capabilities can be effectively composed without any additional multi-modal training data. To mitigate parameter interference during cross-modal integration, we introduce Optimal Brain Merging (OBM), a saliency-aware merging algorithm that identifies task-critical parameters based on their impact on model loss using only a small set of calibration samples. Extensive experiments on search-intensive benchmarks (e.g., InfoSeek, MMSearch) reveal that: (1) Model merging secures a reasonable performance floor as a zero-shot agent, with OBM achieving superior search rates; (2) OBM significantly raises the performance ceiling as a warm-start strategy, achieving faster convergence and higher peak accuracy than standard VLM initialization.

</details>


### [495] [SciDER: Scientific Data-centric End-to-end Researcher](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01421)
*Ke Lin,Yilin Lu,Shreyas Bhat,Xuehang Guo,Junier Oliva,Qingyun Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SciDER is a data-centric, end-to-end system of collaborating LLM-driven agents that parse raw scientific data, generate hypotheses and experimental designs, write/execute code, and outperform general-purpose agents on three benchmarks via a self-evolving memory and critic-led feedback loop. It is released as modular Python/PyPI packages with a lightweight web UI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM agents struggle to autonomously process raw experimental data and to ground hypotheses/experiments in data characteristics, leaving a gap for truly end-to-end, data-driven scientific discovery.

Method: A team of specialized agents that collaboratively: (1) parse and analyze raw scientific data; (2) generate data-grounded hypotheses and experimental designs; (3) write and execute corresponding code. The system incorporates a self-evolving memory to retain and refine knowledge across steps and a critic-led feedback loop to iteratively improve outputs. Delivered as a modular Python package with PyPI distributions and a lightweight web interface.

Result: Across three benchmarks, SciDER reportedly excels at specialized, data-driven scientific discovery and outperforms general-purpose agents and state-of-the-art models, with gains attributed to its self-evolving memory and critic feedback mechanisms.

Conclusion: SciDER advances autonomous, data-driven research by enabling end-to-end workflows from raw data to executable experiments and aims for broad accessibility via modular packaging and a web UI, suggesting potential to accelerate scientific discovery.

Abstract: Automated scientific discovery with large language models is transforming the research lifecycle from ideation to experimentation, yet existing agents struggle to autonomously process raw data collected from scientific experiments. We introduce SciDER, a data-centric end-to-end system that automates the research lifecycle. Unlike traditional frameworks, our specialized agents collaboratively parse and analyze raw scientific data, generate hypotheses and experimental designs grounded in specific data characteristics, and write and execute corresponding code. Evaluation on three benchmarks shows SciDER excels in specialized data-driven scientific discovery and outperforms general-purpose agents and state-of-the-art models through its self-evolving memory and critic-led feedback loop. Distributed as a modular Python package, we also provide easy-to-use PyPI packages with a lightweight web interface to accelerate autonomous, data-driven research and aim to be accessible to all researchers and developers.

</details>


### [496] [Decoding Answers Before Chain-of-Thought: Evidence from Pre-CoT Probes and Activation Steering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01437)
*Kyle Cox,Darius Kianersi,Adrià Garriga-Alonso*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Instruction‑tuned LLMs often decide the answer before generating chain‑of‑thought (CoT). Linear probes on the pre‑CoT residual stream predict answers with ~0.9 AUC, and causally steering those activations flips answers in >50% of cases. Induced errors reveal two failure modes—non‑entailment and confabulation—indicating CoT is frequently post‑hoc and not fully faithful.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CoT is widely used to improve LLM reasoning and is proposed as an interpretability tool, but its value depends on faithfulness—whether verbalized reasoning reflects the true decision process. The authors aim to test whether models actually determine answers prior to CoT and to assess the causal status of CoT with respect to decisions.

Method: Train linear probes on residual stream activations at the last token before CoT to predict the model’s final answer across tasks; evaluate predictive performance (AUC). Perform activation steering along the learned probe directions to test causal influence by observing answer flips relative to orthogonal baselines. When steering yields wrong answers, categorize error modes (non‑entailment vs confabulation).

Result: Probes achieve ~0.9 AUC in predicting final answers on most tasks. Steering activations along probe directions flips answers in >50% of trials, significantly exceeding orthogonal baselines. When steering induces incorrect outputs, two distinct failure modes emerge: non‑entailment (correct premises, unsupported conclusions) and confabulation (fabricated premises).

Conclusion: Instruction‑tuned LLMs frequently commit to an answer before CoT; CoT often functions as post‑hoc justification rather than a faithful window into computation. While CoT can be helpful when the pre‑CoT belief is correct, it may exacerbate errors when beliefs are wrong. Interpretability and safety practices should not assume CoT faithfulness and should consider probing/steering analyses and methods that promote rationale faithfulness.

Abstract: As chain-of-thought (CoT) has become central to scaling reasoning capabilities in large language models (LLMs), it has also emerged as a promising tool for interpretability, suggesting the opportunity to understand model decisions through verbalized reasoning. However, the utility of CoT toward interpretability depends upon its faithfulness -- whether the model's stated reasoning reflects the underlying decision process. We provide mechanistic evidence that instruction-tuned models often determine their answer before generating CoT. Training linear probes on residual stream activations at the last token before CoT, we can predict the model's final answer with 0.9 AUC on most tasks. We find that these directions are not only predictive, but also causal: steering activations along the probe direction flips model answers in over 50% of cases, significantly exceeding orthogonal baselines. When steering induces incorrect answers, we observe two distinct failure modes: non-entailment (stating correct premises but drawing unsupported conclusions) and confabulation (fabricating false premises). While post-hoc reasoning may be instrumentally useful when the model has a correct pre-CoT belief, these failure modes suggest it can result in undesirable behaviors when reasoning from a false belief.

</details>


### [497] [Scaling Tasks, Not Samples: Mastering Humanoid Control through Multi-Task Model-Based Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01452)
*Shaohuai Liu,Weirui Ye,Yilun Du,Le Xie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They argue that for online robotic learning, scaling the number of tasks (not samples per task) favors model-based RL because shared physical dynamics enable a single world model to learn across tasks. They propose EfficientZero-Multitask (EZ-M), which achieves state-of-the-art, highly sample-efficient performance on HumanoidBench without massive parameter scaling, highlighting task scaling as a key path to generalist robots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalist robots need to master many skills via interaction, but scaling parameters and offline data is ill-suited to robotics. Online learning should exploit cross-task invariances; dynamics are shared across tasks, suggesting that a single model can aggregate experience. Model-free methods face gradient interference when tasks conflict, motivating a model-based, multi-task approach that benefits from task diversity as a regularizer.

Method: Introduce EZ-M, a multi-task model-based RL algorithm for online learning that shares a world model across tasks to learn task-agnostic dynamics representations. It aggregates experience from many tasks and leverages EfficientZero-style planning/learning, aiming for high sample efficiency without extreme parameter scaling.

Result: On HumanoidBench (a challenging whole-body control benchmark), EZ-M attains state-of-the-art performance with significantly better sample efficiency than strong baselines, demonstrating advantages of task scaling for online multi-task learning.

Conclusion: Scaling the number of tasks provides a structural advantage for model-based RL in robotics by exploiting invariant dynamics, mitigating interference, and improving sample efficiency. EZ-M validates this, suggesting task scaling is a critical axis for building scalable, generalist robotic systems without heavy parameter inflation.

Abstract: Developing generalist robots capable of mastering diverse skills remains a central challenge in embodied AI. While recent progress emphasizes scaling model parameters and offline datasets, such approaches are limited in robotics, where learning requires active interaction. We argue that effective online learning should scale the \emph{number of tasks}, rather than the number of samples per task. This regime reveals a structural advantage of model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL). Because physical dynamics are invariant across tasks, a shared world model can aggregate multi-task experience to learn robust, task-agnostic representations. In contrast, model-free methods suffer from gradient interference when tasks demand conflicting actions in similar states. Task diversity therefore acts as a regularizer for MBRL, improving dynamics learning and sample efficiency. We instantiate this idea with \textbf{EfficientZero-Multitask (EZ-M)}, a sample-efficient multi-task MBRL algorithm for online learning. Evaluated on \textbf{HumanoidBench}, a challenging whole-body control benchmark, EZ-M achieves state-of-the-art performance with significantly higher sample efficiency than strong baselines, without extreme parameter scaling. These results establish task scaling as a critical axis for scalable robotic learning. The project website is available \href{https://yewr.github.io/ez_m/}{here}.

</details>


### [498] [ProtRLSearch: A Multi-Round Multimodal Protein Search Agent with Large Language Models Trained via Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01464)
*Congying Liu,Taihao Li,Ming Huang,Xingyuan Wei,Peipei Liu,Yiqing Shen,Yanxu Mao,Tiehan Cui*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ProtRLSearch is a multi-round, multimodal (protein sequence + text) search agent trained with multi-dimensional RL rewards to support protein-centric reasoning (e.g., disease variant interpretation). The authors also release ProtMCQs, a 3,000-question benchmark evaluating sequence-constrained and comprehensive protein reasoning across difficulty levels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clinical and research protein analysis requires reasoning under sequence constraints, but existing search agents are single-round, text-only, and use RL that rewards only final answers—offering little guidance on search steps and keyword selection, which leads to brittle reasoning.

Method: Develop ProtRLSearch: a real-time, multi-turn search agent that jointly ingests protein sequences and text, guided by multi-dimensional RL rewards that shape both process (e.g., keyword choice, reasoning trajectory) and outcomes; outputs high-quality reports. Construct ProtMCQs with 3,000 MCQs at three difficulty tiers covering tasks from sequence-constrained function/phenotype reasoning to integration of sequence features with pathways/regulatory networks.

Result: The work introduces the agent and the ProtMCQs benchmark and claims high-quality reporting; the abstract does not provide quantitative performance results or comparative benchmarks.

Conclusion: Incorporating protein sequences as a first-class modality and supervising search with process-aware, multi-dimensional rewards promises more reliable protein-centric inference; ProtMCQs offers a standardized way to evaluate such multimodal, multi-step reasoning systems.

Abstract: Protein analysis tasks arising in healthcare settings often require accurate reasoning under protein sequence constraints, involving tasks such as functional interpretation of disease-related variants, protein-level analysis for clinical research, and similar scenarios. To address such tasks, search agents are introduced to search protein-related information, providing support for disease-related variant analysis and protein function reasoning in protein-centric inference. However, such search agents are mostly limited to single-round, text-only modality search, which prevents the protein sequence modality from being incorporated as a multimodal input into the search decision-making process. Meanwhile, their reliance on reinforcement learning (RL) supervision that focuses solely on the final answer results in a lack of search process constraints, making deviations in keyword selection and reasoning directions difficult to identify and correct in a timely manner. To address these limitations, we propose ProtRLSearch, a multi-round protein search agent trained with multi-dimensional reward based RL, which jointly leverages protein sequence and text as multimodal inputs during real-time search to produce high quality reports. To evaluate the ability of models to integrate protein sequence information and text-based multimodal inputs in realistic protein query settings, we construct ProtMCQs, a benchmark of 3,000 multiple choice questions (MCQs) organized into three difficulty levels. The benchmark evaluates protein query tasks that range from sequence constrained reasoning about protein function and phenotype changes to comprehensive protein reasoning that integrates multi-dimensional sequence features with signal pathways and regulatory networks.

</details>


### [499] [Harmonizing Dense and Sparse Signals in Multi-turn RL: Dual-Horizon Credit Assignment for Industrial Sales Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01481)
*Haojin Yang,Ai Jian,Xinyue Huang,Yiwei Wang,Weipeng Zhang,Ke Zeng,Xunliang Cai,Jingqing Ruan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DuCA separates credit assignment for short-term linguistic quality and long-term commercial outcomes in RL for sales LLMs. Its HIAN module normalizes advantages per horizon before fusing them, preventing session-level rewards from swamping turn-level signals. In simulation, it beats a GRPO baseline with higher conversion and better language quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial sales assistants must optimize long-horizon business metrics (e.g., conversion) while obeying immediate linguistic constraints (fluency, compliance). Collapsing heterogeneous rewards into a single signal lets high-magnitude, delayed rewards overpower subtle, turn-level cues, causing instability and reward hacking.

Method: Dual-Horizon Credit Assignment (DuCA) decouples optimization across time scales. Horizon-Independent Advantage Normalization (HIAN) computes advantages from turn-level and session-level rewards, normalizes them separately, then fuses them so gradients from each horizon are balanced during policy updates. Evaluation uses a high-fidelity user simulator and compares against a GRPO baseline.

Result: Relative conversion rate improves by 6.82%, inter-sentence repetition drops by 82.28%, and identity detection rate falls by 27.35% versus GRPO in simulated experiments.

Conclusion: Disentangling and separately normalizing credit across horizons stabilizes training and yields better strategic and linguistic performance for sales LLMs. DuCA is a promising approach for balancing immediate conversational quality with long-term business goals.

Abstract: Optimizing large language models for industrial sales requires balancing long-term commercial objectives (e.g., conversion rate) with immediate linguistic constraints such as fluency and compliance. Conventional reinforcement learning often merges these heterogeneous goals into a single reward, causing high-magnitude session-level rewards to overwhelm subtler turn-level signals, which leads to unstable training or reward hacking. To address this issue, we propose Dual-Horizon Credit Assignment (DuCA), a framework that disentangles optimization across time scales. Its core, Horizon-Independent Advantage Normalization (HIAN), separately normalizes advantages from turn-level and session-level rewards before fusion, ensuring balanced gradient contributions from both immediate and long-term objectives to the policy update. Extensive experiments with a high-fidelity user simulator show DuCA outperforms the state-of-the-art GRPO baseline, achieving a 6.82% relative improvement in conversion rate, reducing inter-sentence repetition by 82.28%, and lowering identity detection rate by 27.35%, indicating a substantial improvement for an industrial sales scenario that effectively balances the dual demands of strategic performance and naturalistic language generation.

</details>


### [500] [Agentic Multi-Source Grounding for Enhanced Query Intent Understanding: A DoorDash Case Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01486)
*Emmanuel Aboah Boateng,Kyle MacDonald,Akshad Viswanathan,Sudeep Das*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Agentic, multi-source grounded LLM maps ambiguous marketplace queries to an ordered set of intents using catalog retrieval and autonomous web search, then applies deterministic disambiguation; outperforms ungrounded LLMs and a legacy system and is deployed at DoorDash at scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Marketplace queries are often short and ambiguous (e.g., “Wildflower”) and can denote multiple entities across verticals. Traditional classifiers force single-label predictions and LLMs can hallucinate non-existent inventory. A system is needed to resolve ambiguity reliably, avoid hallucinations, and support multi-intent outcomes across domains.

Method: A decoupled Agentic Multi-Source Grounded architecture: (i) staged catalog entity retrieval to ground to proprietary inventory; (ii) an autonomous agentic web-search tool for cold-start/coverage gaps; the model emits an ordered multi-intent set rather than a single label; a configurable disambiguation layer applies deterministic business policies and is extensible to personalization. The design allows marketplaces to plug in their own grounding sources and rules without altering the core system.

Result: On DoorDash multi-vertical search: +10.9pp accuracy over ungrounded LLM baseline, +4.6pp over legacy production. On long-tail queries: +8.3pp from catalog grounding, +3.2pp from agentic web search, +1.5pp from dual-intent disambiguation, for 90.7% accuracy (+13.0pp over baseline). Deployed in production serving >95% of daily search impressions.

Conclusion: Grounding foundation models in proprietary catalogs and real-time web knowledge, combined with multi-intent prediction and deterministic disambiguation, effectively resolves ambiguous queries at scale. The modular design generalizes across marketplaces and supports future personalization, establishing a reusable paradigm for context-sparse decision problems.

Abstract: Accurately mapping user queries to business categories is a fundamental Information Retrieval challenge for multi-category marketplaces, where context-sparse queries such as "Wildflower" exhibit intent ambiguity, simultaneously denoting a restaurant chain, a retail product, and a floral item. Traditional classifiers force a winner-takes-all assignment, while general-purpose LLMs hallucinate unavailable inventory. We introduce an Agentic Multi-Source Grounded system that addresses both failure modes by grounding LLM inference in (i) a staged catalog entity retrieval pipeline and (ii) an agentic web-search tool invoked autonomously for cold-start queries. Rather than predicting a single label, the model emits an ordered multi-intent set, resolved by a configurable disambiguation layer that applies deterministic business policies and is designed for extensibility to personalization signals. This decoupled design generalizes across domains, allowing any marketplace to supply its own grounding sources and resolution rules without modifying the core architecture. Evaluated on DoorDash's multi-vertical search platform, the system achieves +10.9pp over the ungrounded LLM baseline and +4.6pp over the legacy production system. On long-tail queries, incremental ablations attribute +8.3pp to catalog grounding, +3.2pp to agentic web search grounding, and +1.5pp to dual intent disambiguation, yielding 90.7% accuracy (+13.0pp over baseline). The system is deployed in production, serving over 95% of daily search impressions, and establishes a generalizable paradigm for applications requiring foundation models grounded in proprietary context and real-time web knowledge to resolve ambiguous, context-sparse decision problems at scale.

</details>


### [501] [LLM-assisted Semantic Option Discovery for Facilitating Adaptive Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01488)
*Chang Yao,Jinghui Qin,Kebing Jin,Hankz Hankui Zhuo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose an LLM-driven, closed-loop DRL framework that maps natural-language instructions into executable rules and semantically annotated options to enable skill reuse and real-time constraint monitoring, improving data efficiency, interpretability, and transfer across tasks; validated on Office World and Montezuma’s Revenge with superior performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional DRL struggles with data inefficiency, poor interpretability, limited cross-environment transfer, and policy sensitivity to environment changes that threaten safety/compliance. Prior indications that LLMs plus symbolic planning can help motivate a framework that leverages language and semantics to address these gaps.

Method: Integrate LLMs with symbolic planning in a closed loop: (1) translate natural-language instructions into executable constraints/rules; (2) automatically create options (temporally extended actions) and semantically annotate them for interpretability and reuse; (3) use LLM general knowledge to guide exploration and adapt options across similar environments; (4) monitor constraints in real time to ensure behavioral safety/compliance.

Result: On Office World and Montezuma’s Revenge, the framework shows superior data efficiency, better adherence to constraints, and improved cross-task transferability compared to baselines (no specific numbers provided in the abstract).

Conclusion: LLM-driven semantic skill reuse and real-time constraint monitoring can enhance DRL’s efficiency, safety/compliance, interpretability, and transfer, suggesting a promising direction for practical, trustworthy RL across related environments.

Abstract: Despite achieving remarkable success in complex tasks, Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) is still suffering from critical issues in practical applications, such as low data efficiency, lack of interpretability, and limited cross-environment transferability. However, the learned policy generating actions based on states are sensitive to the environmental changes, struggling to guarantee behavioral safety and compliance. Recent research shows that integrating Large Language Models (LLMs) with symbolic planning is promising in addressing these challenges. Inspired by this, we introduce a novel LLM-driven closed-loop framework, which enables semantic-driven skill reuse and real-time constraint monitoring by mapping natural language instructions into executable rules and semantically annotating automatically created options. The proposed approach utilizes the general knowledge of LLMs to facilitate exploration efficiency and adapt to transferable options for similar environments, and provides inherent interpretability through semantic annotations. To validate the effectiveness of this framework, we conduct experiments on two domains, Office World and Montezuma's Revenge, respectively. The results demonstrate superior performance in data efficiency, constraint compliance, and cross-task transferability.

</details>


### [502] [Multimodal Mixture-of-Experts with Retrieval Augmentation for Protein Active Site Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01511)
*Jiayang Wu,Jiale Zhou,Xingyi Zhang,Xun Lin,Tianxu Lv,Leong Hou U,Rubo Wang,Yefeng Zheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MERA is a retrieval-augmented, multimodal mixture-of-experts framework that predicts residue-level protein active sites, using reliability-aware fusion to outperform prior methods (up to 90% AUPRC) on benchmark datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Residue-level active-site identification is vital for function annotation and drug discovery, but current models struggle with sparse training signals for single proteins and with multimodal fusion that can be misled when unreliable modalities dominate.

Method: Introduce MERA: (1) hierarchical multi-expert retrieval that gathers contextual evidence from chain-, sequence-, and active-site–level sources; (2) residue-level mixture-of-experts gating to dynamically weight experts per residue; (3) reliability-aware multimodal fusion via Dempster–Shafer evidence theory with belief mass functions and learnable discounting coefficients to downweight untrustworthy modalities.

Result: On ProTAD-Gen and TS125, MERA achieves state-of-the-art performance, including 90% AUPRC on active-site prediction, and shows significant improvements for peptide-binding site identification.

Conclusion: Retrieval-augmented multi-expert modeling combined with Dempster–Shafer–based reliability-guided fusion mitigates data sparsity and modality unreliability, delivering SOTA residue-level active-site and peptide-binding predictions and offering a principled, generalizable multimodal framework.

Abstract: Accurate identification of protein active sites at the residue level is crucial for understanding protein function and advancing drug discovery. However, current methods face two critical challenges: vulnerability in single-instance prediction due to sparse training data, and inadequate modality reliability estimation that leads to performance degradation when unreliable modalities dominate fusion processes. To address these challenges, we introduce Multimodal Mixture-of-Experts with Retrieval Augmentation (MERA), the first retrieval-augmented framework for protein active site identification. MERA employs hierarchical multi-expert retrieval that dynamically aggregates contextual information from chain, sequence, and active-site perspectives through residue-level mixture-of-experts gating. To prevent modality degradation, we propose a reliability-aware fusion strategy based on Dempster-Shafer evidence theory that quantifies modality trustworthiness through belief mass functions and learnable discounting coefficients, enabling principled multimodal integration. Extensive experiments on ProTAD-Gen and TS125 datasets demonstrate that MERA achieves state-of-the-art performance, with 90% AUPRC on active site prediction and significant gains on peptide-binding site identification, validating the effectiveness of retrieval-augmented multi-expert modeling and reliability-guided fusion.

</details>


### [503] [Pharmacology Knowledge Graphs: Do We Need Chemical Structure for Drug Repurposing?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01537)
*Youssef Abo-Dahab,Ruby Hernandez,Ismael Caleb Arechiga Duran*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Under strict temporal validation and hard negatives, knowledge graph topology plus protein embeddings outperformed models that also used explicit drug chemical structure; more data helped more than bigger models, and several top predictions were externally validated.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify, with realistic temporal rigor, how model capacity, data volume, and feature modalities (especially explicit chemical structure) each contribute to KG-based drug repurposing performance, addressing overestimation from leakage and unclear value of structure encoders.

Method: Construct a ChEMBL 36 pharmacology KG (5,348 entities: 3,127 drugs, 1,156 proteins, 1,065 indications). Enforce a temporal split (train ≤2022; test 2023–2025) and mine biologically verified hard negatives from failed assays/clinical trials. Benchmark five KGE models and a 3.44M-parameter GNN using drug graph-attention structure encoders and ESM-2 protein embeddings. Conduct parameter (0.78–9.75M), data-scaling (25–100%), and feature-ablation studies; evaluate primarily with PR-AUC on drug–protein (and repurposing to indications) and measure compute (VRAM).

Result: - Removing the drug structure GAT and using only topological embeddings + ESM-2 proteins increased drug–protein PR-AUC from 0.5631 to 0.5785 and cut VRAM from 5.30 GB to 353 MB.
- Replacing the structure encoder with Morgan fingerprints further degraded performance, suggesting explicit chemical encodings can be detrimental for pharmacological network prediction.
- Model size >2.44M parameters yielded diminishing returns; more training data consistently improved performance.
- External validation: 6 of top 14 novel indications were later confirmed as established therapeutics.

Conclusion: Accurate pharmacological interaction and repurposing predictions can be achieved with target-centric information and drug network topology alone; explicit chemical structure representations may hurt under temporal evaluation. Emphasis should shift to data quantity/quality and temporal rigor rather than scaling model size or adding complex drug structure encoders.

Abstract: The contributions of model complexity, data volume, and feature modalities to knowledge graph-based drug repurposing remain poorly quantified under rigorous temporal validation. We constructed a pharmacology knowledge graph from ChEMBL 36 comprising 5,348 entities including 3,127 drugs, 1,156 proteins, and 1,065 indications. A strict temporal split was enforced with training data up to 2022 and testing data from 2023 to 2025, together with biologically verified hard negatives mined from failed assays and clinical trials. We benchmarked five knowledge graph embedding models and a standard graph neural network with 3.44 million parameters that incorporates drug chemical structure using a graph attention encoder and ESM-2 protein embeddings. Scaling experiments ranging from 0.78 to 9.75 million parameters and from 25 to 100 percent of the data, together with feature ablation studies, were used to isolate the contributions of model capacity, graph density, and node feature modalities. Removing the graph attention based drug structure encoder and retaining only topological embeddings combined with ESM-2 protein features improved drug protein PR-AUC from 0.5631 to 0.5785 while reducing VRAM usage from 5.30 GB to 353 MB. Replacing the drug encoder with Morgan fingerprints further degraded performance, indicating that explicit chemical structure representations can be detrimental for predicting pharmacological network interactions. Increasing model size beyond 2.44 million parameters yielded diminishing returns, whereas increasing training data consistently improved performance. External validation confirmed 6 of the top 14 novel predictions as established therapeutic indications. These results show that drug pharmacological behavior can be accurately predicted using target-centric information and drug network topology alone, without requiring explicit chemical structure representations.

</details>


### [504] [Graph-Based Self-Healing Tool Routing for Cost-Efficient LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01548)
*Neeraj Bholani*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Self-Healing Router is a deterministic, fault-tolerant orchestration layer for tool-using LLM agents that reframes most control flow as shortest-path routing over a cost-weighted tool graph with health monitors; it auto-recovers from tool failures without invoking the LLM and reserves the LLM only for infeasible paths, matching ReAct’s correctness while cutting control-plane LLM calls by 93% and eliminating silent failures across varied topologies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tool-using LLM agents face a reliability–cost dilemma: routing every step through the LLM improves correctness but is slow/expensive; static graphs are cheaper but brittle, especially under compound tool failures. There is a need for runtime fault tolerance, deterministic recovery, and explicit observability to avoid silent failures while minimizing LLM involvement.

Method: Design an orchestration architecture with: (i) parallel health monitors producing priority scores for runtime conditions (tool outages, risk signals), and (ii) a cost-weighted tool graph where Dijkstra’s algorithm yields deterministic shortest-path routing. On failure mid-execution, failed edges are set to infinite cost and the path is recomputed for automatic recovery. The LLM is invoked only when no feasible path exists, to demote goals or escalate. The focus is runtime fault tolerance with binary observability, distinct from prior graph planners centered on tool selection/planning.

Result: Across 19 scenarios covering linear pipelines, dependency DAGs, and parallel fan-out, the system matches ReAct’s correctness while cutting control-plane LLM calls by 93% (9 vs 123) and removing silent-failure cases seen in a strong static workflow under compound failures.

Conclusion: Treating agent control flow as deterministic routing with health-aware costs provides robust, low-cost tool orchestration. Automatic recovery and explicit escalation enable both high reliability and transparency, outperforming static workflows under failures while maintaining ReAct-level accuracy and greatly reducing LLM control overhead.

Abstract: Tool-using LLM agents face a reliability-cost tradeoff: routing every decision through the LLM improves correctness but incurs high latency and inference cost, while pre-coded workflow graphs reduce cost but become brittle under unanticipated compound tool failures. We present Self-Healing Router, a fault-tolerant orchestration architecture that treats most agent control-flow decisions as routing rather than reasoning. The system combines (i) parallel health monitors that assign priority scores to runtime conditions such as tool outages and risk signals, and (ii) a cost-weighted tool graph where Dijkstra's algorithm performs deterministic shortest-path routing. When a tool fails mid-execution, its edges are reweighted to infinity and the path is recomputed -- yielding automatic recovery without invoking the LLM. The LLM is reserved exclusively for cases where no feasible path exists, enabling goal demotion or escalation. Prior graph-based tool-use systems (ControlLLM, ToolNet, NaviAgent) focus on tool selection and planning; our contribution is runtime fault tolerance with deterministic recovery and binary observability -- every failure is either a logged reroute or an explicit escalation, never a silent skip. Across 19 scenarios spanning three graph topologies (linear pipeline, dependency DAG, parallel fan-out), Self-Healing Router matches ReAct's correctness while reducing control-plane LLM calls by 93% (9 vs 123 aggregate) and eliminating the silent-failure cases observed in a well-engineered static workflow baseline under compound failures.

</details>


### [505] [State-Action Inpainting Diffuser for Continuous Control with Delay](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01553)
*Dongqi Han,Wei Wang,Enze Zhang,Dongsheng Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SAID reframes delayed RL as a joint state–action sequence inpainting problem, unifying model-based dynamics priors with model-free policy optimization to plan directly; it delivers state-of-the-art, robust performance on delayed continuous control and works in both online and offline settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Signal delays break the Markov assumption in continuous control, making learning and control difficult. Existing solutions split into (1) model-free state augmentation and (2) model-based latent belief inference. The paper aims to bridge these paradigms to better handle delays without sacrificing policy performance or applicability across data regimes.

Method: Introduce State-Action Inpainting Diffuser (SAID), a generative framework that treats delayed RL as sequence inpainting over joint state–action trajectories. By integrating the inductive bias of dynamics learning with direct policy optimization, SAID implicitly models dynamics while generating coherent action plans. Its generative formulation enables straightforward use in both online and offline RL.

Result: On delayed continuous control benchmarks, SAID attains state-of-the-art results with strong robustness across tasks, outperforming prior model-free augmentation and model-based belief-tracking approaches.

Conclusion: Formulating delayed RL as generative sequence inpainting effectively marries model-based and model-free strengths, yielding robust, high-performing control under delays and offering a practical path forward for both online and offline RL.

Abstract: Signal delay poses a fundamental challenge in continuous control and reinforcement learning (RL) by introducing a temporal gap between interaction and perception. Current solutions have largely evolved along two distinct paradigms: model-free approaches which utilize state augmentation to preserve Markovian properties, and model-based methods which focus on inferring latent beliefs via dynamics modeling. In this paper, we bridge these perspectives by introducing State-Action Inpainting Diffuser (SAID), a framework that integrates the inductive bias of dynamics learning with the direct decision-making capability of policy optimization. By formulating the problem as a joint sequence inpainting task, SAID implicitly captures environmental dynamics while directly generating consistent plans, effectively operating at the intersection of model-based and model-free paradigms. Crucially, this generative formulation allows SAID to be seamlessly applied to both online and offline RL. Extensive experiments on delayed continuous control benchmarks demonstrate that SAID achieves state-of-the-art and robust performance. Our study suggests a new methodology to advance the field of RL with delay.

</details>


### [506] [S5-HES Agent: Society 5.0-driven Agentic Framework to Democratize Smart Home Environment Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01554)
*Akila Siriweera,Janani Rangila,Keitaro Naruse,Incheon Paik,Isuru Jayanada*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: S5-HES Agent is an open-source, agentic smart home simulator that uses interchangeable LLMs and a RAG pipeline to enable natural-language, end-to-end simulation setup. Evaluations show near-optimal knowledge retrieval, realistic device/threat behaviors aligned with real IoT datasets, and predictable scalability—positioning it as a foundation for Society 5.0 smart home research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Smart home research must broaden beyond expert developers to meet Society 5.0 goals across security, energy, health, climate, and socio-economics. Existing simulators demand high technical skill, are hard to adapt, and lack automated evolution—barriers that limit inclusive, efficient experimentation by diverse researchers and practitioners.

Method: An agentic simulation framework that orchestrates specialized agents via interchangeable LLMs to configure and run simulations from natural-language prompts. A retrieval-augmented generation pipeline combining semantic, keyword, and hybrid search supplies domain knowledge for configuration and scenario generation. The system supports end-to-end simulation without programming expertise.

Result: Comprehensive evaluation indicates: (1) the RAG pipeline achieves near-optimal retrieval fidelity; (2) simulated device behaviors and threat scenarios align with observations in real-world IoT datasets; and (3) the simulation engine scales predictably across different home configurations. Source code is provided under MIT license.

Conclusion: S5-HES Agent addresses the accessibility, adaptability, and automation gaps in current smart home simulators, helping democratize research aligned with Society 5.0. Its validated retrieval quality, realistic scenario modeling, and scalability suggest it can serve as a stable, extensible base for cross-domain smart home studies.

Abstract: The smart home is a key domain within the Society 5.0 vision for a human-centered society. Smart home technologies rapidly evolve, and research should diversify while remaining aligned with Society 5.0 objectives. Democratizing smart home research would engage a broader community of innovators beyond traditional limited experts. This shift necessitates inclusive simulation frameworks that support research across diverse fields in industry and academia. However, existing smart home simulators require significant technical expertise, offer limited adaptability, and lack automated evolution, thereby failing to meet the holistic needs of Society 5.0. These constraints impede researchers from efficiently conducting simulations and experiments for security, energy, health, climate, and socio-economic research. To address these challenges, this paper presents the Society 5.0-driven Smart Home Environment Simulator Agent (S5-HES Agent), an agentic simulation framework that transforms traditional smart home simulation through autonomous AI orchestration. The framework coordinates specialized agents through interchangeable large language models (LLMs), enabling natural-language-driven end-to-end smart home simulation configuration without programming expertise. A retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline with semantic, keyword, and hybrid search retrieves smart home knowledge. Comprehensive evaluation on S5-HES Agent demonstrates that the RAG pipeline achieves near-optimal retrieval fidelity, simulated device behaviour and threat scenarios align with real-world IoT datasets, and simulation engine scales predictably across home configurations, establishing a stable foundation for Society 5.0 smart home research. Source code is available under the MIT License at https://github.com/AsiriweLab/S5-HES-Agent.

</details>


### [507] [Benchmarking LLM Summaries of Multimodal Clinical Time Series for Remote Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01557)
*Aditya Shukla,Yining Yuan,Ben Tamo,Yifei Wang,Micky Nnamdi,Shaun Tan,Jieru Li,Benoit Marteau,Brad Willingham,May Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose an event-level evaluation framework for LLM summaries of clinical time series, using rule-derived “ground-truth” daily events from a dementia monitoring dataset. They show standard semantic metrics don’t reflect clinical event fidelity; a vision-based pipeline best matches events (45.7% abnormality recall, 100% duration recall), highlighting the need for event-aware evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs can produce fluent summaries of clinical time series, but it’s unclear if they faithfully capture clinically important, sustained abnormalities. Current evaluations emphasize semantic similarity and fluency, not whether key clinical events are correctly identified, described, and temporally characterized.

Method: Construct an event-based evaluation pipeline on the TIHM-1.5 dementia monitoring dataset: (1) derive structured daily clinical events using rule-based abnormal thresholds and persistence; (2) align model-generated summaries to these events; (3) compute metrics—abnormality recall, duration recall, measurement coverage, and hallucinated event mentions. Benchmark three summarization approaches: zero-shot prompting, statistical prompting, and a vision-based pipeline that uses rendered time-series visualizations as input to the model.

Result: Conventional semantic/linguistic metrics are decoupled from clinical event correctness; models with high semantic similarity can have near-zero abnormality recall. The vision-based approach yields the strongest alignment with ground-truth events, achieving 45.7% abnormality recall and 100% duration recall.

Conclusion: Event-aware evaluation is essential for reliable clinical time-series summarization. Vision-based pipelines may better capture clinically significant events than text-only prompting, and standard semantic metrics are insufficient to judge clinical fidelity.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) can generate fluent clinical summaries of remote therapeutic monitoring time series. However, it remains unclear whether these narratives faithfully capture clinically significant events, such as sustained abnormalities. Existing evaluation metrics primarily focus on semantic similarity and linguistic quality, leaving event-level correctness largely unmeasured.
  To address this gap, we introduce an event-based evaluation framework for multimodal time-series summarization using the Technology-Integrated Health Management (TIHM)-1.5 dementia monitoring dataset. Clinically grounded daily events are derived through rule-based abnormal thresholds and temporal persistence criteria. Model-generated summaries are then aligned with these structured facts.
  Our evaluation protocol measures abnormality recall, duration recall, measurement coverage, and hallucinated event mentions. We benchmark three approaches: zero-shot prompting, statistical prompting, and a vision-based pipeline that uses rendered time-series visualizations. The results reveal a striking decoupling between conventional metrics and clinical event fidelity. Models that achieve high semantic similarity scores often exhibit near-zero abnormality recall. In contrast, the vision-based approach demonstrates the strongest event alignment, achieving 45.7% abnormality recall and 100% duration recall.
  These findings underscore the importance of event-aware evaluation to ensure reliable clinical time-series summarization.

</details>


### [508] [RubricBench: Aligning Model-Generated Rubrics with Human Standards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01562)
*Qiyuan Zhang,Junyi Zhou,Yufei Wang,Fuyuan Lyu,Yidong Ming,Can Xu,Qingfeng Sun,Kai Zheng,Peng Kang,Xue Liu,Chen Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RubricBench is a curated benchmark of 1,147 difficult pairwise comparisons with expert-crafted, atomic rubrics to evaluate rubric-based reward modeling; experiments show a large performance gap between human- and model-generated rubrics, with SOTA models failing to specify valid criteria reliably.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM alignment is moving beyond simple completions toward complex generation, pushing reward models to use rubric-guided evaluation. The field lacks a unified, discriminative benchmark with ground-truth rubric annotations to rigorously assess this paradigm.

Method: Create RubricBench by filtering for hard cases with nuanced inputs and misleading surface biases using a multi-dimensional pipeline. Augment each case with expert-annotated, atomic rubrics strictly derived from instructions. Conduct comprehensive experiments comparing evaluations driven by human-annotated rubrics versus model-generated rubrics.

Result: Human-annotated rubrics yield significantly more reliable evaluations than model-generated ones. Even state-of-the-art models struggle to autonomously produce valid evaluation criteria, lagging notably behind human-guided performance.

Conclusion: RubricBench fills a critical benchmarking gap for rubric-based evaluation and shows that current models are not yet capable of reliably generating robust rubrics. Human oversight and expert rubric design remain necessary for trustworthy evaluation.

Abstract: As Large Language Model (LLM) alignment evolves from simple completions to complex, highly sophisticated generation, Reward Models are increasingly shifting toward rubric-guided evaluation to mitigate surface-level biases. However, the community lacks a unified benchmark to assess this evaluation paradigm, as existing benchmarks lack both the discriminative complexity and the ground-truth rubric annotations required for rigorous analysis. To bridge this gap, we introduce RubricBench, a curated benchmark with 1,147 pairwise comparisons specifically designed to assess the reliability of rubric-based evaluation. Our construction employs a multi-dimensional filtration pipeline to target hard samples featuring nuanced input complexity and misleading surface bias, augmenting each with expert-annotated, atomic rubrics derived strictly from instructions. Comprehensive experiments reveal a substantial capability gap between human-annotated and model-generated rubrics, indicating that even state-of-the-art models struggle to autonomously specify valid evaluation criteria, lagging considerably behind human-guided performance.

</details>


### [509] [Beyond Length Scaling: Synergizing Breadth and Depth for Generative Reward Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01571)
*Qiyuan Zhang,Yufei Wang,Tianhe Wu,Can Xu,Qingfeng Sun,Kai Zheng,Xue Liu,Chen Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Mix-GRM disentangles and trains two complementary Chain-of-Thought styles—Breadth (principle coverage) and Depth (soundness)—inside Generative Reward Models, then uses SFT and RL with verifiable rewards to adaptively switch between them by task, achieving new SOTA (+8.2% over leading open-source RMs across five benchmarks).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Simply making rationales longer improves GRM evaluation but ignores that different reasoning mechanisms work better for different task types. The authors aim to structure reasoning (breadth vs depth) and align it with task demands to improve reliability and performance.

Method: A modular synthesis pipeline restructures raw rationales into Breadth-CoT (multi-dimensional coverage) and Depth-CoT (rigorous judgment). Models are trained via Supervised Fine-Tuning to internalize each style and optimized with Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR). RLVR encourages the model to ‘switch’ and amplify the appropriate style per task.

Result: Across five benchmarks, Mix-GRM sets a new SOTA, outperforming top open-source reward models by an average of 8.2%. Analyses show Breadth-CoT favors subjective preference judgments, Depth-CoT favors objective correctness. Using the wrong style for a task harms performance. RLVR induces an emergent polarization that allocates reasoning style to task demands.

Conclusion: Reasoning structure matters more than sheer rationale length. Aligning Breadth vs Depth CoT to task type is crucial, and RLVR can serve as a switching amplifier that adaptively selects the right style. Code, data, and models are released for reproducibility and adoption.

Abstract: Recent advancements in Generative Reward Models (GRMs) have demonstrated that scaling the length of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning considerably enhances the reliability of evaluation. However, current works predominantly rely on unstructured length scaling, ignoring the divergent efficacy of different reasoning mechanisms: Breadth-CoT (B-CoT, i.e., multi-dimensional principle coverage) and Depth-CoT (D-CoT, i.e., substantive judgment soundness). To address this, we introduce Mix-GRM, a framework that reconfigures raw rationales into structured B-CoT and D-CoT through a modular synthesis pipeline, subsequently employing Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) to internalize and optimize these mechanisms. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that Mix-GRM establishes a new state-of-the-art across five benchmarks, surpassing leading open-source RMs by an average of 8.2\%. Our results reveal a clear divergence in reasoning: B-CoT benefits subjective preference tasks, whereas D-CoT excels in objective correctness tasks. Consequently, misaligning the reasoning mechanism with the task directly degrades performance. Furthermore, we demonstrate that RLVR acts as a switching amplifier, inducing an emergent polarization where the model spontaneously allocates its reasoning style to match task demands. The synthesized data and models are released at \href{https://huggingface.co/collections/DonJoey/mix-grm}{Hugging Face}, and the code is released at \href{https://github.com/Don-Joey/Mix-GRM}{Github}.

</details>


### [510] [CARE: Towards Clinical Accountability in Multi-Modal Medical Reasoning with an Evidence-Grounded Agentic Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01607)
*Yuexi Du,Jinglu Wang,Shujie Liu,Nicha C. Dvornek,Yan Lu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CARE is a modular, evidence-grounded agentic framework that pairs VLM reasoning with expert visual grounding and a coordinator to improve accuracy and accountability in medical VQA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end medical VLMs act as black boxes, diverging from clinicians’ evidence-based workflows and risking shortcut learning, hallucination, and low accountability. Explicit ROI evidence from expert grounding can improve trust and reasoning but is underused in current generalist models.

Method: Decompose reasoning and grounding into coordinated sub-modules: (1) a compact VLM proposes relevant medical entities; (2) an expert entity-referring segmentation model generates pixel-level ROI evidence; (3) a grounded VLM reasons over the full image augmented by ROI hints. Optimize VLMs via reinforcement learning with verifiable, evidence-aligned rewards. Optionally add a VLM coordinator to plan tool invocation and review evidence–answer consistency (CARE-Coord); a coordinator-free variant (CARE-Flow) omits planning/review.

Result: On standard medical VQA benchmarks, CARE-Flow (coordinator-free) improves average accuracy by 10.9% over a same-size (10B) SOTA. With dynamic planning and answer review, CARE-Coord achieves further gains, outperforming a heavily pre-trained SOTA by 5.2%.

Conclusion: Emulating clinical workflows with decoupled specialized modules and explicit, verifiable evidence yields more accurate and accountable medical reasoning than monolithic end-to-end VLMs; agentic control further enhances performance and reliability.

Abstract: Large visual language models (VLMs) have shown strong multi-modal medical reasoning ability, but most operate as end-to-end black boxes, diverging from clinicians' evidence-based, staged workflows and hindering clinical accountability. Complementarily, expert visual grounding models can accurately localize regions of interest (ROIs), providing explicit, reliable evidence that improves both reasoning accuracy and trust. In this paper, we introduce CARE, advancing Clinical Accountability in multi-modal medical Reasoning with an Evidence-grounded agentic framework. Unlike existing approaches that couple grounding and reasoning within a single generalist model, CARE decomposes the task into coordinated sub-modules to reduce shortcut learning and hallucination: a compact VLM proposes relevant medical entities; an expert entity-referring segmentation model produces pixel-level ROI evidence; and a grounded VLM reasons over the full image augmented by ROI hints. The VLMs are optimized with reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards to align answers with supporting evidence. Furthermore, a VLM coordinator plans tool invocation and reviews evidence-answer consistency, providing agentic control and final verification. Evaluated on standard medical VQA benchmarks, our CARE-Flow (coordinator-free) improves average accuracy by 10.9% over the same size (10B) state-of-the-art (SOTA). With dynamic planning and answer review, our CARE-Coord yields a further gain, outperforming the heavily pre-trained SOTA by 5.2%. Our experiments demonstrate that an agentic framework that emulates clinical workflows, incorporating decoupled specialized models and explicit evidence, yields more accurate and accountable medical AI.

</details>


### [511] [Evaluating and Understanding Scheming Propensity in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01608)
*Mia Hopman,Jannes Elstner,Maria Avramidou,Amritanshu Prasad,David Lindner*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They build realistic testbeds to measure when autonomous LLM agents actually "scheme" (covertly pursue misaligned goals). Despite strong opportunities, scheming is rare unless the agent is primed with adversarial, agency-inducing prompt snippets; even then, behavior is brittle and sensitive to tools and oversight. They also argue low scheming isn’t just evaluation awareness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work shows agents can scheme in principle, but we lack evidence about how often they do so in realistic deployment-like scenarios. Understanding true propensity requires disentangling agent-side design choices from environmental incentives to guide safer deployment.

Method: Propose an incentive decomposition into agent factors (e.g., agency, goal-directedness via prompt scaffolds) and environmental factors (e.g., tools, oversight, resource opportunities). Create realistic tasks with instrumentally convergent temptations (self-preservation, resource grab, goal-guarding). Systematically vary factors, introduce adversarial system-prompt snippets to induce agency, manipulate tool availability and oversight, and test for evaluation awareness. Use model organisms built from these snippets to probe scheming dynamics.

Result: Across varied high-incentive environments, observed scheming was minimal and unlikely explained by evaluation awareness. Adversarial agency-inducing snippets can drive high scheming, but such snippets are uncommon in real scaffolds. In snippet-based model organisms, scheming is highly brittle: removing one tool cut rates from 59% to 3%; surprisingly, more oversight sometimes increased scheming by up to 25%.

Conclusion: In realistic settings, current LLM agents rarely scheme by default, but certain prompt designs can trigger it, and the behavior is fragile to context and oversight. Their decomposition provides a practical measurement framework for deployment-relevant risk assessments, underscoring the need for careful scaffold design and nuanced oversight strategies as agents take on higher-stakes tasks.

Abstract: As frontier language models are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents pursuing complex, long-term objectives, there is increased risk of scheming: agents covertly pursuing misaligned goals. Prior work has focused on showing agents are capable of scheming, but their propensity to scheme in realistic scenarios remains underexplored. To understand when agents scheme, we decompose scheming incentives into agent factors and environmental factors. We develop realistic settings allowing us to systematically vary these factors, each with scheming opportunities for agents that pursue instrumentally convergent goals such as self-preservation, resource acquisition, and goal-guarding. We find only minimal instances of scheming despite high environmental incentives, and show this is unlikely due to evaluation awareness. While inserting adversarially-designed prompt snippets that encourage agency and goal-directedness into an agent's system prompt can induce high scheming rates, snippets used in real agent scaffolds rarely do. Surprisingly, in model organisms (Hubinger et al., 2023) built with these snippets, scheming behavior is remarkably brittle: removing a single tool can drop the scheming rate from 59% to 3%, and increasing oversight can raise rather than deter scheming by up to 25%. Our incentive decomposition enables systematic measurement of scheming propensity in settings relevant for deployment, which is necessary as agents are entrusted with increasingly consequential tasks.

</details>


### [512] [ToolRLA: Fine-Grained Reward Decomposition for Tool-Integrated Reinforcement Learning Alignment in Domain-Specific Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01620)
*Pengbo Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ToolRLA is a three-stage post-training pipeline (SFT → GRPO → DPO) for tool-using LLM agents that optimizes a multiplicative, fine-grained reward over tool calls (format, selection, efficiency, compliance). In a live financial advisory copilot with 15+ APIs, it boosts task completion from 62% to 91%, slashes tool errors and regulatory violations, and keeps sub-2s latency; ablations and public benchmarks support the design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Binary success/failure rewards poorly guide nuanced tool invocation and regulatory adherence in high-stakes, domain-specific deployments. The paper seeks a training signal that captures step-level tool correctness and compliance to make production agents both effective and safe.

Method: A post-training stack: (1) Supervised Fine-Tuning on domain data; (2) Group Relative Policy Optimization using a fine-grained, multiplicatively composed reward across four dimensions—format validity, tool selection correctness (prioritized), invocation efficiency, and domain compliance—with a strong negative penalty (λ=10) for violations; (3) Direct Preference Optimization to align with preferences. Evaluated in production and on ToolBench/API-Bank, with ablations contrasting multiplicative vs additive rewards.

Result: In production (80+ advisors, ~1.2k daily queries, 15+ APIs): end-to-end completion 62% → 91% (+47% relative), tool invocation errors 38% → 14% (−63%), regulatory violations 12% → 0.8% (−93%), latency <2s over 3 months. Ablations attribute +7 pp to fine-grained multiplicative rewards; external benchmarks indicate generalization.

Conclusion: Fine-grained, multiplicative reward shaping within an SFT→GRPO→DPO pipeline materially improves accuracy, efficiency, and safety of tool-integrated agents in regulated domains. Emphasizing correct tool selection and heavy compliance penalties is key; the approach generalizes beyond the deployment domain while maintaining practical latency.

Abstract: Tool-integrated reasoning agents interleaving natural language deliberation with external API calls show promise for complex multi-step tasks. However, aligning such agents for high-stakes domain-specific deployment is challenging, as existing reinforcement learning uses coarse binary rewards (success/failure) that insufficiently guide nuanced tool invocation in production. We present ToolRLA, a three-stage post-training pipeline (Supervised Fine-Tuning, Group Relative Policy Optimization, Direct Preference Optimization) for domain-specific tool-integrated agents. Its core is a fine-grained reward function with multiplicative correctness decomposition, evaluating tool invocation across four dimensions: format validity, tool selection correctness, invocation efficiency, and domain constraint compliance. Multiplicative composition prioritizes correct tool selection (a prerequisite for meaningful parameter evaluation), while a large negative compliance penalty (λ=10) ensures regulatory adherence. Deployed on a real-world financial advisory copilot (80+ advisors, 1,200+ daily queries, 15+ heterogeneous APIs), ToolRLA achieves 47% higher end-to-end task completion (62% to 91%), 63% lower tool invocation error (38% to 14%), 93% lower regulatory violation (12% to 0.8%), and sub-2-second latency after three months. Ablation studies confirm fine-grained reward decomposition contributes 7 percentage points over coarse additive rewards; generalizability is validated on ToolBench and API-Bank.

</details>


### [513] [SEED-SET: Scalable Evolving Experimental Design for System-level Ethical Testing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01630)
*Anjali Parashar,Yingke Li,Eric Yang Yu,Fei Chen,James Neidhoefer,Devesh Upadhyay,Chuchu Fan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SEED-SET is a Bayesian experimental design framework that fuses objective metrics and stakeholder value judgments via hierarchical Gaussian Processes and a new acquisition strategy to ethically benchmark autonomous systems, yielding more optimal test cases and better search coverage than baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous systems in safety-critical, human-centric settings need rigorous ethical alignment checks. Automated benchmarking is hard due to missing universal metrics and stakeholder-dependent, subjective preferences that resist analytic modeling.

Method: Model objective evaluations and subjective stakeholder preferences separately with hierarchical Gaussian Processes; learn qualitative preferences and objectives; use a novel acquisition function to balance exploration–exploitation and propose ethically informative test candidates aligned with stakeholder values.

Result: On two application domains, SEED-SET outperforms baselines: produces up to 2× more optimal test candidates and achieves 1.25× better coverage of high-dimensional search spaces, while offering interpretable trade-offs between exploration and exploitation.

Conclusion: SEED-SET provides an interpretable, efficient framework for ethical benchmarking of autonomous agents that integrates subjective stakeholder values with objective metrics, improving test generation and coverage and supporting safer, less biased deployments.

Abstract: As autonomous systems such as drones, become increasingly deployed in high-stakes, human-centric domains, it is critical to evaluate the ethical alignment since failure to do so imposes imminent danger to human lives, and long term bias in decision-making. Automated ethical benchmarking of these systems is understudied due to the lack of ubiquitous, well-defined metrics for evaluation, and stakeholder-specific subjectivity, which cannot be modeled analytically. To address these challenges, we propose SEED-SET, a Bayesian experimental design framework that incorporates domain-specific objective evaluations, and subjective value judgments from stakeholders. SEED-SET models both evaluation types separately with hierarchical Gaussian Processes, and uses a novel acquisition strategy to propose interesting test candidates based on learnt qualitative preferences and objectives that align with the stakeholder preferences. We validate our approach for ethical benchmarking of autonomous agents on two applications and find our method to perform the best. Our method provides an interpretable and efficient trade-off between exploration and exploitation, by generating up to $2\times$ optimal test candidates compared to baselines, with $1.25\times$ improvement in coverage of high dimensional search spaces.

</details>


### [514] [Learning Structured Reasoning via Tractable Trajectory Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01641)
*Po-Nien Kung,Zhen Yang,Jeffrey Luo,Cheng-Fu Yang,Haikang Deng,Zi-Yi Dou,Yinfei Yang,Nanyun Peng,Zhe Gan,Kai-Wei Chang*

Main category: cs.AI
